topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classification ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mass Transfer Operations (MTO)** are defined as those **unit operations** in chemical engineering in which one or more components of a mixture are transferred from one phase to another, across a **concentration gradient**, without any chemical reaction occurring in the process. The transfer is driven purely by a **chemical potential gradient** (or practically, a **concentration gradient** for isothermal systems). **Classification** of Mass Transfer Operations refers to the systematic grouping of these unit operations based on criteria such as the **number and nature of phases involved**, the **direction of mass transfer**, the **state (steady or unsteady) of the system**, and the **degree of equilibrium attained per stage**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a crowded coffee shop (Phase A) on a rainy morning.** Suddenly, the aroma of freshly brewed coffee starts spreading into the street (Phase B). Why? Because coffee molecules are "jumping" from the high-concentration cup into the low-concentration air. This is **mass transfer** — components moving from a region of high chemical potential to one of low chemical potential until equilibrium is reached. Just like how we categorize transport phenomena in everyday life: - **Water boiling in a covered pan** → Liquid → Vapor (Vaporization) - **Sugar dissolving in tea** → Solid → Liquid (Leaching) - **Drying wet clothes on a line** → Liquid → Vapor (from solid surface) The "Classification" framework simply gives engineers a structured taxonomy so that the **correct mathematical model**, **equipment**, and **design equations** can be selected for each scenario. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Mass Transfer Flux, $N_A$** | $\text{kmol}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$ | Rate of mass transfer per unit area | | **Concentration, $C_A$** | $\text{kmol}/\text{m}^3$ | Molar quantity per unit volume | | **Diffusivity, $D_{AB}$** | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Resistance to molecular motion | | **Driving Force, $\Delta y$ or $\Delta x$** | mole fraction | Difference in chemical potential | --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Mass Transfer Operations Mass transfer operations are classified based on multiple criteria. Below is the systematic breakdown required for KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation. #### **Criterion 1: Based on the Phases Involved** > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **most commonly tested** classification in KTU Board Exams. | # | Operation | From Phase | To Phase | Industrial Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Distillation** | Liquid | Vapor | Crude oil refining | | 2 | **Absorption** | Gas | Liquid | SO₂ removal using water | | 3 | **Stripping (Desorption)** | Liquid | Gas | Removing NH₃ from wastewater | | 4 | **Humidification** | Liquid | Gas | Cooling tower operation | | 5 | **Dehumidification** | Gas | Liquid | Condensation of vapor | | 6 | **Drying** | Solid (wet) | Gas | Paddy drying, paper drying | | 7 | **Leaching** | Solid | Liquid | Extraction of oil from seeds | | 8 | **Extraction (LLE)** | Liquid | Liquid | Separation of aromatics from paraffins | | 9 | **Adsorption** | Fluid (G/L) | Solid | Activated charcoal filters | | 10 | **Crystallization** | Liquid (solution) | Solid | Sugar crystallization | | 11 | **Membrane Separation** | Fluid | Fluid (via membrane) | Reverse osmosis, dialysis | | 12 | **Sublimation** | Solid | Vapor | Freeze-drying of foods | #### **Criterion 2: Based on the Direction of Mass Transfer** - **Unidirectional**: Component moves from Phase 1 → Phase 2 only (e.g., pure absorption of a gas into a non-volatile solvent). - **Counter-Directional / Bidirectional**: Both phases exchange components (e.g., distillation, where the more volatile component moves from liquid to vapor AND the less volatile component moves from vapor to liquid). #### **Criterion 3: Based on the State of the System** - **Steady-State**: Flux and concentration profiles do not change with time. $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = 0$ - **Unsteady-State (Transient)**: Profiles vary with time, typical in batch operations like drying, leaching. #### **Criterion 4: Based on the Mode of Contact** - **Stage-wise Contact**: Phases are brought into equilibrium in discrete stages (e.g., tray columns in distillation). Operates with **equilibrium stages**. - **Differential (Continuous) Contact**: Phases flow continuously against each other with no discrete equilibrium (e.g., packed columns). #### **Criterion 5: Based on the Driving Force Direction** - **Positive (Co-current) System**: Bulk flow and diffusion occur in the same direction. - **Negative (Counter-current) System**: Bulk flow opposes molecular diffusion — the most common industrial setup. #### **Criterion 6: Based on the Number of Components** - **Binary Systems**: Two components (e.g., benzene–toluene). - **Multicomponent Systems**: More than two components (e.g., crude oil distillation). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table gives the **fundamental flux equations** that students must memorize and apply across all classified operations. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown parsing errors. | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Application | |---|---|---| | **Fick's First Law (Molar Basis)** | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$ | Steady molecular diffusion | | **Equimolar Counter-Diffusion** | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{(C_{A2} - C_{A1})}{z}$ | $N_A = -N_B$ | | **Diffusion through Stagnant B (A → B stagnant)** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot z} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{B2}}{1 - y_{B1}}\right)$ | Absorption, humidification | | **Equilibrium Relation** | $y^* = m \cdot x$ | Linear equilibrium (Henry's law form) | | **Stage Efficiency (Murphree)** | $E_{M} = \dfrac{y_{n} - y_{n+1}}{y^*_{n} - y_{n+1}}$ | Tray column performance | | **Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient** | $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ | Two-film theory | | **Mole Fraction Constraint** | $\sum x_i = \sum y_i = 1$ | All classification problems | > [!TIP] > **Real-world utility:** This classification system is the foundation for selecting equipment. For example, a **packed tower** is preferred for absorption (gas–liquid), a **rotary dryer** for solid drying, and a **mixer-settler** for liquid–liquid extraction. Misclassification leads to severe design errors in real chemical plants. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Fick's First Law of Molecular Diffusion (Steady State) This is the **governing equation** behind virtually every classified mass transfer operation. It is essential for KTU Part-B questions. **Step 1 — Statement of the Hypothesis** For steady-state diffusion of component A through a stagnant film of component B, the molar flux of A is directly proportional to its concentration gradient. $$ N_A \;\propto\; -\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} $$ **Step 2 — Introducing the Proportionality Constant** Introducing the proportionality constant called the **mass diffusivity** $D_{AB}$ (in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$): $$ N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} $$ **Step 3 — Accounting for Bulk (Convective) Flow** When bulk flow occurs in the same direction as diffusion, the convective contribution $x_A(N_A + N_B)$ must be added. The total flux becomes: $$ N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\,(N_A + N_B) $$ **Step 4 — Solving for the Case of Stagnant B ($N_B = 0$)** When component B is stagnant, $N_B = 0$, so $x_A(N_A + N_B) = x_A N_A$. $$ N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} $$ Expressing in terms of gas-phase mole fraction $y_A = \dfrac{C_A}{C}$, with $C = \dfrac{P}{RT}$: $$ N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T (1 - y_A)} \cdot \dfrac{dy_A}{dz} $$ **Step 5 — Integration Across the Stagnant Film (from $z = 0$ to $z = z$)** $$ \int_{0}^{z} N_A\, dz = -\dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T} \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} \dfrac{dy_A}{1 - y_A} $$ Since $y_B = 1 - y_A$, the right-hand side becomes: $$ N_A \cdot z = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right) $$ **Step 6 — Final Steady-State Flux Equation for A through Stagnant B** $$ \boxed{\,N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, z}\;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)\,} $$ **Step 7 — Interpretation** - As $y_{A1} \to 0$, $\ln(1 - y_{A2}) \approx -y_{A2}$, simplifying to $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$. - This is the **dilute solution form** used in nearly all absorber design problems. ### 3.2 Derivation: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMD) For EMD, $N_A = -N_B$ (typical of binary distillation). The net bulk flow is zero, so: $$ N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} $$ Integrating across film thickness $z$: $$ N_A \cdot z = -D_{AB} (C_{A2} - C_{A1}) $$ $$ \boxed{\,N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z}\,(C_{A1} - C_{A2})\,} $$ This simple linear form is the **foundation of the McCabe–Thiele method** in distillation classification. ### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style) **Problem:** Ammonia ($A$) is absorbed from a gas mixture into water at $25\,\text{°C}$. The total pressure is $1\,\text{atm}$, and the stagnant gas film has thickness $z = 0.01\,\text{m}$. The gas-phase ammonia mole fractions at the two sides of the film are $y_{A1} = 0.20$ and $y_{A2} = 0.05$. Given $D_{AB} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, calculate the steady-state flux of ammonia. **Solution (Step-by-Step):** $$ N_A = \dfrac{(2.0 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.01)} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - 0.05}{1 - 0.20}\right) $$ $$ N_A = \dfrac{(2.0 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.01)} \cdot \ln(1.1875) $$ $$ N_A = 8.174 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \ln(1.1875) = 8.174 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.17185 $$ $$ \boxed{\,N_A \approx 1.405 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{kmol}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always carry out the unit analysis:** $\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{Pa}}{\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mol}} \cdot \text{K} \cdot \text{m}} = \dfrac{\text{mol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}$. This confirms dimensional consistency for KTU valuation. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Mass Transfer Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Mass Transfer Operations] --> B1[Based on Phases] A0 --> B2[Based on Direction] A0 --> B3[Based on System State] A0 --> B4[Based on Contact Mode] B1 --> C1A[Gas to Liquid
Absorption Stripping] B1 --> C1B[Liquid to Vapor
Distillation Evaporation] B1 --> C1C[Solid to Fluid
Leaching Drying Adsorption] B1 --> C1D[Fluid to Fluid
Extraction Membrane Separation] B2 --> C2A[Unidirectional
Absorption] B2 --> C2B[Bidirectional
Distillation] B3 --> C3A[Steady State
Continuous Columns] B3 --> C3B[Unsteady State
Batch Drying] B4 --> C4A[Stage Wise
Tray Columns] B4 --> C4B[Differential Contact
Packed Columns] C1A --> D1[Selection of
Equipment and Model] C1B --> D1 C1C --> D1 C1D --> D1 C2A --> D1 C2B --> D1 C3A --> D1 C3B --> D1 C4A --> D1 C4B --> D1 ``` ### 4.2 Schematic of the Two-Film Theory (Foundation of Classification) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Bulk Gas Phase
y_A1 high] --> F1[Gas Film
Thickness delta y] F1 --> IF[Interface
y_Ai equals x_Ai times m] IF --> F2[Liquid Film
Thickness delta x] F2 --> P2[Bulk Liquid Phase
x_A2 high] P1 -.Flux N_A.-> F1 F2 -.Flux N_A.-> P2 IF -.Equilibrium.-> IF2[Interface obeys y equals m x] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation for KTU students:** Every classified mass transfer operation (absorption, stripping, distillation, extraction) ultimately reduces to mass transfer through these two idealized films. The **overall resistance** is the sum of individual film resistances, which justifies the equation $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$. ### 4.3 Process Flow: Classification-to-Equipment Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Identify Operation Type] --> S1{Phase Pair?} S1 -->|Gas-Liquid| S2[Absorber or Stripper] S1 -->|Liquid-Vapor| S3[Distillation Column] S1 -->|Solid-Liquid| S4[Leach Tank or Extractor] S1 -->|Fluid-Solid| S5[Adsorber Bed] S2 --> S2a[Choose Packed or Tray] S3 --> S3a[Choose Sieve Valve Bubble Cap] S4 --> S4a[Choose Batch or Continuous] S5 --> S5a[Choose Fixed or Fluidized] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **Q1.** *Classify mass transfer operations based on the phases involved. Give two industrial examples for each class.* **Model Answer:** Mass transfer operations are classified into four major phase-based categories: 1. **Gas → Liquid:** Absorption (e.g., scrubbing of $\text{SO}_2$ from flue gas using water), Stripping (e.g., removal of $\text{NH}_3$ from wastewater). 2. **Liquid → Vapor:** Distillation (e.g., separation of ethanol–water mixture), Evaporation. 3. **Solid → Fluid:** Leaching (e.g., extraction of sucrose from sugar beet), Drying (e.g., milk powder production). 4. **Fluid → Fluid:** Liquid–liquid extraction (e.g., aromatics recovery using sulfolane), Membrane separation (e.g., desalination via reverse osmosis). --- > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Q2.** *Distinguish between stage-wise contact and differential contact equipment. Name one example of each.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Stage-wise Contact | Differential Contact | |---|---|---| | **Equilibrium** | Approached in discrete stages | Never fully attained | | **Flow** | Discrete, step-wise | Continuous | | **Equipment Example** | Sieve tray distillation column | Packed absorption column | | **Design Basis** | Number of stages ($N$) | Height of packing (HETP / HTU) | | **Application** | Distillation, multistage extraction | Absorption, humidification | --- ### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** *Explain in detail the classification of mass transfer operations based on phases involved, direction of transfer, system state, and contact mode. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** Mass transfer operations are classified as follows: **(i) Based on Phases Involved:** - Gas–Liquid: Absorption, Stripping, Humidification - Liquid–Vapor: Distillation, Evaporation - Solid–Liquid: Leaching, Crystallization - Solid–Gas/Vapor: Drying, Sublimation - Fluid–Fluid via Membrane: Reverse osmosis, Ultrafiltration **(ii) Based on Direction of Transfer:** - **Unidirectional:** Component moves in one direction only (e.g., absorption of pure A into B). - **Counter-directional / Bidirectional:** Both components exchange (e.g., distillation). **(iii) Based on System State:** - **Steady-state:** $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = 0$ (e.g., continuous absorption column). - **Unsteady-state:** Concentration profiles vary with time (e.g., batch drying). **(iv) Based on Contact Mode:** - **Stage-wise (Equilibrium) Contact:** Discrete stages like trays; uses Murphree efficiency. - **Differential (Continuous) Contact:** Uses HTU-NTU concept in packed columns. --- **(b)** *Derive the steady-state molar flux equation for component A diffusing through a stagnant gas film of thickness $z$. State clearly all assumptions. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state condition $\left(\dfrac{\partial N_A}{\partial t} = 0\right)$. 2. Temperature and total pressure are constant. 3. Ideal gas behavior holds in the gas phase. 4. Component B is stagnant, i.e., $N_B = 0$. 5. Unidirectional diffusion along the $z$-axis. **Starting Equation (Fick's First Law + bulk flow):** $$ N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\,N_A $$ **Rearrangement:** $$ N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} $$ **Substitute $C = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ and $x_A = y_A$:** $$ N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT(1 - y_A)}\,\dfrac{dy_A}{dz} $$ **Separation of variables and integration from $z = 0$ to $z = z$, $y_A = y_{A1}$ to $y_A = y_{A2}$:** $$ \int_{0}^{z} N_A\,dz = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}}\dfrac{dy_A}{1 - y_A} $$ **Evaluation of the integral using $\int \dfrac{dy}{1 - y} = -\ln(1 - y)$:** $$ N_A \cdot z = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right) $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\,N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)\,} $$ For dilute systems ($y_{A1}, y_{A2} \ll 1$): $$ N_A \approx \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z}(C_{A1} - C_{A2}) $$ This is the **working equation** for absorption and humidification design. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** *With a neat schematic, explain the two-film theory of mass transfer. Discuss its significance in classifying mass transfer equipment. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** The **two-film theory** proposed by **Whitman (1923)** assumes that: - Two thin stagnant films exist at the gas–liquid interface — one on the gas side, one on the liquid side. - All resistance to mass transfer is concentrated in these films. - The interface itself offers **zero resistance**, and at the interface, equilibrium exists: $y_{A,i} = m \cdot x_{A,i}$. **Schematic:** ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Bulk Gas y1] -->|k_y| F1[Gas Film] F1 -->|Interface| IF[Equilibrium y equals m x] IF -->|k_x| F2[Liquid Film] F2 -->|k_x| P2[Bulk Liquid x2] ``` **Derivation of Overall Coefficient:** Flux in gas film: $$ N_A = k_y (y_1 - y_i) $$ Flux in liquid film: $$ N_A = k_x (x_i - x_2) $$ Using $y_i = m x_i$ and eliminating interface compositions: $$ N_A = K_y (y_1 - m x_2) = K_x (y_1/m - x_2) $$ This yields: $$ \boxed{\,\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}\,} $$ **Significance in Classification:** - Absorption is **gas-film controlled** when $k_y \ll m k_x$. - Stripping is **liquid-film controlled** when $k_x \ll k_y/m$. - This determines whether to use tray columns or packed towers. --- **(b)** *A mixture of $\text{NH}_3$ and air is brought into contact with water at $293\,\text{K}$ and $1\,\text{atm}$. At the gas–liquid interface, the partial pressure of $\text{NH}_3$ is $6.66\,\text{kPa}$. At a distance of $2\,\text{mm}$ from the interface, the partial pressure is $20\,\text{kPa}$. The diffusivity of $\text{NH}_3$ in air is $1.76 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the steady-state flux of $\text{NH}_3$. *(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $P = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$, $T = 293\,\text{K}$, $D_{AB} = 1.76 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ - $z = 2\,\text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$ - $p_{A1} = 20\,\text{kPa}$ (bulk gas), $p_{A2} = 6.66\,\text{kPa}$ (interface) **Mole fractions:** $$ y_{A1} = \dfrac{20}{101.325} = 0.1974 $$ $$ y_{A2} = \dfrac{6.66}{101.325} = 0.0657 $$ **Apply stagnant-B flux equation:** $$ N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right) $$ **Numerator:** $D_{AB}\,P = (1.76 \times 10^{-5})(101325) = 1.7833$ **Denominator:** $R\,T\,z = (8.314)(293)(2 \times 10^{-3}) = 4.872$ $$ N_A = \dfrac{1.7833}{4.872} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - 0.0657}{1 - 0.1974}\right) $$ $$ N_A = 0.3660 \cdot \ln(1.1645) = 0.3660 \cdot 0.1522 $$ $$ \boxed{\,N_A \approx 5.57 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{kmol}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})\,} $$ **Unit check:** $\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{Pa}}{\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}\cdot\text{m}} = \dfrac{\text{mol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}$ ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Missing units deduction (–1 mark):** Always write the final answer with proper units ($\text{kmol}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$). Bare numbers lose credit. > 2. **Confusing stagnant-B with EMD (–2 marks):** If the problem states *one component is stagnant*, **never** use the simple linear EMD form $N_A = D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})/z$. > 3. **Pressure unit mix-up (–1 mark):** KTU typically uses $\text{kPa}$ for partial pressures but $\text{Pa}$ in $R \cdot T$. Convert carefully: $1\,\text{kPa} = 10^3\,\text{Pa}$. > 4. **Forgetting $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$ (–1 mark):** Using $0.0821\,\text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$ with wrong pressure unit causes a dimensional catastrophe. > 5. **Skipping assumption statements (–1 mark):** In Part B derivations, list all assumptions before starting the integration step. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Classification is the conceptual foundation** of every mass transfer operation — memorize the phase-based table. - [ ] **Four primary phase categories:** Gas–Liquid, Liquid–Vapor, Solid–Fluid, Fluid–Fluid (membrane). - [ ] **Distillation is bidirectional**; absorption is **unidirectional**. - [ ] **Steady-state** systems satisfy $\dfrac{\partial N_A}{\partial t} = 0$. - [ ] **Two-film theory** justifies $K_y = \dfrac{1}{(1/k_y) + (m/k_x)}$. - [ ] **Fick's First Law** with stagnant B: $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RTz}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)$. - [ ] **Equimolar counter-diffusion** gives $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$. - [ ] **Henry's law form:** $y^* = m \cdot x$ — used in absorption equilibrium. - [ ] **Dilute approximation:** When $y_A \ll 1$, $\ln(1 - y) \approx -y$, simplifying the flux equation. - [ ] **Unit vigilance:** Always convert $\text{kPa} \to \text{Pa}$ before using $R = 8.314$. - [ ] **Murphree efficiency** quantifies real-stage performance vs. ideal equilibrium stages. - [ ] **HTU-NTU** model applies to differential (packed) contact equipment. - [ ] **Equipment mapping:** Absorption → Packed/Tray column; Drying → Rotary/Flash dryer; Leaching → Mixer-Settler. - [ ] **For KTU 14-mark questions,** always show: Assumptions → Governing Equation → Integration → Final Boxed Result → Unit Verification. ---

topics.title: Fick's Law of Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fick's Law of Diffusion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Fick's Law of Diffusion** is the foundational constitutive equation of mass transfer that quantifies the rate at which a chemical species migrates through a medium (gas, liquid, or solid) under the influence of a **concentration gradient**. It is the mass-transfer analogue of Fourier's Law (heat conduction) and Newton's Law (momentum transfer), and is mathematically expressed through two related statements: - **Fick's First Law** (Steady-state diffusion): relates the molar flux of a component to its concentration gradient. - **Fick's Second Law** (Unsteady-state diffusion): relates the time-rate of change of concentration to the spatial curvature of the concentration profile. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must master both laws in vector/tensor form and scalar (one-dimensional) form, and be able to derive the second law from the first using a differential mass balance. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you open a bottle of perfume in one corner of a still room. Initially, the concentration of perfume molecules is **extremely high** near the bottle and **zero** at the opposite corner. You can *smell* the fragrance first near the bottle, and after some time, even at the far end. What happened? Random molecular motion (Brownian motion) caused the perfume molecules to **spread out** from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration until the room smelled uniformly. This spontaneous "spreading" driven by a concentration difference is **diffusion**. **Fick's First Law** simply puts a number on this intuition: the **flux** (how many moles pass through an imaginary plane per second per unit area) is **directly proportional** to the **concentration gradient** (how steeply concentration changes with position). The proportionality constant is the **diffusivity** $D_{AB}$, which depends on the molecule, the medium, temperature, and pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constant Spotlight:** The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ has units of **m²/s** (or cm²/s in CGS). For gases, $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s; for liquids, $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s; for solids, $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-13}$ to $10^{-14}$ m²/s. This **9 orders of magnitude** difference explains why solid-state diffusion is the rate-limiting step in metallurgy and semiconductor doping. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and the "How" ### 2.1.1 Fick's First Law — Why It Works Fick (1855) **postulated** that diffusion is mathematically analogous to Fourier's heat conduction. The hypothesis has three underlying physical premises: 1. **Linearity:** Flux is directly proportional to the driving force (concentration gradient). 2. **Isotropy:** The medium's properties are the same in all directions (or are characterized by a tensor). 3. **Local equilibrium:** The system is only infinitesimally displaced from thermodynamic equilibrium at every point, so a macroscopic driving force is meaningful. ### 2.1.2 Sign Convention and Direction of Flux The negative sign in Fick's First Law enforces a fundamental physical principle: **species naturally flow down the gradient**, from high concentration to low concentration. If $\frac{dC_A}{dz} > 0$ (concentration rising with $z$), the flux $N_A$ is in the **negative $z$ direction**, hence the minus sign produces a negative value. ### 2.1.3 Vector Form and Anisotropic Media In three dimensions, Fick's First Law for an anisotropic medium becomes a tensor relationship: $$\vec{J}_A = -[D_{AB}] \cdot \nabla C_A$$ For isotropic media, $[D_{AB}]$ reduces to a scalar $D_{AB}$ times the identity matrix. ### 2.1.4 Fick's Second Law — How It Evolves Combining Fick's First Law with a **differential mass balance** over an elementary control volume yields the diffusion equation (also called the heat equation analogue): $$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \nabla^{2} C_A$$ This is a **parabolic partial differential equation** whose solution, subject to appropriate initial and boundary conditions, gives $C_A(x,y,z,t)$ — the concentration distribution as a function of space and time. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table verbatim — these equations form the backbone of 70% of Module 1 numerical questions.** | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Driving Force / Coordinate | Units of Each Term | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fick's First Law (1-D, molar flux) | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Concentration gradient in $z$ | $N_A$: kmol/(m²·s); $D_{AB}$: m²/s; $C_A$: kmol/m³; $z$: m | | 2 | Fick's First Law (mass flux) | $j_A = -\rho D_{AB} \dfrac{d\omega_A}{dz}$ | Mass fraction gradient | $j_A$: kg/(m²·s); $\rho$: kg/m³; $\omega_A$: kg/kg | | 3 | Fick's Second Law (1-D) | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^{2} C_A}{\partial z^{2}}$ | Curvature of $C_A(z,t)$ | $t$: s | | 4 | Fick's Second Law (3-D) | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \nabla^{2} C_A$ | Laplacian of concentration field | — | | 5 | Molar average velocity definition | $\vec{v} = \dfrac{\sum_i C_i \vec{v_i}}{\sum_i C_i}$ | Ensemble definition | m/s | | 6 | Diffusion flux relative to $v$ | $\vec{J}_A = C_A(\vec{v}_A - \vec{v})$ | Definition | kmol/(m²·s) | | 7 | Total molar flux identity | $\vec{N}_A = \vec{J}_A + C_A \vec{v}$ | Absolute vs. diffusive | kmol/(m²·s) | | 8 | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) | $N_A = -N_B = \dfrac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}$ | Linear $C_A$ profile | kmol/(m²·s) | | 9 | Stagnant Film (B component non-diffusing) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T z} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)$ | Partial pressure gradient | kmol/(m²·s) | | 10 | Stefan Diffusion (large concentration) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T z} \cdot \dfrac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$ | Log-mean of inert partial pressure | kmol/(m²·s) | | 11 | Binary gas diffusivity — Chapman–Enskog | $D_{AB} = 0.001858 \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{P (\sigma_{AB})^{2} \Omega_D} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B}}$ | Fuller correlation variant | cm²/s (when $T$ in K, $P$ in atm) | | 12 | Liquid diffusivity — Wilke–Chang | $D_{AB}^{L} = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \dfrac{(\phi_B M_B)^{1/2} T}{\mu_B V_{bA}^{0.6}}$ | Stokes–Einstein variant | cm²/s | > [!NOTE] > **Where these are used in industry:** > - **EMCD (Eq. 8):** Distillation of close-boiling binaries (e.g., benzene–toluene); membrane permeation when permeate carries away equal moles. > - **Stagnant Film (Eq. 9/10):** Absorption of a soluble gas into a liquid (A diffuses through stagnant B); humidification; evaporation into still air. > - **Chapman–Enskog (Eq. 11):** Design of gas absorption columns, catalytic reactors, and atmospheric dispersion modelling. > - **Wilke–Chang (Eq. 12):** Liquid–liquid extraction columns, pharmaceutical crystallization, fermentation broth aeration. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law We perform a **differential mass balance** on species $A$ over a stationary, differential control volume of dimensions $dz$, $dy$, $dx$ in a Cartesian frame. ### Step 1: Define the molar flux into and out of the control volume Let $N_{A,z}\big\vert_z$ denote the molar flux of $A$ entering the face at position $z$, and $N_{A,z}\big\vert_{z+dz}$ denote the flux leaving at $z + dz$. Similar definitions hold for the $y$ and $x$ directions. ### Step 2: Apply the conservation law Rate of accumulation of $A$ in CV = Rate of $A$ in − Rate of $A$ out + Rate of generation Assuming **no chemical reaction**, generation = 0. ### Step 3: Write the accumulation term The total moles of $A$ in the CV = $C_A \cdot dx\,dy\,dz$. Hence, accumulation rate = $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \, dx\,dy\,dz$. ### Step 4: Write the net in–out term for the $z$-direction Rate in at $z$ = $N_{A,z}\big\vert_z \, dx\,dy$. Rate out at $z+dz$ = $N_{A,z}\big\vert_{z+dz} \, dx\,dy$. Net contribution in $z$ = $\left(N_{A,z}\big\vert_z - N_{A,z}\big\vert_{z+dz}\right) dx\,dy$. ### Step 5: Taylor-expand the outgoing flux By a first-order Taylor expansion: $$N_{A,z}\big\vert_{z+dz} = N_{A,z}\big\vert_z + \dfrac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} dz$$ Subtracting gives the net inflow in the $z$-direction: $$\left(N_{A,z}\big\vert_z - N_{A,z}\big\vert_z - \dfrac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} dz\right) dx\,dy = -\dfrac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} dx\,dy\,dz$$ ### Step 6: Sum contributions over all three coordinate directions Total net inflow = $-\left(\dfrac{\partial N_{A,x}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial N_{A,y}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z}\right) dx\,dy\,dz$ Equivalently, this is $-\nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A \cdot dV$. ### Step 7: Equate accumulation and net inflow $$\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx\,dy\,dz = -\nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A \, dx\,dy\,dz$$ Dividing through by $dx\,dy\,dz$: $$\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A = 0$$ This is the **general continuity equation for species $A$** (with no reaction). ### Step 8: Substitute Fick's First Law For one-dimensional, constant-diffusivity, equimolar counter-diffusion, $\vec{N}_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}\hat{z}$. Therefore: $$\nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right)$$ ### Step 9: Assume $D_{AB}$ is constant (most KTU exam assumption) $$\nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^{2} C_A}{\partial z^{2}}$$ ### Step 10: Final assembly $$\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \left(-D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^{2} C_A}{\partial z^{2}}\right) = 0$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^{2} C_A}{\partial z^{2}}}$$ This is **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**. ## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Flux Through a Stagnant Film (Stefan Diffusion) Consider species $A$ diffusing through a stagnant layer of $B$ of thickness $z_2 - z_1$ at total pressure $P$ and temperature $T$. At steady state, $N_B = 0$. ### Step 1: Start from the absolute molar flux identity $$N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$ Since $N_B = 0$ (stagnant film assumption): $$N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A N_A$$ ### Step 2: Convert concentration to partial pressure using ideal gas law $$C_A = \dfrac{p_A}{RT}, \quad x_A = \dfrac{p_A}{P}$$ Substituting: $$N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{1}{RT}\dfrac{dp_A}{dz} + \dfrac{p_A}{P} N_A$$ ### Step 3: Isolate the terms involving $N_A$ $$N_A\!\left(1 - \dfrac{p_A}{P}\right) = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT}\dfrac{dp_A}{dz}$$ $$N_A \cdot \dfrac{P - p_A}{P} = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT}\dfrac{dp_A}{dz}$$ ### Step 4: Separate variables and integrate $$N_A \, dz = -\dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \cdot \dfrac{dp_A}{P - p_A}$$ Integrating from $z_1$ to $z_2$ (with $p_{A1}$ at $z_1$ and $p_{A2}$ at $z_2$): $$N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -\dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \dfrac{dp_A}{P - p_A}$$ ### Step 5: Evaluate the integrals The right-hand side integral gives $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)$: $$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)$$ ### Step 6: Final expression for Stefan diffusion $$\boxed{N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** When $p_A \ll P$ (highly dilute $A$), the log term can be linearized via $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$, recovering the simple linear-gradient form. This simplification yields the **linear driving force (LDF)** approximation used extensively in absorption/stripping column design. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases & Limits | Physical Scenario | Mathematical Limit | Resulting Form | Common Mistake to Avoid | |---|---|---|---| | EMCD, dilute, constant $D_{AB}$ | $N_B = -N_A$, $C_T$ constant | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}$ | Students often use partial pressures; concentrations are correct here. | | Stagnant $B$, ideal gas | $N_B = 0$ | Stefan equation (Section 3.2) | Forgetting the $\ln$ and using arithmetic mean gives wrong answers. | | Dilute $A$ in stagnant $B$ | $p_A \ll P$ | $N_A \approx \dfrac{D_{AB} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}{R T (z_2 - z_1)}$ | This is **not** the same as EMCD — even when dilute, the stagnant film introduces the $\ln$ factor unless fully linearized. | | Knudsen diffusion (porous solid, small pores) | $\lambda \gg d_{pore}$ | $N_A = -\dfrac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M_A}} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Often confused with molecular diffusion; Knudsen dominates in nanopores. | | Binary liquid diffusion | $D_{AB}^{L} \ll D_{AB}^{G}$ | Use Wilke–Chang or Stokes–Einstein | Don't blindly apply gas-diffusion correlations to liquids. | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Microscopic Randomness to Macroscopic Flux The following Mermaid diagram captures the **logical chain** that links molecular-scale Brownian motion to the macroscopic form of Fick's Law used by engineers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Random Molecular Motion
Brownian Kinetics] --> A2[Net Statistical Drift
from High C to Low C] A2 --> A3[Define Molar Flux NA
mol per m2 per s] A3 --> A4[Ficks First Law Postulate
NA proportional to gradient] A4 --> A5[Introduce Diffusivity DAB
as proportionality constant] A5 --> A6[Differential Mass Balance
on Elementary Control Volume] A6 --> A7[Taylor Expand Flux
at CV Faces] A7 --> A8[Apply Continuity Equation
Accumulation equals Net Inflow] A8 --> A9[Ficks Second Law
Unsteady State PDE] A9 --> A10{System Type?} A10 -->|Steady State| A11[First Law Sufficient
Constant dCA per dz] A10 -->|Unsteady State| A12[Solve Second Law PDE
with Initial and Boundary Conditions] A10 -->|Stagnant Film| A13[Stefan Diffusion
Logarithmic Driving Force] A12 --> A14[Concentration Profile CA z t] A11 --> A15[Linear CA Profile
Analytical Flux] A13 --> A16[Nonlinear CA Profile
Logarithmic Solution] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Engineering Application Mapping The diagram below maps how Fick's Law feeds into real mass-transfer equipment designs encountered in KTU viva and industrial practice. ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[Input: Concentration
Boundary Conditions] --> B2{Diffusion Regime?} B2 -->|Gaseous| B3[Chapman Enskog or Fuller
Equation for DAB] B2 -->|Liquid| B4[Wilke Chang or Stokes Einstein
Equation for DAB Liquid] B2 -->|Porous Solid| B5[Knudsen plus Molecular
Combined Diffusivity] B3 --> B6[Ficks Law Solver
1D Steady State] B4 --> B6 B5 --> B6 B6 --> B7{Equipment Type?} B7 -->|Absorption Column| B8[Steady State EMCD
or Stagnant Film Model] B7 -->|Membrane Separator| B9[Solution Diffusion Model
using Ficks First Law] B7 -->|Catalytic Pellet| B10[Effectiveness Factor
Thiele Modulus Analysis] B7 -->|Drying Solid| B11[Unsteady State
Ficks Second Law] B8 --> B12[Output: NA Flux
kmol per m2 per s] B9 --> B12 B10 --> B12 B11 --> B12 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — 1-D Stagnant Film Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR C1[Gas Phase Boundary z1
Partial Pressure pA1 High
Bulk Concentration CA1] -->|Diffusion Across
Stagnant Film of B| C2[Liquid Interface z2
Partial Pressure pA2 Low
Equilibrium Concentration CA2] C1 -.- C3[Species A Moves Down
Its Own Gradient] C1 -.- C4[Species B Stationary
NB equals zero] C2 -.- C5[Absorption into Liquid
or Reaction at Interface] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Define each term with units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a species $A$ by molecular diffusion is proportional to the concentration gradient of $A$ and occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration. > > $$N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$$ > > Where: > - $N_A$ = molar flux of $A$ in the $z$-direction, **kmol/(m²·s)**. > - $D_{AB}$ = diffusivity of $A$ through $B$, **m²/s**. > - $\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ = concentration gradient of $A$ in the $z$-direction, **kmol/m⁴**. > > The negative sign indicates flux occurs from high to low concentration. (3 marks) ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between molar flux $N_A$ and diffusion flux $\vec{J}_A$. Why is the distinction important in non-equimolar diffusion?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **diffusion flux $\vec{J}_A$** is the flux of $A$ relative to the **molar-average velocity** $\vec{v}$ of the mixture. It arises purely from random molecular motion. > > $$\vec{J}_A = C_A(\vec{v}_A - \vec{v})$$ > > The **absolute (or total) molar flux $N_A$** is the flux of $A$ relative to a **stationary coordinate frame**. > > $$\vec{N}_A = \vec{J}_A + C_A \vec{v}$$ > > The distinction is critical when molar flows are not equal and opposite (e.g., $A$ is absorbed, $B$ is non-diffusing). In such cases $\vec{v} \neq 0$ and the bulk flow contribution $C_A \vec{v}$ adds a convective term to $\vec{J}_A$, leading to the **Stefan diffusion** form. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of Diffusion starting from a differential mass balance. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Ammonia ($A$) is diffusing through a stagnant film of nitrogen ($B$) of thickness 2 mm at 1 atm total pressure and 298 K. The partial pressure of ammonia at one side of the film is 0.2 atm and at the other side is 0.05 atm. Given $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, calculate the steady-state molar flux of ammonia. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Derivation of Fick's Second Law** (7 marks) **Assumptions:** 1. Binary system $A$–$B$ only. 2. No chemical reaction. 3. Constant temperature and pressure. 4. Constant diffusivity $D_{AB}$. 5. One-dimensional diffusion along $z$. **Steps (1 mark each for key steps, 2 marks for final equation):** Perform a differential mass balance on species $A$ over an elementary control volume $dx\,dy\,dz$: 1. **Accumulation term:** $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx\,dy\,dz$ 2. **Net inflow (z-direction):** $-\dfrac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} dx\,dy\,dz$ 3. **Total net inflow (3-D):** $-\left(\dfrac{\partial N_{A,x}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial N_{A,y}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z}\right) dx\,dy\,dz$ 4. **Equating accumulation to net inflow:** $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A$ 5. **Substituting Fick's First Law:** $\vec{N}_A = -D_{AB} \nabla C_A$ 6. **Combining with constant $D_{AB}$:** $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \nabla^{2} C_A$ 7. **For 1-D:** $\boxed{\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^{2} C_A}{\partial z^{2}}}$ **(b) Steady-state flux calculation** (7 marks) Given: - $z_2 - z_1 = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $P = 1 \text{ atm} = 101325$ Pa - $T = 298$ K - $p_{A1} = 0.2$ atm, $p_{A2} = 0.05$ atm - $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) Using the **Stefan diffusion equation** (since $B$ is stagnant): $$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)$$ **Step 1 — Compute the logarithmic term:** $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - 0.05}{1 - 0.2}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.80}\right) = \ln(1.1875) = 0.1719$$ **Step 2 — Compute the prefactor:** $$\dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} = \dfrac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8.314)(298)(2 \times 10^{-3})}$$ Numerator: $2.30 \times 10^{-5} \times 101325 = 2.3305$ (units: m²·Pa/s) Denominator: $8.314 \times 298 \times 0.002 = 4.955$ (units: J/mol = Pa·m³/mol → check) Let us redo in consistent units using $P$ in atm and $R = 0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K), then convert. $$N_A = \dfrac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1)}{(0.08206)(298)(2 \times 10^{-3})} \times \ln(1.1875)$$ Prefactor = $\dfrac{2.30 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0489} = 4.704 \times 10^{-4}$ kmol/(m²·s) Wait, units of $R$ in SI must be used. Using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $P$ in Pa: $$\dfrac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8.314)(298)(2 \times 10^{-3})} = \dfrac{2.3305}{4.9552} = 0.4703 \text{ mol/(m²·s)}$$ **Step 3 — Final calculation:** $$N_A = 0.4703 \times 0.1719 = 0.0808 \text{ mol/(m²·s)}$$ Converting to kmol/(m²·s): $$\boxed{N_A = 8.08 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/(m²·s)}}$$ (7 marks — 2 marks for selecting Stefan equation, 2 marks for correct log calculation, 2 marks for correct prefactor, 1 mark for final numerical answer with units) --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the physical significance of the diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$. Discuss how it varies with temperature and pressure for gases and liquids, citing relevant correlations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A binary gas mixture of oxygen ($A$) and nitrogen ($B$) undergoes equimolar counter-diffusion at 1.5 atm total pressure and 310 K through a tube of length 0.5 m. The mole fraction of oxygen at one end is 0.35 and at the other is 0.08. Using the Chapman–Enskog equation parameters $\sigma_{AB} = 3.48$ Å, $\Omega_D = 1.0$, $M_A = 32$, $M_B = 28$, calculate the molar flux of oxygen. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Physical significance and dependence of $D_{AB}$** (7 marks) **Significance (3 marks):** - $D_{AB}$ quantifies how *fast* species $A$ spreads through medium $B$. - It is a **transport property**, analogous to thermal conductivity $k$ and momentum diffusivity $\nu$. - Units: **m²/s**; represents the area covered by a diffusing molecule per unit time (mean-square displacement grows linearly with time, $\langle r^{2} \rangle \sim D_{AB} t$). - Larger $D_{AB}$ → faster mixing, thinner boundary layers, higher mass-transfer coefficients. **Gases (2 marks):** - $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}$ (kinetic-theory prediction: faster molecular motion at higher $T$). - $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$ (higher pressure → more collisions → slower diffusion). - Predicted by **Chapman–Enskog** or simpler **Fuller–Schettler–Giddings** correlation. **Liquids (2 marks):** - $D_{AB}^{L}$ is **much smaller** (~$10^{-9}$ m²/s vs. $10^{-5}$ m²/s for gases). - $D_{AB}^{L} \propto T / \mu_B$ (Stokes–Einstein relation; viscosity $\mu_B$ strongly decreases with $T$, giving exponential-like rise with $T$). - Pressure has **weak** effect for liquids. - Common correlation: **Wilke–Chang equation**. **(b) EMCD flux calculation** (7 marks) Given: - $P = 1.5$ atm, $T = 310$ K, $z_2 - z_1 = 0.5$ m - $x_{A1} = 0.35$, $x_{A2} = 0.08$ - $\sigma_{AB} = 3.48$ Å $= 3.48 \times 10^{-8}$ cm - $\Omega_D = 1.0$, $M_A = 32$, $M_B = 28$ **Step 1 — Calculate $D_{AB}$ using Chapman–Enskog:** $$D_{AB} = 0.001858 \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{P (\sigma_{AB})^{2} \Omega_D} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B}}$$ (Units: cm²/s when $T$ in K, $P$ in atm, $\sigma$ in Å) Compute each piece: - $T^{3/2} = (310)^{1.5} = 310 \times \sqrt{310} = 310 \times 17.606 = 5457.9$ - $P (\sigma_{AB})^{2} \Omega_D = 1.5 \times (3.48)^{2} \times 1.0 = 1.5 \times 12.1104 = 18.166$ - $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{32} + \dfrac{1}{28}} = \sqrt{0.03125 + 0.03571} = \sqrt{0.06696} = 0.2588$ $$D_{AB} = 0.001858 \times \dfrac{5457.9}{18.166} \times 0.2588$$ $$D_{AB} = 0.001858 \times 300.45 \times 0.2588 = 0.1444 \text{ cm²/s}$$ $$D_{AB} = 1.444 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m²/s}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate EMCD molar flux:** $$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} C_T (x_{A1} - x_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}$$ Where total concentration $C_T = \dfrac{P}{RT} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 101325}{8.314 \times 310} = \dfrac{151987.5}{2577.34} = 58.97$ mol/m³. $$N_A = \dfrac{(1.444 \times 10^{-5})(58.97)(0.35 - 0.08)}{0.5}$$ $$N_A = \dfrac{(1.444 \times 10^{-5})(58.97)(0.27)}{0.5}$$ $$N_A = \dfrac{2.301 \times 10^{-4}}{0.5}$$ $$\boxed{N_A = 4.602 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/(m²·s)}}$$ (7 marks — 2 marks for Chapman–Enskog substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for $C_T$ calculation, 2 marks for EMCD equation and final flux) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing EMCD and stagnant-film equations:** Many students apply the linear flux form $\frac{D_{AB}\Delta C}{z}$ for stagnant-film problems. If $B$ is stagnant, **you must use the logarithmic Stefan equation** — the linear form is only valid for EMCD or highly dilute systems. > 2. **Unit mismatch in Chapman–Enskog:** The constant 0.001858 requires $T$ in K, $P$ in atm, $\sigma$ in Å, and gives $D_{AB}$ in cm²/s. Mixing SI and CGS units is the #1 cause of incorrect $D_{AB}$. > 3. **Sign errors:** In $N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$, ensure you use $(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ with $z_2 - z_1$ (both positive) for a positive flux magnitude. > 4. **Forgetting $C_T$ in EMCD:** Students often write $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\Delta x_A}{z}$, missing the total concentration factor $C_T = P/RT$. > 5. **Misreading the problem type:** KTU questions explicitly state "equimolar counter-diffusion" or "through stagnant film." Read carefully — the equation selection is half the marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Fick's First Law** is a **constitutive postulate**, not derived from first principles — it is the mass-transfer equivalent of Fourier's Law. - ✅ **Fick's Second Law** is a **consequence** of the First Law plus the continuity equation. - ✅ The **negative sign** in $N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$ encodes that species flow **down** the concentration gradient. - ✅ **EMCD vs Stagnant Film** is the most-tested distinction: EMCD → linear; stagnant → logarithmic (Stefan). - ✅ **Units:** $D_{AB}$ in **m²/s** (SI) or **cm²/s** (CGS); Chapman–Enskog constant 0.001858 is calibrated for **cm²/s**. - ✅ **Order of magnitude:** Gas $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s; Liquid $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s; Solid $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-13}$ m²/s. - ✅ **Temperature dependence:** Gas $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}/P$; Liquid $D_{AB} \propto T/\mu_B$. - ✅ **3-D form** of Second Law: $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\nabla^{2}C_A$ — this is a parabolic PDE solved with IC + BCs. - ✅ **Knudsen diffusion** dominates when pore size $d_p < $ mean free path $\lambda$ (nanoporous catalysts, membrane separators). - ✅ Always check: **EMCD** uses $N_A = -N_B$; **stagnant film** uses $N_B = 0$; **dilute approximation** linearizes the $\ln$ term.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diffusivity Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Diffusivity Measurement — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Mass Transfer Operations, **diffusivity** (also called the **mass diffusivity** or **diffusion coefficient**, denoted $D_{AB}$) is a fundamental transport property that quantifies the rate at which a species $A$ moves through a medium of species $B$ under the influence of a concentration gradient. Mathematically, it is the proportionality constant in **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**:

$$J_{A}^{*} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_{A}}{dz}$$

where $J_{A}^{*}$ is the molar flux of species $A$ relative to the molar average velocity, $\frac{dC_{A}}{dz}$ is the concentration gradient in the $z$-direction, and $D_{AB}$ has units of **m²/s (SI)** or **cm²/s (CGS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Diffusivity is classified into:
> - **Gas-phase diffusivity** ($D_{AB}$): typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$ cm²/s
> - **Liquid-phase diffusivity** ($D_{AB}$): typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ cm²/s
> - **Solid-phase diffusivity**: typically $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-15}$ cm²/s

**Diffusivity measurement** refers to the experimental and analytical techniques employed to determine the numerical value of $D_{AB}$ for a specific species pair under prescribed conditions of temperature, pressure, and composition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since direct theoretical prediction of $D_{AB}$ from first principles is often unreliable, especially in liquids and concentrated solutions, **experimental measurement** is the most trusted method for generating engineering design data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ink Drop in a Still Pond"

Imagine dropping a single drop of **red ink** into a perfectly still glass of clear water. The molecules of ink begin to slowly spread out in all directions, driven purely by their **random thermal motion** (Brownian motion), not by any stirring or external force.

- The **speed** at which the red color spreads throughout the water is a direct visual analog of the **diffusivity**. A thicker, more viscous fluid would slow down the spread (small $D_{AB}$), while warm water would speed it up (larger $D_{AB}$).
- The **concentration gradient** is the visible "color front" — steeper at the drop's center, gentler at the edges.
- Diffusivity measurement is essentially **"timing how fast the ink spreads"** in a controlled geometry, then back-calculating $D_{AB}$ using the physics of Fick's laws.

## 1.3 Classification of Measurement Techniques

The major experimental techniques for measuring diffusivity are summarized below:

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Classification**
>
> | Category | Key Methods | Typical Use |
> |----------|-------------|-------------|
> | **Steady-State Methods** | Stefan Tube (Wickel Method), Arnold Diffusion Cell | Gas-Phase $D_{AB}$ |
> | **Unsteady-State Methods** | Diaphragm Cell, Two-Bulb Method, NMR, Radioactive Tracer | Liquid-Phase $D_{AB}$ |
> | **Indirect / Correlation Methods** | Wilke-Chang, Fuller-Schettler-Giddings, Chapman-Enskog | Quick estimation when experimental data is unavailable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two fundamentally different approaches (steady vs. unsteady)?**
> - **Steady-state** experiments are mathematically simpler (no time dependence), easier to interpret, and ideal for **gases** where diffusion is rapid and equilibrium is reached quickly.
> - **Unsteady-state** experiments are more suitable for **liquids** where diffusion is slow and steady-state would take impractically long to establish.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Diffusivity Measurement — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Two Governing Laws

Every diffusivity measurement technique is anchored in one of the two Fick's laws.

### Fick's First Law (Steady-State)

The molar flux under steady, one-dimensional diffusion is:

$$N_{A} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_{A}}{dz} + x_{A}(N_{A} + N_{B})$$

For **equimolar counter-diffusion** ($N_{A} = -N_{B}$), this simplifies to:

$$N_{A} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_{A}}{dz}$$

For **diffusion of A through stagnant B** ($N_{B} = 0$), we obtain:

$$N_{A} = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot p_{BM}} \cdot \frac{dp_{A}}{dz}$$

> **Why the $p_{BM}$ term appears:** Because the stagnant film assumption forces the bulk flow to drag A along, the effective flux is enhanced. The logarithmic-mean partial pressure of B, $p_{BM}$, accounts for this convective correction.

### Fick's Second Law (Unsteady-State)

$$\frac{\partial C_{A}}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^{2} C_{A}}{\partial z^{2}}$$

This is the **diffusion equation** whose analytical solution, subject to specific initial and boundary conditions, yields the working equations for the diaphragm cell and two-bulb methods.

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Logic of Each Method

### A. Stefan Tube (Wickel) Method — Steady-State Gas Diffusion

- **Geometry:** A narrow vertical glass tube of cross-section $A$ and length $L$ is partially filled with pure liquid A. The top is swept with a gas B that does not dissolve in A.
- **Process:** Liquid A evaporates and diffuses through the stagnant gas column B. As evaporation proceeds, the liquid level falls; the rate of fall is precisely timed.
- **Driving Force:** The partial pressure difference $(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ — saturated vapor pressure at the interface versus zero (or low) at the top.
- **Measured Quantity:** The drop in liquid level $\Delta z$ over time $\Delta t$.
- **Key Advantage:** Absolute measurement; no calibration needed.

### B. Diaphragm Cell Method — Unsteady-State Liquid Diffusion

- **Geometry:** Two well-stirred compartments of volumes $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$ separated by a porous glass or sintered-metal diaphragm of thickness $L$ and effective cross-section $A$.
- **Process:** Initially compartment 1 has concentration $C_{A1,0}$ and compartment 2 has $C_{A2,0}$. With time, A diffuses through the diaphragm until equilibrium.
- **Measured Quantity:** Concentration difference $(C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t))$ as a function of time, typically by sampling and analyzing each compartment.
- **Key Advantage:** Suited for slow liquid diffusion; very high precision when calibrated.

### C. Two-Bulb Method — Unsteady-State Gas/Liquid Diffusion

- **Geometry:** Two bulbs of volumes $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$ connected by a narrow tube of cross-section $A$ and length $L$.
- **Process:** Bulbs filled with different concentrations; allowed to mix by pure diffusion (no convection).
- **Measured Quantity:** Decay of concentration difference with time.
- **Key Advantage:** Simple geometry; well-defined initial conditions.

### D. Arnold Diffusion Cell — Steady-State Liquid-Liquid Diffusion

- **Geometry:** Two horizontal capillaries connected by a vertical diffusion tube.
- **Process:** Liquid A diffuses from one capillary into a flowing stream of solvent B, mimicking dilute conditions.
- **Key Advantage:** Good for **very low concentration** liquid diffusion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Equations for Diffusivity Measurement**

| Method | Working Equation | Variables | Typical Units |
|--------|------------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Stefan Tube** | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{N_{A} \cdot R \cdot T \cdot p_{BM} \cdot L}{P \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$ | $N_{A}$ = flux, $L$ = tube length | cm²/s |
| **Stefan Tube (falling level)** | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{\rho_{L} \cdot R \cdot T \cdot p_{BM} \cdot (\Delta z / \Delta t) \cdot L}{M_{A} \cdot P \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$ | $\rho_{L}$ = liquid density, $M_{A}$ = molar mass | cm²/s |
| **Log-mean $p_{B}$** | $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ | Partial pressures at boundaries | atm or Pa |
| **Diaphragm Cell** | $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)}{C_{A1,0} - C_{A2,0}}\right] = -\beta \, D_{AB} \, t$ | $\beta$ = cell constant (calibration) | dimensionless |
| **Cell constant** | $\beta = \dfrac{A}{L}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{V_{1}} + \dfrac{1}{V_{2}}\right)$ | $A$ = area, $L$ = diaphragm thickness | cm⁻² |
| **Two-Bulb (equal volumes)** | $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{\Delta C_{A}(t)}{\Delta C_{A,0}}\right] = -D_{AB}\left(\dfrac{2A}{L \cdot V}\right) t$ | $\Delta C_{A}$ = concentration difference | dimensionless |
| **Arnold Cell** | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot L}{A \cdot \ln(C_{A,in}/C_{A,out})}$ | $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow rate | cm²/s |
| **Chapman-Enskog (gas, predictive)** | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^{2} \, \Omega_{D}} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_{A}} + \dfrac{1}{M_{B}}}$ | $\sigma_{AB}$ = collision diameter, $\Omega_{D}$ = collision integral | cm²/s |
| **Wilke-Chang (liquid, predictive)** | $D_{AB}^{o} = \dfrac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, (\phi M_{B})^{0.5} \, T}{\mu_{B} \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$ | $\phi$ = association factor, $\mu_{B}$ in cP, $V_{A}$ in cm³/mol | cm²/s |
| **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings** | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{1.00 \times 10^{-3} \, T^{1.75}}{P \, [(\Sigma v_{A})^{1/3} + (\Sigma v_{B})^{1/3}]^{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_{A}} + \dfrac{1}{M_{B}}}$ | $\Sigma v$ = atomic diffusion volumes | cm²/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> - Designing **distillation columns** requires $D_{AB}$ for accurate stage calculations.
> - **Gas absorption** and **stripping** design depends on liquid-phase diffusivity in the mass-transfer coefficient $k_{L}$.
> - **Membrane separation** economics depend on diffusivity through polymer matrices.
> - **Polymer processing** and **drying operations** rely on solid-state $D_{AB}$ values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Diffusivity Measurement — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Stefan Tube Working Equation

Consider the Stefan tube setup described in Section 2.2.A. Liquid A evaporates at the gas-liquid interface ($z = 0$) and diffuses through stagnant gas B to the tube exit ($z = L$), where it is swept away by a carrier stream. We want to derive the expression for $D_{AB}$.

### Step 1 — Start with Fick's First Law for A through stagnant B

For a binary system in which B is stagnant ($N_{B} = 0$), the molar flux of A is:

$$N_{A} = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot C_{total}}{1 - x_{A}} \cdot \frac{dx_{A}}{dz}$$

In terms of partial pressure using the ideal gas law $C = P/(RT)$ and $p_{A} = x_{A} P$:

$$N_{A} = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot (P - p_{A})} \cdot \frac{dp_{A}}{dz}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the steady-state condition

Under steady state, the flux $N_{A}$ is constant along the length of the tube. Rearranging and integrating from $z = 0$ (interface, $p_{A} = p_{A1}$) to $z = L$ (top, $p_{A} = p_{A2}$):

$$N_{A} \int_{0}^{L} dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_{A}}{P - p_{A}}$$

The right side uses the fact that $P - p_{A} = p_{B}$, and we substitute the variable:

$$N_{A} \cdot L = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \int_{p_{B1}}^{p_{B2}} \frac{dp_{B}}{p_{B}}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the integral

$$\int_{p_{B1}}^{p_{B2}} \frac{dp_{B}}{p_{B}} = \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right) = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{p_{BM}}$$

where the **log-mean partial pressure of B** is defined as:

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$$

### Step 4 — Final working equation for $D_{AB}$

$$\boxed{D_{AB} = \frac{N_{A} \cdot R \cdot T \cdot p_{BM} \cdot L}{P \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}}$$

### Step 5 — Express flux as falling liquid level

The amount of A evaporating per unit time is determined by measuring the fall in liquid level $\Delta z$ over time $\Delta t$:

$$N_{A} = \frac{\rho_{L}}{M_{A}} \cdot \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta t}$$

Substituting into the boxed equation:

$$\boxed{D_{AB} = \frac{\rho_{L} \cdot R \cdot T \cdot p_{BM} \cdot L}{M_{A} \cdot P \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})} \cdot \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions Used in This Derivation:**
> 1. At $z = 0$: $p_{A} = p_{A1}$ (saturation vapor pressure of liquid A at temperature T)
> 2. At $z = L$: $p_{A} = p_{A2} \approx 0$ (rapid sweep gas removes A instantly)
> 3. Steady state: $\partial p_{A} / \partial t = 0$
> 4. Total pressure P is constant; ideal-gas behavior assumed.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Diaphragm Cell Working Equation

Consider the diaphragm cell described in Section 2.2.B. The cell is initially filled with concentrations $C_{A1,0}$ and $C_{A2,0}$ in compartments 1 and 2 respectively.

### Step 1 — Diaphragm as a "series" diffusion element

Because the bulk compartments are well-stirred, the concentration gradient exists only across the diaphragm. The instantaneous flux through the diaphragm is:

$$N_{A}(t) = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot A}{L} \, [C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)]$$

### Step 2 — Mass balance on compartment 1

The rate of change of moles of A in compartment 1 equals the negative of the flux into the diaphragm:

$$V_{1} \frac{dC_{A1}}{dt} = -N_{A}(t) = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot A}{L} \, [C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)]$$

Similarly, for compartment 2:

$$V_{2} \frac{dC_{A2}}{dt} = +\frac{D_{AB} \cdot A}{L} \, [C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)]$$

### Step 3 — Subtract and simplify

Subtracting the two equations:

$$\frac{d(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{dt} = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot A}{L} \left(\frac{1}{V_{1}} + \frac{1}{V_{2}}\right)(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$$

Defining the **cell constant** $\beta$:

$$\beta = \frac{A}{L}\left(\frac{1}{V_{1}} + \frac{1}{V_{2}}\right)$$

we obtain the first-order ODE:

$$\frac{d(\Delta C_{A})}{dt} = -\beta \, D_{AB} \, \Delta C_{A}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate from 0 to t

$$\int_{\Delta C_{A,0}}^{\Delta C_{A}(t)} \frac{d(\Delta C_{A})}{\Delta C_{A}} = -\beta \, D_{AB} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

### Step 5 — Final working equation

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left[\frac{C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)}{C_{A1,0} - C_{A2,0}}\right] = -\beta \, D_{AB} \, t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration of the cell constant $\beta$:**
> The diaphragm's effective area $A$ and pore length $L$ are difficult to measure directly. Hence $\beta$ is determined experimentally using a solute of known $D_{AB}$ (e.g., KCl in water at 25°C, $D_{AB} = 1.87 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s) and then used for unknown systems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Stefan Tube)

> **Problem:** Acetone (A) is diffusing through air (B) in a Stefan tube at 1 atm and 25°C. The tube length is 20 cm. The vapor pressure of acetone at 25°C is 0.326 atm. The liquid level fell by 0.5 cm in 12 hours. Density of acetone is 0.792 g/cm³. Calculate $D_{AB}$.

**Given:**
- $L = 20$ cm; $\Delta z = 0.5$ cm; $\Delta t = 12 \times 3600 = 43200$ s
- $T = 298$ K; $P = 1$ atm; $p_{A1} = 0.326$ atm; $p_{A2} = 0$
- $\rho_{L} = 0.792$ g/cm³; $M_{A} = 58.08$ g/mol; $R = 82.06$ cm³·atm/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Compute $p_{B1}$ and $p_{B2}$:**

$$p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 1 - 0.326 = 0.674 \text{ atm}$$
$$p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 1 - 0 = 1 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $p_{BM}$:**

$$p_{BM} = \frac{1 - 0.674}{\ln(1/0.674)} = \frac{0.326}{0.3945} = 0.8265 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the molar flux (rate of evaporation):**

$$N_{A} = \frac{\rho_{L}}{M_{A}} \cdot \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.792}{58.08} \cdot \frac{0.5}{43200} = 1.578 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/(cm}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $D_{AB}$:**

$$D_{AB} = \frac{N_{A} \cdot R \cdot T \cdot p_{BM} \cdot L}{P \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{(1.578\times10^{-6})(82.06)(298)(0.8265)(20)}{(1)(0.326)}$$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{6.385 \times 10^{-2}}{0.326} = 0.1092 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{D_{AB} \approx 0.109 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}}$$

This is consistent with typical **gas-phase diffusivity** values (order of 10⁻¹ cm²/s).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Diffusivity Measurement — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Diffusivity Measurement

```mermaid
graph TD
    problem[Diffusivity Measurement Problem] --> classify{Phase State?}
    classify -->|Gas Phase| stefan[Stefan Tube Method]
    classify -->|Liquid Phase| diaph[Diaphragm Cell Method]
    classify -->|Alternative Geometry| twobulb[Two Bulb Method]
    classify -->|Specialized Liquid| arnold[Arnold Cell Method]
    classify -->|No Data Available| corr[Correlation Methods]

    stefan --> s1[Steady State Assumption]
    s1 --> s2[Measure Delta z over Delta t]
    s2 --> s3[Compute pBM and N_A]
    s3 --> s4[Apply Ficks First Law]
    s4 --> sout[Output D_AB value]

    diaph --> d1[Unsteady State Assumption]
    d1 --> d2[Calibrate Beta with Known Solute]
    d2 --> d3[Measure Concentration vs Time]
    d3 --> d4[Linearize using log function]
    d4 --> d5[Slope gives Beta times D_AB]
    d5 --> dout[Output D_AB value]

    twobulb --> t1[Equal Volume Assumption]
    t1 --> t2[Track Decay of Concentration Difference]
    t2 --> t3[Apply log linear form]
    t3 --> tout[Output D_AB value]

    arnold --> a1[Flowing Solvent Stream]
    a1 --> a2[Measure Inlet Outlet Concentration]
    a2 --> a3[Apply log mean form]
    a3 --> aout[Output D_AB value]

    corr --> c1{System Type}
    c1 -->|Gas| chapman[Chapman Enskog Equation]
    c1 -->|Liquid| wilke[Wilke Chang Equation]
    c1 -->|Gas Estimate| fuller[Fuller Schettler Giddings]

    chapman --> cout[Output D_AB Estimate]
    wilke --> cout
    fuller --> cout
```

## 4.2 Stefan Tube Functional Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    inlet[Sweep Gas B In] --> top[Top of Tube z equals L]
    top --> stagnant[Stagnant Gas Column B with Diffusing A]
    stagnant --> interface[Gas Liquid Interface z equals 0]
    interface --> liquid[Pure Liquid A Reservoir]
    liquid -->|Evaporation| interface
    interface -->|Diffusion through Stagnant B| top
    top --> outlet[Sweep Gas with A Out]
    stagnant --> measure[Instrumentation: Measure Delta z over Delta t]
    measure --> calc[Compute D_AB from Working Equation]
    style inlet fill:#cce5ff
    style outlet fill:#cce5ff
    style stagnant fill:#ffe1cc
    style interface fill:#ccffcc
    style liquid fill:#e1ccff
    style measure fill:#ffcccc
    style calc fill:#ffffcc
```

## 4.3 Diaphragm Cell Functional Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    comp1[Compartment 1 Volume V1] -->|Stirred Continuously| dia[Porous Diaphragm Area A Thickness L]
    comp2[Compartment 2 Volume V2] -->|Stirred Continuously| dia
    dia -->|Molecular Diffusion| comp1
    dia -->|Molecular Diffusion| comp2
    comp1 --> sample1[Sample and Analyze C_A1 vs Time]
    comp2 --> sample2[Sample and Analyze C_A2 vs Time]
    sample1 --> logplot[Plot ln of Concentration Difference vs Time]
    sample2 --> logplot
    logplot --> slope[Slope equals negative Beta times D_AB]
    slope --> known[Use Calibrated Beta from Known Solute]
    known --> solve[Solve for D_AB]
    style comp1 fill:#cce5ff
    style comp2 fill:#ffe1cc
    style dia fill:#e1ccff
    style sample1 fill:#ccffcc
    style sample2 fill:#ccffcc
    style logplot fill:#ffcccc
    style slope fill:#ffffcc
    style solve fill:#ffccff
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Appropriate Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Need D_AB value] --> q1{Is system Gas or Liquid}
    q1 -->|Gas| q2{Steady or Unsteady feasible}
    q1 -->|Liquid| q3{Is high precision needed}
    q2 -->|Yes| m1[Use Stefan Tube]
    q2 -->|Diffusion too slow| m2[Use Two Bulb]
    q3 -->|Yes| m3[Use Diaphragm Cell with Calibration]
    q3 -->|Low concentration| m4[Use Arnold Cell]
    start --> q4{Experimental data available}
    q4 -->|No| m5[Use Predictive Correlation]
    m5 --> q5{System type}
    q5 -->|Non polar gas| m6[Chapman Enskog]
    q5 -->|Polar gas| m7[Fuller Schettler Giddings]
    q5 -->|Dilute liquid| m8[Wilke Chang]
    style start fill:#cce5ff
    style m1 fill:#ccffcc
    style m2 fill:#ccffcc
    style m3 fill:#ccffcc
    style m4 fill:#ccffcc
    style m5 fill:#ffcccc
    style m6 fill:#ffffcc
    style m7 fill:#ffffcc
    style m8 fill:#ffffcc
```

# Diffusivity Measurement — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "diffusivity" and explain how it is measured using the Stefan tube method.**

**Model Answer:**

**Diffusivity** is the proportionality constant in Fick's First Law of diffusion that relates the molar flux of a species to its concentration gradient. It has units of m²/s or cm²/s and represents the rate at which molecules of a species spread through a medium under a concentration driving force.

In the **Stefan tube method**, a vertical tube of known length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is partially filled with the pure liquid whose vapor (A) is to be studied. The top of the tube is swept with a non-dissolving gas (B) that maintains a near-zero partial pressure of A at the exit. Liquid A evaporates and diffuses through the stagnant gas column B. The rate of fall of the liquid level $\Delta z$ is measured over a time interval $\Delta t$. The diffusivity is then calculated from:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{\rho_{L} \cdot R \cdot T \cdot p_{BM} \cdot L \cdot (\Delta z/\Delta t)}{M_{A} \cdot P \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$$

where $p_{BM}$ is the log-mean partial pressure of B. This is an **absolute steady-state method** requiring no prior calibration.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Distinguish between steady-state and unsteady-state methods of measuring diffusivity. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Steady-State Method | Unsteady-State Method |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Definition** | Concentration profile is time-invariant; flux is constant | Concentration profile evolves with time |
| **Working Equation** | Based on Fick's First Law (no time derivative) | Based on Fick's Second Law (with time derivative) |
| **Typical Use** | Gas-phase systems (rapid diffusion) | Liquid-phase systems (slow diffusion) |
| **Example** | Stefan Tube, Arnold Cell | Diaphragm Cell, Two-Bulb Method |
| **Calibration** | Not required (absolute) | Usually requires calibration of cell constant |
| **Measurement Time** | Long (waiting for steady state) | Moderate (tracking decay) |

**Example:** *Stefan tube* (steady-state) — measures rate of fall of liquid level under constant flux. *Diaphragm cell* (unsteady-state) — measures concentration decay in two stirred compartments with time.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the working equation for measuring gas-phase diffusivity using the **Stefan tube (Wickel) method**. State all assumptions and boundary conditions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a Stefan tube experiment, benzene vapor (A) diffuses through air (B) at 1 atm total pressure and 30°C. The tube length is 25 cm. The vapor pressure of benzene at 30°C is 0.157 atm. The liquid level falls by 0.8 cm in 18 hours. Calculate the gas-phase diffusivity of benzene in air. Data: $\rho_{L,\text{benzene}} = 0.876$ g/cm³, $M_{\text{benzene}} = 78.11$ g/mol.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Assumptions:**
1. Diffusion is one-dimensional and steady-state.
2. Gas mixture behaves ideally.
3. Total pressure P and temperature T are uniform and constant.
4. Gas B is stagnant ($N_{B} = 0$).
5. At the top of the tube, the sweep gas instantly removes A, so $p_{A2} \approx 0$.
6. At the gas-liquid interface, the gas is saturated with A, so $p_{A1} = p_{A}^{sat}(T)$.

**Derivation:**

For diffusion of A through stagnant B, Fick's First Law gives:

$$N_{A} = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot p_{B}} \cdot \frac{dp_{A}}{dz}$$

Under steady state, $N_{A}$ is constant. Integrating from $z = 0$ ($p_{A} = p_{A1}$) to $z = L$ ($p_{A} = p_{A2}$):

$$N_{A} \cdot L = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{-dp_{A}}{P - p_{A}} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \int_{p_{B1}}^{p_{B2}} \frac{dp_{B}}{p_{B}}$$

The integral evaluates to:

$$\int_{p_{B1}}^{p_{B2}} \frac{dp_{B}}{p_{B}} = \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right) = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{p_{BM}}$$

where the **log-mean partial pressure of B** is:

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$$

The molar flux of A can be expressed using the rate of fall of liquid level:

$$N_{A} = \frac{\rho_{L}}{M_{A}} \cdot \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta t}$$

Combining these:

$$\boxed{D_{AB} = \frac{\rho_{L} \cdot R \cdot T \cdot p_{BM} \cdot L}{M_{A} \cdot P \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})} \cdot \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta t}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $L = 25$ cm; $\Delta z = 0.8$ cm; $\Delta t = 18 \times 3600 = 64800$ s
- $T = 303$ K; $P = 1$ atm; $p_{A1} = 0.157$ atm; $p_{A2} = 0$
- $\rho_{L} = 0.876$ g/cm³; $M_{A} = 78.11$ g/mol
- $R = 82.06$ cm³·atm/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Compute partial pressures of B:**

$$p_{B1} = 1 - 0.157 = 0.843 \text{ atm}$$
$$p_{B2} = 1 - 0 = 1 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $p_{BM}$:**

$$p_{BM} = \frac{1 - 0.843}{\ln(1/0.843)} = \frac{0.157}{0.1705} = 0.9208 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $N_{A}$:**

$$N_{A} = \frac{0.876}{78.11} \cdot \frac{0.8}{64800} = 1.384 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/(cm}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $D_{AB}$:**

$$D_{AB} = \frac{(1.384 \times 10^{-7})(82.06)(303)(0.9208)(25)}{(1)(0.157)}$$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{8.04 \times 10^{-2}}{0.157} = 0.0903 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{D_{AB} \approx 0.0903 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 9.03 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $p_{BM}$:** Students often use arithmetic mean instead of log-mean for the partial pressure of B. This causes ~5–10% error and is a guaranteed mark deduction.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency with R:** When R = 82.06 cm³·atm/(mol·K), all lengths must be in **cm** and pressure in **atm**. Mixing SI R = 8.314 with CGS L is a fatal mistake.
> 3. **Time unit:** Convert hours to seconds **before** substitution.
> 4. **Sign of $\Delta z$:** Always positive (fall in level), not negative.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the working equation for the **diaphragm cell** method of measuring liquid-phase diffusivity. Explain why calibration with a solute of known diffusivity is necessary.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A diaphragm cell is used to measure the diffusivity of KCl in water at 25°C. The cell has $V_{1} = V_{2} = 50$ cm³, diaphragm area $A = 10$ cm², and thickness $L = 0.2$ cm. Initial concentrations: $C_{A1,0} = 0.5$ mol/L, $C_{A2,0} = 0.0$ mol/L. After 5 hours, the concentration difference is observed to be 0.20 mol/L. Calculate the cell constant and the diffusivity of KCl in water. Verify with the known value of $1.87 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Setup:** Two well-stirtained compartments of volumes $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$ are separated by a porous diaphragm of cross-section $A$ and thickness $L$.

**Assumptions:**
1. Bulk compartments are perfectly mixed — concentration gradient exists only in the diaphragm.
2. The diaphragm pores are filled with the liquid mixture.
3. Isothermal and isobaric conditions.
4. $D_{AB}$ is independent of concentration over the range studied.

**Derivation:**

Instantaneous flux through the diaphragm:

$$N_{A}(t) = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot A}{L} \, [C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)]$$

Mass balance on compartment 1:

$$V_{1} \frac{dC_{A1}}{dt} = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot A}{L} \, [C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)]$$

Mass balance on compartment 2:

$$V_{2} \frac{dC_{A2}}{dt} = +\frac{D_{AB} \cdot A}{L} \, [C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)]$$

Subtracting the second from the first and defining $\Delta C_{A} = C_{A1} - C_{A2}$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta C_{A})}{dt} = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot A}{L}\left(\frac{1}{V_{1}} + \frac{1}{V_{2}}\right) \Delta C_{A} = -\beta \, D_{AB} \, \Delta C_{A}$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{A}{L}\left(\dfrac{1}{V_{1}} + \dfrac{1}{V_{2}}\right)$

Integrating from $t = 0$ to $t$:

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left[\frac{C_{A1}(t) - C_{A2}(t)}{C_{A1,0} - C_{A2,0}}\right] = -\beta \, D_{AB} \, t}$$

**Why Calibration is Necessary:** The effective area $A$ and effective path length $L$ of the diaphragm are not simply its geometric dimensions — tortuosity and porosity of the porous medium modify them. Hence the constant $\beta$ is best determined by running the experiment with a solute of known $D_{AB}$ (e.g., KCl in water) and computing $\beta = -(\text{slope})/D_{AB,\text{known}}$. This calibrated $\beta$ is then used for unknown systems.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $V_{1} = V_{2} = 50$ cm³; $A = 10$ cm²; $L = 0.2$ cm
- $C_{A1,0} = 0.5$ mol/L; $C_{A2,0} = 0$; $t = 5 \times 3600 = 18000$ s
- $\Delta C_{A}(t) = 0.20$ mol/L; $\Delta C_{A,0} = 0.5$ mol/L

**Step 1 — Compute the cell constant $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{10}{0.2}\left(\frac{1}{50} + \frac{1}{50}\right) = 50 \times 0.04 = 2.0 \text{ cm}^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the working equation:**

$$\ln\!\left[\frac{0.20}{0.50}\right] = -\beta \, D_{AB} \, t$$

$$\ln(0.4) = -2.0 \cdot D_{AB} \cdot 18000$$

$$-0.9163 = -36000 \, D_{AB}$$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.9163}{36000} = 2.545 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Verify with the known value:**

The experimental value $D_{AB} = 2.545 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s differs from the known $1.87 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s by about 36%. This discrepancy arises because the **geometric $\beta$** ($2.0$ cm⁻²) differs from the **true calibrated $\beta$** due to tortuosity and porosity of the diaphragm. The proper procedure is to back-calculate:

$$\beta_{\text{true}} = \frac{0.9163}{1.87 \times 10^{-5} \times 18000} = 2.72 \text{ cm}^{-2}$$

and use this calibrated $\beta_{\text{true}}$ for subsequent unknown measurements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Geometric vs. calibrated $\beta$:** A common mistake is to use only the geometric formula. Always state that calibration is essential for accuracy.
> 2. **Concentration units:** Both $C_{A1}$ and $C_{A2}$ must be in the **same units** when computing $\Delta C_{A}$.
> 3. **Sign of slope:** The slope of $\ln(\Delta C_{A})$ vs. $t$ is **negative**. A positive slope indicates an experimental error.
> 4. **Time conversion:** Convert hours → seconds before substituting.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**
>
> - Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ has units of **m²/s** (SI) or **cm²/s** (CGS); always state units in numerical answers.
> - **Stefan tube** is a **steady-state**, **absolute** (no calibration) method — best for **gas** systems.
> - **Diaphragm cell** is an **unsteady-state** method — best for **liquid** systems, **requires calibration**.
> - The **log-mean partial pressure of B** $p_{BM} = (p_{B2} - p_{B1})/\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})$ is **NOT** the arithmetic mean.
> - The Stefan tube flux equation: $D_{AB} = \dfrac{\rho_{L} R T p_{BM} L (\Delta z/\Delta t)}{M_{A} P (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$.
> - The diaphragm cell equation: $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{\Delta C_{A}(t)}{\Delta C_{A,0}}\right] = -\beta D_{AB} t$.
> - Cell constant: $\beta = \dfrac{A}{L}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{V_{1}} + \dfrac{1}{V_{2}}\right)$.
> - For two-bulb (equal volumes): slope of $\ln(\Delta C_{A})$ vs $t$ is $-2A D_{AB}/(LV)$.
> - When experimental data is unavailable, use predictive correlations: **Chapman-Enskog** (gases), **Wilke-Chang** (liquids), **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings** (gas estimation).
