> - Always use **consistent units** — when R = 82.06 cm³·atm/(mol·K), keep length in cm and pressure in atm.
> - **Boundary conditions matter:** clearly state $p_{A1} = p_{A}^{sat}(T)$ at the interface and $p_{A2} \approx 0$ at the sweep-gas exit.
> - **Order of magnitude check:** Gas $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-1}$ cm²/s; Liquid $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}$ cm²/s; Solid $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-9}$–$10^{-15}$ cm²/s.
> - In **derivations**, always begin with Fick's First Law (steady) or Fick's Second Law (unsteady), then state assumptions and BCs **before** integration.
> - **Calibration** is essential for diaphragm cell; geometric $\beta$ is only an estimate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady State Molecular Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steady State Molecular Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Molecular Diffusion?

Molecular diffusion is the **transport of mass caused by the random, spontaneous motion of individual molecules** from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration. This motion is driven purely by the **thermal kinetic energy** of the molecules (no external driving force like pressure or temperature gradient is required for the concentration driving force itself).

When this transport becomes **time-invariant** (i.e., the concentration profile at any point in space does not change with time), the system is said to be in a **Steady State**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Steady State Molecular Diffusion* is the macroscopic, isobaric, isothermal mass transfer process governed by Fick's First Law of Diffusion, where the molar flux of a component is proportional to the negative gradient of its concentration, and this flux remains constant with respect to time at every spatial location within the diffusion path.

---

## 1.2 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1855)

Fick's First Law is the **constitutive equation of mass transfer**, analogous to Fourier's Law in heat conduction and Newton's Law in momentum transfer.

Mathematically expressed for a binary mixture of **A** diffusing through **B**:

$$J_{A}^{*} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_{A}}{dz}$$

where the variables are:

- $J_{A}^{*}$ = **molar diffusion flux of A** with respect to the molar average velocity of the mixture, measured in **kmol/(m²·s)**
- $D_{AB}$ = **mass diffusivity** (or diffusion coefficient) of A in B, measured in **m²/s**
- $\frac{dC_{A}}{dz}$ = concentration gradient of A in the direction of diffusion, measured in **kmol/m⁴**

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Elevator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a packed elevator (B) in which a few extra people (A) are squeezed in on the ground floor. The extra people keep jostling and shuffling upward until the elevator is evenly full. This jostling continues forever because of thermal motion, but in steady state, the **density profile** of those extra people becomes **constant in time**, even though individual molecules keep moving. The net flux depends only on how steep the "crowd gradient" is.

A more engineering-style analogy is a **stagnant tube of air** with an open bottle of perfume at one end: in steady state, a constant rate of perfume molecules travels down the tube, even though any single molecule zig-zags randomly.

---

## 1.4 The Two Reference Flux Definitions

A critical point that confuses students — and is heavily tested — is the difference between the **absolute (total) flux $N_A$** and the **diffusion flux $J_A^*$**:

| Symbol | Name | Frame of Reference |
|---|---|---|
| $N_A$ | Total (absolute) molar flux | Stationary (laboratory) coordinates |
| $J_A^*$ | Diffusive molar flux | Molar-average velocity $v_M$ |

They are related by:

$$N_{A} = J_{A}^{*} + x_{A}\, N$$

where $N = N_A + N_B$ is the total molar flux and $x_A$ is the mole fraction of A.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Steady-State Diffusion Problems

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes steady-state molecular diffusion into **three canonical cases**:

1. **Case 1 — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD):** $N_A = -N_B$. Typical example: distillation.
2. **Case 2 — Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion):** $N_B = 0$. Typical example: evaporation of a pure liquid into a stagnant gas.
3. **Case 3 — Diffusion in Liquids / Through Solids:** Modified expressions for non-ideal condensed phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why steady-state matters in industry:** Distillation columns, gas absorbers, dryers, and membrane separators are designed assuming pseudo-steady-state. If the system is not steady, transient (unsteady) equations — i.e., Fick's Second Law — must be used.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Step-by-Step Logic Tiers

**Tier 1 — Molecular Reality:** Molecules are in constant, random thermal motion (Brownian). In a mixture where A is more concentrated at one point, statistically more A-molecules cross any imaginary plane in that direction than in the opposite direction.

**Tier 2 — Statistical Average:** Because billions of collisions occur per second per molecule, the instantaneous random velocities average out to a single macroscopic velocity field — the **molar-average velocity $v_M$** — and the net transport is described by Fick's First Law.

**Tier 3 — Engineering Use:** The engineer measures **bulk concentrations**, applies **boundary conditions**, integrates Fick's Law across the diffusion path, and obtains a **flux value** that drives equipment design (column heights, membrane areas, drying times).

---

## 2.2 Case-Wise Differential Equations

Starting from $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$ and using $N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$, the governing **steady-state** ODE for a binary system under constant $T$, $P$ is:

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left( \frac{N_A}{D_{AB}} \right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A \text{ is constant across } z$$

This constancy is the **defining feature** of steady-state diffusion.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — it is the single highest-yielding asset for KTU Module-1 calculations.

| # | Case | Governing Equation | Integrated Form (over path $\delta$) | Units Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's First Law (general) | $J_A^{*} = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | $J_A^{*} = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) | $N_A = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 3 | EMCD — pressure form | — | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT\,\delta} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})$ | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 4 | A through stagnant B (Stefan) | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\dfrac{1}{1 - x_A}\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$ | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,\delta\,P_{BM}}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})$ | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | Liquid — A through stagnant B | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C}{\delta\,x_{BM}}(x_{A1} - x_{A2})$ | Same — concentration form | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | Knudsen Diffusion (in pores) | — | $N_A = \dfrac{2}{3} \, r_p \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M_A}} \dfrac{\Delta C_A}{\delta}$ | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 7 | Steady-state through porous solid | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB,e}\,\varepsilon}{\tau}\,\dfrac{\Delta C_A}{\delta}$ | $D_{AB,e}$ = effective diffusivity | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |

**Log-mean term definitions (critical for Cases 4 & 5):**

$$P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} \quad ; \quad x_{BM} = \frac{x_{B2} - x_{B1}}{\ln(x_{B2}/x_{B1})}$$

---

## 2.4 Why Each Term Exists — Engineering Insight

- **The $\dfrac{1}{1-x_A}$ correction** appears because as A diffuses through stagnant B, the bulk flow induced by A's transport drags extra A along with it — this is **Stefan flow**, and ignoring it under-predicts the flux by up to 40 %.
- **The log-mean $P_{BM}$** is needed because $P_B = P - P_A$ is **not linear** with $z$, so a simple arithmetic mean is mathematically invalid.
- **Knudsen diffusion** appears when pore diameter $\approx$ mean free path (catalysts, membranes). Molecules hit walls more than each other.

---

## 2.5 Typical Magnitudes of Diffusivity

| Phase Pair | $D_{AB}$ Order of Magnitude |
|---|---|
| Gas – Gas | $\sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s |
| Liquid – Liquid (dilute) | $\sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s |
| Solid – Solid (interstitial) | $\sim 10^{-11}$ to $10^{-13}$ m²/s |

The **10 000 × difference** between gas and liquid diffusivities is a direct consequence of molecular crowding and viscosity in liquids.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) in Gases

**Step A.1 — Starting Postulate:**
EMCD is defined by $N_A + N_B = 0$, i.e., the total molar flux is zero. This means there is no net bulk (convective) flow. Therefore $N_A = J_A^*$.

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step A.2 — Convert to Partial Pressure Form:**
For an ideal gas at constant $T$, $P$: $C_A = \dfrac{P_A}{RT}$ and $dC_A = \dfrac{dP_A}{RT}$.

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

**Step A.3 — Steady-State Justification:**
At steady state $N_A = \text{const}$, so the RHS must be constant. Integrating from $z=0$ (where $P_A = P_{A1}$) to $z=\delta$ (where $P_A = P_{A2}$):

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} N_A \, dz = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \int_{P_{A1}}^{P_{A2}} dP_A$$

$$N_A \, \delta = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} (P_{A2} - P_{A1}) = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT\,\delta}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}$$

This is the **fundamental EMCD flux equation** used for equimolar stages in distillation column design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)

**Step B.1 — Statement of Physical Condition:**
B is stagnant, i.e., $N_B = 0$. This is the classical **Stefan tube** condition where a pure liquid A evaporates into a stagnant gas B.

From the absolute-flux definition:
$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

Substituting $N_B = 0$:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A N_A$$

**Step B.2 — Rearrange:**
Move the convective term to LHS:

$$N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step B.3 — Convert to Partial Pressures:**
With $C_A = P_A/RT$ and $1 - x_A = P_B / P$ (for ideal gas):

$$N_A \cdot \frac{P_B}{P} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

But $P_B = P - P_A$ and $dP_B = -dP_A$, hence:

$$N_A \cdot \frac{P_B}{P} = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_B}{dz}$$

**Step B.4 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**
Steady state → $N_A =$ const. Integrate from $z=0$ to $z=\delta$ and $P_{B1}$ to $P_{B2}$:

$$N_A \int_{0}^{\delta} dz = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT} \int_{P_{B1}}^{P_{B2}} \frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$

$$N_A \,\delta = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

**Step B.5 — Introduce Log-Mean Partial Pressure of B:**
Multiply numerator and denominator by $(P_{B2} - P_{B1})$ to express as a log-mean:

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,\delta\,P_{BM}}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}$$

where $P_{BM} = \dfrac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$.

This is the **most important equation** in Module-1 calculations — expect a derivation or numerical in every KTU End-Semester Exam.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Steady-State Diffusion in Liquids

For dilute liquid solutions, $C$ (total molar concentration of solution) is approximately constant. The analogous flux expression is:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,C}{\delta\,x_{BM}}(x_{A1} - x_{A2})$$

where $x_{BM} = \dfrac{x_{B2} - x_{B1}}{\ln(x_{B2}/x_{B1})}$ is the **log-mean mole fraction of B**.

The form is identical to the Stefan equation but with mole fractions replacing partial pressures, because the molar density of a liquid is essentially independent of composition.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (Stefan Diffusion — Most Common KTU Question Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified):**
> Ammonia (A) is absorbed from a gas mixture into water at 1 atm, 25 °C. The gas contains 20 mol% NH₃ and 80 mol% air (B). At the gas–liquid interface, the partial pressure of NH₃ is essentially zero (very soluble). The diffusion path through the gas film is estimated at $\delta = 1\text{ mm}$. $D_{AB,NH_3\text{-air}} = 0.236 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux of NH₃ through the stagnant air film.

**Solution:**

Given: $P_{A1} = 0.20 \times 101.325 = 20.265$ kPa, $P_{A2} \approx 0$ kPa, hence $P_{B1} = 80.0$ kPa, $P_{B2} = 101.325$ kPa. $T = 298$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $\delta = 0.001$ m.

Compute $P_{BM}$:

$$P_{BM} = \frac{101.325 - 80.0}{\ln(101.325/80.0)} = \frac{21.325}{\ln(1.2666)} = \frac{21.325}{0.2362} \approx 90.28 \text{ kPa}$$

Plug into Stefan equation:

$$N_A = \frac{(0.236 \times 10^{-4})(101325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.001)(90280)} (20265 - 0)$$

$$N_A = \frac{2.3913}{22320.6} \times 20265 \approx 2.17 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{N_A \approx 2.17 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

This corresponds to roughly **$1.95 \times 10^{-2}$ kg NH₃ per m² per second** — a tangible rate justifying the need for packed columns in ammonia scrubbing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Steady-State Molecular Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEADY STATE MOLECULAR DIFFUSION"]:::root --> B["GAS PHASE"]
    A --> C["LIQUID PHASE"]
    A --> D["SOLID POROUS MEDIA"]

    B --> B1["Equimolar Counter Diffusion EMCD"]
    B --> B2["A through Stagnant B - Stefan Diffusion"]

    C --> C1["Dilute Solution - Constant C"]
    C --> C2["Concentrated Solution - Variable C"]

    D --> D1["Fickian Molecular Diffusion"]
    D --> D2["Knudsen Diffusion - small pores"]
    D --> D3["Surface Diffusion"]
    D --> D4["Combined Molecular plus Knudsen"]

    B1 --> E1["NA = -NB  Total flux zero"]
    B2 --> E2["NB equals zero  Bulk flow present"]
    C1 --> E3["xBM log mean used"]
    D1 --> E4["Effective diffusivity De"]
    D2 --> E5["Pore radius rp dominant factor"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,font-weight:bold
    classDef leaf fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#0f172a
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — The Classic Stefan Tube Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST["Stefan Tube vertical glass column"]:::eq --> Z1["z equals 0  Bottom liquid A"]
    ST --> Z2["z equals delta  Top gas exit"]

    Z1 --> FL["Pure Liquid A evaporates"]
    FL --> VF["Vapor front - PA1 fixed at saturation"]
    VF --> DF["Stagnant gas film  B does not move"]
    DF --> Z2
    Z2 --> OUT["Bulk gas mixture  PA2 low"]

    classDef eq fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#451a03,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Governing Equation Set

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0["Ficks First Law - Microscopic Statement"]:::core --> Q1["Convert to total flux form"]
    Q1 --> Q2["Apply boundary condition - constant T P"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Integrate across path delta"]

    Q3 --> R1["Case EMCD - NA equals -NB"]
    Q3 --> R2["Case Stefan - NB equals zero"]
    Q3 --> R3["Case Liquid - C constant"]
    Q3 --> R4["Case Solid - De and tortuosity tau"]

    R1 --> S1["NA equals DAB times CA1 minus CA2 over delta"]
    R2 --> S2["NA equals DAB times P over RT delta PBM times PA1 minus PA2"]
    R3 --> S3["NA equals DAB times C over delta xBM times xA1 minus xA2"]
    R4 --> S4["NA equals De times CA1 minus CA2 over delta"]

    classDef core fill:#0f766e,stroke:#134e4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,font-weight:bold
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart for Solving a KTU Diffusion Numerical

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K0["Read the problem carefully"] --> K1["Identify phases - gas liquid solid"]
    K1 --> K2["Which component is stagnant"]
    K2 --> K3a["Nothing stagnant - EMCD"]
    K2 --> K3b["B stagnant - Stefan equation"]
    K2 --> K3c["Liquid phase - xBM form"]
    K2 --> K3d["Solid porous - De form"]

    K3a --> K4["Compute NA from EMCD equation"]
    K3b --> K5["Compute PBM log mean first"]
    K5 --> K4
    K3c --> K6["Compute xBM log mean first"]
    K6 --> K4
    K3d --> K7["Check De using porosity and tortuosity"]
    K7 --> K4

    K4 --> K8["Report with units kmol per m2 s"]
    K8 --> K9["Validate by checking DAB magnitude"]

    classDef step fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef terminal fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#14532d,font-weight:bold
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Define molar diffusivity and mention its units in SI.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a component A by diffusion is proportional to the concentration gradient of A and occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration.

$$J_A^{*} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Molar Diffusivity $D_{AB}$** is defined as the proportionality constant between the diffusion flux and the negative concentration gradient; physically it represents the **ease with which molecules of A move through B**.

**SI Units:** $D_{AB}$ is in **m²/s** (square metres per second).

*Key points for full marks:* (i) sign convention, (ii) direction of flux, (iii) SI unit, (iv) statement that the proportionality constant is the diffusivity.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Differentiate between equimolar counter-diffusion and diffusion of A through stagnant B. Give one industrial example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | EMCD | A through Stagnant B |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $N_A = -N_B$ | $N_B = 0$ |
| Net bulk flow | Zero | Non-zero (Stefan flow) |
| Driving force | Pure concentration gradient | Concentration gradient + convection |
| Industrial example | Distillation column | Absorption of NH₃ in water, evaporation of water into air |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> > [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget the $P_{BM}$ log-mean term and use arithmetic mean — expect **deduction of 2 marks** in every numerical.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Writing $D_{AB}$ in cm²/s when the path length is in metres — unit-mismatch costs **1 to 2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing $J_A^*$ (diffusion flux) with $N_A$ (total flux) — KTU expects clarity; write down which definition you are using.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In liquid-phase problems, leaving out the log-mean $x_{BM}$ is a **favourite trap** — it changes the answer by 10–30 %.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting that $P_{A2} = 0$ in absorption problems where the solute is infinitely soluble gives full credit only when explicitly stated.

---

### Question A — Case Study (14 Marks)  [Internal Choice Option 1]

> **Q.A(a) [7 Marks].** Derive the equation for steady-state molar flux of A diffusing through stagnant B at constant temperature and pressure. Clearly state the assumptions and define the log-mean partial pressure term.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state — concentration profile is time-invariant.
2. Constant $T$ and $P$ throughout the diffusion path.
3. Ideal gas behaviour.
4. B is stagnant ($N_B = 0$).
5. One-dimensional diffusion along $z$.
6. Equimolar counter-diffusion is NOT applicable.

**Derivation Outline (already shown in Section 3.2 above):**

Starting from total-flux relation with $N_B=0$:

$$N_A(1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Convert to partial pressures using $C_A = P_A/RT$, $1 - x_A = P_B/P$, and $dP_A = -dP_B$:

$$N_A \cdot \frac{P_B}{P} = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_B}{dz}$$

Integrate across the film $z = 0$ to $z = \delta$:

$$N_A \int_{0}^{\delta} dz = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \int_{P_{B1}}^{P_{B2}} \frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$

$$N_A \delta = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

Multiply and divide by $(P_{B2} - P_{B1})$:

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,\delta\,P_{BM}}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}$$

**Log-Mean Definition:**

$$P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$

**Engineering Significance:** This is the cornerstone equation for gas-film controlled processes — absorption, humidification, evaporation.

---

> **Q.A(b) [7 Marks].** Water at 25 °C evaporates into a large body of stagnant air. The water surface is at $z = 0$ with $P_{H_2O,1} = 3.17$ kPa; the top of the stagnant layer at $z = 5$ mm has $P_{H_2O,2} = 1.0$ kPa. Total pressure $P = 101.325$ kPa. $D_{AB,water\text{-air}} = 0.260 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s. Calculate (i) the molar flux of water, and (ii) the partial pressure of water at $z = 2.5$ mm.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Molar flux:**

- $P_{B1} = 101.325 - 3.17 = 98.155$ kPa
- $P_{B2} = 101.325 - 1.0 = 100.325$ kPa

$$P_{BM} = \frac{100.325 - 98.155}{\ln(100.325/98.155)} = \frac{2.170}{0.02196} \approx 98.82 \text{ kPa}$$

$$N_A = \frac{(0.260 \times 10^{-4})(101325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.005)(98820)}(3170 - 1000)$$

Numerator: $(2.604 \times 10^{-5})(101325) = 2.6385$
Denominator: $(2477.6)(98820) = 2.448 \times 10^{8}$

$$N_A = \frac{2.6385 \times 2170}{2.448 \times 10^{8}} = 2.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

$$\boxed{N_A \approx 2.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

**(ii) Partial Pressure at mid-point:**

For a linear flux and the integrated form, use the same equation solved for $P_A(z)$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT\,z\,P_{B,M}(z)}(P_{A1} - P_A(z))$$

Compute $P_{B,M}(z=2.5\text{mm})$ where $P_B(z)$ is unknown. An iterative or analytical solution gives $P_A \approx 1.85$ kPa.

*Acceptable approximate answer:* **$P_{A}(2.5\,\text{mm}) \approx 1.83\text{–}1.90$ kPa** depending on rounding.

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)  [Internal Choice Option 2]

> **Q.B(a) [7 Marks].** Derive the expression for steady-state equimolar counter-diffusion in a binary gas system. State the conditions under which EMCD is applicable, and list any **three** industrial applications.

**Model Solution:**

(See Section 3.1 for full derivation.)

**Final Equation:**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT\,\delta}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$

**Conditions for EMCD:**

1. Total molar flux is zero: $N = N_A + N_B = 0$.
2. Equal and opposite molar fluxes: $N_A = -N_B$.
3. Constant $T$ and $P$.
4. No net bulk motion of the mixture.
5. Diffusion occurs in a closed, isobaric, isothermal system.

**Industrial Applications (any 3):**

1. **Distillation** — vapour and liquid phases approach equilibrium through EMCD at the interface.
2. **Molecular diffusion in porous catalysts** under Knudsen-free regime.
3. **Counter-diffusion in gas mixtures** during metal heat treatment (e.g., carburizing, decarburizing).
4. **Reverse-osmosis feed-side analysis** at the membrane interface (with appropriate correction).

---

> **Q.B(b) [7 Marks].** Helium (A) and nitrogen (B) undergo equimolar counter-diffusion at 25 °C and 1 atm through a 2-mm-thick porous plug. At one face, $P_{He} = 60$ kPa; at the other, $P_{He} = 20$ kPa. Given $D_{AB,He\text{-}N_2} = 0.687 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s, calculate (i) the molar flux of He and (ii) the molar flux of N₂.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Flux of He:**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT\,\delta}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$

$$N_A = \frac{0.687 \times 10^{-4}}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)} \times (60000 - 20000)$$

$$N_A = \frac{0.687 \times 10^{-4}}{4.955} \times 40000 = 5.547 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

$$\boxed{N_{He} \approx 5.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

**(ii) Flux of N₂:** By the EMCD condition:

$$N_B = -N_A = -5.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

The negative sign indicates that N₂ diffuses in the **opposite** direction to He, as expected.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steady State Molecular Diffusion**

- **Fick's First Law** is the **constitutive equation of mass transfer**, analogous to Fourier's and Newton's laws.
- **Diffusivity $D_{AB}$** has SI units of **m²/s**; gas-phase values lie near $10^{-5}$, liquid-phase near $10^{-9}$.
- Two reference frames coexist: **diffusive flux $J_A^*$** (with respect to molar-average velocity) and **total flux $N_A$** (with respect to stationary coordinates).
- **EMCD** has $N_A = -N_B$ and uses the simplest flux form $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT\delta}(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$.
- **Stefan Diffusion** has $N_B = 0$ and **must** include the log-mean term $P_{BM}$ to be correct.
- The **log-mean** is **never** replaced by arithmetic mean in KTU examinations — a guaranteed mark loss.
- **Liquid-phase diffusion** uses mole-fraction log-mean $x_{BM}$ instead of $P_{BM}$.
- For **porous solids**, the effective diffusivity $D_{e} = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,\varepsilon}{\tau}$ accounts for porosity $\varepsilon$ and tortuosity $\tau$.
- **Knudsen diffusion** dominates when pore radius is comparable to molecular mean free path.
- The mass-transfer analogy: **Heat ↔ Mass ↔ Momentum** transfer equations have **identical mathematical forms** (Reynolds, Prandtl, Schmidt, Lewis numbers).
- Always **state the assumption** of constant $T$, constant $P$, steady state, and 1-D diffusion before writing any equation.
- Convert partial pressures to **Pa** (not kPa) before substituting into $RT$ if $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K — unit traps cost marks.
- For dilute systems $x_{BM} \approx 1$ and the log-mean collapses to a simple average — but show the substitution step.
- **Engineering units matter**: A typical answer of $\sim 10^{-5}$ kmol/(m²·s) for gas-phase absorption is reasonable; $\sim 10^{-8}$ is too low; $\sim 10^{-3}$ is too high — sanity-check your answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Most Important Takeaway:**
> The **steady-state diffusion equation set** you have studied in this module is **the foundation** for understanding **absorption, humidification, drying, and membrane separation** in subsequent modules. Every one of those processes reduces, at the molecular scale, to one of the four boxed equations in Section 2.3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: One Component Transferring to Non-Diffusing Component and Equimolar Counter Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** One Component Transferring to Non-Diffusing Component and Equimolar Counter Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In binary molecular diffusion (Module 1 of Mass Transfer Operations), the diffusion behaviour is governed by the relative motion of the two species **A** and **B** present in the mixture. The two limiting cases treated in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case 1 — Diffusion of A through Non-Diffusing (Stagnant) B (Stefan's Diffusion):**
> Component **A** diffuses through a medium in which component **B** is *stationary* on a molar basis. Mathematically, **N_B = 0**, while **N_A ≠ 0**. Examples: evaporation of water into still air, absorption of a single soluble gas from an insoluble carrier gas, sublimation of naphthalene into air, humidification operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case 2 — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMD):**
> Both components **A** and **B** diffuse in *opposite directions* at *equal molar rates*. Mathematically, **N_A = −N_B**, and the total molar flux **N_A + N_B = 0**, so the mixture exhibits no net bulk motion. Examples: binary distillation at constant pressure, counter-diffusion of O₂ and N₂, mutual diffusion in a perfectly symmetric membrane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Foundation:** Both cases are derived from the **Stefan–Maxwell / Fick's Law framework** for binary systems:
>
> $$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A+N_B)$$
>
> $$N_B = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_B}{dz} + x_B(N_A+N_B)$$
>
> The *boundary conditions* (whether B is stagnant or moves counter to A) dictate which terms survive after simplification.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy for Stagnant B (Stefan's Diffusion):**
Imagine a small bottle of *room freshener* (component A) opened in one corner of a *closed, still room* (component B = air). The freshener molecules slowly drift across the room, but the air molecules themselves do **not** travel on a net basis — they are *stagnant*. The freshener has to "push" its way through the motionless air using only its own random molecular motion. The *bulk flow* of A is partially retarded by the presence of the stationary B. This is **Stefan's tube experiment** in action.

**Analogy for Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMD):**
Picture a *narrow corridor* where exactly the same number of people (A) walk from left → right as people (B) walking from right → left, simultaneously. The net crowd flow is *zero*, so neither group feels a "bulk drag" from the other. Each species diffuses purely by its *own concentration gradient*, unimpeded by convective flow. This represents perfect, symmetric counter-diffusion.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **R** | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (SI) or **0.0821 L·atm·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (CGS) |
| **P** | Total System Pressure | **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa** |
| **T** | Absolute Temperature | **K** (always Kelvin) |
| **D_AB** | Binary Diffusivity | **m²·s⁻¹** (typical gas values ~10⁻⁵ m²/s; liquids ~10⁻⁹ m²/s) |
| **N_A, N_B** | Molar Fluxes | **kg-mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹** |
| **z** | Diffusion Path Length | **m** |

> [!NOTE]
> The use of **P, T, R** under the ideal-gas assumption is the *standard KTU 2024 convention* for diffusion problems involving gases.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Step Decomposition — Stefan's Diffusion (A through Stagnant B)

The derivation follows a strict sequence of five logical steps. Each is essential for KTU 14-mark derivations.

**Step 1 — Start with Fick's First Law in molar form (for a binary system):**
We define the *diffusive flux* relative to the *molar-average velocity* as
$$J_A^* = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$
The total molar flux in the stationary frame is
$$N_A = J_A^* + C_A\,v_m \quad \text{where} \quad v_m = \frac{N_A+N_B}{C_{\text{total}}}$$
So, in component form:
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A+N_B) \quad (1)$$
$$N_B = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_B}{dz} + x_B(N_A+N_B) \quad (2)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Stagnant-B constraint (the "Why"):**
Because B is non-diffusing,
$$N_B = 0$$
Substituting into (1):
$$N_A(1-x_A) = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$
Since $1-x_A = x_B$:
$$N_A\,x_B = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} \quad (3)$$

**Step 3 — Convert concentration to partial pressure using ideal gas law (the "How"):**
For an ideal gas, $C_A = P_A/(RT)$ and $x_B = P_B/P$. Also, at constant T and P, the constraint of constant total pressure gives $dP_A = -dP_B$, and so $dC_A = (1/RT)\,dP_A = -(1/RT)\,dP_B$.

Substituting into (3):
$$N_A\,\frac{P_B}{P} = -D_{AB}\cdot\frac{1}{RT}\cdot\frac{dP_A}{dz} = D_{AB}\cdot\frac{1}{RT}\cdot\frac{dP_B}{dz}$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate over the diffusion path z₁ → z₂ (boundary conditions):**
At steady state, N_A is constant along z. Therefore:
$$N_A\int_{z_1}^{z_2}\!\!dz = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\int_{P_{B1}}^{P_{B2}}\!\!\frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$
$$N_A\,z = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Express in the canonical KTU form (the final form to memorise):**
$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P-P_{A2}}{P-P_{A1}}\right)\;}$$

The flux can also be written in the *linear* (driving-force) form by introducing the **log-mean partial pressure of B**:
$$P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2}-P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$
which leads to the alternative expression:
$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z\,P_{BM}}\,(P_{A1}-P_{A2})\;}$$

---

### 2.2 Logical Step Decomposition — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMD)

**Step 1 — Apply the EMD constraint (the "Why"):**
For equimolar counter-diffusion:
$$N_A = -N_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A + N_B = 0$$

**Step 2 — Simplify Fick's Law with this constraint (the "How"):**
Substituting $N_A+N_B = 0$ into equation (1):
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\cdot 0 = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute ideal-gas relations:**
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\cdot\frac{1}{RT}\cdot\frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate (boundary conditions z₁ → z₂, P_A1 → P_A2):**
$$N_A\int_{0}^{z}\!\!dz = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT}\int_{P_{A1}}^{P_{A2}}\!\!dP_A$$
$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT\,z}(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$$

**Step 5 — Canonical KTU form (memorise this):**
$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT\,z}(P_{A1}-P_{A2}) = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_{\text{total}}}{z}(x_{A1}-x_{A2})\;}$$
and symmetrically $N_B = -N_A$.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Scenario | Stagnant B (Stefan) | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| Distillation column | ✗ Not applicable (here **both** components move — EMD applies) | ✓ McCabe–Thiele analysis assumes EMD |
| Humidification / Dehumidification | ✓ Water vapour diffusing into air | ✗ |
| Gas absorption (single soluble component) | ✓ Solute A in carrier gas B | ✗ |
| Sublimation of solids (e.g., naphthalene, iodine) | ✓ | ✗ |
| Membrane permeation (symmetric membrane) | ✗ | ✓ |
| Drying of porous solids | ✓ (often simplified as Stefan flow) | ✗ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Many numerical problems in the KTU ESE ask students to *identify the correct governing equation* before substitution. Confusing the two cases typically costs **3–4 marks** per question.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Situation | Governing Equation | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's Law (general binary) | $N_A = -D_{AB}\,dC_A/dz + x_A(N_A+N_B)$ | Foundation equation |
| 2 | Stagnant B — log form | $N_A = (D_{AB}P)/(RTz)\cdot \ln[(P-P_{A2})/(P-P_{A1})]$ | Most-used form |
| 3 | Stagnant B — linear form | $N_A = (D_{AB}P)/(RTz\,P_{BM})\cdot(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$ | Uses $P_{BM}$ |
| 4 | Log-mean of B | $P_{BM} = (P_{B2}-P_{B1})/\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})$ | Always positive |
| 5 | Equimolar counter-diffusion | $N_A = (D_{AB})/(RTz)\cdot(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$ | No P-factor |
| 6 | EMD in mole-fraction form | $N_A = (D_{AB}\,C_{\text{total}})/z\cdot(x_{A1}-x_{A2})$ | Useful for liquids |
| 7 | Constraint comparison | $N_B = 0$ (Stagnant) vs $N_A = -N_B$ (EMD) | Distinguishes cases |
| 8 | Total flux ratio | Stagnant: $N_A > J_A^*$; EMD: $N_A = J_A^*$ | Bulk-flow contribution |

> [!NOTE]
> Note the absence of the factor $P/P_{BM}$ in the EMD equation — this is the **key mathematical fingerprint** separating the two cases.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Stefan's Equation (Stagnant B)

We begin with the rigorous general binary flux equation. Each algebraic transition is fully written out.

**Starting Point — Fick's Law for component A in a binary mixture:**

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\,(N_A+N_B)$$

**Line 1 → Constraint applied ($N_B = 0$):**

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\cdot N_A$$

**Line 2 → Rearrange to isolate $N_A$:**

$$N_A - x_A N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

$$N_A(1-x_A) = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Line 3 → Apply $1-x_A = x_B$:**

$$N_A\,x_B = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Line 4 → Use ideal-gas identities** ($C_A = P_A/RT$, $C_B = P_B/RT$, $x_B = P_B/P$, and $dP_A = -dP_B$ at constant T,P):

$$N_A \cdot \frac{P_B}{P} = -D_{AB}\cdot\frac{1}{RT}\cdot\frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

Since $dP_A/dz = -dP_B/dz$:

$$N_A\cdot\frac{P_B}{P} = D_{AB}\cdot\frac{1}{RT}\cdot\frac{dP_B}{dz}$$

**Line 5 → Rearrange for separation of variables:**

$$N_A\,dz = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\cdot\frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$

**Line 6 → Integrate across the diffusion path** with boundary conditions: $z = z_1 = 0$ (point 1) and $z = z_2 = z$ (point 2); correspondingly $P_B = P_{B1}$ and $P_B = P_{B2}$:

$$\int_{0}^{z}\!N_A\,dz = \int_{P_{B1}}^{P_{B2}}\!\frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\cdot\frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$

**Line 7 → At steady state, $N_A$ is constant along z (this is the "Why" of being able to take it outside):**

$$N_A\cdot z = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

**Line 8 → Solve for $N_A$ (the final canonical form):**

$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)\;}$$

**Line 9 → Express in terms of partial pressure of A** (using $P_{B} = P - P_A$):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right)$$

**Line 10 → Alternative linear form using the log-mean of $P_B$:**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z\,P_{BM}}\,(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$$

where

$$P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$

**Verification of the log-mean identity** (used in Line 10):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right) = \frac{(P_{B2}-P_{B1})}{P_{BM}}$$

by the *definition of the logarithmic mean*. The numerator $P_{B2}-P_{B1} = (P - P_{A2}) - (P - P_{A1}) = P_{A1} - P_{A2}$, so

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\cdot\frac{P_{A1}-P_{A2}}{P_{BM}} = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z\,P_{BM}}\,(P_{A1}-P_{A2}) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion

**Starting Point:**

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A+N_B)$$

**Line 1 → Apply EMD constraint** ($N_B = -N_A$, hence $N_A+N_B=0$):

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\cdot 0$$

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Line 2 → Convert to partial pressures:**

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT}\frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

**Line 3 → Separate variables and integrate:**

$$N_A\int_{0}^{z}dz = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT}\int_{P_{A1}}^{P_{A2}}dP_A$$

$$N_A\cdot z = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT}\,(P_{A2}-P_{A1})$$

**Line 4 → Final canonical form:**

$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{R\,T\,z}\,(P_{A1}-P_{A2})\;}$$

In mole-fraction form (using $P_{A} = x_A P$ for ideal gases):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,(x_{A1}-x_{A2}) = \frac{D_{AB}\,C_{\text{total}}}{z}\,(x_{A1}-x_{A2}) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

**Problem:** Ammonia (A) is diffusing through a stagnant layer of air (B) at 1 atm total pressure and 25 °C. The diffusion path length is z = 0.02 m. At point 1, $P_{A1} = 0.10$ atm; at point 2, $P_{A2} = 0.02$ atm. The binary diffusivity $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux $N_A$.

**Solution (every step shown):**

**Step 1 — Identify the case.** Since B (air) is stagnant, this is **Stefan's diffusion**. Use the linear form:
$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z\,P_{BM}}(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_{B1}$ and $P_{B2}$:**
$$P_{B1} = P - P_{A1} = 1.00 - 0.10 = 0.90\ \text{atm}$$
$$P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 1.00 - 0.02 = 0.98\ \text{atm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P_{BM}$:**
$$P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} = \frac{0.98 - 0.90}{\ln(0.98/0.90)} = \frac{0.08}{\ln(1.0889)} = \frac{0.08}{0.08520} = 0.9389\ \text{atm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply ideal gas constant and temperature:**
$R = 0.0821$ L·atm·(mol·K)⁻¹, but in SI units $R = 8.314$ J·(mol·K)⁻¹; using SI gives $N_A$ in mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹. Convert pressure: $P = 1$ atm $= 1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa. T = 298.15 K.

**Step 5 — Substitute all values (in SI units):**

$$N_A = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1.01325 \times 10^5)}{(8.314)(298.15)(0.02)(0.9389 \times 1.01325 \times 10^5)}\cdot(0.10 - 0.02)\times 1.01325\times 10^5$$

Using pressure in atm directly with $R = 0.0821$ m³·atm·(kmol·K)⁻¹ is simpler. Convert $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s; using $R = 0.0821$ m³·atm·(kmol·K)⁻¹:

$$N_A = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1.00)}{(0.0821)(298.15)(0.02)(0.9389)}\,(0.08)$$

**Step 6 — Numerator:**
$(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1.00)(0.08) = 1.84 \times 10^{-6}$

**Step 7 — Denominator:**
$(0.0821)(298.15)(0.02)(0.9389) = 0.0821 \times 298.15 \times 0.018778 = 0.4596$

**Step 8 — Final result:**

$$N_A = \frac{1.84 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4596} = 4.00 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

Equivalently $N_A \approx 4.00 \times 10^{-3}$ mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked for flux, *always* state the units explicitly. Forgetting units is the **single most common mark-deduction** in 14-mark Stefan-diffusion problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Architecture — Two Regimes of Binary Molecular Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Binary Molecular Diffusion Start] --> A1{Net molar flux of B}
    A1 -- NB equals 0 --> A2[Stagnant Film Case\nStefans Diffusion]
    A1 -- NA equals minus NB --> A3[Equimolar Counter Diffusion\nEMD]

    A2 --> B1[Governing Constraint:\nNB equals 0]
    B1 --> B2[Simplified Fick:\nNA xB equals minus DAB dCA by dz]
    B2 --> B3[Ideal Gas Substitution:\nCA equals PA by RT]
    B3 --> B4[Integrate z1 to z2\nP_B1 to P_B2]
    B4 --> B5[Final Form:\nNA equals DAB P by RT z times ln PB2 over PB1]

    A3 --> C1[Governing Constraint:\nNA plus NB equals 0]
    C1 --> C2[Simplified Fick:\nNA equals minus DAB dCA by dz]
    C2 --> C3[Ideal Gas Substitution:\nCA equals PA by RT]
    C3 --> C4[Integrate z1 to z2\nPA1 to PA2]
    C4 --> C5[Final Form:\nNA equals DAB by RT z times PA1 minus PA2]

    B5 --> D1[Applications:\nHumidification, Drying,\nAbsorption of single solute]
    C5 --> D2[Applications:\nBinary Distillation,\nEquimolar Membrane Transport]

    B5 --> E1[Key Fingerprint:\nFactor P by P_BM present]
    C5 --> E2[Key Fingerprint:\nNo P by P_BM factor]

    classDef startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef caseNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stepNode fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef resultNode fill:#548235,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef appNode fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff

    class A0 startNode
    class A1,A2,A3 caseNode
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,C1,C2,C3,C4 stepNode
    class B5,C5 resultNode
    class D1,D2,E1,E2 appNode
```

### 4.2 Boundary-Condition and Concentration-Profile Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stagnant B: Stefans Diffusion]
        S1A[Z equals 0\nPA equals PA1 high\nPB equals P minus PA1] -->|Diffusion through stagnant B| S1B[Z equals z\nPA equals PA2 low\nPB equals P minus PA2]
        S1A --> S1C[NB equals 0 at all z]
        S1B --> S1C
        S1C --> S1D[Bulk flow term:\nxA times NA retarding]
    end

    subgraph S2[Equimolar Counter Diffusion]
        S2A[Z equals 0\nPA equals PA1 high\nPB equals PB1 low] -->|Equal and opposite flux| S2B[Z equals z\nPA equals PA2 low\nPB equals PB2 high]
        S2A --> S2C[NB equals minus NA at all z]
        S2B --> S2C
        S2C --> S2D[Bulk flow term:\nzero net molar flow]
    end

    classDef stagnantStyle fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef emdStyle fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef pointStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef condStyle fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    class S1A,S1B,S2A,S2B pointStyle
    class S1C,S2C condStyle
    class S1D stagnantStyle
    class S2D emdStyle
```

### 4.3 Engineering Decision Tree — Which Equation to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Problem Statement Given] --> Q1{Is component B\nstationary?}
    Q1 -- Yes Stagnant --> Q2{Are partial\npressures given?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Are NA and NB\nequal and opposite?}
    Q3 -- Yes EMD --> Q4[Use N_A equals DAB by RT z times PA1 minus PA2]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5[General binary diffusion:\nFicks Law with bulk term]

    Q2 -- Yes --> Q6[Use linear form:\nN_A equals DAB P by RT z P_BM times PA1 minus PA2]
    Q2 -- No --> Q7[Use log form:\nN_A equals DAB P by RT z times ln of PB2 over PB1]

    Q6 --> R1[Compute P_BM:\nP_B2 minus P_B1 over ln PB2 over PB1]
    Q7 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Substitute R, T, P, z, D_AB]
    R2 --> R3[Final flux N_A with units]

    Q4 --> R2
    Q5 --> R2

    classDef questionStyle fill:#f4b084,stroke:#833c0c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef yesStyle fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef noStyle fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef resultStyle fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#1f3864,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef formulaStyle fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    class Q0 questionStyle
    class Q1,Q3 questionStyle
    class Q2 questionStyle
    class Q6,Q7 yesStyle
    class Q4,Q5 yesStyle
    class R1,R2,R3 resultStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The teal/red boxes highlight the *case* (Stagnant vs EMD); the blue boxes show the *derivation pipeline*; the yellow boxes mark the *final computed result*. In KTU board answers, this colour-coding logic maps directly to the **heading structure** expected by the examiner.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define equimolar counter-diffusion in a binary gas mixture. Why is the bulk-flow term absent in the final flux expression?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Equimolar counter-diffusion (EMD) is a special case of isothermal, isobaric binary molecular diffusion in which the two components **A** and **B** move in **opposite directions at equal molar rates**, i.e., $N_A = -N_B$. The bulk-flow (convective) term $x_A(N_A + N_B)$ vanishes identically because $N_A + N_B = 0$, so the total molar flux in the stationary frame equals the pure diffusive flux $J_A^*$. The resulting expression is $N_A = (D_{AB}/RTz)(P_{A1} - P_{A2})$.
**(1 mark** for definition**, 1 mark** for the bulk-flow argument**, 1 mark** for final equation**.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Stefan's law of diffusion. Under what condition is the log-mean partial pressure $P_{BM}$ used in the flux expression?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stefan's law gives the steady-state molar flux of a component **A** diffusing through a stagnant film of **B**:
$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z\,P_{BM}}\,(P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$
The log-mean $P_{BM} = (P_{B2} - P_{B1})/\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})$ is used when the equation is recast in the *linear driving-force form* — i.e., when the flux is expressed as a *constant* times a *linear* difference in partial pressures of A. It is mathematically equivalent to the logarithmic form $N_A = (D_{AB}P/RTz)\ln[(P - P_{A2})/(P - P_{A1})]$.
**(1 mark** for Stefan's law**, 1 mark** for $P_{BM}$ definition**, 1 mark** for equivalence statement**.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always identify the case (Stagnant vs EMD) first.** Examiners award 1–2 marks *just* for explicitly stating which case applies and the constraint ($N_B = 0$ or $N_A = -N_B$). Skipping this step is the **#1 cause of lost marks**.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse the units of R.** Use $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ with Pa and m, or $R = 0.0821$ L·atm·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ with atm and litres, or $R = 0.0821$ m³·atm·kmol⁻¹·K⁻¹ with atm, m³, kmol. Mixing units = **−2 marks minimum**.
> 3. **Forgetting the log-mean arithmetic** is the most common 3–4 mark loss in numerical problems. Show the substitution $P_{B1} = P - P_{A1}$, $P_{B2} = P - P_{A2}$ *before* writing $P_{BM}$.
> 4. **No unit on the final answer = −1 mark.** KTU strict.
> 5. **Negative flux values** indicate a *sign convention error*, not necessarily a wrong magnitude. Re-check point-1 and point-2 orientation.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for steady-state molar flux of component A diffusing through a stagnant gas B, starting from Fick's first law for a binary mixture. Clearly state the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 marks]** In a Stefan's tube experiment, water vapour (A) diffuses through stagnant air (B) at 30 °C and 1 atm total pressure. The diffusion path length is 0.015 m. The partial pressure of water vapour at the water surface is 0.0425 atm (saturation) and at the top of the tube is 0.012 atm. The binary diffusivity of water vapour in air at 30 °C is $2.60 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate (i) the molar flux of water vapour, and (ii) the rate of evaporation in kg/h if the tube cross-section is $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m².

---

##### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumption block (1 mark):*
1. Steady state.
2. Isothermal, isobaric system ($T$, $P$ constant).
3. Ideal-gas behaviour.
4. One-dimensional diffusion along z.
5. Component B is stagnant: $N_B = 0$.

*Derivation steps (6 marks):*

Step 1 — Start from Fick's first law for binary mixture:
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A+N_B)$$

Step 2 — Impose $N_B = 0$:
$$N_A(1-x_A) = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$
$$N_A\,x_B = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Step 3 — Ideal-gas identities ($C_A = P_A/RT$, $x_B = P_B/P$, $dP_A = -dP_B$):
$$N_A\cdot\frac{P_B}{P} = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT}\frac{dP_B}{dz}$$

Step 4 — Separate and integrate:
$$N_A\int_{0}^{z}dz = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\int_{P_{B1}}^{P_{B2}}\frac{dP_B}{P_B}$$
$$N_A\,z = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

Step 5 — Final form (1 mark):
$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P-P_{A2}}{P-P_{A1}}\right)\;}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute stagnant-B partial pressures:
$$P_{B1} = 1.00 - 0.0425 = 0.9575\ \text{atm}$$
$$P_{B2} = 1.00 - 0.0120 = 0.9880\ \text{atm}$$

Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute log-mean:
$$P_{BM} = \frac{0.9880 - 0.9575}{\ln(0.9880/0.9575)} = \frac{0.0305}{\ln(1.03186)} = \frac{0.0305}{0.03136} = 0.9726\ \text{atm}$$

Step 3 (1 mark) — Apply Stefan's linear form:
$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z\,P_{BM}}\,(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$$

Step 4 (2 marks) — Substitute values (use $R = 0.0821$ m³·atm·kmol⁻¹·K⁻¹; convert $D_{AB} = 2.60 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s; $T = 303.15$ K; $z = 0.015$ m; $P = 1$ atm):

$$N_A = \frac{(2.60 \times 10^{-5})(1.00)(0.0425 - 0.0120)}{(0.0821)(303.15)(0.015)(0.9726)}$$

Numerator: $(2.60 \times 10^{-5})(0.0305) = 7.93 \times 10^{-7}$
Denominator: $(0.0821)(303.15)(0.015)(0.9726) = 0.3633$

$$N_A = \frac{7.93 \times 10^{-7}}{0.3633} = 2.183 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

Or equivalently $N_A = 2.183 \times 10^{-3}$ mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹. (1 mark)

Step 5 (2 marks) — Rate of evaporation:
$$W_A = N_A \cdot A \cdot M_A = (2.183 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1})(5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2)(3600\ \text{s/h})(18\ \text{kg/kmol})$$

$$W_A = (2.183 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-4})(64800)(18) = 1.273 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\;W_A \approx 1.27 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/h} = 1.27\ \text{g/h}\;}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the steady-state molar flux in equimolar counter-diffusion of a binary ideal-gas mixture, starting from Fick's first law.

**(b) [7 marks]** Hydrogen (A) and carbon dioxide (B) undergo equimolar counter-diffusion in a circular tube of length 0.10 m and inside diameter 0.025 m at 1 atm and 20 °C. The partial pressures are $P_{A1} = 0.05$ atm, $P_{A2} = 0.02$ atm at the two ends. The binary diffusivity $D_{AB} = 6.10 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate (i) the molar flux of H₂, and (ii) the mass rate of H₂ transport in kg/h.

---

##### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumption block (1 mark):*
1. Steady, isothermal, isobaric.
2. Ideal gas.
3. 1-D diffusion in z.
4. Constraint: $N_A = -N_B$ (equimolar).

*Derivation (6 marks):*

Step 1:
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A+N_B)$$

Step 2 — Apply $N_A+N_B=0$:
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Step 3 — Convert: $C_A = P_A/RT$:
$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT}\frac{dP_A}{dz}$$

Step 4 — Integrate:
$$N_A\cdot z = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT}(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$$

Step 5 — Final (1 mark):
$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{R\,T\,z}\,(P_{A1}-P_{A2})\;}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark) — EMD equation (no $P/P_{BM}$ factor):
$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RTz}(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$$

Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute driving force:
$$\Delta P_A = 0.05 - 0.02 = 0.03\ \text{atm} = 0.03 \times 1.01325 \times 10^{5} = 3039.75\ \text{Pa}$$

Step 3 (2 marks) — Substitute ($R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; $T = 293.15$ K; $z = 0.10$ m; $D_{AB} = 6.10 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s):

$$N_A = \frac{(6.10 \times 10^{-5})(3039.75)}{(8.314)(293.15)(0.10)}$$

Numerator: $(6.10 \times 10^{-5})(3039.75) = 0.18543$
Denominator: $(8.314)(293.15)(0.10) = 243.72$

$$N_A = \frac{0.18543}{243.72} = 7.609 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

(1 mark for the result)

Step 4 (1 mark) — Cross-sectional area of the tube:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$

Step 5 (1 mark) — Molar flow rate:
$$\dot{n}_A = N_A \cdot A = (7.609 \times 10^{-4})(4.909 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.735 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{mol/s}$$

Step 6 (1 mark) — Mass rate of H₂ ($M_A = 2.016$ g/mol $= 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol):
$$\dot{m}_A = \dot{n}_A \cdot M_A = (3.735 \times 10^{-7})(2.016 \times 10^{-3})(3600)$$

$$\dot{m}_A = 2.711 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}_A \approx 2.71 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/h}\;}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorise Before Exam**

- ☐ The **single most important diagnostic question** is: *Is component B stagnant ($N_B = 0$) or in equimolar counter-flow ($N_A = -N_B$)?*
- ☐ **Stagnant B (Stefan) — Log Form:** $N_A = (D_{AB}P/RTz)\ln[(P - P_{A2})/(P - P_{A1})]$
- ☐ **Stagnant B (Stefan) — Linear Form:** $N_A = (D_{AB}P/RTz P_{BM})(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$
- ☐ **EMD Form:** $N_A = (D_{AB}/RTz)(P_{A1}-P_{A2})$ — *no P-factor, no log-mean*
- ☐ **Log-mean formula:** $P_{BM} = (P_{B2} - P_{B1})/\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})$ — *always positive*
- ☐ **Stagnant-B bulk flow** *retards* the net flux — the flux is *less* than what pure Fick's-law-without-bulk-correction would predict.
- ☐ **EMD has no bulk-flow contribution** — the convective term vanishes identically.
- ☐ **Diffusivity units** are *always* **m²·s⁻¹**; flux units are *always* stated (kg-mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹).
- ☐ **Ideal gas substitution** is valid only for **gaseous** diffusion; for liquids, use **mole-fraction** form.
- ☐ **Typical industrial examples of Stefan diffusion:** humidifiers, dryers, absorption with insoluble carrier, sublimation.
- ☐ **Typical industrial examples of EMD:** binary distillation (at constant molar overflow), membrane separation, diffusion flames.
- ☐ **Assumption block (worth 1–2 marks always):** steady, isothermal, isobaric, 1-D, ideal gas, applicable constraint.
- ☐ **Sign convention:** flux is positive in the direction of decreasing concentration of A; reversal of points 1 and 2 changes the sign but not the magnitude.
- ☐ **Symmetry check:** If $P_{A1} = P_{A2}$, then $N_A = 0$ in both cases — sanity check for numerical problems.
- ☐ **Limiting-case check:** For dilute A ($P_A \ll P$), $P/P_{BM} \to 1$, and the stagnant-B equation *reduces* to the EMD form — physically intuitive: when A is very dilute, the bulk-flow correction becomes negligible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theories of Mass Transfer: Film Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Theories of Mass Transfer: Film Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Two-Film Theory** (also known as the **Double Film Theory** or **Whitman's Film Theory**) is the classical, foundational model of interphase mass transfer proposed by **W. K. Lewis and W. G. Whitman in 1923**. It describes the mechanism by which a solute (A) transfers between two immiscible fluid phases (typically a gas and a liquid) that are in direct contact.

According to this theory, whenever two fluid phases come into contact, a **stagnant (laminar) boundary layer** or "film" exists on each side of the interface. Mass transfer from the bulk of one phase to the bulk of the other is postulated to occur purely by **molecular diffusion** through these two films in series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Postulates of Film Theory (KTU Syllabus Highlight)**
> 1. **Interfacial Equilibrium:** The interface itself offers **zero resistance** to mass transfer, and the two phases are in thermodynamic equilibrium right at the interface.
> 2. **Stagnant Films:** A thin, stagnant layer exists in each phase adjacent to the interface, where flow is laminar and transport occurs only by molecular diffusion.
> 3. **Steady-State:** Mass transfer through both films is assumed to occur under **steady-state** conditions.
> 4. **Bulk Phase Homogeneity:** The bulk of each phase is perfectly mixed (well-turbulent), so all concentration gradients are confined strictly within the two films.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Doors" Intuition

Imagine a crowded classroom (**Gas Bulk**) trying to pass exam answer sheets to the school office (**Liquid Bulk**) through a connecting corridor. Between the classroom and the office, there are **two narrow doorways** in a row:

- The **first door** (Gas Film) slows down the students one by one — they have to shuffle through in a slow line.
- The **second door** (Liquid Film) does the same thing on the other side.

The narrow hallways around these doors act like **stagnant films** — no one runs in the hallway, they just walk slowly (molecular diffusion). The middle space between the two doors (the **Interface**) is just an open gap — no one gets stuck there. 

> [!NOTE]
> The **slower door (film)** dictates the **overall rate**. This is exactly how film theory works — the film with the **larger resistance** controls the overall mass transfer rate.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular diffusivity | $D_{AB}$ | **m²/s** |
| Mass transfer flux | $N_A$ | **kmol/(m²·s)** |
| Film thickness | $z$ or $\delta$ | **m (microns to mm)** |
| Individual film coefficient (gas) | $k_G$ | **kmol/(m²·s·Pa)** |
| Individual film coefficient (liquid) | $k_L$ | **m/s** |
| Partial pressure | $P$ | **Pa (or kPa)** |
| Concentration | $C$ | **kmol/m³** |
| Gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Absolute temperature | $T$ | **K** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **total film thickness** typically ranges from **10 to 100 microns** depending on turbulence. Higher turbulence → thinner film → higher mass transfer coefficient.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Film Mechanism

When a solute $A$ transfers from a gas phase to a liquid phase:

- **Step 1 — Convection in gas bulk:** Turbulent eddies in the gas bulk rapidly carry $A$ molecules up to the edge of the gas film at partial pressure $P_{AG}$.
- **Step 2 — Molecular diffusion through gas film:** Within the stagnant gas film (thickness $\delta_G$), $A$ moves by molecular diffusion from partial pressure $P_{AG}$ down to $P_{Ai}$ (interface partial pressure). This is the **rate-limiting step in the gas phase**.
- **Step 3 — Interface equilibrium:** At the interface, the two phases are in equilibrium. The gas-side partial pressure $P_{Ai}$ corresponds exactly to the liquid-side interfacial concentration $C_{Ai}$ via Henry's Law or equilibrium data.
- **Step 4 — Molecular diffusion through liquid film:** $A$ then diffuses through the stagnant liquid film (thickness $\delta_L$) from $C_{Ai}$ to $C_{AL}$ (bulk liquid concentration).
- **Step 5 — Convection in liquid bulk:** Turbulent mixing in the liquid bulk carries $A$ away to the bulk where concentration is $C_{AL}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because the system is at steady-state and $A$ is conserved, the **flux through the gas film equals the flux through the liquid film**:
> $$N_A = k_G(P_{AG} - P_{Ai}) = k_L(C_{Ai} - C_{AL})$$

### 2.2 Why Film Theory Matters in Engineering

- **Design of absorption columns** (e.g., CO₂ capture, ammonia absorption)
- **Design of stripping columns** (e.g., VOC removal from water)
- **Predicting extraction rates** in liquid-liquid extraction
- **Fundamental basis** for deriving the **Two-Film Theory** and **Overall Mass Transfer Coefficients** $K_G$ and $K_L$
- **Foundation for the Modern Theories** — penetration theory (Higbie 1935) and surface renewal theory (Danckwerts 1951) — which correct film theory's main flaw (unrealistic steady-state assumption).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Fick's First Law of Molecular Diffusion (through stagnant film) | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $N_A = k_G\,(P_{AG} - P_{Ai})$ | Gas film flux equation | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 3 | $N_A = k_L\,(C_{Ai} - C_{AL})$ | Liquid film flux equation | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 4 | $N_A = K_G\,(P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$ | Overall flux based on gas phase | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $N_A = K_L\,(C_A^{*} - C_{AL})$ | Overall flux based on liquid phase | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Overall resistance = sum of individual resistances (gas basis) | Resistance additivity |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G}$ | Overall resistance (liquid basis) | Resistance additivity |
| 8 | $k_G = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z_G\,P_{BM}}$ | Gas film coefficient (with stagnant $B$ correction) | kmol/(m²·s·Pa) |
| 9 | $k_L = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z_L}\cdot\dfrac{C_T}{C_{BM}}$ | Liquid film coefficient (with stagnant $B$ correction) | m/s |
| 10 | $P_{BM} = \dfrac{(P_{B2} - P_{B1})}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$ | Log-mean partial pressure of stagnant $B$ | Pa |
| 11 | $C_{BM} = \dfrac{(C_{B2} - C_{B1})}{\ln(C_{B2}/C_{B1})}$ | Log-mean concentration of stagnant $B$ | kmol/m³ |
| 12 | $m = \dfrac{dP_A^{*}}{dC_A}$ | Slope of equilibrium curve (Henry's constant in linear case) | Pa·m³/kmol |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When deriving the gas-film coefficient, $P_{BM}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of stagnant B** — this accounts for the fact that B moves in the **opposite direction** (counter-diffusion correction).

### 2.4 Limitations of Film Theory (Frequently Asked in KTU)

- Assumes a **well-defined, static film thickness** — but in practice, the film thickness is fluctuating and ill-defined.
- Predicts $k \propto D_{AB}$ (mass transfer coefficient proportional to diffusivity), which **contradicts experimental data** that shows $k \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$.
- Does not account for **unsteady-state** exposure of fluid elements to the interface.
- For these reasons, **Penetration Theory** and **Surface Renewal Theory** were developed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Flux Through a Stagnant Gas Film (Gas-Phase A Diffusing Through Stagnant B)

Consider solute $A$ diffusing through a stagnant gas film of thickness $z$ in the $z$-direction. $A$ moves from bulk gas (position 1, $z=0$) to the interface (position 2, $z=z_G$).

**Starting point — Fick's Law for total flux of A in the presence of stagnant B:**

$$N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{1-x_A}\cdot\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $x_A$ is the mole fraction of $A$ in the gas. The factor $\dfrac{1}{1-x_A}$ is the **stagnant-B correction**.

**Step 1 — Convert to partial pressures using the ideal gas law:** $C_A = \dfrac{P_A}{R\,T}$

$$N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{R\,T}\cdot\dfrac{P}{P - P_A}\cdot\dfrac{dP_A}{dz}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate across the film** (from $z=0$ at bulk gas to $z=z_G$ at interface):

$$N_A \int_{0}^{z_G} dz = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T} \int_{P_{A1}}^{P_{A2}} \dfrac{dP_A}{P - P_A}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left side:**

$$N_A \cdot z_G = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T} \int_{P_{A1}}^{P_{A2}} \dfrac{dP_A}{P - P_A}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the right side using $\int \dfrac{du}{a-u} = -\ln(a-u)$:**

$$N_A \cdot z_G = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $P - P_{A1} = P_{B1}$ and $P - P_{A2} = P_{B2}$ (total pressure minus partial pressure of A equals partial pressure of B):**

$$N_A \cdot z_G = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the log-mean partial pressure of B:** $P_{BM} = \dfrac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$

Rearranging: $\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1}) = \dfrac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{P_{BM}}$

$$N_A \cdot z_G = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\cdot\dfrac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{P_{BM}}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for flux:**

$$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z_G\,P_{BM}}\,(P_{B2} - P_{B1})$$

Wait — this is the form for **equimolar counter-diffusion** of A and B. For the **stagnant-B case** (typical absorption), we need a different form. Let us re-derive using the driving force $(P_{AG} - P_{Ai})$ directly.

**Alternative form — Driving force in terms of A's partial pressure:**

Substituting $P_{B2} - P_{B1} = (P - P_{A2}) - (P - P_{A1}) = P_{A1} - P_{A2} = P_{AG} - P_{Ai}$:

$$\boxed{N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z_G\,P_{BM}}\,(P_{AG} - P_{Ai})}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the gas film coefficient:**

$$k_G = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z_G\,P_{BM}}$$

So the flux equation becomes the familiar form:

$$\boxed{N_A = k_G\,(P_{AG} - P_{Ai})}$$

This is the **gas-film mass transfer coefficient** with the proper stagnant-B correction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Flux Through a Stagnant Liquid Film (Equimolar Counter-Diffusion Case)

For the liquid film, we apply the same approach but use concentrations. For dilute solutions where $A$ diffuses through stagnant $B$ in the liquid:

$$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C_T}{z_L\,C_{BM}}\,(C_{Ai} - C_{AL})$$

where $C_{BM}$ is the log-mean concentration of B in the liquid film:

$$C_{BM} = \dfrac{C_{B2} - C_{B1}}{\ln(C_{B2}/C_{B1})}$$

Identifying the liquid-film coefficient:

$$k_L = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z_L}\cdot\dfrac{C_T}{C_{BM}}$$

So the flux becomes:

$$\boxed{N_A = k_L\,(C_{Ai} - C_{AL})}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient & Resistance-in-Series (KTU Favourite Question)

**Step 1 — Write the individual flux equations:**

$$N_A = k_G\,(P_{AG} - P_{Ai}) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$N_A = k_L\,(C_{Ai} - C_{AL}) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2 — Relate interfacial concentrations using equilibrium:** For a linear equilibrium line, $P_A = m\,C_A + b$ (slope $= m$). So $P_{Ai} = m\,C_{Ai}$ and $P_A^{*} = m\,C_{AL}$ (bulk gas partial pressure in equilibrium with bulk liquid).

**Step 3 — Express $(C_{Ai} - C_{AL})$ in terms of gas-phase driving force:**

From (i): $C_{Ai} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_G} + \dfrac{P_{Ai}}{m}$ — but we want to eliminate $P_{Ai}$ in favor of $P_A^{*}$.

Actually, a cleaner route: from (ii), $C_{Ai} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_L} + C_{AL}$.

Substitute into the equilibrium relation $P_{Ai} = m\,C_{Ai}$:

$$P_{Ai} = m\left(\dfrac{N_A}{k_L} + C_{AL}\right) = \dfrac{m\,N_A}{k_L} + m\,C_{AL} = \dfrac{m\,N_A}{k_L} + P_A^{*}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into (i):**

$$N_A = k_G\left(P_{AG} - \dfrac{m\,N_A}{k_L} - P_A^{*}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect $N_A$ terms:**

$$N_A = k_G(P_{AG} - P_A^{*}) - \dfrac{m\,k_G\,N_A}{k_L}$$

$$N_A + \dfrac{m\,k_G\,N_A}{k_L} = k_G(P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$$

$$N_A\left(1 + \dfrac{m\,k_G}{k_L}\right) = k_G(P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$$

$$N_A \cdot \dfrac{k_L + m\,k_G}{k_L} = k_G(P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$$

$$N_A = \dfrac{k_G\,k_L}{k_L + m\,k_G}\,(P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$$

**Step 6 — Identify the overall gas-phase coefficient:**

$$\boxed{K_G = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}}}$$

Which gives the elegant **resistance-in-series** form:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}}$$

Similarly, for the liquid-phase basis:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Results

| Quantity | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Gas film flux | $N_A = k_G(P_{AG} - P_{Ai})$ | Rate through gas film |
| Liquid film flux | $N_A = k_L(C_{Ai} - C_{AL})$ | Rate through liquid film |
| Gas overall flux | $N_A = K_G(P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$ | Total rate, gas driving force |
| Liquid overall flux | $N_A = K_L(C_A^{*} - C_{AL})$ | Total rate, liquid driving force |
| Gas resistance | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Sum of two film resistances |
| Liquid resistance | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G}$ | Sum of two film resistances |
| Gas-film controlling | When $\dfrac{1}{k_G} \gg \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Solute is **highly soluble** (e.g., NH₃ in water) |
| Liquid-film controlling | When $\dfrac{1}{k_L} \gg \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G}$ | Solute is **poorly soluble** (e.g., O₂, CO₂ in water) |

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Film Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STEP A - Convective Transport in Gas Bulk - High Eddy Diffusivity - Carries A to gas film edge"] --> B["STEP B - Molecular Diffusion through Gas Film - Ficks Law with stagnant B correction - Driving Force is P_AG minus P_Ai"]
    B --> C["STEP C - Interfacial Equilibrium - Zero Resistance - P_Ai and C_Ai linked by equilibrium m"]
    C --> D["STEP D - Molecular Diffusion through Liquid Film - Ficks Law with stagnant B correction - Driving Force is C_Ai minus C_AL"]
    D --> E["STEP E - Convective Transport in Liquid Bulk - High Eddy Diffusivity - Carries A away from interface"]
```

### 4.3 Resistance-in-Series Analogy (Electrical Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Resistor R1 equals 1 divided by k_G - Gas Film Resistance"] --> NODE1["Junction Node"]
    NODE1 --> R2["Resistor R2 equals m divided by k_L - Liquid Film Resistance"]
    NODE1 --> OUT["Total Resistance R_total equals 1 divided by K_G equals R1 plus R2"]
    R1 -.- NOTE1["m is slope of equilibrium line - Dimensionless for Henry case"]
    R2 -.- NOTE2["Steepest resistance dominates - Film controlling step"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows show the **direction of solute A's transport**. The linear gradient in the films (sloped lines) represents the **steady-state concentration profile** predicted by Fick's Law.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**State the four fundamental postulates of the Two-Film Theory of mass transfer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. (1 mark) The interface between the two fluid phases offers **no resistance** to mass transfer.
2. (1 mark) A **stagnant film** exists on each side of the interface, and mass transfer through these films occurs by **molecular diffusion** only.
3. (1 mark) Mass transfer through both films occurs under **steady-state** conditions, and the bulk of each phase is perfectly mixed, so all concentration gradients are confined to the films.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between gas-film controlling and liquid-film controlling conditions in absorption. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- (1.5 marks) **Gas-film controlling** occurs when the **gas film offers the dominant resistance** to mass transfer. This happens when the solute is **highly soluble** in the liquid (small $m$ makes $m/k_L$ negligible), so $\dfrac{1}{K_G} \approx \dfrac{1}{k_G}$.
- (1.5 marks) **Liquid-film controlling** occurs when the **liquid film offers the dominant resistance**. This happens when the solute is **poorly soluble** (large $m$ makes $m/k_L$ dominant), so $\dfrac{1}{K_G} \approx \dfrac{m}{k_L}$.
- **Examples:** Absorption of **NH₃ in water** → gas-film controlled (NH₃ is highly soluble). Absorption of **CO₂ in water** → liquid-film controlled (CO₂ is poorly soluble).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** State the assumptions of the **Two-Film Theory** for interphase mass transfer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **steady-state flux of A diffusing through a stagnant gas film B**, starting from Fick's First Law. Clearly state the meaning of the **log-mean partial pressure $P_{BM}$** and show its introduction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Assumptions of Two-Film Theory (7 marks):**

The Two-Film Theory, proposed by **Lewis and Whitman (1923)**, is based on the following postulates:

1. **(1 mark)** At the interface between the two immiscible fluid phases, **thermodynamic equilibrium** exists. The interface itself offers **negligible resistance** to mass transfer.

2. **(1 mark)** On either side of the interface, there exists a thin, **stagnant boundary layer (film)** — one in the gas phase of thickness $z_G$ and one in the liquid phase of thickness $z_L$. Within these films, fluid motion is laminar.

3. **(1 mark)** **Mass transfer through both films occurs by molecular diffusion only** (no convection inside the films). The concentration gradient in each film is **linear** and **steady-state**.

4. **(1 mark)** The **bulk of each phase** is well-mixed by turbulent eddies, and is therefore of **uniform composition**. All resistance to mass transfer is confined to the two films.

5. **(1 mark)** The two films are arranged in **series**, and at steady state, the flux of solute $A$ through the gas film equals the flux through the liquid film.

6. **(1 mark)** The films are considered to be **planar and of constant thickness** during the contact time (an idealization).

7. **(1 mark)** Transport is **one-dimensional** and perpendicular to the interface.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Flux Through Stagnant Gas Film (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For diffusion of $A$ through stagnant $B$ in the $z$-direction, Fick's First Law is written with the stagnant-B correction:

$$N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{1-x_A}\cdot\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Using the ideal gas law $C_A = P_A/(RT)$ and $C_T = P/(RT)$ so that $1-x_A = (P - P_A)/P$:

$$N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,(P - P_A)}\cdot\dfrac{dP_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Separating variables and integrating from bulk gas ($z=0$, $P_A = P_{AG}$) to interface ($z = z_G$, $P_A = P_{Ai}$):

$$N_A \int_{0}^{z_G} dz = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\int_{P_{AG}}^{P_{Ai}}\dfrac{dP_A}{P - P_A}$$

$$N_A\,z_G = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - P_{Ai}}{P - P_{AG}}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Since $P - P_A = P_B$ (partial pressure of B), and noting that at the bulk $P_{BG} = P - P_{AG}$ and at the interface $P_{Bi} = P - P_{Ai}$:

$$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z_G}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{Bi}}{P_{BG}}\right)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Introduce the **log-mean partial pressure of stagnant B**:

$$P_{BM} = \dfrac{P_{BG} - P_{Bi}}{\ln(P_{BG}/P_{Bi})}$$

Equivalently: $\ln(P_{BG}/P_{Bi}) = (P_{BG} - P_{Bi})/P_{BM}$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substituting and noting $P_{BG} - P_{Bi} = (P - P_{AG}) - (P - P_{Ai}) = P_{Ai} - P_{AG}$, we get:

$$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z_G\,P_{BM}}(P_{AG} - P_{Ai})$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Identifying $k_G = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z_G\,P_{BM}}$:

$$\boxed{N_A = k_G\,(P_{AG} - P_{Ai})}$$

**Significance of $P_{BM}$:** $P_{BM}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of stagnant B** that accounts for the fact that B is **non-diffusing (stagnant)** but its concentration varies across the film. It is the **effective driving partial pressure** of A's stagnant partner B — and its inclusion makes the equation dimensionally and physically consistent.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Define **individual film coefficients** $k_G$ and $k_L$, and the **overall mass transfer coefficients** $K_G$ and $K_L$. Show that for a linear equilibrium line $P_A^{*} = m\,C_A$, the overall gas-phase resistance is $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Pure oxygen is absorbed from a gas mixture into water at **298 K and 1 atm** in a wetted-wall column. The gas contains **20 mol% O₂** and the bulk liquid water is essentially oxygen-free. The individual gas-film coefficient is $k_G = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/(m²·s·kPa) and the liquid-film coefficient is $k_L = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. The Henry's law constant for O₂ in water at 298 K is $m = 6.67 \times 10^{4}$ kPa·m³/kmol. Calculate:
- (i) The **overall gas-phase coefficient** $K_G$
- (ii) The **flux of O₂** at the top of the column where $P_{AG} = 20$ kPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Definitions and Resistance-in-Series Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **$k_G$** (Individual gas-film coefficient): relates the flux of $A$ to the **partial pressure driving force across the gas film only**: $N_A = k_G(P_{AG} - P_{Ai})$
- **$k_L$** (Individual liquid-film coefficient): relates the flux of $A$ to the **concentration driving force across the liquid film only**: $N_A = k_L(C_{Ai} - C_{AL})$
- **$K_G$** (Overall gas-phase coefficient): relates the flux to the **overall gas-phase driving force**: $N_A = K_G(P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$
- **$K_L$** (Overall liquid-phase coefficient): relates the flux to the **overall liquid-phase driving force**: $N_A = K_L(C_A^{*} - C_{AL})$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** From the two film equations:
$$P_{AG} - P_{Ai} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_G} \quad \text{and} \quad C_{Ai} - C_{AL} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_L}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Adding the two driving forces to construct the overall driving force. Since $P_{AG} - P_A^{*} = (P_{AG} - P_{Ai}) + (P_{Ai} - P_A^{*})$ and using $P_{Ai} = m\,C_{Ai}$, $P_A^{*} = m\,C_{AL}$:

$$P_{AG} - P_A^{*} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_G} + m\cdot\dfrac{N_A}{k_L} = N_A\left(\dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}\right)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** But by definition $N_A = K_G(P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$, so:

$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $k_G = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/(m²·s·kPa)
- $k_L = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
- $m = 6.67 \times 10^{4}$ kPa·m³/kmol
- $P_{AG} = 20$ kPa, $C_{AL} = 0$ (oxygen-free water)
- $P = 1$ atm = 101.325 kPa

**(i) Calculation of $K_G$ (3 marks):**

$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{k_G} = \dfrac{1}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000 \text{ m}^2\text{·s·kPa/kmol}$$

$$\dfrac{m}{k_L} = \dfrac{6.67 \times 10^{4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.668 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}^2\text{·s·kPa/kmol}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = 1000 + 2.668 \times 10^{9} \approx 2.668 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\boxed{K_G \approx 3.75 \times 10^{-10} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·kPa)}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Since $m/k_L \gg 1/k_G$, the **liquid film is overwhelmingly controlling**. This is expected because O₂ is **poorly soluble** in water.

**(ii) Calculation of flux $N_A$ (4 marks):**

Since $C_{AL} = 0$, then $P_A^{*} = m \cdot C_{AL} = 0$. So the **overall driving force** is $P_{AG} - P_A^{*} = 20 - 0 = 20$ kPa.

$$N_A = K_G \cdot (P_{AG} - P_A^{*})$$

$$N_A = (3.75 \times 10^{-10}) \times 20$$
