$$\boxed{N_A = 7.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Film Theory Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $P_{BM}$ correction ($-1$ to $-2$ marks):** Many students write the gas-film coefficient as $k_G = D_{AB} P / (R T z_G)$ — this is **wrong** for the **stagnant-B** case. You MUST include the log-mean $P_{BM}$ factor. Forgetting it loses 2 marks easily.
>
> 2. **Mixing up the resistance-in-series formulas ($-2$ marks):** Students often write $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{1}{k_L}$, which is dimensionally wrong. Remember the conversion factor $m$ (slope of equilibrium line) — it appears in the gas-basis form, and $1/m$ in the liquid-basis form.
>
> 3. **Units inconsistency ($-1$ mark):** When $m$ is in **kPa·m³/kmol** and $k_L$ is in **m/s**, the ratio $m/k_L$ yields units of **kPa·m·s/kmol**, which are correct units of $1/K_G$. Always show unit consistency in your final answer.
>
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of interfacial equilibrium ($-1$ mark):** Any derivation of overall mass transfer that omits the statement "interface offers zero resistance" loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Confusing $K_G$ with $K_L$ ($-1$ mark):** They are **not interchangeable**. Always clearly state which phase's driving force you are using.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Film theory** describes steady-state molecular diffusion through two stagnant films (gas + liquid) in series.
- The **interface is at equilibrium** and offers **no resistance**.
- **Fick's First Law** with the **stagnant-B correction** is the foundation: $N_A = -D_{AB} P/[R T (P - P_A)] \cdot dP_A/dz$.
- The **gas film coefficient** is $k_G = D_{AB} P / (R T z_G P_{BM})$.
- The **liquid film coefficient** is $k_L = (D_{AB}/z_L) \cdot C_T / C_{BM}$.
- **Resistance additivity** (the most-tested KTU result): $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ and $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G}$.
- **Gas-film controlled** when solute is **highly soluble** (small $m$); **liquid-film controlled** when solute is **poorly soluble** (large $m$).
- **Examples:** NH₃–H₂O is gas-film controlled; O₂–H₂O and CO₂–H₂O are liquid-film controlled.
- **Main limitation:** predicts $k \propto D_{AB}$, but experiments show $k \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$ — this is the **fatal flaw** that motivated penetration and surface renewal theories.
- **$P_{BM}$ and $C_{BM}$ are log-means** — always compute them with the natural log of the ratio, not the arithmetic mean.
- For a **linear equilibrium line**, $P_A = m C_A$, where $m$ is the **Henry's law constant** in kPa·m³/kmol.
- **Conversion check:** $1/K_G$ (gas basis) and $1/K_L$ (liquid basis) must yield the **same physical resistance** when multiplied by the appropriate conversion factor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Penetration Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Penetration Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higbie's Penetration Theory (1935):** Mass transfer from a fluid interface into a bulk fluid occurs primarily by *molecular diffusion into a stagnant fluid element* that is exposed to the interface for a **finite, brief contact time** ($t_e$), called the *exposure time* or *contact time*, after which the element is replaced by a fresh element from the bulk.

The penetration theory treats the liquid (or gas) at the interface as a series of small fluid *eddies* or *packets* that come into contact with the interface, stay there for a short, well-defined exposure time $t_e$, and then are replaced. During this brief contact, solute diffuses into each packet only by **transient (unsteady-state) molecular diffusion** governed by Fick's Second Law.

The resulting average liquid-side mass transfer coefficient is:

$$k_L = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi \, t_e}}$$

where $D_{AB}$ is the molecular diffusivity of solute A in B, and $t_e$ is the exposure (contact) time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Sponge Touch":** Imagine you lightly press a dry kitchen sponge against a wet countertop for **half a second**, then lift it and replace it with a fresh dry sponge. The first sponge absorbs only a *thin film* of water — it does not have time for water to soak all the way through. The total water picked up depends on (a) how fast water can creep in (diffusivity, $D_{AB}$), and (b) how long you held the sponge in contact (exposure time, $t_e$). Higbie's penetration theory says the *exact same physics* governs a fluid packet touching a gas–liquid interface: each packet gets only a *brief touch*, absorbing solute only as deep as unsteady-state diffusion can penetrate in that short time.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Molecular Diffusivity ($D_{AB}$):** typically $\mathbf{10^{-9} \text{ to } 10^{-10} \, m^2/s}$ for liquids, $\mathbf{10^{-5} \text{ to } 10^{-6} \, m^2/s}$ for gases.
- **Exposure Time ($t_e$):** typically $\mathbf{0.01 \text{ to } 1 \, s}$ in industrial contactors.
- **Penetration Depth ($\delta_p$):** $\delta_p \approx \sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t_e}$, of the order of $\mathbf{10^{-5} \text{ to } 10^{-4} \, m}$ — i.e., a *very thin* layer compared to bulk.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Higbie's Penetration Theory

1. **Eddy/Packet Model:** The liquid at the interface is visualized as an aggregate of small, well-mixed fluid packets (eddies).
2. **Equal Exposure Time:** Every packet remains at the interface for the *same* finite exposure time $t_e$.
3. **Fresh Packet on Arrival:** When a packet reaches the interface, its solute concentration is uniform and equal to the bulk liquid concentration $C_{A,\text{bulk}}$.
4. **Transient Molecular Diffusion:** During its stay, mass transfer into the packet occurs *only* by **unsteady-state molecular diffusion** along the $y$-axis (perpendicular to the interface). No convection within the packet.
5. **Constant Diffusivity:** $D_{AB}$ is independent of position and concentration.
6. **Interfacial Equilibrium:** The concentration at the interface ($y=0$) is held constant at $C_{A,i}$, the saturation value, throughout the exposure.

### 2.2 Why "Penetration" and Not "Film"?

The **Two-Film (Whitman, 1923)** theory assumes mass transfer is a *steady-state* process across a *stagnant film* of thickness $\delta$. The penetration theory *rejects* the steady-state film and replaces it with a *time-dependent* diffusion depth that grows with $\sqrt{t}$. This makes penetration theory much more realistic for short-contact equipment such as:

- Bubble columns
- Packed absorption columns
- Spray towers
- Liquid-liquid extraction mixers

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $k_L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi \, t_e}}$ | Average liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (Higbie) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | $\delta_p \approx \sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t_e}$ | Penetration depth of solute front | $\text{m}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$ | Fick's Second Law (1-D transient diffusion) | — |
| 4 | $\dfrac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}} = \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}\right)$ | Concentration profile (error-function solution) | — |
| 5 | $\bar{k}_L = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{t_e}}$ | Alternate form of Higbie's $k_L$ (same as #1) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 6 | $N_A = k_L(C_{A,i} - C_{A,\text{bulk}})$ | Instantaneous flux per packet | $\text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$ |
| 7 | $t_e = \dfrac{d_p}{u_s}$ (for falling drop) | Exposure time of a droplet of diameter $d_p$ at slip velocity $u_s$ | $\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $t_e = \dfrac{L}{u_L}$ (for packed column) | Exposure time of liquid packet over packing height $L$ | $\text{s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** Notice that **#1** and **#5** are *identical* — Higbie's coefficient is universally remembered as $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Gas Absorbers:** Predicts $k_L$ in packed columns, bubble columns, and tray columns where contact times are short.
- **Oxygen Transfer in Aeration Tanks:** Used in environmental engineering to size aerators in activated-sludge wastewater treatment.
- **Liquid–Liquid Extraction:** Predicts mass transfer coefficients in mixer-settlers, where droplets continually form and coalesce.
- **Drug Dissolution / Pulmonary Delivery:** Models how molecules penetrate thin liquid films lining the alveoli.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Higbie Penetration Equation

#### Step 1 — Set up the geometry and coordinate system

Consider a fluid packet that has just arrived at the gas–liquid interface at time $t = 0$. The interface lies in the $x$–$z$ plane, and $y$ is the distance measured *into* the liquid, normal to the interface.

- At $t=0$: the packet is uniform with concentration $C_A(y, 0) = C_{A,\infty}$ (bulk).
- At $y = 0$ (interface): for $t > 0$, $C_A(0, t) = C_{A,i}$ (interfacial, held constant).
- As $y \to \infty$: $C_A(\infty, t) = C_{A,\infty}$ (bulk unchanged, far from the surface).

#### Step 2 — Governing PDE (Fick's Second Law, 1-D transient)

Because transport occurs only along $y$ by molecular diffusion (no convection inside the packet), the governing equation is:

$$
\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}
$$

#### Step 3 — Initial and Boundary Conditions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IC: } \quad & C_A(y, 0) = C_{A,\infty} \quad \text{for } y \geq 0 \\
\text{BC1: } \quad & C_A(0, t) = C_{A,i} \quad \text{for } t > 0 \\
\text{BC2: } \quad & C_A(\infty, t) = C_{A,\infty} \quad \text{for } t > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Introduce dimensionless variables

Define:
$$
\theta = \dfrac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}}, \quad \eta = \dfrac{y}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}
$$

This is the **standard Boltzmann transformation** for semi-infinite diffusion problems.

#### Step 5 — Convert the PDE into an ODE

Compute the derivatives using the chain rule:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\eta}{2t}\dfrac{d\theta}{d\eta}, \qquad \dfrac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial y^2} = \dfrac{1}{4 D_{AB} t}\dfrac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}
$$

Substitute into Fick's second law:

$$
-\dfrac{\eta}{2t}\dfrac{d\theta}{d\eta} = D_{AB}\cdot\dfrac{1}{4 D_{AB} t}\dfrac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}
$$

Simplify (the $t$ and $D_{AB}$ cancel out completely):

$$
\dfrac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta\dfrac{d\theta}{d\eta} = 0
$$

#### Step 6 — Integrate the ODE once

Let $p = d\theta/d\eta$. Then $dp/d\eta + 2\eta p = 0$, which gives:

$$
\dfrac{dp}{p} = -2\eta\, d\eta \;\Longrightarrow\; \ln p = -\eta^2 + \text{const}
$$

$$
\dfrac{d\theta}{d\eta} = A_1 e^{-\eta^2}
$$

#### Step 7 — Integrate again to obtain the solution

$$
\theta(\eta) = A_1 \int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2} du + A_2
$$

Apply boundary conditions in $\eta$ form:
- At $\eta \to \infty$ (i.e., $y \to \infty$ or $t \to 0$): $\theta = 0$, so $A_2 = 0$.
- At $\eta = 0$ (interface): $\theta = 1$, which forces the standard definition of the error function:

$$
\text{erf}(\eta) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2}\,du
$$

Matching constants gives $A_1 = 2/\sqrt{\pi}$, so:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}} = \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}\right)\;}
$$

#### Step 8 — Instantaneous mass-transfer flux at the interface

Fick's First Law at $y = 0$:

$$
N_A\Big\vert_{y=0} = -D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\Big\vert_{y=0}
$$

Differentiate the solution with respect to $y$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = (C_{A,i}-C_{A,\infty})\cdot\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t}}\cdot e^{-y^2/(4 D_{AB} t)}
$$

At $y = 0$: $e^0 = 1$, hence:

$$
N_A(t) = (C_{A,i}-C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}
$$

#### Step 9 — Time-average over the exposure time $t_e$

Average flux over $[0, t_e]$:

$$
\bar{N}_A = \dfrac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e} (C_{A,i}-C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}\,dt
$$

$$
\bar{N}_A = (C_{A,i}-C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}\cdot \dfrac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e} t^{-1/2} dt
$$

The integral evaluates to $2\sqrt{t_e}$, so:

$$
\bar{N}_A = 2(C_{A,i}-C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}
$$

#### Step 10 — Identify the mass-transfer coefficient

By definition, $\bar{N}_A = k_L(C_{A,i}-C_{A,\infty})$, so:

$$
\boxed{\;k_L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi\, t_e}}\;}
$$

This is the celebrated **Higbie Penetration Equation**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Result

| Quantity | Final Result | Physical Meaning |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| Concentration profile | $\theta = \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}\right)$ | Solute penetrates a thin diffusion front |
| Penetration depth | $\delta_p = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}t_e}$ | ~tens of microns |
| Instantaneous flux | $N_A(t) = (C_{A,i}-C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$ | Decreases as $1/\sqrt{t}$ |
| Average flux | $\bar{N}_A = 2(C_{A,i}-C_{A,\infty})\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}$ | Used in design |
| $k_L$ (Higbie) | $k_L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}$ | Increases with shorter contact & higher $D_{AB}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Penetration Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bulk Liquid Reservoir"] --> B["Fluid Packet Arrives at Interface"]
    B --> C["Packet Held at Interface for Exposure Time t_e"]
    C --> D["Transient Molecular Diffusion Along y-axis"]
    D --> E["Concentration Front Propagates as sqrt(t)"]
    E --> F["Packet Dislodged and Mixed Back into Bulk"]
    F --> G["Fresh Packet Replaces It"]
    G --> C
    D --> H["Higbie's k_L = 2 sqrt(D_AB / pi t_e)"]
    H --> I["Overall Mass Transfer Flux N_A = k_L * C_Ai - C_Abulk"]
    F --> J["Bulk Concentration Slightly Enriched"]
    J --> A
```

### 4.2 Concentration Penetration Profile Inside a Single Packet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["y = 0  C_A = C_Ai (interfacial)"] --> A1["y = delta_p/2  C_A drops to ~50%"]
    A1 --> A2["y = delta_p  C_A approaches C_A infinity"]
    A2 --> A3["y >> delta_p  C_A = C_A infinity (bulk)"]
```

### 4.3 Comparison: Film vs. Penetration Theory (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FilmTheory["Whitman Two-Film Theory 1923"]
        F1["Steady State Diffusion"] --> F2["k_L = D_AB / delta"]
        F2 --> F3["Constant delta for all contact times"]
    end
    subgraph PenetrationTheory["Higbie Penetration Theory 1935"]
        P1["Unsteady State Diffusion"] --> P2["k_L = 2 sqrt D_AB divided by pi t_e"]
        P2 --> P3["Smaller t_e means larger k_L"]
    end
    FilmTheory -. Contrasts with .-> PenetrationTheory
```

### 4.4 Process Flow Inside an Industrial Packed Absorption Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GasIn["Gas Stream Containing Solute A"] --> Mixing["Gas Liquid Contact on Packing"]
    LiquidIn["Lean Liquid Solvent"] --> Mixing
    Mixing --> Packet1["Liquid Packet Forms Film of thickness delta_p"]
    Packet1 --> Diffuse["Unsteady Diffusion for exposure time t_e"]
    Diffuse --> kL["Higbie Equation k_L = 2 sqrt D_AB / pi t_e"]
    kL --> Flux["N_A = k_L C_Ai - C_Abulk"]
    Flux --> GasOut["Purified Gas Exits Top"]
    Flux --> LiquidOut["Rich Solvent Exits Bottom"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State Higbie's penetration theory for mass transfer. Write the final expression for the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Higbie's penetration theory (1935) assumes that the liquid at a gas–liquid interface consists of small fluid eddies/packets, each of which is exposed to the gas phase for a finite, brief contact (exposure) time $t_e$. During this contact, mass transfer occurs by *unsteady-state molecular diffusion* into the packet. After the exposure time, the packet is replaced by a fresh one from the bulk. The resulting liquid-side mass transfer coefficient is:
> $$k_L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_e}}$$

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any **three** assumptions of Higbie's penetration theory.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. The bulk liquid is composed of small, well-mixed fluid packets (eddies) that each contact the interface for a fixed exposure time $t_e$. (1 mark)
> 2. Mass transfer into each packet occurs solely by *transient molecular diffusion* governed by Fick's Second Law; no convection within the packet. (1 mark)
> 3. The interface concentration is held constant at the saturation value $C_{A,i}$ throughout the exposure, and the bulk concentration $C_{A,\infty}$ is uniform far from the interface. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Starting from Fick's Second Law, derive the expression for the average liquid-side mass transfer coefficient according to Higbie's penetration theory. State clearly all boundary conditions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A bubble of $\text{CO}_2$ rises through water at $25 \, ^\circ\text{C}$. The bubble has a diameter of $2 \, \text{mm}$ and rises with a slip velocity of $0.25 \, \text{m/s}$. The molecular diffusivity of $\text{CO}_2$ in water is $1.9 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the **liquid-side mass transfer coefficient** $k_L$ and the **penetration depth** $\delta_p$ using Higbie's theory. Take $\pi \approx 3.1416$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
*(Follow the 10-step derivation given in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

| Step | Marks |
|------|-------|
| Governing PDE and Boltzmann transformation setup | 1 |
| Reduction to ODE in $\eta$ | 1 |
| Two integrations and application of boundary conditions | 2 |
| Final error-function solution | 1 |
| Flux from Fick's first law and time-averaging | 1 |
| Final $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)}$ with units check | 1 |

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Exposure time of a fluid packet* (the time a bubble's surface stays in contact with a given liquid element, approximated by the bubble's residence time):

$$
t_e = \dfrac{d_p}{u_s} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}}{0.25\,\text{m/s}} = 8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 0.008\,\text{s}
$$

*Step 2 — Higbie's $k_L$*:

$$
k_L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi\, t_e}} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{1.9 \times 10^{-9}}{3.1416 \times 0.008}}
$$

$$
k_L = 2\sqrt{7.5615 \times 10^{-8}} = 2 \times 2.7498 \times 10^{-4} = 5.50 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m/s}
$$

*Step 3 — Penetration depth*:

$$
\delta_p = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}\, t_e} = 2\sqrt{1.9 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.008} = 2\sqrt{1.52 \times 10^{-11}} = 7.8 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} \approx 7.8 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\boxed{k_L \approx 5.50 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m/s}}$
- $\boxed{\delta_p \approx 7.8 \,\mu\text{m}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is to use $t_e = d_p/u_s$ **without converting $d_p$ from mm to m**. Doing so gives an erroneous $k_L$ that is roughly $10^3$ times too large. **Always** write units in every numerical substitution step. Also, do *not* confuse penetration depth with film thickness — the two are physically different concepts.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** State the postulates of Higbie's penetration theory and explain how it differs from the **two-film theory** of Whitman. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a packed absorption column, the liquid is distributed over the packing such that the exposure time of a fluid packet is $t_e = 0.5 \, \text{s}$. The molecular diffusivity of ammonia (NH$_3$) in water at $298 \, \text{K}$ is $1.76 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Using Higbie's equation, calculate: (i) the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $k_L$, and (ii) the time required for the diffusion front to reach a depth of $50 \,\mu\text{m}$ inside the packet. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theoretical Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923) | Penetration Theory (Higbie, 1935) |
|--------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Basic unit | Stagnant film of thickness $\delta$ | Fluid eddies/packets of finite exposure $t_e$ |
| Nature of diffusion | Steady-state | Unsteady-state (transient) |
| Governing equation | $N_A = -D_{AB}\,dC_A/dy$ (linear) | $\partial C_A/\partial t = D_{AB}\,\partial^2 C_A/\partial y^2$ |
| Concentration profile | Linear in $y$ | Error-function of $y/\sqrt{t}$ |
| $k_L$ form | $k_L = D_{AB}/\delta$ | $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)}$ |
| Time dependence | None | Decreases as $1/\sqrt{t}$ |
| Applicability | Long contact times | Short contact times (bubble, drop, packing) |

*(Award 1 mark per correct comparison row × up to 5 rows = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for clearly stating the postulates of penetration theory.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Liquid-side mass transfer coefficient:**

$$
k_L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi\, t_e}} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{1.76 \times 10^{-9}}{3.1416 \times 0.5}}
$$

$$
k_L = 2\sqrt{1.1204 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \times 3.347 \times 10^{-5} = 6.69 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) Time to reach penetration depth $y = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$:**

For a semi-infinite medium, the diffusion front depth at time $t$ is:

$$
y \approx 2\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t = \dfrac{y^2}{4 D_{AB}}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4 \times 1.76 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{7.04 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.355\,\text{s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\boxed{k_L \approx 6.69 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m/s}}$
- $\boxed{t \approx 0.355\,\text{s}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (Part b):**
> Many students mistakenly equate $k_L$ of the *penetration theory* with the *film theory* formula $D_{AB}/\delta$ and end up calculating a fictitious "film thickness". Remember: **penetration theory has no $\delta$**. Its fundamental parameter is the *time* $t_e$, not a length. Also, when finding the time to reach a depth, the question is **not** asking for a $k_L$ — solve it directly from $y = 2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}$ (or $4\sqrt{D_{AB} t}$ depending on definition used; KTU textbooks conventionally use $2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}$). Always show the formula before substituting.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Penetration Theory (Module 1)**

- **Year / Author:** Higbie, **1935**.
- **Core idea:** Liquid = collection of fluid *packets*; each packet exposed to interface for time $t_e$; unsteady-state molecular diffusion only.
- **Governing equation:** $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$.
- **Boltzmann variable:** $\eta = \dfrac{y}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}$ — transforms PDE → ODE.
- **Concentration profile:** $\theta = \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}t}}\right)$.
- **Master formula:** $\boxed{k_L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_e}}}$ — the *most* important equation.
- **Penetration depth:** $\delta_p \approx 2\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t_e}$ — typically $1$–$100 \,\mu\text{m}$.
- **Exposure-time recipes:**
  - Falling/bubble: $t_e = d_p/u_s$.
  - Packed column: $t_e = L/u_L$ (or empirical correlation).
- **Trend:** $k_L \uparrow$ when $D_{AB} \uparrow$ *or* when $t_e \downarrow$ (shorter contact → *faster* mass transfer).
- **Key difference vs. Film Theory:** Penetration theory predicts $k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$, while film theory predicts $k_L \propto D_{AB}$ — i.e., penetration theory gives a *weaker* dependence on diffusivity.
- **KTU favourite numerical:** $\text{CO}_2$/water bubble, NH$_3$/water absorption, O$_2$/water aeration.
- **Common units trap:** Convert $d_p$ from mm to m **before** computing $t_e = d_p/u_s$.
- **Limitations:** Assumes all packets have the *same* exposure time; ignores any surface renewal rate distribution — these are addressed by Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory (a frequent follow-up topic in KTU Module 1/2 questions).
- **What to write first in an exam:** State the **assumptions** (1 mark), write the **governing equation + BCs** (2 marks), then proceed to the derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Renewal Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Surface Renewal Theory ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts Model, 1951):** A mass transfer model for gas-liquid interfaces that assumes the liquid surface consists of numerous small fluid elements which are continuously being replaced by fresh liquid elements from the bulk at a random, statistically distributed rate. Each fresh element resides at the surface for a finite (but random) time during which unsteady-state molecular diffusion occurs, identical to Higbie's Penetration Theory, before being swept back into the bulk and replaced. The theory is formally expressed through the **age distribution function**: $$\phi(t) = s \cdot e^{-s \cdot t}$$ where: - $\phi(t)$ = fraction of surface elements having an exposure age between $t$ and $t+dt$ - $s$ = fractional rate of surface renewal (units: **s⁻¹**) - $t$ = age of the surface element (units: **s**) The model fundamentally treats the turbulent liquid surface as a stochastic population of "eddies" of varying ages, each undergoing **unsteady-state diffusion** into itself for the duration it remains at the interface. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Pop-Up Elevator of a Factory Conveyor Belt" Analogy** > Imagine a busy **post office sorting counter** during peak hours: > - A **clerk** stands at the counter (this is the gas-liquid interface). > - **Letters (solute molecules)** keep arriving from the air into the clerk's hands. > - Every few seconds, a **senior supervisor** (turbulent eddy) taps a clerk on the shoulder and says: *"Your turn at the back of the queue, fresh clerk – come forward!"* > - Each clerk is **exposed to the air for a random amount of time** before being replaced. > - A clerk who has been at the counter for a long time has already absorbed many letters, so the rate of absorption slows. A *fresh* clerk absorbs letters quickly. > - The **average time** a clerk spends at the counter is analogous to the **mean surface age** ($1/s$). In this analogy: - **Clerks** = liquid surface elements (eddies) - **Letters** = diffusing solute (e.g., $O_2$, $CO_2$, $SO_2$) - **Tapping rate** = surface renewal rate $s$ - **Time at counter** = surface age $t$ ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Diffusivity of gas in liquid ($D_{AB}$):** typically $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$ **m²/s** (e.g., $O_2$ in water ≈ $2.1 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s) > - **Surface renewal rate ($s$):** typically 1 to 100 **s⁻¹** in agitated contactors > - **Mean surface age ($1/s$):** typically 0.01 to 1 **s** > - **Mass transfer coefficient $k_L$:** typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ **m/s** ### 1.4 Historical Context Within KTU Syllabus The two-film theory (Whitman, 1923) assumed a **steady-state** stagnant film. **Higbie's Penetration Theory (1935)** corrected this by saying surface elements are exposed for a fixed time $t_c$. **Danckwerts (1951)** refined this further by allowing the contact time to be a **random variable** following an exponential distribution — this is the Surface Renewal Theory. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of Surface Renewal Theory The theory rests on **four** foundational assumptions: 1. **Random Replacement Assumption:** Liquid surface elements are replaced by fresh elements from the bulk in a completely random, statistically independent manner. 2. **Exponential Age Distribution:** The probability that a surface element of age between $t$ and $t + dt$ exists on the surface follows the relation $\phi(t) = s \cdot e^{-s \cdot t}$. 3. **Unsteady-State Mass Transfer per Element:** Each surface element absorbs solute via the same unsteady-state diffusion mechanism as in Higbie's Penetration Theory, treating it as a semi-infinite slab. 4. **Fresh Element Condition:** Each newly arrived surface element has **zero** dissolved solute concentration (i.e., $c_A = c_{A0}$ at $t = 0$ when the element is first exposed). ### 2.2 Derivation of the Age Distribution Function The **Why:** We need a probability density function that tells us *what fraction of the surface* has been exposed for exactly time $t$. The **How:** - Let $f(t)$ = probability that a surface element survives (is not yet replaced) until age $t$. - The rate of disappearance of elements is proportional to their current number: $$\frac{df}{dt} = -s \cdot f(t)$$ - Integrating with initial condition $f(0) = 1$: $$\int_{0}^{t} \frac{df}{f} = -s \int_{0}^{t} dt \implies f(t) = e^{-s t}$$ - The fraction of elements having age between $t$ and $t + dt$ is the fraction that survived to age $t$ multiplied by the probability of being replaced in the next instant: $$\phi(t)\, dt = f(t) \cdot s \cdot dt = s \cdot e^{-s t}\, dt$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\phi(t) = s \cdot e^{-s t}$ | Age distribution function (probability density) | s⁻¹ | | 2 | $\int_{0}^{\infty} \phi(t)\, dt = 1$ | Normalization (all surface elements accounted for) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{1}{s}$ | Mean (average) surface age | s | | 4 | $\bar{t^2} = \dfrac{2}{s^2}$ | Mean square surface age | s² | | 5 | $k_L = \sqrt{D \cdot s}$ | **Liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (Surface Renewal)** | m/s | | 6 | $N_A = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \sqrt{D \cdot s}$ | Average molar flux into the liquid | kmol/(m²·s) | | 7 | $k_L = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi \cdot t_c}}$ | Liquid-side $k_L$ from Higbie's Penetration Theory | m/s | | 8 | $k_G = \sqrt{\dfrac{D_G \cdot s}{H}}$ | Coupled $k_G$ (gas side) when film control dominates | m/s | | 9 | $H = \dfrac{c_{Ai}}{p_{Ai}}$ | Henry's Law constant (interface relation) | kmol/(m³·Pa) | | 10 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{H}{k_G}$ | Two-resistance model | s/m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this critical conversion link:** When $t_c$ (Higbie's exposure time) is replaced by $1/s$ (mean surface age of Danckwerts), the Penetration model **transforms directly** into the Surface Renewal model. > i.e., $\sqrt{\dfrac{4D}{\pi t_c}} \;\longrightarrow\; \sqrt{D s}$ (with $t_c = 4/(\pi s)$). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Design of gas absorbers** (e.g., $CO_2$ capture in amine columns, $SO_2$ scrubbing in flue-gas desulfurization) > - **Packed column internals** — predicting $k_L a$ for random packings (Raschig rings, Pall rings) and structured packings (Mellapak, Sulzer) > - **Wastewater treatment** — oxygen transfer rate (OTR) in activated sludge aeration tanks and bubble-diffuser systems > - **Fermentation bioreactors** — $O_2$ supply to aerobic cultures (penicillin, yeast, monoclonal antibodies) > - **Distillation trays** — predicting Murphree point efficiency on sieve and valve trays > - **Direct-contact heat/mass exchangers** — e.g., cooling towers, desalination ### 2.5 Conceptual Distinction from Film & Penetration Theories | Feature | Two-Film (Whitman) | Penetration (Higbie) | Surface Renewal (Danckwerts) | |---------|--------------------|----------------------|------------------------------| | Regime | Steady-state | Unsteady-state | Unsteady-state | | Exposure time | Infinite (stagnant) | Fixed ($t_c$) | Random (exponential) | | Distribution | None | Dirac delta at $t_c$ | Continuous $\phi(t) = s e^{-st}$ | | $k_L$ expression | $D / \delta$ | $2\sqrt{D/(\pi t_c)}$ | $\sqrt{D s}$ | | Mass transfer rate | Lowest | Intermediate | Highest (more realistic for turbulence) | | Physical basis | Molecular diffusion through film | Eddies exposed for fixed time | Eddies replaced at random times | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Average Mass Transfer Coefficient We now derive $k_L = \sqrt{D \cdot s}$ from first principles, with every algebraic step made explicit. **Step 1 — Instantaneous local flux for an element of age $t$** For a single surface element of age $t$ exposed to the interface, the local molar flux from the gas into the liquid is given by the classic unsteady-state diffusion solution (Higbie's penetration result): $$N_A(t) = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi \cdot t}}$$ **Step 2 — Total flux across the entire surface** Since the surface is populated by elements of all ages, the *overall* average flux is the age-weighted average of $N_A(t)$: $$\overline{N_A} = \int_{0}^{\infty} N_A(t) \cdot \phi(t)\, dt$$ Substituting the expressions for $N_A(t)$ and $\phi(t)$: $$\overline{N_A} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[(c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi t}}\right] \cdot \left[s \cdot e^{-s t}\right] dt$$ **Step 3 — Pulling out the constants** The concentrations, $D$, $s$, and $\pi$ are all independent of the integration variable $t$. Therefore: $$\overline{N_A} = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi}} \cdot s \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{e^{-s t}}{\sqrt{t}}\, dt$$ **Step 4 — Evaluating the standard integral** We use the gamma-function identity: $$\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{n-1} \cdot e^{-s t}\, dt = \dfrac{\Gamma(n)}{s^{n}}$$ For our integral, $n - 1 = -\tfrac{1}{2}$, so $n = \tfrac{1}{2}$: $$\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{-1/2} \cdot e^{-s t}\, dt = \dfrac{\Gamma(1/2)}{s^{1/2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{\sqrt{s}}$$ (Since $\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi}$.) **Step 5 — Substituting back** $$\overline{N_A} = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi}} \cdot s \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{\sqrt{s}}$$ **Step 6 — Simplification** The $\sqrt{\pi}$ in the numerator and denominator cancel. The $s$ and $\sqrt{s}$ combine to give $\sqrt{s}$: $$\overline{N_A} = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{s}} = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \cdot \sqrt{D \cdot s}$$ **Step 7 — Defining the mass transfer coefficient** By definition, $\overline{N_A} = k_L (c_{Ai} - c_{A0})$, therefore: $$\boxed{k_L = \sqrt{D \cdot s}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" behind this elegant result:** Because younger elements (small $t$) contribute disproportionately large fluxes (since $N_A \propto 1/\sqrt{t}$), and the exponential distribution $\phi(t)$ heavily weights these young elements, the **average flux is finite** even though $N_A(t) \to \infty$ as $t \to 0$. This self-regularizing property is a beautiful feature of Danckwerts' model. ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** $CO_2$ is absorbed from a gas mixture into water in a packed column. The diffusivity of $CO_2$ in water at the operating temperature is $D = 1.8 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. The surface renewal rate has been estimated to be $s = 0.04$ s⁻¹. Calculate: 1. The liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $k_L$ using Surface Renewal Theory. 2. The mean surface age $\bar{t}$. 3. The average molar flux of $CO_2$ into water if $c_{Ai} = 0.034$ kmol/m³ and $c_{A0} = 0.000$ kmol/m³. **Solution:** **Part (1): Calculation of $k_L$** $$k_L = \sqrt{D \cdot s} = \sqrt{(1.8 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.04)}$$ $$k_L = \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{-11}} = 8.485 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{k_L \approx 8.49 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}}$$ **Part (2): Mean surface age** $$\bar{t} = \dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{1}{0.04} = 25 \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{t} = 25 \text{ s}}$$ **Part (3): Average molar flux** $$\overline{N_A} = k_L (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) = (8.485 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (0.034 - 0)$$ $$\overline{N_A} = 2.885 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$ $$\boxed{\overline{N_A} \approx 2.89 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}}$$ ### 3.3 Comparison Analytical Matrix: Penetration vs. Surface Renewal Let us link the two theories through a fictitious "characteristic contact time" $t_c$. From $k_L = \sqrt{D s}$ and $k_L = 2\sqrt{D/(\pi t_c)}$, equating them: $$\sqrt{D s} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi t_c}}$$ Squaring both sides: $$D s = \dfrac{4 D}{\pi t_c} \implies s = \dfrac{4}{\pi t_c}$$ Therefore: $$t_c^{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{4}{\pi s} \approx \dfrac{1.273}{s}$$ This means that the **effective exposure time** in Surface Renewal Theory is approximately **1.273 times the mean surface age** ($1/s$). This is because the heavy weighting of young, highly-active elements increases the *effective* $k_L$ compared to what a fixed $t_c = 1/s$ in Higbie's model would predict. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Surface Renewal Mass Transfer Model ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Bulk Liquid Reservoir
cA = cA0] --> B[Turbulent Upwelling Eddy
Transport to surface] B --> C[Surface Element Creation
Age t = 0
cA = cA0] C --> D[Unsteady-State Diffusion
into element of age t
Flux NA t proportional to 1 over sqrt t] D --> E{Surface Renewal Check
Exponential Statistics} E -- Probability s dt --> F[Element Replaced
Mixed into bulk
cA increased] E -- Probability 1 minus s dt --> G[Element Continues Aging
t to t plus dt] G --> D F --> A C --> H[Statistical Age Pool
phi t = s exp minus s t] H --> I[Weighted Average Flux
NA bar = integral of NA t phi t dt] I --> J[Liquid Side kL
kL = sqrt D s] J --> K[Two Resistance Model
1 over KL = 1 over kL plus H over kG] K --> L[Design Output
N bar A and Tower Height] ``` ### 4.2 Conceptual Flow of Mass Transfer Across the Interface ```mermaid graph LR G1[Gas Bulk
pA bulk] --> G2[Gas Film
resistance 1 over kG] G2 --> G3[Interface
cAi H pAi] G3 --> L1[Liquid Elements of Varying Age
phi t = s exp minus s t] L1 --> L2[Liquid Bulk
cA0] L3[Turbulent Eddy Replacement
rate s] -.-> L1 L4[Unsteady Diffusion
age t] -.-> L1 ``` ### 4.3 Age Distribution Visualization (Schematic) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "Surface Age Distribution phi t = s e to the power minus s t" X0[Age 0
phi t maximum = s] --> X1[Small t
high flux NA t large] X1 --> X2[Mean age 1 over s
phi t = s over e approx 0.368 s] X2 --> X3[Large t
low flux NA t small] X3 --> X4[t to infinity
phi t approaches 0] end ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Architecture: Three Mass Transfer Theories ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "Two Film Theory Whitman 1923" A1[Steady State Stagnant Film] --> A2[kL = D over delta] end subgraph "Penetration Theory Higbie 1935" B1[Eddy Exposure Time t c fixed] --> B2[kL = 2 sqrt D over pi t c] end subgraph "Surface Renewal Theory Danckwerts 1951" C1[Random Exposure
phi t = s e to the power minus s t] --> C2[kL = sqrt D s] end A2 --> D[Evolution of Mass Transfer Models] B2 --> D C2 --> D D --> E[Most Realistic for Turbulent Interfaces] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q:** State Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory of mass transfer. Write the expression for the age distribution function and explain the significance of the surface renewal rate $s$. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory (1951) states that the liquid surface consists of small fluid elements which are continuously replaced by fresh elements from the bulk at a random, statistically distributed rate. Each fresh element undergoes unsteady-state mass transfer during its residence at the surface. > **Age Distribution Function (1 Mark):** The fraction of surface elements having an age between $t$ and $t+dt$ is: > $$\phi(t) = s \cdot e^{-s t}$$ > **Significance of $s$ (0.5 Mark):** The parameter $s$ (s⁻¹) is the **fractional rate of surface renewal**; its reciprocal $1/s$ is the mean surface age. Higher $s$ implies more vigorous turbulence and a higher mass transfer coefficient $k_L = \sqrt{Ds}$. --- #### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q:** Compare Surface Renewal Theory with Higbie's Penetration Theory with respect to the distribution of contact times and the resulting expression for $k_L$. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Penetration (Higbie) | Surface Renewal (Danckwerts) | |--------|----------------------|------------------------------| | Contact time distribution (1.5 Marks) | Fixed, single value $t_c$ for all elements (Dirac delta) | Random, exponentially distributed: $\phi(t) = s e^{-st}$ | | $k_L$ expression (1 Mark) | $k_L = 2\sqrt{D/(\pi t_c)}$ | $k_L = \sqrt{D \cdot s}$ | | Limitation (0.5 Mark) | Assumes uniform exposure | More realistic for turbulent systems | --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Q:** A gas absorption column is being designed for absorbing $SO_2$ from a flue gas stream into water. The diffusivity of $SO_2$ in water is $1.7 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. The surface renewal rate has been experimentally correlated as $s = 0.05\,s^{-1}$. **(a) (7 Marks):** Derive the expression for the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $k_L$ using Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory. State clearly the assumptions used. **(b) (7 Marks):** Using the derived expression, calculate (i) the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $k_L$, (ii) the mean surface age, and (iii) the average molar flux if the interfacial concentration of $SO_2$ is $c_{Ai} = 1.5$ kmol/m³ and the bulk concentration is negligible. Comment on the effect of doubling the turbulence intensity (i.e., doubling $s$) on the absorption rate. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** 1. **State assumptions (1.5 Marks):** - Liquid surface comprises small eddies replaced randomly. - Age distribution is exponential: $\phi(t) = s e^{-st}$. - Each eddy undergoes unsteady-state diffusion (Higbie's result applies locally). - Fresh eddies have bulk concentration $c_{A0}$. 2. **Local flux for an element of age $t$ (1.5 Marks):** $$N_A(t) = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi t}}$$ 3. **Average flux over all ages (2 Marks):** $$\overline{N_A} = \int_{0}^{\infty} N_A(t) \phi(t) dt = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\pi}} \cdot s \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{-1/2} e^{-s t} dt$$ 4. **Evaluate using gamma function (1.5 Marks):** $$\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{-1/2} e^{-s t} dt = \dfrac{\Gamma(1/2)}{s^{1/2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{\sqrt{s}}$$ 5. **Final expression (0.5 Mark):** $$\overline{N_A} = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) \sqrt{D s} \implies k_L = \sqrt{D s}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) Calculation of $k_L$ (2 Marks):** $$k_L = \sqrt{D s} = \sqrt{(1.7 \times 10^{-9}) \times 0.05} = \sqrt{8.5 \times 10^{-11}}$$ $$k_L = 9.22 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Mean surface age (2 Marks):** $$\bar{t} = \dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ s}$$ **(iii) Average molar flux (2 Marks):** $$\overline{N_A} = k_L (c_{Ai} - c_{A0}) = (9.22 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.5 = 1.383 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$ **Comment on doubling $s$ (1 Mark):** Since $k_L \propto \sqrt{s}$, doubling $s$ (from 0.05 to 0.10 s⁻¹) increases $k_L$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. Therefore, the average flux also increases by **41.4%**, not 100%, indicating diminishing returns at higher turbulence — a key practical insight for column designers. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Q:** A two-resistance model is to be applied to a gas-liquid absorption system. The gas-film coefficient is $k_G = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/(m²·s·kPa), the Henry's law constant is $H = 0.85$ kmol/(m³·kPa), and the surface renewal rate is $s = 0.08$ s⁻¹. The diffusivity of solute in the liquid is $D = 2.0 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. **(a) (7 Marks):** Show that for a gas-liquid system with significant gas-phase resistance, the gas-side mass transfer coefficient from Surface Renewal Theory can be expressed as $k_G = \sqrt{D_G \cdot s / H}$ under appropriate conditions. Define all terms. **(b) (7 Marks):** Calculate (i) the liquid-side $k_L$, (ii) the overall $K_L$ using the two-resistance model, and (iii) the overall $K_G$. Comment on whether the gas phase or the liquid phase is rate-controlling. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Conceptual Derivation (7 Marks):** Starting from the two-resistance model: $$\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{H}{k_G}$$ For a **liquid-controlled** system ($H/k_G \ll 1/k_L$), $K_L \approx k_L$. Conversely, when the **gas film** controls and the gas concentration in the liquid interface is governed by Henry's law $c_{Ai} = H \cdot p_{Ai}$, an analogous surface renewal expression for the gas side yields: $$k_G \cdot H = \sqrt{D_G \cdot s}$$ Rearranging: $$k_G = \dfrac{\sqrt{D_G \cdot s}}{H}$$ (2 Marks for the manipulation, 2 Marks for defining $D_G$ as gas-phase diffusivity and $H$ as Henry's constant, 3 Marks for stating assumptions of the model.) **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) Liquid-side $k_L$ (2 Marks):** $$k_L = \sqrt{D s} = \sqrt{(2.0 \times 10^{-9}) \times 0.08} = \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-10}}$$ $$k_L = 1.265 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Overall $K_L$ (2.5 Marks):** $$\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{1.265 \times 10^{-5}} + \dfrac{0.85}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{K_L} = 7.905 \times 10^{4} + 340 = 7.908 \times 10^{4} \text{ s/m}$$ $$K_L = 1.265 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$ **(iii) Overall $K_G$ (1.5 Marks):** $$K_G = \dfrac{K_L}{H} = \dfrac{1.265 \times 10^{-5}}{0.85} = 1.488 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$ (Equivalently, $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{1}{H k_L} = 400 + 92{,}963 \approx 93{,}363$ s·m/kPa $\implies K_G \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-5}$ kmol/(m²·s·kPa).) **Controlling resistance (1 Mark):** The liquid-film resistance ($1/k_L \approx 7.9 \times 10^{4}$) is more than **200 times** the gas-film resistance ($H/k_G = 340$). Hence, the **liquid phase is rate-controlling** in this system. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes — How Marks Are Lost:** > 1. **Confusing $s$ with $t_c$:** Students often mix the Surface Renewal rate $s$ (s⁻¹) with the Penetration contact time $t_c$ (s). Remember: $\bar{t} = 1/s$, and a larger $s$ means **more vigorous** replacement and **higher** $k_L$. > 2. **Forgetting units of $k_L$:** Always state $k_L$ in **m/s**, not cm/s. KTU examiners strictly penalize unit errors (0.5 mark per instance). > 3. **Sign error in flux:** $N_A = k_L (c_{Ai} - c_{A0})$, with $c_{Ai} > c_{A0}$. Swapping these gives a negative flux and full marks cut. > 4. **Missing the gamma function identity:** In derivations, students frequently skip the $\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{-1/2} e^{-st} dt = \sqrt{\pi/s}$ step. This is a **mandatory intermediate step** for full credit. > 5. **Treating Danckwerts' model as a steady-state film model:** This is fundamentally wrong. Surface Renewal is inherently **unsteady-state**, with random exposure. Examiners specifically test this understanding. > 6. **No mention of assumptions:** A derivation without explicitly listing the four assumptions will lose **1.5 marks** even if the algebra is perfect. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Surface Renewal Theory** - ✅ Danckwerts (1951) introduced Surface Renewal Theory to overcome limitations of Film and Penetration models. - ✅ Core idea: surface elements are replaced at **random** intervals, not fixed ones. - ✅ **Age distribution function:** $\phi(t) = s \cdot e^{-s t}$ (probability density). - ✅ **Mean surface age:** $\bar{t} = 1/s$ (always state in seconds). - ✅ **Master equation:** $k_L = \sqrt{D \cdot s}$ — the single most-tested formula. - ✅ **Average flux:** $\overline{N_A} = (c_{Ai} - c_{A0})\sqrt{Ds}$. - ✅ **Link to Higbie:** When $t_c = 4/(\pi s)$, both models give the same $k_L$. - ✅ **Assumptions:** (i) random replacement, (ii) exponential age distribution, (iii) unsteady-state per element, (iv) fresh element has $c_{A0}$. - ✅ **$k_L \propto \sqrt{s}$** — turbulence has **diminishing returns** (square-root dependence). - ✅ **Use gamma identity** $\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi}$ for derivations. - ✅ **Order of theories (oldest → newest):** Two-Film (1923) → Penetration (1935) → Surface Renewal (1951). - ✅ **Real-world applications:** gas absorption, fermentation $O_2$ supply, cooling towers, distillation trays. - ✅ **Coupling with two-resistance model:** $1/K_L = 1/k_L + H/k_G$. - ✅ **Unit check:** $D$ in m²/s, $s$ in s⁻¹, $k_L$ in m/s.

topics.title: Convective Mass Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Convective Mass Transfer # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convective Mass Transfer** is the macroscopic transport of a chemical species (mass) from one location to another in a fluid medium due to the combined action of **molecular diffusion** and **bulk fluid motion (convection)**. It is mathematically characterized by a transport equation in which the molar flux $N_A$ is expressed as the sum of a diffusive term and a convective term: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$ The *rate-determining resistance* to this transport is encapsulated in the **mass transfer coefficient** $k_c$ (or $k_y, k_x, k_G, k_L$), whose units depend on the driving force employed. In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, convective mass transfer is classified under **interphase mass transfer** and forms the basis for designing equipment such as packed columns, tray towers, driers, absorbers, and humidifiers. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smell of Coffee in a Room" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners):** Imagine you walk into a closed room where someone is brewing **freshly ground coffee**. Within seconds, you can smell the aroma across the room. Why? 1. **Molecular Diffusion** — The aroma molecules (volatile organic compounds) randomly collide with air molecules and slowly drift from the cup toward your nose. This is **slow** and **inefficient** in still air. 2. **Natural Convection** — The hot coffee heats the air above it. The warm air rises (buoyancy), creating a circulation current that carries the aroma *much faster* to the far corners of the room. 3. **Forced Convection** — If a ceiling fan is switched ON, the bulk air movement violently sweeps the aroma toward you almost instantly. The **combined effect of diffusion + bulk flow** is **Convective Mass Transfer**. Engineers do not calculate every random molecular collision (that would be impractical); instead, they use an empirical shortcut called the **mass transfer coefficient** ($k_c$), which behaves analogously to the **heat transfer coefficient** ($h$) in heat transfer. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value/Range | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | Mass transfer coefficient (liquid) | $k_L$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ m/s | Used for liquid-side resistance | | Mass transfer coefficient (gas) | $k_G$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ mol/(m²·s·Pa) | Used for gas-side resistance | | Diffusion coefficient (gas) | $D_{AB}$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ m²/s | Highly temperature dependent | | Diffusion coefficient (liquid) | $D_{AB}$ | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s | ~10,000× slower than gas | | Schmidt Number | $Sc$ | $0.6$ to $3000$ | $\nu / D_{AB}$ | | Sherwood Number | $Sh$ | $0.5$ to $10,000$ | Dimensionless mass transfer coefficient | --- ## 1.4 Why Is Convective Mass Transfer Critical? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Relevance** — Without understanding convective mass transfer, you cannot design: > - **Absorption/Stripping columns** (gas-liquid contact) > - **Distillation trays** (vapour-liquid contact) > - **Drying operations** (solid-gas moisture transfer) > - **Membrane separation units** (selective permeation) > - **Catalytic reactors** (external mass transfer to catalyst surface) The **overall rate of mass transfer** governs the **size (height and diameter)** of these contactors. Under-design leads to poor product purity; over-design wastes capital expenditure. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Conceptual Framework (Why & How) The transport of species $A$ through a stagnant or moving fluid $B$ occurs through **two parallel mechanisms** that act *simultaneously*: ### Mechanism 1 — Molecular Diffusion - Driven by a **concentration gradient** ($\partial C_A / \partial z$). - Quantified by **Fick's First Law** of diffusion. - A microscopic, random-walk process at the molecular scale. - **Why it matters:** Dominates in stagnant films, laminar sub-layers, and inside porous solids. ### Mechanism 2 — Bulk (Eddy) Convection - Driven by the **bulk motion of the fluid** (forced or natural). - Caused by pumps, agitators, buoyancy, or pressure gradients. - **Why it matters:** Dominates in turbulent cores, where eddy mixing is 10⁶ times faster than molecular diffusion. ### The Combined Flux Equation The total molar flux of $A$ in the $z$-direction is: $$N_A = \underbrace{-D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}}_{\text{Diffusive}} + \underbrace{x_A (N_A + N_B)}_{\text{Convective (bulk flow)}}$$ For systems where the bulk flow is negligible (low concentration, equimolar counter-diffusion, or dilute solutions), the second term vanishes, and we recover pure **Fickian diffusion**. --- ## 2.2 The Mass Transfer Coefficient — Concept & Forms > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** The mass transfer coefficient $k$ is a *proportionality constant* that relates the molar flux of a species to its concentration difference (the driving force) between the bulk fluid and the interface. Because the driving force can be expressed in many ways (concentration, mole fraction, partial pressure), the coefficient's units and symbol change accordingly: | Driving Force Used | Coefficient Symbol | Flux Equation | Units of $k$ | |---|---|---|---| | Concentration $(C_A^* - C_{A,\infty})$ | $k_c$ | $N_A = k_c (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$ | m/s | | Mole fraction $(y_{A,i} - y_{A,\infty})$ | $k_y$ | $N_A = k_y (y_{A,i} - y_{A,\infty})$ | kmol/(m²·s) | | Partial pressure $(p_{A,i} - p_{A,\infty})$ | $k_G$ | $N_A = k_G (p_{A,i} - p_{A,\infty})$ | kmol/(m²·s·Pa) | | Liquid concentration (Henry basis) | $k_L$ | $N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$ | m/s | | Liquid mole fraction | $k_x$ | $N_A = k_x (x_{A,i} - x_{A,\infty})$ | kmol/(m²·s) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inter-conversion (KTU favourite):** Since the total concentration $C_T = P/RT$ for an ideal gas, the coefficients are related as: > $$k_c = k_y / C_T = k_G \cdot RT$$ > Students frequently lose marks by mixing up these conversions in numerical problems. --- ## 2.3 The Mass Transfer Boundary Layer When a fluid flows past a solid surface (or a fluid-fluid interface), a thin layer develops adjacent to the surface where the velocity drops from the free-stream value to zero (no-slip condition). Analogously, a **concentration boundary layer** develops where the species concentration changes from $C_{A,s}$ (at the interface) to $C_{A,\infty}$ (in the bulk). **Two sub-regimes exist:** 1. **Laminar sub-layer** — Molecular diffusion dominates, $\delta_c \propto \sqrt{x}$. 2. **Turbulent core** — Eddy diffusion dominates, concentration is nearly uniform. The **effective mass transfer coefficient** emerges from integrating the diffusion equation across this boundary layer. --- ## 2.4 The Three Analogies (Reynolds, Prandtl, Chilton-Colburn) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 module places strong emphasis on **transfer analogies**, which link momentum, heat, and mass transfer through dimensionless numbers. ### 2.4.1 Reynolds Analogy (Simplest Form) For **Prandtl Number** $Pr = 1$ and **Schmidt Number** $Sc = 1$ (e.g., gases), the turbulent transport of momentum, heat, and mass are equivalent: $$\frac{St}{1} = \frac{C_f}{2} = \frac{k}{u_{\infty}}$$ where: - $C_f$ = skin friction coefficient (dimensionless) - $St$ = Stanton number for mass transfer $= k_c / u_{\infty}$ ### 2.4.2 Prandtl Analogy Accounts for the **laminar sub-layer** by introducing $Pr$: $$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$ ### 2.4.3 von Kármán Analogy Two-layer model with explicit sub-layer + buffer layer + turbulent core, valid for a wider $Pr$ range. ### 2.4.4 Chilton–Colburn Analogy (Most Used in KTU Problems) > [!NOTE] > **The J-factor analogy** is the workhorse of chemical engineering practice: $$j_D = j_H \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{k_c}{u_{\infty}} Sc^{2/3} = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u_{\infty}} Pr^{2/3}$$ Or in the form usually tested: $$St_{mass} \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2} = St_{heat} \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$ This is valid for **$0.6 < Sc < 3000$** and **$0.6 < Pr < 100$** — covers most engineering fluids. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N_A = k_c (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$ | Basic flux definition | Single-phase, dilute system | | 2 | $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ | Sherwood number | Correlate $k_c$ from dimensionless groups | | 3 | $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ | Schmidt number | Ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity | | 4 | $Re = \rho u L / \mu$ | Reynolds number | Flow regime check | | 5 | $Gr_m = g L^3 (\rho_\infty - \rho_s)/\nu^2$ | Grashof (mass) | Natural convection | | 6 | $St = k_c / u_\infty = Sh / (Re \cdot Sc)$ | Stanton number | Used in analogies | | 7 | $j_D = St \cdot Sc^{2/3}$ | Mass transfer j-factor | Chilton–Colburn | | 8 | $j_H = St_h \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ | Heat transfer j-factor | Chilton–Colburn | | 9 | $j_D = j_H$ | **Chilton–Colburn analogy** | Equate heat & mass transfer | | 10 | $k_c = (D_{AB}/L) \cdot Sh$ | Solve for $k_c$ | Final step in problems | | 11 | $k_G = k_c / RT$ | Gas-side conversion | Ideal gas systems | | 12 | $k_y = k_c \cdot C_T$ | Mole-fraction basis | Total concentration known | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Chilton–Colburn** is used to **predict $k_c$ from $h$** (heat transfer data) — useful when mass transfer experiments are costly but heat transfer data exists. - **Reynolds analogy** is used in **high-speed gas flow** over aircraft wings and turbine blades (mass + heat transfer to the surface). - **Schmidt number** quantifies the relative thickness of the velocity vs. concentration boundary layers. For $Sc \gg 1$ (liquids), the concentration boundary layer is *thinner* than the velocity boundary layer. - The **Sherwood number correlation** $Sh = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Sc^{0.33}$ is analogous to the **Nusselt correlation** for turbulent flow in pipes — students must memorize the form. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Analogy (Starting from Friction and Mass Flux) ### Starting Premise Consider a fluid flowing turbulently over a flat plate. Let: - $\tau_s$ = wall shear stress (momentum flux) - $N_A$ = mass flux of species $A$ - $q$ = heat flux - $u_\infty$ = free-stream velocity - $\rho$ = fluid density - $\mu, \nu$ = dynamic / kinematic viscosity - $D_{AB}$ = diffusivity - $k$ = thermal conductivity ### Step 1 — Define Skin Friction Coefficient $$C_f = \frac{\tau_s}{\frac{1}{2} \rho u_\infty^2}$$ ### Step 2 — Define Stanton Number for Mass Transfer $$St_m = \frac{N_A}{u_\infty (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}$$ ### Step 3 — Define Stanton Number for Heat Transfer $$St_h = \frac{q}{\rho c_p u_\infty (T_s - T_\infty)}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Assumption $Pr = Sc = 1$ When the molecular and eddy diffusivities of momentum, heat, and mass are all equal (only true for certain gases at moderate conditions), the turbulent transport of all three quantities is governed by the same eddy diffusivity $\varepsilon_M = \varepsilon_H = \varepsilon_A$. The momentum, heat, and mass equations all reduce to the *same* differential form, and the ratio of flux to driving force is identical for all three. ### Step 5 — Equate the Ratios $$\frac{\tau_s / \rho}{u_\infty} = \frac{q / (\rho c_p)}{T_s - T_\infty} = \frac{N_A}{C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}}$$ ### Step 6 — Express in Dimensionless Form $$\frac{C_f}{2} = St_h = St_m$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion of the derivation:** > $$\boxed{\frac{C_f}{2} = St_m = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty}}$$ > This is the **Reynolds Analogy**. It is the *starting point* for all higher-order analogies. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Chilton–Colburn Analogy ### Step 1 — Modification for Non-Unity Pr and Sc The Reynolds analogy fails for liquids (large $Sc$) and liquid metals (small $Pr$). Prandtl proposed the correction factor $Pr^{2/3}$ based on the assumption of a thin laminar sub-layer where transport is purely molecular. ### Step 2 — Define the $j$-Factors $$j_H = St_h \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} Pr^{2/3}$$ $$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty} Sc^{2/3}$$ ### Step 3 — Experimental Observation Chilton and Colburn (1930s) showed experimentally that for gases and liquids in turbulent flow: $$j_H \approx j_D$$ ### Step 4 — Final Form $$\boxed{\frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty} Pr^{2/3} = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty} Sc^{2/3}}$$ Or equivalently: $$\frac{St_h}{St_m} = \left( \frac{Pr}{Sc} \right)^{2/3}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validity range:** $0.6 < Sc < 3000$, $0.6 < Pr < 100$, $Re > 10^4$ (turbulent). Outside this range, use the more rigorous von Kármán analogy. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: From Chilton–Colburn to Sherwood Number Correlation ### Step 1 — Recall Definitions $$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$ $$St_m = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty}$$ $$C_f = \frac{0.074}{Re^{0.2}} \quad \text{(turbulent flow over flat plate, Blasius)}$$ ### Step 2 — Combine $$\frac{k_c}{u_\infty} Sc^{2/3} = \frac{0.074}{2 Re^{0.2}}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for $k_c$ $$k_c = \frac{0.037 \, u_\infty}{Re^{0.2} Sc^{2/3}}$$ ### Step 4 — Express as Sherwood Number Using $Re = \rho u_\infty L / \mu$ and $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$: $$k_c = \frac{0.037 \, u_\infty}{(u_\infty L / \nu)^{0.2} Sc^{2/3}}$$ Multiplying both sides by $L / D_{AB}$: $$\frac{k_c L}{D_{AB}} = \frac{0.037 \, u_\infty L / D_{AB}}{(u_\infty L / \nu)^{0.2} Sc^{2/3}}$$ ### Step 5 — Simplify $$\frac{k_c L}{D_{AB}} = 0.037 \left(\frac{u_\infty L}{\nu}\right)^{0.8} \left(\frac{\nu}{D_{AB}}\right)^{1/3}$$ $$\boxed{Sh = 0.037 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{1/3}}$$ This is the classic **Sherwood number correlation for turbulent flow over a flat plate**, derived directly from the Chilton–Colburn analogy. Students should be able to reproduce this derivation in a KTU 14-mark problem. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Layer Thickness Relations For a fluid with $Sc \neq 1$, the **concentration boundary layer** thickness $\delta_c$ is related to the **hydrodynamic boundary layer** thickness $\delta$ by: $$\frac{\delta}{\delta_c} = Sc^{1/3}$$ **Derivation sketch:** 1. The mass transfer equation inside the boundary layer (assuming thin layer, no convection in $x$, constant $D_{AB}$): $$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$$ 2. Similarity transform $\eta = y \sqrt{u_\infty / (\nu x)}$ reduces it to an ODE. 3. The solution, analogous to the Blasius equation, gives the concentration profile. Setting $C_A = 0.99 C_{A,\infty}$ as the edge of the layer yields $\delta_c \propto (\nu x / u_\infty)^{1/2} Sc^{-1/3}$. 4. Comparing with $\delta \propto (\nu x / u_\infty)^{1/2}$ gives the boxed relation above. > [!WARNING] > **Common error:** Students often write $\delta_c = \delta / Sc$ (linear) — this is **wrong**. The correct relation is the cube root, $\delta_c = \delta / Sc^{1/3}$. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Overall Architecture: Convective Mass Transfer Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Driving Force: Concentration Gradient] --> B[Mass Transfer Zone] C[Bulk Fluid Motion
Forced or Natural] --> B D[Molecular Diffusion] --> B B --> E{Region?} E --> F[Laminar Sub-layer
Molecular Diffusion Dominates] E --> G[Buffer Layer
Both Mechanisms] E --> H[Turbulent Core
Eddy Diffusion Dominates] F --> I[Effective Mass Transfer Coefficient kc] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Final Flux Equation: NA = kc times CAi minus CAbulk] I --> K[Dimensionless Correlations: Sh = f Re, Sc] I --> L[Analogies: Reynolds, Prandtl, Chilton-Colburn] ``` ## 4.2 Three-Analogies Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reynolds Analogy
Cf over 2 = Stm = Sth] --> B[Valid only for Pr = Sc = 1] B --> C[Improvement: Prandtl] C --> D[St Pr two thirds = Cf over 2] D --> E[Improvement: Chilton Colburn] E --> F[jH = jD
Stm Sc two thirds = Sth Pr two thirds] F --> G[Applied to: Packed Towers
Heat Exchangers
Boundary Layers] ``` ## 4.3 Boundary Layer Structure Over a Flat Plate ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Leading Edge of Plate] --> B[Laminar Region Re less than 5 times 10 to power 5] B --> C[Velocity Boundary Layer delta grows as x to power half] C --> D[Concentration Boundary Layer delta c grows as x to power half times Sc to power minus 1/3] B --> E[Transition Region] E --> F[Turbulent Region Re greater than 5 times 10 to power 5] F --> G[Turbulent Mixing Dominates] G --> H[Effective kc predicted by Chilton Colburn] D --> H H --> I[Total Mass Flux NA integrated over surface] ``` ## 4.4 Mass Transfer Coefficient Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need to Calculate Mass Transfer Flux] --> Q1{System Type?} Q1 -->|Gas Liquid| Q2[Use Overall kG or kL with Two Film Theory] Q1 -->|Gas Solid| Q3[Use External Flow Correlation Sh = a Re to power b Sc to power c] Q1 -->|Liquid Liquid| Q4[Use Continuous and Dispersed Phase Coefficients] Q1 -->|Inside Pipe| Q5[Use Dittus Boelter Type: Sh = 0.023 Re to power 0.8 Sc to power 0.33] Q2 --> Use[Apply Flux Equation NA = k times Driving Force] Q3 --> Use Q4 --> Use Q5 --> Use Use --> Output[Final Flux in kmol per m square per s] ``` ## 4.5 Functional Architecture — How a Student Should Solve a KTU Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Identify Phases and Direction of Transfer] --> S2[Step 2: Write Down All Given Data and Find Sc, Re] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Choose Appropriate Analogy or Sherwood Correlation] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate Dimensionless Numbers Re and Sc] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Sh from Correlation] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Back-calculate kc from Sh = kc L over DAB] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Convert kc to kg or kG if Required] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Apply NA = kc times Driving Force for Final Flux] S8 --> Final[Final Answer with Correct Units] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block above represents a *decision point* or *computation stage* that mirrors the expected KTU valuation scheme. Examiners reward students who follow a structured, step-by-step methodology. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define convective mass transfer coefficient. List any two forms in which it is expressed depending on the driving force chosen.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The convective mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ is defined as the proportionality constant that relates the molar flux of a species to its concentration driving force between the bulk fluid and the interface: $$N_A = k_c (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$$ Two common forms: 1. **On a mole-fraction basis:** $N_A = k_y (y_{A,i} - y_{A,\infty})$ 2. **On a partial-pressure basis:** $N_A = k_G (p_{A,i} - p_{A,\infty})$ [1 mark: definition, 1 mark: equation, 1 mark: two forms] --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **State the Reynolds analogy and indicate when it is applicable.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Reynolds analogy states that the ratios of flux to driving force are equal for momentum, heat, and mass transfer when $Pr = Sc = 1$: $$\frac{C_f}{2} = St_h = St_m$$ It is applicable to **turbulent flow of gases** (where $Sc \approx 0.6$ to $1.0$ and $Pr \approx 0.7$ to $1.0$), typically for $Re > 10^4$. [1 mark: equation, 1 mark: equality, 1 mark: applicability condition] --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You may attempt *either* Question A *or* Question B. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the **Reynolds analogy** starting from the basic definitions of skin friction coefficient and Stanton number. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks) **(b)** Air at $30°C$ and $1 \text{ atm}$ flows over a wet flat plate of length $0.5 \text{ m}$ at a velocity of $10 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate the mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ for water vapour evaporating into air. Use the Chilton–Colburn analogy. Given: $D_{AB,water\text{-}air} = 0.26 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $\nu_{air} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(a):** **Step 1 — Define Skin Friction Coefficient (1 mark):** $$C_f = \frac{\tau_s}{\frac{1}{2} \rho u_\infty^2}$$ **Step 2 — Define Mass Transfer Stanton Number (1 mark):** $$St_m = \frac{k_c}{u_\infty} = \frac{N_A}{u_\infty (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}$$ **Step 3 — Define Heat Transfer Stanton Number (1 mark):** $$St_h = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u_\infty}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Assumption $Pr = Sc = 1$ (2 marks):** When the molecular and eddy diffusivities of momentum, heat, and mass are all equal, the governing transport equations for all three are identical in form. Therefore, the ratio of flux to driving force must be equal. **Step 5 — Conclude Reynolds Analogy (2 marks):** $$\boxed{\frac{C_f}{2} = St_h = St_m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to state the *assumptions* ($Pr = Sc = 1$, turbulent flow, negligible viscous dissipation). Loss of 2 marks is common. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(b):** **Given Data:** - $L = 0.5$ m - $u_\infty = 10$ m/s - $\nu = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s - $D_{AB} = 0.26 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s **Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds Number (1 mark):** $$Re_L = \frac{u_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{10 \times 0.5}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.125 \times 10^5$$ Since $Re_L > 5 \times 10^5$ is not met, flow is **laminar** over the plate. **Step 2 — Calculate Schmidt Number (1 mark):** $$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{0.26 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.615$$ **Step 3 — Use Chilton–Colburn / Analogous Correlation (1 mark):** For laminar flow over a flat plate, the analogous heat-mass transfer correlation is: $$Sh_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{0.5} \, Sc^{1/3}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate Sherwood Number (1.5 marks):** $$Sh_L = 0.664 \times (3.125 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \times (0.615)^{1/3}$$ $$Sh_L = 0.664 \times 559.0 \times 0.851 = 315.8$$ **Step 5 — Calculate $k_c$ (1.5 marks):** $$k_c = \frac{Sh_L \cdot D_{AB}}{L} = \frac{315.8 \times 0.26 \times 10^{-4}}{0.5}$$ $$\boxed{k_c = 0.0164 \text{ m/s} = 1.64 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 6 — State Final Units (1 mark):** m/s, consistent with concentration driving force $(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Warning:** A *very common* mistake is to use the turbulent correlation ($Sh = 0.037 Re^{0.8} Sc^{1/3}$) for a laminar $Re$. Always **check the flow regime first**. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternate]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of the **mass transfer boundary layer** formed when a fluid flows over a flat plate. Compare it with the hydrodynamic boundary layer. (7 marks) **(b)** Oxygen from air is being absorbed into water in a packed column operating at $25°C$ and $1 \text{ atm}$. At a particular point in the column, the gas-phase mass transfer coefficient is $k_G = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·atm)}$ and the liquid-phase coefficient is $k_L = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$. The equilibrium relation is $y = 30x$ (Henry's law in mole fractions). If the bulk gas contains $y_{A,\infty} = 0.21$ and bulk liquid contains $x_{A,\infty} = 0.0$, calculate the **overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient $K_G$** and the **local molar flux $N_A$**. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(a):** **Step 1 — Define Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer (2 marks):** The hydrodynamic (velocity) boundary layer is the region adjacent to a surface where the fluid velocity rises from zero (at the surface, no-slip condition) to approximately $0.99 u_\infty$ (free stream). Its thickness $\delta$ grows as $\delta \propto \sqrt{x}$ for laminar flow over a flat plate. **Step 2 — Define Mass Transfer Boundary Layer (2 marks):** The mass transfer boundary layer is the analogous region where the **concentration** of a species changes from $C_{A,s}$ (at the surface) to $C_{A,\infty}$ (in the bulk). Its thickness $\delta_c$ is generally *smaller* than $\delta$ for liquids ($Sc \gg 1$) and nearly equal for gases ($Sc \approx 1$). **Step 3 — Relation Between Thicknesses (1.5 marks):** $$\frac{\delta}{\delta_c} = Sc^{1/3}$$ **Step 4 — Sketch Explanation (1.5 marks):** *In an exam, draw a flat plate with two curves drawn from the leading edge:* - Outer curve = velocity profile $u(y)$. - Inner curve = concentration profile $C_A(y)$. - Mark $\delta$ at $u = 0.99 u_\infty$. - Mark $\delta_c$ at $C_A = 0.99 (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) + C_{A,\infty}$. - Indicate laminar → transition → turbulent regions. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students draw only ONE curve. The question explicitly asks for comparison. **2 marks** are reserved for the sketch and the comparison arrows. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(b):** **Step 1 — Identify the Two-Film Concept (1 mark):** For a gas-liquid system, mass transfer must overcome *two* resistances in series — gas film and liquid film. The overall coefficient $K_G$ satisfies: $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$ where $m$ is the slope of the equilibrium line $y^* = m x$. **Step 2 — Determine $m$ (1 mark):** From $y = 30x$, slope $m = 30$. **Step 3 — Unit Conversion of $k_L$ (1.5 marks):** Since $k_G$ is on a partial-pressure (atm) basis and $k_L$ is on a concentration (mol/m³) basis, we need to convert. Using Henry's law $p_A = H x_A$ and $C_A = p_A / (RT)$: $$\frac{m}{k_L} \text{ must be in atm·m²·s/kmol}$$ Convert $k_L$ to a gas-phase equivalent: $$k_{L,eq} = \frac{k_L}{H} \cdot RT \quad \text{or use } \frac{m}{k_L} = \frac{m \cdot RT}{H \cdot k_L \cdot RT}$$ A simpler equivalent formulation uses the mole-fraction form. With $k_L$ (m/s) and $C_{T,L} = \rho_L / M_L \approx 55.5 \text{ kmol/m}^3$ for dilute water: $$\frac{m}{k_L} = \frac{30}{(1.8 \times 10^{-4})(55.5)} = 3003 \text{ atm·m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate $K_G$ (1.5 marks):** $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} + 3003 = 4000 + 3003 = 7003 \text{ atm·m}^2\text{·s/kmol}$$ $$K_G = \frac{1}{7003} = 1.428 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·atm)}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate Flux (1 mark):** $$N_A = K_G (y_{A,\infty} - y_A^*) = K_G (y_{A,\infty} - m \cdot x_{A,\infty})$$ With $x_{A,\infty} = 0$: $$N_A = 1.428 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.21 - 0) = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$ $$\boxed{K_G = 1.43 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·atm)}, \quad N_A = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The gas-film resistance dominates here (4000 vs 3003 in the same units), indicating that the gas side is rate-limiting. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Warning:** The **unit conversion of $k_L$** is the trickiest part. Many students directly use $\frac{m}{k_L}$ without proper dimensional analysis and lose **3 marks**. The equilibrium slope $m$ must always be in the same basis as the chosen $K_G$. --- ## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Convective mass transfer** = molecular diffusion + bulk fluid motion acting simultaneously. - **Mass transfer coefficient** $k_c$ is NOT a constant — it depends on geometry, $Re$, $Sc$, and turbulence. - **Three driving-force forms:** concentration ($k_c$), mole fraction ($k_y$), partial pressure ($k_G$). - **Reynolds analogy** is valid only for $Pr = Sc = 1$ (gases). - **Chilton–Colburn analogy:** $j_D = j_H \Rightarrow St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = St_h \cdot Pr^{2/3}$. - **Sherwood number** $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ is the dimensionless $k_c$ — analogous to Nusselt number. - **Schmidt number** $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ — high $Sc$ (liquids) ⇒ thin concentration boundary layer. - **For laminar flow** over a flat plate: $Sh = 0.664 \, Re^{0.5} \, Sc^{1/3}$. - **For turbulent flow** over a flat plate: $Sh = 0.037 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{1/3}$. - **For flow inside a pipe** (Dittus–Boelter analogous form): $Sh = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Sc^{0.33}$. - **Two-film theory** gives $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$ — **units must match**. - **Industrial use:** packed columns, absorbers, driers, humidifiers, membrane modules. - **Conversion formulas:** $k_c = k_G RT$, $k_y = k_c C_T$, $k_c = k_x / C_T$. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Exam Hall — "RPD" Rule:** > - **R**eynolds ⇒ only for $Pr = Sc = 1$ > - **P**randtl ⇒ introduces $Pr^{2/3}$ > - **C**hilton-Colburn (D = Diffusion) ⇒ introduces $Sc^{2/3}$ and is **most widely applicable**

topics.title: Mass Transfer Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Mass Transfer Coefficients # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_c$)** is defined as the proportionality constant that relates the **molar flux** ($N_A$) of a species diffusing through a stagnant or moving medium to the **concentration driving force** ($\Delta C_A$) acting across a defined interface or boundary layer. Mathematically, the **individual mass transfer coefficient** is expressed as: $$N_A = k_c \cdot (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$$ Where: - $N_A$ = Molar flux of component **A** [$\text{kmol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$] - $k_c$ = Mass transfer coefficient based on concentration [$\text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$] - $C_{A,i}$ = Concentration of A at the interface [$\text{kmol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$] - $C_{A,\infty}$ = Concentration of A in the bulk phase [$\text{kmol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$] > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 NEP-Aligned Definition:** The mass transfer coefficient is *NOT* a thermodynamic property of the fluid — it is a **transport kinetic parameter** that depends on the system geometry, flow regime (laminar/turbulent), physical properties of the fluid ($\rho, \mu, D_{AB}$), and the prevailing concentration driving force. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Sweetening" Intuition Imagine you drop a sugar cube into a still cup of black coffee ☕. The sugar dissolves, and the sweetness slowly spreads outward.
