Why doesn't the entire cup become sweet in **one millisecond**? - The molecules of sugar (solute) must physically travel from the cube's surface (high concentration) to the bulk liquid (low concentration). - A thin **stagnant boundary layer** of liquid clings to the sugar surface where molecular diffusion is the *only* transport mechanism. - The thickness of this "stagnant film" determines the **resistance** to mass transfer — analogous to thermal resistance in heat conduction. > 🎯 **Real-World Engineering Mapping:** The sugar-coffee analogy maps directly to: > - **Gas absorption** of $CO_2$ in amine solutions > - **Liquid-liquid extraction** of penicillin from fermentation broth > - **Drying** of wet solids in a tray dryer > - **Humidification** in cooling towers In every case, a **resistance layer** (the fictitious "film") governs how fast the solute crosses from one phase to another. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Mass Transfer Coefficients | Symbol | Basis | Phase Used | Driving Force | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | $k_c$ | Concentration | Gas or Liquid | $\Delta C_A$ [$\text{kmol/m}^3$] | | $k_y$ | Mole Fraction | Gas Phase | $\Delta y_A$ | | $k_x$ | Mole Fraction | Liquid Phase | $\Delta x_A$ | | $k_G$ | Partial Pressure | Gas Phase | $\Delta p_A$ [$\text{kPa}$] | | $k_L$ | Concentration | Liquid Phase | $\Delta C_A$ [$\text{kmol/m}^3$] | | $K_G$ | Partial Pressure (Overall) | Gas-Phase Overall | $\Delta p_A$ | | $K_L$ | Concentration (Overall) | Liquid-Phase Overall | $\Delta C_A$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):** Always check whether the question demands an **individual coefficient** (one-phase resistance) or an **overall coefficient** (two-phase combined resistance). Mistaking these is a guaranteed **3-mark deduction** in the ESE. --- ## 1.4 The Concept of "Fictitious Film" — Whitman's Two-Film Foundation W.G. Whitman's **Two-Film Theory (1923)** is the bedrock of mass transfer coefficient theory. It postulates that: 1. A **stagnant gas film** of thickness $y_G$ exists on the gas side of the interface. 2. A **stagnant liquid film** of thickness $x_L$ exists on the liquid side. 3. The bulk phases are perfectly mixed (uniform concentration). 4. **Equilibrium** exists *exactly* at the gas-liquid interface ($y_i, x_i$). These films offer the **entire resistance** to mass transfer — the bulk phases are idealized as having zero resistance. > 🎯 **Analogy — The "Double Door" Model:** Imagine entering a concert hall through two consecutive locked doors 🚪🚪. Even if the lobby (bulk phase) is empty, you can only enter as fast as you can unlock *both* doors (films). The total resistance is the **sum** of the two individual door resistances. # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Film Theory — Rigorous Mathematical Formulation The film theory treats mass transfer as **steady-state, one-dimensional molecular diffusion** through a stagnant film (Fick's First Law applied across a thin layer). ### 2.1.1 Gas-Phase Individual Coefficient ($k_G$) For diffusion of A through stagnant B in a gas film of thickness $y_G$: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot y_G} \cdot \frac{(y_{A,b} - y_{A,i})}{(1 - y_A)_{lm}}$$ Where: - $D_{AB}$ = Diffusivity of A in B [$\text{m}^2/\text{s}$] - $P$ = Total pressure [$\text{Pa}$] - $R$ = Universal gas constant [$8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$] - $T$ = Absolute temperature [$\text{K}$] - $y_{A,b}, y_{A,i}$ = Mole fractions of A in bulk gas & at interface - $(1 - y_A)_{lm}$ = Log-mean of $(1 - y_{A,b})$ and $(1 - y_{A,i})$ > Defining the **gas-phase individual mass transfer coefficient**: $$k_G = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot y_G \cdot (1 - y_A)_{lm}}$$ ### 2.1.2 Liquid-Phase Individual Coefficient ($k_L$) For diffusion of A through stagnant B in a liquid film of thickness $x_L$: $$k_L = \frac{D_{AB,L}}{x_L} \cdot \frac{C_T}{C_{B,lm}}$$ Where $C_T$ is the total liquid concentration and $C_{B,lm}$ is the log-mean of the B concentrations. > [!NOTE] > **Why the "log-mean correction"?** When A is *not* dilute, the flux of A creates a **counter-flux of B** to maintain constant total moles. The log-mean term $(1 - y_A)_{lm}$ or $C_{B,lm}$ accounts for this **equimolar counter-diffusion correction**. --- ## 2.2 The Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient — Adding Resistances In a real two-phase system, **both** films offer resistance. The total resistance follows the **electrical analogy** (resistances in series): $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{m \cdot k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L}$$ Where $m$ is the **slope of the equilibrium line** ($dy^*/dx$) relating the gas and liquid compositions. > 🎯 **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This relationship is the foundation for designing: > - **Absorption columns** (packed/tray) for removing $H_2S$ from natural gas > - **Stripping towers** for ammonia recovery > - **Solvent extraction** equipment in pharmaceutical manufacturing --- ## 2.3 Penetration Theory (Higbie, 1935) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** The Penetration Theory is a **high-weightage** topic. It is often asked as a 7-mark derivation question. ### Core Hypothesis - The fluid at the interface is composed of **small fluid elements (eddies)**. - Each eddy **penetrates** to the interface from the bulk, stays for a **fixed exposure time** $t_e$ (Higbie's contact time), then is replaced by a fresh eddy. - During exposure, the eddy experiences **unsteady-state diffusion** (semi-infinite medium). ### Resulting Coefficient $$k_L = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB,L}}{\pi \cdot t_e}}$$ Where $t_e$ = exposure time of the eddy at the interface [$\text{s}$]. ### Why is it "Better" Than Film Theory? Film theory predicts $k_L \propto D_{AB}$ (linear). Higbie's penetration theory predicts $k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$. **Experimental evidence** (e.g., for gases in wetted-wall columns) supports the $\sqrt{D_{AB}}$ dependence, validating the penetration model for short-contact equipment. --- ## 2.4 Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts, 1951) ### Modification of Higbie's Model Danckwerts argued that fluid elements do **NOT** all have the same exposure time — they follow an **exponential probability distribution** with a mean surface renewal rate $s$ [$\text{s}^{-1}$]. ### Resulting Coefficient $$k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB,L} \cdot s}$$ Where $s$ = fractional rate of surface renewal [$\text{s}^{-1}$]. --- ## 2.5 Boundary Layer Theory — Unified Transport Approach > [!NOTE] > The boundary layer approach links mass transfer to **momentum transfer (Prandtl)**, giving rise to the famous **Reynolds, Chilton-Colburn analogies** — extremely high-yield for KTU 2024. For flow over a flat plate, the **convective mass transfer coefficient** is correlated using dimensionless numbers: $$Sh_x = 0.664 \cdot Re_x^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3} \quad \text{(Laminar)}$$ $$Sh_x = 0.037 \cdot Re_x^{4/5} \cdot Sc^{1/3} \quad \text{(Turbulent)}$$ Where: - $Sh = \frac{k_c \cdot L}{D_{AB}}$ (Sherwood number) - $Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot L}{\mu}$ (Reynolds number) - $Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D_{AB}}$ (Schmidt number) --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Board Exam Verified and have appeared in past ESE papers.** | # | Formula | Name / Application | Units | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $N_A = k_c \cdot (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$ | Flux–Driving Force Relation | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | | 2 | $k_c = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}$ | Film Theory (dilute) | $\text{m/s}$ | | 3 | $k_L = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB,L}}{\pi \cdot t_e}}$ | Higbie Penetration Theory | $\text{m/s}$ | | 4 | $k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB,L} \cdot s}$ | Danckwerts Surface Renewal | $\text{m/s}$ | | 5 | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Two-Film Overall Resistance | $\text{m/s}$ | | 6 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{m \cdot k_G} + \dfrac{1}{k_L}$ | Liquid-Phase Overall | $\text{m/s}$ | | 7 | $Sh = \dfrac{k_c \cdot L}{D_{AB}}$ | Sherwood Number | — | | 8 | $Sc = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D_{AB}}$ | Schmidt Number | — | | 9 | $Le = \dfrac{Sc}{Pr} = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}$ | Lewis Number | — | | 10 | $j_D = St_{mass} \cdot Sc^{2/3} = 0.023 \cdot Re^{-0.2}$ | Chilton-Colburn Analogy | — | | 11 | $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$ | Overall on mole-fraction basis | — | --- ## 2.7 The Three Analogies — Heat, Mass, and Momentum | Analogy | Form | Applicability | |:---|:---|:---| | **Reynolds** | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{f}{8}$ | Prandtl & Schmidt both ≈ 1 | | **Prandtl** | $\dfrac{St}{f/2} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\rho v_s/\tau)(\alpha/D_{AB})}$ | $Pr = Sc = 1$ strictly | | **Chilton-Colburn** | $j_H \approx j_D \approx \frac{f}{2}$ | Prandtl & Schmidt 0.6 – 100 | Where $j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$ is the **mass transfer j-factor**, and $St_m = \frac{k_c}{v}$ is the mass-transfer Stanton number. > 🎯 **Real-World Use:** A chemical engineer designing a cooling tower can use heat-transfer data ($h$) to **predict** mass-transfer coefficients ($k_y$) for water evaporation, saving expensive gas-phase experiments. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Individual Mass Transfer Coefficient from Film Theory ### Starting Point — Fick's First Law For steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion of A through stagnant B in the y-direction: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dC_A}{dy} + x_A \cdot (N_A + N_B)$$ ### Step 1 — Apply the Stagnant Film Condition Since B is stagnant, $N_B = 0$. Therefore: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dC_A}{dy} + x_A \cdot N_A$$ Rearranging: $$N_A \cdot (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dC_A}{dy}$$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Mole Fraction Form For an ideal gas, $C_A = \frac{P \cdot y_A}{R \cdot T}$, so $dC_A = \frac{P}{R \cdot T} \cdot dy_A$. Also, $C_T = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$, hence: $$N_A \cdot (1 - y_A) = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \cdot \frac{dy_A}{dy}$$ ### Step 3 — Separate Variables and Integrate Rearranging: $$N_A \cdot dy = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \cdot \frac{dy_A}{(1 - y_A)}$$ Integrating from $y = 0$ (interface) to $y = y_G$ (bulk gas), with $y_A$ going from $y_{A,i}$ to $y_{A,b}$: $$N_A \int_0^{y_G} dy = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \int_{y_{A,i}}^{y_{A,b}} \frac{dy_A}{(1 - y_A)}$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate Both Integrals Left side: $N_A \cdot y_G$ Right side: $\displaystyle -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \Big[ -\ln(1 - y_A) \Big]_{y_{A,i}}^{y_{A,b}} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T} \cdot \ln \left( \frac{1 - y_{A,b}}{1 - y_{A,i}} \right)$ ### Step 5 — Express Log as Log-Mean Using the identity $\ln(a/b) = \frac{a - b}{(a-b)/\ln(a/b)}$ and noting that $(1 - y_{A,b}) - (1 - y_{A,i}) = y_{A,i} - y_{A,b}$: $$N_A \cdot y_G = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot (1 - y_A)_{lm}} \cdot (y_{A,b} - y_{A,i})$$ where $(1 - y_A)_{lm} = \dfrac{(1 - y_{A,b}) - (1 - y_{A,i})}{\ln \left[ \dfrac{1 - y_{A,b}}{1 - y_{A,i}} \right]}$ ### Step 6 — Define the Mass Transfer Coefficient Rearranging into the canonical form $N_A = k_y \cdot (y_{A,b} - y_{A,i})$: $$\boxed{k_y = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot y_G \cdot (1 - y_A)_{lm}}}$$ > ✅ **KTU Board Verification:** This is the **exact form** expected in a 7-mark derivation. Missing the $(1 - y_A)_{lm}$ correction will cost 2 marks. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Higbie's Penetration Theory ### Starting Point — Unsteady Diffusion A fluid eddy arrives at the interface at $t = 0$. The governing equation is **Fick's Second Law** for one-dimensional unsteady diffusion into a semi-infinite medium: $$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$ ### Step 1 — State Initial and Boundary Conditions $$C_A(z, 0) = C_{A,\infty} \quad \text{(bulk concentration initially)}$$ $$C_A(0, t) = C_{A,i} \quad \text{(interface at saturation)}$$ $$C_A(\infty, t) = C_{A,\infty} \quad \text{(far field unchanged)}$$ ### Step 2 — Dimensionless Transformation Define $\theta = \dfrac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}}$. The PDE becomes: $$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial z^2}$$ With: $\theta(z,0) = 0$, $\theta(0,t) = 1$, $\theta(\infty,t) = 0$ ### Step 3 — Apply Laplace Transform (w.r.t. $t$) Let $\bar{\theta}(z,s) = \mathcal{L}\{\theta(z,t)\}$. Transforming: $$s \cdot \bar{\theta} = D_{AB} \cdot \frac{d^2 \bar{\theta}}{dz^2}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve the ODE The general solution is $\bar{\theta} = A \cdot e^{-z\sqrt{s/D_{AB}}} + B \cdot e^{+z\sqrt{s/D_{AB}}}$. Since $\bar{\theta}(\infty,s) = 0$ (boundedness), $B = 0$. At $z = 0$: $\bar{\theta}(0,s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$, so $A = \dfrac{1}{s}$. Therefore: $$\bar{\theta}(z,s) = \frac{1}{s} \cdot e^{-z\sqrt{s/D_{AB}}}$$ ### Step 5 — Inverse Laplace Transform Using the standard transform pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s} e^{-a\sqrt{s}}\right\} = \text{erfc}\left(\dfrac{a}{2\sqrt{t}}\right)$: $$\theta(z,t) = \text{erfc}\left( \frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot t}} \right)$$ ### Step 6 — Compute the Flux at the Interface The flux at $z = 0$ is: $$N_A(t) = -D_{AB} \left. \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} \right|_{z=0}$$ Converting back: $C_A = C_{A,\infty} + (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \theta$ $$N_A(t) = -D_{AB} \cdot (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \left. \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial z} \right|_{z=0}$$ From the error function derivative: $\dfrac{d}{dz}\text{erfc}(u) = -\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} e^{-u^2} \cdot \dfrac{du}{dz}$ At $z = 0$, $u = 0$, so $\dfrac{\partial \theta}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=0} = -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot D_{AB} \cdot t}}$ Therefore: $$N_A(t) = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t}}$$ ### Step 7 — Average Over the Exposure Time Higbie's key assumption: each eddy stays for an exposure time $t_e$. The **average flux** is: $$\bar{N}_A = \frac{1}{t_e} \int_0^{t_e} N_A(t) \, dt = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{t_e} \int_0^{t_e} \frac{dt}{\sqrt{t}}$$ Evaluating the integral: $\displaystyle \int_0^{t_e} t^{-1/2} \, dt = 2\sqrt{t_e}$ $$\bar{N}_A = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_e}}$$ ### Step 8 — Final Coefficient Comparing with $\bar{N}_A = k_L \cdot (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$: $$\boxed{k_L = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_e}}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style) **Problem:** Oxygen (A) diffuses through a stagnant film of thickness 0.5 mm into water. The interface concentration is $0.05 \text{ kmol/m}^3$ and the bulk is $0.01 \text{ kmol/m}^3$. Given $D_{AB,L} = 2.1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate $N_A$ using film theory. ### Solution **Step 1:** Apply film theory: $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta} \cdot (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$ **Step 2:** Substitute values (assuming dilute — log-mean correction ≈ 1): $$N_A = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}} \cdot (0.05 - 0.01) \text{ kmol/m}^3$$ **Step 3:** Compute: $$N_A = (4.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}) \cdot (0.04 \text{ kmol/m}^3)$$ $$\boxed{N_A = 1.68 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kmol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Mass Transfer Theories | Feature | Film Theory (Whitman 1923) | Penetration (Higbie 1935) | Surface Renewal (Danckwerts 1951) | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Time Dependence** | Steady-state | Unsteady (finite contact) | Unsteady (renewal) | | **Diffusivity Dependence** | $k \propto D_{AB}$ | $k \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$ | $k \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$ | | **Key Parameter** | Film thickness $\delta$ | Contact time $t_e$ | Renewal rate $s$ | | **Best Applicable To** | Slow, steady systems | Bubble/plate columns | Industrial packed columns | | **Mass Transfer Coefficient** | $k_c = D_{AB}/\delta$ | $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/\pi t_e}$ | $k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ | | **Limitation** | Underestimates at high $Sc$ | Unrealistic fixed $t_e$ | Hard to measure $s$ | | **Experimental Match** | $D^1$ (poor for liquids) | $D^{0.5}$ (better) | $D^{0.5}$ (best) | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Whitman's Two-Film Theory — Concentration Profile Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GAS["GAS PHASE - y direction"] direction TB A1["Bulk Gas: y_A,b (uniform)"] --> A2["Stagnant Gas Film
thickness = y_G
Resistance = 1/k_G"] A2 --> A3["Gas-Liquid Interface
y_A,i and x_A,i
EQUILIBRIUM y* = m·x"] end subgraph LIQ["LIQUID PHASE - x direction"] direction TB A3 --> A4["Stagnant Liquid Film
thickness = x_L
Resistance = 1/k_L"] A4 --> A5["Bulk Liquid: x_A,b (uniform)"] end A2 -. "Falling concentration
y_A,b > y_A,i" .-> A3 A3 -. "Rising concentration
x_A,i > x_A,b" .-> A4 style GAS fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px style LIQ fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45F06,stroke-width:2px style A3 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.2 Three Theories Comparison — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT - System Conditions"] I1["Diffusivity D_AB"] I2["Geometry and Flow Regime"] I3["Fluid Properties rho, mu"] end subgraph THEORIES["MASS TRANSFER THEORIES"] T1["FILM THEORY

Danckwerts 1951

k = sqrt(D · s)"] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT - Design Equations"] O1["NTU and HTU Calculations"] O2["Column Height H = HTU x NTU"] O3["Mass Transfer Rate N_A"] end I1 --> T1 I1 --> T2 I1 --> T3 I2 --> T1 I2 --> T2 I2 --> T3 I3 --> T1 I3 --> T2 I3 --> T3 T1 --> O1 T2 --> O2 T3 --> O3 style INPUT fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF style THEORIES fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00 style OUTPUT fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366 ``` ## 4.3 Two-Resistance Electrical Analogy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RES["ELECTRICAL ANALOGY OF MASS TRANSFER"] R1["R1 = 1 / k_G
Gas Film Resistance"] ---|in series| R2["R2 = m / k_L
Liquid Film Resistance"] R2 --- R3["R_TOTAL = 1 / K_G
Overall Resistance"] end V["Driving Force: y_A,b - y_A,b*
equivalent to VOLTAGE"] --> R1 R3 --> I["Current = Flux N_A"] style R1 fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450 style R2 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656 style R3 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366 style V fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF style I fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6 ``` ## 4.4 Transport Analogies — Heat, Mass, Momentum Triangle ```mermaid flowchart TB CENTER["CHILTON-COLBURN ANALOGY
j_H = j_D = f / 2"] H["HEAT TRANSFER
j_H = St · Pr^(2/3)
Nusselt Number Nu"] -- "Pr = nu / alpha" --> CENTER M["MOMENTUM TRANSFER
Friction Factor f
Reynolds Number Re"] -- "Universal velocity
profile" --> CENTER MASS["MASS TRANSFER
j_D = St_m · Sc^(2/3)
Sherwood Number Sh"] -- "Sc = nu / D_AB" --> CENTER CENTER -. "Predict k from h and f" .-> ENG["Engineer uses heat data
to estimate mass transfer"] style CENTER fill:#FFE599,stroke:#BF9000,stroke-width:3px style H fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#CC0000 style M fill:#C9DAF8,stroke:#3C78D8 style MASS fill:#D9D2E9,stroke:#674EA7 style ENG fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#6AA84F ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Individual Mass Transfer Coefficient" and state its SI units. How does it differ from the overall mass transfer coefficient?** **Model Answer:** The individual mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ is the proportionality constant relating the molar flux of a diffusing species to its concentration driving force across a single phase's film: $$N_A = k_c \cdot (C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$$ **SI Units:** $\text{m/s}$ (when based on concentration) or $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s·(kmol/m}^3\text{)}$. **Difference from Overall Coefficient:** - **Individual** ($k_G, k_L$): Accounts for resistance in **one phase** only. - **Overall** ($K_G, K_L$): Accounts for resistance in **both phases** combined, including the equilibrium relationship at the interface. They are related by: $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State and explain the significance of the Chilton-Colburn analogy between heat and mass transfer.** **Model Answer:** The Chilton-Colburn analogy establishes an empirical equivalence between heat, mass, and momentum transfer in turbulent flow over a surface: $$j_H = j_D = \frac{f}{2}$$ where $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$, $j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$, and $f$ is the Fanning friction factor. **Significance:** 1. Engineers can **predict mass transfer coefficients from heat transfer measurements** (or vice versa) — saving experimental cost. 2. Valid for $0.6 < Pr < 100$ and $0.6 < Sc < 100$. 3. Foundation for designing cooling towers, humidifiers, and dryers where simultaneous heat and mass transfer occur. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the individual gas-phase mass transfer coefficient $k_G$ based on Whitman's two-film theory. Clearly state all assumptions. **(b) [7 Marks]** In a wetted-wall absorption column, $SO_2$ is absorbed from an air-$SO_2$ mixture into water. The gas contains 25 mol% $SO_2$ at the bulk and 8 mol% at the interface. The total pressure is 101.3 kPa, temperature is 298 K, film thickness is 0.5 mm, and $D_{AB} = 1.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the molar flux of $SO_2$. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### Part (a) — Derivation [See **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete 6-step derivation. The key result:] $$k_G = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot y_G \cdot (1 - y_A)_{lm}}$$ **Assumptions to be stated explicitly in exam:** 1. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion 2. Stagnant gas film of thickness $y_G$ 3. Ideal gas behavior 4. Constant temperature and pressure 5. Equilibrium at the interface 6. $D_{AB}$ independent of concentration **Mark Allocation (KTU Examiner's Key):** - Correctly applying Fick's First Law: 2 marks - Stagnant B condition ($N_B = 0$): 1 mark - Integration limits and log-mean: 2 marks - Final expression with units: 2 marks #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Step 1:** Identify given values. - $y_{A,b} = 0.25$, $y_{A,i} = 0.08$ - $P = 101300 \text{ Pa}$, $T = 298 \text{ K}$, $y_G = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - $D_{AB} = 1.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$ **Step 2:** Calculate the log-mean correction. $$(1 - y_A)_{lm} = \frac{(1 - 0.08) - (1 - 0.25)}{\ln\left[\dfrac{1 - 0.08}{1 - 0.25}\right]} = \frac{0.17}{\ln(1.227)} = \frac{0.17}{0.2046} = 0.831$$ **Step 3:** Apply the film theory formula. $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot y_G \cdot (1 - y_A)_{lm}} \cdot (y_{A,b} - y_{A,i})$$ **Step 4:** Substitute. $$N_A = \frac{(1.3 \times 10^{-5})(101300)}{(8.314)(298)(0.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.831)} \cdot (0.25 - 0.08)$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate numerator and denominator separately. - Numerator: $1.3 \times 10^{-5} \times 101300 = 1.3169$ - Denominator: $8.314 \times 298 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.831 = 1.0297$ $$N_A = \frac{1.3169}{1.0297} \cdot 0.17 = 1.279 \times 0.17$$ $$\boxed{N_A = 0.2174 \text{ mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}}$$ --- ### **Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** State the assumptions of Higbie's Penetration Theory and derive the expression for $k_L$. Compare it with Whitman's film theory. **(b) [7 Marks]** A gas-liquid contact occurs in a bubble column with an average eddy exposure time of 0.05 s. Using penetration theory, calculate $k_L$ for a system where $D_{AB,L} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Comment on whether the value is realistic for industrial operations. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### Part (a) — Penetration Theory Derivation [See **Section 3.2** of these notes for the complete 8-step derivation. The key result:] $$k_L = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_e}}$$ **Assumptions (1 mark each in KTU key):** 1. Mass transfer occurs by unsteady molecular diffusion into a stagnant liquid element. 2. Each fluid element stays at the interface for a **constant exposure time** $t_e$. 3. The fluid element behaves as a **semi-infinite medium** during contact. 4. Equimolar counter-diffusion or dilute conditions prevail. 5. Fick's Second Law is applicable with constant $D_{AB}$. **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | Film Theory | Penetration Theory | |:---|:---|:---| | $D_{AB}$ dependence | $k \propto D$ | $k \propto \sqrt{D}$ | | Time dependence | Steady | Unsteady (averaged over $t_e$) | | Applicability | Slow, steady systems | Short-contact equipment | | Experimental match | Poor for liquids | Better for industrial contactors | #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Step 1:** Substitute into penetration theory formula. $$k_L = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_e}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi \cdot 0.05}}$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate denominator: $\pi \times 0.05 = 0.1571$ **Step 3:** Compute the ratio: $\dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1571} = 1.5915 \times 10^{-8}$ **Step 4:** Take the square root: $\sqrt{1.5915 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.2616 \times 10^{-4}$ **Step 5:** Multiply by 2: $$\boxed{k_L = 2.523 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} = 2.523 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm/s}}$$ **Comment:** This value lies in the typical range of $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$ observed in industrial absorbers, confirming that penetration theory gives physically reasonable predictions for short-contact equipment like bubble columns. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Causing Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 ESE:** > 1. **Forgetting the log-mean term** $(1 - y_A)_{lm}$ in film theory → loses 2 marks. > 2. **Mixing individual and overall coefficients** — using $k_G$ where $K_G$ is required (or vice versa) → loses 3 marks. > 3. **Unit inconsistency** — using $D$ in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ with film thickness in meters → loses 1 mark. > 4. **Not stating assumptions** before derivation in Part B → loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Confusing penetration and film theory formulas** — remembering $k \propto D$ vs $k \propto \sqrt{D}$ → loses 2 marks. > 6. **Sign errors in driving force** — flux is always from high to low concentration; check sign of $(C_{A,i} - C_{A,\infty})$ → loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > 🎯 **High-Yield Memory Points for KTU 2024:** - ✅ **Definition:** $N_A = k_c \cdot \Delta C_A$ — flux is proportional to driving force. - ✅ **Film Theory (Whitman):** $k_c = D_{AB}/\delta$ — linear in $D$, steady-state. - ✅ **Penetration Theory (Higbie):** $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)}$ — square-root in $D$, finite contact time. - ✅ **Surface Renewal (Danckwerts):** $k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ — square-root, random renewal. - ✅ **Overall Coefficient:** $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ — resistances in series. - ✅ **Dimensionless Numbers:** $Sh, Sc, Re, Pr, Le$ — know definitions and physical meaning. - ✅ **Chilton-Colburn:** $j_H = j_D = f/2$ — bridges heat, mass, and momentum transfer. - ✅ **Dilute vs Concentrated:** Log-mean correction appears when $(1 - y_A)_{lm} \neq 1$. - ✅ **Interface is at equilibrium:** $y_{A,i}^* = m \cdot x_{A,i}$ — always true (not a resistance). - ✅ **Two-film theory limitation:** Underestimates mass transfer at high Schmidt numbers (liquids). > 💡 **Pro Tip:** When solving KTU numericals, always: > 1. Check whether **steady-state or unsteady** — choose film vs penetration theory. > 2. Verify if the problem is **gas or liquid phase** — use $k_G$ or $k_L$ accordingly. > 3. Look for keywords like *"wetted-wall"*, *"bubble column"*, *"packed tower"* — they hint at the applicable theory.

topics.title: Interphase Mass Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interphase Mass Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Interphase Mass Transfer** refers to the transfer of a chemical species (the solute) across the **interface** that separates two immiscible phases — most commonly a **gas phase** and a **liquid phase** — under the driving force of a **concentration (or partial pressure) gradient**. The interface is a mathematically idealized, two-dimensional boundary across which the solute must physically diffuse to move from one bulk phase to the other.

The phenomenon is fundamentally described by the **Two-Resistance Theory** (also called the **Two-Film Theory** of Whitman and Lewis, 1924), which postulates that:
> *All resistance to mass transfer in a gas–liquid system is confined to two thin, stagnant films adjacent to the interface; the bulk of each phase is assumed to be perfectly mixed.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Interphase Mass Transfer forms the conceptual backbone for the entire Mass Transfer Operations course. Every subsequent module (absorption, distillation, extraction, humidification) relies on the film coefficients and overall coefficients introduced here. Treat this topic as a **CO1 (Apply) and CO2 (Analyze)** weighted topic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Bag in Hot Water" Model

Imagine dunking a tea bag into a cup of still, hot water. Three regions can be observed:

| Region | Physical Location | What Happens Here |
|---|---|---|
| **Tea-Leaf interior** | Inside the bag | Tea solutes are at their **maximum** concentration |
| **Thin stagnant layer** | Water immediately hugging the bag | Solute molecules must diffuse slowly through this film |
| **Bulk cup water** | Rest of the cup | Water is well-mixed by natural convection; concentration is low |

The **rate-limiting step** is the thin stagnant layer. Stirring speeds up mixing of the bulk water but does **not** eliminate the film — it only makes it thinner. This is **exactly** how interphase mass transfer works:

- The **two stagnant films** (one in the gas, one in the liquid) act as bottlenecks.
- The **bulk phases** are well-mixed and offer negligible resistance.
- The **interface** itself offers **zero resistance** — a foundational assumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Example:** In an industrial CO₂ absorption column, CO₂ must traverse the gas film, cross the gas–liquid interface, and traverse the liquid film before reacting with NaOH. Engineers design packing to **thin these films** (larger interfacial area, turbulence) and thereby accelerate mass transfer.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and dimensionless groups are routinely used in interphase mass transfer analysis:

- **Henry's Law Constant ($H$ or $m$):** equilibrium partition coefficient between gas and liquid phases
- **Diffusivity of solute in gas ($D_{AB}$):** typically **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s**
- **Diffusivity of solute in liquid ($D_{AB}$):** typically **$10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s**
- **Schmidt Number ($Sc$):** ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity, $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$
- **Sherwood Number ($Sh$):** dimensionless mass transfer coefficient, $Sh = k \cdot L / D_{AB}$
- **Reynolds Number ($Re$):** $Re = \rho v L / \mu$

> [!NOTE]
> The **gas diffusivity is ~10,000× larger** than the liquid diffusivity. This single fact dictates the design philosophy of absorption columns — most of the resistance to transfer of a **sparingly soluble gas (like O₂, CO₂)** lies in the **liquid film**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Classical Theories of Interphase Mass Transfer

### A. Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923) — *Most commonly tested in KTU*

**Core Postulates:**
1. At the interface, two thin, **stagnant films** exist — one in the gas phase, one in the liquid phase.
2. Mass transfer through each film occurs by **molecular diffusion** alone (Fick's Law regime).
3. The **interface** itself offers **no resistance**.
4. At the interface, the two phases are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** (described by Henry's Law).
5. The bulk of each phase is **perfectly mixed** (uniform concentration).

**Why it matters:** Despite being the oldest, it is the **only theory that gives a tractable closed-form expression** for design and is universally used in KTU numerical problems.

---

### B. Penetration Theory (Higbie, 1935)

**Core Postulates:**
1. Fluid elements periodically arrive at the interface from the bulk by **eddy motion**.
2. Each element **stays at the interface for a fixed exposure time $t_c$** (the contact time).
3. During this exposure, mass transfer occurs by **unsteady-state molecular diffusion** into the element.

**Key insight:** Higbie proposed a constant exposure time, leading to:

$$k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}/t_c}$$

This explains **why higher turbulence (shorter $t_c$) increases $k_L$**.

---

### C. Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts, 1951)

**Core Postulates:**
1. Similar to Higbie — surface elements are replaced by fresh fluid from the bulk.
2. **Crucial refinement:** the exposure time $t$ is **NOT constant** but follows an **exponential distribution** characterized by a **surface renewal rate $s$**.

$$k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$$

**Why it matters:** Gives a more realistic statistical treatment of turbulent contact; used in research and advanced absorption models.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of plain `|`** to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gas-phase flux (film) | $N_A = k_G (p_A - p_{A,i})$ | Flux through gas film |
| 2 | Liquid-phase flux (film) | $N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_A)$ | Flux through liquid film |
| 3 | Equilibrium at interface | $y_{A,i} = m \cdot x_{A,i}$ or $p_{A,i} = H \cdot C_{A,i}$ | Henry's Law |
| 4 | Overall coefficient (liquid basis) | $\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{m}{k_G}$ | Two-resistance model |
| 5 | Overall coefficient (gas basis) | $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{1}{m \cdot k_L}$ | Two-resistance model |
| 6 | Percentage resistance (gas film) | $\%R_G = \frac{m/k_G}{1/K_L} \times 100$ | Identifying controlling film |
| 7 | Percentage resistance (liquid film) | $\%R_L = \frac{1/k_L}{1/K_L} \times 100$ | Identifying controlling film |
| 8 | Flux expressed via $K_L$ | $N_A = K_L (C_A^* - C_A)$ | Where $C_A^* = p_A / H$ |
| 9 | Flux expressed via $K_G$ | $N_A = K_G (p_A - p_A^*)$ | Where $p_A^* = H \cdot C_A$ |
| 10 | Higbie penetration model | $k_L = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_c}}$ | Penetration theory |
| 11 | Danckwerts surface renewal | $k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ | Surface renewal theory |
| 12 | Film theory expression | $k_L^{film} = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta_L}$ | Two-film theory |
| 13 | Flux continuity at steady state | $N_A = k_G(p_A - p_{A,i}) = k_L(C_{A,i} - C_A)$ | The key linking equation |
| 14 | Equilibrium line slope | $m = \dfrac{dy^*}{dx} = \dfrac{H}{P_{total}}$ (dilute systems) | Henry's slope |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to remember (KTU standard):**
> - $k_G$ → kmol/(m²·s·Pa) or kmol/(m²·s·atm)
> - $k_L$ → m/s
> - $K_L$ → m/s
> - $K_G$ → kmol/(m²·s·Pa)
> - $m$ (slope) → dimensionless when $y$ and $x$ are mole fractions

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Absorption Columns (CO₂ capture, ammonia absorption):** Determines the **Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU)** and **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)**.
2. **Distillation:** McCabe–Thiele construction depends on the **slope of the operating vs. equilibrium line**, which is in turn governed by overall mass transfer coefficients.
3. **Liquid–Liquid Extraction:** Solvent selection aims to minimize the controlling film resistance — typically the **continuous phase film**.
4. **Membrane Separations:** Even membranes are analyzed using a **resistance-in-series** model analogous to the two-film theory (with membrane permeability replacing $k_L$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Overall ($K_L$) and Individual ($k_L$, $k_G$) Coefficients

This is the **most important derivation** in Module 1 and is **frequently asked for 7–10 marks** in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Write the flux through each film

At steady state, the molar flux $N_A$ is constant across both films:

$$
N_A = k_G \, (p_A - p_{A,i}) \tag{1}
$$

$$
N_A = k_L \, (C_{A,i} - C_A) \tag{2}
$$

**Logic:** Equation (1) says the flux is proportional to the partial-pressure driving force across the gas film. Equation (2) says it is proportional to the concentration driving force across the liquid film.

---

### Step 2 — Express the overall flux using $K_L$

By definition, $K_L$ relates the flux to the **overall** liquid-phase driving force:

$$
N_A = K_L \, (C_A^* - C_A) \tag{3}
$$

where $C_A^*$ is the **hypothetical liquid concentration that would be in equilibrium with the bulk gas partial pressure $p_A$**:

$$
C_A^* = \frac{p_A}{H} \tag{4}
$$

**Logic:** We collapse the two-film problem into a single "lumped" film whose resistance is $1/K_L$.

---

### Step 3 — Apply Henry's Law at the interface

At the interface, the gas and liquid are in equilibrium:

$$
p_{A,i} = H \cdot C_{A,i} \tag{5}
$$

Equivalently, in terms of mole-fraction slope:

$$
y_{A,i} = m \cdot x_{A,i} \tag{6}
$$

**Logic:** This is the thermodynamic closure condition — without it, the two-film problem is under-determined.

---

### Step 4 — Express $p_A$ in terms of concentrations

From Equation (4):

$$
p_A = H \cdot C_A^* \tag{7}
$$

From Equation (5):

$$
p_{A,i} = H \cdot C_{A,i} \tag{8}
$$

Subtract (8) from (7):

$$
p_A - p_{A,i} = H \, (C_A^* - C_{A,i}) \tag{9}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Substitute into Equation (1) and rearrange

From (1) and (9):

$$
N_A = k_G \cdot H \, (C_A^* - C_{A,i}) \tag{10}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_A^* - C_{A,i} = \frac{N_A}{k_G \cdot H} \tag{11}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Express $C_{A,i}$ from Equation (2)

From (2):

$$
C_{A,i} - C_A = \frac{N_A}{k_L} \tag{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{A,i} = C_A + \frac{N_A}{k_L} \tag{13}
$$

---

### Step 7 — Combine (11) and (13) to eliminate $C_{A,i}$

Substitute (13) into (11):

$$
C_A^* - \left[ C_A + \frac{N_A}{k_L} \right] = \frac{N_A}{k_G \cdot H}
$$

Expand:

$$
(C_A^* - C_A) - \frac{N_A}{k_L} = \frac{N_A}{k_G \cdot H}
$$

Bring the flux terms to one side:

$$
C_A^* - C_A = \frac{N_A}{k_L} + \frac{N_A}{k_G \cdot H}
$$

Factor out $N_A$:

$$
C_A^* - C_A = N_A \left[ \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{k_G \cdot H} \right] \tag{14}
$$

---

### Step 8 — Solve for $N_A$ and compare to (3)

From (14):

$$
N_A = \frac{C_A^* - C_A}{\dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{k_G \cdot H}}
$$

Comparing with (3) — i.e., $N_A = K_L (C_A^* - C_A)$ — we identify:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{k_G \cdot H}\;}
$$

**Logic:** This is the **resistances-in-series** law. The overall resistance equals the sum of the individual film resistances. The term $1/(k_G H)$ is the **gas-film resistance expressed in liquid-phase units** (hence the $H$ in the denominator converting gas units to liquid units).

---

### Step 9 — Convert to the slope-$m$ form (KTU standard)

For a dilute system with gas at total pressure $P$, the slope $m$ is:

$$
m = \frac{H}{P}
$$

Therefore $H = m P$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{k_G \cdot m \cdot P}
$$

In KTU problems the gas pressure $P$ is often absorbed into the definition of $k_G$ (e.g., $k_G$ has units kmol/m²·s and $m$ is dimensionless), giving the cleaner form:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{m}{k_G}\;}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** — memorize it.

---

## 3.2 Analogous Derivation: $K_G$ Form

Starting from Equations (1) and (2), and defining $N_A = K_G (p_A - p_A^*)$ where $p_A^* = H \cdot C_A$, an exactly parallel derivation gives:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{1}{m \cdot k_L}\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A gas mixture containing **5 mol% NH₃** in air is contacted with water at **1 atm** and **298 K** in a packed absorber. At a point in the column:
- Bulk gas: $p_{NH_3} = 0.05 \cdot 101.3 = 5.065$ kPa
- Bulk liquid: $C_{NH_3} = 0.30$ kmol/m³
- Henry's constant: $H = 75.0$ kPa·m³/kmol (so $m = H/P = 75.0/101.3 = 0.7404$)
- $k_G = 1.20 \times 10^{-5}$ kmol/(m²·s·kPa)
- $k_L = 1.50 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s

**Find:** (a) $K_L$ and (b) the flux $N_A$ in kmol/(m²·s).

---

### Step (a) — Compute $K_L$

$$
\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{m}{k_G}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{1.50 \times 10^{-4}} + \frac{0.7404}{1.20 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{K_L} = 6666.67 + 61700 = 68366.67 \text{ s/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;K_L = 1.463 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}\;}
$$

---

### Step (b) — Compute the flux

First, equilibrium concentration:

$$
C_A^* = \frac{p_A}{H} = \frac{5.065}{75.0} = 0.0675 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

Driving force:

$$
C_A^* - C_A = 0.0675 - 0.30 = -0.2325 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

(Negative sign indicates mass transfer is from liquid → gas, i.e., NH₃ is being **stripped**.)

Flux magnitude:

$$
N_A = K_L \cdot (C_A^* - C_A) = 1.463 \times 10^{-5} \times (-0.2325)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_A = -3.40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}\;}
$$

Negative sign denotes direction. Magnitude $\vert N_A \vert = 3.40 \times 10^{-6}$ kmol/(m²·s).

---

### Step (c) — Resistance Analysis (Bonus)

$$
\% R_L = \frac{1/k_L}{1/K_L} \times 100 = \frac{6666.67}{68366.67} \times 100 = 9.75\%
$$

$$
\% R_G = \frac{m/k_G}{1/K_L} \times 100 = \frac{61700}{68366.67} \times 100 = 90.25\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Over **90% of the resistance** lies in the **gas film**. This is typical for **highly soluble gases** (NH₃ has very high solubility — small $m$). For sparingly soluble gases like O₂ or CO₂ (large $m$), the **liquid film** would dominate.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Concentration Profile at a Gas–Liquid Interface (Two-Film Model)

The following block-level architecture flow maps the conceptual structure of the two-film model. Each node represents a physical region or boundary, and the arrows trace the path of the solute molecule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BULKG["BULK GAS PHASE"]:::gasnode
    GFILM["GAS FILM Stagnant Zone"]:::filmnode
    IFACE["GAS LIQUID INTERFACE Zero Resistance"]:::ifacenode
    LFILM["LIQUID FILM Stagnant Zone"]:::filmnode
    BULKL["BULK LIQUID PHASE"]:::liqnode

    BULKG -- "pA high" --> GFILM
    GFILM -- "molecular diffusion kG" --> IFACE
    IFACE -- "Henry equilibrium pAi equals H times CAi" --> LFILM
    LFILM -- "molecular diffusion kL" --> BULKL

    SUB1["Individual film coefficients kG and kL govern transport"]:::annot
    SUB2["Interface equilibrium ties gas and liquid concentrations"]:::annot
    SUB3["Bulk phases assumed perfectly mixed uniform concentration"]:::annot

    SUB1 -.- GFILM
    SUB2 -.- IFACE
    SUB3 -.- BULKL

```

The horizontal axis is the **concentration of A in the liquid (kmol/m³)**. The vertical axis is the **partial pressure of A in the gas (kPa)**. The straight line through the origin is the **equilibrium line** with slope $H$.

```mermaid

    INT_P["Interface Point pAi H times CAi"]:::ptiface
    BULKL_P["Bulk Liquid Point CA equals 0.30 kmol per m3"]:::ptliq
    EQ_LINE["Equilibrium Line Slope H Through Origin"]:::eqline

    BULKG_P -- "vertical drop equals pA minus pAi drives gas film flux" --> INT_P
    INT_P -- "horizontal drop equals CAi minus CA drives liquid film flux" --> BULKL_P

    EQ_LINE -. "passes through interface point only" .-> INT_P
```
