<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define relative volatility. Explain its significance in distillation column design.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Relative volatility ($\alpha$) of a binary mixture is defined as the ratio of the K-values (or equilibrium vapor-liquid partition coefficients) of the more volatile component (MVC) to the less volatile component (LVC):
> $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{y_A/x_A}{y_B/x_B}$$
> By convention, $\alpha \geq 1$.
>
> **Significance (3 key points expected for full marks):**
> 1. It indicates the **ease of separation** of the two components by distillation. Higher $\alpha$ means easier separation.
> 2. It determines the **minimum number of theoretical stages** required via the Fenske equation.
> 3. It governs the **shape of the VLE curve** — the deviation from the 45° line. For $\alpha = 1$, the curve coincides with $y = x$ and no separation is possible (azeotrope).
> 4. It is used to compute the **minimum reflux ratio** via Underwood's equation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**For a binary system obeying Raoult's Law, express relative volatility in terms of vapor pressures. Under what condition is separation by distillation impossible?**

**Model Answer:**

> For a binary system following Raoult's Law, $K_A = P_A^{sat}/P_{total}$ and $K_B = P_B^{sat}/P_{total}$. Therefore:
> $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$
> where $P_A^{sat}$ and $P_B^{sat}$ are the saturation vapor pressures of the MVC and LVC, respectively, at the system temperature.
>
> **Condition for impossible separation:** Separation by distillation becomes impossible when the relative volatility $\alpha = 1$. In this case, the vapor and liquid compositions are equal ($y_A = x_A$), and the VLE curve coincides with the 45° line. This typically occurs at the **azeotropic composition** of the mixture.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]

**(a) A binary mixture of benzene (A) and toluene (B) at 1 atm has a relative volatility of 2.47, assumed constant. Calculate the equilibrium vapor composition $y_A$ when $x_A = 0.30$. Comment on the ease of separation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Identify the system.
- $\alpha = 2.47$, $x_A = 0.30$, A = benzene (MVC).

Step 2: Apply the binary VLE relation.

$$y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) \, x_A}$$

Step 3: Substitute numerical values.

$$y_A = \frac{2.47 \times 0.30}{1 + (2.47 - 1) \times 0.30}$$

Step 4: Numerator.

$$2.47 \times 0.30 = 0.741$$

Step 5: Denominator.

$$1 + 1.47 \times 0.30 = 1 + 0.441 = 1.441$$

Step 6: Final division.

$$y_A = \frac{0.741}{1.441} = 0.5143$$

**Answer:** $y_A = 0.514$.

**Comment on ease of separation:** Since $\alpha = 2.47$ is moderately above 1 but significantly less than infinity, the separation of benzene from toluene is **moderately easy**. Industrial columns for this system typically require 25–35 actual trays (accounting for efficiency of ~50–70%), making benzene-toluene a classic textbook distillation example. The vapor is enriched in benzene (0.514 > 0.30), confirming that benzene preferentially vaporizes.

---

**(b) The relative volatility of an n-pentane / n-hexane system varies from 2.45 at the column bottom (bubble point 80 °C) to 1.95 at the column top (dew point 65 °C). The distillate is 92 mol% pentane and the bottoms are 4 mol% pentane. Using Fenske's equation, determine the minimum number of equilibrium stages required at total reflux. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Identify the data.
- $\alpha_{top} = 1.95$, $\alpha_{bot} = 2.45$
- $x_D = 0.92$, $x_W = 0.04$

Step 2: Compute average relative volatility (Kirschbaum's geometric mean).

$$\alpha_{avg} = \sqrt{1.95 \times 2.45} = \sqrt{4.7775} = 2.186$$

Step 3: Write Fenske's equation.

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W} \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{avg}}$$

Step 4: Compute the mole-fraction ratios.

$$\frac{x_D}{1-x_D} = \frac{0.92}{0.08} = 11.50$$

$$\frac{1-x_W}{x_W} = \frac{0.96}{0.04} = 24.00$$

Step 5: Product of ratios.

$$11.50 \times 24.00 = 276.0$$

Step 6: Logarithms.

$$\ln(276.0) = 5.620$$

$$\ln(2.186) = 0.782$$

Step 7: Compute $N_{min}$.

$$N_{min} = \frac{5.620}{0.782} = 7.19$$

Step 8: Round up to the next integer.

**Answer:** $N_{min} = 8$ equilibrium stages.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain how the equilibrium curve on a y-x diagram changes with relative volatility. Discuss the special case of $\alpha = 1$ and $\alpha = \infty$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Recall the binary VLE equation.

$$y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) \, x_A}$$

Step 2: Tabulate the curve for different $\alpha$ values at $x_A = 0.2, 0.5, 0.8$:

| $x_A$ | $\alpha = 1$ | $\alpha = 1.5$ | $\alpha = 2.5$ | $\alpha = 5.0$ |
|:-----:|:------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|
| 0.2 | 0.200 | 0.273 | 0.385 | 0.556 |
| 0.5 | 0.500 | 0.600 | 0.714 | 0.833 |
| 0.8 | 0.800 | 0.857 | 0.909 | 0.952 |

Step 3: **Sketch description (to be drawn by student):**
- Draw the 45° diagonal ($y = x$).
- Draw a family of curves between the diagonal and the horizontal line $y = 1$.
- Each curve corresponds to a different $\alpha$, with higher $\alpha$ curves bowing more strongly toward the upper-right corner.
- The area between the curve and the diagonal represents the **mass-transfer driving force**.

Step 4: Special case $\alpha = 1$.
The equation reduces to $y = x$, i.e., the curve coincides with the 45° line. **No separation is possible** by distillation. This occurs at the **azeotropic composition** of minimum- or maximum-boiling azeotropes.

Step 5: Special case $\alpha = \infty$.
The equation reduces to $y = 1$ for any $x > 0$ and $y = 0$ for $x = 0$. The curve forms a step. **Complete separation in a single equilibrium stage** is theoretically possible.

Step 6: Conclude.

**Answer:** The equilibrium curve bows more away from the 45° line as $\alpha$ increases. At $\alpha = 1$, the curve is the 45° line and separation is impossible. At $\alpha = \infty$, the curve becomes a step and separation is perfect.

---

**(b) A mixture of methanol (A) and water (B) is to be separated in a distillation column. The relative volatility at the top of the column is 3.5 and at the bottom is 4.2. The distillate contains 98 mol% methanol and the bottoms contain 2 mol% methanol. Calculate the minimum number of theoretical stages using Fenske's equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Identify data.
- $\alpha_{top} = 3.5$, $\alpha_{bot} = 4.2$
- $x_D = 0.98$, $x_W = 0.02$

Step 2: Compute $\alpha_{avg}$ (geometric mean).

$$\alpha_{avg} = \sqrt{3.5 \times 4.2} = \sqrt{14.70} = 3.834$$

Step 3: Apply Fenske's equation.

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W} \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{avg}}$$

Step 4: Mole-fraction ratios.

$$\frac{x_D}{1-x_D} = \frac{0.98}{0.02} = 49.00$$

$$\frac{1-x_W}{x_W} = \frac{0.98}{0.02} = 49.00$$

Step 5: Product.

$$49.00 \times 49.00 = 2401$$

Step 6: Logarithms.

$$\ln(2401) = 7.783$$

$$\ln(3.834) = 1.344$$

Step 7: Final calculation.

$$N_{min} = \frac{7.783}{1.344} = 5.79$$

Step 8: Round up.

**Answer:** $N_{min} = 6$ theoretical stages.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Warning:**
> 1. **Always round UP** the Fenske result, not to the nearest integer. If you get 5.79, the answer is 6, not 5 or 6.79. Marks are lost for rounding down.
> 2. **Do not confuse $x_D$ and $x_W$** in the Fenske formula. A common error is writing $\frac{x_D}{x_W}$ instead of the correct $\frac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \frac{1-x_W}{x_W}$. The latter accounts for the LVC mole fractions in both streams.
> 3. **Use the geometric mean** for $\alpha_{avg}$ when $\alpha$ varies, not the arithmetic mean, unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.
> 4. **Do not use $\alpha$ without identifying MVC and LVC.** If you accidentally invert the ratio, $\alpha < 1$ and your Fenske denominator becomes negative — a guaranteed 7-mark loss.
> 5. **Units are dimensionless** for $\alpha$ — never write units. Examiners deduct for spurious units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — Relative Volatility: Rapid Revision Cheat Sheet**

- ✅ **Definition:** $\alpha_{AB} = (y_A/x_A)/(y_B/x_B) = K_A/K_B$, **always $\alpha \geq 1$**.
- ✅ **MVC convention:** A is the More Volatile Component (lower boiling point, higher vapor pressure).
- ✅ **Raoult's Law ideal form:** $\alpha = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$ — temperature dependent.
- ✅ **Modified form for non-ideal systems:** $\alpha = (\gamma_A P_A^{sat})/(\gamma_B P_B^{sat})$.
- ✅ **Binary VLE equation:** $y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ — must be memorized.
- ✅ **Azeotrope** = $\alpha = 1$, curve = 45° line, **separation impossible by simple distillation**.
- ✅ **$\alpha \to \infty$** = complete separation in one stage, curve = step at $x = 0$.
- ✅ **Fenske Equation:** $N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \frac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \frac{1-x_W}{x_W} \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ — links $\alpha$ to column design.
- ✅ **Average $\alpha$:** Use **geometric mean** $\sqrt{\alpha_{top} \cdot \alpha_{bot}}$ (Kirschbaum's rule).
- ✅ **$\alpha$ is NOT constant** in real columns — it varies with $T$ and $P$ from top to bottom.
- ✅ **Round Fenske result UP**, never to nearest, never down.
- ✅ **Unit of $\alpha$:** **Dimensionless** — never write units.
- ✅ **Underwood, Gilliland correlations** use $\alpha$ as a key input (covered in later modules).
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** Higher $\alpha$ = fewer trays, lower reflux, smaller column, lower CAPEX/OPEX.
- ✅ **Real-world enhancers of $\alpha$:** Azeotropic distillation (entrainer), extractive distillation (solvent), vacuum distillation (lowers T and exploits $P^{sat}(T)$ differences).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Azeotropes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Azeotropes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
An **azeotrope** (from the Greek *a* = no, *zein* = boil, *tropos* = change) is a liquid mixture of two or more components whose proportions cannot be altered or "broken" by simple distillation. Because the vapor produced has the same composition as the liquid, the relative volatility becomes unity ($\alpha_{AB} = 1.0$) and no further separation is possible beyond the azeotropic point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCBBT503 – Module 2):**
> An azeotrope is a mixture of two or more liquids in such a proportion that its composition cannot be changed by simple distillation. This is because the vapor produced has the same composition as the liquid — i.e., $y_i = x_i$ and the equilibrium curve touches the diagonal of the $xy$-diagram.

### Quantitative Identification Criteria
A binary mixture is identified as azeotropic when **any one** of the following equivalent mathematical conditions is satisfied at the azeotropic composition ($x_{1,az}$):

$$y_{1} = x_{1} \quad \text{(compositional equality)}$$

$$\alpha_{12} = 1.0 \quad \text{(unity relative volatility)}$$

$$\left( \frac{dP}{dx_{1}} \right)_{T} = 0 \quad \text{(extremum on P-x diagram at constant T)}$$

$$\left( \frac{dT}{dx_{1}} \right)_{P} = 0 \quad \text{(extremum on T-x diagram at constant P)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Pearl:** At the azeotropic point, the activity coefficients satisfy the strict thermodynamic constraint $\gamma_1 \cdot \gamma_2 = 1.0$ for an *ideal vapor / non-ideal liquid* system. This condition is tested frequently in KTU exams.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview
Imagine two friends — one highly energetic (volatile, low boiling) and one lazy (non-volatile, high boiling). If you mix them in the right ratio, the lazy friend **drags the energetic friend down** so much that the mixture boils at a *lower* temperature than the energetic friend alone (minimum boiling azeotrope). Conversely, the energetic friend can **drag the lazy friend up** to boil at a *higher* temperature (maximum boiling azeotrope). In either case, the escaping vapor carries away the mixture in *exactly the same ratio* as the liquid — so no matter how long you boil, the composition of the liquid remains locked at that magic ratio. This "magical ratio" is the **azeotropic composition**.

| Property | Minimum Boiling Azeotrope | Maximum Boiling Azeotrope |
|---|---|---|
| Boiling behaviour | Boils at **lowest T** in the mixture family | Boils at **highest T** in the mixture family |
| Deviation from Raoult's law | **Positive** ($P_{total} > P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat}$) | **Negative** ($P_{total} < P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat}$) |
| Vapour pressure | Shows a **maximum** at azeotrope | Shows a **minimum** at azeotrope |
| Industrial example | Ethanol–Water (95.6 mol% EtOH) | HCl–Water (20.2 wt% HCl), Nitric acid–Water |
| Distillate vs Residue | Distillate approaches azeotrope | Residue approaches azeotrope |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Origin of Azeotropes
Azeotropes are a direct consequence of **liquid-phase non-ideality**. Raoult's Law for an ideal solution,

$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$$

must be modified for non-ideal systems using **activity coefficients** $\gamma_i$:

$$P_i = x_i \cdot \gamma_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$$

The total pressure becomes:

$$P = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why azeotropes form:** Intermolecular forces between unlike molecules (1-2) are either *weaker* than the average of 1-1 and 2-2 forces (causing positive deviation → minimum boiling azeotrope) or *stronger* (causing negative deviation → maximum boiling azeotrope). Pure-component reference $\gamma_i \to 1.0$ as $x_i \to 1.0$ (Lewis-Randall limit).

### 2.2 Two Fundamental Azeotrope Categories

**A. Minimum-Boiling (Positive Deviation) Azeotropes**

Logical sequence of cause and effect:

- Unlike molecules repel each other (e.g., ethanol OH and water H-bonding competition).
- Molecules "escape" more easily from the liquid surface.
- $\gamma_1 > 1$ and $\gamma_2 > 1$ in the intermediate composition region.
- Total vapour pressure $P$ shows a **maximum**.
- Boiling point shows a **minimum** on the $T$-$x$-$y$ diagram.
- $\alpha_{12}$ crosses unity at the azeotropic composition.

**B. Maximum-Boiling (Negative Deviation) Azeotropes**

- Unlike molecules attract strongly (e.g., chloroform–acetone hydrogen bonding).
- Molecules are "held back" in the liquid.
- $\gamma_1 < 1$ and $\gamma_2 < 1$ in the intermediate composition region.
- Total vapour pressure $P$ shows a **minimum**.
- Boiling point shows a **maximum** on the $T$-$x$-$y$ diagram.
- $\alpha_{12}$ again equals unity at the azeotropic composition.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$ | Total pressure of non-ideal binary liquid |
| 2 | $y_1 = \dfrac{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}}{P}$ | Modified Raoult's law combined with Dalton's law |
| 3 | $\alpha_{12} = \dfrac{y_1/x_1}{y_2/x_2} = \dfrac{\gamma_1 P_1^{sat}}{\gamma_2 P_2^{sat}}$ | Relative volatility (non-ideal) |
| 4 | $\alpha_{12} = 1$ | Azeotropic condition (numerical) |
| 5 | $\gamma_1 \gamma_2 = 1$ | Azeotropic condition (thermodynamic) at $x_{1,az}$ |
| 6 | $\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x_1}\right)_{T,x_1=x_{1,az}} = 0$ | Extremum on $P$-$x$ diagram at azeotrope |
| 7 | $\left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x_1}\right)_{P,x_1=x_{1,az}} = 0$ | Extremum on $T$-$x$ diagram at azeotrope |
| 8 | $\ln \gamma_1 = x_2^{2}\left[ A_{12} + 2(A_{21} - A_{12})x_1 \right]$ | **Margules 2-suffix (3-parameter)** activity coefficient |
| 9 | $\ln \gamma_2 = x_1^{2}\left[ A_{21} + 2(A_{12} - A_{21})x_2 \right]$ | Companion Margules equation |
| 10 | $\ln \gamma_1 = \dfrac{A_{12} x_2^{2}}{\left[ \dfrac{A_{12}}{A_{21}} x_1 + x_2 \right]^{2}}$ | **van Laar** activity coefficient for component 1 |
| 11 | $\ln \gamma_2 = \dfrac{A_{21} x_1^{2}}{\left[ x_1 + \dfrac{A_{21}}{A_{12}} x_2 \right]^{2}}$ | **van Laar** activity coefficient for component 2 |
| 12 | $\gamma_{1}^{\infty} = \exp(A_{12})$ | Infinite-dilution activity coefficient (Margules form) |
| 13 | $A = \ln \gamma_{1}^{\infty} = \dfrac{B}{RT}$ | $B$ = interaction energy parameter (J/mol) |
| 14 | $x_{1,az} = \dfrac{1 - \sqrt{A_{21}/A_{12}}}{1 - (A_{21}/A_{12})}$ | Azeotropic composition from **van Laar** (valid for $A_{12}A_{21}>0$) |
| 15 | $T_{az} = \dfrac{(x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat})}{(1-x_1)\gamma_2 P_2^{sat}}$ rearranged via Clausius-Clapeyron | Used to predict azeotropic T at given $P$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Azeotrope identification is the *first diagnostic step* in distillation column design. If an azeotrope exists between key components, the engineer must specify a *special separation technique* (extractive distillation, pressure-swing distillation, or heterogeneous azeotropic distillation) rather than a conventional column, or else guarantee the *maximum achievable purity* of the column top/bottom product.

### 2.4 Distillation Behaviour at an Azeotrope
At the azeotropic composition:

- The **operating line** of a McCabe-Thiele stage calculation intersects the equilibrium curve exactly at the **diagonal** ($y = x$).
- An **infinite number of theoretical stages** is required to cross the azeotropic pin-point.
- Practically, the column will *accumulate* the azeotropic composition at the appropriate end (top for minimum boiling, bottom for maximum boiling), and the other end will be pure-component.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Azeotropic Condition from First Principles

**Starting point:** Modified Raoult's law (component 1):

$$y_1 = \frac{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}}{P}$$

Substituting $P$ from the total-pressure relation $P = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$:

$$y_1 = \frac{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}}{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}}$$

**Step 1 – Apply azeotropic criterion $y_1 = x_1$:**

$$x_1 = \frac{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}}{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}}$$

**Step 2 – Cross-multiply to eliminate denominator:**

$$x_1 \cdot \left( x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat} \right) = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}$$

**Step 3 – Expand the left-hand side and cancel $x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}$ on both sides:**

$$x_1^{2} \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_1 x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat} = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}$$

**Step 4 – Rearrange by moving all terms to one side and dividing by $x_1$ (since $x_1 \neq 0$ at the azeotropic composition):**

$$x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} - x_1^{2} \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = x_1 x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$$

$$x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} (1 - x_1) = x_1 x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$$

**Step 5 – Recognise that $1 - x_1 = x_2$ in a binary system:**

$$x_1 x_2 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = x_1 x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$$

**Step 6 – Cancel $x_1 x_2$ from both sides (since both are non-zero away from pure components):**

$$\gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$$

**Step 7 – Rearrange into the canonical form of the azeotropic condition:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_2} = \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}}\;}$$

This is the **fundamental azeotropic equation** for a non-ideal binary at constant temperature.

### 3.2 Derivation of Azeotropic Composition using van Laar Equation

The van Laar equations for the two activity coefficients are:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12} x_2^{2}}{\left[ \dfrac{A_{12}}{A_{21}} x_1 + x_2 \right]^{2}}$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21} x_1^{2}}{\left[ x_1 + \dfrac{A_{21}}{A_{12}} x_2 \right]^{2}}$$

At the azeotropic point, the relation derived above is $\gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$, which we re-write as:

$$\frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_2} = \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}}$$

Define the **azeotropic pressure ratio** as $K = \dfrac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}}$.

**Step 1 – Solve for $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ explicitly:**

$$\gamma_1 = \exp\!\left[ \frac{A_{12} x_2^{2}}{\left( \dfrac{A_{12}}{A_{21}} x_1 + x_2 \right)^{2}} \right]$$

$$\gamma_2 = \exp\!\left[ \frac{A_{21} x_1^{2}}{\left( x_1 + \dfrac{A_{21}}{A_{12}} x_2 \right)^{2}} \right]$$

**Step 2 – Take ratio $\gamma_1 / \gamma_2$ and equate to $K$:**

$$K = \exp\!\left\{ \frac{A_{12} x_2^{2}}{\left( \dfrac{A_{12}}{A_{21}} x_1 + x_2 \right)^{2}} - \frac{A_{21} x_1^{2}}{\left( x_1 + \dfrac{A_{21}}{A_{12}} x_2 \right)^{2}} \right\}$$

**Step 3 – Apply the relation $x_2 = 1 - x_1$ in a binary:**

Let $x_1 = X$ and $x_2 = 1 - X$. After algebraic manipulation (replacing the ratio of $A$-values with the constant $k = A_{21}/A_{12}$ and simplifying the two denominators which both reduce to $\left(1 + (k-1)X\right)^{2}$):

$$\ln K = \frac{A_{12}(1-X)^{2} - A_{21} X^{2}}{\left[ 1 + (k-1)X \right]^{2}}$$

**Step 4 – Take the square root of both sides (since the denominator inside the van Laar form is squared):**

$$\pm\sqrt{\ln K} \cdot \left[ 1 + (k-1)X \right] = \sqrt{A_{12}}(1-X) - \sqrt{A_{21}}\,X$$

**Step 5 – Rearrange as a linear equation in $X$:**

$$\pm\sqrt{\ln K} + \left[ (k-1)\sqrt{\ln K} + \sqrt{A_{12}} + \sqrt{A_{21}} \right] X = \sqrt{A_{12}}$$

**Step 6 – Solve for $X \equiv x_{1,az}$:**

$$\boxed{\;x_{1,az} = \frac{\sqrt{A_{12}} - \sqrt{\ln K}}{(k-1)\sqrt{\ln K} + \sqrt{A_{12}} + \sqrt{A_{21}}}\;}$$

For the special case where $A_{12} = A_{21}$ (symmetric system), this simplifies to the celebrated KTU textbook result:

$$x_{1,az} = \frac{1 - \sqrt{A_{21}/A_{12}}}{1 - (A_{21}/A_{12})}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Question Type)

**Problem:** At 1 atm, the vapour pressures of ethanol (1) and water (2) are $P_1^{sat} = 1.013$ bar and $P_2^{sat} = 1.013$ bar (both at the azeotropic boiling temperature of 78.2 °C, by definition they equal the total pressure). For the Margules 2-suffix model, the interaction parameters at this temperature are $A_{12} = 1.50$ and $A_{21} = 0.90$. Estimate the azeotropic composition using the simplified relation above.

**Solution:**

$$K = \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}} = 1.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln K = 0$$

When $\ln K = 0$ (equal pure-component vapour pressures), the numerator of the boxed expression becomes $\sqrt{A_{12}} - 0 = \sqrt{1.50}$:

$$\text{Numerator} = \sqrt{1.50} = 1.2247$$

Denominator (note: $k - 1 = A_{21}/A_{12} - 1 = 0.6 - 1 = -0.4$):

$$\text{Denominator} = (-0.4)(0) + \sqrt{1.50} + \sqrt{0.90} = 1.2247 + 0.9487 = 2.1734$$

Therefore:

$$x_{1,az} = \frac{1.2247}{2.1734} = 0.5635$$

Compare with the **experimental value of 0.894 mole fraction ethanol** (or 95.6 wt%). The deviation confirms the well-known limitation that the simple Margules form under-predicts azeotropic compositions for highly hydrogen-bonded systems; the **Wilson** or **NRTL** model is preferred for alcohol-water systems in industrial practice.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Predicting Azeotrope Type from $\gamma_i$ Behaviour

| Observation on $\gamma_1, \gamma_2$ | Total Pressure Curve | Boiling Point Curve | Azeotrope Type | Direction of Deviation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\gamma_1 > 1$, $\gamma_2 > 1$ (both positive deviation) | Maximum in $P$ | Minimum in $T$ | **Minimum boiling** | **Positive** from Raoult's law |
| $\gamma_1 < 1$, $\gamma_2 < 1$ (both negative deviation) | Minimum in $P$ | Maximum in $T$ | **Maximum boiling** | **Negative** from Raoult's law |
| $\gamma_1 = \gamma_2 = 1$ everywhere | Linear (ideal) | Linear (ideal) | **No azeotrope** | Ideal behaviour |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Distillation Pinch Phenomenon

The following diagram illustrates *why* simple distillation becomes impossible once the mixture reaches its azeotropic composition. Notice the equilibrium curve and the 45° diagonal meeting exactly at the azeotropic pinch-point, which makes any McCabe-Thiele stepping horizontal.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Feed Mixture xF] --> B[Simple Distillation Column]
    B --> C{Is xF == x_az?}
    C -- No --> D[Proceed with normal distillation]
    D --> E[Top product approaches x_az OR Bottom product approaches x_az]
    E --> F{Are we blocked at azeotrope?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Switch to Special Separation]
    G --> G1[Extractive Distillation]
    G --> G2[Pressure-Swing Distillation]
    G --> G3[Heterogeneous Azeotropic Distillation]
    F -- No --> H[Pure components obtained]
    C -- Yes --> G
```

### 4.2 Phase Diagram Architecture (P-x-y at constant T and T-x-y at constant P)

The following block diagram represents the architecture of the two complementary phase diagrams used to identify and characterise azeotropes.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CONST_T_DIAGRAM[Constant Temperature Pxy Diagram]
        PA[x1 = 0] --> PB[Liquid Line starts at P2sat]
        PB --> PC[P Total Curve Shows Maximum or Minimum]
        PC --> PD[Vapour Line lies BELOW or ABOVE Liquid Line]
        PD --> PE[x1 = 1 ends at P1sat]
    end
    subgraph CONST_P_DIAGRAM[Constant Pressure Txy Diagram]
        TA[x1 = 0] --> TB[Liquid Line starts at T2b]
        TB --> TC[T Boiling Curve Shows Minimum or Maximum]
        TC --> TD[Vapour Line touches Liquid Line at Azeotrope]
        TD --> TE[x1 = 1 ends at T1b]
    end
    PC -.->|Same mixture, complementary view| TC
    PD -.->|Touches diagonal at azeotrope| TD
```

### 4.3 Process Flowsheet — Heterogeneous Azeotropic Distillation (Ethanol-Water Case)

This block diagram summarises how an industry breaks the ethanol-water azeotrope using benzene as an entrainer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    F[Fermentation Broth Feed 8 wt% Ethanol] --> PRC[Pre-Concentration Column]
    PRC --> T1[Top: 95.6 wt% EtOH Azeotrope]
    PRC --> B1[Bottom: Water Waste]
    T1 --> ADC[Azeotropic Distillation Column]
    ADC --> T2[Top: Benzene-Ethanol-Water Ternary Two-Phase Distillate]
    ADC --> B2[Bottom: Anhydrous Ethanol 99.8 wt%]
    T2 --> DEC[Decanter]
    DEC --> ORG[Organic Phase Rich in Benzene Recycle to ADC]
    DEC --> AQ[Aqueous Phase Returns to PRC]
```

### 4.4 Identification Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Binary Liquid Mixture] --> Q1{Compute total pressure P at constant T?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{P shows an extremum?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Compute bubble T at constant P?}
    Q2 -- Maximum --> MIN[Minimum Boiling Azeotrope]
    Q2 -- Minimum --> MAX[Maximum Boiling Azeotrope]
    Q2 -- None --> IDEAL[Ideal - No Azeotrope]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{T shows an extremum?}
    Q3 -- No --> IDEAL
    Q4 -- Minimum --> MIN
    Q4 -- Maximum --> MAX
    Q4 -- None --> IDEAL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define an azeotrope. State the mathematical criteria that must be satisfied at the azeotropic composition.

**Model Answer:**

An azeotrope is a liquid mixture of two or more components that boils at a constant temperature and produces a vapour having the same composition as the liquid. As a result, the mixture cannot be further separated by simple distillation.

At the azeotropic composition, the following equivalent conditions are simultaneously satisfied:

1. $y_1 = x_1$ (vapour and liquid compositions are identical),
2. Relative volatility $\alpha_{12} = 1.0$,
3. Thermodynamic condition $\gamma_1 \cdot \gamma_2 = 1.0$ at that composition,
4. $\left( \dfrac{dP}{dx_1} \right)_T = 0$ or $\left( \dfrac{dT}{dx_1} \right)_P = 0$ (extremum on phase diagram).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between minimum-boiling and maximum-boiling azeotropes with one industrial example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Minimum Boiling Azeotrope | Maximum Boiling Azeotrope |
|---|---|---|
| Boiling Point | Lowest in the system | Highest in the system |
| Vapour Pressure | Maximum at azeotrope | Minimum at azeotrope |
| Deviation from Raoult's Law | Positive ($\gamma_i > 1$) | Negative ($\gamma_i < 1$) |
| Distillate | Approaches azeotrope at top | Residue approaches azeotrope at bottom |
| Industrial Example | Ethanol–Water (95.6 mol% EtOH, bp 78.2 °C) | HCl–Water (20.2 wt% HCl, bp 110 °C) |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is *within* the module. The questions below are independent alternatives.

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the thermodynamic condition for the existence of a binary azeotrope starting from modified Raoult's law. Show that at the azeotropic composition, $\gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$.

**(b)** Using the van Laar equation, derive an expression for the azeotropic composition in terms of the van Laar constants $A_{12}$ and $A_{21}$. For the system acetone(1)–chloroform(2), $A_{12} = 0.84$ and $A_{21} = -0.61$ at 1 atm, and the azeotrope is known to be a maximum-boiling type. Estimate the azeotropic composition using the simplified formula $x_{1,az} = (1 - \sqrt{A_{21}/A_{12}})/(1 - A_{21}/A_{12})$. Comment on whether the result is consistent with the known behaviour.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Full derivation as given in **Section 3.1** above. Starting from:

$$y_1 = \frac{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat}}{x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}}$$

Setting $y_1 = x_1$ at the azeotropic point, expanding, cancelling $x_1 x_2$ terms, and using $x_1 + x_2 = 1$, we obtain:

$$\gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = \gamma_2 P_2^{sat} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_2} = \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}}$$

This is the **fundamental azeotropic equation** — 7 marks.

**(b)** Use the simplified van Laar azeotropic composition expression:

$$x_{1,az} = \frac{1 - \sqrt{A_{21}/A_{12}}}{1 - A_{21}/A_{12}}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\frac{A_{21}}{A_{12}} = \frac{-0.61}{0.84} = -0.7262$$

$$\sqrt{-0.7262} = i \cdot 0.8522 \;\;(\text{imaginary because } A_{21}<0)$$

Since the simplified formula is only valid for $A_{12}A_{21}>0$ (positive deviation systems), it **fails** for this negative-deviation (maximum boiling) system. The proper general van Laar form derived in Section 3.2 must be used:

$$x_{1,az} = \frac{\sqrt{A_{12}} - \sqrt{\ln K}}{(k-1)\sqrt{\ln K} + \sqrt{A_{12}} + \sqrt{A_{21}}}$$

with $K = P_2^{sat}/P_1^{sat}$ evaluated at the azeotropic temperature. **Conclusion:** The student must recognise that the simplified formula applies only to minimum-boiling systems, and the experimental azeotropic composition of acetone-chloroform is $x_1 = 0.34$ (maximum boiling at 64.5 °C). — 7 marks.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Sketch the $P$-$x$-$y$ and $T$-$x$-$y$ diagrams for (i) a minimum-boiling azeotrope and (ii) a maximum-boiling azeotrope, showing clearly the position of the azeotropic composition, the pure-component points, and the vapour and liquid curves.

**(b)** A distillation column operating at 1 atm is fed with a saturated liquid mixture containing 30 mol% ethanol and 70 mol% water. The mixture forms a minimum-boiling azeotrope at 95.6 mol% ethanol, 78.2 °C. Describe the product compositions at the top and bottom of the column for the following cases:
  (i) using a *theoretically infinite number of stages* (total reflux),
  (ii) using a *finite but large* number of stages (industrial practice),
  (iii) if the column is operated at 10 atm instead of 1 atm.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Sketch the four diagrams:

- **P-x-y at constant T (minimum-boiling):** The total pressure curve $P_{total}$ shows a maximum at $x_{az}$; the vapour curve $y_1$ lies *above* the liquid curve $x_1$, both meeting at the azeotropic pinch.
- **P-x-y at constant T (maximum-boiling):** The total pressure curve $P_{total}$ shows a minimum at $x_{az}$; the vapour curve $y_1$ lies *below* the liquid curve $x_1$, both meeting at the azeotropic pinch.
- **T-x-y at constant P (minimum-boiling):** Boiling temperature curve shows a minimum at $x_{az}$; the vapour curve $y_1$ lies *below* the liquid curve $x_1$.
- **T-x-y at constant P (maximum-boiling):** Boiling temperature curve shows a maximum at $x_{az}$; the vapour curve $y_1$ lies *above* the liquid curve $x_1$.

Marks: 7 for clarity and correct labelling.

**(b)** Analysis using McCabe-Thiele reasoning:

- **(i) Total reflux (infinite stages):** Top product → pure azeotrope (95.6 mol% EtOH). Bottom product → pure water. The pinch at the azeotropic composition forces this limit.
- **(ii) Finite stages:** Top → approach but never reach 95.6 mol%; in practice, ~92–94 mol% EtOH. Bottom → very dilute aqueous solution (close to pure water with trace ethanol).
- **(iii) Pressure change to 10 atm:** Ethanol–water azeotrope composition **shifts** with pressure (typically becomes richer in water at higher pressure). At 10 atm the azeotrope moves to ~95 mol% ethanol or less. Pressure-swing distillation exploits this shift to "break" the azeotrope.

Marks: 7 distributed as 2 + 3 + 2.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that $P_1^{sat} \neq P_2^{sat}$ generally.** Many students substitute both as 1 atm in numericals — they are equal *only* when the azeotropic temperature coincidentally makes them so.
> 2. **Confusing minimum-boiling with maximum-boiling diagrams.** The vapour curve lies *above* the liquid curve in P-x-y for minimum boiling but *below* for maximum boiling. Reversing these costs 4–5 marks instantly.
> 3. **Applying the simplified van Laar formula to negative-deviation systems.** Always check the sign of $A_{21}/A_{12}$; the formula requires both to be positive.
> 4. **Stating "azeotrope cannot be separated by any method."** Wrong — extractive, pressure-swing, and heterogeneous azeotropic distillation are standard industrial solutions. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **Missing units in numerical answers.** Always include mol fraction, wt%, °C, or atm.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Azeotrope = constant-boiling mixture** where vapour and liquid compositions are identical ($y_i = x_i$).
- **Two types:** minimum boiling (positive deviation, ethanol-water) and maximum boiling (negative deviation, acetone-chloroform).
- **Five equivalent mathematical criteria:** $y_1 = x_1$, $\alpha_{12} = 1$, $\gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$, extremum on phase diagram, $\gamma_1 \gamma_2 = 1$ at azeotrope.
- **Origin:** non-ideality in liquid phase, captured by activity coefficients $\gamma_i \neq 1$.
- **Distillation consequence:** at the azeotropic composition, the equilibrium curve meets the 45° diagonal → infinite stages needed → effectively a **distillation barrier**.
- **Industrial escape routes:** extractive distillation (add high-boiling solvent), pressure-swing distillation (shift azeotrope composition with P), heterogeneous azeotropic distillation (form two liquid phases + entrainer).
- **van Laar constants $A_{12}, A_{21}$:** empirical parameters determined from experimental VLE data; sign indicates positive ($> 0$) or negative ($< 0$) deviation.
- **Examination hot-spots:** identify azeotrope type from $T$-$x$-$y$ shape, derive $\gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$, sketch P-x-y and T-x-y diagrams correctly, and discuss pressure dependence.
- **Mnemonic:** "**M**inimum azeotrope has a **M**aximum vapour pressure" (both M's go together).
- **Real-world impact:** ethyl alcohol for industrial/medical use (anhydrous ethanol) *cannot* be made by simple distillation past 95.6 wt% — every chemical engineering student must remember this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash Vaporization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flash Vaporization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Flash Vaporization** (also called **Equilibrium Distillation** or **Single-Stage Distillation**) is a separation process in which a liquid mixture is partially vaporized in a single stage (typically a throttling valve + flash drum) such that the vapor and liquid products leaving the drum are in **thermodynamic vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE)** with each other. The liquid feed is suddenly depressurized (or heated), causing a portion to "flash" into vapor, after which the two phases are mechanically separated.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Anchor):**
> *Flash vaporization is a single-stage equilibrium separation in which a feed mixture of known composition, temperature, and pressure is partially vaporized to yield a vapor product richer in the more volatile component and a liquid product richer in the less volatile component, with both phases leaving in equilibrium at the drum temperature and pressure.*

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you have a sealed pressure cooker containing a mixture of **water and alcohol**. When you suddenly open the valve (release the pressure), a portion of the hot liquid "flashes" instantly into steam. Because alcohol is more volatile than water, the flashed vapor is **rich in alcohol vapor**, while the remaining liquid in the cooker is **richer in water**. If you channel that vapor into a separate cold container and condense it, you have a crude alcohol concentrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Flash" is the key intuition:** A sudden drop in pressure (or rise in temperature) at constant composition causes a fraction of the liquid to convert to vapor *instantaneously* — like steam puffing out of a kettle's spout. We then capture that puff in a drum so it can be separated from the residual liquid.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Equilibrium Constant (Kᵢ):** $K_i = \dfrac{y_i}{x_i}$ — ratio of vapor-to-liquid mole fraction of component *i* at equilibrium. Dimensionless.
- **Vapor Fraction (ψ or f):** $\psi = \dfrac{V}{F}$ — mol of vapor produced per mol of feed.
- **Feed Quality (q):** Describes the thermal condition of feed: $q = \dfrac{\text{mol of saturated liquid formed per mol of feed}}{}$.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** For binary isothermal flash, DOF = **2** (typically *T* and *P* or *T* and *ψ* are specified).
- **Standard pressure for K-value correlations:** Often reported at low pressure (≈ 1 atm) where Raoult's law holds: $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P_{total}$.

## 1.4 Syllabus-Highlight Callouts

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU loves this topic:** Flash vaporization is a *gateway* problem — it appears in nearly every mass-transfer unit (column design, refinery simulation, refrigeration). Mastering it is mandatory.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Flash vaporization is **not** simple boiling. It is a *single equilibrium contact* — one theoretical stage — not a multi-stage distillation. There are **no trays, no reflux, no reboiler** in a true flash.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Step-by-Step Logic

A flash vaporizer consists of the following sequential elements (the "operational anatomy"):

1. **Feed Line (F):** A liquid (or two-phase) mixture enters at composition $z_F$, temperature $T_F$, and pressure $P_F$.
2. **Heater / Pressure-Reduction Valve (Throttling):** The feed is brought to drum pressure *P* and drum temperature *T* (which lies between the bubble point and dew point of the mixture).
3. **Flash Drum (Separator):** A vessel where partial vaporization occurs, the two phases disengage, and each phase leaves in mutual equilibrium.
4. **Vapor Outlet (V, y):** Vapors rich in light key leave the top.
5. **Liquid Outlet (L, x):** Liquid rich in heavy key leaves the bottom.

The underlying logic is built on **two fundamental balances** plus **one constitutive (equilibrium) relation**:

### 2.1.1 Total Material Balance

For a closed flash drum at steady state:

$$F = V + L$$

where F = molar feed rate, V = molar vapor rate, L = molar liquid rate (all in kmol/h or mol/s).

### 2.1.2 Component Material Balance (for each component *i*)

$$F \cdot z_{F,i} = V \cdot y_i + L \cdot x_i$$

### 2.1.3 Equilibrium Relation (for each component *i*)

$$y_i = K_i \, x_i$$

## 2.2 The Rachh-Rice Equation (Core Working Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Rachh-Rice equation is the **single most important formula** in flash vaporization. KTU frequently asks students to (a) derive it, and (b) use it to solve numerical problems.

For a multicomponent mixture, combining the three relations above gives:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{z_{F,i} \, (K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi (K_i - 1)} = 0$$

This is the **Rachh-Rice equation** (also called the flash convergence equation). It relates the unknown vapor fraction ψ to the known feed composition $z_{F,i}$ and equilibrium constants $K_i$.

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why material balance?** Mass is conserved; nothing is created or destroyed in the drum.
- **Why equilibrium relation?** In a well-designed flash drum, the residence time is long enough for the two phases to reach thermodynamic equilibrium, but **short enough** that only *one* theoretical stage is achieved.
- **Why Rachh-Rice?** It collapses three sets of equations (one per component) into a *single* scalar equation in the unknown ψ, enabling iterative solution.

## 2.4 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Analysis

Using the phase rule for a flash drum:

$$\text{DOF} = C - P + 2$$

For a binary system (*C* = 2) with two phases (*P* = 2): DOF = **2**.

In practice, this means we must specify **two** of the following four: *T, P, ψ,* and overall feed condition. The most common specifications are:

| Specification Type | Specified Variables | Solved Variables |
|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal Flash** | *T* and *P* (or equivalently *T* and *q*) | ψ, $x_i$, $y_i$ |
| **Adiabatic Flash** | *P* and feed enthalpy (i.e., $T_F$ fixed) | *T* (drum), ψ, $x_i$, $y_i$ |

## 2.5 Types of Flash Vaporization

### 2.5.1 Isothermal Flash (Constant Temperature)
The drum is maintained at a fixed temperature (usually via a heater/thermostat). The K-values are constants at the given *T, P*, and the only unknown is ψ. Solve Rachh-Rice iteratively.

### 2.5.2 Adiabatic Flash (No External Heat, Q = 0)
The feed enthalpy equals the enthalpy of the products:

$$F \cdot h_F = V \cdot H_V + L \cdot h_L$$

Combined with material balance and equilibrium, this gives **three** equations in *T* and ψ. Solution requires **double iteration** (nested loops on *T* and ψ).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = V + L$ | Total material balance | F, V, L | kmol/h |
| 2 | $F \cdot z_{F,i} = V \cdot y_i + L \cdot x_i$ | Component material balance | $z_{F,i}, y_i, x_i$ | mole fraction |
| 3 | $y_i = K_i \, x_i$ | Vapor–liquid equilibrium | $K_i$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\psi = \dfrac{V}{F}$ | Vapor fraction definition | ψ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{C}\frac{z_{F,i}(K_i-1)}{1+\psi(K_i-1)} = 0$ | Rachh–Rice equation | — | implicit in ψ |
| 6 | $x_i = \dfrac{z_{F,i}}{1+\psi(K_i-1)}$ | Liquid composition from ψ | — | mole fraction |
| 7 | $y_i = \dfrac{K_i \, z_{F,i}}{1+\psi(K_i-1)}$ | Vapor composition from ψ | — | mole fraction |
| 8 | $DOF = C - P + 2$ | Gibbs phase rule | — | — |
| 9 | $K_i = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$ | Raoult's law (low pressure) | $P_i^{sat}$ | kPa, bar, atm |
| 10 | $F \cdot h_F = V \cdot H_V + L \cdot h_L$ | Energy balance (adiabatic flash) | h, H | kJ/kmol |
| 11 | $q = \dfrac{H_V - h_F}{H_V - h_L}$ | Feed thermal condition (q-line) | q | dimensionless |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flash vaporization is the **backbone of petroleum refining**:

- **Crude Oil Distillation Topping:** Crude is partially flashed at the top of the column, with the vapor routed to side draws.
- **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) Production:** Multi-stage flash cascades separate methane from heavier hydrocarbons.
- **Refrigeration Cycles:** Throttle valves cause refrigerant to flash, producing cooling.
- **Waste-Water Treatment:** Stripping dissolved VOCs via flash steam.
- **Polymer Devolatilization:** Removing residual monomer from polymer melt.

> [!NOTE]
> Every modern process simulator (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, ChemCAD) uses the Rachh-Rice equation as the **inner loop** of every flash block.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rachh-Rice Equation (Exhaustive)

**Starting point:** Total and component material balances, plus equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Total Material Balance**

The feed stream splits into vapor and liquid streams leaving the drum:

$$F = V + L \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

This expresses conservation of total moles in the steady-state control volume around the flash drum.

**Step 2 — Component Material Balance (for component *i*)**

Each mole of component *i* in the feed must leave either in the vapor or in the liquid:

$$F \cdot z_{F,i} = V \cdot y_i + L \cdot x_i \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

**Step 3 — Express L in Terms of F and V**

Rearranging Eq. 1:

$$L = F - V$$

Substituting into Eq. 2:

$$F \cdot z_{F,i} = V \cdot y_i + (F - V) \cdot x_i$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Right-Hand Side**

$$F \cdot z_{F,i} = V \cdot y_i + F \cdot x_i - V \cdot x_i$$

Group the V terms:

$$F \cdot z_{F,i} = F \cdot x_i + V(y_i - x_i)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Equilibrium Relation**

At equilibrium, $y_i = K_i \, x_i$, so $y_i - x_i = K_i x_i - x_i = x_i(K_i - 1)$.

Substitute:

$$F \cdot z_{F,i} = F \cdot x_i + V \cdot x_i (K_i - 1)$$

Factor $x_i$:

$$F \cdot z_{F,i} = x_i \left[ F + V(K_i - 1) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $x_i$**

$$x_i = \frac{F \cdot z_{F,i}}{F + V(K_i - 1)}$$

**Step 7 — Introduce the Vapor Fraction ψ**

Divide numerator and denominator by *F* and use $\psi = V/F$:

$$x_i = \frac{z_{F,i}}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)}$$

**Step 8 — Obtain $y_i$ via Equilibrium**

$$y_i = K_i \, x_i = \frac{K_i \, z_{F,i}}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} \quad \text{...(Eq. 4)}$$

**Step 9 — Apply the Summation Constraint $\sum x_i = 1$**

Summing Eq. 3 over all components:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{z_{F,i}}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 1$$

**Step 10 — Equivalent Form Using $\sum y_i = 1$**

Adding $\sum z_{F,i} = 1$ to itself in a different grouping gives the more symmetric form:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{z_{F,i}(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 0$$

This is the **Rachh–Rice equation** (also called the *flash equation* or *convergence equation*).

$$\boxed{\;\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{z_{F,i}(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 0\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The boxed equation is the **target answer** for any "Derive the Rachh-Rice equation" KTU question. Memorize it AND the derivation path.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Operating (Lever-Arm) Equation for Binary System

For a **binary system** (*C* = 2), the operating equation gives a straight line on the *x-y* diagram with slope ψ/(1−ψ).

**Step 1 — Component balance for the more-volatile component:**

$$F \cdot z_F = V \cdot y + L \cdot x$$

**Step 2 — Express y in terms of x using material balance only (not equilibrium):**

Divide the component balance by *F*:

$$z_F = \psi \cdot y + (1 - \psi) \cdot x$$

**Step 3 — Solve for y:**

$$y = \frac{z_F - (1-\psi)x}{\psi} = -\frac{1-\psi}{\psi} x + \frac{z_F}{\psi}$$

This is a straight line with slope $- (1-\psi)/\psi$ and intercept $z_F/\psi$ on the y-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric interpretation:** On the *x-y* diagram, the **operating line** (slope $- (1-\psi)/\psi$) intersects the **equilibrium curve** at the point $(x, y)$, which represents the actual compositions of the liquid and vapor leaving the drum. The feed composition $z_F$ lies on the operating line at the point where it crosses the diagonal.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Energy Balance for Adiabatic Flash

**Energy balance around the drum** (steady state, no shaft work, $Q = 0$):

$$F \cdot h_F(T_F, P) = V \cdot H_V(T, P) + L \cdot h_L(T, P)$$

Dividing by *F* and using $\psi = V/F$:

$$h_F = \psi \cdot H_V + (1 - \psi) \cdot h_L$$

Solving for ψ:

$$\psi = \frac{h_F - h_L}{H_V - h_L} \quad \text{...(Eq. 5)}$$

This is the **enthalpic form of the operating equation**. Combined with the Rachh-Rice equation, we have two equations in two unknowns (*T* and ψ), solved by **nested iteration**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Binary, Isothermal)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A binary mixture of n-pentane (1) and n-hexane (2) with $z_F = 0.5$ mole fraction pentane is flashed isothermally at *T* = 100 °C and *P* = 1 atm. The equilibrium constants are $K_1 = 2.1$ and $K_2 = 0.4$. Find ψ, $x_1$, $y_1$.

**Step 1 — Write the Rachh-Rice equation for 2 components:**

$$\frac{z_{F,1}(K_1 - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_1 - 1)} + \frac{z_{F,2}(K_2 - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_2 - 1)} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\frac{0.5 \cdot (2.1 - 1)}{1 + \psi(2.1 - 1)} + \frac{0.5 \cdot (0.4 - 1)}{1 + \psi(0.4 - 1)} = 0$$

$$\frac{0.55}{1 + 1.1\,\psi} - \frac{0.30}{1 - 0.6\,\psi} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$0.55 \cdot (1 - 0.6\,\psi) = 0.30 \cdot (1 + 1.1\,\psi)$$

$$0.55 - 0.33\,\psi = 0.30 + 0.33\,\psi$$

$$0.25 = 0.66\,\psi$$

$$\psi = 0.3788 \;\;(\approx 0.379)$$

**Step 4 — Compute $x_1$ using Eq. 3:**

$$x_1 = \frac{z_{F,1}}{1 + \psi(K_1 - 1)} = \frac{0.5}{1 + 0.3788 \cdot 1.1} = \frac{0.5}{1.4167} = 0.353$$

**Step 5 — Compute $y_1$ using equilibrium:**

$$y_1 = K_1 \cdot x_1 = 2.1 \cdot 0.353 = 0.741$$

**Step 6 — Verify by material balance:**

$$V \cdot y_1 + L \cdot x_1 = 0.3788 \cdot 0.741 + 0.6212 \cdot 0.353 = 0.2808 + 0.2193 = 0.5001 \approx 0.5 \;\checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** ψ must lie between 0 and 1. If you get ψ < 0, the temperature is below the bubble point (no flash). If ψ > 1, the temperature is above the dew point (all vapor). For a valid flash, $T_{bubble} \le T \le T_{dew}$.

## 3.5 Algorithmic Summary (Isothermal Flash Calculation)

| Iteration Step | Action | Convergence Check |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specify $z_{F,i}$, *T*, *P*; calculate $K_i(T, P)$ | — |
| 2 | Guess initial $\psi^{(0)}$ (e.g., 0.5) | — |
| 3 | Compute Rachh-Rice residual: $g(\psi) = \sum \frac{z_i(K_i-1)}{1+\psi(K_i-1)}$ | $\vert g(\psi) \vert < 10^{-4}$? |
| 4 | If not converged, update ψ via Newton–Raphson: $\psi^{(k+1)} = \psi^{(k)} - g/g'$ | Iterate Step 3 |
| 5 | Once ψ converged, compute $x_i$ from Eq. 3 and $y_i$ from Eq. 4 | $\sum x_i = \sum y_i = 1$ |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Process Flow Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FEED F zF TF PF] --> B[HEATER VALVE]
    B --> C[FLASH DRUM T P]
    C --> D[VAPOR OUTLET V yi]
    C --> E[LIQUID OUTLET L xi]
    D --> F[CONDENSER]
    E --> G[REBOILER OPTIONAL]
    F --> H[DISTILLATE PRODUCT]
    G --> I[BOTTOMS PRODUCT]
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**

- The **Feed** stream enters with composition $z_F$, temperature $T_F$, and pressure $P_F$.
- The **Heater Valve** block brings the feed to drum conditions (*T, P*).
- The **Flash Drum** block is the single equilibrium stage where vapor–liquid disengagement occurs.
- The **Vapor Outlet** stream (composition $y_i$) and **Liquid Outlet** stream (composition $x_i$) are the two equilibrium products.
- Optional downstream units (condenser, reboiler) are shown but are NOT part of the flash drum itself.

## 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart — Isothermal Flash Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Input zF, T, P] --> B[Compute Ki using T and P]
    B --> C[Initialize psi = 0.5]
    C --> D[Compute Rachh Rice residual g psi]
    D --> E{abs g less than 1e-4}
    E -- NO --> F[Newton Raphson update psi]
    F --> D
    E -- YES --> G[Compute xi from Eq 3]
    G --> H[Compute yi from Eq 4]
    H --> I[Verify sum xi = 1 and sum yi = 1]
    I --> J[OUTPUT psi, xi, yi]
    J --> K[END]
```

**Interpretation of the algorithm:**

- The outer loop **initializes** a guess for ψ.
- The inner loop computes the **Rachh-Rice residual** and updates ψ via **Newton-Raphson** iteration.
- The convergence check $\vert g(\psi) \vert < 10^{-4}$ ensures the Rachh-Rice equation is satisfied.
- After convergence, the algorithm returns **three outputs**: the vapor fraction ψ, the liquid mole fractions $x_i$, and the vapor mole fractions $y_i$.

## 4.3 Binary Flash — Geometric (x-y) Diagram Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[X-AXIS xi mole fraction of light key] --> B[EQUILIBRIUM CURVE yi vs xi]
    B --> C[DIAGONAL yi = xi]
    C --> D[OPERATING LINE slope psi by psi minus 1]
    D --> E[INTERSECTION POINT xi star yi star]
    E --> F[FEED POINT zF on diagonal]
```

**Interpretation of the geometric representation:**

- The **x-axis** is the mole fraction of the more-volatile component in the liquid, $x_i$.
- The **equilibrium curve** $y_i = K_i x_i$ (or a curved VLE line) lies above the diagonal.
- The **operating line** has slope $\psi / (1-\psi)$ and intercept $z_F / \psi$.
- The **intersection point** of the operating line and the equilibrium curve gives the actual exit compositions $(x_i^*, y_i^*)$.
- The **feed point** $z_F$ always lies on the 45° diagonal.
