# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: State Rayleigh's Equation and explain its significance in batch distillation.**

**Model Answer:**

Rayleigh's Equation is the differential material balance relation governing simple batch distillation:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = \frac{dx_A}{y_A - x_A}$$

**Significance:**
1. It relates the change in liquid composition (x_A) to the change in total liquid hold-up (N) in the still.
2. It enables the prediction of the **amount of distillate** collected, the **residue composition**, and the **average distillate composition** during a batch distillation.
3. It is derived from the assumption of **quasi-equilibrium** between the escaping vapor and the residual liquid at every instant.
4. It forms the basis of design calculations for single-stage batch separations without reflux, including solvent recovery, essential oil extraction, and small-scale alcohol distillation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Equation statement (1 mark) + Significance explanation (2 marks)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between simple batch distillation and continuous distillation in terms of the use of Rayleigh's Equation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple Batch Distillation | Continuous Distillation |
|---|---|---|
| **Applicable?** | **Yes** — Rayleigh's Equation directly applies | **No** — Uses McCabe-Thiele method instead |
| **Operating mode** | Time-varying; no steady state | Steady state |
| **Reflux** | No reflux (single vaporization) | Reflux returned to column |
| **Equation form** | Differential, time-integrated | Stage-by-stage algebraic |
| **Composition of distillate** | Varies with time (cumulative average x_D) | Constant at steady state |
| **Liquid hold-up N** | Continuously decreases | Constant |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A batch still contains 150 kmol of a mixture of 70 mol% A and 30 mol% B, where A is the more volatile component with a relative volatility α = 2.5. The mixture is distilled until the composition of A in the residue drops to 0.50. Calculate:**
- **(a)** The total moles of distillate collected and the total moles of residue. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The average composition of the distillate and the percentage recovery of A. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:** N₀ = 150 kmol, x₀ = 0.70, x_f = 0.50, α = 2.5

#### Part (a) Solution — Moles of Distillate and Residue

**Step 1: Apply the closed-form Rayleigh Equation**

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_0}{N_f}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_f}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_f}{1 - x_0}\right)\right]$$

Note: We use x_0/x_f and (1-x_f)/(1-x_0) to ensure positive terms (since x_f < x_0).

**Step 2: Compute the logarithmic terms**

$$\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{0.70}{0.50}\right) = \ln(1.4) = 0.3365$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{1 - x_f}{1 - x_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{0.50}{0.30}\right) = \ln(1.6667) = 0.5108$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the equation**

$$\ln\left(\frac{150}{N_f}\right) = \frac{1}{2.5 - 1}\left[0.3365 + 2.5 \times 0.5108\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{1.5}\left[0.3365 + 1.2770\right] = \frac{1.6135}{1.5} = 1.0757$$

**Step 4: Solve for N_f**

$$\frac{150}{N_f} = e^{1.0757} = 2.932$$

$$N_f = \frac{150}{2.932} = 51.16 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 5: Calculate distillate quantity**

$$D = N_0 - N_f = 150 - 51.16 = 98.84 \text{ kmol}$$

#### Part (b) Solution — Average Distillate Composition and % Recovery

**Step 1: Apply the overall mass balance**

$$N_0 \cdot x_0 = D \cdot x_D + N_f \cdot x_f$$

$$150 \times 0.70 = 98.84 \cdot x_D + 51.16 \times 0.50$$

$$105 = 98.84 \cdot x_D + 25.58$$

$$98.84 \cdot x_D = 79.42$$

$$x_D = \frac{79.42}{98.84} = 0.8035$$

**Step 2: Calculate % Recovery of A**

$$\% \text{ Recovery} = \frac{D \cdot x_D}{N_0 \cdot x_0} \times 100\% = \frac{98.84 \times 0.8035}{150 \times 0.70} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{79.42}{105} \times 100\% = 75.64\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Residue:** N_f = 51.16 kmol
- **Distillate:** D = 98.84 kmol
- **Average distillate composition:** x_D = 0.8035
- **% Recovery of A:** 75.64%

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**A simple batch distillation unit is charged with 200 kmol of an ethanol–water mixture containing 30 mol% ethanol. The relative volatility of ethanol with respect to water is approximately constant at α = 2.0. The distillation is continued until 50% of the ethanol initially charged has been vaporized into the distillate. Determine:**
- **(a)** The composition of the residue in the still. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The total moles of distillate collected and its average composition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:** N₀ = 200 kmol, x₀ = 0.30, α = 2.0, Recovery of ethanol = 50%

**Step 1: Set up the recovery condition**

50% of ethanol vaporized means the distillate contains 50% of the initial ethanol:

$$\text{Moles of A in distillate} = 0.50 \times N_0 \cdot x_0 = 0.50 \times 200 \times 0.30 = 30 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 2: Express moles of A remaining in the residue**

$$\text{Moles of A in residue} = N_f \cdot x_f = 200 \times 0.30 - 30 = 60 - 30 = 30 \text{ kmol}$$

So: **N_f · x_f = 30** — (Equation 1)

#### Part (a) Solution — Residue Composition

**Step 1: Use the closed-form Rayleigh Equation**

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_0}{N_f}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x_f}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_f}{1 - x_0}\right)\right]$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{200}{N_f}\right) = \frac{1}{2 - 1}\left[\ln\left(\frac{0.30}{x_f}\right) + 2.0 \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_f}{0.70}\right)\right]$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{200}{N_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{0.30}{x_f}\right) + 2.0 \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_f}{0.70}\right)$$

**Step 2: Iterate to find x_f (Trial & Error or Direct Method)**

Combining with Equation 1: N_f = 30/x_f, so N₀/N_f = 200·x_f/30 = 6.667·x_f

Substitute:

$$\ln(6.667 \cdot x_f) = \ln(0.30/x_f) + 2 \ln[(1-x_f)/0.70]$$

$$\ln(6.667) + \ln(x_f) = \ln(0.30) - \ln(x_f) + 2\ln(1-x_f) - 2\ln(0.70)$$

$$1.8971 + \ln(x_f) = -1.2040 - \ln(x_f) + 2\ln(1-x_f) + 0.7133$$

$$1.8971 + \ln(x_f) = -0.4907 - \ln(x_f) + 2\ln(1-x_f)$$

$$2\ln(x_f) - 2\ln(1-x_f) = -0.4907 - 1.8971 = -2.3878$$

$$\ln\left[\frac{x_f^2}{(1-x_f)^2}\right] = -2.3878$$

$$\frac{x_f^2}{(1-x_f)^2} = e^{-2.3878} = 0.0919$$

$$\frac{x_f}{1-x_f} = \sqrt{0.0919} = 0.3032$$

$$x_f = \frac{0.3032}{1 + 0.3032} = 0.2327$$

**Step 3: Verify and find N_f**

$$N_f = 30 / 0.2327 = 128.9 \text{ kmol}$$

**Verification:** N_f · x_f = 128.9 × 0.2327 = 30.0 ✓

#### Part (b) Solution — Distillate Quantity and Composition

**Step 1: Distillate quantity**

$$D = N_0 - N_f = 200 - 128.9 = 71.1 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 2: Average distillate composition (Mass Balance)**

$$N_0 \cdot x_0 = D \cdot x_D + N_f \cdot x_f$$

$$200 \times 0.30 = 71.1 \cdot x_D + 128.9 \times 0.2327$$

$$60 = 71.1 \cdot x_D + 30$$

$$71.1 \cdot x_D = 30$$

$$x_D = \frac{30}{71.1} = 0.4219$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Residue composition:** x_f = 0.2327 (i.e., 23.27 mol% ethanol)
- **Residue quantity:** N_f = 128.9 kmol
- **Distillate quantity:** D = 71.1 kmol
- **Average distillate composition:** x_D = 0.4219 (42.19 mol% ethanol)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** The most common mistake is **flipping the integration limits incorrectly**. When x_f < x_0, the integrand in Rayleigh's Equation is positive over the integration range **only if integrated from x_f to x_0**, not from x_0 to x_f. Students often lose 2-3 marks for this sign error.
> 2. **Forgetting the Average Distillate Composition:** Many students compute D and N_f correctly but then forget to apply the **overall mass balance** to find x_D. This is a separate 3-4 mark sub-part.
> 3. **Unit Confusion:** N₀, N_f, and D are all in **moles** (not mass). For % recovery, the formula is **(D · x_D)/(N₀ · x₀) × 100**, and students often mistakenly use the residue.
> 4. **Neglecting the Closed-Form Condition:** Rayleigh's closed-form solution is valid **only for constant relative volatility**. If α varies, the student must resort to **graphical integration** (Simpson's rule or trapezoidal rule on 1/(y − x) vs x data) — a common test of deeper understanding.
> 5. **Rounding Errors:** Always retain 4 significant figures in intermediate steps. Premature rounding causes errors to propagate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rayleigh's Equation**

### Conceptual Recap
- ☑ **Rayleigh's Equation applies ONLY to simple batch (differential) distillation — no reflux.**
- ☑ It is derived from the **differential component material balance** on the more volatile component (MVC).
- ☑ The differential form: $\dfrac{dN}{N} = \dfrac{dx_A}{y_A - x_A}$ — first-order separable ODE.
- ☑ The integrated form: $\ln\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N_f}\right) = \displaystyle\int_{x_f}^{x_0} \dfrac{dx_A}{y_A - x_A}$ — integrate from x_f to x_0 (lower to higher).

### Formula Arsenal (Must Memorize)
- ☑ **Differential Rayleigh:** $dN/N = dx_A/(y_A - x_A)$
- ☑ **Integrated Rayleigh (general):** $\ln(N_0/N_f) = \int dx_A/(y_A - x_A)$
- ☑ **Closed-form (constant α):** $\ln(N_0/N_f) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\ln(x_0/x_f) + \alpha \ln((1-x_f)/(1-x_0))\right]$
- ☑ **Distillate quantity:** $D = N_0 - N_f$
- ☑ **Average distillate composition:** $x_D = (N_0 x_0 - N_f x_f)/D$
- ☑ **% Recovery of MVC:** $(D \cdot x_D)/(N_0 \cdot x_0) \times 100\%$

### Key Assumptions
- ☑ Quasi-equilibrium between vapor and residual liquid
- ☑ Perfect mixing in the still
- ☑ Negligible vapor hold-up
- ☑ Constant α (for closed-form); otherwise graphical integration
- ☑ No reflux — single vaporization stage

### Physical Insight
- ☑ As distillation proceeds, **x_A decreases** (still becomes MVC-depleted).
- ☑ **N_f < N₀** always (mass leaves as vapor).
- ☑ The distillate is **richer in MVC** than the residue (separation achieved).
- ☑ Larger α → faster separation (less distillate needed for same purity).

### Common Pitfalls
- ☑ Sign error in integration limits
- ☑ Using the closed-form equation for non-constant α
- ☑ Confusing % recovery of MVC with % of total distillate
- ☑ Forgetting to compute x_D separately
- ☑ Mixing up moles vs. masses
- ☑ Premature rounding of intermediate values

### Industrial Applications
- ☑ Solvent recovery from waste streams
- ☑ Essential oil extraction
- ☑ Small-scale alcohol distillation
- ☑ Isotope enrichment
- ☑ Pharmaceutical intermediate purification

### Cross-Connections to Other Modules
- ☑ **Module 1 (Distillation basics):** Rayleigh is for batch; McCabe-Thiele is for continuous
- ☑ **Module 3 (VLE):** Uses Raoult's Law / modified Raoult's Law for y = f(x)
- ☑ **Module 4 (Column design):** Batch columns use Rayleigh as a starting point, but add reflux

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra:** *In Rayleigh's Equation, the integration goes from the **residue composition (x_f)** — the smaller, MVC-depleted value — to the **initial composition (x_0)** — the larger, MVC-rich value. This ensures that ln(N₀/N_f) is positive, as physically required when material is removed from the still.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Distillation - Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Steam Distillation - Applications # Steam Distillation — Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (PCBBT503 — Module 2):** > *Steam Distillation is a specialized separation technique in which steam (or saturated water vapor) is bubbled through a mixture containing a high-boiling, water-immiscible, and heat-sensitive organic component. The mixture boils at a temperature significantly **lower** than the normal boiling point of either pure component, because the **total vapor pressure** of the immiscible pair equals atmospheric pressure at that reduced temperature.* ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners Imagine you have a **dirty, greasy rag soaked in motor oil** sitting on a stove. If you heat the rag alone, the oil will not boil easily — it has a very high boiling point (~300 °C), and by the time it vaporizes, the rag itself may char or burn. Now, suppose you **place the rag above a pot of boiling water** and direct the steam *through* the rag. The steam and the oil vaporize **together** at a temperature near **100 °C** — far below oil's normal boiling point. The mixed vapor travels onward, cools, and condenses into a two-phase liquid where oil separates naturally from water because they are immiscible. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Steam distillation is essentially a *gentle co-boiling* technique. It is the "low-temperature rescue" for separating thermally fragile organic compounds that would otherwise decompose if heated to their actual boiling points. ### 1.2 Standard Engineering Metrics & Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{T}$ | **101.325 kPa** (1 atm) | | Critical Temperature of Water | $T_c$ | **373.99 °C** | | Latent Heat of Vaporization (Water at 100 °C) | $\lambda_w$ | **2257 kJ/kg** | | Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | ### 1.3 Why Steam Distillation? — Engineering Motivation > [!IMPORTANT] > Steam distillation is selected whenever **all three** of the following conditions hold: > 1. The target organic component is **immiscible** with water. > 2. The target organic component has a **high boiling point** ($T_b > 150$ °C) or is **heat-sensitive** (decomposes below $T_b$). > 3. The target organic component has appreciable **vapor pressure** at temperatures near 100 °C. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Governing Principle — Dalton's Law for Immiscible Liquids When two liquids are **mutually immiscible**, each exerts its own vapor pressure **independently** of the other. This is in stark contrast to miscible (Raoult's Law) systems where intermolecular interactions suppress vapor pressure. $$P_{T} \;=\; P_A^{sat} \;+\; P_B^{sat}$$ where the subscript $A$ typically denotes **water/steam** and $B$ denotes the **high-boiling organic component**. ### 2.2 Physical Reasoning — Why Does the Mixture Boil at Lower Temperature? - Each liquid behaves as if the other **does not exist** (no molecular interaction in the liquid phase). - Both contribute their **saturation vapor pressures** simultaneously. - The mixture boils the moment the **sum** of these two pressures equals the external pressure (1 atm). - Since $P_A^{sat}$ (water) at 100 °C = 101.325 kPa, and $P_B^{sat}$ (organic) is *small but finite* at 100 °C, the sum easily exceeds 1 atm at temperatures **below 100 °C**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The presence of steam acts like a "molecular tow truck" — it carries along organic molecules that cannot vaporize on their own at safe temperatures. ## 2.3 Derivation of the Steam Consumption Equation ### Starting Assumptions - Ideal gas behavior in the vapor phase - Two immiscible liquid phases (water phase + organic phase) - Total pressure $P_T = P_A + P_B$ at the boiling temperature $T_b$ - Mole fractions in vapor: $y_A = P_A/P_T$, $y_B = P_B/P_T$ ### Mass Ratio of Steam to Organic Component Using the ideal gas relation, the moles of each component in the vapor are proportional to their partial pressures. Therefore, the **mass ratio** of water (steam) to organic component in the vapor stream is: $$\frac{m_{steam}}{m_{org}} \;=\; \frac{P_{water} \cdot M_{water}}{P_{org} \cdot M_{org}}$$ This ratio is the **minimum steam requirement** per kg of organic component distilled. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_T = P_A + P_B$ | Total pressure = sum of independent vapor pressures | kPa | | 2 | $T_b$ when $P_A^{sat}(T_b) + P_B^{sat}(T_b) = P_T$ | Boiling point of immiscible mixture | °C or K | | 3 | $y_B = P_B / (P_A + P_B)$ | Mole fraction of organic in vapor | dimensionless | | 4 | $\dfrac{m_{steam}}{m_{org}} = \dfrac{P_A \cdot M_{steam}}{P_B \cdot M_{org}}$ | Steam-to-organic mass ratio (minimum) | kg/kg | | 5 | $S_{min} = \dfrac{P_{water} \cdot M_{water}}{P_{org} \cdot M_{org}}$ | Minimum steam consumption | kg steam/kg org | | 6 | $A = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{T} - P_{water}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{water}}{M_{org}}$ | Steam requirement factor (alternative form) | kg/kg | | 7 | $\rho_{mix}$ (vapor) ≈ ideal gas | Vapor density via $\rho = PM/RT$ | kg/m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used in the table above.** Vertical bars in formulas are typeset using `\mid` for absolute clarity in markdown rendering. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical Extraction:** Isolation of menthol, eucalyptol, and camphor from plant biomass without thermal degradation. - **Petroleum Refining:** Recovery of high-boiling lubricating oil fractions from contaminated or oxidized feedstocks. - **Flavor & Fragrance Industry:** Extraction of essential oils (lavender, peppermint, citronella) used in perfumes and food flavoring. - **Spice Industry:** Isolation of oleoresins from turmeric, pepper, and cardamom. - **Wastewater Treatment:** Stripping of trace volatile organics (benzene, toluene) from aqueous effluent streams. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Minimum Steam Requirement ### Given Data (Standard Problem Format) - Organic component (e.g., turpentine): $M_B = 136 \text{ g/mol}$, $P_B^{sat}(95°\text{C}) = 114 \text{ mmHg}$ - Water: $M_A = 18 \text{ g/mol}$, $P_A^{sat}(95°\text{C}) = 634 \text{ mmHg}$ - Total pressure: $P_T = 760 \text{ mmHg}$ (atmospheric) ### Step 1: Verify that the mixture boils at the given temperature We need to confirm whether the sum of vapor pressures equals atmospheric pressure at $T = 95°\text{C}$. $$P_A^{sat}(95°\text{C}) + P_B^{sat}(95°\text{C}) = 634 + 114 = 748 \text{ mmHg}$$ This is **less than** 760 mmHg, so the mixture boils at a slightly **higher** temperature (closer to 96 °C where the sum exactly equals 760 mmHg). For this derivation we accept the data as given by the problem statement. ### Step 2: Write Dalton's Law expression for the vapor phase $$P_T \;=\; P_A^{sat}(T_b) \;+\; P_B^{sat}(T_b)$$ At the boiling point, both liquids are in equilibrium with the vapor, and each exerts its saturation pressure. ### Step 3: Express mole fractions in vapor using partial pressures For component A (water): $$y_A \;=\; \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_T}$$ For component B (organic): $$y_B \;=\; \frac{P_B^{sat}}{P_T}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the ideal gas law to convert mole fractions to mass ratios The total molar flow rate of vapor $\dot{n}_{total}$ relates to the molar flow rates of each component as: $$\dot{n}_A \;=\; y_A \cdot \dot{n}_{total}, \quad \dot{n}_B \;=\; y_B \cdot \dot{n}_{total}$$ The mass flow rate is obtained by multiplying by molecular weight: $$\dot{m}_A \;=\; y_A \cdot \dot{n}_{total} \cdot M_A, \quad \dot{m}_B \;=\; y_B \cdot \dot{n}_{total} \cdot M_B$$ ### Step 5: Form the mass ratio $$\frac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_{org}} \;=\; \frac{y_A \cdot M_A}{y_B \cdot M_B}$$ Substituting $y_A = P_A^{sat}/P_T$ and $y_B = P_B^{sat}/P_T$: $$\frac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_{org}} \;=\; \frac{(P_A^{sat}/P_T) \cdot M_A}{(P_B^{sat}/P_T) \cdot M_B}$$ The $P_T$ terms cancel cleanly: $$\frac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_{org}} \;=\; \frac{P_A^{sat} \cdot M_A}{P_B^{sat} \cdot M_B}$$ ### Step 6: Insert numerical values $$\frac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_{org}} \;=\; \frac{634 \times 18}{114 \times 136}$$ Computing the numerator: $$634 \times 18 \;=\; 11412$$ Computing the denominator: $$114 \times 136 \;=\; 15504$$ Forming the ratio: $$\frac{\dot{m}_{steam}}{\dot{m}_{org}} \;=\; \frac{11412}{15504} \;=\; 0.736 \text{ kg steam/kg organic}$$ ### Step 7: Interpret the result > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** A minimum of **0.736 kg of steam** is required to vaporize **1 kg of turpentine** at 95 °C. In practice, engineers use **20–50 % excess** steam to ensure complete stripping, account for heat losses, and provide driving force for mass transfer. So the actual steam consumption in design would be ~**0.9 to 1.1 kg steam/kg organic**. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Matrix — Common Solvent Examples | Component | $M$ (g/mol) | $P^{sat}$ at 95 °C (mmHg) | $T_{b,normal}$ (°C) | $T_{b,steam\,dist.}$ (°C) | Steam Ratio (kg/kg) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Turpentine | 136 | 114 | 160 | ~96 | 0.74 | | Camphor | 152 | 60 | 204 | ~99 | 2.96 | | Eucalyptol | 154 | 75 | 176 | ~98 | 2.36 | | Menthol | 156 | 32 | 212 | ~99 | 5.55 | | Citral | 152 | 22 | 226 | ~99.5 | 8.07 | > [!NOTE] > **Trend Visible in the Matrix:** As the normal boiling point of the organic component rises, its vapor pressure at 95 °C falls, so a **larger** quantity of steam is required to co-distill each kg of the component. This explains why camphor requires nearly 3 kg steam per kg product. ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Diphenyl Ether **Given:** Diphenyl ether has $M_B = 170 \text{ g/mol}$. At $T_b = 99.5 °\text{C}$, $P_B^{sat} = 14 \text{ mmHg}$. Water has $P_A^{sat}(99.5°\text{C}) = 746 \text{ mmHg}$. **Find:** (a) The boiling temperature of the mixture. (b) Steam-to-organic ratio. **Solution (a):** At $T = 99.5 °\text{C}$, sum of vapor pressures: $$P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat} = 746 + 14 = 760 \text{ mmHg} = P_T$$ Hence, the mixture boils at **$T_b = 99.5 °\text{C}$**. **Solution (b):** Steam ratio: $$\frac{m_{steam}}{m_{org}} \;=\; \frac{P_A^{sat} \cdot M_A}{P_B^{sat} \cdot M_B} \;=\; \frac{746 \times 18}{14 \times 170}$$ $$\frac{m_{steam}}{m_{org}} \;=\; \frac{13428}{2380} \;=\; 5.64 \text{ kg steam/kg diphenyl ether}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** Diphenyl ether requires **~5.64 kg of steam per kg** distilled due to its very low vapor pressure (only 14 mmHg at 99.5 °C). This high steam demand is a key economic constraint in plant design. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Industrial Steam Distillation Plant The diagram below shows the block-level interaction of equipment in a continuous steam distillation plant used for essential oil recovery. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Plant Biomass
Feed Hopper] --> B1[Steam Generator
Boiler Unit] B1 --> C1[Superheated
Steam Manifold] A1 --> C1 C1 --> D1[Steam Distillation
Still Column] D1 --> E1[Vapor Mixture
Outlet Line] E1 --> F1[Condenser
Shell and Tube] F1 --> G1[Two-Phase
Decanter Separator] G1 --> H1[Organic Phase
Essential Oil Tank] G1 --> I1[Aqueous Phase
Water Recycle Loop] I1 --> B1 H1 --> J1[Final Purification
Vacuum Distillation] J1 --> K1[Packaged Product
Storage Drum] D1 -. heat losses .-> L1[Insulation Jacket
and Reflux System] ``` ### 4.2 Equipment-Level Block Description | Block ID | Equipment | Primary Function | |---|---|---| | A1 | Feed Hopper | Stores crushed plant biomass (leaves, stems, flowers) | | B1 | Boiler | Generates saturated steam at 1–4 atm | | C1 | Steam Manifold | Distributes steam uniformly into the still | | D1 | Distillation Still | Primary mass transfer vessel where co-boiling occurs | | F1 | Condenser | Condenses mixed vapors into a two-phase liquid | | G1 | Decanter | Phase separates oil from water by gravity | | I1 | Water Recycle | Returns condensate to boiler for re-use | | J1 | Vacuum Column | Polishes crude oil to desired purity grade | ## 4.3 Process Flow Logic — Sequence of Operations ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Charge still
with biomass] --> S2[Step 2: Generate saturated steam
in boiler] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Inject steam
into still base] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Steam co-distills
volatile oils] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Vapor mixture
rises to condenser] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Condensation forms
two-phase liquid] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Decanter separates
oil and water phases] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Oil collected
water recycled] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Schematic Note:** The vapor-liquid equilibrium in the still is governed by Dalton's Law for immiscible liquids (Section 2). The decanter exploits the **density difference** ($\rho_{oil} < \rho_{water}$) for gravity-based phase separation. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Q:** Define steam distillation. Mention **two** situations where it is preferred over simple distillation. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Steam distillation is a separation process in which steam is passed through an immiscible, high-boiling organic mixture, causing the components to co-distill at a temperature **below** 100 °C due to the additive vapor pressures of the immiscible pair. It is preferred over simple distillation when: 1. The organic component has a **high boiling point** (e.g., turpentine, camphor) and direct distillation would require excessive energy. 2. The component is **heat-sensitive** and would undergo thermal decomposition at its normal boiling point. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Q:** State **Dalton's Law** as applied to steam distillation of immiscible liquids. **Model Answer (3 marks):** For two mutually immiscible liquids A (water) and B (organic) in equilibrium with their vapor, the **total pressure** of the system equals the **sum of the individual saturation vapor pressures** of each component, independent of their liquid-phase composition: $$P_T \;=\; P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat}$$ The mixture boils when this sum equals the external (typically atmospheric) pressure. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]` **(a)** A mixture of water and bromobenzene is to be steam distilled at 1 atm total pressure. At the distillation temperature, the vapor pressure of water is 95.3 kPa and that of bromobenzene is 16.0 kPa. Bromobenzene has a molecular weight of 157 g/mol. Calculate: - (i) The boiling temperature of the mixture if the boiling point of pure bromobenzene is 156 °C. **(4 marks)** - (ii) The mole fraction of bromobenzene in the vapor. **(3 marks)** **(b)** A steam distillation unit processes 500 kg/h of a turpentine-rich feed. Calculate the minimum steam requirement and the total vapor flow rate leaving the still, assuming the operating data given below. **(7 marks)** > Operating Data: At the operating boiling point, $P_A^{sat} = 642 \text{ mmHg}$ (water) and $P_B^{sat} = 118 \text{ mmHg}$ (turpentine). $M_{water} = 18 \text{ g/mol}$, $M_{turpentine} = 136 \text{ g/mol}$. --- #### Model Solution for Question A ### Part (a)(i): Boiling Temperature Total pressure must equal 1 atm = **101.325 kPa**. $$P_T = P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat} = 95.3 + 16.0 = 111.3 \text{ kPa}$$ Since the sum (**111.3 kPa**) exceeds 1 atm (101.325 kPa), the actual boiling temperature lies **slightly below** the temperature at which these pressures were reported. The operating temperature corresponds to the temperature at which water vapor pressure alone equals ~85.3 kPa (so that adding 16 kPa gives 101.325 kPa). From steam tables, water vapor pressure equals 85.3 kPa at approximately **94.5 °C**. > **∴ Boiling Temperature ≈ 94.5 °C (4 marks)** ### Part (a)(ii): Mole Fraction of Bromobenzene in Vapor $$y_B \;=\; \frac{P_B^{sat}}{P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat}} \;=\; \frac{16.0}{95.3 + 16.0} \;=\; \frac{16.0}{111.3}$$ $$y_B \;=\; 0.1438 \approx 0.144$$ > **∴ $y_B \approx 0.144$ (3 marks)** ### Part (b): Minimum Steam Requirement **Step 1:** Verify boiling condition. Sum of vapor pressures = 642 + 118 = **760 mmHg = 1 atm ✓**. Hence the data is internally consistent. **Step 2:** Apply the steam ratio formula: $$\frac{m_{steam}}{m_{turpentine}} \;=\; \frac{P_A^{sat} \cdot M_A}{P_B^{sat} \cdot M_B}$$ $$\frac{m_{steam}}{m_{turp}} \;=\; \frac{642 \times 18}{118 \times 136} \;=\; \frac{11556}{16048} \;=\; 0.7202 \text{ kg steam/kg turpentine}$$ **Step 3:** Compute steam flow rate for 500 kg/h turpentine feed: $$\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.7202 \times 500 = 360.1 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 4:** Compute total vapor flow rate leaving the still: $$\dot{m}_{vapor} = \dot{m}_{steam} + \dot{m}_{turp} = 360.1 + 500 = 860.1 \text{ kg/h}$$ > **∴ Minimum Steam Required = 360.1 kg/h ; Total Vapor Flow = 860.1 kg/h (7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **industrial applications** of steam distillation in **(i)** essential oil extraction and **(ii)** petroleum refining. Discuss the engineering advantages in each case. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Aniline ($M = 93 \text{ g/mol}$) is to be steam distilled at a total pressure of 760 mmHg. The vapor pressure of aniline at 98 °C is 50 mmHg and that of water at 98 °C is 707 mmHg. Determine: - (i) Whether the mixture boils at 98 °C. **(3 marks)** - (ii) The minimum mass of steam required to distil 100 kg of aniline. **(4 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B ### Part (a): Industrial Applications (7 marks) **(i) Essential Oil Extraction:** In essential oil recovery, plant biomass (lavender flowers, eucalyptus leaves, etc.) is charged into a steam still. **Live steam** is injected from a boiler through a sparger at the base of the still. The volatile essential oils, which are immiscible with water but possess appreciable vapor pressure above 80 °C, co-distill with the steam. The mixed vapor is condensed and routed to a **decanter** where oil separates from water by density difference. *Engineering Advantages:* - Operating temperature stays **below 100 °C**, preventing thermal degradation of sensitive terpenes. - Avoids vacuum equipment, reducing capital cost. - Continuous regeneration of steam makes the process economical. **(ii) Petroleum Refining:** In lube oil refining, **wet steam** is injected into vacuum residue or heavy hydrocarbon streams. The high-boiling hydrocarbons (boiling points > 400 °C) are carried over with the steam at temperatures below 250 °C. The condensate is decanted to recover purified hydrocarbon fractions. *Engineering Advantages:* - Prevents **cracking** and coking of heat-sensitive hydrocarbons. - Reduces fouling of heat exchange surfaces. - Allows recovery of valuable high-MW fractions at moderate temperatures. --- ### Part (b)(i): Does the Mixture Boil at 98 °C? Compute the total vapor pressure at 98 °C: $$P_T = P_{water}^{sat} + P_{aniline}^{sat} = 707 + 50 = 757 \text{ mmHg}$$ Since 757 mmHg < 760 mmHg, the sum is just below atmospheric. The mixture boils at a **slightly higher temperature** (≈98.4 °C), but at 98 °C the system is **not yet at its boiling point**. > **∴ The mixture does NOT boil at 98 °C; it boils at ~98.4 °C. (3 marks)** ### Part (b)(ii): Minimum Steam for 100 kg Aniline Apply the steam ratio formula: $$\frac{m_{steam}}{m_{aniline}} \;=\; \frac{P_{water} \cdot M_{water}}{P_{aniline} \cdot M_{aniline}} \;=\; \frac{707 \times 18}{50 \times 93}$$ $$\frac{m_{steam}}{m_{aniline}} \;=\; \frac{12726}{4650} \;=\; 2.737 \text{ kg steam/kg aniline}$$ For 100 kg of aniline: $$m_{steam} = 2.737 \times 100 = 273.7 \text{ kg steam}$$ > **∴ Minimum Steam Required = 273.7 kg (4 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing $P_A$ with $P_A^{sat}$:** In steam distillation, the water phase is essentially pure, so $P_A = P_A^{sat}$. However, students often treat water as a dilute solute and apply Raoult's Law, leading to incorrect answers. > 2. **Forgetting molecular weights in the mass ratio:** The mole ratio $(P_A/P_B)$ must be converted to mass ratio using molecular weights. Many students report the mole ratio and lose 2–3 marks. > 3. **Mixing up immiscible vs. miscible formulas:** Raoult's Law ($P_A = x_A P_A^{sat}$) applies to **miscible** systems. For steam distillation (immiscible), each component exerts its **full** saturation pressure. > 4. **Unit mismatches in $P$:** Always convert vapor pressures to a **consistent unit** (all mmHg or all kPa) before adding. > 5. **Excess steam neglected:** Examiners often award bonus marks for stating the practical design allowance (~20–50 % excess over minimum). > 6. **No verification of boiling condition:** Always check that $P_A + P_B = P_T$ at the assumed boiling temperature before applying formulas. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Know Points Before Exam:** > - ☐ Steam distillation is a **co-distillation** technique applicable to **immiscible** systems. > - ☐ It obeys **Dalton's Law for immiscible liquids**: $P_T = P_A + P_B$. > - ☐ Boiling occurs **below 100 °C** because each component independently contributes vapor pressure. > - ☐ Mass ratio: $\dfrac{m_{steam}}{m_{org}} = \dfrac{P_{water} \cdot M_{water}}{P_{org} \cdot M_{org}}$. > - ☐ Applicable only when the organic component is **immiscible** with water. > - ☐ Used for **heat-sensitive**, **high-boiling** organics (essential oils, fatty acids, turpentine, camphor, aniline). > - ☐ Industrial domains: **pharma**, **food**, **flavors & fragrances**, **petroleum refining**, **biomass extraction**. > - ☐ Equipment chain: **Boiler → Still → Condenser → Decanter → Storage**. > - ☐ Practical design uses **20–50 % excess steam** over the theoretical minimum. > - ☐ Vapor composition: $y_B = P_B / (P_A + P_B)$ — independent of liquid composition. > - ☐ Limitations: Not suited for **miscible** systems, components with **negligible** vapor pressure at 100 °C, or **water-soluble** organics (since they are not immiscible).

topics.title: General Characteristics of Tray and Packed Towers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Characteristics of Tray and Packed Towers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tray Towers (Plate Columns)

A **Tray Tower** (also called a **Plate Column**) is a vertical cylindrical pressure vessel used for continuous counter-current contact between a rising **vapor phase** and a descending **liquid phase**. The contact is achieved in **discrete stages** (trays/plates) where the two phases are brought into intimate contact, mass transfer occurs, and the phases are then mechanically separated before moving to the next stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tray towers operate on the **stage-wise contact principle**, and theoretical analysis is performed using the **Murphree Tray Efficiency** concept (covered in Module 2/3 of PCBBT503).

**Formal Components of a Tray Tower:**
- **Shell:** The outer cylindrical body, typically constructed of **carbon steel** or **stainless steel**.
- **Trays (Plates):** Horizontal contact devices mounted internally at fixed vertical spacing (typically **0.45 m to 0.9 m**).
- **Downcomers:** Liquid transport channels that carry liquid from one tray to the tray below.
- **Weir:** A dam on each tray that maintains a designed liquid level.
- **Vapor Risers / Perforations:** Openings through which the vapor flows up into the liquid.
- **Inlet/Outlet Nozzles:** For feed introduction and product withdrawal.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine a **multi-story parking garage** where cars (vapor) move upward from floor to floor, and pedestrians (liquid) move downward via staircases (downcomers). At every floor (tray), the cars and pedestrians meet briefly — drivers can drop off passengers (heavier/volatile component transferred from liquid to vapor) or pick up passengers (lighter component transferred from vapor to liquid) — before continuing their journey. Each "meeting" is a single mass transfer **equilibrium stage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** In a tray tower, mass transfer happens in **discrete jumps** (stage-by-stage), while in a packed tower, mass transfer happens in a **continuous manner** along the height — like walking up an escalator (packed) versus stepping up staircases (trays).

---

## 1.2 Packed Towers

A **Packed Tower** is a vertical cylindrical vessel filled with **packing material** (small shaped objects) that provides a large interfacial area for continuous contact between the counter-current flowing vapor and liquid phases. There are **no discrete trays** — mass transfer occurs continuously along the entire height of the packed section.

**Formal Components of a Packed Tower:**
- **Shell:** The outer cylindrical body.
- **Packing Support Plate:** A grid or perforated plate at the bottom that holds the packing while allowing both liquid and vapor to pass.
- **Packing (Random or Structured):** The contact medium.
- **Liquid Distributor:** Ensures uniform initial liquid distribution across the top of the packing.
- **Liquid Redistributors:** Placed at intervals to correct maldistribution.
- **Top Demister / Entrainment Eliminator:** Removes liquid droplets from the exiting vapor.
- **Bottom Support / Hold-down Plate:** Prevents packing from fluidizing.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Think of a packed tower as a **tall sponge column** where rainwater (liquid) trickles down through the sponge, while air (vapor) is blown upward from the bottom. The enormous internal surface area of the sponge provides continuous contact between the two fluids. Anywhere along the height, both phases are present and exchanging material — there are no defined "floors" or stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** Packed towers are modeled as **differential contactors** using concepts like the **Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU)** and **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP)**, whereas tray towers use the **McCabe–Thiele method** with discrete stages.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Internal Devices (Overview)

| Device Type | Example | Contact Nature |
|-------------|---------|----------------|
| Tray | Sieve, Valve, Bubble-cap | Stage-wise (discrete) |
| Random Packing | Raschig rings, Pall rings, Berl saddles | Differential (continuous) |
| Structured Packing | Sulzer Mellapak, Koch-Glitsch | Differential (continuous, ordered) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** Tray efficiency in modern columns ranges from **60% to 90%** (Murphree), while packed towers commonly achieve **HETP values of 0.4 m to 0.6 m** for distillation of common systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Trays

### 2.1.1 Sieve Trays
- Flat plates perforated with **uniform circular holes** (typically **3 mm to 12 mm** diameter).
- Vapor flows upward through the holes, contacting the liquid layer above.
- **Simple, cheap, low pressure drop**, but limited turndown ratio.
- Operating range: **Vapor velocity 0.3 to 1.5 m/s** typical.

### 2.1.2 Valve Trays
- Plates with **liftable valve caps** (round or rectangular) sitting over holes.
- Vapor lifts the valves, opening the orifice area proportionally to vapor flow.
- **Excellent turndown ratio** (operates over a wide range of flow rates).
- More expensive than sieve trays.

### 2.1.3 Bubble-Cap Trays
- Each hole is fitted with an inverted cap (chimney + cap).
- Vapor always bubbles out beneath the liquid, even at very low flow rates.
- **Best turndown ratio**, but high pressure drop and high cost.
- Largely obsolete in modern installations.

### 2.1.4 Other Special Trays
- **Dual-flow trays:** No downcomers; vapor and liquid flow counter-currently through the same perforations.
- **Shower decks (disc-and-donut):** Used in fouling services.
- **High-capacity trays** (e.g., Nye, Max-Frac): For very high liquid rates.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Packings

### 2.2.1 Random Packings
- Dumped into the column and settle randomly.
- **First-generation:** Raschig rings (ceramic/metal).
- **Second-generation:** Berl saddles, Intalox saddles.
- **Third-generation:** Pall rings, Hy-Pak, IMTP (Intalox Metal Tower Packing) — significantly lower pressure drop and higher capacity.

### 2.2.2 Structured Packings
- Corrugated metal sheets or gauze assembled in a precise geometric pattern.
- Provide **ordered, predictable flow channels**.
- Much **lower pressure drop per theoretical stage** and **higher capacity** than random packings.
- Standard products: **Mellapak (Sulzer), Flexipac (Koch-Glitech), Montz-Pak**.

### 2.2.3 Key Packing Characteristics

| Characteristic | Definition | Typical Range |
|----------------|------------|---------------|
| Surface Area ($a$) | Wetted interfacial area per unit volume | 100 – 1500 m²/m³ |
| Void Fraction ($\varepsilon$) | Free volume fraction available for flow | 0.65 – 0.95 |
| Packing Factor ($F_p$) | Empirical resistance parameter | 20 – 2000 m⁻¹ |
| Bulk Density ($\rho_b$) | Mass of packing per unit volume | 50 – 1000 kg/m³ |
| Size (Nominal) | Diameter/characteristic dimension | 16 mm – 75 mm |

---

## 2.3 Operating Characteristics — Tray Towers

### 2.3.1 Hydraulic Operating Regimes
For a given tray design and pressure, four distinct operating regimes exist as vapor velocity is increased:

1. **Weeping Regime:** Vapor velocity too low; liquid drips through the perforations. Inefficient contact.
2. **Flooding Regime:** Vapor velocity too high; liquid cannot flow down through the downcomers. Inoperable.
3. **Entrainment Regime:** Liquid droplets carried upward by vapor — reduces effective driving force.
4. **Optimum Operating Range:** Between weeping and flooding; maximum tray efficiency is achieved here.

> [!NOTE]
> The **flooding velocity** for a tray tower can be estimated using the **Fair correlation**, while the **weeping velocity** is estimated using the **Eduljee correlation** — both are KTU-relevant for design problems.

### 2.3.2 Pressure Drop in Trays
Total tray pressure drop is the sum of three components:

$$\Delta P_{tray} = \Delta P_{dry} + \Delta P_{liquid} + \Delta P_{residual}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P_{dry}$ = dry tray pressure drop (orifice equation)
- $\Delta P_{liquid}$ = hydrostatic head of liquid on the tray
- $\Delta P_{residual}$ = residual pressure drop due to surface tension

**Dry Tray Pressure Drop (Orifice Equation):**

$$\Delta P_{dry} = \frac{\rho_V \, u_h^2}{2 \, C_d^2}$$

Where:
- $\rho_V$ = vapor density (kg/m³)
- $u_h$ = hole velocity (m/s)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈ 0.65 – 0.85)

---

## 2.4 Operating Characteristics — Packed Towers

### 2.4.1 Packed Tower Operating Regimes
As gas velocity is increased at constant liquid rate:

1. **Pre-loading:** Both phases flow freely; film flow regime.
2. **Loading:** Liquid begins to accumulate in the packing voids.
3. **Flooding:** Liquid cannot flow downward; the column floods.

### 2.4.2 Pressure Drop in Packed Towers
**Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC, Eckert):**

$$\Delta P = f\left(F_{LV}, F_p\right) \quad \text{(read from GPDC chart)}$$

Where the **Capacity Parameter (Flow Parameter)** is:

$$F_{LV} = \frac{L}{V} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}$$

And the **Gas Load Factor** is:

$$F_{SG} = u_V \sqrt{\rho_V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC)** is the most commonly tested chart-based calculation in PCBBT503 for packed tower design. Students must learn to read the chart for given $F_{LV}$ and $F_p$.

### 2.4.3 Flooding Velocity in Packed Towers
Empirical correlation (simplified form):

$$u_{V,flood} = \frac{C}{\sqrt{\rho_V}} \cdot \phi$$

Where $C$ is a constant (depends on packing type and $F_{LV}$), and $\phi$ is a property ratio involving densities and viscosity.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet covers all key equations tested in PCBBT503 Module 2.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Murphree Tray Efficiency | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{y_{n} - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Overall Tray Efficiency | $E_o = \dfrac{\text{Number of theoretical stages}}{\text{Actual number of trays}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Dry Tray $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P_{dry} = \dfrac{\rho_V u_h^2}{2 C_d^2}$ | Pa (use $\rho_V$ in kg/m³, $u_h$ in m/s) |
| 4 | Capacity Parameter (Packed) | $F_{LV} = \dfrac{L}{V}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Gas Load Factor | $F_{SG} = u_V \sqrt{\rho_V}$ | (kg/m·sec²)^0.5 |
| 6 | HETP Definition | $\text{HETP} = \dfrac{Z}{N_t}$ | m/stage |
| 7 | HTU–NTU Approach | $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} = H_{OL} \cdot N_{OL}$ | m |
| 8 | Flooding Velocity (Packed) | Read from Sherwood–Eckert chart | m/s |
| 9 | Specific Surface Area | $a = \dfrac{A_p}{V_p}$ | m²/m³ |
| 10 | Packing Factor | $F_p = \dfrac{a}{\varepsilon^3}$ | m⁻¹ (approx.) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tray towers** dominate in **refinery distillation** (crude, vacuum, FCC main fractionators) due to high capacity, mechanical robustness, and ability to handle solids/fouling.
- **Packed towers** dominate in **fine chemicals, pharmaceutical, and high-vacuum distillation** (e.g., tall oil, fatty acids, specialty separations) due to low pressure drop, low liquid hold-up, and superior performance with corrosive or heat-sensitive materials.
- Structured packings are now standard in **ethylene plant primary fractionators, demethanizers, and deethanizers** where minimizing pressure drop translates directly into compression cost savings.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Murphree Vapor-Phase Tray Efficiency

**Starting Premise:** On a tray, vapor entering has composition $y_{n+1}$ and leaving has composition $y_n$. The vapor is in contact with liquid of mean composition $x_n$. The vapor composition in equilibrium with this liquid is $y_n^*$.

**Step 1 — Define the maximum possible change (equilibrium):**
If the vapor leaving the tray were in perfect equilibrium with the exiting liquid, the change in vapor composition would be:

$$\Delta y_{\max} = y_n^* - y_{n+1}$$

**Step 2 — Define the actual change:**
The actual change in vapor composition is:

$$\Delta y_{\text{actual}} = y_n - y_{n+1}$$

**Step 3 — Define efficiency as the ratio of actual to maximum change:**

$$E_{MV} = \frac{\Delta y_{\text{actual}}}{\Delta y_{\max}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions:**

$$E_{MV} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$

This is the **Murphree Vapor-Phase Tray Efficiency**.

**Step 5 — Similar form for liquid phase:**
By symmetric reasoning applied to the liquid phase:

$$E_{ML} = \frac{x_{n-1} - x_n}{x_{n-1} - x_n^*}$$

**Step 6 — Relationship between vapor and liquid efficiencies (for dilute systems, $\lambda = mV/L$):**

$$E_{MV} = \frac{E_{ML}}{E_{ML} + \lambda \, (1 - E_{ML})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When the operating line and equilibrium line are straight and parallel ($mV/L = 1$), $E_{MV} = E_{ML}$. The Murphree efficiency is **always** greater than or equal to the **point efficiency** ($E_{OV}$) at the same tray, due to horizontal mixing effects.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate)

**Premise:** Packed towers don't have discrete stages. To compare with tray towers, we define a fictitious quantity — the **height of packing that gives the same separation as one theoretical plate**.

**Step 1 — Define the number of theoretical stages:**
For a given separation, using the **McCabe–Thiele method**, we obtain $N_t$ theoretical stages.

**Step 2 — Define total packed height:**
The total height $Z$ of the packed section is distributed among these $N_t$ stages.

**Step 3 — Definition of HETP:**

$$\text{HETP} = \frac{Z}{N_t}$$

**Step 4 — Express total height:**

$$Z = \text{HETP} \times N_t$$

**Step 5 — Practical example:**
If $Z = 6$ m of packing achieves the separation equivalent to $N_t = 10$ theoretical stages:

$$\text{HETP} = \frac{6 \text{ m}}{10 \text{ stages}} = 0.6 \text{ m/stage}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: HTU-NTU Approach for Packed Towers

**Premise:** For a packed tower where mass transfer is described by a continuous differential model:

**Step 1 — Mass balance on a differential height $dz$:**

$$d\left(V y\right) = -N_A \, a \, dz \cdot A_c$$

**Step 2 — Define the overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient $K_y$:**

$$N_A = K_y (y - y^*)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange:**

$$\frac{dy}{y - y^*} = -\frac{K_y \, a \, A_c}{V} dz$$

**Step 4 — Define the Height of a Transfer Unit (gas phase):**

$$H_{OG} = \frac{V}{K_y \, a \, A_c}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Number of Transfer Units (gas phase):**

$$N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

**Step 6 — Total height equation:**

$$Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$$

**Step 7 — Analogous liquid-phase form:**

$$Z = H_{OL} \cdot N_{OL}$$

**Step 8 — Conversion between HETP and HTU (approximate):**

$$\text{HETP} \approx H_{OG} \cdot \frac{y - y^*}{\Delta y_{lm}}$$

Where $\Delta y_{lm}$ is the log-mean driving force.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Tray vs Packed Tower

| Parameter | Tray Tower | Packed Tower |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **Contact Type** | Stage-wise (discrete) | Differential (continuous) |
| **Design Basis** | Number of theoretical stages | HTU-NTU / HETP |
| **Pressure Drop** | Higher (≈ 0.4 – 1.0 kPa/stage) | Lower (≈ 0.05 – 0.3 kPa/stage for structured) |
| **Turndown Ratio** | Limited (1.5 – 2 for sieve) | Wider (3 – 4) |
| **Liquid Hold-up** | High | Low (good for heat-sensitive) |
| **Fouling Service** | Better (cleanable, accessible) | Worse (packing clogs) |
| **Capital Cost (small columns)** | Higher | Lower |
| **Capital Cost (large columns)** | Lower | Higher |
| **Scale-up Predictability** | More reliable | Less reliable (distribution issues) |
| **Internal Access** | Possible for maintenance | Not possible without removing packing |
| **Best Application** | Refineries, large throughput | Vacuum, corrosive, low $\Delta P$ services |
| **Efficiency (typical)** | 60 – 90 % Murphree | HETP ≈ 0.4 – 0.6 m |
| **Maldistribution Sensitivity** | Low | High (needs distributors) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (KTU Standard):** Choose **tray towers** when liquid rates are very high, solids are present, or column diameter > 3 m. Choose **packed towers** when low pressure drop is critical, materials are corrosive (use ceramic/plastic packing), or heat-sensitive materials are processed.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example — Packed Tower Pressure Drop

**Given:**
- Column diameter: $D = 0.6$ m
- Air flow rate: $V = 0.5$ kg/s
- Water flow rate: $L = 2.0$ kg/s
- Air density: $\rho_V = 1.2$ kg/m³
- Water density: $\rho_L = 1000$ kg/m³
- Packing: 25 mm metal Pall rings, $F_p = 156$ m⁻¹
- Column cross-section: $A_c = \pi (0.6)^2/4 = 0.2827$ m²

**Step 1 — Calculate volumetric flow rates:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{V}{\rho_V} = \frac{0.5}{1.2} = 0.4167 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Superficial gas velocity:**

$$u_V = \frac{\dot{V}}{A_c} = \frac{0.4167}{0.2827} = 1.474 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Flow Parameter $F_{LV}$:**

$$F_{LV} = \frac{L}{V}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}} = \frac{2.0}{0.5}\sqrt{\frac{1.2}{1000}} = 4 \times 0.0346 = 0.1385$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Gas Load Factor $F_{SG}$:**

$$F_{SG} = u_V \sqrt{\rho_V} = 1.474 \times \sqrt{1.2} = 1.474 \times 1.0954 = 1.615 \text{ (m/s)(kg/m³)}^{0.5}$$

**Step 5 — Read the GPDC chart at $F_{LV} = 0.1385$ and $F_p = 156$:**
- At flooding curve: $F_{SG,\text{flood}} \approx 2.4$ (m/s)(kg/m³)^0.5
- At the operating point: pressure drop per meter ≈ **45 mm H₂O/m**

**Step 6 — Operating point as fraction of flooding:**

$$\frac{F_{SG}}{F_{SG,\text{flood}}} = \frac{1.615}{2.4} \approx 0.67$$

This is within the safe operating envelope (typically 50 – 80% of flooding).

**Step 7 — Total pressure drop for 6 m of packing:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 45 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O/m} \times 6 \text{ m} = 270 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O} = 2.65 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Operating at 67% of flooding velocity provides a robust design with ~33% safety margin. The low pressure drop (2.65 kPa) is characteristic of packed towers and is the reason they are preferred in vacuum distillation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tray Tower Architecture (Mermaid Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet: Vapor Stream V_in] --> B[Bottom Tray: Tray 1]
    B --> C[Tray 2]
    C --> D[Tray 3]
    D --> E[Tray 4]
    E --> F[Tray 5 - Top]
    F --> G[Outlet: Vapor V_out to Condenser]
    H[Inlet: Liquid L_in] --> F
    F -->|Downcomer| E
    E -->|Downcomer| D
    D -->|Downcomer| C
    C -->|Downcomer| B
    B --> I[Outlet: Liquid L_out to Reboiler]
    style A fill:#ffcc99
    style G fill:#ff9999
    style H fill:#99ccff
    style I fill:#6699ff
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- Vapor enters at the bottom and rises through each tray, contacting liquid counter-currently.
- Liquid enters at the top and cascades down via **downcomers** from tray to tray.
- Each rectangular block represents a discrete mass transfer stage.
- Arrows on the right represent downcomer liquid flow; arrows on the left represent vapor riser flow.

---

## 4.2 Packed Tower Architecture (Mermaid Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet: Vapor V_in] --> B[Packing Support Plate]
    B --> C[Packed Section 1: Bottom Layer]
    C --> D[Liquid Redistributor 1]
    D --> E[Packed Section 2: Middle Layer]
    E --> F[Liquid Redistributor 2]
    F --> G[Packed Section 3: Top Layer]
    G --> H[Top Liquid Distributor]
    H --> I[Demister / Entrainment Eliminator]
    I --> J[Outlet: Vapor V_out]
    K[Inlet: Liquid L_in] --> H
    H -->|Film Flow| G
    G -->|Film Flow| F
    F -->|Film Flow| C
    C --> L[Liquid Collectors at Bottom]
    L --> M[Outlet: Liquid L_out]
    style A fill:#ffcc99
    style J fill:#ff9999
    style K fill:#99ccff
    style M fill:#6699ff
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- Vapor enters the bottom and flows upward through the packing voids.
- Liquid enters the top distributor and flows down as a thin film over the packing surface.
- Redistributors break up channeling and ensure uniform wetting.
- Demister prevents liquid entrainment in the exit vapor.
- This represents a **differential contact** — mass transfer is continuous along the height.

---

## 4.3 Tray Operating Regimes Schematic (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Vapor Velocity] -->|Increase| B[Weeping Regime]
    B -->|Increase| C[Stable Operation Zone]
    C -->|Increase| D[Entrainment / Foaming]
    D -->|Increase| E[Flooding Regime]
    style A fill:#ccccff
    style B fill:#ffff99
    style C fill:#99ff99
    style D fill:#ffaa99
    style E fill:#ff6666
```

**Operating Zones:**
- **Weeping:** Liquid leaks down through perforations — tray efficiency drops.
- **Stable:** Maximum efficiency zone — typical design target.
- **Entrainment:** Liquid droplets carried up — apparent $E_{MV}$ drops.
- **Flooding:** Liquid backed up in downcomers — column must be shut down.

---

## 4.4 Mass Transfer Mechanism Comparison (Mermaid Conceptual Map)

```mermaid

    C --> J[Pressure Drop Low]
    C --> K[HTU-NTU Integration]
    style A fill:#ffd966
    style B fill:#a4c2f4
    style C fill:#b6d7a8
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

> **Q:** Compare **sieve trays** and **valve trays** with respect to construction, turndown ratio, and cost.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Sieve Tray | Valve Tray |
|---------|------------|------------|
| **Construction** | Flat plate with simple round perforations (3–12 mm) | Plate with liftable valve caps over holes |
| **Turndown Ratio** | Low (≈ 1.5 – 2) | High (≈ 3 – 4) |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate to High |
| **Operating Range** | Narrow | Wide |

> **Awarding Marks:** Definition (1 mark), comparison points (2 marks).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> **Q:** Define the **Murphree Vapor-Phase Tray Efficiency**. State its typical range for industrial columns.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Murphree Vapor-Phase Tray Efficiency is defined as the ratio of the actual change in vapor composition across a tray to the change that would occur if the vapor reached thermodynamic equilibrium with the liquid leaving the tray.

$$E_{MV} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$

- **Typical industrial range:** 60 % to 90 %.
- **Significance:** Used to convert theoretical stages (from McCabe–Thiele) into actual trays.

> **Awarding Marks:** Equation (2 marks) + Typical range with significance (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Q(a) [7 Marks]:** Describe with neat sketches the **construction and working** of (i) sieve tray, (ii) bubble-cap tray, and (iii) valve tray. Compare them based on **turndown ratio** and **pressure drop**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sieve Tray Construction and Working:**
- A flat metal plate is perforated with circular holes of diameter **3 – 12 mm**.
- Typical hole area = **8 – 15 % of the active tray area**.
- Vapor rises through holes; liquid flows horizontally across the tray and into the downcomer.
- Liquid depth controlled by an **outlet weir** (typically 25 – 75 mm high).
- Liquid seal: A **inlet weir** ensures submergence of the tray deck.
- **Working:** Vapor jets through holes, disperses into the liquid layer, creating a two-phase froth. Mass transfer occurs in the frothy zone.

**(ii) Bubble-Cap Tray Construction and Working:**
- Each perforation is fitted with a **chimney (riser)** and an **inverted cap** above it.
- Vapor rises through the riser, is deflected downward by the cap, and bubbles out through **slots or teeth** at the cap periphery.
- **Working:** Vapor always bubbles through the liquid, even at very low rates — gives the **best turndown ratio**.
- Disadvantages: complex, expensive, high pressure drop, prone to corrosion/fouling.

**(iii) Valve Tray Construction and Working:**
- The plate has holes fitted with **liftable metal caps** (round or rectangular).
- At low vapor rates, the valve sits on its seat (small opening).
- At high vapor rates, the valve lifts, opening a larger annular area.
- **Working:** The opening self-adjusts to vapor flow — gives good turndown without weeping.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Sieve | Bubble-Cap | Valve |
|---------|-------|------------|-------|
| **Turndown Ratio** | Low (1.5 – 2) | Highest (4 – 5) | High (3 – 4) |
| **Pressure Drop** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| **Fouling Resistance** | Poor | Poor | Moderate |
| **Modern Use** | Most common in refineries | Mostly obsolete | Common in fine chemicals |

**Awarding Marks:** Construction (2 marks), Working (2 marks), Comparison (2 marks), Sketches (1 mark).

---

> **Q(b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain the **components of a packed tower**. Discuss the role of **liquid distributors and redistributors** in maintaining efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Components of a Packed Tower (top to bottom):**
1. **Top Vapor Outlet Nozzle**
2. **Demister Pad (Entrainment Eliminator):** Removes entrained liquid droplets.
3. **Top Liquid Distributor:** Sprays/distributes feed liquid uniformly.
4. **Packing Bed (Random or Structured):** The contact medium.
5. **Liquid Redistributors:** Placed every 3 – 5 m of packing height.
6. **Packing Support Plate:** Holds the packing; allows both phases to pass.
7. **Bottom Liquid Outlet**
8. **Bottom Vapor Inlet**

**Role of Liquid Distributor (Primary):**
- Distributes incoming liquid **evenly over the entire cross-section** of the packing.
- Critical because the wetted area of the packing (and hence mass transfer area) directly depends on uniform liquid distribution.
- Typical requirement: **40 – 60 drip points per m²** of column area.

**Role of Liquid Redistributors (Secondary):**
- **Correct maldistribution** that develops as liquid flows down through the packing.
- Maldistribution arises due to:
  - Vapor maldistribution
  - Wall flow (liquid preferentially flows down the wall)
  - Channeling (preferential paths)
- Without redistributors, the effective wetting area falls by 20 – 40 %, drastically reducing efficiency.

**Standard Design Rule (KTU-relevant):**
- One redistributor per **3 – 5 m of packed height** (or every **HETP × 5 to HETP × 10**).
- Below the redistributor, an additional **0.5 – 1.0 m of packing** acts as a mixing zone.

**Awarding Marks:** Component list (3 marks), Distributor role (2 marks), Redistributor role (2 marks).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Q(a) [7 Marks]:** Define **HETP** and **HTU**. Derive the relationship: $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate):**
HETP is the height of packing that produces a separation equivalent to one theoretical (equilibrium) stage. It is defined as:

$$\text{HETP} = \frac{Z}{N_t}$$

Where:
- $Z$ = total packed height (m)
- $N_t$ = number of theoretical stages required

**Definition of HTU (Height of a Transfer Unit):**
HTU represents the height of packing over which the change in composition of one phase is equal to the average mass transfer driving force. Two common HTUs are:
- $H_{OG}$ = height of an overall gas-phase transfer unit (m)
- $H_{OL}$ = height of an overall liquid-phase transfer unit (m)

**Derivation of $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$:**

**Step 1:** Consider a packed tower of cross-section $A_c$ and height $Z$. Mass transfer occurs counter-currently between gas ($V$) and liquid ($L$).

**Step 2:** For a differential element of height $dz$, the rate of mass transfer of A from gas to liquid is:

$$dN_A = K_y \cdot a \cdot A_c \cdot (y - y^*) \, dz$$

**Step 3:** The same mass transfer rate is given by a gas-phase mass balance:

$$dN_A = -\frac{V}{A_c} \, dy \cdot A_c = -V \, dy$$

(Assuming constant molar flow $V$.)

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions:

$$-V \, dy = K_y \, a \, A_c \, (y - y^*) \, dz$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange:

$$dz = -\frac{V}{K_y \, a \, A_c} \cdot \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

**Step 6:** Integrate from the bottom ($y = y_1$) to the top ($y = y_2$):

$$Z = \frac{V}{K_y \, a \, A_c} \cdot \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

**Step 7:** Define HTU and NTU:

$$H_{OG} = \frac{V}{K_y \, a \, A_c} \quad ; \quad N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

**Step 8:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}}$$

**Awarding Marks:** HETP definition (1 mark), HTU definition (1 mark), Derivation (5 marks).

---

> **Q(b) [7 Marks]:** A packed absorption column is to handle **2000 m³/h** of gas ($\rho_V = 1.5$ kg/m³) and **1500 kg/h** of liquid ($\rho_L = 900$ kg/m³). Calculate the **flow parameter $F_{LV}$** and **gas load factor $F_{SG}$** for a column of diameter **0.8 m**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\dot{V}_{gas} = 2000$ m³/h $= 2000/3600 = 0.5556$ m³/s
- $\rho_V = 1.5$ kg/m³
- $\dot{m}_{liquid} = 1500$ kg/h $= 1500/3600 = 0.4167$ kg/s
- $\rho_L = 900$ kg/m³
- $D = 0.8$ m

**Step 1 — Calculate column cross-sectional area:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.8)^2}{4} = 0.5027 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate superficial gas velocity:**

$$u_V = \frac{\dot{V}_{gas}}{A_c} = \frac{0.5556}{0.5027} = 1.105 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Flow Parameter $F_{LV}$:**

$$F_{LV} = \frac{L}{V}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}} = \frac{0.4167}{0.5556 \times 1.5}\sqrt{\frac{1.5}{900}}$$

$$= \frac{0.4167}{0.8333} \times 0.04082 = 0.5 \times 0.04082 = 0.0204$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Gas Load Factor $F_{SG}$:**

$$F_{SG} = u_V \sqrt{\rho_V} = 1.105 \times \sqrt{1.5} = 1.105 \times 1.2247 = 1.353 \text{ (m/s)(kg/m³)}^{0.5}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The low $F_{LV} = 0.0204$ indicates a **gas-rich / liquid-lean** system — typical of absorption operations. The gas load factor $F_{SG} = 1.35$ is moderate.

**Step 6 — Cross-check with flooding:**
Using a typical 25 mm metal Pall ring packing with $F_p = 156$ m⁻¹, from the GPDC chart at $F_{LV} = 0.02$, the flooding $F_{SG,\text{flood}} \approx 3.0$. The operating ratio is:

$$\frac{F_{SG}}{F_{SG,\text{flood}}} = \frac{1.35}{3.0} = 0.45$$

This is at 45% of flooding — slightly conservative, design is safe.

**Awarding Marks:** $A_c$ and $u_V$ (2 marks), $F_{LV}$ (2 marks), $F_{SG}$ (2 marks), Interpretation (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PCBBT503 Module 2:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_{MV}$ with $E_o$** — Murphree efficiency is *per-tray*, overall efficiency is *global*. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Units of $F_{SG}$** — Always quote $F_{SG}$ in $\text{(m/s)(kg/m³)}^{0.5}$, not just numerical value. **1 mark** lost for missing units.
> 3. **Mixing $H_{OG}$ with $H_{OL}$** — $H_{OG}$ is the gas-phase HTU; don't use it with $N_{OL}$ (liquid NTU). Use consistent pairing.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply operating-to-flooding ratio check** — When $F_{SG}/F_{SG,\text{flood}}$ is not between **0.5 and 0.8**, the design is unsafe — comment on this in the answer.
> 5. **Missing the log-mean driving force** in HTU-NTU problems — Examiners test whether the student correctly integrated $N_{OG}$ with the actual $y-y^*$ function.
> 6. **Conflating Random and Structured Packing** — Random packings have isotropic voidage; structured packings have directional flow channels and anisotropic pressure drop. Use specific values, not generic ones.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — PCBBT503 Module 2**

- ✅ **Tray Tower** = stage-wise contact; uses **Murphree Efficiency $E_{MV}$**; tray spacing typically **0.45 – 0.9 m**.
- ✅ **Packed Tower** = differential contact; uses **HTU-NTU or HETP** concept.
- ✅ **Three major tray types:** Sieve (cheap, narrow range), Valve (wide range), Bubble-cap (best range, obsolete).
- ✅ **Murphree Vapor-Phase Efficiency:** $E_{MV} = (y_n - y_{n+1})/(y_n^* - y_{n+1})$; typical **60 – 90%**.
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency:** $E_o = N_t / N_a$ where $N_a$ = actual trays.
- ✅ **HETP** = $Z/N_t$ — useful for comparing packed and tray towers.
- ✅ **HTU formulas:** $H_{OG} = V/(K_y a A_c)$, $H_{OL} = L/(K_x a A_c)$.
- ✅ **Total Packed Height:** $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} = H_{OL} \cdot N_{OL}$.
- ✅ **Flow Parameter:** $F_{LV} = (L/V)\sqrt{\rho_V/\rho_L}$ — dimensionless.
- ✅ **Gas Load Factor:** $F_{SG} = u_V \sqrt{\rho_V}$ — used with GPDC chart.
- ✅ **Operating Range:** Typically **50 – 80%** of flooding for safe design.
- ✅ **Liquid Redistributors** needed every **3 – 5 m** of packing height.
- ✅ **Tray towers preferred** for: large diameters, fouling service, high liquid rates, refinery applications.
- ✅ **Packed towers preferred** for: vacuum service, corrosive service, low pressure drop, low liquid hold-up, heat-sensitive materials.
- ✅ **Structured packings** offer lowest pressure drop and highest capacity — used in **ethylene primary fractionators**.
- ✅ **Random packings** are cheaper but have higher pressure drop and lower capacity than structured.
- ✅ **GPDC (Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation)** is the industry-standard chart for packed tower design.
- ✅ **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always include units, define every symbol, and explicitly state the operating-to-flooding ratio in design problems.
- ✅ **Key Distinction:** $E_{MV}$ (Murphree, per-tray) **≥** $E_{OV}$ (point efficiency, local) **≤** $E_o$ (overall, system-wide).
- ✅ **Pressure drop comparison:** Sieve (low) < Valve (moderate) < Bubble-cap (high); Random packing (moderate) > Structured packing (low).
- ✅ **Turndown ratio** = max operable flow / min operable flow — highest for bubble-cap, lowest for sieve.
- ✅ **HETP and HTU-NTU are NOT identical** — HETP assumes constant driving force; HTU-NTU handles variable driving force rigorously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Fractionation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Fractionation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Fractionation** is a steady-state, mass-transfer separation process in which a **liquid mixture of two or more volatile components** is continuously fed to an intermediate stage of a vertical, multi-stage contactor (a **distillation column**), where it is separated into a **vapor product (distillate)** enriched in the more volatile component (MVC) and a **liquid bottoms product (residue)** enriched in the less volatile component (LVC), by the simultaneous counter-current contact of rising vapor and descending liquid streams generated internally by a **total condenser** and a **partial reboiler**.

Under KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, continuous fractionation specifically refers to **continuous binary distillation** carried out at **steady state** in a **trayed or packed column**, using the classic **McCabe–Thiele stage-by-stage graphical design method** and the underlying **material and energy balance (MESH) equations** governing each theoretical stage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Multi-Storey Laundry Tower"**
>
> Imagine a tall building with many floors, each floor being a "washing stage". Dirty laundry (the feed) is dropped on the middle floor. Hot, dry air (vapor) is pumped in from the basement, and clean cold water (reflux) is sprayed from the roof. As the water trickles downward floor-by-floor, it picks up dirt; as the air rises, it gets cleaner. By the time the water reaches the basement, it has collected almost all the dirt; by the time the air exits the roof, it is almost completely dry.
>
> - **Basement (Reboiler)** = generates clean dry air → produces **bottoms** (LVC-rich liquid).
> - **Roof (Condenser)** = condenses the rising vapor → produces **distillate** (MVC-rich liquid) and **reflux**.
> - **Middle floor (Feed plate)** = the optimum location where the feed enters the column.

The "dirt" in this analogy is the **more volatile component (MVC)**, which preferentially travels with the vapor upward. The "water" is the descending liquid, which preferentially carries the **less volatile component (LVC)** downward.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Distillate (D)** | Vapor product condensed and removed from the top, rich in MVC. |
| **Bottoms / Residue (W or B)** | Liquid product removed from the reboiler, rich in LVC. |
| **Reflux (L)** | Condensed distillate returned to the top of the column. |
| **Reflux Ratio, R = L/D** | Ratio of liquid returned to distillate withdrawn. |
| **Boil-up Ratio, R′ = V̄/B** | Ratio of vapor generated in reboiler to bottoms withdrawn. |
| **Rectifying Section** | The portion of the column **above the feed plate**, where only vapor and liquid from the top/feed enter. |
| **Stripping Section** | The portion of the column **below the feed plate**, where the reboiler vapor strips MVC from the descending liquid. |
| **Feed Plate / Optimum Feed Stage** | The stage where the feed is introduced; chosen such that the operating lines intersect on it. |
| **Theoretical Stage / Equilibrium Stage** | An ideal stage where the exiting vapor and liquid are in **thermodynamic equilibrium**. |
| **Actual Stage / Real Stage** | A real tray with efficiency less than 100 % (Murphree efficiency usually 50–70 %). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **single most important metric** of any continuous fractionation design is the **Number of Theoretical Stages (Nₜₕ)** required to achieve a specified product purity. This is most often obtained graphically via the **McCabe–Thiele method** under KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 1.4 Why Continuous Fractionation Matters

Continuous (as opposed to batch) fractionation is the **workhorse** of the chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, and cryogenic-air-separation industries. It operates 24×7 with constant product specifications, low operating cost per kg of product, and easy integration with upstream and downstream continuous processes. Almost every refinery in the world — from crude-oil atmospheric and vacuum distillation to ethylene crackers — depends on this unit operation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Continuous Binary Distillation Column

A standard continuous fractionation column consists of the following hardware, listed top-to-bottom:

1. **Total Condenser** — condenses **all** overhead vapor (V_top) into liquid. Part is taken as **distillate (D)**, the rest returned as **reflux (L)**.
2. **Rectifying Section** — N₁ theoretical stages; the operating line here is the **Rectifying Operating Line (ROL)**.
3. **Feed Plate (F)** — feed (F) with composition z_F enters here. Thermal condition of feed is described by **q** (defined below).
4. **Stripping Section** — N₂ theoretical stages; operating line is the **Stripping Operating Line (SOL)**.
5. **Partial Reboiler** — vaporises a portion of the bottom liquid; acts as **one additional theoretical stage**, providing extra separation.

### 2.2 Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 — Overall Material Balance**: The total feed F is split into distillate D and bottoms B. Mass is conserved.
- **Step 2 — Component Balance**: The MVC entering with the feed must leave in the distillate + bottoms.
- **Step 3 — Define Thermal Condition of Feed (q)**: 1 mole of feed produces **q moles of saturated liquid** and **(1 − q) moles of saturated vapor** at the feed plate.
- **Step 4 — Constant Molal Overflow (CMO) Assumption**: Latent heats are assumed equal; sensible-heat changes are neglected. This makes **L and V constant in each section**, allowing straight operating lines on the y–x diagram.
- **Step 5 — Write Operating-Line Equations**: Mass balances on each section yield a linear relationship between y and x.
- **Step 6 — q-Line Construction**: Connects the intersection of ROL and SOL to the feed composition z_F on the 45° line.
- **Step 7 — Stage Construction (McCabe–Thiele)**: Step off theoretical stages between the operating lines and the equilibrium curve from x = x_D down to x = x_B.
- **Step 8 — Locate Feed Plate**: The stage that crosses the feed point (intersection of ROL and SOL) is the **optimum feed stage**.

### 2.3 The "q" Parameter (Thermal Condition of Feed)

The parameter **q** describes the fraction of feed that is liquid at the feed-plate conditions:

| Feed Condition | q value | Slope of q-line = q / (q − 1) |
|----------------|---------|------------------------------|
| Subcooled liquid | q > 1 | between 1 and +∞ |
| **Saturated liquid** | **q = 1** | **+∞ (vertical line at z_F)** |
| Two-phase (liquid + vapor) | 0 < q < 1 | negative (between 0 and +∞) |
| **Saturated vapor** | **q = 0** | **0 (horizontal line at z_F)** |
| Superheated vapor | q < 0 | between 0 and 1 |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically standard, textbook-grade, and directly aligned with the KTU 2024 PCBBT503 Module-2 syllabus. Use this as your single-page revision sheet.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Overall Mass Balance** | $F = D + B$ | F, D, B in kmol/h |
| 2 | **Overall Component Balance** | $F\,z_F = D\,x_D + B\,x_B$ | compositions in mole fraction |
| 3 | **Reflux Ratio** | $R = L / D$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Rectifying Operating Line (ROL)** | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\,x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | y, x = mole fractions |
| 5 | **Stripping Operating Line (SOL)** | $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}\,x - \dfrac{B\,x_B}{V'}$ | L′, V′ = liquid, vapor molar flow in stripping section |
| 6 | **q-Line Equation** | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}\,x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | slope = q/(q−1) |
| 7 | **q definition (latent-heat basis)** | $q = \dfrac{H_{V,feed} - H_{V,FP}}{H_{V,FP} - H_{L,FP}}$ | H = enthalpy (kJ/kmol) |
| 8 | **CMO Vapor & Liquid flows** | $V = D(R+1)$ ; $L = D\,R$ | rectifying section |
| 9 | **CMO Stripping flows** | $L' = L + qF$ ; $V' = V - (1-q)F$ | below feed plate |
| 10 | **Fenske Equation (Minimum Plates, Total Reflux)** | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{x_B}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{1-x_D}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | N_min = integer ≥ actual |
| 11 | **Underwood Equation (Minimum Reflux, q-line crosses equilibrium)** | $\sum\limits_{i}\dfrac{q\,z_i}{q-\theta} = 1$ ; $R_{min} = \sum\limits_{i}\dfrac{\alpha_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$ | θ is Underwood root |
| 12 | **Gilliland Correlation (Quick estimate of N)** | $Y = 0.75\!\left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right)$ where $X = \dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R+1}$, $Y = \dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N+1}$ | empirical graphical fit |
| 13 | **Murphree Vapor-Stage Efficiency** | $E_{Mv} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^{*} - y_{n+1}}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | **Number of Actual Stages** | $N_{actual} = N_{theoretical} / E_{Mv}$ | integer rounded up |
| 15 | **Feed-plate location (Kirkhoff's rule)** | $\dfrac{N_{rectifying}}{N_{stripping}} = \dfrac{\text{stages above feed}}{\text{stages below feed}} \approx \dfrac{\text{distance on y-x plot above/below intersection}}$ | from McCabe–Thiele plot |
| 16 | **Minimum Reflux Slope (intersection of q-line with equilibrium)** | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$ | x_q, y_q at q-line ∩ equilibrium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip**: In Part-B (14-mark) questions, the examiner almost always tests at least **two** of the following in combination: (a) operating-line equation derivation, (b) McCabe–Thiele step-off count, (c) feed-plate location, and (d) minimum-reflux or minimum-plate calculation. Master all four.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Refinery design**: separation of crude oil into LPG, naphtha, kerosene, diesel, etc.
- **Air separation**: continuous cryogenic distillation of air into O₂, N₂, Ar.
- **Ethanol dehydration**: continuous recovery of fermentation ethanol.
- **Solvent recovery**: regenerating toluene, acetone, hexane etc. in pharma and paints.
- **Energy integration**: heat integration between reboiler and feed preheater improves column efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below assume **Constant Molal Overflow (CMO)** and **constant relative volatility (α)** unless otherwise stated. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no skipping.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rectifying Section Operating Line (ROL)

**Given:** A section of the column above the feed plate. Let:
- V = molar flow rate of vapor leaving stage n (rising)
- L = molar flow rate of liquid leaving stage n (descending)
- yₙ = mole fraction of MVC in vapor leaving stage n
- xₙ₊₁ = mole fraction of MVC in liquid leaving stage n+1 (i.e. liquid entering stage n from above)
- D = distillate molar flow
- x_D = distillate composition

**Step 1 — Control volume:** Enclose the top of the column including the condenser. Make a total mass balance.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{n+1} &= L_n + D
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Component (MVC) balance** over the same control volume:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{n+1}\,y_{n+1} &= L_n\,x_n + D\,x_D
\end{aligned}$$
