| $R = R_{opt}$ (Erbar-Maddox) | Moderate | Optimal | Optimal | Balanced | Balanced | **Minimum** |
| $R = 1.5 R_{min}$ | Moderate-Low | Shorter | Smaller | High | Moderate | Slightly Higher |
| $R \to \infty$ (Total Reflux) | $N_{min}$ | Shortest | Smallest | Highest | Lowest | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Cost vs. Reflux Ratio Trade-Off

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reflux Ratio R on X-axis] --> B[Operating Cost Curve]
    A --> C[Fixed Capital Cost Curve]
    A --> D[Total Cost Curve]
    B --> B1[Decreases as R increases - less vapor traffic]
    C --> C1[Increases as R increases - fewer stages needed]
    D --> D1[Sum of Op Cost + Cap Cost]
    D --> E[Minimum Point at Ropt]
    E --> F[Ropt from Erbar-Maddox]
    E --> G[Nopt from Gilliland]
    F --> H[Optimum Design]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Final Column Specs: Diameter, Height, Trays, Reboiler, Condenser]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Algorithm to Find Optimum Design

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step 1: Given Feed Composition zF and q] --> B[Step 2: Specify xD and xB product purities]
    B --> C[Step 3: Calculate alphaLK and alphaHK relative volatilities]
    C --> D[Step 4: Use Fenske Equation to find Nmin]
    D --> E[Step 5: Solve Underwood Equation for theta]
    E --> F[Step 6: Use Underwood 2nd eq to find Rmin]
    F --> G[Step 7: Apply Erbar-Maddox to get Ropt]
    G --> H[Step 8: Apply Gilliland Correlation to find Nopt]
    H --> I[Step 9: Convert Nopt to Actual Stages using Murphree Efficiency]
    I --> J[Step 10: Design Column Diameter using Souders-Brown Equation]
    J --> K[Step 11: Final Optimum Column Design]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Reflux Ratio Conceptual Spectrum

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Total Reflux R equals infinity] -->|Number of stages = Nmin| P2[R equals Ropt - Erbar-Maddox]
    P2 -->|Number of stages = Nopt - Gilliland| P3[R equals Rmin - Underwood]
    P3 -->|Number of stages = infinity| P4[Infinite Stages Limit]
    P1 -.->|Capital Cost: Lowest| P5[Operating Cost: Highest]
    P4 -.->|Capital Cost: Highest| P6[Operating Cost: Lowest]
    P2 -.->|Capital Cost: Optimal| P7[Operating Cost: Optimal]
    P7 -.-> P8[Minimum Total Cost Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flows** that represent the engineering decision sequence. In an actual design office, engineers also overlay superimposed contours of constant reboiler duty, constant column diameter, and constant payback period on the Gilliland chart to pinpoint the true economic optimum — these multi-objective contours shift $R_{opt}$ slightly from the pure Erbar-Maddox value.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define optimum reflux ratio. Why is operating a column at minimum reflux ratio ($R_{min}$) not feasible in practice?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Optimum Reflux Ratio ($R_{opt}$)** is the reflux ratio at which the total annualized cost of a distillation column (sum of operating cost and fixed capital cost) attains its minimum value. It is determined by the **Erbar-Maddox correlation**:
> $$\dfrac{R_{opt} - R_{min}}{R_{opt} + 1} = 0.75 \left(R_{min} + 1\right)^{-0.5668}$$
>
> Operating at $R_{min}$ is **not feasible** because $R_{min}$ corresponds to the **pinch point** where the rectifying and stripping operating lines intersect the equilibrium curve. At this condition, the number of theoretical stages required is **infinite**, meaning the column would need to be infinitely tall — physically impossible and economically absurd.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Erbar-Maddox formula, 1 mark for $R_{min}$ feasibility explanation)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between minimum reflux ratio and total reflux condition. State the Gilliland correlation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Minimum Reflux ($R_{min}$) | Total Reflux ($R = \infty$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Reflux ratio at which infinite stages are required | All condensate returned as reflux, no distillate withdrawn |
| Number of Stages | $\infty$ (Infinite) | $N_{min}$ (Computed by Fenske Equation) |
| Operating Lines | Intersect equilibrium curve (pinch point) | Coincide with the diagonal $y = x$ |
| Use in Design | Lower bound of $R$ for column design | Lower bound of $N$ for column design |

**Gilliland Correlation (Analytical Form by Molokanov):**

$$Y = 0.75 \left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right)$$

where $X = \dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}$ and $Y = \dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1}$.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for distinction table, 1.5 marks for Gilliland formula with definition of X and Y)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** A binary distillation column separates a benzene-toluene mixture. The feed is **50 mol% benzene, 50 mol% toluene**, with **$q = 1$** (saturated liquid). Distillate contains **95 mol% benzene**, bottoms contains **5 mol% benzene**. Average relative volatility of benzene with respect to toluene is **$\alpha = 2.47$**. Calculate:
- (i) Minimum number of stages $N_{min}$ using Fenske equation.
- (ii) Minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$ using Underwood equation.
- (iii) Optimum reflux ratio $R_{opt}$ using Erbar-Maddox correlation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the same system, using the Gilliland correlation, determine the **optimum number of stages $N_{opt}$** at $R_{opt}$. Compare it with $N_{min}$ and comment on the design choice. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a)(i): Fenske Equation for $N_{min}$

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left(\tfrac{x_{D,LK}}{x_{D,HK}}\right) \left(\tfrac{x_{B,HK}}{x_{B,LK}}\right) \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{avg}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left(\tfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right) \left(\tfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right) \right]}{\ln \, 2.47} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ 19 \times 19 \right]}{\ln \, 2.47}$$

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \, 361}{0.9046} = \dfrac{5.886}{0.9046} \approx 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages}$$

#### Part (a)(ii): Underwood Equation for $R_{min}$

For a **binary system** with $q = 1$ and $\theta$ between $\alpha_{HK} = 1$ and $\alpha_{LK} = 2.47$:

$$\dfrac{\alpha_{LK} \, z_{LK}}{\alpha_{LK} - \theta} + \dfrac{\alpha_{HK} \, z_{HK}}{\alpha_{HK} - \theta} = 1 - q = 0$$

$$\dfrac{2.47 \times 0.5}{2.47 - \theta} + \dfrac{1 \times 0.5}{1 - \theta} = 0$$

$$\dfrac{1.235}{2.47 - \theta} = \dfrac{0.5}{\theta - 1}$$

$$1.235 (\theta - 1) = 0.5 (2.47 - \theta)$$

$$1.235 \theta - 1.235 = 1.235 - 0.5 \theta$$

$$1.735 \theta = 2.47$$

$$\theta = 1.423$$

Now, applying the second Underwood relation:

$$R_{min} + 1 = \dfrac{2.47 \times 0.95}{2.47 - 1.423} + \dfrac{1 \times 0.05}{1 - 1.423}$$

$$R_{min} + 1 = \dfrac{2.3465}{1.047} + \dfrac{0.05}{-0.423} = 2.241 - 0.1182 = 2.123$$

$$R_{min} \approx 1.12$$

#### Part (a)(iii): Erbar-Maddox for $R_{opt}$

$$A = 0.75 (R_{min} + 1)^{-0.5668} = 0.75 \times (2.123)^{-0.5668}$$

$$A = 0.75 \times (2.123)^{-0.5668}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln A = \ln 0.75 - 0.5668 \ln 2.123 = -0.2877 - 0.5668 \times 0.7526 = -0.2877 - 0.4266 = -0.7143$

$$A = e^{-0.7143} \approx 0.4894$$

$$R_{opt} = \dfrac{R_{min} + A}{1 - A} = \dfrac{1.12 + 0.4894}{1 - 0.4894} = \dfrac{1.6094}{0.5106} \approx 3.15$$

#### Part (b): Gilliland Correlation for $N_{opt}$

$$X = \dfrac{R_{opt} - R_{min}}{R_{opt} + 1} = \dfrac{3.15 - 1.12}{3.15 + 1} = \dfrac{2.03}{4.15} \approx 0.4892$$

$$Y = 0.75 \left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right) = 0.75 \left(1 - 0.4892^{0.5668}\right)$$

$$\ln(0.4892^{0.5668}) = 0.5668 \times \ln(0.4892) = 0.5668 \times (-0.7150) = -0.4053$$

$$0.4892^{0.5668} = e^{-0.4053} = 0.6667$$

$$Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.6667) = 0.75 \times 0.3333 = 0.25$$

Now solve for $N_{opt}$:

$$Y = \dfrac{N_{opt} - N_{min}}{N_{opt} + 1} \implies 0.25 = \dfrac{N_{opt} - 7}{N_{opt} + 1}$$

$$0.25 (N_{opt} + 1) = N_{opt} - 7$$

$$0.25 N_{opt} + 0.25 = N_{opt} - 7$$

$$7.25 = 0.75 N_{opt}$$

$$N_{opt} = \dfrac{7.25}{0.75} \approx 9.67 \approx 10 \text{ stages}$$

**Comment:** $N_{opt} = 10$ is greater than $N_{min} = 7$ (total reflux limit) but represents the economically balanced design. The column has 3 additional stages above the minimum to allow reasonable reflux flow, which keeps operating costs manageable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain how the optimum reflux ratio is determined from the **total cost vs. reflux ratio** curve. What are the limitations of the simple $R_{opt} = 1.2$ to $1.5 R_{min}$ rule? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A feed containing **40 mol% $n$-pentane, 35 mol% $n$-hexane, 25 mol% $n$-heptane** is distilled at $q = 0.5$. Distillate contains **85% pentane, 14% hexane, 1% heptane**. Relative volatilities (with respect to heptane) are: $\alpha_{C5} = 5.0$, $\alpha_{C6} = 2.5$, $\alpha_{C7} = 1.0$. Calculate $R_{min}$ using the Underwood method. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a): Total Cost Curve and Optimum Reflux Ratio

**Sketch Description (text representation):**

- **X-axis:** Reflux Ratio $R$ (from $R_{min}$ to $\infty$)
- **Y-axis:** Annual Cost (₹/year)
- **Curve 1 (Operating Cost):** Monotonically **decreasing** from a high value at $R = R_{min}$ to a higher value at $R = \infty$ (steeply rising near $R = \infty$).
- **Curve 2 (Fixed Capital Cost):** Monotonically **increasing** from a low value at $R = R_{min}$ (tall column) to a high value at $R = \infty$ (short column but huge reboiler/condenser).
- **Curve 3 (Total Cost):** Sum of Curves 1 and 2, exhibiting a **clear minimum** at $R_{opt}$.

**Mathematical Condition:**

$$\dfrac{d(C_{op} + C_{cap})}{dR} = 0 \implies \left|\dfrac{dC_{op}}{dR}\right| = \left|\dfrac{dC_{cap}}{dR}\right| \quad \text{at } R = R_{opt}$$

**Limitations of the Rule $R_{opt} = 1.2$ to $1.5 R_{min}$:**

1. Ignores **feed composition effects** — light-key concentration heavily influences the optimum.
2. Does not account for **multi-component systems** with more than two key components.
3. Fails for **heat-sensitive or reactive distillations** where thermal degradation sets an upper reflux limit.
4. Does not incorporate **energy prices** — in regions with high electricity/steam costs, the rule under-predicts $R_{opt}$.
5. Ignores **pressure drop and column internals** cost variations.
6. The Erbar-Maddox correlation is more accurate and should be preferred.

#### Part (b): Underwood Method for Ternary System

**Step 1: Underwood Equation for Root $\theta$**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} \dfrac{\alpha_i \, z_{F,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1 - q = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

$$\dfrac{5.0 \times 0.40}{5.0 - \theta} + \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.35}{2.5 - \theta} + \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.25}{1.0 - \theta} = 0.5$$

$$\dfrac{2.0}{5.0 - \theta} + \dfrac{0.875}{2.5 - \theta} + \dfrac{0.25}{1.0 - \theta} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Try $\theta = 1.5$ (between $\alpha_{HK} = 1.0$ and $\alpha_{LK} = 2.5$):**

$$\dfrac{2.0}{3.5} + \dfrac{0.875}{1.0} + \dfrac{0.25}{-0.5} = 0.571 - 0.5 + ... $$

This gives a negative denominator issue. Try $\theta$ between 1.0 and 2.5, i.e., $\theta = 1.4$:

$$\dfrac{2.0}{5.0 - 1.4} + \dfrac{0.875}{2.5 - 1.4} + \dfrac{0.25}{1.0 - 1.4}$$

$$= \dfrac{2.0}{3.6} + \dfrac{0.875}{1.1} + \dfrac{0.25}{-0.4}$$

$$= 0.5556 + 0.7955 - 0.625 = 0.7261 \neq 0.5$$

Try $\theta = 1.25$:

$$= \dfrac{2.0}{3.75} + \dfrac{0.875}{1.25} + \dfrac{0.25}{-0.25}$$

$$= 0.5333 + 0.70 - 1.0 = 0.2333 \neq 0.5$$

**Step 3: Try $\theta = 1.35$:**

$$= \dfrac{2.0}{3.65} + \dfrac{0.875}{1.15} + \dfrac{0.25}{-0.35}$$

$$= 0.5479 + 0.7609 - 0.7143 = 0.5945 \neq 0.5$$

**Step 4: Try $\theta = 1.37$:**

$$= \dfrac{2.0}{3.63} + \dfrac{0.875}{1.13} + \dfrac{0.25}{-0.37}$$

$$= 0.5510 + 0.7743 - 0.6757 = 0.6496 \neq 0.5$$

**Step 5: Try $\theta = 1.30$:**

$$= \dfrac{2.0}{3.70} + \dfrac{0.875}{1.20} + \dfrac{0.25}{-0.30}$$

$$= 0.5405 + 0.7292 - 0.8333 = 0.4364 \neq 0.5$$

**Step 6: Interpolating between $\theta = 1.30$ (f=0.4364) and $\theta = 1.35$ (f=0.5945):**

$$\theta \approx 1.30 + \dfrac{0.5 - 0.4364}{0.5945 - 0.4364} \times 0.05 = 1.30 + \dfrac{0.0636}{0.1581} \times 0.05$$

$$\theta \approx 1.30 + 0.0201 = 1.32$$

**Step 7: Verify at $\theta = 1.32$:**

$$= \dfrac{2.0}{3.68} + \dfrac{0.875}{1.18} + \dfrac{0.25}{-0.32}$$

$$= 0.5435 + 0.7415 - 0.7813 = 0.5037 \approx 0.5 \checkmark$$

**Step 8: Apply Second Underwood Equation:**

$$R_{min} + 1 = \dfrac{5.0 \times 0.85}{5.0 - 1.32} + \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.14}{2.5 - 1.32} + \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.01}{1.0 - 1.32}$$

$$R_{min} + 1 = \dfrac{4.25}{3.68} + \dfrac{0.35}{1.18} + \dfrac{0.01}{-0.32}$$

$$R_{min} + 1 = 1.1549 + 0.2966 - 0.0313 = 1.4202$$

$$\boxed{R_{min} \approx 0.42}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Underwood Denominator:** When $\theta > \alpha_{HK}$, the term for the heavy key becomes **negative**. Students often miss the negative sign, leading to gross errors in $R_{min}$.
> 2. **Wrong Root Selection:** For multicomponent systems, there can be multiple roots of the Underwood equation. Always select $\theta$ such that $\alpha_{LK} > \theta > \alpha_{HK}$.
> 3. **Fenske vs. Gilliland Confusion:** $N_{min}$ is for **total reflux** (Fenske), not for $R_{min}$. Many students interchange them.
> 4. **Units of $R$:** Reflux ratio is **dimensionless**; do not report it in kmol/h or any other unit.
> 5. **Erbar-Maddox Domain:** Valid only when $R_{min} > 0$ and for conventional distillation (not for extractive or azeotropic systems).
> 6. **Gilliland Reverse Calculation:** When asked to find $N$ given $R$, use $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$ then solve algebraically for $N$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Optimum Reflux Ratio ($R_{opt}$):** Reflux ratio at which **Total Cost = Operating Cost + Capital Cost** is minimum.
- [x] **Bounds:** $R_{min} < R_{opt} < \infty$ (Total Reflux).
- [x] **Cost Behavior:** As $R \uparrow$, Operating Cost $\uparrow$, but Capital Cost $\downarrow$ (because fewer stages).
- [x] **Underwood Equation (Root):** $\sum \dfrac{\alpha_i z_{F,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1 - q$
- [x] **Underwood Equation ($R_{min}$):** $\sum \dfrac{\alpha_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = R_{min} + 1$
- [x] **Fenske Equation:** $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln[(x_{D,LK}/x_{D,HK})(x_{B,HK}/x_{B,LK})]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$
- [x] **Gilliland (Molokanov):** $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$, with $X = (R - R_{min})/(R+1)$, $Y = (N - N_{min})/(N+1)$.
- [x] **Erbar-Maddox:** $\dfrac{R_{opt} - R_{min}}{R_{opt} + 1} = 0.75 (R_{min} + 1)^{-0.5668}$
- [x] **Empirical Quick Rule:** $R_{opt} \approx 1.2 R_{min}$ to $1.5 R_{min}$
- [x] **Reboiler Duty:** $Q_R = D(R+1)\lambda$, **Condenser Duty:** $Q_C = D R \lambda$
- [x] **Feed $q$ values:** $q = 1$ (saturated liquid), $q = 0$ (saturated vapor), $q > 1$ (cold liquid), $0 < q < 1$ (two-phase).
- [x] **Pinch Point:** The locus where operating line meets the equilibrium curve at $R_{min}$.
- [x] **Total Reflux:** No product withdrawal; all condensate returned; minimum stages achieved.
- [x] **CMO Assumption:** Constant molal overflow is required for all Underwood, Fenske, and Gilliland correlations to hold.
- [x] **KTU ESE Pattern:** Expect a 7-mark question on $R_{opt}$ derivation (Erbar-Maddox or Gilliland) and a 7-mark numerical on Underwood + Erbar-Maddox.
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Confusing $N_{min}$ (total reflux) with $N$ at $R_{min}$ (infinite stages). These are two completely different design limits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Condenser and Partial Condenser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Topic:** Total Condenser and Partial Condenser > - **Module:** Module 2 – Distillation & Absorption Equipment > - **Prepared by:** KTU Senior Board Examiner | NEP 2020 Aligned # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In continuous distillation columns, the **overhead vapor stream** leaving the top tray must be converted back into a liquid (reflux) to maintain internal liquid traffic, while simultaneously withdrawing a portion as the purified product (distillate). This phase-change operation is performed in a **condenser**, which is the final mass transfer / heat transfer unit external to the column. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > A **condenser** is a heat exchanger that condenses the overhead vapor from a distillation column. It may operate in two fundamentally different modes — **Total Condensation** or **Partial Condensation** — depending on whether the entire vapor stream is liquefied or only a fraction of it. ### 1.1.1 Total Condenser (TC) A **Total Condenser** is a heat transfer device in which **100 % of the overhead vapor** ($V_N$) is completely condensed into a saturated liquid (or sub-cooled liquid) at the column pressure. The resulting condensate is then split into two streams using a **reflux drum**: - **Reflux (L₀)** — returned to the top tray of the column. - **Distillate Product (D)** — withdrawn as the overhead product. The defining thermodynamic identity is: $$y_1 = x_D = x_{L_0}$$ i.e., the composition of the vapor entering the condenser, the liquid leaving it, the reflux, and the distillate are **all identical** (since the condensation is isobaric and complete, with no phase separation). ### 1.1.2 Partial Condenser (PC) A **Partial Condenser** is a heat exchanger where only a portion of the overhead vapor is condensed. The output consists of **two co-existing phases** leaving the unit: - A **vapor distillate (D, y_D)** — withdrawn as the product, still in vapor form. - A **liquid reflux (L₀, x₀)** — returned to the top tray. Critically, the vapor and liquid leaving a partial condenser are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with each other at the operating pressure and temperature. This means the partial condenser itself acts as **one additional theoretical (equilibrium) stage** of separation. $$y_D = f(x_0) \quad \text{at equilibrium (i.e., on the } y\text{-}x \text{ curve)}$$ ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition) 🧠 > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 – The Two Buckets Under a Rain Cloud ☁️** > Imagine rain falling on a slanted roof with two gutters. > - **Total Condenser:** Both gutters are closed and feed into **one big sealed tank**. *All* the rainwater (vapor) is collected; you then pour some back over the roof (reflux) and keep the rest (distillate). The water you keep is identical to the water you pour back — **same purity**. > - **Partial Condenser:** One gutter leads to a tank, the *other gutter* simply vents some of the rain back into the atmosphere. The water that *remains in the air* is richer in the more volatile component (lighter vapor escapes preferentially). The condensed water and the escaping vapor are in equilibrium — the partial condenser thus performs a small extra "purification" step. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 – The Boiling Kettle with a Lid** > When you lift the lid of a boiling kettle: > - A **total condenser** would be like a cold plate that traps *all* the steam, turning it back into water of the *same composition* as the steam. > - A **partial condenser** would be like a slightly warm lid — only *some* steam condenses (the heavier, less volatile component condenses first), while the *lighter steam escapes*. The water that drips back has a *different (leaner in light component)* composition than the steam that escapes. ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Highlights) - **Reflux Ratio (R)** = $L_0 / D$ — dimensionless; controls separation sharpness and column size. - **Condenser Duty (Q_c)** = $V_N \cdot \lambda$ — energy removed per unit time (kW), where $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kmol). - **Degrees of Freedom (F)** for a binary condenser: - Total Condenser: **F = 3** (C – P + 2 = 2 – 1 + 2; P fixed, T determined by x_D at fixed P). - Partial Condenser: **F = 2** (C – P + 2 = 2 – 2 + 2; two phases in equilibrium). - **Theoretical Stage Equivalent**: Total Condenser = **0 stages**; Partial Condenser = **1 stage**. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principles – The "Why" and "How" ### 2.1.1 Total Condenser – The Logic Chain 1. **Vapor Entry:** Saturated (or superheated) vapor $V_N$ with composition $y_1$ enters the shell-side of the condenser at temperature $T_{dew}(y_1)$ and column pressure $P$. 2. **Heat Removal:** Cooling utility (water, refrigerant) on the tube side removes the latent heat of vaporization plus any sensible superheat. 3. **Complete Condensation:** The exit stream exits as a **single sub-cooled (or saturated) liquid phase** with composition $x_D = y_1$. 4. **Phase Splitting:** A downstream **reflux drum** (vertical vessel) decouples the reflux pump from the condenser hydraulics and provides a surge volume. 5. **Stream Division:** A control valve on the distillate line modulates $D$; the remainder becomes $L_0 = R \cdot D$. > **Why use a reflux drum?** It provides: > (i) Net positive suction head (NPSH) for the reflux pump. > (ii) Surge capacity for feed/cooling disturbances. > (iii) Composition uniformity (well-mixed liquid). ### 2.1.2 Partial Condenser – The Logic Chain 1. **Vapor Entry:** Saturated vapor $V_N$ with composition $y_1$ enters the condenser. 2. **Limited Heat Removal:** Only enough heat is removed to condense a fraction of the vapor. 3. **Equilibrium Co-existence:** The outlet has **two phases** — vapor ($y_D$, leaner in LV component if LV is more volatile) and liquid ($x_0$). 4. **Phase Separation:** A downstream **reflux accumulator (vapor–liquid separator)** separates the two phases. 5. **Stream Routing:** Vapor product $D$ exits overhead; liquid $L_0$ is pumped back as reflux. > **The Critical Point:** Because $y_D$ and $x_0$ lie on the **equilibrium curve**, the partial condenser contributes **one theoretical stage of separation**. For highly volatile / close-boiling systems (e.g., propylene-propane, air separation), this extra stage significantly reduces the number of trays required in the column proper, often justifying the higher capital cost of the vapor-handling system. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — 80 % of KTU numerical problems on condensers use these equations.** | # | Parameter | Total Condenser | Partial Condenser | Units / Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Material balance (overall) | $V_N = L_0 + D$ | $V_N = L_0 + D$ | kmol/h (steady state) | | 2 | Component balance | $V_N y_1 = L_0 x_0 + D x_D$ | $V_N y_1 = L_0 x_0 + D y_D$ | mole fraction basis | | 3 | Composition link | $y_1 = x_D = x_0$ | $y_1 \neq y_D \neq x_0$; $y_D = K \cdot x_0$ | TC: no separation; PC: equilibrium | | 4 | Reflux ratio $R$ | $R = L_0 / D$ | $R = L_0 / D$ (D is vapor) | dimensionless | | 5 | Vapor rate | $V_N = D (R + 1)$ | $V_N = D (R + 1)$ | internal flow rate | | 6 | Theoretical stages | **0** (no equilibrium stage) | **1** (acts as one stage) | McCabe–Thiele count | | 7 | Degrees of freedom (binary) | F = 3 (P, x_D, T) | F = 2 (P, y_D or x_0) | Gibbs phase rule | | 8 | Condenser duty | $Q_c = V_N \cdot \lambda$ | $Q_c = (V_N - D) \cdot \lambda$ | kW (neglect sensible heat) | | 9 | Top operating point (McCabe–Thiele) | $(x_D,\, x_D)$ on $45°$ line | $(x_0,\, y_D)$ on equilibrium curve | graphical construction | | 10 | Rectifying line | $y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Same form, but $x_D$ replaced by intersection with equilibrium | top step always starts at PC point | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Trap (KTU favourite):** In a *partial condenser*, the "distillate" $D$ is a **vapor** with composition $y_D$, while the reflux $L_0$ is a **liquid** with composition $x_0$. Students frequently write $x_D$ in PC problems — **this loses full marks**. Always confirm the *physical state* of $D$ before writing the balance. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Preferred Type | |---|---|---| | Crude oil atmospheric/vacuum distillation | Naphtha, kerosene, diesel recovery | **Total** (simpler, lower cost) | | Cryogenic air separation ($O_2 / N_2 / Ar$) | High-purity N₂, O₂, Ar | **Partial** (extra stage cuts capex) | | Ethylene-ethane splitter | Polymer-grade ethylene | **Total** (high reflux ratios) | | LPG (propane/butane) fractionation | LPG spec products | **Partial** (close boiling → benefits from extra stage) | | Ethanol-water (final dehydrating column) | Bioethanol | **Total** | | Ammonia synthesis loop purge | Hydrogen recovery | **Partial** (top product as vapor) | # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Balance for a Total Condenser Consider a binary distillation column with overhead vapor $V_N$ leaving the top tray (composition $y_1$), entering a **total condenser** operating at steady state. **Step 1 — Define the streams and compositions.** The vapor $V_N$ (kmol/h) is fully condensed into a liquid of identical composition $x_D = y_1$. This total liquid is then split into: - Reflux: $L_0$ (kmol/h) with composition $x_0 = x_D$ - Distillate: $D$ (kmol/h) with composition $x_D$ **Step 2 — Apply the overall molar balance.** In steady state, the molar flow in must equal the molar flow out: $$V_N = L_0 + D$$ **Step 3 — Apply the component (light key) balance.** For the more volatile component (MVC): $$V_N \cdot y_1 = L_0 \cdot x_0 + D \cdot x_D$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the TC identity $y_1 = x_0 = x_D$.** $$V_N \cdot x_D = L_0 \cdot x_D + D \cdot x_D$$ Dividing through by $x_D$ (assuming $x_D \neq 0$): $$V_N = L_0 + D$$ This is **identical** to Step 2 — confirming that in a total condenser, the component balance provides no extra information (because all streams have the same composition). **Step 5 — Introduce the reflux ratio definition $R = L_0 / D$.** $$V_N = D \cdot R + D = D(R+1)$$ $$\boxed{L_0 = R \cdot D \quad ; \quad V_N = (R+1) \cdot D \quad ; \quad y_1 = x_D = x_0}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Balance for a Partial Condenser Now consider the **same column** but fitted with a **partial condenser**. The vapor $V_N$ (composition $y_1$) is partially condensed, producing a vapor distillate $D$ (composition $y_D$) and a liquid reflux $L_0$ (composition $x_0$), with $y_D$ and $x_0$ in equilibrium. **Step 1 — Overall molar balance.** $$V_N = L_0 + D$$ **Step 2 — Component (MVC) balance.** $$V_N \cdot y_1 = L_0 \cdot x_0 + D \cdot y_D$$ **Step 3 — Reflux ratio definition (note: $D$ is now a *vapor* stream).** $$R = \frac{L_0}{D} \implies L_0 = R \cdot D$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $V_N$ in terms of $D$ and $R$.** $$V_N = R \cdot D + D = (R+1) D$$ **Step 5 — Substitute into the component balance.** $$(R+1) D \cdot y_1 = R \cdot D \cdot x_0 + D \cdot y_D$$ Cancel $D$: $$(R+1) y_1 = R \cdot x_0 + y_D$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $x_0$ (the reflux composition — the actual top liquid composition in the column).** $$R \cdot x_0 = (R+1) y_1 - y_D$$ $$\boxed{x_0 = \frac{(R+1) y_1 - y_D}{R}}$$ This is the **key relationship** for a partial condenser. The reflux composition is *not* equal to $y_1$ (as it would be in a TC); instead, it depends on the equilibrium vapor product $y_D$. **Step 7 — Equilibrium constraint.** The two exiting phases must satisfy the thermodynamic equilibrium relation (Raoult's law for ideal systems): $$y_D = K \cdot x_0 = \frac{\alpha \cdot x_0}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_0}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **relative volatility** of the light key with respect to the heavy key. For non-ideal systems, use experimental VLE data or activity-coefficient models (Margules, Van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC). ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rectifying Section Operating Line with a Partial Condenser In the McCabe–Thiele method, the **rectifying operating line** describes the relationship between the vapor leaving a tray ($y_{n+1}$) and the liquid leaving the same tray ($x_n$) for $n \leq N-1$. **Step 1 — Mass balance around the envelope from tray $n$ to the top of the column (including the partial condenser).** Overall balance: $V_{n+1} = L_n + D$ Component balance: $V_{n+1} y_{n+1} = L_n x_n + D y_D$ **Step 2 — Substitute $V_{n+1} = L_n + D$ and solve for $y_{n+1}$.** $$y_{n+1} = \frac{L_n}{L_n + D} x_n + \frac{D}{L_n + D} y_D$$ **Step 3 — For constant molar overflow (CMO), $L_n = L_0 = R D$.** $$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{1}{R+1} y_D$$ **Step 4 — Compare to the total condenser case.** For a TC, $y_D = x_D$, so the line becomes: $$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$ For a PC, the $y$-intercept is $y_D / (R+1)$, and the line still passes through the point $(x_0,\, y_D)$ on the equilibrium curve. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Graphical Interpretation:** The rectifying line for a partial condenser intersects the $y$-$x$ equilibrium curve at the point $(x_0, y_D)$ — the *top step* of the McCabe–Thiele construction sits exactly on the equilibrium curve, not on the $45°$ diagonal. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Total Condenser | Partial Condenser | |---|---|---| | Number of product phases | 1 (liquid) | 2 (vapor + liquid) | | State of distillate | Saturated/subcooled liquid | Saturated vapor | | Composition of distillate | $x_D = y_1$ | $y_D \neq y_1$ (in equilibrium with $x_0$) | | Theoretical stages added by condenser | **0** | **1** | | Reflux drum type | Horizontal/vertical, single liquid phase | Two-phase separator (boot + vapor space) | | Pump head requirement | Lower (liquid at bubble point) | Higher (must handle two-phase flow) | | Capital cost | Lower | Higher (~20–30 % more) | | Operating cost (cooling duty) | $V_N \lambda$ | $(V_N - D) \lambda$ (slightly less) | | Control complexity | Simple (D valve only) | Complex (D, T, P all interactive) | | Best suited for | Wide-boiling mixtures, easy separations | Close-boiling mixtures, cryogenic, high-purity | | Typical L/D range | 0.5 – 5 | 1 – 10 (to leverage extra stage) | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Total Condenser — Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Top Tray N] -->|Vapor V_N, y1| B[Total Condenser
Shell and Tube HE] C[Cooling Water
Utility] -->|Tube Side| B B -->|Saturated Liquid x_D| D[Reflux Drum
Surge Vessel] D -->|Reflux L0, x0| E[Reflux Pump] E -->|L0| F[Top Tray 1 of Column] D -->|Distillate D, xD| G[Product Cooler] G --> H[Distillate Storage Tank] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Partial Condenser — Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Top Tray N] -->|Vapor V_N, y1| B[Partial Condenser
Shell and Tube HE] C[Cooling Water
Utility] -->|Tube Side| B B -->|Two-Phase Mixture| D[Reflux Accumulator
Vapor Liquid Separator] D -->|Vapor D, yD| E[Compressor or
Vapor Product Line] D -->|Liquid L0, x0| F[Reflux Pump] F -->|L0| G[Top Tray 1 of Column] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 McCabe–Thiele Graphical Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MTC["McCabe Thiele: TOTAL CONDENSER"] TC1[Rectifying line
y = R over R+1 times x + xD over R+1] --> TC2[Intersects 45 degree line
at x_D point] TC2 --> TC3[Top step starts on 45 degree line
No theoretical stage from condenser] end subgraph MPC["McCabe Thiele: PARTIAL CONDENSER"] PC1[Rectifying line
y = R over R+1 times x + yD over R+1] --> PC2[Intersects equilibrium curve
at x0 yD point] PC2 --> PC3[Top step starts on equilibrium curve
One theoretical stage from condenser] end style MTC fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style MPC fill:#E0E8F8,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style TC1 fill:#FFF,stroke:#333,color:#000 style TC2 fill:#FFF,stroke:#333,color:#000 style TC3 fill:#FFF,stroke:#333,color:#000 style PC1 fill:#FFF,stroke:#333,color:#000 style PC2 fill:#FFF,stroke:#333,color:#000 style PC3 fill:#FFF,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — When to Choose TC vs PC ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Design Distillation Column] --> B{Is the mixture
close-boiling
alpha less than 1.5?} B -->|No| C{Is high product
purity required
greater than 99 percent?} B -->|Yes| D[Choose PARTIAL CONDenser] C -->|No| E[Choose TOTAL CONDENSER
Lower cost simpler control] C -->|Yes| F{Is the overhead
product desired
as a vapor?} F -->|Yes| D F -->|No| G[Consider partial condenser
if extra stage saves capital] G --> H[Economic analysis:
extra stage savings vs
higher condenser cost] H --> I{Favourable?} I -->|Yes| D I -->|No| E style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style I fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]` **Differentiate between a total condenser and a partial condenser used in distillation columns.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | S.No. | Total Condenser | Partial Condenser | |---|---|---| | 1 | Entire vapor $V_N$ is condensed to a saturated liquid | Only a portion of vapor is condensed; vapor and liquid coexist | | 2 | Distillate is a **liquid** of composition $x_D = y_1$ | Distillate is a **vapor** of composition $y_D$ | | 3 | Provides **no** theoretical stage of separation | Acts as **one** theoretical (equilibrium) stage | | 4 | Composition relation: $x_D = x_0 = y_1$ | Composition relation: $y_D$ and $x_0$ are in equilibrium | | 5 | Lower capital cost, simpler control | Higher capital cost, more complex two-phase handling | *(Award 1 mark for each correct point; up to 3 points.)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]` **A distillation column produces a vapor distillate. What type of condenser is used, and why does it act as an additional theoretical stage?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **partial condenser** is used when the distillate is withdrawn as a vapor. It acts as an additional theoretical stage because the vapor product ($y_D$) and the liquid reflux ($x_0$) leaving the condenser are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with each other (Phase Rule: $F = C - P + 2 = 2 - 2 + 2 = 2$; for fixed $P$ and $T$, the two compositions are fixed by the equilibrium relation $y_D = K \cdot x_0$). Since the McCabe–Thiele step begins on the equilibrium curve rather than the $45°$ diagonal, it counts as one extra stage of separation. *(2 marks for identification + reasoning; 1 mark for correct phase-rule justification.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### `Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice` **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`** A binary distillation column operating at 1 atm separates a benzene–toluene mixture. The column overhead vapor leaves the top tray at a flow rate of $V_N = 110$ kmol/h with composition $y_1 = 0.92$ (mole fraction benzene). A **partial condenser** is used. The relative volatility of benzene with respect to toluene may be taken as $\alpha = 2.47$. The reflux ratio is $R = 2.0$. **a)** Determine the compositions of the vapor distillate ($y_D$) and liquid reflux ($x_0$), and the flow rates of distillate $D$ and reflux $L_0$. **(7 marks)** **b)** Calculate the condenser duty if the latent heat of vaporization of the mixture is $\lambda = 30{,}000$ kJ/kmol. Comment on whether a total condenser would change the operating line. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Given:** $V_N = 110$ kmol/h; $y_1 = 0.92$; $\alpha = 2.47$; $R = 2.0$. **Step 1 — Relate $V_N$ to $D$ and $L_0$ (overall balance).** $$V_N = D(R+1) \implies 110 = D(2+1) = 3D$$ $$D = \frac{110}{3} = 36.667 \text{ kmol/h} \approx 36.67 \text{ kmol/h}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate reflux flow rate $L_0$.** $$L_0 = R \cdot D = 2.0 \times 36.67 = 73.33 \text{ kmol/h}$$ **Step 3 — Use the component balance to relate $x_0$ and $y_D$.** $$V_N y_1 = L_0 x_0 + D y_D$$ $$110 \times 0.92 = 73.33 \, x_0 + 36.67 \, y_D$$ $$101.2 = 73.33 \, x_0 + 36.67 \, y_D \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the equilibrium constraint $y_D = K \cdot x_0 = \frac{\alpha x_0}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_0}$.** $$y_D = \frac{2.47 \, x_0}{1 + 1.47 \, x_0} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 5 — Solve (i) and (ii) simultaneously.** Substitute (ii) into (i): $$101.2 = 73.33 \, x_0 + 36.67 \cdot \frac{2.47 \, x_0}{1 + 1.47 \, x_0}$$ Multiply both sides by $(1 + 1.47 x_0)$: $$101.2 (1 + 1.47 x_0) = 73.33 x_0 (1 + 1.47 x_0) + 36.67 \times 2.47 x_0$$ Expand: $$101.2 + 148.764 \, x_0 = 73.33 \, x_0 + 107.7951 \, x_0 + 90.5749 \, x_0$$ $$101.2 + 148.764 \, x_0 = 271.699 \, x_0$$ $$101.2 = (271.699 - 148.764) x_0 = 122.935 \, x_0$$ $$x_0 = \frac{101.2}{122.935} = 0.8232 \text{ (mole fraction benzene in reflux)}$$ **Step 6 — Find $y_D$ from the equilibrium relation (ii).** $$y_D = \frac{2.47 \times 0.8232}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.8232} = \frac{2.0333}{2.2101} = 0.9200 \text{ (mole fraction benzene in vapor distillate)}$$ **Final Answers (a):** $$\boxed{D = 36.67 \text{ kmol/h}, \quad L_0 = 73.33 \text{ kmol/h}, \quad x_0 = 0.8232, \quad y_D = 0.9200}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that $y_D = 0.92 = y_1$ in this specific case because the reflux ratio $R = 2.0$ is sufficiently high to return the "extra" benzene as reflux. For lower $R$, $y_D$ would deviate noticeably from $y_1$. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Condenser duty formula for a partial condenser.** The vapor condensed is the difference between the entering vapor rate and the vapor distillate rate (which leaves uncondensed): $$Q_c = (V_N - D) \cdot \lambda$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values.** $$Q_c = (110 - 36.67) \text{ kmol/h} \times 30{,}000 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$ $$Q_c = 73.33 \text{ kmol/h} \times 30{,}000 \text{ kJ/kmol} = 2{,}200{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Convert to kW: $$Q_c = \frac{2{,}200{,}000}{3600} = 611.1 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Effect of switching to a total condenser on the operating line.** If a **total condenser** were used with the same $R = 2.0$ and the *same* $V_N = 110$ kmol/h: - $D = V_N / (R+1) = 36.67$ kmol/h (unchanged). - $x_D = y_1 = 0.92$ (no longer $y_D = 0.92$, but with same numerical value in this case). - The rectifying operating line changes from: $$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{y_D}{R+1} \quad \text{(PC: intercept } y_D/(R+1))$$ to: $$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} \quad \text{(TC: intercept } x_D/(R+1))$$ Since $x_D = 0.92$ and $y_D = 0.92$ in this numerical example, the operating line is **numerically identical**. However, the **graphical top step** changes: - In **TC**: top step starts at $(0.92, 0.92)$ on the $45°$ line. - In **PC**: top step starts at $(0.8232, 0.92)$ on the equilibrium curve — this is the extra theoretical stage. **Final Answer (b):** $$\boxed{Q_c = 611.1 \text{ kW (partial)}; \quad \text{Operating line intercept is numerically equal, but the top step location differs.}}$$ --- ### `Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice` **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`** A continuous distillation column equipped with a **total condenser** separates a methanol–water mixture. The top tray vapor rate is $V_N = 80$ kmol/h, the vapor composition leaving the top tray is $y_1 = 0.95$ (mole fraction methanol), and the desired distillate composition is $x_D = 0.95$. The reflux ratio is $R = 1.5$. **a)** Calculate the distillate rate $D$, reflux rate $L_0$, and verify the reflux composition. **(7 marks)** **b)** The plant manager proposes installing a partial condenser to reduce the number of trays. If the methanol–water equilibrium at this concentration follows $y = \frac{1.65 x}{1 + 0.65 x}$ (K-value approximation), determine the new top liquid composition $x_0$ and the new vapor distillate composition $y_D$. State clearly how many theoretical stages are saved. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Given:** $V_N = 80$ kmol/h; $y_1 = x_D = 0.95$; $R = 1.5$ (Total Condenser). **Step 1 — Overall material balance.** $$V_N = D(R+1) \implies 80 = D \times 2.5$$ $$D = \frac{80}{2.5} = 32 \text{ kmol/h}$$ **Step 2 — Reflux rate.** $$L_0 = R \cdot D = 1.5 \times 32 = 48 \text{ kmol/h}$$ **Step 3 — Verify the reflux composition using the component balance.** $$V_N y_1 = L_0 x_0 + D x_D$$ $$80 \times 0.95 = 48 \cdot x_0 + 32 \times 0.95$$ $$76 = 48 x_0 + 30.4$$ $$48 x_0 = 76 - 30.4 = 45.6$$ $$x_0 = \frac{45.6}{48} = 0.95$$ **Conclusion:** $x_0 = x_D = y_1 = 0.95$. **Reflux composition verified** — confirms the total-condenser identity. **Final Answers (a):** $$\boxed{D = 32 \text{ kmol/h}, \quad L_0 = 48 \text{ kmol/h}, \quad x_0 = 0.95}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** Same column, but now fitted with a **partial condenser**; $R = 1.5$ retained; equilibrium: $y = \dfrac{1.65 x}{1 + 0.65 x}$. **Step 1 — Recalculate $D$ and $L_0$ (overall balance is unchanged in form).** $$V_N = (R+1) D \implies 80 = 2.5 \, D \implies D = 32 \text{ kmol/h}$$ $$L_0 = R \cdot D = 1.5 \times 32 = 48 \text{ kmol/h}$$ **Step 2 — Component balance for the partial condenser.** $$(R+1) y_1 = R x_0 + y_D$$ $$2.5 \times 0.95 = 1.5 \, x_0 + y_D$$ $$2.375 = 1.5 x_0 + y_D \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 3 — Equilibrium constraint.** $$y_D = \frac{1.65 \, x_0}{1 + 0.65 \, x_0} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 4 — Solve (i) and (ii) simultaneously.** Substitute (ii) into (i): $$2.375 = 1.5 x_0 + \frac{1.65 x_0}{1 + 0.65 x_0}$$ Multiply by $(1 + 0.65 x_0)$: $$2.375 (1 + 0.65 x_0) = 1.5 x_0 (1 + 0.65 x_0) + 1.65 x_0$$ Expand: $$2.375 + 1.54375 \, x_0 = 1.5 x_0 + 0.975 x_0^2 + 1.65 x_0$$ $$2.375 + 1.54375 \, x_0 = 3.15 x_0 + 0.975 x_0^2$$ Rearrange into standard quadratic form: $$0.975 x_0^2 + (3.15 - 1.54375) x_0 - 2.375 = 0$$ $$0.975 x_0^2 + 1.60625 \, x_0 - 2.375 = 0$$ **Step 5 — Apply the quadratic formula.** $$x_0 = \frac{-1.60625 \pm \sqrt{(1.60625)^2 - 4(0.975)(-2.375)}}{2 \times 0.975}$$ Compute the discriminant: $$\Delta = 2.580 + 9.2625 = 11.8425 \quad ; \quad \sqrt{\Delta} = 3.4413$$ $$x_0 = \frac{-1.60625 + 3.4413}{1.95} = \frac{1.83505}{1.95} = 0.9411$$ (Rejecting the negative root as physically impossible.) **Step 6 — Find $y_D$ from (ii).** $$y_D = \frac{1.65 \times 0.9411}{1 + 0.65 \times 0.9411} = \frac{1.5528}{1.6117} = 0.9635$$ **Step 7 — Count theoretical stages saved.** By switching from a TC to a PC, the **condenser itself contributes 1 theoretical stage**. Therefore, the number of trays inside the column proper **decreases by 1**. The total theoretical stages required (from reboiler to distillate) is reduced by exactly one equilibrium contact. **Final Answers (b):** $$\boxed{x_0 = 0.9411, \quad y_D = 0.9635, \quad \text{Stages saved} = 1}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 Mistakes Students Make:** > 1. **Using $x_D$ instead of $y_D$ in a partial condenser** → wrong mass balance; loses 4–5 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the equilibrium constraint** between $y_D$ and $x_0$ → cannot close the system of equations; loses 3–4 marks. > 3. **Not converting condenser duty to kW** when the answer is required in kJ/h → unit-mismatch deduction of 1 mark. Always state units explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Total Condenser vs Partial Condenser** - ✅ **Total Condenser (TC):** 100 % condensation; $y_1 = x_D = x_0$; distillate is a **liquid**; contributes **0** theoretical stages. - ✅ **Partial Condenser (PC):** Partial condensation; $y_D \neq x_0$ but $y_D = K \cdot x_0$ (equilibrium); distillate is a **vapor**; contributes **1** theoretical stage. - ✅ **Material balance (both types):** $V_N = D(R+1)$, $L_0 = R \cdot D$. - ✅ **TC component balance** is trivially satisfied (all compositions equal) — no extra information. - ✅ **PC component balance** yields $x_0 = [(R+1) y_1 - y_D] / R$ — coupled with the equilibrium relation, this gives a unique solution. - ✅ **Degrees of Freedom (Phase Rule):** TC → F = 3; PC → F = 2. - ✅ **Operating Line Intercept:** TC uses $x_D/(R+1)$; PC uses $y_D/(R+1)$. - ✅ **McCabe–Thiele Top Step:** TC → starts on the **$45°$ diagonal** at $(x_D, x_D)$; PC → starts on the **equilibrium curve** at $(x_0, y_D)$. - ✅ **Condenser Duty:** TC → $Q_c = V_N \cdot \lambda$; PC → $Q_c = (V_N - D) \cdot \lambda$. - ✅ **Selection Criterion:** PC preferred for **close-boiling** mixtures ($\alpha < 1.5$), cryogenic separation, or when vapor product is desired; TC preferred for **wide-boiling** mixtures, lower capital cost, and simpler control. - ✅ **Reflux Drum:** TC → single-phase liquid drum; PC → two-phase V/L separator (boot + vapor space). - ✅ **Common Mistake:** Writing $x_D$ in a PC problem — always confirm the *physical state* of the distillate stream first. - ✅ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the equilibrium constraint in a PC — without it, the system of equations is underdetermined.

topics.title: Reboiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reboiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 REBOILERS — The Vapor Generator of Distillation Columns

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under **Module 2: Distillation** and directly supports **CO2** — *Apply mass transfer principles to design and analyze distillation column auxiliaries including reboilers and condensers.*

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **reboiler** (also called a **vaporizer** or **boiler**) is a specialized shell-and-tube (or kettle-type) **heat exchanger** installed at the bottom of a fractional distillation column. Its primary function is to:

> **Provide the descending liquid (bottoms) with the latent heat of vaporization required to generate the rising vapor stream that drives the mass-transfer process inside the column.**

The reboiler acts as the **primary vapor source** of the distillation column — without it, no stripping section, no rectifying section, and no separation.

| Parameter | Typical KTU Notation | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Duty | $Q_R$ | **W (Watts)** or **kW** |
| Heat Transfer Area | $A$ | **m²** |
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U$ | **W/(m²·K)** |
| Temperature Difference (LMTD) | $\Delta T_m$ | **K** or **°C** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | $\lambda$ | **kJ/kmol** or **kJ/kg** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Reboilers are classified as a **column auxiliary**. They do NOT perform mass transfer between two bulk phases — instead, they supply the **vapor boil-up** ($V'$) needed to maintain the internal reflux and mass-transfer driving force.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kettle on a Stove" Model 🍲

Imagine you are making **filter coffee** (a classic Kerala analogy ☕):

- The **bottom vessel (davara)** contains the coffee mixture (your column bottoms).
- You heat it on a stove → the **stove is the reboiler**.
- The vapor (steam + aroma) rises up through the **suzvar** (decanter) — this is the **rising vapor $V'$** entering the column.
- The water that condenses and drips back is your **internal reflux**.

**Why is this analogy powerful?**
1. Just as you control the stove knob to maintain steady coffee strength, the reboiler controls the **vapor boil-up rate** $V'$, which sets the separation efficiency.
2. If the stove is too weak → coffee is weak (low boil-up → poor separation).
3. If the stove is too strong → it boils over (flooding of the column!).

> [!TIP]
> **The reboiler is the "energy heart" of the distillation column** — every kmol of distillate produced requires a proportional amount of energy supplied through the reboiler.

---

## 1.3 Engineering Importance of Reboilers

| Real-World Application | Role of Reboiler |
|---|---|
| **Crude Oil Distillation (Refineries)** | Provides vapor for separating naphtha, diesel, kerosene |
| **Air Separation Units (ASUs)** | Vaporizes LOX/LIN at the bottom of cryogenic columns |
| **Ethylene Plants** | Maintains reboiled-absorber and demethanizer performance |
| **Pharma / Solvent Recovery** | Recovers pure ethanol, methanol, acetone |
| **Cogeneration & Power** | Feedwater heaters act like reboilers in deaerators |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse a **reboiler** with a **reboiler-condenser** (used in heat-pump distillation). A *reboiler* vaporizes the bottoms; a *reboiler-condenser* condenses an overhead vapor using column bottoms as coolant — **different function!**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — Types, Design, and Heat Transfer

---

## 2.1 Classification of Reboilers

KTU 2024 emphasizes the **three principal configurations** of reboilers. The classification is based on the **circulation mechanism** of the process fluid.

### 2.1.1 Kettle Reboiler (Forced Circulation, Pool Boiling)

- The process liquid **pools** in the shell side.
- Heating medium (usually steam) flows through the tubes.
- Vapor separates naturally in a large disengagement space.
- **No net circulation** — operates under **natural convection / nucleate boiling**.

**Engineering "Why" and "How":**
- ✅ *Why pool boiling?* — Higher heat flux possible (up to ~63 kW/m²).
- ✅ *How does it work?* — Bubbles nucleate on tube surface, detach, and rise; liquid naturally circulates due to density difference.
- ❌ *Limitation:* — Requires large shell diameter; expensive; residence time is high (may cause thermal degradation of heat-sensitive materials).

### 2.1.2 Vertical Thermosiphon Reboiler

- A **vertical column** is mounted at the bottom of the distillation column.
- Process fluid circulates by **natural convection** driven by density difference between the two-phase mixture in the tubes and the subcooled liquid in the column base.
- **No mechanical pump** required.

**Engineering Logic:**
- Step 1: Subcooled liquid enters tubes from the column sump.
- Step 2: Heat is added → liquid partially vaporizes (typically 10–30% vapor quality).
- Step 3: Two-phase mixture has lower density → **buoyancy-driven upward flow**.
- Step 4: Vapor-liquid separates in the column base.

### 2.1.3 Horizontal Thermosiphon Reboiler

- Similar principle to vertical, but laid out **horizontally**.
- Longer tubes, easier to install above grade.
- Used in **refinery crude units** due to mechanical simplicity.

### 2.1.4 Forced Circulation Reboiler

- A **mechanical pump** drives the liquid through the reboiler at high velocity.
- Used for **viscous**, **heat-sensitive**, or **fouling** fluids.
- Higher capital cost but excellent control.

---

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Feature | Kettle | Vertical Thermosiphon | Horizontal Thermosiphon | Forced Circulation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Circulation** | Natural (pool) | Natural (density) | Natural (density) | Mechanical pump |
| **Vapor Quality (x)** | Up to ~80% | 10–30% | 10–30% | 10–25% |
| **Heat Flux (kW/m²)** | 25–63 | 25–50 | 25–50 | Up to 75 |
| **Tube-side ΔP** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Capital Cost** | Highest | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Best For** | Clean liquids | General purpose | Refineries | Viscous/fouling |
| **Fouling Resistance** | Good | Limited | Limited | Excellent |
| **Residence Time** | High | Low | Low | Very Low |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE Part B question on reboilers.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_R = V' \cdot \lambda$ | Heat duty = vapor boil-up × latent heat |
| 2 | $Q_R = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$ | Standard heat exchanger equation |
| 3 | $Q_R = m_s (H_s^{out} - H_s^{in})$ | Heat supplied by utility (e.g., steam) |
| 4 | $\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD for counter-current flow |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{Q_R}{U \cdot \Delta T_m}$ | Area calculation |
| 6 | $V' = L_0 - B$ (for total condenser) | Vapor boil-up from mass balance |
| 7 | $V' = (1+q)F - B$ | General case (q = feed thermal condition) |
| 8 | $F = 0.30 \cdot \rho_l^{0.5} \cdot \lambda \cdot \dfrac{(T_w - T_{sat})^{0.6}}{\sigma^{0.6} \cdot \mu_l^{0.3}}$ | Zuber pool-boiling correlation (max heat flux) |
| 9 | $h_{nb} = 0.00122 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_l^{0.79} \cdot \rho_l^{0.49}}{\sigma^{0.5} \cdot \mu_l^{0.29} \cdot \lambda_v^{0.24} \cdot \rho_v^{0.24}} \cdot \Delta T_{sat}^{0.24} \cdot \Delta P_{sat}^{0.75}$ | Mostinski correlation for nucleate boiling |
| 10 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \dfrac{1}{h_{nb}} + R_w$ | Combined coefficient (convection + boiling) |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Tip for KTU Numerical:** In a thermosiphon, the **driving force** for circulation is the **density difference** between the inlet (single-phase liquid) and outlet (two-phase mixture). The standard correlation is:
> $$\Delta P_{driving} = (\rho_l - \rho_{tp}) \cdot g \cdot H$$
> where $H$ is the tube length and $\rho_{tp}$ is the two-phase density evaluated as $\rho_{tp} = \rho_l \cdot (1-x) + \rho_v \cdot x$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Reboiler Design Equations

- **Refinery Design:** Determines the size of fired heaters ($Q_R$ can be MW-scale).
- **Energy Integration (Pinch Analysis):** Reboilers are often the **largest energy consumer** in a chemical plant — pinch analysis minimizes $Q_R$ through heat integration.
- **Process Safety:** Oversized reboilers can cause **runaway reactions** in heat-sensitive systems.
- **Environmental:** Lower $Q_R$ → lower CO₂ emissions → greener process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Sustainability Hook:** Questions on "reboiler energy savings" via **vapor recompression (VRC)** or **mechanical vapor recompression (MVR)** are increasingly common in the **Sustainable Engineering** cross-module sections.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Core Reboiler Energy Balance

Consider a distillation column operating at **steady state**. A reboiler supplies the boil-up vapor $V'$ (kmol/s) with latent heat of vaporization $\lambda$ (kJ/kmol).

### Step 1: Energy In = Energy Out + Heat Supplied

At steady state, the **net energy input to the column from the reboiler** must equal the energy carried by the vapor leaving the reboiler.

$$Q_R = V' \cdot \lambda$$

**Conversion Logic:** The total heat required to vaporize a stream is the product of the molar flow rate of vapor generated and the molar latent heat of vaporization. Units: $\left(\dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{s}}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kmol}}\right) = \text{kW}$.

### Step 2: From the Reboiler's Heat-Transfer Side

The heat exchanger side of the reboiler obeys the standard rate equation:

$$Q_R = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

**Conversion Logic:** $U$ (overall heat transfer coefficient) captures the resistance of tube wall, scale, and both convective films. $A$ is the heat-transfer area. $\Delta T_m$ is the log-mean temperature difference between the heating medium (steam at $T_s$) and the boiling process fluid (at $T_b$).

### Step 3: Equating the Two Sides

Since both expressions represent the same physical quantity $Q_R$:

$$U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m = V' \cdot \lambda$$

### Step 4: Solving for the Required Area

$$A = \dfrac{V' \cdot \lambda}{U \cdot \Delta T_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE KTU equation** — it appears in nearly every Part B reboiler design question. Memorize it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LMTD for Steam-Heated Reboiler

The heating medium is saturated steam at temperature $T_s$, condensing at constant temperature. The process fluid boils at $T_b$ (essentially constant). This is a **constant-temperature** exchange on both sides.

**Step 1:** Define terminal temperature differences.
- Hot end: $\Delta T_1 = T_s - T_b$ (steam entering vs. boiling liquid)
- Cold end: $\Delta T_2 = T_s - T_b$ (condensate leaving vs. boiling liquid)

**Step 2:** Observe that $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ because both fluids are isothermal!

**Step 3:** Therefore, the LMTD reduces to the arithmetic mean:

$$\Delta T_m = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = T_s - T_b$$

**Conversion Logic:** For pure condensation heating a pure boiling liquid, the LMTD equals the simple temperature difference. This greatly simplifies the KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Typical KTU 14-Mark Style

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern - Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> A distillation column produces **$D = 50 \text{ kmol/h}$** of distillate with reflux ratio $R = 1.5$. The column uses a **total condenser** and saturated steam at **$T_s = 160 °C$** heats the reboiler. The bottoms boil at **$T_b = 140 °C$**. Latent heat of vaporization of bottoms = **$\lambda = 30{,}000 \text{ kJ/kmol}$**. Overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 850 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$.
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Reboiler heat duty $Q_R$ in kW.
> (b) Required heat-transfer area $A$ in m².
> (c) Daily steam consumption (in kg) if latent heat of steam = 2200 kJ/kg.

### Part (a): Heat Duty $Q_R$

**Step 1:** Vapor boil-up rate from overall mass and energy balance.
- Reflux $L = R \cdot D = 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ kmol/h}$
- Vapor from reboiler: $V' = L + D = 75 + 50 = 125 \text{ kmol/h}$

**Step 2:** Convert to per-second basis.

$$V' = \dfrac{125}{3600} \text{ kmol/s} = 0.03472 \text{ kmol/s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply $Q_R = V' \cdot \lambda$.

$$Q_R = 0.03472 \times 30{,}000 = 1041.67 \text{ kW}$$

### Part (b): Required Area $A$

**Step 4:** Calculate LMTD.

$$\Delta T_m = T_s - T_b = 160 - 140 = 20 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5:** Apply $A = \dfrac{Q_R}{U \cdot \Delta T_m}$ (with consistent units: $Q_R$ in W).

$$Q_R = 1041.67 \text{ kW} = 1{,}041{,}670 \text{ W}$$

$$A = \dfrac{1{,}041{,}670}{850 \times 20} = \dfrac{1{,}041{,}670}{17{,}000} = 61.27 \text{ m}^2$$

### Part (c): Daily Steam Consumption

**Step 6:** Daily energy required.

$$E_{daily} = Q_R \times 24 \times 3600 = 1{,}041{,}670 \times 86{,}400 = 8.99 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} = 89{,}999 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to steam mass.

$$m_{steam} = \dfrac{89{,}999{,}000 \text{ kJ}}{2200 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 40{,}909 \text{ kg/day} \approx 40.9 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Verification:** In real refineries, reboiler duties range from **500 kW to 50 MW**. Our value of 1.04 MW is consistent with a small-to-medium chemical column. ✅

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operating Variables

| Variable | Increase in $U$ | Increase in $\Delta T_m$ | Increase in $\lambda$ | Net Effect on $A$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Steam Pressure** | — | ↑ (higher $T_s$) | — | ↓ Smaller area needed |
| **Boiling Liquid Viscosity** | ↓ | — | — | ↑ Larger area needed |
| **Fouling on Tube** | ↓ | — | — | ↑ Larger area needed |
| **Column Pressure** | Variable | ↓ (closer to $T_s$) | ↓ | ↑ Larger area needed |
| **Reflux Ratio $R$** | — | — | — | ↑ $V'$ ↑, so $A$ ↑ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why does increasing column pressure increase $A$?**
> - Higher pressure → smaller $\Delta T_m$ (closer to steam $T_s$).
> - Higher pressure → higher boiling point → smaller driving force.
> - Higher pressure → lower $\lambda$ → more vapor needed for same separation.
> All three effects combine to demand a **larger heat-transfer area**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

## 4.1 Reboiler Placement in a Distillation Column System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feed[F Feed In] --> ColDist[Distillation Column PCBBT503]
    ColDist --> VapTop[V Vapor to Condenser]
    VapTop --> Cond[Total Condenser]
    Cond --> RefluxDrum[Reflux Drum]
    RefluxDrum --> Reflux[L Reflux Back to Column]
    RefluxDrum --> Distillate[D Distillate Product]
    ColDist --> Bottoms[B Bottoms Liquid]
    Bottoms --> Reb[REBOILER]
    Steam[Steam Supply T_s] --> Reb
    Reb --> SteamReturn[Condensate Out]
    Reb --> VaporUp[V Rising Vapor to Column]
    VaporUp -.-> ColDist
```

---

## 4.2 Internal Flow Path of a Kettle Reboiler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LiqIn[Liquid Inlet] --> ShellSide[Shell Side Pool]
    ShellSide --> TubeBundle[Tube Bundle]
    TubeBundle --> ShellSide
    TubeBundle --> VaporSpace[Disengagement Space]
    VaporSpace --> VaporOut[Vapor Outlet]
    SteamIn[Steam In] --> TubeIn
    TubeIn --> CondOut[Condensate Out]
    TubeBundle -.-> NucleateBoiling[Nucleate Boiling on Tube Surface]
```

---

## 4.3 Thermosiphon Circulation — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sump[Column Sump Subcooled Liquid] --> TubeInlet[Tube Inlet]
    TubeInlet --> TubeHeat[Heated Section]
    TubeHeat --> TwoPhaseOutlet[Two-Phase Outlet]
    TwoPhaseOutlet --> Separator[Liquid Vapor Separator]
    Separator --> VaporUp1[Vapor to Column]
    Separator --> LiquidBack[Liquid Returns to Sump]
    LiquidBack -.-> Sump
    TubeInlet -.-> DensityDiff[Density Difference Buoyancy Force]
    TwoPhaseOutlet -.-> DensityDiff
```

---

## 4.4 Reboiler Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Reboiler Type] --> Q1{Is fluid heat-sensitive or viscous?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Forced[Forced Circulation Reboiler]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is residence time a concern?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| ThermoV[Vertical Thermosiphon]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Refinery scale and horizontal space?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| ThermoH[Horizontal Thermosiphon]
    Q3 -->|No| KettleOut[Kettle Reboiler]
    Forced --> End
    ThermoV --> End
    ThermoH --> End
    KettleOut --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** In thermosiphon flowcharts, always trace the **density-driven loop** — the two-phase mixture in the heated tube is **lighter** than the subcooled liquid in the sump, which creates a **natural convective loop** without a pump.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**
> **Define a reboiler. List any two types of reboilers used in distillation columns.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **reboiler** is a heat exchanger located at the bottom of a distillation column that supplies the heat required to vaporize a portion of the column bottoms, thereby generating the **rising vapor stream ($V'$)** that drives the mass-transfer process in the stripping section.

**Two types of reboilers** (any two, 1.5 marks each):
1. **Kettle Reboiler** — Pool-boiling type, large shell, vapor disengagement space.
2. **Vertical Thermosiphon Reboiler** — Natural-circulation type, driven by density difference.
3. **Horizontal Thermosiphon Reboiler** — Horizontal layout, common in refinery crude units.
4. **Forced-Circulation Reboiler** — Uses a pump, suitable for viscous/heat-sensitive fluids.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**
> **What is meant by "vapor boil-up" in a distillation column? Why is it important for reboiler design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Vapor boil-up ($V'$)** is the molar flow rate of vapor generated in the reboiler and returned to the bottom of the distillation column. It is typically expressed in **kmol/h** or **kmol/s**.

**Importance in reboiler design (1.5 marks):**
- The reboiler heat duty is directly proportional to $V'$: $Q_R = V' \cdot \lambda$.
- A higher $V'$ requires a larger heat-transfer area: $A = \dfrac{V' \cdot \lambda}{U \cdot \Delta T_m}$.
- $V'$ determines the **stripping-section operating line** and hence the number of theoretical stages.
- It also controls the **flooding margin** — excessive $V'$ leads to column flooding.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Writing $V' = D$ (distillate flow). The correct relationship is $V' = L + D$ (for total condenser) or $V' = D(R+1)$. Losing 1 mark for this is very common.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **kettle reboiler**. Discuss its advantages and limitations.
>
> **(b)** A distillation column reboiler uses saturated steam at **$T_s = 180 °C$** to vaporize a bottoms liquid boiling at **$T_b = 145 °C$**. The required vapor boil-up rate is **$V' = 80 \text{ kmol/h}$** with latent heat **$\lambda = 28{,}000 \text{ kJ/kmol}$**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **$U = 1000 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$**. Calculate the **heat duty** and the **required heat-transfer area**.

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — Kettle Reboiler [7 Marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A kettle reboiler is a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** with a relatively large shell.
- The **tube bundle** is immersed in the process liquid that pools in the shell.
- A **vapor disengagement space** (typically 30–50% of shell diameter) is provided above the tube bundle.
- Heating medium (saturated steam) flows through the tubes; process liquid surrounds the tubes.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Steam condenses on the tube walls, transferring latent heat.
- Process liquid in contact with the hot tubes reaches saturation and **nucleate boiling** occurs.
- Bubbles form at the tube surface, detach, and rise through the bulk liquid.
- The vapor disengages in the upper space and exits to the column.
- Liquid returns naturally to the pool — no external circulation is required.

**Advantages (0.5 marks):**
- Simple design, no pump, handles wide range of vapor qualities.
- Easy maintenance (bundle is removable).

**Limitations (0.5 marks):**
- High residence time → not suitable for heat-sensitive materials.
- Large shell → high capital cost.
- Limited heat flux (~63 kW/m² max).

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical Design [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $T_s = 180 °C$, $T_b = 145 °C$, $V' = 80 \text{ kmol/h}$, $\lambda = 28{,}000 \text{ kJ/kmol}$, $U = 1000 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$.

**Step 1: Convert $V'$ to kmol/s.**

$$V' = \dfrac{80}{3600} = 0.02222 \text{ kmol/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate heat duty $Q_R$.**

$$Q_R = V' \cdot \lambda = 0.02222 \times 28{,}000 = 622.22 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q_R = 622{,}222 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Calculate LMTD.**

Since both fluids are isothermal (steam condensing, liquid boiling):

$$\Delta T_m = T_s - T_b = 180 - 145 = 35 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4: Calculate area $A$.**

$$A = \dfrac{Q_R}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \dfrac{622{,}222}{1000 \times 35} = \dfrac{622{,}222}{35{,}000} = 17.78 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Verification.**
- Sanity check: A 17.78 m² area corresponds to ~30–40 standard 3-m tubes. Reasonable for an 80 kmol/h column. ✅

**Final Answer:** $Q_R = 622.22 \text{ kW}$, $A = 17.78 \text{ m}^2$.

---

### **Question B** (Internal Choice) [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> **(a)** Compare the working principles of **kettle**, **vertical thermosiphon**, and **forced-circulation reboilers** with neat diagrams. Indicate the typical range of heat flux for each.
>
> **(b)** A column bottoms stream of **$2500 \text{ kg/h}$** with specific heat **$C_p = 3.2 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$** is to be vaporized at **$T_b = 130 °C$**. Saturated steam is available at **$T_s = 165 °C$** with latent heat **$\lambda_s = 2200 \text{ kJ/kg}$**. If the overall heat transfer coefficient is **$U = 750 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$** and the reboiler is to be designed with **$A = 25 \text{ m}^2$**, determine the **steam flow rate** required.

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — Comparison [7 Marks]

A clean comparison table is expected (full table gets 7 marks; partial table gets 4–5 marks).

| Feature | Kettle | Vertical Thermosiphon | Forced Circulation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Circulation Mechanism | Natural (pool boiling) | Natural (density-driven) | Mechanical pump |
| Layout | Horizontal, large shell | Vertical, attached to sump | External pump loop |
| Vapor Quality | Up to 80% | 10–30% | 10–25% |
| Heat Flux Range | 25–63 kW/m² | 25–50 kW/m² | Up to 75 kW/m² |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Best For | Clean, non-sensitive | General purpose | Viscous, fouling fluids |
| Residence Time | High | Low | Very Low |
| Maintenance | Easy (removable bundle) | Moderate | High (pump, seals) |

**Key Explanation Points (each 1 mark for the working principle):**
- **Kettle:** Boiling on submerged tubes, no net flow, vapor separates in upper dome.
- **Thermosiphon:** Density difference between two-phase mixture (lighter) and subcooled liquid (heavier) drives natural circulation.
- **Forced:** A pump ensures high velocity → better heat transfer, prevents fouling and thermal degradation.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — Steam Flow Rate [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $m_{bottoms} = 2500 \text{ kg/h} = 0.6944 \text{ kg/s}$, $C_p = 3.2 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $T_b = 130 °C$, $T_s = 165 °C$, $\lambda_s = 2200 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $U = 750 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$, $A = 25 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 1: Interpret the problem.**

The problem says the stream is "to be **vaporized**" — we calculate the heat required for vaporization based on heating it from feed to boiling. For simplification, we assume the feed is at $T_b$ and we need to supply heat equivalent to vaporization (using $Q_R = m_{steam} \cdot \lambda_s$).

**Step 2: Calculate LMTD.**

$$\Delta T_m = T_s - T_b = 165 - 130 = 35 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Calculate heat duty $Q_R$ from heat-transfer side.**

$$Q_R = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m = 750 \times 25 \times 35 = 656{,}250 \text{ W} = 656.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Calculate steam mass flow rate.**

$$m_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_R}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{656.25}{2200} = 0.2983 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5: Convert to kg/h.**

$$m_{steam} = 0.2983 \times 3600 = 1073.85 \text{ kg/h} \approx 1.07 \text{ tonnes/h}$$

**Verification:** A steam rate of ~1 tonne/h is realistic for a small-to-medium column reboiler. ✅

**Final Answer:** $m_{steam} \approx 1073.85 \text{ kg/h}$ (or 0.2983 kg/s).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency (1 mark lost):** Converting $V'$ in kmol/h but using $U$ in W/(m²·K) — must convert $Q_R$ to **Watts** consistently.
> 2. **Wrong LMTD (1 mark lost):** Using arithmetic mean instead of LMTD for non-isothermal streams. For pure steam + pure boiling, both are equal — so don't over-complicate.
> 3. **Forgetting $V' = L + D$** (not $V' = D$) — loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $h_{nb}$ (nucleate boiling) with $h_{conv}$ (convective boiling)** in thermosiphon problems — both contribute, with $h_{nb}$ usually dominating.
> 5. **Not specifying reboiler type** in design problems — KTU expects a justification sentence ("A kettle reboiler is selected because…").

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **reboiler** is the **vapor generator** at the bottom of a distillation column — supplies latent heat to the bottoms.
- ✅ **Three main types:** Kettle (pool boiling), Thermosiphon (natural circulation), Forced Circulation (pumped).
- ✅ **Master equation:** $Q_R = V' \cdot \lambda = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$.
- ✅ **Area formula:** $A = \dfrac{V' \cdot \lambda}{U \cdot \Delta T_m}$.
- ✅ **For steam-heated reboilers**, $\Delta T_m = T_s - T_b$ (simple arithmetic).
- ✅ **Vapor boil-up** $V' = L + D$ (for total condenser) or $V' = (1+q)F - B$ (general case).
- ✅ **Thermosiphon circulation** is driven by the **density difference** between the two-phase mixture in the tube and the subcooled liquid in the sump.
- ✅ **Kettle reboiler** is preferred for **high vapor quality** and **clean fluids**; **Forced Circulation** is used for **viscous, fouling, or heat-sensitive** materials.
- ✅ **Pinch Analysis & VRC/MVR** are modern ways to reduce reboiler energy consumption — increasingly important for **Sustainable Engineering** questions.
- ✅ **Mostinski correlation** is the standard for estimating nucleate boiling heat transfer coefficient.
- ✅ **Maximum heat flux (Zuber's correlation)** sets the upper limit to prevent **burnout**.
- ✅ **Fouling resistance** must always be included in real designs (typically $R_f = 0.0002 \text{ to } 0.001 \text{ m²·K/W}$).
- ✅ **Common KTU pitfall:** Distinguishing **boiling** (in reboilers) from **forced convection** (in standard heat exchangers) — the heat transfer mechanism is fundamentally different.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid-Liquid Extraction: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liquid-Liquid Extraction: Principle**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid-Liquid Extraction (LLE)**, also termed **Solvent Extraction**, is a **diffusion-based separation operation** in which a **solute (or solutes)** is preferentially transferred from one **immiscible (or partially miscible) liquid phase** — the *carrier* or *feed* phase — into a second liquid phase — the *solvent* phase — by exploiting differences in **equilibrium distribution (solubility)** of the solute between the two contacting liquids. The operation is governed by **two-film mass transfer theory** at the liquid–liquid interface and is described quantitatively by the **distribution (partition) coefficient, $K_D$**, and the **selectivity (separation factor), $\beta$**.

In KTU-speak: the solute $A$ sits in feed liquid $B$ (often aqueous). We add a second liquid $S$ (the solvent, often organic) that is **immiscible with B**. After intimate mixing and phase disengagement, $A$ preferentially partitions into $S$ — yielding a **solvent-rich extract phase** (rich in $A$ + $S$) and a **solvent-lean raffinate phase** (depleted in $A$, still containing $B$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dirty T-Shirt & Petrol Bowl"

Imagine you spill turmeric (yellow stain) on your white cotton T-shirt (the *feed phase*). Water alone cannot remove it because turmeric is sparingly soluble in water. But if you dunk the shirt in a bowl of petrol (the *solvent phase*), the yellow turmeric molecules — which "prefer" the non-polar petrol — **jump out of the fabric fibres into the petrol**. The shirt emerges cleaner; the petrol turns yellow.

Three subtle things to notice:
1. The fabric (carrier) and the petrol (solvent) do **not** mix — they remain as two distinct layers (the *immiscibility requirement*).
2. The driving force is **chemical affinity / equilibrium solubility**, not temperature or pressure alone.
3. The operation is **selective** — if both turmeric and salt were on the shirt, the turmeric would go into the petrol while the salt would stay with the water-wetted fabric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics & Constants used in LLE:**
> - **Distribution Coefficient** $K_D$ — dimensionless (ratio of mole fractions or mass fractions at equilibrium).
> - **Selectivity** $\beta$ — dimensionless, $\beta \geq 1$ for a feasible separation.
> - **Operating line slopes** — dimensionless on ternary diagrams.
> - **Stage efficiency** — expressed as a percentage (Murphree / overall).

## 1.3 When is LLE the Preferred Separation?

| Situation | Why LLE Wins |
|---|---|
| Solute is **heat-sensitive** (e.g., antibiotics, vitamins) | Operates at **ambient temperature** — no distillation damage. |
| Relative volatility is **near unity** (e.g., close-boiling isomers) | Distillation fails; selectivity via solvent works. |
| **Dilute** solute in a large feed volume | Extraction concentrates the solute into a smaller solvent stream. |
| Components form **azeotropes** | Solvent breaks the azeotrope. |
| Aqueous / non-aqueous split is required (e.g., wastewater phenol removal) | Direct phase transfer is the cheapest option. |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU exam:** If the question gives a *ternary mixture* of liquids A–B–C with A distributed between B and C, and asks for stages — it is **LLE**, not distillation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Principle — Why Does the Solute Move?

The transfer is driven by a **chemical potential gradient**. At the interface, the escaping tendency (fugacity, $\mu_i$) of the solute in the two phases must equalise at equilibrium. Since the phases are largely immiscible, the activity of the solute in each phase differs enormously — forcing mass transfer from the "high $\mu$" phase to the "low $\mu$" phase until equilibrium is reached.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Mass Transfer Event

1. **Approach & Contact:** The two immiscible liquids are dispersed (one into the other) to maximise interfacial area $a$ (m²/m³).
2. **Film Diffusion:** Solute $A$ diffuses through the *raffinate-side film* to the interface — governed by film coefficient $k_{R}$.
3. **Interface Equilibrium:** At the interface, the partition law holds: $y_{A,i} = K_D \, x_{A,i}$.
4. **Film Diffusion (Solvent side):** Solute $A$ diffuses through the *solvent-side film* into the bulk — governed by $k_{S}$.
5. **Coalescence & Phase Separation:** Droplets re-form bulk phases under gravity in the settler; extract and raffinate are withdrawn.

> [!NOTE]
> **Nernst's Distribution Law (1891):** *"At constant temperature and at equilibrium, a solute distributes itself between two immiscible liquids in a constant ratio of concentrations, provided the molecular state of the solute is the same in both phases."*

## 2.2 Distribution Coefficient (Partition Coefficient)

$$K_D \;=\; \frac{C_{A,\text{solvent}}}{C_{A,\text{raffinate}}} \;=\; \frac{y_A}{x_A} \quad \text{(at equilibrium)}$$

where $y_A$ and $x_A$ are equilibrium concentrations of $A$ in the solvent (extract) and raffinate phases respectively. If the equilibrium curve is linear, $y_A = m \, x_A$ where the slope $m$ is the constant $K_D$.

## 2.3 Selectivity (Separation Factor), $\beta$

For a system separating $A$ from $B$ using solvent $S$:

$$\beta \;=\; \frac{K_{D,A}}{K_{D,B}} \;=\; \frac{(y_A / x_A)}{(y_B / x_B)}$$

- $\beta = 1$ → **no separation possible** by single contact.
- $\beta > 1$ → $A$ preferentially goes to the solvent phase (favoured case).
- $\beta \to \infty$ → perfect one-stage separation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Legend | KTU Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_D = y_A / x_A$ | $y_A, x_A$ = equilibrium conc. of $A$ in extract & raffinate | Equilibrium tie-line |
| 2 | $y = m x$ (linear equilibrium) | $m$ = slope of equilibrium line | Simplifies single-stage problems |
| 3 | $\beta = K_{D,A} / K_{D,B}$ | Separation factor | Solvent selection check |
| 4 | $F + S = E + R$ | Total material balance (overall) | Total flow balance |
| 5 | $F x_{F,A} + S y_{S,A} = E y_{A} + R x_{A}$ | Solute $A$ balance | Component balance |
