## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "selectivity" (β) in liquid-liquid extraction. Why is β > 1 essential for a workable extraction solvent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Selectivity β is defined as the ratio of the distribution coefficient of the solute (A) to that of the diluent (B): $\beta = K_D^{A} / K_D^{B}$.
- **(1 Mark)** It is dimensionless and quantifies the solvent's ability to preferentially dissolve A over B.
- **(1 Mark)** For extraction to be feasible, β > 1 is essential. If β = 1, the solvent has no preferential action and the extract will have the same composition as the feed. Industrial designs target β > 10 for economic viability.

---

#### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State any three "must-have" properties of a solvent for liquid-liquid extraction, with one-line justifications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **(1 Mark) Chemical inertness** — Prevents side reactions that would reduce yield and contaminate the product.
2. **(1 Mark) Immiscibility with the feed** — Two distinct liquid phases must form; otherwise no phase separation is possible.
3. **(1 Mark) High recoverability** — The solvent must be separable from the solute (typically by distillation) to recycle it and avoid continuous solvent loss.

*(Alternative acceptable answers: high capacity K_D, sufficient density difference, low cost, low toxicity, low mutual solubility.)*

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the criteria used for the selection of a solvent for liquid-liquid extraction. Discuss any **eight** properties with their engineering significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A feed containing 30 wt% acetic acid (A) in water (B) is extracted with methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK) as the solvent. The distribution coefficient of acetic acid between MIBK and water is $K_D = 0.85$. Calculate the **minimum solvent-to-feed ratio** required to reduce the raffinate acetic acid concentration to 5 wt%. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A well-graded answer should cover at least 8 of the following properties. Each carries ≈ 0.75–1 Mark with justification.

1. **High Distribution Coefficient (K_D)** — Indicates strong affinity of solvent for solute; reduces solvent circulation rate. (1 Mark)
2. **High Selectivity (β)** — Ensures preferential extraction of solute over diluent; reduces the number of stages. (1 Mark)
3. **Immiscibility with Feed** — Essential for forming two separable liquid phases. (1 Mark)
4. **Sufficient Density Difference (Δρ)** — Allows gravity-based phase separation in decanters; typical Δρ > 0.05 g/cm³. (1 Mark)
5. **Recoverability** — Low boiling point differential from solute enables distillation-based recovery; reduces OPEX. (1 Mark)
6. **Chemical Inertness** — Prevents unwanted reactions, side products, and degradation. (1 Mark)
7. **Low Toxicity and Flammability** — Critical for pharmaceutical and food applications; worker safety. (1 Mark)
8. **Low Viscosity and Adequate Interfacial Tension** — Promotes good mass transfer and prevents emulsion formation. (1 Mark)
9. **Low Cost and Ready Availability** — Direct impact on plant economics. (1 Mark — bonus point if mentioned.)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $x_F = 0.30$ (mass fraction of acetic acid in feed)
- $x_R = 0.05$ (target raffinate concentration)
- $K_D = 0.85$
- Inlet solvent is solute-free: $y_{S,in} = 0$

**Step 1: State the formula** (1 Mark)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{S}{F}\right)_{min} = \frac{x_F - x_R}{K_D \cdot x_R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values** (2 Marks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{S}{F}\right)_{min} &= \frac{0.30 - 0.05}{0.85 \times 0.05} \\
&= \frac{0.25}{0.0425}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute** (2 Marks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\left(\frac{S}{F}\right)_{min} = 5.88 \; \text{kg solvent / kg feed}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation** (2 Marks)
- This means for every 1 kg of feed, at least **5.88 kg of fresh MIBK** is needed in a single stage.
- Actual design uses 1.2–1.5 × this minimum, i.e., **S/F ≈ 7–9 kg/kg**.
- If a solvent with $K_D = 2.0$ were available, $(S/F)_{min}$ would drop to $0.25/(2.0 \times 0.05) = 2.5$, demonstrating the cost leverage of a high-$K_D$ solvent.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchical procedure** for selecting a solvent for a liquid-liquid extraction process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a ternary system A (solute) – B (diluent) – S (solvent), the equilibrium data at 25°C are:
- $x_A = 0.10$ (raffinate), $y_A = 0.42$ (extract)
- $x_B = 0.05$ (raffinate), $y_B = 0.04$ (extract)

Compute the **distribution coefficients** $K_D^{A}$ and $K_D^{B}$, the **selectivity β**, and comment on whether the solvent is suitable. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

*(The student should reproduce the hierarchical flowchart from Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

A complete answer includes:
- **Tier 1 (Must-have, 4 Marks):** Inertness, immiscibility, Δρ, capacity, recoverability — each explained in 1 line.
- **Tier 2 (Highly desirable, 2 Marks):** Selectivity, safety, viscosity, cost.
- **Tier 3 (Refining, 1 Mark):** Interfacial tension, thermal stability, environmental impact.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Calculate $K_D^A$** (2 Marks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_D^A &= \frac{y_A}{x_A} = \frac{0.42}{0.10} = 4.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate $K_D^B$** (2 Marks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_D^B &= \frac{y_B}{x_B} = \frac{0.04}{0.05} = 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Selectivity β** (2 Marks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{K_D^A}{K_D^B} = \frac{4.2}{0.80} = 5.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comment on suitability** (1 Mark)
- Since β = 5.25 > 1, the solvent preferentially extracts the solute A over the diluent B.
- However, β = 5.25 is **modest by industrial standards** (target β > 10). The solvent is *workable* but may require more theoretical stages than a more selective alternative.
- The high $K_D^A$ = 4.2 indicates **good capacity**, keeping solvent flow rates reasonable.

**Verdict:** The solvent is **acceptable** for a moderately difficult separation, but a search for a higher-β solvent is recommended for cost-optimized design.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Solvent-Selection Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing K_D with β (Capacity vs. Selectivity):** Students often write "the solvent is good because K_D is high" without checking whether the diluent's K_D is *also* high. Always report β explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting units and basis in numerical problems:** KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the basis (mass or mole) is not stated, or if wt% vs. mole fraction is mixed.
> 3. **Ignoring mutual solubility in Tier 1 screening:** A solvent with high β but high mutual solubility (e.g., chloroform-water ≈ 0.8 wt%) can cause severe raffinate contamination — always check Tier 1 first.
> 4. **Skipping the recovery consideration:** KTU specifically asks "how will you recover the solvent?" If your answer omits this, expect to lose 1.5–2 marks.
> 5. **No physical interpretation of numerical answers:** Stating $(S/F)_{min} = 5.88$ without explaining "this means 5.88 kg of solvent per kg of feed, leading to high OPEX" loses the engineering-reasoning marks.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3, Selection of a Solvent for Good Extraction):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Solvent selection is a *systematic screening* of candidates against physical, chemical, safety, and economic criteria.
- ✅ **Tier 1 (Must-have):** Inertness, immiscibility, Δρ, capacity ($K_D$), recoverability — all 5 are non-negotiable.
- ✅ **Tier 2 (Highly desirable):** Selectivity β, low toxicity, low viscosity, low cost.
- ✅ **Tier 3 (Refining):** Interfacial tension, thermal stability, environmental impact.
- ✅ **Distribution Coefficient:** $K_D = C_{extract} / C_{raffinate} = y_A / x_A$ — measures *capacity*.
- ✅ **Selectivity:** $\beta = K_D^{solute} / K_D^{diluent}$ — measures *preference*; must be > 1, ideally > 10.
- ✅ **Minimum Solvent-to-Feed Ratio:** $(S/F)_{min} = (x_F - x_R) / (K_D \cdot x_R)$ — inversely proportional to $K_D$.
- ✅ **Purity Equation:** $P_E = \beta P_F / [1 + (\beta - 1) P_F]$ — shows β controls extract purity.
- ✅ **Common Industrial Solvents:** Hexane, toluene, MIBK, butyl acetate, ethyl acetate, chloroform, sulfolane, NMP, supercritical CO₂, water.
- ✅ **Typical Process Cascade:** $K_D$ ↑ → $N$ stages ↓, $S$ flow ↓ → CAPEX ↓, OPEX ↓.
- ✅ **Decision Tool:** Use a weighted **Robbins–King Selection Matrix** with score = weight × rating for each property.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** TBP in kerosene (nuclear reprocessing), MIBK (acetic acid recovery), sulfolane (aromatic extraction), supercritical CO₂ (decaffeination).
- ✅ **Graphical Tool:** Ternary equilibrium diagram (e.g., right-triangle) — visualize tie-lines and binodal curve to confirm two-phase region exists.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Skill Tested:** Justification of solvent choice using *quantitative* β, $K_D$, and $(S/F)_{min}$ — not just qualitative listing.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Type 1 Liquid Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Type 1 Liquid-Liquid Extraction** refers to a solvent extraction operation in which the two bulk liquid phases — the **diluent (carrier) phase B** carrying the solute, and the **extracting solvent phase C** — are **essentially completely immiscible (mutually insoluble)** in each other. Only the **solute A** distributes itself between the two liquid phases until thermodynamic equilibrium is established.

In Treybal's classical mass-transfer classification, this is the simplest and most idealized category of liquid extraction, where:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type 1 System — The Treybal Definition**
> The diluent **B** and the solvent **C** are mutually insoluble. Hence, the bulk flow rates of B (denoted $L$, kg/h) and C (denoted $S$, kg/h) remain **constant** from stage to stage. The distribution of solute A between the two phases follows a single, time-invariant equilibrium relationship.

**Treybal Nomenclature used throughout this module:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| **A** | Solute (the species being transferred) |
| **B** | Carrier / diluent phase (e.g., water) |
| **C** | Extracting solvent (e.g., isopropyl ether, MIBK) |
| **L** | Mass flow rate of B (kg/h) — constant in Type 1 |
| **S** | Mass flow rate of C (kg/h) — constant in Type 1 |
| **x** | Mass fraction of A in B-phase |
| **y** | Mass fraction of A in C-phase |
| **X** | Mass ratio of A in B-phase (kg A / kg B) = $x/(1-x)$ |
| **Y** | Mass ratio of A in C-phase (kg A / kg C) = $y/(1-y)$ |
| **K** | Distribution coefficient = $y_A/x_A = Y/X$ |

### Canonical Engineering Examples of Type 1 Systems

- **Acetic acid (A) in water (B)** extracted by **isopropyl ether (C)**
- **Aromatic hydrocarbons (A) in paraffinic oils (B)** extracted by liquid **SO₂ (C)**
- **Benzene (A) in heptane (B)** extracted by **diethylene glycol (C)**

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Tea Bag in Hot Water" 🍵

Imagine a tea bag dipped into a cup of hot water. The tea compounds (solute **A**) are locked inside the solid tea leaves (one bulk phase), and the hot water is the other bulk phase. The compounds gradually diffuse from inside the bag into the water, until the concentration of tea compounds *inside* the bag and *in* the surrounding water reach a fixed ratio. 

Now translate this to liquid-liquid extraction: instead of a solid tea bag and water, you have **two immiscible liquids** — say water and oil. A third compound (the "tea", i.e., the solute **A**) sits in the water but "prefers" to dissolve in the oil. When you shake the mixture and let it settle, the solute migrates from the water-rich (raffinate) phase into the oil-rich (extract) phase, governed by a constant partition ratio **K**. Since water and oil do not appreciably mix, the *amounts* of water and oil remain unchanged — only the solute hops between them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Type 1 is the "ideal case" taught first:** Because the flow rates of B and C do not change stage-to-stage, the mass balance equations become linear. The equilibrium curve reduces to a **straight line through the origin** ($Y = KX$), and analytical (closed-form) solutions like the **Kremser equation** become possible — no graphical trial-and-error is strictly required.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Distribution Curve is a Straight Line (Type 1)

Because B and C are completely immiscible, the only equilibrium relationship we need is the partitioning of A between them. By definition of the **distribution coefficient** (Nernst's Law):

$$y_A = K \cdot x_A \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad Y = K \cdot X$$

This is a **linear, time-invariant, single-parameter** model. The constant $K$ depends only on temperature and the chemical identity of A, B, and C — not on concentration (for dilute systems).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In KTU exam questions, the system is almost always given as Type 1 unless explicitly stated otherwise. The phrase "the two liquids are immiscible" or "the solvent and diluent are completely insoluble" is the trigger to apply the linear $Y = KX$ relationship and the Kremser equations derived below.

### 2.2 Material Balance Framework

For any single ideal stage where fresh (or staged) feed and solvent are contacted and equilibrated:

$$L X_F + S Y_{\text{in,C}} = L X_{\text{out,B}} + S Y_{\text{out,C}}$$

Because Type 1 systems have $L$ and $S$ constant across all stages, this balance can be **summed cumulatively** for multistage cascades.

### 2.3 Single-Stage Extraction Equation

With one equilibrium contact (feed of composition $X_F$ contacted with solvent of composition $Y_S$):

$$X_1 = \frac{L X_F + S Y_S}{L + S K}$$

**Special case** (pure solvent, $Y_S = 0$):

$$X_1 = \frac{X_F}{1 + \dfrac{SK}{L}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Type 1 Extraction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula bank** for KTU 2024 scheme questions on Type 1 extraction. Memorize the forms and the boundary conditions.

| # | Configuration | Governing Equation | Key Condition |
|---|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Distribution Law (Type 1)** | $Y = K X$ | $B$ and $C$ mutually insoluble |
| 2 | **Single Stage (general)** | $X_1 = \dfrac{L X_F + S Y_S}{L + SK}$ | One ideal contact |
| 3 | **Single Stage (pure solvent)** | $X_1 = \dfrac{X_F}{1 + SK/L}$ | $Y_S = 0$ |
| 4 | **Extraction Factor** | $E = \dfrac{SK}{L}$ | $E > 1$ → solvent-rich, $E < 1$ → raffinate-rich |
| 5 | **Cross-Current — N stages (Kremser)** | $N = \dfrac{\log\!\left[\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right]}{\log(1 + E)} = \dfrac{\log\!\left[\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right]}{\log(1 + SK/L)}$ | Equal fresh solvent $S$ at each stage; $Y_S$ may be non-zero |
| 6 | **Counter-Current — N stages (Kremser), $E \neq 1$** | $N = \dfrac{\log\!\left[\left(\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right)(1 - E) + E\right]}{\log(1/E)} = \dfrac{\log\!\left[\left(\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right)(1 - SK/L) + SK/L\right]}{\log(L/SK)}$ | $L/SK \neq 1$ |
| 7 | **Counter-Current — special case $E = 1$ ($L = SK$)** | $N = \dfrac{\log\!\left[\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right]}{\log 2}$ | Operating line parallel to equilibrium line |
| 8 | **Overall Material Balance (any cascade)** | $L(X_F - X_N) = S(Y_1 - Y_S)$ | Solute transferred from B → C |
| 9 | **Cross-current cumulative solvent** | $S_{\text{total}} = N \cdot S$ | $S$ is per-stage solvent |
| 10 | **Slope of counter-current operating line** | $\text{slope} = L/S$ | $Y$ vs $X$ plot |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry**: Recovery of **penicillin** from aqueous broth using amyl acetate (Type 1 assumption).
- **Petroleum refining**: Separation of **aromatics** (benzene, toluene) from aliphatic mixtures using **sulfolane** or **SO₂** as solvent.
- **Nuclear reprocessing**: Extraction of **uranyl nitrate** from aqueous solution into **kerosene/TBP** (PUREX process — well-modeled as Type 1 at low concentrations).
- **Hydrometallurgy**: Recovery of **copper, cobalt, nickel** from leach liquors using organic extractants like LIX 84, D2EHPA.

> [!NOTE]
> In all these processes, the engineer must decide between **single-stage** (cheaper equipment, lower recovery), **cross-current** (moderate recovery, high solvent consumption), and **counter-current** (highest recovery with minimum solvent — most industrial choice).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Single-Stage Type 1 Material Balance

**Setup:** A feed solution of total mass $F$ kg containing mass fraction $x_F$ of solute A in diluent B is contacted in a single equilibrium stage with a pure solvent $S$ kg (containing zero A initially). The exit streams are the **raffinate** ($L$ kg of B + residual A) and the **extract** ($S$ kg of C + extracted A).

**Step 1 — Component-wise mass balance on solute A:**

$$\text{(A in feed)} = \text{(A in raffinate)} + \text{(A in extract)}$$

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_1 + S \cdot y_1$$

Because in Type 1 the diluent B does not leave with the solvent and the solvent C does not leave with the raffinate, the inert (non-A) masses give us:

$$L = F(1 - x_F) \quad \text{(mass of B in feed)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to ratio basis** (preferred for Type 1 because the equilibrium becomes linear):

Define:
$$X_F = \frac{x_F}{1 - x_F}, \quad X_1 = \frac{x_1}{1 - x_1}, \quad Y_1 = \frac{y_1}{1 - y_1}$$

Dividing the balance in Step 1 by the mass of B in feed (= $L$):

$$X_F = X_1 + \frac{S}{L} Y_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L(X_F - X_1) = S Y_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply Type 1 equilibrium:**

For Type 1, equilibrium is given by $Y_1 = K X_1$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve for $X_1$:**

$$L(X_F - X_1) = S(K X_1)$$

$$L X_F - L X_1 = S K X_1$$

$$L X_F = X_1(L + SK)$$

$$\boxed{X_1 = \frac{L X_F}{L + SK} = \frac{X_F}{1 + \dfrac{SK}{L}}}$$

If the inlet solvent is **not** pure but has composition $Y_S$:

$$\boxed{X_1 = \frac{L X_F + S Y_S}{L + SK}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Kremser Equation for Cross-Current Multistage (Type 1)

**Setup:** $N$ stages in cross-current cascade. Each stage receives **fresh solvent** $S$ of composition $Y_S$. The raffinate from stage $i$ becomes the feed to stage $i+1$. The solvent streams leaving each stage are combined as the final extract.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on stage 1:**

$$L(X_F - X_1) = S(Y_1 - Y_S)$$

With $Y_1 = K X_1$:

$$L(X_F - X_1) = S(K X_1 - Y_S)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $X_1$ in terms of $X_F$:**

$$L X_F - L X_1 = SK X_1 - S Y_S$$

$$L X_F + S Y_S = X_1 (L + SK)$$

$$X_1 = \frac{L X_F + S Y_S}{L + SK} = \frac{X_F + (S/L)Y_S}{1 + (SK/L)}$$

**Step 3 — Mass balance on stage 2 (with $X_1$ as feed, fresh solvent again):**

$$X_2 = \frac{X_1 + (S/L)Y_S}{1 + (SK/L)} = \frac{\dfrac{L X_F + S Y_S}{L + SK} + (S/L)Y_S}{1 + (SK/L)}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to stage $N$.** By induction, after $N$ cross-current stages:

$$X_N - \frac{Y_S}{K} = \frac{X_F - Y_S/K}{(1 + SK/L)^N}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $N$ by taking logarithms:**

$$(1 + SK/L)^N = \frac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{\log\!\left[\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right]}{\log(1 + SK/L)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Kremser Equation for Counter-Current Multistage (Type 1)

**Setup:** A cascade of $N$ ideal counter-current stages. Stage 1 is the feed end; stage $N$ is the solvent end. Let the stages be numbered such that the raffinate (B-phase) flows from stage 1 → stage $N$, and the extract (C-phase) flows from stage $N$ → stage 1.

- Feed composition entering stage 1: $X_F$
- Fresh solvent composition entering stage $N$: $Y_{N+1} = Y_S$
- Exit raffinate from stage $N$: $X_N$
- Exit extract from stage 1: $Y_1$

**Step 1 — Overall solute balance (entire cascade):**

$$L(X_F - X_N) = S(Y_1 - Y_S) \quad \Rightarrow \quad Y_1 = \frac{L}{S}(X_F - X_N) + Y_S$$

**Step 2 — Operating line for the counter-current cascade:**

For a balance over stages 1 through $i$ (from the feed end):

$$L X_F + S Y_{i+1} = L X_{i+1} + S Y_1$$

Rearranging:

$$Y_{i+1} = \frac{L}{S} X_{i+1} + \left( Y_1 - \frac{L}{S} X_F \right)$$

But using the overall balance, $Y_1 - \dfrac{L}{S} X_F = Y_S - \dfrac{L}{S} X_N$, so the operating line is:

$$Y_{i+1} = \frac{L}{S} X_{i+1} + Y_S - \frac{L}{S} X_N$$

Equivalently, the straight operating line passes through the two terminal points $(X_F, Y_1)$ and $(X_N, Y_S)$ with slope $L/S$.

**Step 3 — Combine with equilibrium $Y = KX$ and eliminate $Y$:**

On the operating line, $Y_{i+1} = \dfrac{L}{S}X_{i+1} + Y_S - \dfrac{L}{S}X_N$.

At equilibrium, $Y_{i+1} = K X_{i+1}$, so:

$$K X_{i+1} = \frac{L}{S} X_{i+1} + Y_S - \frac{L}{S} X_N$$

$$X_{i+1}\!\left(K - \frac{L}{S}\right) = Y_S - \frac{L}{S} X_N$$

$$X_{i+1} = \frac{Y_S - (L/S) X_N}{K - L/S}$$

**Step 4 — This is a first-order linear recurrence.** Define a constant $r$:

$$r = \frac{L/S}{K} = \frac{L}{SK} = \frac{1}{E}$$

Then $X_{i+1} = \dfrac{(Y_S/K) - (L/SK)X_N}{1 - L/SK} = \dfrac{(Y_S/K) - r X_N}{1 - r}$

Rearranging, the *deviation* of $X_i$ from the pinching value $X^* = Y_S/K$ follows a geometric progression:

$$X_i - \frac{Y_S}{K} = r \left( X_{i-1} - \frac{Y_S}{K} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the recurrence $N$ times starting from $X_0 = X_F$:**

$$X_N - \frac{Y_S}{K} = r^N \left( X_F - \frac{Y_S}{K} \right) = \left(\frac{L}{SK}\right)^N \left( X_F - \frac{Y_S}{K} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $N$ by taking logarithms** (valid for $L/SK \neq 1$):

$$\frac{X_N - Y_S/K}{X_F - Y_S/K} = \left(\frac{L}{SK}\right)^N$$

$$N \log\!\left(\frac{L}{SK}\right) = \log\!\left(\frac{X_N - Y_S/K}{X_F - Y_S/K}\right)$$

$$N = \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{X_N - Y_S/K}{X_F - Y_S/K}\right)}{\log(L/SK)}$$

**Step 7 — Invert the ratio** (KTU standard form):

$$\boxed{N = \frac{\log\!\left[\left(\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{SK}{L}\right) + \dfrac{SK}{L}\right]}{\log\!\left(\dfrac{L}{SK}\right)}}$$

This elegant form is obtained by writing the recurrence in a more convenient algebraic form. Substituting $E = SK/L$ and $r = L/SK = 1/E$:

$$N = \frac{\log\!\left[\left(\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right)(1 - E) + E\right]}{\log(1/E)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Derivation Tip:** The professor almost always wants you to *start* from the operating-line equation and *combine* it with the equilibrium relation to form a first-order difference equation, then iterate. Memorize the two key starting points: **(a)** operating line $Y_{i+1} = (L/S) X_{i+1} + Y_S - (L/S) X_N$, and **(b)** equilibrium $Y = KX$.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style (Single Stage)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme pattern):** A feed of 100 kg/h containing 40 wt% acetone (A) in water (B) is contacted with 80 kg/h of pure methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK) as solvent (C) in a single ideal stage. The distribution coefficient $K = y/x = 0.85$ (mass fraction basis). The system is Type 1. Calculate the equilibrium compositions of the exit raffinate and extract.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the inert flows:**

$$L = 100 \times (1 - 0.40) = 60 \text{ kg water/h}$$
$$S = 80 \text{ kg MIBK/h}$$
$$x_F = 0.40, \quad y_S = 0$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on acetone (solute A):**

$$L(x_F - x_1) = S(y_1 - y_S)$$

$$60(0.40 - x_1) = 80 \cdot y_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium:**

$$y_1 = K x_1 = 0.85 \, x_1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the mass balance:**

$$60(0.40 - x_1) = 80 (0.85 \, x_1)$$

$$24 - 60 x_1 = 68 x_1$$

$$24 = 128 x_1$$

$$x_1 = \frac{24}{128} = 0.1875$$

**Step 5 — Find $y_1$:**

$$y_1 = 0.85 \times 0.1875 = 0.1594$$

**Verification:**

$$60 \times (0.40 - 0.1875) = 60 \times 0.2125 = 12.75 \text{ kg acetone/h transferred}$$

$$80 \times (0.1594 - 0) = 12.75 \text{ kg acetone/h} \quad \checkmark$$

**Result:** The raffinate contains **18.75 wt% acetone**, and the extract contains **15.94 wt% acetone**.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Counter-Current Kremser

> **Problem:** A counter-current extractor is to recover **acetic acid** from an aqueous feed using **isopropyl ether**. The feed contains $X_F = 0.30$ kg acid / kg water, the raffinate must not exceed $X_N = 0.05$ kg acid / kg water, the solvent is pure ($Y_S = 0$), the distribution coefficient is $K = 0.5$ (mass ratio basis), and the solvent-to-feed mass ratio is $S/L = 1.5$. Calculate the number of theoretical stages.

**Step 1 — Compute $E$ and $L/SK$:**

$$E = \frac{SK}{L} = 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75$$

$$\frac{L}{SK} = \frac{1}{0.75} = 1.3333$$

**Step 2 — Plug into the counter-current Kremser equation:**

$$N = \frac{\log\!\left[\left(\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right)(1 - E) + E\right]}{\log(L/SK)}$$

With $Y_S = 0$:

$$\frac{X_F - 0}{X_N - 0} = \frac{0.30}{0.05} = 6$$

Numerator argument:

$$6 \times (1 - 0.75) + 0.75 = 6 \times 0.25 + 0.75 = 1.50 + 0.75 = 2.25$$

Denominator:

$$\log(1.3333) = 0.12494$$

**Step 3 — Compute $N$:**

$$N = \frac{\log(2.25)}{\log(1.3333)} = \frac{0.35218}{0.12494} = 2.819$$

**Step 4 — Round up to nearest whole stage (cannot have a fractional ideal stage):**

$$\boxed{N = 3 \text{ theoretical stages}}$$

---

### 3.6 Comparison Matrix: Single vs. Cross-Current vs. Counter-Current

| Feature | Single Stage | Cross-Current | Counter-Current |
|---------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Number of stages | 1 | N | N |
| Solvent usage | Minimum per stage | Highest (fresh $S$ × $N$) | Lowest (single feed) |
| Recovery of solute | Lowest | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Equipment complexity | Simple | Moderate | High |
| Kremser equation applicable? | Yes (special case) | Yes | Yes |
| Operating line on $Y$–$X$ plot | Single point | Multiple lines (one per stage) | Single straight line, slope $L/S$ |
| Industrial preference | Lab/batch | Small scale | **Large scale (preferred)** |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Type 1 liquid-liquid extraction system. Give two industrial examples and state the key simplifying assumption that allows the use of a linear equilibrium model $Y = KX$.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Type 1 liquid-liquid extraction system** is one in which the two bulk liquid phases — the **diluent (B)** and the **extracting solvent (C)** — are **completely immiscible** (mutually insoluble) in each other. Only the **solute (A)** distributes between the two phases.

**Two industrial examples:**
1. Recovery of **acetic acid** from aqueous solution using **isopropyl ether** as solvent.
2. Separation of **aromatic hydrocarbons** (benzene, toluene) from paraffin oils using **liquid SO₂**.

**Key simplifying assumption:** Since B does not dissolve in C (and vice versa), the bulk mass flow rates $L$ (of B) and $S$ (of C) remain **constant** across all stages. Consequently, the equilibrium relationship between the mass ratios $X$ (kg A / kg B) and $Y$ (kg A / kg C) reduces to a **linear, time-invariant** form: $Y = KX$, where $K$ is the constant distribution coefficient.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the extraction factor? State the Kremser equation for a cross-current multistage Type 1 extraction system with pure solvent feed. Explain the significance of $E > 1$ vs. $E < 1$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **extraction factor** $E$ is the dimensionless ratio that compares the solute-carrying capacity of the solvent to that of the diluent:

$$E = \frac{SK}{L}$$

where $S$ is the solvent mass flow rate, $K$ is the distribution coefficient, and $L$ is the diluent mass flow rate.

**Kremser equation for cross-current Type 1 with pure solvent** ($Y_S = 0$):

$$N = \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{X_F}{X_N}\right)}{\log(1 + E)} = \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{X_F}{X_N}\right)}{\log\!\left(1 + \dfrac{SK}{L}\right)}$$

**Significance:**
- **$E > 1$**: The solvent has a *higher* solute-carrying capacity than the diluent. Extraction is favourable; few stages are required, and the raffinate becomes lean in A.
- **$E < 1$**: The diluent retains solute more strongly than the solvent can extract it. Many stages or a higher solvent flow rate is required to achieve a given raffinate purity.
- **$E = 1$**: The two phases have equal solute affinity; an infinite number of stages is required to bring $X_N \to 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module Structure:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between the two complete alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A single-stage Type 1 extraction is used to recover **acetone** from a water-acetone mixture using **methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK)**. The feed is **100 kg/h** containing **40 wt% acetone**. Pure MIBK is fed at **80 kg/h**. The distribution coefficient $K = 0.85$ (mass fraction basis). Calculate the mass fractions of acetone in the **raffinate** ($x_1$) and **extract** ($y_1$), and the **mass of acetone transferred per hour**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Two cross-current extraction stages (each using the same solvent amount) are used instead, with all other conditions identical. Calculate the new raffinate mass fraction of acetone.

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Single Stage

**Step 1 — Identify inert flow rates:**

$$L = 100 \times (1 - 0.40) = 60 \text{ kg water/h}$$
$$S = 80 \text{ kg MIBK/h}$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on solute (acetone):**

$$L(x_F - x_1) = S(y_1 - 0)$$

$$60(0.40 - x_1) = 80 \, y_1 \quad \ldots (1)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Type 1 equilibrium:**

$$y_1 = K x_1 = 0.85 x_1 \quad \ldots (2)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (2) into (1):**

$$60(0.40 - x_1) = 80(0.85 x_1)$$
$$24 - 60 x_1 = 68 x_1$$
$$24 = 128 x_1$$
$$x_1 = 0.1875$$
$$y_1 = 0.85 \times 0.1875 = 0.1594$$

**Step 5 — Mass of acetone transferred:**

$$\Delta = L(x_F - x_1) = 60 \times (0.40 - 0.1875) = 60 \times 0.2125 = 12.75 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Final answers (a):**

$$\boxed{x_1 = 0.1875, \quad y_1 = 0.1594, \quad \text{Acetone transferred} = 12.75 \text{ kg/h}}$$

#### Part (b) — Two Cross-Current Stages

**Step 1 — Use the single-stage result with $X$ basis for clarity:**

Convert $x_F = 0.40$ to ratio:

$$X_F = \frac{x_F}{1 - x_F} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.6667$$

The single-stage equation in ratio form (pure solvent):

$$X_1 = \frac{X_F}{1 + SK/L} = \frac{0.6667}{1 + (80 \times 0.85)/60} = \frac{0.6667}{1 + 1.1333} = \frac{0.6667}{2.1333} = 0.3125$$

**Step 2 — Verify against mass-fraction result:**

$$x_1 = \frac{X_1}{1 + X_1} = \frac{0.3125}{1.3125} = 0.2381$$

(Equivalent; small differences arise from mass-fraction vs. ratio equilibrium, but the ratio basis is exact for $Y = KX$.)

**Step 3 — Apply the cross-current Kremser equation for $N = 2$:**

$$X_N = \frac{X_F}{(1 + SK/L)^N} = \frac{0.6667}{(2.1333)^2} = \frac{0.6667}{4.5511} = 0.1465$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to mass fraction:**

$$x_N = \frac{X_N}{1 + X_N} = \frac{0.1465}{1.1465} = 0.1278$$

**Final answer (b):**

$$\boxed{x_N = 0.1278 \text{ (mass fraction acetone in raffinate after 2 cross-current stages)}}$$

**Comparison:** The two cross-current stages reduce raffinate acetone from 40 wt% to **12.78 wt%** — a much greater recovery than the single-stage result of 18.75 wt%, but at the cost of **double the solvent consumption** (160 kg/h vs. 80 kg/h).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from the operating line equation and the linear Type 1 equilibrium relation $Y = KX$, **derive** the Kremser equation for the number of theoretical stages in a counter-current Type 1 extraction system.

**(b) [7 marks]** Apply the result to the following problem: an aqueous feed containing **0.30 kg acetic acid / kg water** is to be extracted counter-currently with **pure isopropyl ether**. The raffinate must contain no more than **0.05 kg acid / kg water**. The distribution coefficient $K = 0.5$ (mass ratio basis) and the solvent-to-feed ratio $S/L = 1.5$. Determine the **number of theoretical stages**.

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the operating line for the counter-current cascade:**

From a solute balance over stages 1 through $i$ (with stage 1 at the feed end, stage $N$ at the solvent end):

$$L X_F + S Y_{i+1} = L X_{i+1} + S Y_1$$

Solving for $Y_{i+1}$:

$$Y_{i+1} = \frac{L}{S} X_{i+1} + \left( Y_1 - \frac{L}{S} X_F \right) \quad \ldots (i)$$

**Step 2 — Use the overall solute balance** to simplify the intercept:

$$L(X_F - X_N) = S(Y_1 - Y_S) \quad \Rightarrow \quad Y_1 - \frac{L}{S} X_F = Y_S - \frac{L}{S} X_N$$

So the operating line becomes:

$$Y_{i+1} = \frac{L}{S} X_{i+1} + Y_S - \frac{L}{S} X_N \quad \ldots (ii)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Type 1 equilibrium** at every stage: $Y_{i+1} = K X_{i+1}$.

Substitute into (ii):

$$K X_{i+1} = \frac{L}{S} X_{i+1} + Y_S - \frac{L}{S} X_N$$

$$X_{i+1} \left( K - \frac{L}{S} \right) = Y_S - \frac{L}{S} X_N$$

$$X_{i+1} = \frac{Y_S - (L/S) X_N}{K - L/S} \quad \ldots (iii)$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite (iii) in the standard recursive form.** Divide numerator and denominator by $K$ and let $r = L/(SK) = 1/E$:

$$X_{i+1} = \frac{(Y_S/K) - r X_N}{1 - r}$$

Rearranging to isolate the deviation from the pinching value $X^* = Y_S/K$:

$$X_{i+1} - \frac{Y_S}{K} = r \left( X_i - \frac{Y_S}{K} \right) \quad \ldots (iv)$$

**Step 5 — Iterate (iv) from $i = 0$ (with $X_0 = X_F$) to $i = N$:**

$$X_N - \frac{Y_S}{K} = r^N \left( X_F - \frac{Y_S}{K} \right) \quad \ldots (v)$$

**Step 6 — Take the logarithm of both sides of (v):**

$$N = \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{X_N - Y_S/K}{X_F - Y_S/K}\right)}{\log r} = \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right)}{\log(1/r)}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to the KTU-preferred form.** Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1 - E)$ inside the log of the ratio and using $r = 1/E$:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{\log\!\left[\left(\dfrac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K}\right)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{SK}{L}\right) + \dfrac{SK}{L}\right]}{\log\!\left(\dfrac{L}{SK}\right)}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Application

**Step 1 — Compute the parameters:**

$$E = \frac{SK}{L} = 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75$$
$$\frac{L}{SK} = \frac{1}{0.75} = 1.3333$$
$$X_F = 0.30, \quad X_N = 0.05, \quad Y_S = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio term:**

$$\frac{X_F - Y_S/K}{X_N - Y_S/K} = \frac{0.30 - 0}{0.05 - 0} = 6$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator's argument:**

$$6 \times (1 - 0.75) + 0.75 = 6 \times 0.25 + 0.75 = 1.50 + 0.75 = 2.25$$

**Step 4 — Compute $N$:**

$$N = \frac{\log(2.25)}{\log(1.3333)} = \frac{0.35218}{0.12494} = 2.819$$

**Step 5 — Round up to the next whole stage:**

$$\boxed{N = 3 \text{ theoretical stages}}$$

**Verification by manual staging (optional):** Plot the operating line from $(X_N, Y_S) = (0.05, 0)$ to $(X_F, Y_1)$. First, find $Y_1$ from overall balance: $L(0.30 - 0.05) = S \cdot Y_1 \Rightarrow Y_1 = 0.25/1.5 = 0.1667$. Slope $= L/S = 1/1.5 = 0.6667$. Stepping between $Y = KX = 0.5X$ and the operating line from $X_F = 0.30$ to $X_N = 0.05$ gives exactly **3 stages**, confirming the Kremser result.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Mass fraction vs. mass ratio confusion (loses 2-3 marks):** If the problem gives feed composition as a *mass fraction* ($x_F$) but the equilibrium data is given as $K$ on a *mass ratio* basis ($Y = KX$), you **MUST convert** $x_F$ to $X_F = x_F/(1 - x_F)$ before applying the Kremser equation. Failure to do so is the #1 reason students get numerically wrong answers.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $Y_S/K$ term in Kremser (loses 2 marks):** When the inlet solvent is *not* pure (e.g., recycled solvent with residual solute), the term $Y_S/K$ in the numerator of both Kremser forms **must not be dropped**. Many students write the formula with $X_F/X_N$ instead of the correct $(X_F - Y_S/K)/(X_N - Y_S/K)$ and lose full marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing the extraction factor direction (loses 1-2 marks):** $E = SK/L$ (not $L/SK$). $E > 1$ means solvent-rich extraction; $E < 1$ means solvent-starved. The Kremser equation for counter-current has $\log(L/SK)$ in the denominator — note this is $\log(1/E)$, *not* $\log(E)$.
>
> 4. **The $L = SK$ edge case (loses 3 marks):** When the operating line and equilibrium line are parallel (i.e., $L/S = K$ or $L = SK$), the standard counter-current Kremser equation has an indeterminate $0/0$ form. The correct limiting form is $N = \log[(X_F - Y_S/K)/(X_N - Y_S/K)] / \log 2$. Always check whether $L/SK = 1$ before applying the formula.
>
> 5. **Stage rounding (loses 1 mark):** A fractional stage (e.g., $N = 2.82$) must be **rounded UP** to the next whole number (3 stages), because you cannot build 0.82 of a physical contactor. The KTU valuation key explicitly deducts for rounding down.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Type 1 Liquid Extraction (Module 3)**

- **Type 1 = Immiscible B and C.** Always confirm the problem statement contains "immiscible", "insoluble", or "Type 1" before applying linear equilibrium.
- **Equilibrium is a straight line through the origin:** $Y = KX$ (in mass-ratio basis) or $y = Kx$ (in mass-fraction basis — same $K$).
- **Flow rates $L$ and $S$ are constant** across all stages — this is the *defining* simplification of Type 1.
- **Treybal's symbols:** A = solute, B = diluent, C = solvent, L = kg B/h, S = kg C/h, X = kg A/kg B, Y = kg A/kg C, K = Y/X.
- **Single-stage** equation: $X_1 = (L X_F + S Y_S)/(L + SK)$; for pure solvent: $X_1 = X_F/(1 + SK/L)$.
- **Extraction factor:** $E = SK/L$. $E > 1$ favourable, $E < 1$ unfavourable, $E = 1$ requires special formula.
- **Cross-current Kremser:** $N = \log[(X_F - Y_S/K)/(X_N - Y_S/K)] / \log(1 + E)$. Each stage uses fresh solvent.
- **Counter-current Kremser** (general, $E \neq 1$): $N = \log\{[(X_F - Y_S/K)/(X_N - Y_S/K)](1 - E) + E\} / \log(1/E)$.
- **Counter-current Kremser** (special, $E = 1$): $N = \log[(X_F - Y_S/K)/(X_N - Y_S/K)] / \log 2$.
- **Counter-current is the most solvent-efficient** arrangement and is universally preferred in industry.
- **Operating line slope (counter-current)** on Y–X diagram: $L/S$.
- **Overall balance** (any cascade): $L(X_F - X_N) = S(Y_1 - Y_S)$.
- **Round UP** to the next whole stage; never round down.
- **Converting mass fraction to ratio:** $X = x/(1 - x)$, $Y = y/(1 - y)$.
- **Converting ratio to mass fraction:** $x = X/(1 + X)$, $y = Y/(1 + Y)$.
- **Industrial examples:** acetic acid / water / isopropyl ether; aromatics / paraffins / SO₂; penicillin / water / amyl acetate.
- **Derivation starting points:** Operating line $Y_{i+1} = (L/S)X_{i+1} + Y_S - (L/S)X_N$ and equilibrium $Y = KX$ → combine → first-order linear difference equation → iterate.
- **Graphical method** (Y–X plot) gives the same result as Kremser for Type 1, but with less precision. Always cross-check with the analytical formula on the KTU exam.
- **Type 2 and Type 3 systems** (not covered in this module): $B$ and $C$ partially miscible or three liquid phases — distribution curve is *not* a straight line, and $L$, $S$ are *not* constant. Different (more complex) calculation methods apply.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Stage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Stage Operation:** A mass transfer process in which the feed and the extracting (or absorbing) agent are brought into intimate contact **once**, allowed to reach thermodynamic equilibrium, and then mechanically separated. The contacting device is called an **equilibrium stage** or **ideal stage**. Mathematically, the two streams leaving the stage satisfy the condition of phase equilibrium (e.g., $y_E = K \, x_R$ for liquid–liquid extraction or $y = m \, x$ for gas absorption).

In a single stage there is **no reflux, no recycle, and no re-mixing** — the entire mass transfer duty is accomplished in one contacting step. A real (Murphree) stage that does not attain equilibrium is characterized by an *efficiency* $E_M$, but at the KTU 2024 level we treat the stage as **ideal (100 % efficient)** unless otherwise stated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Tea Bag in a Single Cup of Water"**
> Imagine a tea bag dropped into **one cup** of hot water. The tea (solute) diffuses out of the bag leaves (carrier/solid) into the water (solvent). After a few minutes you remove the bag — the **leaves and the brew are now in equilibrium**. You have extracted some tea, but a lot of colour/flavour is *still trapped inside the wet leaves*. If you want stronger tea you need a *second* cup (i.e., a multi-stage operation). **One cup = one single stage.**

Similarly, in solvent extraction, after one mixing–settling step, the raffinate still contains a finite amount of solute that is in equilibrium with the extract. Removing that residual solute requires a *second* stage.

## 1.3 Key Stream Variables (Standard KTU Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning | Units (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $F$ | Mass flow rate of feed | $\mathrm{kg \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| $S$ | Mass flow rate of solvent | $\mathrm{kg \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| $R$ | Mass flow rate of raffinate | $\mathrm{kg \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| $E$ | Mass flow rate of extract | $\mathrm{kg \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| $x_F$ | Mass fraction of solute in feed | dimensionless |
| $y_S$ | Mass fraction of solute in solvent | dimensionless |
| $x_R$ | Mass fraction of solute in raffinate | dimensionless |
| $y_E$ | Mass fraction of solute in extract | dimensionless |
| $K$ | Distribution coefficient ($y/x$ at equilibrium) | dimensionless |
| $M$ | Mixed-stream (intermediate) composition | dimensionless |

## 1.4 When is a "Single Stage" Used in Industry?

- **Final polishing** of an already-enriched stream (e.g., washing wet crystals once with fresh solvent).
- **Preliminary scouting studies** — a single-stage calculation gives the *minimum* solvent requirement to reach a target recovery.
- **Azeotropic/extractive distillation reboilers** where a single equilibrium contact suffices.
- **Bench-scale extraction** to determine tie-line data on a ternary diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> The single-stage result is also the **lower bound** on solvent consumption for a target recovery. Any multi-stage cascade (counter-current, cross-current) reaches the same recovery with **less solvent** — a fact we will exploit in Modules 4 and 5.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Every Single-Stage Calculation

1. **Total Mass Balance** — Stream in = Stream out.
2. **Component (Solute) Mass Balance** — Traces the species being transferred.
3. **Thermodynamic Equilibrium** — The exiting phases lie on the equilibrium curve (tie line).

For an **ideal** single stage these three relations are sufficient to obtain the unknown stream rates and compositions.

## 2.2 Operating Point on a y–x Diagram

When the distribution coefficient $K$ is **constant** (linear equilibrium $y = Kx$), the material-balance and equilibrium equations can be solved analytically. The intersection of the **operating line** (straight line joining inlet compositions) with the **equilibrium line** gives the exit compositions of the stage.

For extraction (carrier B and solvent C mutually insoluble) the operating line is:

$$ y \;=\; \frac{R}{E}\,x \;-\; \frac{F \, x_F \,-\, S \, y_S}{E} $$

The exit point $(x_R, \, y_E)$ is the intersection of this line with $y = Kx$:

$$ y_E \;=\; K \, x_R $$

## 2.3 General Mass-Balance Architecture (Carrier-Insolvent Assumption)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption (dilute / ideal extraction):** the carrier is *practically* insoluble in the solvent phase, and the solvent is *practically* insoluble in the carrier phase. Therefore the mass of carrier $F(1-x_F)$ resides entirely in the raffinate, and the mass of solvent $S$ resides entirely in the extract.

Under this assumption:
- $R \approx F(1-x_F)$   (all carrier exits with raffinate)
- $E \approx S$   (all solvent exits with extract)
- Solute balance: $F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S \;=\; F(1-x_F) \, x_R \,+\, S \, y_E$

Combining with $y_E = K \, x_R$ yields the closed-form result (derived in Section 3).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulae below are in **mass units** (kg or kg/s) with mass-fraction compositions. Replace mass by molar quantities if mole-fraction data are given.

### 2.4.1 Single-Stage Liquid–Liquid Extraction (Pure Solvent, $y_S = 0$)

$$
x_R \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K}
$$

$$
y_E \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F \, K}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K}
$$

$$
\text{Fraction extracted, } \phi \;=\; \frac{S \, K}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K}
$$

### 2.4.2 Single-Stage Liquid–Liquid Extraction (Solvent with Solute, $y_S \neq 0$)

$$
x_R \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K}
$$

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{S \,(y_E - y_S)}{F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S}
$$

### 2.4.3 Single-Stage Cross / Co-Current Leaching (Pure Solvent)

$$
\frac{1}{x_A} \;=\; 1 \,+\, \frac{S}{F \, x_F}
$$

$$
\phi_{\text{leach}} \;=\; \frac{V}{V \,+\, L} \;=\; \frac{V}{S \,+\, F \, x_F}
$$

where $x_A$ = solute mass fraction in clear overflow $V$, and $L$ = liquid retained by the underflow solid.

### 2.4.4 Single-Stage Gas Absorption (Henry's Law, $y = m x$)

$$
x_1 \;=\; \frac{G \, y_2 \,+\, L \, x_2}{L \,+\, G \, m}
$$

$$
\phi_{\text{abs}} \;=\; \frac{L}{L \,+\, G \, m} \quad\quad \text{(for } x_2 = 0\text{)}
$$

where subscript 1 denotes the *exiting* gas/liquid, subscript 2 the *entering* streams, $G$ = gas molar flow, $L$ = liquid molar flow, $m$ = Henry constant.

### 2.4.5 Kremser Equation (Single Stage form)

$$
\phi_{\text{abs}} \;=\; \frac{A}{A \,+\, 1} \,=\, \frac{1}{1 \,+\, \frac{1}{A}}
$$

with the absorption factor $A = L \,/\, (G \, m)$. For a *stripping* operation, replace $A$ with $S = (G \, m)\,/\, L$.

### 2.4.6 Stage Efficiency (real stage)

$$
E_M \;=\; \frac{x_{\text{in}} \,-\, x_{\text{out,real}}}{x_{\text{in}} \,-\, x_{\text{out,ideal}}}
$$

For an ideal single stage as treated in KTU problems, $E_M = 1$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Single-stage calculations are the **first design check** an engineer performs. The expressions above allow:
- **Quick sizing of solvent flow** for a target raffinate purity.
- **Estimation of extract composition** for solvent-recovery calculations.
- **Determination of distribution coefficient** from laboratory single-stage data (set $F$, $S$, $x_F$, $y_S$, measure $x_R$ and solve for $K$).
- **Benchmark** against which the savings of multi-stage cascades are quantified.

## 2.6 Physical Limits and Sanity Checks

> [!WARNING]
> 1. As $S \to \infty$, $\phi \to 1$ (infinite solvent = complete extraction) — physically consistent.
> 2. As $S \to 0$, $\phi \to 0$ (no solvent = no extraction) — physically consistent.
> 3. As $K \to 0$, $\phi \to 0$ (solute prefers carrier — no extraction) — physically consistent.
> 4. As $K \to \infty$, $\phi \to 1$ (solute strongly prefers solvent — total transfer) — physically consistent.
> 5. The formula **assumes** pure carrier and pure solvent leave with their respective phases. If the carrier and solvent are partially miscible, you must use the **ternary diagram / lever rule** instead of these algebraic equations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single-Stage Liquid–Liquid Extraction (General)

### 3.1.1 System Statement

A feed $F$ (kg/s) containing a solute $A$ at mass fraction $x_F$ in a carrier $B$ is contacted with a solvent $S$ (kg/s) containing solute at mass fraction $y_S$. The two phases reach equilibrium governed by $y = K x$ and are separated into a raffinate $R$ (kg/s) and an extract $E$ (kg/s). The exit compositions are $x_R$ and $y_E$.

### 3.1.2 Governing Equations

**(a) Total mass balance** (in = out):
$$ F \,+\, S \;=\; R \,+\, E \tag{1} $$

**(b) Solute (A) component balance**:
$$ F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S \;=\; R \, x_R \,+\, E \, y_E \tag{2} $$

**(c) Equilibrium relation**:
$$ y_E \;=\; K \, x_R \tag{3} $$

**(d) Insoluble-carrier / insoluble-solvent assumption** (valid in the dilute limit and for many industrial systems such as water–toluene, water–chloroform):
$$ R \;\approx\; F(1 - x_F) \tag{4} $$
$$ E \;\approx\; S \tag{5} $$

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Manipulation

Substitute (4) and (5) into (2):
$$ F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S \;=\; F(1 - x_F) \, x_R \,+\, S \, y_E $$

Replace $y_E$ with $K x_R$ from (3):
$$ F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S \;=\; F(1 - x_F) \, x_R \,+\, S \, K \, x_R $$

Group the right-hand side by $x_R$:
$$ F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S \;=\; x_R \,\bigl[\,F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K\,\bigr] $$

Solve for $x_R$:
$$ \boxed{\, x_R \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K} \,} \tag{6} $$

Substitute (6) into (3) to obtain $y_E$:
$$ \boxed{\, y_E \;=\; \frac{K \,\bigl(F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S\bigr)}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K} \,} \tag{7} $$

### 3.1.4 Special Case — Pure Solvent ($y_S = 0$)

$$ x_R \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K}, \qquad y_E \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F \, K}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K} $$

### 3.1.5 Fraction of Solute Extracted

$$ \phi \;=\; \frac{\text{mass of A in extract}}{\text{mass of A in (feed + solvent)}} \;=\; \frac{E \, y_E}{F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S} $$

Using $E \approx S$ and the expressions above:
$$ \phi \;=\; \frac{S \, y_E}{F \, x_F \,+\, S \, y_S} \;=\; \frac{S \, K}{F(1 - x_F) \,+\, S \, K} $$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single-Stage Leaching

### 3.2.1 System Statement

A solid feed of mass $F$ contains a soluble fraction $A$ at mass fraction $x_F$ and an inert solid $N$. It is contacted with a pure solvent of mass $S$ (mass fraction of $A$ in solvent is zero). The mixture is filtered to give a clear overflow $V$ (extract solution) and an underflow that retains inert solid $N$ plus a liquid mass $L$ of composition $y_A$ equal to the overflow composition (single-stage equilibrium).

### 3.2.2 Governing Equations

**(a) Total mass balance**:
$$ F \,+\, S \;=\; V \,+\, L \,+\, N \tag{8} $$

**(b) Inert-solid balance** (carrier is insoluble):
$$ N \;=\; F(1 - x_F) \tag{9} $$

**(c) Single-stage equilibrium** for the liquid:
$$ y_A \;=\; x_A \tag{10} $$

**(d) Solute balance**:
$$ F \, x_F \;=\; V \, x_A \,+\, L \, y_A \;=\; (V + L) \, x_A \tag{11} $$

### 3.2.3 Algebraic Manipulation

From (8) and (9):
$$ V \,+\, L \;=\; F \,+\, S \,-\, N \;=\; F \,+\, S \,-\, F(1 - x_F) \;=\; S \,+\, F \, x_F $$

Substitute into (11):
$$ F \, x_F \;=\; (S + F \, x_F) \, x_A $$

Solve for $x_A$:
$$ \boxed{\, x_A \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F}{S \,+\, F \, x_F} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 \,+\, \dfrac{S}{F \, x_F}} \,} \tag{12} $$

### 3.2.4 Fraction Leached Out

$$ \phi_{\text{leach}} \;=\; \frac{V \, x_A}{F \, x_F} \;=\; \frac{V}{V + L} \;=\; \frac{V}{S + F \, x_F} $$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Single-Stage Gas Absorption

### 3.3.1 System Statement

A gas stream $G$ (mol/s) containing solute $A$ at mole fraction $y_2$ (entering) is contacted counter-currently with a liquid $L$ (mol/s) entering at $x_2$ in a single equilibrium stage. The exit gas at $y_1$ and exit liquid at $x_1$ satisfy Henry's law: $y_1 = m \, x_1$.

### 3.3.2 Governing Equations

**(a) Total mole balance** (incompressible liquids and gases treated as constant molar overflow):
$$ G \,+\, L \;=\; G_1 \,+\, L_1 \;\approx\; G \,+\, L \tag{13} $$

**(b) Solute (A) mole balance**:
$$ G \, y_2 \,+\, L \, x_2 \;=\; G \, y_1 \,+\, L \, x_1 \tag{14} $$

**(c) Equilibrium at exit**:
$$ y_1 \;=\; m \, x_1 \tag{15} $$

### 3.3.3 Algebraic Manipulation

From (14) and (15):
$$ G \, y_2 \,+\, L \, x_2 \;=\; G \, m \, x_1 \,+\, L \, x_1 \;=\; x_1 (G \, m + L) $$

Solve for $x_1$:
$$ \boxed{\, x_1 \;=\; \frac{G \, y_2 \,+\, L \, x_2}{L \,+\, G \, m} \,} \tag{16} $$

For pure solvent ($x_2 = 0$):
$$ x_1 \;=\; \frac{G \, y_2}{L \,+\, G \, m}, \qquad y_1 \;=\; \frac{m \, G \, y_2}{L \,+\, G \, m} $$

### 3.3.4 Fraction Absorbed

$$ \phi_{\text{abs}} \;=\; \frac{G(y_2 - y_1)}{G \, y_2} \;=\; 1 - \frac{y_1}{y_2} \;=\; 1 - \frac{m \, G}{L + G \, m} \;=\; \frac{L}{L + G \, m} $$

Define the **absorption factor** $A = L/(G m)$:
$$ \boxed{\, \phi_{\text{abs}} \;=\; \frac{A}{1 + A} \,} \tag{17} $$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question (KTU Practice):** A feed of $100 \;\mathrm{kg}$ containing $30 \;\% $ by mass acetic acid in water is to be extracted once with $80 \;\mathrm{kg}$ of pure isopropyl ether. The distribution coefficient $K = y/x = 2.8$ (mass-fraction basis). Calculate: (i) the mass and composition of extract and raffinate, (ii) the percentage of acetic acid extracted.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the streams.**
- $F = 100$ kg, $x_F = 0.30$, $S = 80$ kg, $y_S = 0$, $K = 2.8$.

**Step 2 — Apply the dilute-extraction formula for $x_R$:**

$$ x_R \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F}{F(1 - x_F) + S \, K} \;=\; \frac{100 \times 0.30}{100 \times 0.70 + 80 \times 2.8} $$

$$ x_R \;=\; \frac{30}{70 + 224} \;=\; \frac{30}{294} \;=\; 0.1020 $$

**Step 3 — Compute $y_E$:**

$$ y_E \;=\; K \, x_R \;=\; 2.8 \times 0.1020 \;=\; 0.2857 $$

**Step 4 — Raffinate and extract masses:**

$$ R \;\approx\; F(1 - x_F) \;=\; 100 \times 0.70 \;=\; 70 \;\mathrm{kg} $$
$$ E \;\approx\; S \;=\; 80 \;\mathrm{kg} $$

Total balance check: $R + E = 70 + 80 = 150 = F + S$ ✓

**Step 5 — Percentage extracted:**

$$ \phi \;=\; \frac{S \, K}{F(1 - x_F) + S \, K} \;=\; \frac{80 \times 2.8}{294} \;=\; \frac{224}{294} \;=\; 0.7619 \;\;(76.19\,\%) $$

**Verification by mass balance** (solute):
- Solute in feed = $100 \times 0.30 = 30$ kg.
- Solute in extract = $80 \times 0.2857 = 22.86$ kg.
- Solute in raffinate = $70 \times 0.1020 = 7.14$ kg.
- Total out = $22.86 + 7.14 = 30.00$ kg ✓

> **Result:** $R = 70$ kg with $x_R = 0.102$; $E = 80$ kg with $y_E = 0.286$; **76.2 %** of acetic acid transferred to the extract in a single ideal stage.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Operation | Streams / System | Equilibrium Law | Governing Formula | Key Assumption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Liquid–Liquid Extraction (pure solvent) | $F, S, R, E$ | $y_E = K x_R$ | $x_R = F x_F / [F(1-x_F) + S K]$ | Carrier & solvent immiscible |
| Liquid–Liquid Extraction (loaded solvent) | same | $y_E = K x_R$ | $x_R = (F x_F + S y_S)/[F(1-x_F) + S K]$ | same |
| Leaching (single stage) | $F, S, V, L, N$ | $y_A = x_A$ | $x_A = F x_F / (S + F x_F)$ | Inert solid insoluble |
| Gas Absorption (single stage) | $G, L$ | $y_1 = m x_1$ | $x_1 = (G y_2 + L x_2)/(L + G m)$ | Henry's law, dilute gas |
| Gas Stripping (single stage) | $G, L$ | $y_1 = m x_1$ | $y_1 = (G y_2 + L x_2)/(G + L/m)$ | Henry's law, dilute |
| Ternary (partial miscibility) | $F, S$ | tie line on ternary | Lever rule + tie-line data | Graphical / numerical |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Mixer–Settler Single Stage

The canonical industrial arrangement for a single-stage liquid–liquid extraction is a **mixer** (intimate contact) followed by a **settler** (gravity decantation). The block diagram below maps the streams and unit operations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FIn["Feed F (carrier B + solute A)"] --> MIX
    SIn["Solvent S (pure C)"] --> MIX
    MIX["MIXER (agitated vessel)"]
    MIX -->|"Mixture M"| SET
    SET["SETTLER (gravity decanter)"]
    SET -->|"Raffinate R (B-rich phase)"| ROut["Raffinate R, x_R"]
    SET -->|"Extract E (C-rich phase)"| EOut["Extract E, y_E = K x_R"]
    style MIX fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SET fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ROut fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style EOut fill:#F5D5F5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Material-Balance Architecture

The diagram below shows how the total balance, the solute balance, and the equilibrium relation are coupled. Each block is a *constraint*; their intersection gives the unknown exit compositions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Known: F, x_F, S, y_S, K"] --> B["Total balance: F + S = R + E"]
    A --> C["Solute balance: F x_F + S y_S = R x_R + E y_E"]
    A --> D["Equilibrium: y_E = K x_R"]
    A --> E["Insolubility: R = F (1 - x_F), E = S"]
    B --> F["Solve simultaneously"]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Outputs: x_R, y_E, R, E, phi"]
    style F fill:#FFE08C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#B5E6A2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 y–x Diagram (Schematic) for Single-Stage Extraction

Although Mermaid cannot render Cartesian plots, the equivalent textual map below conveys the geometric picture used in KTU graphical solutions. The operating line (straight line through the inlet compositions) intersects the equilibrium curve $y = Kx$ at the **operating point** $(x_R, y_E)$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["(0, yS)"] --- P2["(xF, yS)"]
    P2 --- P3["Operating line"]
    P3 --- P4["(xR, yE) - operating point"]
    P4 --- P5["(xF, yF_in)"]
    subgraph EQ["Equilibrium y = K x"]
        EQC["straight line through origin slope K"]
    end
    P4 --- EQC
    style P4 fill:#FFCC66,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style EQ fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Drop a vertical line from $(x_R, y_E)$ down to the $x$-axis to read the raffinate composition; drop a horizontal line to the $y$-axis to read the extract composition. The slope of the operating line is $R/E$ (raffinate to extract mass ratio).

## 4.4 Single-Stage Absorption / Stripping Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GIn["Gas in: G, y2 (rich in A)"] --> STAGE
    LIn["Liquid in: L, x2 (lean in A)"] --> STAGE
    STAGE["SINGLE IDEAL STAGE - counter-current contact"]
    STAGE -->|"Exit gas G, y1 = m x1"| GOut["Lean gas y1"]
    STAGE -->|"Exit liquid L, x1"| LOut["Rich liquid x1"]
    style STAGE fill:#FFB870,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style GOut fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style LOut fill:#C8D8F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Block: Extension to Multi-Stage

A single stage is the *atomic unit* of every cascade. The block diagram below shows how $N$ single stages in series (or counter-current) combine to give a multi-stage cascade — a topic fully developed in Module 4 of PCBBT503.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage 1"] --> S2["Stage 2"] --> S3["Stage 3"] --> SN["Stage N"]
    S1 --- F1["Feed F enters Stage 1"]
    SN --- SN1["Final raffinate leaves Stage N"]
    S2 --- L1["Solvent S enters last stage"]
    style S1 fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S2 fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S3 fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style SN fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mathematics of the *single* stage is exactly the building block used in **Kremser / Colburn / McCabe–Thiele** analyses. A confident grasp of the derivations in Section 3 is therefore non-negotiable for the rest of the syllabus.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question must be answered in **30–60 words** with a crisp definition or one-line numerical result. Marks are awarded for *correct key words* and *units*.

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define an "ideal stage" or "equilibrium stage" in mass transfer operations. Why is the assumption of ideality used in design calculations?**

**Model Answer:**
An **ideal (or equilibrium) stage** is a theoretical contacting device in which the outgoing streams are in complete thermodynamic equilibrium, i.e., the compositions of the two phases leaving the stage satisfy the equilibrium relation (e.g., $y_E = K x_R$ or $y = m x$).

It is used in design because (i) it represents the **best possible performance** of a real stage, providing a *conservative upper bound* on the number of stages required; (ii) real-stage performance is then obtained by introducing a stage efficiency $E_M$ (typically Murphree); and (iii) the algebraic/graphical treatment is greatly simplified.

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State, with the governing equation, the distribution coefficient $K$ in liquid–liquid extraction. What does $K \gg 1$ and $K \ll 1$ imply about the choice of solvent?**

**Model Answer:**
$$ K \;=\; \left(\frac{y_A}{x_A}\right)_{\text{eq}} $$

where $y_A$ is the mass (or mole) fraction of solute $A$ in the extract (solvent-rich) phase and $x_A$ the fraction in the raffinate (carrier-rich) phase, both measured at equilibrium at the system temperature.

- $K \gg 1$: solute strongly prefers the solvent → **good solvent choice**, low solvent-to-feed ratio required.
- $K \ll 1$: solute prefers the carrier → **poor solvent**, very high $S/F$ needed; consider a different solvent.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, usually split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Show all material balances, state assumptions, substitute numerical values with units, and box the final answers. The KTU 2024 scheme allows internal choice — **both alternatives A and B are provided below**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — Single-Stage Liquid–Liquid Extraction (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** An aqueous feed of $200 \;\mathrm{kg/h}$ containing $25 \;\%$ by mass of **acetone** is to be extracted once with **pure benzene** at a solvent-to-feed mass ratio of $1.5$. The distribution coefficient of acetone between benzene (extract) and water (raffinate) is $K = y/x = 1.85$. Determine:
   (i) the mass of extract and raffinate produced,
   (ii) the mass fractions of acetone in each exit stream,
   (iii) the percentage recovery of acetone in the extract.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If two such single stages are connected in a **cross-current** cascade (each stage using fresh benzene at the same $S/F$ ratio), calculate the overall percentage recovery. Comment on the difference.

---

### ⭐ Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — Stream data.**
$F = 200$ kg/h, $x_F = 0.25$, $S = 1.5 \times 200 = 300$ kg/h, $y_S = 0$, $K = 1.85$.

**Step 2 — Assume carrier and solvent immiscible (justified for benzene–water).** Then $R \approx F(1-x_F) = 200 \times 0.75 = 150$ kg/h and $E \approx S = 300$ kg/h.

**Step 3 — Apply the single-stage formula:**

$$ x_R \;=\; \frac{F \, x_F}{F(1-x_F) + S \, K} \;=\; \frac{200 \times 0.25}{200 \times 0.75 + 300 \times 1.85} $$

$$ x_R \;=\; \frac{50}{150 + 555} \;=\; \frac{50}{705} \;=\; 0.0709 $$

**Step 4 — Extract composition:**

$$ y_E \;=\; K \, x_R \;=\; 1.85 \times 0.0709 \;=\; 0.1312 $$

**Step 5 — Percentage recovery (single stage):**

$$ \phi_1 \;=\; \frac{S \, K}{F(1-x_F) + S \, K} \;=\; \frac{555}{705} \;=\; 0.7872 \;\;(78.72\,\%) $$

**(i)** $E = 300$ kg/h, $R = 150$ kg/h. Total out = $450$ kg/h = $F + S$ ✓
**(ii)** $x_R = 0.071$, $y_E = 0.131$.
**(iii)** Recovery = $\mathbf{78.72 \;\%}$.

#### Part (b) — Cross-Current Two Stages

In a cross-current cascade, the **raffinate of stage 1 becomes the feed of stage 2** with a fresh batch of solvent. The total solvent used is $2S$, but the solute load in stage 2 is reduced.

**Stage 2 — input:** $F' = R_1 = 150$ kg/h (with acetone at $x_R = 0.0709$). Fresh solvent $S = 300$ kg/h, $y_S = 0$, $K = 1.85$.

**Step 6 — Apply the formula again:**

$$ x_{R,2} \;=\; \frac{F' \, x_{R,1}}{F'(1 - x_{R,1}) + S \, K} \;=\; \frac{150 \times 0.0709}{150 \times 0.9291 + 300 \times 1.85} $$

$$ x_{R,2} \;=\; \frac{10.635}{139.36 + 555} \;=\; \frac{10.635}{694.36} \;=\; 0.01532 $$

**Step 7 — Recovery in stage 2:**

$$ \phi_2 \;=\; \frac{S \, K}{F'(1-x_{R,1}) + S \, K} \;=\; \frac{555}{694.36} \;=\; 0.7992 $$

**Step 8 — Overall recovery (fraction of original acetone that left in *both* extracts):**

$$ \text{Acetone in feed} \;=\; 200 \times 0.25 \;=\; 50 \;\mathrm{kg/h} $$

$$ \text{Acetone in final raffinate} \;=\; 150 \times 0.9291 \times 0.01532 \;=\; 2.135 \;\mathrm{kg/h} $$

$$ \text{Overall recovery} \;=\; \frac{50 - 2.135}{50} \;=\; 0.9573 \;\;(95.73\,\%) $$

**Comment:** Two cross-current stages with the **same total solvent** (600 kg/h) recover $95.7 \;\%$ acetone vs. $78.7 \;\%$ in a single stage — a $\sim 17$ percentage-point improvement. This justifies multi-stage operation, which is covered in Module 4 of the syllabus.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — Single-Stage Absorption (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A gas stream of $100 \;\mathrm{kmol/h}$ containing $8 \;\%$ by mole of **$\mathrm{SO_2}$** in air is to be treated in a **single-stage absorber** using pure water at a flow rate of $250 \;\mathrm{kmol/h}$. The system follows Henry's law with $m = y/x = 30$ (mole-fraction basis) at the operating conditions. Calculate:
   (i) the exit gas and liquid mole fractions of $\mathrm{SO_2}$,
   (ii) the fraction of $\mathrm{SO_2}$ absorbed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The plant manager wishes to achieve **$95 \;\%$** absorption in a single stage. Determine the new solvent (water) flow rate required. State the physical limitation of a single stage as the target approaches $100 \;\%$.

---

### ⭐ Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — Stream data.**
$G = 100$ kmol/h, $y_2 = 0.08$ (entering), $L = 250$ kmol/h, $x_2 = 0$ (pure water), $m = 30$.

**Step 2 — Apply the single-stage absorption formula:**

$$ x_1 \;=\; \frac{G \, y_2 + L \, x_2}{L + G \, m} \;=\; \frac{100 \times 0.08 + 0}{250 + 100 \times 30} $$

$$ x_1 \;=\; \frac{8}{250 + 3000} \;=\; \frac{8}{3250} \;=\; 0.00246 $$

**Step 3 — Exit gas composition (Henry's law):**

$$ y_1 \;=\; m \, x_1 \;=\; 30 \times 0.00246 \;=\; 0.0738 $$

**Step 4 — Fraction absorbed:**

$$ \phi_{\text{abs}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{y_1}{y_2} \;=\; 1 - \frac{0.0738}{0.08} \;=\; 0.0775 \;\;(7.75\,\%) $$

Or equivalently using the absorption factor $A = L/(G m) = 250/3000 = 0.0833$:
$$ \phi_{\text{abs}} \;=\; \frac{A}{1+A} \;=\; \frac{0.0833}{1.0833} \;=\; 0.0769 \;\;(7.69\,\%) $$

> *(The two values differ by the small round-off in $x_1$; both are within numerical tolerance.)*

**(i)** $x_1 = 0.00246$, $y_1 = 0.0738$.
**(ii)** Fraction absorbed = $\mathbf{7.7 \;\%}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why so low?** Because $A = L/(Gm) = 0.083 \ll 1$, the absorption factor is *much* smaller than unity. The solvent flow is too low to drive significant mass transfer in a single stage. The KTU examiner will award full marks for **recognizing this design flaw** and recommending multi-stage operation.

#### Part (b) — Required Solvent Flow for 95 % Recovery

**Step 5 — Set $\phi = 0.95$ and solve for $A$:**

$$ 0.95 \;=\; \frac{A}{1+A} \;\Longrightarrow\; 0.95(1+A) \;=\; A \;\Longrightarrow\; 0.95 \;=\; 0.05 A $$

$$ A \;=\; 19 $$

**Step 6 — Solve for $L$:**

$$ A \;=\; \frac{L}{G \, m} \;\Longrightarrow\; L \;=\; A \, G \, m \;=\; 19 \times 100 \times 30 \;=\; 57\,000 \;\mathrm{kmol/h} $$

**Physical limitation as $\phi \to 1$:** As $A \to \infty$, $L \to \infty$. In a single stage, you can *never* reach 100 % absorption with a finite solvent flow — a stage always leaves a non-zero exit gas concentration. The only way to approach 100 % is to use an **infinite number of stages** (a counter-current cascade) or an **infinitely tall** packed column. The single stage is therefore always a *finite-fraction* device; design for *finite* recovery and use multi-stage for ultra-high purities.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in PCBBT503:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1 - x_F)$ factor** in the denominator of the single-stage extraction formula. Many students write $x_R = F x_F/(F + S K)$ — this is **only** valid for very dilute solutions. Always show the $F(1-x_F) + SK$ form unless the problem explicitly states "dilute".
> 2. **Confusing $y_S$ with zero.** If the problem says "the solvent contains 2 % solute," you **must** use the general formula with $y_S = 0.02$ — not the simplified pure-solvent version.
> 3. **Mismatched units.** If $F$ is in kg/h and $K$ is on a mole-fraction basis, the algebra is wrong. Convert all streams to a common basis (mass or moles) **before** substituting.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of carrier/solvent immiscibility.** KTU examiners *require* this assumption to be written explicitly. Omitting it typically costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify mass balance.** Always check $R + E = F + S$ at the end — a balanced check guarantees the algebra is right.
> 6. **In absorption problems, mixing up $y_1$ and $y_2$.** Subscript 1 = exit; subscript 2 = inlet. Drawing a labelled sketch before the calculation prevents this.
> 7. **Not reporting the percentage recovery in % units** — KTU wants "76.2 %" not "0.762".

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- A **single ideal stage** = one mixer + one settler, with exit phases at equilibrium.
- **Three governing equations**: total mass balance, solute balance, equilibrium relation.
- **Carrier–solvent immiscibility assumption** unlocks the simple analytical form $x_R = (F x_F + S y_S) / [F(1-x_F) + SK]$.
- **Pure solvent** ($y_S = 0$) further simplifies to $x_R = F x_F / [F(1-x_F) + SK]$.
- **Fraction extracted** $\phi = SK / [F(1-x_F) + SK]$ — the ratio of "solvent power" to "total resistance."
- For **leaching**: $1/x_A = 1 + S/(F x_F)$; the underflow liquid has the same composition as the overflow in a single stage.
- For **absorption**: $x_1 = (G y_2 + L x_2)/(L + G m)$ with $y_1 = m x_1$.
- **Kremser single-stage form**: $\phi_{\text{abs}} = A/(1+A)$ where $A = L/(Gm)$.
- **Sanity checks**: as $S \to \infty$, $\phi \to 1$; as $K \to 0$, $\phi \to 0$.
- **Single stage = upper bound on solvent**; multi-stage cascades always use **less solvent** for the same recovery.
- **Real stage efficiency** $E_M$ converts an *ideal* stage to a *real* (Murphree) stage; for KTU problems, default is $E_M = 1$ unless stated.
- **Triangular diagrams and the lever rule** are used when the carrier and solvent are *partially* miscible; algebraic formulas fail in this regime.
- **Graphical method on $y$–$x$ diagram**: operating line meets equilibrium curve at the stage exit point.
- **Always box final numerical answers** with units and a "% recovery" wherever applicable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross Current and Counter Current Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cross Current and Counter Current Extraction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Liquid–Liquid Extraction** is a separation technique in which a solute (**A**) is transferred from a carrier liquid (**B**) into a solvent (**S**) that is **immiscible** with the carrier. The solute-rich solvent phase is termed the **Extract (E)** and the depleted carrier phase is called the **Raffinate (R)**. When a **single contacting stage** is insufficient to achieve the desired recovery or purity, the operation is repeated in a **cascade** of stages. Two canonical cascade arrangements are mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 3: | Cascade Type | Solvent Addition Pattern | Industrial Usage | |---|---|---| | **Cross Current Extraction** | Fresh solvent supplied to **every** stage | Batch washing; bench-scale recovery | | **Counter Current Extraction** | Fresh solvent enters only the **last** stage; flows opposite to feed | Continuous industrial extractors (Podbielniak, RDC, Kühni) | ### Terminology Used Throughout These Notes Let: - $L_0, L_1, \dots, L_N$ = mass flow rate of **raffinate** (carrier + residual solute) leaving stages 0, 1, …, N - $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_{N+1}$ = mass flow rate of **extract** (solvent + transferred solute) - $x_0, x_1, \dots, x_N$ = mass fraction of solute **A in raffinate** - $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{N+1}$ = mass fraction of solute **A in extract** - $K_d$ or $m$ = **distribution (partition) coefficient** = equilibrium ratio of solute between extract and raffinate The fundamental equilibrium tie-line relationship is: $$ y^{*} \;=\; m\,x $$ where $m$ is assumed constant (valid for **dilute solutions** and constant temperature). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea-Washing" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine rinsing a stained cloth under a tap.** > - **Single Stage:** You hold the cloth once under the tap. Some stain comes out, but not all. > - **Cross Current:** You repeat the wash **with fresh clean water each time**. Each rinse uses a brand-new batch of clean solvent. Effective, but uses a lot of water. > - **Counter Current:** The cloth moves one way through a series of basins; clean water flows the opposite way. The dirtiest cloth meets the cleanest water. Uses the **least solvent** for the same cleaning. In extraction: - The **cloth** = carrier phase (e.g., water) loaded with solute - The **stain** = solute (e.g., acetic acid) - The **tap water / clean water** = pure solvent (e.g., benzene) - The **dirty water leaving** = extract phase - The **basins** = ideal mixer–settler stages This single analogy explains **why counter current extraction is the most solvent-efficient multi-stage arrangement** — the richest feed always contacts the most-spent solvent only at the feed end, and the most dilute raffinate contacts the freshest solvent at the other end, maximizing the driving concentration gradient in every stage. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stage-wise Material Balance — The Heart of Multi-Stage Extraction ### 2.1.1 Single Equilibrium Stage (Foundational Building Block) For an **ideal mixer–settler stage** where pure solvent ($y_S = 0$) contacts feed: $$ L\,x_{\text{in}} \;=\; L\,x_{\text{out}} \;+\; V\,y_{\text{out}} $$ Combined with the equilibrium tie-line $y_{\text{out}} = m\,x_{\text{out}}$: $$ L\,x_{\text{in}} \;=\; (L + mV)\,x_{\text{out}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{x_{\text{out}}}{x_{\text{in}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{mV}{L}}\;} $$ ### 2.1.2 Cross Current Cascade (Fresh Solvent Every Stage) ``` Stage 1: L0 + V ──► [mixer–settler] ──► L1 + V1 Stage 2: L1 + V ──► [mixer–settler] ──► L2 + V2 ⋮ Stage N: L(N-1) + V ──► [mixer–settler] ──► LN + VN ``` Each stage behaves identically (same $L$, same $V$, same $m$). Applying the single-stage result **N times in series**: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{x_N}{x_0} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + K_s}\right)^{N}\;} $$ where the **stage extraction factor** $K_s = \dfrac{mV}{L}$. ### 2.1.3 Counter Current Cascade ``` L0 (x0) ──► [Stage 1] ──► L1 (x1) ──► [Stage 2] ──► … ──► LN (xN) │ │ V1 (y1) VN+1 (yN+1) ▼ ▼ extract leaves fresh solvent in ``` Overall balance: $$ L_0\,x_0 \;+\; V_{N+1}\,y_{N+1} \;=\; L_N\,x_N \;+\; V_1\,y_1 $$ **Operating line** (derived from a partial balance around stages $n$ through $N$): $$ \boxed{\;y_{n+1} \;=\; y_1 \;+\; \dfrac{L}{V}\,(x_n - x_N)\;} $$ Slope = $L/V$; intercept passes through $(x_N,\,y_1)$. **Stage-wise operating equation + equilibrium** yields the **Kremser equation** for counter current extraction (pure solvent case): $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{x_N}{x_0} \;=\; \dfrac{A - 1}{A^{\,N+1} - 1}\;} $$ Solving for **N**: $$ \boxed{\;N \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right)\dfrac{x_0}{x_N} + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical edge case:** When $A = 1$, the logarithmic form above is indeterminate. Use: > $$N \;=\; \dfrac{x_0}{x_N} \;-\; 1 \quad \text{(for } A = 1,\; \text{pure solvent)}$$ ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Equilibrium tie-line | $y^{*} = m\,x$ | $m$ dimensionless, $T$ = const | | 2 | Stage extraction factor | $K_s = \dfrac{mV}{L}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Absorption factor | $A = \dfrac{L}{mV} = \dfrac{1}{K_s}$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Cross-current residual | $\dfrac{x_N}{x_0} = (1 + K_s)^{-N}$ | Fresh solvent each stage | | 5 | Counter-current residual | $\dfrac{x_N}{x_0} = \dfrac{A-1}{A^{N+1}-1}$ | Pure solvent ($y_{N+1}=0$) | | 6 | Counter-current stages | $N = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(1-\dfrac{1}{A}\right)\dfrac{x_0}{x_N}+\dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}$ | $A \neq 1$ | | 7 | Counter-current $A=1$ case | $N = \dfrac{x_0}{x_N} - 1$ | $L = mV$ | | 8 | Overall operating line | $y_{n+1} = y_1 + \dfrac{L}{V}(x_n - x_N)$ | Dilute, constant flows | | 9 | Overall material balance | $L_0 x_0 = L_N x_N + V_1 y_1$ | Pure solvent in | | 10 | Fraction extracted (cross) | $\phi_A = 1 - (1+K_s)^{-N}$ | Dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for KTU Examinations:** > - **Cross Current** → exponent is **N** (geometry: stages multiply). > - **Counter Current** → exponent is **N+1** (geometry: stages-plus-one in the denominator). This is the single most-failed detail in valuation scripts. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Kremser Equation for Cross Current Extraction **Starting point:** A single equilibrium stage with fresh pure solvent. **Step 1** — Write the solute material balance for the single stage. $$ L\,x_{\text{in}} \;+\; V\cdot 0 \;=\; L\,x_{\text{out}} \;+\; V\,y_{\text{out}} $$ **Step 2** — Replace $y_{\text{out}}$ with the equilibrium value $m\,x_{\text{out}}$. $$ L\,x_{\text{in}} \;=\; L\,x_{\text{out}} \;+\; V\,m\,x_{\text{out}} $$ **Step 3** — Factor the right-hand side. $$ L\,x_{\text{in}} \;=\; (L + mV)\,x_{\text{out}} $$ **Step 4** — Isolate the composition ratio. $$ \dfrac{x_{\text{out}}}{x_{\text{in}}} \;=\; \dfrac{L}{L + mV} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{mV}{L}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + K_s} $$ **Step 5** — Apply the same logic to **N identical stages in series**.
