Because each stage starts where the previous one ended and the solvent in every stage is fresh (identical composition $y_S = 0$), the ratios multiply: $$ \dfrac{x_1}{x_0} \cdot \dfrac{x_2}{x_1} \cdot \dfrac{x_3}{x_2} \cdots \dfrac{x_N}{x_{N-1}} \;=\; \dfrac{x_N}{x_0} $$ **Step 6** — Substitute the single-stage ratio $N$ times: $$ \dfrac{x_N}{x_0} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + K_s}\right)^{N} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(1 + K_s)^N} $$ **Step 7** — Solve for $N$ (target residual composition $x_N$ given $x_0$): $$ (1 + K_s)^N \;=\; \dfrac{x_0}{x_N} $$ $$ N\,\ln(1+K_s) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_N}\right) $$ $$ \boxed{\;N_{\text{cross}} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_N}\right)}{\ln(1 + K_s)}\;} $$ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Kremser Equation for Counter Current Extraction **Starting point:** Write the solute balance on stage $n$ alone (stage $n$ receives raffinate from stage $n-1$ and solvent-bearing extract from stage $n+1$): $$ L\,x_{n-1} \;+\; V\,y_{n+1} \;=\; L\,x_n \;+\; V\,y_n $$ **Step 1** — Rearrange to express the inlet extract composition: $$ V\,y_{n+1} \;=\; L\,(x_n - x_{n-1}) \;+\; V\,y_n $$ **Step 2** — Sum this equation from $n = 1$ to $n = N$. The $V\,y_n$ terms telescope. $$ \sum_{n=1}^{N} V\,y_{n+1} - V\,y_n \;=\; L\,\sum_{n=1}^{N}(x_n - x_{n-1}) + V\,y_{N+1} - V\,y_1 $$ The left side telescopes to $V\,y_{N+1} - V\,y_1$, so we obtain the **overall balance**: $$ V\,y_{N+1} - V\,y_1 \;=\; L\,(x_N - x_0) + V\,y_{N+1} - V\,y_1 $$ which is simply the global balance (an identity — not yet a working equation). **Step 3** — Sum the same balance but **stop at stage $n$** instead of $N$. Now the right side telescopes from $n = 1$ to current $n$, and the left side leaves a residual: $$ V\,y_{n+1} - V\,y_1 \;=\; L\,(x_n - x_0) + V\,y_{n+1} - V\,y_{N+1} $$ Wait — let us instead take a **partial balance from stage $n$ to the last stage $N$**: $$ V\,y_{n+1} \;-\; V\,y_{N+1} \;=\; L\,x_n \;-\; L\,x_N $$ **Step 4** — Set $y_{N+1} = 0$ (pure solvent entering the last stage) and rearrange: $$ V\,y_{n+1} \;=\; L\,(x_n - x_N) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y_{n+1} \;=\; \dfrac{L}{V}\,(x_n - x_N) $$ This is the **operating line** of counter current extraction. **Step 5** — Impose equilibrium at stage $n$: $y_{n+1} = m\,x_{n+1}$. Substitute: $$ m\,x_{n+1} \;=\; \dfrac{L}{V}\,(x_n - x_N) $$ $$ x_{n+1} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{A}\,(x_n - x_N), \qquad A = \dfrac{L}{mV} $$ **Step 6** — This is a first-order linear recurrence. Solve by induction starting from $x_N$. - For $n = N-1$: $\;x_{N-1} = A\,x_N + x_N = (1+A)\,x_N$ - For $n = N-2$: $\;x_{N-2} = A\,x_{N-1} + x_N = (A^2 + A + 1)\,x_N$ - In general: $\;x_0 = x_N\,(1 + A + A^2 + \dots + A^{N})$ **Step 7** — The geometric series sums to: $$ x_0 \;=\; x_N \cdot \dfrac{A^{N+1} - 1}{A - 1} \qquad (A \neq 1) $$ **Step 8** — Rearrange to obtain the Kremser residual ratio: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{x_N}{x_0} \;=\; \dfrac{A - 1}{A^{N+1} - 1}\;} $$ **Step 9** — Solve for $N$. Multiply both sides by $A^{N+1} - 1$: $$ A^{N+1} \;=\; (A-1)\,\dfrac{x_0}{x_N} \;+\; 1 $$ Take the natural logarithm and divide by $\ln A$: $$ N+1 \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[(A-1)\,\dfrac{x_0}{x_N} + 1\right]}{\ln A} $$ Subtract 1 and simplify the bracket: $$ (A-1)\,\dfrac{x_0}{x_N} + 1 \;=\; \dfrac{(A-1)\,x_0 + x_N}{x_N} \;=\; \dfrac{A\,x_0 - x_0 + x_N}{x_N} $$ Dividing by $A$ to reach the standard KTU form: $$ \dfrac{(A-1)\,x_0/x_N + 1}{A} \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right)\,\dfrac{x_0}{x_N} + \dfrac{1}{A} $$ Therefore: $$ N+1 \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right)\dfrac{x_0}{x_N} + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A} + 1 $$ giving the final result: $$ \boxed{\;N \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right)\,\dfrac{x_0}{x_N} + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}\;} $$ For $A = 1$, the geometric sum collapses to $x_0 = (N+1)\,x_N$, so $N = \dfrac{x_0}{x_N} - 1$. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Example 1 — Cross Current Extraction (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem) > **[KTU-Style Numerical]** Aqueous feed containing **30 wt% acetic acid** flows at **100 kg/h**. Pure benzene is used as solvent at **40 kg/h per stage** in a cross current cascade. Equilibrium: $y = 0.4\,x$. Calculate the number of stages required to reduce acid concentration in raffinate to **5 wt%**. Assume dilute-solution behaviour (raffinate and extract flows constant across stages). **Solution** **Step 1** — Identify the parameters. $$ x_0 = 0.30,\qquad x_N = 0.05,\qquad m = 0.4,\qquad L = 100\;\text{kg/h},\qquad V = 40\;\text{kg/h} $$ **Step 2** — Compute the stage extraction factor. $$ K_s \;=\; \dfrac{mV}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{0.4 \times 40}{100} \;=\; \dfrac{16}{100} \;=\; 0.16 $$ **Step 3** — Apply the cross-current Kremser equation. $$ \dfrac{x_N}{x_0} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(1 + K_s)^{N}} $$ $$ \dfrac{0.05}{0.30} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(1.16)^{N}} $$ $$ (1.16)^{N} \;=\; 6 $$ **Step 4** — Take natural logarithms. $$ N\,\ln(1.16) \;=\; \ln(6) $$ $$ N \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(6)}{\ln(1.16)} \;=\; \dfrac{1.7918}{0.14842} \;=\; 12.07 $$ **Step 5** — Round up to the next whole stage (a partial stage is physically unrealizable). $$ \boxed{\;N_{\text{cross}} \;=\; 13\;\text{stages}\;} $$ **Verification by direct computation** (successive application of $x_{n+1} = x_n / 1.16$): | Stage $n$ | $x_n$ (in) | $x_{n+1} = x_n / 1.16$ (out) | |---:|---:|---:| | 0 | 0.3000 | 0.2586 | | 5 | 0.1359 | 0.1172 | | 10 | 0.0616 | 0.0531 | | 12 | 0.0495 | 0.0427 | | **13** | **0.0427** | **0.0368** ← below 5 % ✓ | --- ## 3.4 Numerical Example 2 — Counter Current Extraction (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem) > **[KTU-Style Numerical]** Same feed as Example 1 (30 wt% acetic acid, 100 kg/h) is now treated in a **counter current extractor** using the same pure benzene, but at a **total** rate of **40 kg/h** entering the last stage only. Target raffinate: **5 wt%**. Find the number of ideal stages. **Solution** **Step 1** — Identify parameters. $$ x_0 = 0.30,\qquad x_N = 0.05,\qquad m = 0.4,\qquad L = 100,\qquad V = 40,\qquad y_{N+1} = 0 $$ **Step 2** — Compute the absorption factor. $$ A \;=\; \dfrac{L}{mV} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{0.4 \times 40} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{16} \;=\; 6.25 $$ **Step 3** — Apply the Kremser counter-current formula for $N$. $$ N \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right)\,\dfrac{x_0}{x_N} + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A} $$ Compute the bracket: $$ 1 - \dfrac{1}{6.25} \;=\; 1 - 0.16 \;=\; 0.84 $$ $$ 0.84 \times \dfrac{0.30}{0.05} \;=\; 0.84 \times 6 \;=\; 5.04 $$ $$ 5.04 + \dfrac{1}{6.25} \;=\; 5.04 + 0.16 \;=\; 5.20 $$ **Step 4** — Take logarithms. $$ N \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(5.20)}{\ln(6.25)} \;=\; \dfrac{1.6487}{1.8326} \;=\; 0.8996 $$ **Step 5** — Round up. $$ \boxed{\;N_{\text{counter}} \;=\; 1\;\text{stage}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comparative Insight:** Cross current needs **13 stages**; counter current needs only **1 stage** for the **identical separation**. This is precisely why industrial extraction trains are almost universally counter current. **Verification** using $x_N = x_0 (A-1)/(A^{N+1}-1)$: For $N = 1$: $$ x_1 \;=\; 0.30 \times \dfrac{6.25 - 1}{6.25^{2} - 1} \;=\; 0.30 \times \dfrac{5.25}{38.0625} \;=\; 0.30 \times 0.1379 \;=\; 0.0414 $$ This is below 0.05, confirming that one stage is sufficient. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The diagrams below use **Mermaid flowchart syntax**. Node identifiers are intentionally alphanumeric (e.g., `stageA`, `feedIn`, `solvIn`) so that the renderer does not conflict with reserved keywords. ## 4.1 Cross Current Cascade — Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR feedIn["Feed L0
x0 high"] --> stageA["STAGE 1
Mixer Settler"] solvA["Fresh Solvent V
y equals zero"] --> stageA stageA --> raffA["Raffinate L1
x1"] stageA --> extrA["Extract V1
y1 high"] raffA --> stageB["STAGE 2
Mixer Settler"] solvB["Fresh Solvent V
y equals zero"] --> stageB stageB --> raffB["Raffinate L2
x2"] stageB --> extrB["Extract V2
y2"] raffB --> stageC["STAGE N
Mixer Settler"] solvC["Fresh Solvent V
y equals zero"] --> stageC stageC --> raffC["Raffinate LN
xN low target"] stageC --> extrC["Extract VN
yN"] extrA --> combineOut["Combined
Extract V1 to VN"] extrB --> combineOut extrC --> combineOut ``` **Reading the diagram:** Fresh solvent enters **every** stage independently. The raffinate composition decreases monotonically stage by stage ($x_0 > x_1 > \dots > x_N$), and each extract stream leaves separately — they are blended downstream. ## 4.2 Counter Current Cascade — Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR feedIn["Feed L0
x0 high"] --> stageA["STAGE 1
Mixer Settler"] stageA --> raffA["Raffinate L1
x1"] stageA --> extrA["Extract V1
y1 highest"] raffA --> stageB["STAGE 2
Mixer Settler"] stageB --> raffB["Raffinate L2
x2"] stageB --> extrB["Extract V2
y2"] raffB --> stageC["STAGE N
Mixer Settler"] stageC --> raffC["Raffinate LN
xN low target"] stageC --> extrC["Extract VN
yN"] solvIn["Fresh Solvent
V N plus 1
y equals zero"] --> stageC ``` **Reading the diagram:** Solvent enters **only at the last stage**, while feed enters **only at the first stage**. Both phases flow **in opposite directions**. The solvent leaving stage 1 (extract V1) is the richest in solute because it has just contacted the richest feed — this is the thermodynamic driving force that minimises solvent consumption. ## 4.3 McCabe-Thiele Style Stage-Stepping Diagram (Conceptual Block) ```mermaid flowchart TD blockA["DIAGRAM XY PLANE
y axis extract composition
x axis raffinate composition"] blockB["Draw diagonal
y equals x reference line"] --> blockA blockC["Plot equilibrium line
y star equals m times x
passes through origin"] --> blockA blockD["Plot operating line
from point xN y1
to point x0 yN plus 1
slope L over V"] --> blockA blockE["Step off stages
vertical to operating line
horizontal to equilibrium line
count intersections N"] --> blockA ``` **How to "read" this on a graph:** 1. Mark point A on the $y$-axis side at $(x_N,\,y_1)$ — this is the extract-leaving-stage-1 ↔ raffinate-leaving-stage-$N$ pair. 2. Mark point B at $(x_0,\,y_{N+1})$ — the feed inlet ↔ fresh-solvent inlet pair. 3. Draw the straight operating line connecting A to B (slope $L/V$). 4. Draw the equilibrium line $y = m\,x$ through the origin. 5. From point A, draw a **vertical** line up to the operating line (this represents crossing a stage boundary on the extract composition), then **horizontally** to the equilibrium curve (reaching equilibrium), then **vertically** to the operating line again, and so on, zig-zagging between the operating line and the equilibrium curve. 6. The number of complete zig-zag pairs equals the **number of stages $N$**. ## 4.4 Functional Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart TD cc["CROSS CURRENT EXTRACTION"] --> c1["Fresh solvent each stage"] cc --> c2["Stages calculated by
xN over x0 equals one over bracket one plus Ks power N"] cc --> c3["Solvent consumption HIGH"] cc --> c4["Suitable for batch and
small scale operations"] ctc["COUNTER CURRENT EXTRACTION"] --> d1["Fresh solvent only in last stage"] ctc --> d2["Stages calculated by Kremser
xN over x0 equals A minus one over A power N plus one minus one"] ctc --> d3["Solvent consumption LOW"] ctc --> d4["Suitable for continuous
industrial scale operations"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]** > Differentiate between **single-stage extraction** and **multi-stage extraction**. Under what circumstances is a **counter current cascade** preferred over a **cross current cascade**? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Single Stage | Multi-Stage Cross Current | Multi-Stage Counter Current | |---|---|---|---| | Number of contacts | One | N (fresh solvent each) | N (one solvent feed) | | Solvent usage | Lowest for one contact | Highest | Lowest | | Achievable separation | Limited by single equilibrium | Better than single | Best (lowest $N$ for target $x_N$) | | Equipment | Single mixer–settler | Cascade of $N$ units | Cascade of $N$ units | A counter current cascade is preferred when: 1. **Large throughput / continuous operation** is required. 2. **Solvent is expensive or hazardous** — counter current minimises total solvent. 3. **High solute recovery** ($x_N \ll x_0$) is needed with the **fewest stages**. --- ### Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]** > Define the **distribution coefficient** ($m$) and the **extraction factor** ($K_s$) in liquid–liquid extraction. State one condition under which $m$ can be treated as a constant. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Distribution coefficient** $m = y^{*} / x^{*}$, the equilibrium ratio of solute mass fraction in the extract phase to that in the raffinate phase at a given temperature. - **Stage extraction factor** $K_s = mV / L$, the ratio of the solute's capacity in the solvent phase to its capacity in the carrier phase, **per stage**. - **Condition for constant $m$:** (i) **dilute solution regime** (Henry's-law type behaviour), and (ii) **constant temperature** with no chemical reaction between solute and solvent/carrier. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Most Common Mistakes** > 1. **Using $A^{N}$ instead of $A^{N+1}$** in the counter-current Kremser equation. This single error costs **3–4 marks** out of 7. > 2. Forgetting to **round up** the value of $N$ (you cannot build 0.9 of a stage — always take the next integer). > 3. Mixing up the **stage extraction factor** $K_s = mV/L$ with the **absorption factor** $A = L/mV$. They are reciprocals. > 4. Failing to state the **assumption of constant $L$ and $V$** (dilute solution) — board examiners explicitly deduct for not listing this. --- ### Question A — Counter Current Extraction (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the principle and operation of a **counter current extraction cascade**. Derive the **Kremser equation** relating raffinate composition to the number of stages. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution (a):** 1. **Schematic** — Refer to the **Counter Current Cascade block diagram** (Section 4.2). The fresh feed $L_0$ enters stage 1; pure solvent $V_{N+1}$ enters stage $N$. The two phases flow in opposite directions. 2. **Operating principle:** Because the most concentrated feed meets the most solute-loaded solvent (and vice versa), the **driving concentration difference** is maintained at every stage, yielding the minimum number of stages for a given separation. 3. **Derivation** — Use the partial balance from stage $n$ to stage $N$ with pure solvent ($y_{N+1} = 0$): $$ V\,y_{n+1} - V\cdot 0 \;=\; L\,x_n - L\,x_N \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V}(x_n - x_N) $$ 4. **Apply equilibrium** $y_{n+1} = m\,x_{n+1}$: $$ m\,x_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{L}{V}(x_n - x_N) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{A}(x_n - x_N) $$ 5. **Solve the recurrence** by induction: $$ x_0 = x_N(1 + A + A^2 + \dots + A^N) = x_N\,\dfrac{A^{N+1} - 1}{A - 1} $$ 6. **Final Kremser equation:** $$ \boxed{\;\frac{x_N}{x_0} = \frac{A - 1}{A^{N+1} - 1}\;} $$ **(b)** A feed of **200 kg/h** containing **35 wt% nicotine** in water is to be extracted with **kerosene** (pure solvent) at a rate of **80 kg/h** in a counter current extractor. The equilibrium relation is $y = 0.85\,x$. Determine the number of ideal stages required to reduce the nicotine content in the raffinate to **3 wt%**. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1** — Parameters: $$ x_0 = 0.35,\qquad x_N = 0.03,\qquad m = 0.85,\qquad L = 200,\qquad V = 80,\qquad y_{N+1} = 0 $$ **Step 2** — Absorption factor: $$ A \;=\; \frac{L}{mV} \;=\; \frac{200}{0.85 \times 80} \;=\; \frac{200}{68} \;=\; 2.941 $$ **Step 3** — Compute the bracket: $$ 1 - \frac{1}{A} \;=\; 1 - 0.3400 \;=\; 0.6600 $$ $$ 0.6600 \times \frac{0.35}{0.03} \;=\; 0.6600 \times 11.667 \;=\; 7.700 $$ $$ 7.700 + \frac{1}{A} \;=\; 7.700 + 0.3400 \;=\; 8.040 $$ **Step 4** — Apply the Kremser $N$ formula: $$ N \;=\; \frac{\ln(8.040)}{\ln(2.941)} \;=\; \frac{2.084}{1.079} \;=\; 1.93 $$ **Step 5** — Round up to the next whole stage. $$ \boxed{\;N \;=\; 2\;\text{ideal stages}\;} $$ --- ### Question B — Cross Current Extraction (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3 — Alternate]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the concept of a **cross current extraction cascade**. State its **advantages** and **limitations**. Derive the Kremser equation for cross current extraction. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution (a):** 1. **Schematic** — Refer to **Cross Current Cascade block diagram** (Section 4.1). Fresh solvent $V$ enters every stage; raffinate flows $L_0 \to L_1 \to \dots \to L_N$. 2. **Advantages:** Simpler construction; each stage operates independently (ideal for batch or analytical work); lower individual stage-solvent inventory per stage. 3. **Limitations:** **Very high solvent consumption** (sum of $V$ over all stages); **poor stage economy** for the same separation; extract streams are dilute and need separate recovery. 4. **Derivation** — Single-stage material balance with pure solvent: $$ L\,x_{\text{in}} = (L + mV)\,x_{\text{out}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{x_{\text{out}}}{x_{\text{in}}} = \frac{1}{1 + mV/L} = \frac{1}{1 + K_s} $$ 5. **$N$ identical stages in series**, each receiving fresh solvent: ratios multiply: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{x_N}{x_0} = \left(\frac{1}{1 + K_s}\right)^N = \frac{1}{(1 + K_s)^N}\;} $$ 6. **Solving for $N$:** $$ \boxed{\;N = \frac{\ln(x_0/x_N)}{\ln(1 + K_s)}\;} $$ **(b)** Aqueous solution containing **40 wt% acetone** is fed at **150 kg/h** to a three-stage cross current extractor. Pure toluene is used at **50 kg/h per stage**. Equilibrium: $y = 0.6\,x$. Find the **exit raffinate composition** and the **total solvent requirement** for the same separation using counter current extraction (assume one ideal stage). **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1** — Cross current: parameters and stage factor. $$ x_0 = 0.40,\qquad L = 150,\qquad V = 50,\qquad m = 0.6,\qquad K_s = \frac{mV}{L} = \frac{0.6 \times 50}{150} = 0.20 $$ **Step 2** — Apply Kremser for $N = 3$: $$ x_3 \;=\; \frac{x_0}{(1 + K_s)^3} \;=\; \frac{0.40}{(1.20)^3} \;=\; \frac{0.40}{1.728} \;=\; 0.2315 $$ $$ \boxed{\;x_3 \;\approx\; 0.232 \;\text{(23.2 wt% acetone)}\;} $$ **Step 3** — Counter current, one ideal stage ($N=1$). We need to find the **minimum solvent** $V_{cc}$ that achieves the same separation ($x_N = 0.232$). With pure solvent, $N=1$: $$ \frac{x_N}{x_0} = \frac{A - 1}{A^2 - 1} = \frac{1}{A + 1} $$ Therefore: $$ A + 1 = \frac{x_0}{x_N} = \frac{0.40}{0.232} = 1.724 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A = 0.724 $$ But $A = L/(mV_{cc})$, so: $$ V_{cc} \;=\; \frac{L}{m\,A} \;=\; \frac{150}{0.6 \times 0.724} \;=\; \frac{150}{0.4344} \;=\; 345.3\;\text{kg/h} $$ **Wait** — this $V_{cc}$ is larger than the total cross-current solvent! Why? Because cross-current used **3 × 50 = 150 kg/h** of solvent at the *same* $L$ but achieved a lower $x_N$ (0.232). To match the **same $x_N$** with **only one counter-current stage**, more solvent is needed. This highlights a subtle but important point: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The real comparison** is: For the **same total solvent** ($V_{cc} = 150$ kg/h), counter current needs **far fewer stages** to achieve the same $x_N$, not the same number of stages. Re-doing with $V_{cc} = 150$: $$ A \;=\; \frac{L}{m\,V_{cc}} \;=\; \frac{150}{0.6 \times 150} \;=\; 1.667 $$ For pure solvent with $A = 1.667$: $$ x_N \;=\; x_0 \cdot \frac{A - 1}{A^{N+1} - 1} $$ For $N = 1$: $$ x_1 \;=\; 0.40 \times \frac{0.667}{1.667^2 - 1} \;=\; 0.40 \times \frac{0.667}{1.779} \;=\; 0.40 \times 0.375 \;=\; 0.150 $$ This is much better than the cross-current 0.232 using the **same** total solvent. **Step 4** — Final answer for the question as posed: $$ \boxed{\;x_3 \;\approx\; 0.232\;\text{(cross current)},\qquad V_{cc} \;\approx\; 150\;\text{kg/h (same total solvent gives counter current } x_1 = 0.150\text{)}\;} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiners' pitfall:** Students often confuse "same solvent per stage" vs "same total solvent" between cross current and counter current. Always clarify in your answer which definition you are using. **KTU board examiners deduct 1 mark** if the comparison basis is not stated explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cross Current & Counter Current Extraction** ### Conceptual Hierarchy - **Single stage → Multi-stage cross current → Multi-stage counter current** (in order of increasing solvent efficiency). - **Counter current is the industrial default** for continuous extraction. ### Definition Recall - **Distribution coefficient** $m = y^*/x^*$ — equilibrium ratio at operating temperature. - **Stage extraction factor** $K_s = mV/L$ — solute capacity ratio per stage. - **Absorption factor** $A = L/(mV) = 1/K_s$ — the inverse. ### Core Equations (Memorize) - **Cross current residual:** $\;x_N/x_0 = (1 + K_s)^{-N}$ - **Counter current residual:** $\;x_N/x_0 = (A - 1)/(A^{N+1} - 1)$ - **Operating line (counter current):** $\;y_{n+1} = y_1 + (L/V)(x_n - x_N)$ ### Critical Distinctions - Cross current exponent on the bracket is **$N$**; counter current exponent on $A$ is **$N+1$**. - Pure solvent assumption ($y_{N+1}=0$) is the **default in KTU problems** unless stated otherwise. - $A = 1$ case requires the alternative formula $N = x_0/x_N - 1$. ### Assumptions Always to be Stated in Solutions 1. **Dilute solution** → $L$ and $V$ constant stage-to-stage. 2. **Equilibrium in each stage** (ideal stages; Murphree efficiency = 1 unless given otherwise). 3. **Carrier and solvent immiscible**. 4. **Constant temperature** → $m$ is a true constant. ### Graphical Method - **Plot on $x$–$y$ plane:** equilibrium line through origin (slope $m$), operating line from $(x_N, y_1)$ to $(x_0, y_{N+1})$ with slope $L/V$. - **Step off stages** by zig-zagging vertically and horizontally between the two lines. ### Industrial Equipment Mapping - Cross current → individual mixer–settlers in parallel trains. - Counter current → RDC columns, Kühni columns, Podbielniak extractors, Scheibel columns. ### Numerical Safety Checks - **Cross current always needs ≥ stages** than counter current for the same target. - Always **round up** the computed $N$. - Verify by plugging the rounded $N$ back into the residual equation — it should be **≤ target $x_N$**. ---

topics.title: Liquid-Liquid Extraction Equipments - Centrifugal Extractors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liquid-Liquid Extraction Equipments - Centrifugal Extractors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A **Centrifugal Extractor** is a continuous liquid-liquid extraction device that utilizes **centrifugal force** to achieve rapid, intimate contacting and subsequent phase separation of two immiscible liquid phases inside a rotating bowl or drum, thereby replacing (or augmenting) the gravitational settling force used in conventional columns and mixer-settlers.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you have a mixture of oil and vinegar that refuses to separate on the counter because the density difference is too tiny or because an emulsion has formed. If you place the bottle in a **very high-speed spinning drum**, the heavy liquid is thrown to the outer wall and the light liquid is squeezed toward the centre, where each can be drawn off continuously. That is exactly what a centrifugal extractor does — it is the **"industrial washing-machine"** for chemical separations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use gravity?** Because:
> 1. Some liquids (e.g., antibiotic broths, lube oil raffinates) form emulsions or have very small Δρ (≈ 0.05 g/cm³).
> 2. Short contact time is critical when solutes are **thermolabile** (e.g., penicillin).
> 3. Compact, low-stage-height design is desired when floor space is limited.

### 1.2 Operating Principle at a Glance

- A rotating element (drum or bowl) creates an **acceleration field** vastly greater than **g (9.81 m/s²)**.
- Liquid-liquid dispersion is generated inside the rotor, and separation occurs simultaneously by centripetal stratification.
- Heavy phase is flung to the **periphery**; light phase is pushed toward the **axis**.

**Key Operating Metric (must memorize):**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\omega^{2} r}{g}
$$

where **α = Acceleration Factor (g-force equivalent)** — the ratio of centrifugal to gravitational acceleration. Industrial machines operate between **α = 200** and **α ≈ 5000**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Centrifugal Force Works — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Centrifugal Force Generation:** When a mass *m* rotates at radius *r* with angular velocity *ω*, the centripetal force required is $F_c = m \omega^{2} r$.
2. **Effective Gravity Enhancement:** Because *g* and $\omega^{2} r$ both act like body forces on fluid elements, the effective settling acceleration becomes $\omega^{2} r + g \cos\theta$. In a horizontal rotor, *g*'s radial component vanishes, so the centrifugal term dominates.
3. **Droplet Migration:** A dispersed droplet of diameter *d_p* migrates outward at a velocity predicted by a modified Stokes law.
4. **Countercurrent Flow:** The two phases flow past each other counter-currently inside the rotor — mass transfer occurs as in any extractor, but residence time is on the order of **seconds** rather than minutes.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` in place of `|` for absolute-value expressions inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table syntax.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega^{2} r}{g} = \left(\dfrac{\pi N}{30}\right)^{2} \dfrac{r}{g}$ | Acceleration factor (g-force) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $F_c = m \omega^{2} r = m \, a_c$ | Centrifugal force on mass *m* | N (kg·m/s²) |
| 3 | $v_{s,c} = v_{s,g} \cdot \alpha = \dfrac{d_p^{2}\, \Delta\rho \, g}{18\mu} \cdot \alpha$ | Settling velocity in centrifugal field | m/s |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{2\pi N_{\text{stages}}}{60\,\dot Q_{\text{tot}}} V_{\text{rotor}}$ | Mean residence time per stage | s |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{\text{perf}} = \dfrac{\rho \, v_{h}^{2}}{2 \, C_{d}^{2}}$ | Pressure drop across perforated sheet | Pa |
| 6 | $H_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}}{2 \alpha \, d_p} \cdot \dfrac{18\mu}{\Delta\rho\,g\,d_p}$ | Effective settling path length | m |
| 7 | $NTU_{\text{oc}} = \dfrac{K_{oc}\, a \, V_{\text{active}}}{L_{c}}$ | Number of overall transfer units (light phase) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{x_{\text{in}} - x_{\text{out}}}{x_{\text{in}} - x^{*}}$ | Murphree stage efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V_{\text{centrifuge}} = \pi (r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}) \, L$ | Effective annular rotor volume | m³ |
| 10 | $P = \dot m \, \omega^{2} r$ | Power required to accelerate liquid | W |

Where:
- $\omega$ = angular velocity in rad/s, $N$ = rpm
- $d_p$ = droplet diameter (m), $\Delta\rho = \rho_{H} - \rho_{L}$ (kg/m³)
- $\mu$ = continuous-phase viscosity (Pa·s), $C_d$ ≈ 0.6 for perforated plate
- $r_o$, $r_i$ = outer and inner radii of rotor (m), $L$ = axial length (m)

### 2.3 Classification of Centrifugal Extractors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the *distinguishing features* of each extractor type. Memorize the **axis orientation**, **stage count**, and **typical application**.

| Type | Axis | Stages | Typical Application |
|------|------|--------|---------------------|
| **Podbielniak** (most common) | Horizontal | Multi-stage (up to 12) | Antibiotics, lube oil refining, pharmaceuticals |
| **Luwesta** (similar to Pod) | Horizontal | 3–6 stages | Petrochemical dearomatization |
| **De Laval** | Vertical | Single stage | Nuclear fuel reprocessing |
| **Robatel** | Vertical | Single / multi | Specialty chemicals |
| **Quadronic** | Vertical | Multi | Waste-water treatment |
| **Westfalia** | Vertical | Multi-stage | Edible oil refining |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Recovery of penicillin, streptomycin — short contact time prevents thermal degradation.
- **Petroleum refining:** Lube oil solvent extraction (furfural, NMP, phenol).
- **Nuclear reprocessing:** Separation of U/Pu from fission products where Δρ is very small.
- **Food industry:** Decaffeination of coffee and tea using supercritical/dense-gas solvents.
- **Effluent treatment:** Removal of phenols and chlorinated hydrocarbons from water.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Acceleration Factor (α)

Starting from Newton's second law in the rotating frame, the centripetal acceleration required to keep a particle of mass *m* in a circular orbit of radius *r* is:

$$
a_{c} \;=\; \omega^{2} r
$$

Because **g (9.81 m/s²)** is the reference acceleration for gravity-based separation, define the **acceleration factor**:

$$
\alpha \;\equiv\; \frac{a_{c}}{g} \;=\; \frac{\omega^{2} r}{g}
$$

Substitute $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^{2} r}{g} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} N^{2} r}{900\, g}
$$

**Numerical check (Podbielniak typical):** N = 3000 rpm, r = 0.25 m, g = 9.81 m/s²

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} (3000)^{2} (0.25)}{900 (9.81)} \;\approx\; 2513 \text{ g}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Settling Velocity in a Centrifugal Field

For a rigid sphere of diameter *d_p* settling under Stokes' law in a continuous fluid:

$$
v_{s,g} \;=\; \frac{d_p^{2}\, (\rho_{H} - \rho_{L})\, g}{18 \mu}
$$

Replacing *g* by the centrifugal acceleration $\omega^{2} r$:

$$
v_{s,c} \;=\; \frac{d_p^{2}\, \Delta\rho\, (\omega^{2} r)}{18 \mu}
$$

Because $\alpha = \omega^{2} r / g$:

$$
v_{s,c} \;=\; \alpha \cdot v_{s,g} \;=\; \frac{\alpha \, d_p^{2}\, \Delta\rho \, g}{18 \mu}
$$

**Interpretation:** The settling velocity is amplified by exactly the acceleration factor — this is why emulsions that need hours under gravity separate in **fractions of a second** inside a centrifuge.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Settling Path & Droplet Trajectory

Consider a droplet formed at radius $r_i$ that must reach $r_o$ before being swept out. The radial migration time is:

$$
t_{\text{set}} \;=\; \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{dr}{v_{s,c}} \;=\; \frac{r_{o} - r_{i}}{v_{s,c}} \quad (\text{average approximation})
$$

Substitute $v_{s,c}$:

$$
t_{\text{set}} \;=\; \frac{18 \mu (r_{o} - r_{i})}{\alpha\, d_p^{2}\, \Delta\rho\, g}
$$

The allowed residence time in the rotor:

$$
t_{\text{res}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{rotor}}}{\dot Q_{\text{total}}}
$$

For **complete phase separation** we require $t_{\text{res}} \ge t_{\text{set}}$, which yields the **minimum rotor volume** for a given throughput:

$$
V_{\text{min}} \;=\; \frac{18 \mu \,\dot Q_{\text{tot}}\, (r_{o} - r_{i})}{\alpha\, d_p^{2}\, \Delta\rho\, g}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Single-Stage Material Balance

For a centrifugal extractor operating as an ideal stage (equilibration at outlet compositions):

$$
L \, (x_{\text{in}} - x_{\text{out}}) \;=\; V \, (y_{\text{out}} - y_{\text{in}})
$$

where $L$ = light (solvent) flow rate (kg/s), $V$ = heavy (raffinate) flow rate (kg/s), *x* and *y* are mass fractions in light and heavy phases.

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{L}{V} \;=\; \frac{y_{\text{out}} - y_{\text{in}}}{x_{\text{in}} - x_{\text{out}}}
$$

Defining the **distribution coefficient** $m = y / x$ at equilibrium and the **extraction factor** $E = mV / L$:

$$
N_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{x_{\text{in}} - y_{\text{in}}/m}{x_{\text{out}} - y_{\text{in}}/m}\right)\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{E}\right) + \dfrac{1}{E}\right]}{\ln E}
$$

This is the **Kremser equation**, used to compute the equivalent ideal stages of any continuous extractor (including centrifugal).

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power Consumption

Energy is needed to bring the incoming liquid up to rotor speed. For a throughput $\dot m$ entering at radius $r$:

$$
P \;=\; \dot m \, \omega^{2} r \;=\; \dot m \, \alpha \, g \, r
$$

**Check:** $\dot m = 5$ kg/s, $\alpha = 2000$, $r = 0.25$ m:

$$
P \;=\; 5 \times 2000 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 \;=\; 24{,}525 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 24.5 \text{ kW}
$$

This is the *minimum* isentropic power; real machines require 1.5–2× this value due to bearing friction and fluid dissipation.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Podbielniak extractor operates at 2500 rpm. The rotor has an inner radius of 0.08 m and outer radius of 0.28 m. The dispersed phase droplet size is 200 µm, with $\rho_H = 980$ kg/m³, $\rho_L = 860$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate:
(a) Acceleration factor at outer radius
(b) Centrifugal settling velocity of a droplet
(c) Minimum residence time for complete migration

**Solution:**

**(a)** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi (2500)}{60} = 261.80$ rad/s

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\omega^{2} r_{o}}{g} \;=\; \frac{(261.80)^{2} \times 0.28}{9.81} \;=\; \frac{19{,}184}{9.81} \;\approx\; 1956
$$

**(b)**

$$
v_{s,c} \;=\; \frac{\alpha \, d_p^{2}\, \Delta\rho\, g}{18 \mu}
$$

Compute each term:
- $d_p^{2} = (2 \times 10^{-4})^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m²
- $\Delta\rho = 980 - 860 = 120$ kg/m³
- Numerator: $1956 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 120 \times 9.81 = 0.0921$
- Denominator: $18 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0216$

$$
v_{s,c} \;=\; \frac{0.0921}{0.0216} \;\approx\; 4.26 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(c)**

$$
t_{\text{set}} \;=\; \frac{r_{o} - r_{i}}{v_{s,c}} \;=\; \frac{0.28 - 0.08}{4.26} \;\approx\; 0.047 \text{ s}
$$

**Conclusion:** A droplet separates in just **47 ms**, illustrating the dramatic speed advantage of centrifugal extraction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Podbielniak Extractor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heavy Phase In]:::in --> B[Rotating Drum]
    C[Light Phase In]:::in --> B
    B --> D{Concentric Perforated Cylinders}
    D --> E[Mixing Zone Stage 1]
    D --> F[Mixing Zone Stage 2]
    D --> G[Mixing Zone Stage N]
    E --> H[Settling Band]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Light Phase Out]:::out
    H --> J[Heavy Phase Out]:::out
    B -.-> K[Shaft and Bearings]
    K -.-> L[Drive Motor 2500 rpm]
    classDef in fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a
    classDef out fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#0f172a
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The two inlet streams enter at opposite ends of the rotor through stationary nozzles via mechanical seals.
- Countercurrent contact occurs across concentric perforated sleeves.
- Settling happens in the annular band at the periphery.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — De Laval Vertical Extractor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Heavy Phase Nozzle]:::in --> M[Central Mixing Chamber]
    P2[Light Phase Nozzle]:::in --> M
    M --> R{Rotating Bowl 4000 rpm}
    R --> S[Centripetal Settling Zone]
    S --> O1[Light Phase Outlet Central]:::out
    S --> O2[Heavy Phase Outlet Peripheral]:::out
    R --> Dr[Drive Shaft Vertical]
    Dr --> Mo[Electric Motor]
    classDef in fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a
    classDef out fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#0f172a
```

### 4.3 Functional Comparison Flow Chart — Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Choose Extractor]) --> Q1{Is Δρ greater than 0.1 g per cm cubed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is liquid thermally sensitive?}
    Q1 -- No --> Centrifuge[Use Centrifugal Extractor]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Centrifuge
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Throughput greater than 50 cubic m per hour?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Column[Use Packed or RDC Column]
    Q3 -- No --> Mixer[Use Mixer Settler]
    Centrifuge --> Sub{Continuous or batch?}
    Sub -- Continuous --> Pod[Podbielniak or Luwesta]
    Sub -- Batch single stage --> Delaval[De Laval]
```

### 4.4 Equivalent Theoretical Stage Concept (Textual Schematic)

```
   Inside Rotor (Cross-section view)
   =================================================
    STAGE N:  ┃  Heavy →  ●●●●●●  ← Light          ┃
              ┃          (Mixing)                   ┃
              ┃   ↓                 ↑               ┃
              ┃ Settle            Settle            ┃
              ┃   ↓                 ↑               ┃
    STAGE 2:  ┃  Heavy →  ●●●●●●  ← Light          ┃
              ┃          (Mixing)                   ┃
              ┃   ↓                 ↑               ┃
              ┃ Settle            Settle            ┃
              ┃   ↓                 ↑               ┃
    STAGE 1:  ┃  Heavy →  ●●●●●●  ← Light          ┃
   =================================================
    HEAVY OUT ←─────────────   ─────────→ LIGHT OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1. What is a centrifugal extractor? Mention its principal operating advantage.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A centrifugal extractor is a liquid-liquid extraction device that uses **centrifugal force** generated by a rotating bowl or drum to achieve simultaneous dispersion and phase separation of two immiscible liquids. The principal advantage is the ability to separate liquids with **very small density difference (Δρ < 0.05 g/cm³)** and to handle **emulsion-forming systems** in a very short contact time (seconds), which protects thermally sensitive solutes such as antibiotics from degradation. *(3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q2. Define the term "acceleration factor" used in centrifugal extractor design.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The acceleration factor (α) is the ratio of centrifugal acceleration at a given radius to standard gravitational acceleration. It quantifies how many "g's" the fluid experiences inside the rotor.

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\omega^{2} r}{g}
$$

For typical industrial Podbielniak extractors, α ranges between **2000 and 5000**. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Podbielniak centrifugal extractor**. Discuss its industrial applications. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A Podbielniak extractor operates at 4000 rpm. The rotor has an effective inner radius 0.10 m and outer radius 0.30 m. Droplet diameter = 150 µm, ρ_H = 950 kg/m³, ρ_L = 870 kg/m³, μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. Calculate the (i) acceleration factor at the outer radius, (ii) centrifugal settling velocity, (iii) time required for a droplet to migrate from inner to outer radius. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction:**
- Horizontal cylindrical drum rotating about a horizontal shaft.
- Inside the drum are **concentric perforated cylinders** (usually 5–12) that divide the annular space into discrete stages.
- Each cylinder is perforated; perforations alternate in direction between adjacent stages to force intimate mixing.
- Two stationary inlet/outlet nozzles pass through the shaft seals — one for heavy phase, one for light phase.

**Working:**
- The drum rotates at high speed (2000–5000 rpm), generating α = 2000–5000 g.
- **Heavy phase** enters at the periphery and flows inward through perforations; **light phase** enters at the centre and flows outward.
- Countercurrent contact across each perforated cylinder achieves mass transfer; centripetal settling separates the phases between stages.

**Applications:**
- Recovery of antibiotics (penicillin, streptomycin)
- Solvent refining of lubricating oils
- Phenol extraction from waste-water
- Vitamin purification

*(7 marks — 2 marks sketch, 3 marks working, 2 marks applications)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i)** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 4000}{60} = 418.88$ rad/s

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{(418.88)^{2} \times 0.30}{9.81} \;=\; \frac{52{,}636}{9.81} \;\approx\; 5366
$$

**(ii)**

- $d_p^{2} = (1.5 \times 10^{-4})^{2} = 2.25 \times 10^{-8}$ m²
- $\Delta\rho = 80$ kg/m³

$$
v_{s,c} \;=\; \frac{5366 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \times 80 \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
v_{s,c} \;=\; \frac{0.0948}{0.018} \;\approx\; 5.27 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii)**

$$
t_{\text{set}} \;=\; \frac{r_{o} - r_{i}}{v_{s,c}} \;=\; \frac{0.30 - 0.10}{5.27} \;\approx\; 0.038 \text{ s}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks each for parts i, ii, iii + 1 mark for correct units and final answer presentation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare **Podbielniak**, **Luwesta**, and **De Laval** centrifugal extractors with respect to axis orientation, stage count, and typical applications. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A centrifugal extractor handles a feed of 8 kg/s. The rotor radius is 0.25 m and it spins at 3500 rpm. Calculate the **minimum power** required to accelerate the liquid to rotor speed. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

| Feature | Podbielniak | Luwesta | De Laval |
|---------|-------------|---------|----------|
| Axis | Horizontal | Horizontal | Vertical |
| Stage count | Multi (up to 12) | 3–6 | Single (typically) |
| Typical use | Pharma, lube oil | Petrochemicals | Nuclear reprocessing |
| Distinguishing feature | Concentric perforated sleeves | Similar to Pod, simpler internals | Centripetal flow in single chamber |

*(7 marks — full table 4 marks + discussion of selection logic 3 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 3500}{60} = 366.52$ rad/s
- Centripetal acceleration: $\omega^{2} r = (366.52)^{2} \times 0.25 = 33{,}572$ m/s²
- Acceleration factor: $\alpha = 33572/9.81 = 3422$ g

Minimum isentropic power:

$$
P_{\min} \;=\; \dot m \, \omega^{2} r \;=\; 8 \times 33{,}572 \;\approx\; 268{,}580 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 268.6 \text{ kW}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks for ω, 2 marks for α, 3 marks for power computation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Wrong angular velocity conversion:** Many students write $\omega = 2\pi N$ instead of $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Always convert rpm → rad/s.
> 2. **Forgetting the g in α:** Some compute $\alpha = \omega^{2} r$ and forget to divide by *g*; the result will be dimensionally wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up phases:** Heavy phase moves *outward* in a horizontal rotor (centrifugal); light phase moves *inward*. Reverse the answer and you lose 1–2 marks immediately.
> 4. **Skipping unit conversions:** $\Delta\rho$ must be in kg/m³ (not g/cm³); *d_p* must be in metres.
> 5. **Forgetting the 1/2 factor or 18μ denominator in Stokes' law:** A classic KTU trap.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What you MUST remember for the exam:**

- ☐ **Acceleration factor:** $\alpha = \omega^{2} r / g = \pi^{2} N^{2} r / (900 g)$. Range: 200–5000 for industrial machines.
- ☐ **Stokes settling velocity in centrifuge:** $v_{s,c} = \alpha \, v_{s,g}$.
- ☐ **Heavy phase goes outward, light phase goes inward** in a horizontal rotor.
- ☐ **Podbielniak = horizontal multi-stage; De Laval = vertical single-stage.**
- ☐ **Kremser equation** is used to calculate equivalent ideal stages.
- ☐ **Minimum residence time** for full separation: $t_{\text{set}} = (r_o - r_i) / v_{s,c}$.
- ☐ **Power:** $P = \dot m \, \omega^{2} r$ (minimum isentropic).
- ☐ **Applications:** pharmaceuticals, lube oils, nuclear reprocessing, food industry, wastewater treatment.
- ☐ **Advantages:** handles low Δρ, short contact time, compact, low hold-up.
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** high capital cost, high power consumption, mechanical complexity, limited to clean (non-particulate) feeds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocating Plate Column Extractors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reciprocating Plate Column Extractors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocating Plate Column (RPC) Extractor:** A vertical, mechanically-agitated liquid–liquid extraction device in which a stack of perforated plates, mounted on a central reciprocating shaft, is driven up and down at a controlled frequency and stroke length inside a stationary cylindrical shell, generating a finely dispersed two-phase flow that enhances interphase mass transfer between a light and a heavy liquid phase flowing counter-currently.

The unit is classified under the family of **mechanically-aided differential contactors** (along with rotary-disk contactors, Kühni columns, and Scheibel columns) and is a workhorse in the pharmaceutical, petrochemical, and nuclear-fuel-reprocessing industries.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The "Laundry Wash Pole" Concept:** Imagine vigorously plunging a perforated basket (the plates) up and down in a bucket of soapy water containing greasy clothes. The reciprocation forces water to jet through the clothes repeatedly — alternating washing and squeezing — which removes dirt far faster than simply letting the clothes soak. In an RPC extractor, the perforated plates act as the plunging basket, and the two immiscible liquids are the "dirt" and the "solvent," with one phase being forced through the perforations of the other to dramatically accelerate solute transfer.

### 1.3 Standard Design & Operating Metrics

The key engineering parameters that govern RPC performance are tabulated below:

| Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Column **diameter** | **50 – 1500 mm** | Throughput capacity |
| Plate **free area** | **20 – 60 %** | Controls interfacial area and pressure drop |
| **Hole** diameter | **3 – 8 mm** | Determines drop size of dispersed phase |
| Plate **spacing** | **25 – 150 mm** | Balances mixing vs. back-mixing |
| **Stroke** length | **5 – 50 mm** | Energy input per oscillation |
| **Frequency** of reciprocation | **50 – 500 strokes·min⁻¹** | Power dissipation & turbulence |
| **Power density** (P/V) | **0.1 – 5 kW·m⁻³** | Direct measure of agitation intensity |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often expect students to quote typical ranges. Memorize the **stroke length (5–50 mm)** and **frequency (50–500 min⁻¹)** as these are the most frequently tested numerical values.

### 1.4 Historical & Industrial Context

The reciprocating-plate concept was pioneered by **van Dijck (1935)** and later commercialized by companies such as **Koch Modular Process Systems** (originally the "Koch extractor"). It is the preferred choice when:
- High throughput is required in a small footprint.
- The system is prone to fouling and needs self-cleaning.
- High interfacial tension systems are being processed (unlike rotary-disk contactors that struggle with viscous or high-IFT systems).
- Plug-flow behaviour with low axial mixing is desired.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Component-wise breakdown)

1. **Stationary outer shell** – A vertical, flanged, cylindrical pressure vessel (typically **stainless steel, glass-lined, or Hastelloy**) that contains the two-phase mixture.
2. **Central reciprocating shaft** – A rigid vertical rod connected at the top to a **variable-speed drive assembly** comprising a motor, gear reducer, and an eccentric (Scotch-yoke) mechanism that converts rotary motion into linear reciprocation.
3. **Perforated plates** – Circular discs of thickness 1–3 mm, drilled with hundreds of holes (usually 3–8 mm diameter). Plates are either:
    - **Open-perforation (no downcomers)** – Most common; both phases must pass through the holes.
    - **Segmental or annular baffles** – For specialized duties requiring phase separation between stages.
4. **Top and bottom settling sections** – Plate-free zones (typically **300 – 600 mm** in height) fitted with **interface controllers** and **phase-distribution rings** to deliver clean phase separation.
5. **Drive housing** – Encloses the motor, flywheel, and eccentric cam; usually mounted on a sturdy support frame above the column.

### 2.2 Working Principle – Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 – Phase Introduction:** The **heavy phase (continuous, typically aqueous)** enters the top, while the **light phase (dispersed, typically organic)** enters the bottom. (This configuration can be reversed depending on which phase is dispersed.)
- **Step 2 – Counter-current Flow:** Both phases flow counter-currently through the column, with the dispersed phase rising as droplets.
- **Step 3 – Reciprocation & Dispersion:** As the central shaft oscillates, droplets are repeatedly sheared, broken, and re-formed at each plate. The high local energy dissipation causes the **dispersed-phase hold-up to increase sharply**.
- **Step 4 – Coalescence & Re-dispersion:** During the *up-stroke* and *down-stroke* cycles, droplets alternately coalesce on the plate surfaces and re-disperse through the holes — this **repetitive coalescence-redispersion** is the *core* mass-transfer-enhancing mechanism.
- **Step 5 – Phase Discharge:** The light phase coalesces in the top settling section and overflows; the heavy phase settles in the bottom and discharges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the reciprocation is the key driver:** Mechanical reciprocation provides **5 – 50 times** the energy dissipation per unit volume compared to gravity-driven (spray) columns, while still maintaining a near-plug-flow residence-time distribution (low Peclet-number axial mixing) — a unique advantage of RPCs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{slip} = \frac{V_d}{\epsilon} + \frac{V_c}{1-\epsilon}$ | Slip velocity; superficial velocities of dispersed and continuous phases; hold-up | m·s⁻¹; fundamental two-phase flow relation |
| 2 | $V_0 = \frac{V_d}{\epsilon \cdot X_d} + \frac{V_c}{1-\epsilon}$ | Characteristic velocity (Richardson–Zaki) | m·s⁻¹; $X_d$ is dispersed phase volume fraction at a reference condition |
| 3 | $\epsilon \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{0.5} \cdot V_0 = V_c + V_d \cdot \left(\frac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon}\right)^{0.5}$ | Flooding correlation for RPCs (slip-velocity form) | m·s⁻¹; used to predict flooding point |
| 4 | $P = \frac{2 \pi^{2}}{3} \cdot N^{3} \cdot \rho_{p} \cdot N_{p} \cdot D_{p}^{5} \cdot C$ | Power input by plate stack | W; $N$ = stroke frequency, $D_p$ = plate diameter, $N_p$ = no. of plates, $C$ = coefficient |
| 5 | $P/V = \rho_{c} \cdot (V_{slip})^2 \cdot g / \text{constant}$ | Power density correlation | W·m⁻³; engineering approximation |
| 6 | $HETS = \frac{H_{column}}{N_{theoretical}}$ | Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Stage | m; KPI for extractor efficiency |
| 7 | $k_L \cdot a \propto (P/V)^{0.4 \text{ to } 0.6}$ | Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient scaling | s⁻¹; widely accepted empirical power-law |
| 8 | $d_{32} \propto \left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho_{c}}\right)^{0.6} \cdot \epsilon^{-0.5} \cdot (P/V)^{-0.4}$ | Sauter mean drop diameter | m; Hinze-Kolmogorov-type relation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formulae pitfall:** In the KTU exam, never confuse $V_d$ (superficial velocity of dispersed phase) with $v_d$ (actual droplet velocity). The slip-velocity relations use the **superficial** (volumetric flow rate ÷ column cross-section), not the actual velocity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear-fuel reprocessing** (PUREX process): RPCs extract uranium and plutonium from nitric acid solutions using kerosene/dodecane-based solvents — the most cited industrial application.
- **Pharmaceutical API purification:** Heat-sensitive biomolecules (antibiotics, alkaloids) are extracted gently at low temperature.
- **Waste-water treatment:** Removal of phenols, aromatics, and chlorinated hydrocarbons from refinery effluents.
- **Vegetable-oil processing:** Deacidification of crude oils using liquid–liquid extraction.
- **Fine chemicals:** High-purity separations of cis–trans isomers, chiral resolutions, etc.

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions on this topic often ask: *"Why is an RPC preferred over a spray column for high-IFT systems?"* — Answer: Because mechanical reciprocation supplies the additional energy needed to overcome the high interfacial-tension resistance to droplet break-up.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Slip-Velocity Relation (Two-Phase Flow)

The slip-velocity concept is foundational for sizing any differential extractor, including the RPC. Below is a fully detailed derivation.

**Starting point — Definition of superficial velocity:**

For a column of cross-sectional area $A_c$, if $Q_d$ and $Q_c$ are the volumetric flow rates of the dispersed and continuous phases respectively:

$$V_d = \frac{Q_d}{A_c} \quad ; \quad V_c = \frac{Q_c}{A_c}$$

**Volume fractions and hold-up:**

If $V_{tot}$ is the total volume of the dispersion and $V_{disp}$ is the volume occupied by the dispersed phase, then the **dispersed-phase hold-up** $\epsilon$ is:

$$\epsilon = \frac{V_{disp}}{V_{tot}}$$

By definition of volume conservation:

$$\epsilon + (1-\epsilon) = 1$$

**Actual (interstitial) velocities:**

The actual velocity of a droplet moving through the continuous phase is greater than $V_d$ because the droplet can only travel through the fraction of cross-section that is occupied by the dispersed phase. Hence:

$$u_d = \frac{V_d}{\epsilon} \quad ; \quad u_c = \frac{V_c}{1-\epsilon}$$

**Definition of slip velocity:**

The slip velocity is the *relative* velocity between the two phases, taken in the direction of dispersed-phase motion:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{slip} &= u_d - u_c \\
&= \frac{V_d}{\epsilon} - \frac{V_c}{1-\epsilon}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental slip-velocity relation** used as a building block for the more complex flooding and hold-up correlations in RPCs.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Characteristic Velocity (Richardson–Zaki Approach)

For a swarm of identical droplets rising in a quiescent continuous phase, Richardson and Zaki proposed that the actual droplet velocity depends on the hold-up:

$$u_{d,\infty} = u_{t} \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{n}$$

where $u_t$ is the single-droplet terminal velocity and $n$ is the Richardson–Zaki index (typically **2.4 – 4.8**, with $n \approx 4.6$ for $Re_t > 500$).

The superficial velocity of the dispersed phase is then:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \epsilon \cdot u_{d,\infty} \\
&= \epsilon \cdot u_t \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{n}
\end{aligned}$$

At a *reference* condition where the column is filled with the dispersed phase alone ($\epsilon \to 1$), the superficial velocity approaches the **characteristic velocity** $V_0$:

$$V_0 = u_t \cdot (1)^{n} = u_t$$

Therefore, in any two-phase flow:

$$\frac{V_d}{\epsilon} = V_0 \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{n}$$

Substituting back into the slip-velocity relation:

$$V_{slip} = V_0 \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{n} - \frac{V_c}{1-\epsilon}$$

This equation is the work-horse of RPC design — it ties the hold-up to the operating flow rates via the characteristic velocity.

### 3.3 Flooding-Velocity Derivation for RPC

At flooding, the hold-up of the dispersed phase reaches a maximum value, $\epsilon_f$, beyond which the continuous phase can no longer flow downward (or dispersed phase upward). The slip velocity at flooding is:

$$V_{slip,f} = V_0 \cdot (1-\epsilon_f)^{n}$$

For a counter-current extractor, the total flow capacity of the column at flooding is constrained by the slip-velocity equation. Solving the slip-velocity relation for the maximum combined throughput gives the **flooding condition** (often called the *Smoot–Babb correlation* for reciprocating columns):

$$V_{c,f} = V_0 \cdot (1-\epsilon_f)^{n} \cdot \epsilon_f^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{n \cdot \epsilon_f}{1-\epsilon_f} + 1\right)^{-1}$$

Equivalently, in terms of the superficial velocities at the flooding point:

$$\frac{V_d}{V_0} + \frac{V_c}{V_0} = \text{constant determined by } \epsilon_f \text{ and } n$$

A simpler engineering form, widely used in KTU problems, is:

$$\boxed{\;\sqrt{V_{c,f}} + \sqrt{V_{d,f}} = \text{constant} = C_{RPC}\;}$$

The constant $C_{RPC}$ depends on the plate geometry (free area, hole diameter), physical properties ($\Delta \rho$, $\mu_c$, $\sigma$), and reciprocation intensity. The design engineer typically operates the column at **50 – 70 %** of this flooding value to leave a safety margin.

### 3.4 HETS Derivation from Stage Efficiency

The **overall stage efficiency** $E$ of an RPC is related to the height of a transfer unit (HTU) and the height of a theoretical stage (HETS) by:

$$HETS = HTU_{ox} + HTU_{or}$$

where the subscripts refer to the extract (organic) and raffinate (aqueous) phases. The HTU is related to the overall mass-transfer coefficient $K$ and the superficial velocity by:

$$HTU = \frac{V}{K \cdot a}$$

Here $a$ is the specific interfacial area (m²·m⁻³). The empirical scaling law (from Section 2.3) is:

$$K \cdot a \propto \left(\frac{P}{V}\right)^{0.4}$$

Therefore, the HETS can be estimated for design:

$$HETS = V \cdot \left(K \cdot a\right)^{-1} = V \cdot C_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P}{V}\right)^{-0.4} = C_2 \cdot V^{1.4} \cdot P^{-0.4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why HETS **decreases** as power input increases — but with diminishing returns. Doubling the power input reduces HETS by only about **24 %** (since $2^{-0.4} \approx 0.76$).

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** An RPC of diameter **150 mm** is to be used to extract acetic acid from water using toluene. The dispersed (organic) phase flow rate is **3.0 × 10⁻⁵ m³·s⁻¹** and the continuous (aqueous) phase flow rate is **4.0 × 10⁻⁵ m³·s⁻¹**. Calculate (a) the superficial velocities, (b) the actual droplet velocity at a hold-up of 0.20, and (c) the slip velocity.

**Step (a) — Superficial velocities:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_c &= \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.150)^2 \\
&= 0.01767 \; \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{Q_d}{A_c} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01767} = 1.697 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·s}^{-1} \\
V_c &= \frac{Q_c}{A_c} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01767} = 2.263 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (b) — Actual droplet velocity at $\epsilon = 0.20$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
u_d &= \frac{V_d}{\epsilon} = \frac{1.697 \times 10^{-3}}{0.20} \\
&= 8.485 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (c) — Slip velocity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
u_c &= \frac{V_c}{1-\epsilon} = \frac{2.263 \times 10^{-3}}{0.80} = 2.829 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·s}^{-1} \\
V_{slip} &= u_d - u_c = 8.485 \times 10^{-3} - 2.829 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 5.656 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board-answer presentation rule:** Always show *units at every step* and *box the final answer*. KTU examiners give full credit only when the dimensional analysis is unambiguous.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Organic Light Phase Inlet] -->|Bottom inlet| B[Bottom Settling Zone]
    C[Aqueous Heavy Phase Inlet] -->|Top inlet| D[Top Settling Zone]
    B -->|Dispersed droplets rise| E[Reciprocating Plate Stack]
    D -->|Continuous phase descends| E
    E -->|Up-stroke shear| F[Drop Break-up Zone]
    E -->|Down-stroke compression| G[Drop Coalescence Zone]
    F -->|Re-dispersion| H[Turbulent Mixing Region]
    G -->|Coalescence on plate| H
    H -->|Counter-current contact| I[Mass Transfer Zone]
    I -->|Acetic acid transfer| J[Solute Partition Interface]
    J -->|Enriched organic overflows| K[Top Phase Outlet]
    J -->|Lean aqueous discharges| L[Bottom Phase Outlet]
    M[Variable-Speed Motor] --> N[Scotch Yoke Eccentric Cam]
    N -->|Linear reciprocation| O[Central Drive Shaft]
    O -->|Connects to| E
    P[Phase Interface Controller] -.->|Level control| B
    Q[Sampling Ports] -.->|On-line monitoring| E
```

### 4.2 Annotated Block Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Shell[Stationary Column Shell]
        direction TB
        TS[Top Settler]
        PS[Plate Stack 25 to 150 mm spacing]
        BS[Bottom Settler]
    end
    subgraph Drive[Drive Assembly Above Shell]
        MOT[Electric Motor] --> GB[Gear Reducer]
        GB --> EYE[Eccentric Cam / Scotch Yoke]
    end
    EYE -->|Converts rotary to linear| SH[Reciprocating Shaft]
    SH -->|Moves plates up and down| PS
    LP[Light Phase] -->|Enters bottom| BS
    HP[Heavy Phase] -->|Enters top| TS
    PS -->|Counter-current contact| LP
    PS -->|Counter-current contact| HP
    TS -->|Organic overflow| OUT1[Raffinate/Extract Outlet]
    BS -->|Aqueous discharge| OUT2[Solvent Outlet]
```

### 4.3 Mass-Transfer Zone Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Feed heavy phase xF] --> S1[Mixing on Plate 1]
    S1 --> S2[Mass transfer]
    S2 --> S3[Plate 2]
    S3 --> S4[Plate 3]
    S4 --> S5[Plate n-1]
    S5 --> S6[Plate n]
    S6 --> S7[Final equilibrium xN]
    S7 --> R2[Raffinate exits]
    Y0[Lean solvent y0] --> S1
    S6 --> YN[Loaded solvent yN exits]
    S1 -.->|Equilibrium line| YEQ1
    S3 -.->|Equilibrium line| YEQ2
    S5 -.->|Equilibrium line| YEQ3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 board exam, if a diagram is requested, a *labelled* block schematic (as above) carrying the names of all components and arrows showing flow direction fetches **full marks**. Hand-drawn sketches in the answer book should follow the same component arrangement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a reciprocating plate column (RPC) extractor. List any two of its advantages over a spray column.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A reciprocating plate column extractor is a vertical liquid–liquid extraction device in which perforated plates mounted on a central shaft are moved up and down at a controlled frequency and stroke length inside a cylindrical shell, providing mechanical agitation to the counter-currently flowing two-phase system. (2 marks)

**Two advantages over a spray column:** (1 mark)
1. **Higher mass-transfer efficiency** (lower HETS) due to forced dispersion and repeated coalescence–re-dispersion of droplets.
2. **Ability to handle high-interfacial-tension systems** because the mechanical energy input supplements gravity to break the dispersed phase into fine droplets.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the role of the settling zones provided at the top and bottom of an RPC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The settling zones are plate-free regions at the top and bottom of the column, typically **300–600 mm** in height, whose primary roles are: (1) to allow the dispersed-phase droplets to **coalesce** into a continuous organic phase for clean phase separation, (2) to provide residence time for **gravity separation** of any entrained phase, and (3) to house the **interface-level controller** that maintains the position of the liquid–liquid interface. (3 marks — 1 mark per correct role)

---

### Part B – Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled schematic, explain the **construction and working principle** of a reciprocating plate column extractor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **effect of stroke length and frequency** on the HETS and flooding behaviour of an RPC. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

The construction of an RPC consists of: (1 mark each for any **seven** components)

- A vertical cylindrical **shell** of stainless steel, glass-lined steel, or special alloys.
- A **central shaft** that runs the full height of the column.
- A stack of **perforated plates** welded/bolted at regular intervals (25–150 mm) on the shaft.
- A **drive mechanism** mounted on the top flange, comprising an electric motor, gear box, and a Scotch-yoke or eccentric-cam that converts rotary motion into linear reciprocation.
- **Phase inlet nozzles** for the light and heavy phases at the bottom and top respectively.
- **Top and bottom settling zones** with phase-distribution rings and an interface-level controller.
- **Sample ports, sight glasses, and instrument tappings** for monitoring temperature, pressure, and phase composition.

**Working Principle:** The heavy (continuous) phase enters the top, and the light (dispersed) phase enters the bottom. As the plates reciprocate, the shear and turbulence cause the dispersed phase to break into fine droplets that pass through the perforations. The high interfacial area thus generated promotes **solute transfer** from the continuous to the dispersed phase. The droplets repeatedly coalesce on each plate surface and re-disperse, providing a multi-stage differential contact. The phases exit through the top (extract) and bottom (raffinate) settling sections. (3 marks)

##### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Effect of stroke length (L) and frequency (N):** (1 mark for each effect below)

1. **Energy dissipation:** Power input scales as $P \propto N^{3} \cdot L^{2.5}$ (approximately). Increasing either parameter increases the power density, which **decreases HETS** (improves mass transfer) up to a point. (2 marks)
2. **Hold-up:** Higher reciprocation intensity increases the dispersed-phase hold-up $\epsilon$ by suppressing droplet rise velocity. (1 mark)
3. **Drop size:** Increased power density produces **smaller droplets** ($d_{32}$ decreases), increasing the interfacial area $a$. (1 mark)
4. **Flooding limit:** At very high $N$ and $L$, the column reaches flooding prematurely because the hold-up becomes excessive. The **flooding velocity decreases** with increasing $N$ and $L$. (1 mark)
5. **Optimum:** A practical optimum exists where HETS is minimized without flooding; typically **stroke = 10–25 mm** and **frequency = 100–300 strokes·min⁻¹** for most systems. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the **slip-velocity relation** for a counter-current liquid–liquid extractor and explain its significance in determining the flooding point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RPC of **0.20 m** diameter handles an aqueous phase at **6.0 × 10⁻⁵ m³·s⁻¹** and an organic phase at **4.5 × 10⁻⁵ m³·s⁻¹**. If the characteristic velocity is **0.025 m·s⁻¹** and the Richardson–Zaki index is **3.5**, calculate the **hold-up** at operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Derivation:** (4 marks)
- The superficial velocity of the dispersed phase is $V_d = Q_d / A_c$, and similarly $V_c = Q_c / A_c$. (1 mark)
- The actual (interstitial) velocity of the dispersed phase is $u_d = V_d / \epsilon$ because the droplets can only travel through the dispersed-phase volume fraction $\epsilon$. (1 mark)
- Similarly, $u_c = V_c / (1 - \epsilon)$. (1 mark)
- The slip velocity is defined as the relative velocity between the two phases: $V_{slip} = u_d - u_c = V_d/\epsilon - V_c/(1-\epsilon)$. (1 mark)

**Significance in flooding:** (3 marks)
- At the flooding point, the hold-up reaches a maximum value $\epsilon_f$ that the column can sustain. (1 mark)
- Substituting $\epsilon_f$ into the slip-velocity equation gives the maximum combined throughput: $V_{c,f} + V_{d,f}$ is constrained by $V_{slip,f} = V_0 (1-\epsilon_f)^n$. (1 mark)
- The **design rule** is to operate the column at **50–70 %** of the flooding superficial velocity to ensure a safety margin against upsets. (1 mark)

##### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Superficial velocities:**

$$V_c = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.03142} = 1.910 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·s}^{-1}$$

$$V_d = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.03142} = 1.432 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Richardson–Zaki equation:**

$$V_d = V_0 \cdot \epsilon \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{n}$$

$$1.432 \times 10^{-3} = 0.025 \cdot \epsilon \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{3.5}$$

Let $f(\epsilon) = \epsilon \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{3.5}$. We need $f(\epsilon) = 5.728 \times 10^{-2}$.

**Step 4 — Solve iteratively:**

- Try $\epsilon = 0.15$: $f(0.15) = 0.15 \cdot (0.85)^{3.5} = 0.15 \cdot 0.567 = 0.0850$ (too high)
- Try $\epsilon = 0.10$: $f(0.10) = 0.10 \cdot (0.90)^{3.5} = 0.10 \cdot 0.698 = 0.0698$ (slightly high)
- Try $\epsilon = 0.085$: $f(0.085) = 0.085 \cdot (0.915)^{3.5} = 0.085 \cdot 0.725 = 0.0616$ (slightly high)
- Try $\epsilon = 0.08$: $f(0.08) = 0.08 \cdot (0.92)^{3.5} = 0.08 \cdot 0.737 = 0.0590$ (slightly low)
- Try $\epsilon = 0.082$: $f(0.082) = 0.082 \cdot (0.918)^{3.5} = 0.082 \cdot 0.7325 = 0.0601$ (slightly high)
- Try $\epsilon = 0.0815$: $f(0.0815) = 0.0815 \cdot (0.9185)^{3.5} \approx 0.0815 \cdot 0.7305 = 0.0595$ (close)

Therefore, $\boxed{\epsilon \approx 0.082}$ (i.e., about **8.2 %** hold-up). (3 marks for the iterative solution, 1 mark for the final answer, 3 marks for steps 1–3)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up $V_d$ with $u_d$:** The slip-velocity equation uses *superficial* velocities. Using actual velocities will lead to an answer that is **out by a factor of 5 to 10** — and full mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the bottom settling zone:** Many students draw the column without the lower settler. This is a 1-mark deduction per component in the construction diagram.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in numericals:** Always express flow rate in **m³·s⁻¹**, diameter in **m**, and velocity in **m·s⁻¹**. Mixing LPH and m³·s⁻¹ is the most common mistake.
> 4. **Conflating the RPC with a pulsed column:** An RPC has *mechanically driven plates*, whereas a pulsed column is *pulsed hydraulically* (no moving parts inside the shell). KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Reciprocating Plate Column Extractors**

- ✔ **Definition** – Vertical, mechanically agitated extractor with perforated plates on a central reciprocating shaft.
- ✔ **Key components** – Shell, shaft, perforated plates, eccentric drive, top & bottom settling zones, interface controller.
- ✔ **Typical operating metrics** – Stroke **5–50 mm**; frequency **50–500 min⁻¹**; plate free area **20–60 %**; hole size **3–8 mm**.
- ✔ **Working principle** – Counter-current two-phase flow with **coalescence–re-dispersion cycles** driving mass transfer.
- ✔ **Slip velocity** – $V_{slip} = V_d/\epsilon - V_c/(1-\epsilon)$ — fundamental two-phase flow relation.
- ✔ **Richardson–Zaki** – $V_d = V_0 \cdot \epsilon \cdot (1-\epsilon)^{n}$, with $n \approx 2.4 \text{ to } 4.8$.
- ✔ **Flooding rule** – Operate at **50–70 %** of flooding superficial velocity.
- ✔ **HETS scaling** – $HETS \propto V^{1.4} \cdot P^{-0.4}$; increases with throughput, decreases with power input.
- ✔ **Mass transfer scaling** – $K \cdot a \propto (P/V)^{0.4}$ — log-log slope of **0.4**.
- ✔ **Drop size scaling** – $d_{32} \propto \sigma^{0.6} \cdot (P/V)^{-0.4}$ — Hinze–Kolmogorov type.
- ✔ **Key applications** – PUREX nuclear reprocessing, pharmaceutical API purification, waste-water phenol removal, vegetable-oil deacidification.
- ✔ **RPC vs. Spray column** – RPC has lower HETS, handles high-IFT systems, but has moving parts and higher capital/maintenance cost.
- ✔ **RPC vs. Pulsed column** – RPC has *internal moving* plates; pulsed column is *externally pulsed* with no internals.
- ✔ **RPC vs. RDC** – RPC handles high-IFT better; RDC has lower axial mixing for viscous systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversed Micellar Extraction and Super Critical Fluid Extraction - Principles and Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reversed Micellar Extraction and Super Critical Fluid Extraction - Principles and Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Reversed Micellar Extraction (RME)

**Formal Definition:**
Reversed Micellar Extraction is a liquid-liquid extraction technique that uses **reversed (or inverse) micelles** — nanometer-sized, thermodynamically stable aggregates of surfactant molecules dispersed in a **non-polar organic phase** — as selective hosts to solubilize and separate **biomolecules** (proteins, enzymes, amino acids, nucleic acids) from an aqueous feed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Reverse micelles have their **hydrophilic (polar) head groups oriented inward** toward a tiny water pool, while their **hydrophobic (non-polar) tails point outward** into the bulk organic solvent. This is the *exact opposite* of a normal micelle in water.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Trojan Horse":**
Imagine you have a valuable guest (a delicate protein) that dissolves happily in water but refuses to enter oil. Normally, oil and water don't mix, so you cannot simply move the protein into the oil phase. Now, picture a **soap bubble floating in oil**: the soap film (surfactant) has its water-loving side facing *inside* the bubble and its oil-loving side facing *outward* into the oil. The protein happily hides inside the water pool inside this bubble, and the whole "bubble-with-cargo" floats around in the oil! This bubble is a **reversed micelle**, and the process of smuggling biomolecules into the organic phase using these bubbles is reversed micellar extraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Surfactant Used in Industry:** Sodium bis(2-ethylhexyl) sulfosuccinate, commonly known as **AOT** (or Aerosol-OT), dissolved in *iso-octane*. The parameter $W_0 = \dfrac{[\text{Water}]}{[\text{Surfactant}]}$ controls the micelle size and solubilizing power.

### 1.2 Super Critical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)

**Formal Definition:**
Super Critical Fluid Extraction (SCFE) is a separation process in which a **supercritical fluid (SCF)** — a substance held *above* its **critical temperature ($T_c$)** and **critical pressure ($P_c$)** — is used as the solvent to selectively dissolve and extract a target component from a solid or liquid matrix. The extract is then recovered by **depressurization**, which collapses the SCF into a gas and precipitates the solute.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Chameleon Solvent":**
Think of a substance that can behave like a gas in some moments and a liquid in others, depending on how much you squeeze it. Above its critical point, a supercritical fluid has the **penetrating power of a gas** (low viscosity, high diffusivity) *combined* with the **dissolving power of a liquid** (high density). It flows easily through coffee grounds or spice particles, dissolves the caffeine or essential oils, then escapes as a harmless gas when the pressure is released, leaving behind the pure extracted product — **nothing left behind, no solvent residue!**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Carbon Dioxide is the "King" of SCFs:**
> - $T_c = 304.1\ \text{K}$ (**31.1 °C**) — gentle enough for heat-sensitive natural products
> - $P_c = 73.8\ \text{bar}$ — easily achievable with commercial pumps
> - **Non-toxic, non-flammable, GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe) by FDA**
> - Cheap, abundantly available as a by-product of fermentation, combustion, and ammonia synthesis
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reversed Micellar Extraction — Mechanism & Process

#### Step 1: Surfactant Aggregation in Organic Phase
When an amphiphilic surfactant is dissolved in an **apolar organic solvent** (e.g., iso-octane) above its **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)**, it spontaneously self-assembles into **reversed micelles**. The driving force is the **hydrophobic effect** combined with minimization of free energy.

#### Step 2: Forward Extraction (Loading the Micelle)
The aqueous feed containing the biomolecule is contacted with the organic reverse-micellar phase. The protein is transferred into the **inner aqueous core** of the reverse micelle through several simultaneous interactions:

- **Electrostatic interactions** — governed by $\text{pH}$ of the aqueous phase relative to the protein's isoelectric point ($pI$)
- **Hydrophobic interactions** — between surfactant tails and hydrophobic patches on the protein surface
- **Steric / size-exclusion effects** — the protein must physically fit inside the water pool (radius $1-10\ \text{nm}$)
- **Specific ligand binding** — affinity surfactants (e.g., AOT analogs with affinity ligands) enable selectivity

#### Step 3: Phase Separation
After equilibrium, the two phases are separated — the **organic (reverse-micellar) phase** now carries the solubilized biomolecule, while the **raffinate (spent aqueous phase)** is discarded or recycled.

#### Step 4: Back Extraction (Stripping / Recovery)
A second aqueous phase (with a different $\text{pH}$ or ionic strength) is contacted with the loaded micellar phase. Changing these conditions **destabilizes the micelle-protein complex**, releasing the protein back into the new aqueous phase for recovery.

#### Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Standard Value | Effect on Extraction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water-to-surfactant molar ratio | $W_0 = \dfrac{[\text{H}_2\text{O}]}{[\text{Surfactant}]}$ | $5 - 50$ | Controls micelle size; higher $W_0$ = larger water pool = accommodates bigger proteins |
| pH of aqueous phase | $\text{pH}$ | $5 - 9$ | Determines protein charge; for $pH < pI$, protein is **cationic** and attracted to anionic surfactants (AOT) |
| Ionic strength | $I = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$ | $0.01 - 1.0\ \text{M}$ | High $I$ screens electrostatic attraction, reduces extraction efficiency |
| Temperature | $T$ | $15 - 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Affects CMC, micelle size, and protein stability |
| Surfactant type | AOT, CTAB, Tween series | — | Determines charge, geometry, and selectivity |

### 2.2 Super Critical Fluid Extraction — Principles

#### The Critical Point — Why SCFs Are Special

A pure substance has three classical phase boundaries: **solid–liquid**, **liquid–vapor**, and **solid–vapor**. These all **terminate at the critical point** $(T_c,\ P_c)$. Above $T_c$, no amount of pressure can liquefy the gas; above $P_c$, no amount of cooling can condense it. The substance becomes a **single, dense, compressible fluid** with hybrid properties.

**Solubility Power in an SCF:**
The solvating power of an SCF is **density-driven**. Solubility $S$ of a solid solute in an SCF is empirically given by the **Chrastil Equation (1982)**:

$$S = \rho^{k} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{A}{T} + B\right)$$

where:
- $S$ = solubility (g solute / g SCF)
- $\rho$ = density of the SCF (g/L) — tuned by $P$ and $T$
- $k$ = association number (typically 1–10), number of SCF molecules clustered around each solute molecule
- $A = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{sol}}}{R}$ (enthalpy of solvation divided by gas constant)
- $B$ = constant depending on solute molecular weight and SCF molar volume
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
