> **Chrastil equation takeaway for KTU:** *Increase pressure* (at constant $T$) → density $\rho$ rises → solubility $S$ rises sharply. *Increase temperature* (at constant $P$) → two competing effects: $S$ may rise (vapor-pressure effect) or fall (density-drop effect), producing a **retrograde solubility region** typical of SCFE.

#### Process Flow Steps

1. **Feed Preparation** — Solid matrix is ground to a uniform particle size (typically $0.5 - 2\ \text{mm}$) to expose surface area and minimize intra-particle diffusion resistance.
2. **Extraction Vessel Loading** — Solid is packed into an extractor; SCF is pumped in at $P > P_c$, $T > T_c$.
3. **Dissolution / Mass Transfer** — SCF penetrates the solid matrix, dissolves the target solute, and the loaded SCF (called the **extract phase**) leaves the extractor.
4. **Pressure Reduction / Separation** — The extract passes through an expansion valve into a **separator** at lower $P$. The SCF density drops, solubility collapses, and the solute **precipitates out**.
5. **SCF Recycle** — The depressurized SCF (now a gas) is condensed, re-pressurized by a compressor/pump, and recycled to the extractor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **high-yield formula cheat sheet** for Module 3 questions. Memorize all entries.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units & Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water-to-surfactant ratio | $W_0 = \dfrac{n_{\text{water}}}{n_{\text{surfactant}}}$ | Dimensionless; typical $5$ to $50$ |
| 2 | Mean reverse-micelle radius (Nagarajan model) | $R_m = \dfrac{3 V_{\text{water}}}{A_s} + \ell_s$ | $\ell_s$ = surfactant tail length (~ $1\ \text{nm}$ for AOT) |
| 3 | Chrastil solubility equation | $S = \rho^{k} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{A}{T} + B\right)$ | $S$ in g/g, $\rho$ in g/L, $T$ in K |
| 4 | SCF reduced properties | $T_r = \dfrac{T}{T_c},\quad P_r = \dfrac{P}{P_c}$ | Both must be $> 1$ for supercritical state |
| 5 | Peng-Robinson EOS for SCF density | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V_m - b} - \dfrac{a\,\alpha(T)}{V_m(V_m+b) + b(V_m-b)}$ | Solved iteratively for $V_m$ at given $T, P$ |
| 6 | Mass fraction of solute in raffinate (extraction yield) | $Y = \dfrac{x_{f,0} - x_f}{x_{f,0} - x_f^{*}}$ | $x_f^{*}$ = equilibrium concentration |
| 7 | Recovery in back extraction | $\%R = \dfrac{C_{\text{strip}}}{C_{\text{feed,initial}}} \times 100$ | Used for RME two-step process |
| 8 | Hildebrand solubility parameter | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta H_v - RT}{V_m}}$ | Used to predict SCF solvating power |
| 9 | Peclet number in SCFE column | $Pe = \dfrac{u \cdot L}{D_L}$ | Indicates axial dispersion (L = bed length) |
| 10 | Extraction rate (mass-transfer controlled) | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_f\,a\,(q^{*} - q)$ | $k_f$ = film mass transfer coefficient, $a$ = specific area |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** SCFE with CO$_2$ extracts **active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs)** from herbal matrices (e.g., artemisinin from *Artemisia annua*, paclitaxel precursors) without leaving toxic solvent residues.
- **Food Industry:** **Decaffeination of coffee and tea** (Kaffree process); extraction of **hops** for beer, **vanilla**, **capsaicin** from chillies, **oils from spices**.
- **Cosmetics & Fragrances:** Extraction of delicate essential oils (rose, jasmine, sandalwood) at low temperatures preserves aromatic compounds.
- **Biotechnology / Downstream Processing:** RME is used as a **gentle first-step capture** of intracellular enzymes (e.g., α-amylase, ribonuclease) from fermentation broth — avoids the denaturation caused by traditional solvent extraction.
- **Environmental Engineering:** SCFE removes **PAHs, PCBs, and pesticides** from contaminated soils; supercritical water oxidation destroys toxic organics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reverse-Micelle Water-Pool Radius

A reverse micelle is modeled as a **spherical droplet of water (radius $R_w$) surrounded by a monolayer of surfactant of tail length $\ell_s$**. The total micelle radius is $R_m = R_w + \ell_s$.

**Step 1 — Volume Balance:**
Let $V_s$ = volume of one surfactant molecule (cm$^3$/molecule) and $A_s$ = head-group area (cm$^2$/molecule). The volume of the surfactant shell per micelle is the number of surfactant molecules per micelle, $N_s$, multiplied by $V_s$:

$$V_{\text{shell}} = N_s \cdot V_s$$

**Step 2 — Surface Balance:**
The same $N_s$ molecules cover the outer surface of the water pool (area $4\pi R_w^{2}$):

$$4\pi R_w^{2} = N_s \cdot A_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_s = \dfrac{4\pi R_w^{2}}{A_s}$$

**Step 3 — Water Volume Inside Micelle:**
If $V_w$ is the volume of a single water molecule (~ $30\ \text{\AA}^3$), the total water volume per micelle is:

$$V_{\text{water,micelle}} = N_s \cdot W_0 \cdot V_w = \dfrac{4\pi R_w^{2} \cdot W_0 \cdot V_w}{A_s}$$

But geometrically, the water pool volume is also $\frac{4}{3}\pi R_w^{3}$. Equating:

$$\dfrac{4}{3}\pi R_w^{3} = \dfrac{4\pi R_w^{2} \cdot W_0 \cdot V_w}{A_s}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification (cancellation of $4\pi R_w^{2}$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{R_w}{3} &= \dfrac{W_0 \cdot V_w}{A_s} \\[4pt]
R_w &= \dfrac{3\, W_0 \cdot V_w}{A_s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Total Micelle Radius:**

$$\boxed{R_m = \dfrac{3\, W_0 \cdot V_w}{A_s} + \ell_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $R_m$ grows linearly with $W_0$. For AOT/iso-octane: $A_s \approx 60\ \text{\AA}^2$, $\ell_s \approx 12\ \text{\AA}$, $V_w \approx 30\ \text{\AA}^3$. So $R_m \approx 1.5\,W_0 + 12$ Å, giving $R_m \approx 37\ \text{\AA}$ at $W_0 = 10$ and $\approx 87\ \text{\AA}$ at $W_0 = 50$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Chrastil Solubility Equation (Heuristic)

**Starting physical picture:** When a solute molecule of molar mass $M_s$ dissolves in an SCF of molar mass $M_f$, it is assumed to associate with $k$ molecules of the SCF to form a "solvato complex" of total molar mass $M_s + k M_f$. The equilibrium is:

$$\text{Solute} + k\,\text{SCF} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Solvato Complex}$$

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Constant:**
At temperature $T$, the equilibrium constant $K$ in terms of concentrations is:

$$K = \dfrac{[\text{Complex}]}{[\text{Solute}]\,[\text{SCF}]^{k}}$$

**Step 2 — Express Concentrations via Density:**
The concentration of a species $i$ in the SCF is $c_i = \dfrac{\rho \cdot w_i}{M_i}$, where $w_i$ is the mass fraction. For the solute (in dilute limit $w_s \ll 1$) and the SCF (bulk, $w_f \approx 1$):

$$[\text{Solute}] \approx \dfrac{\rho\, w_s}{M_s}, \qquad [\text{SCF}] \approx \dfrac{\rho}{M_f}, \qquad [\text{Complex}] \approx \dfrac{\rho\, w_s}{M_s + k M_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Equilibrium Constant:**

$$K = \dfrac{\rho\, w_s \,/\, (M_s + k M_f)}{\left(\rho\, w_s\,/\,M_s\right) \cdot \left(\rho\,/\,M_f\right)^{k}}$$

In the dilute limit, $w_s$ cancels:

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{M_s\, M_f^{k}}{(M_s + k M_f)\,\rho^{k}} \\[4pt]
\ln K &= k \ln \rho + \text{constants (dependent on } M_s, M_f \text{ only)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — van't Hoff Equation for Temperature Dependence:**

$$\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{sol}}}{R\,T} + \text{const} = \dfrac{A}{T} + B$$

**Step 5 — Combine and Solve for Solubility $S = w_s$:**

$$S = \rho^{k}\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{A}{T} + B\right)$$

This is the **Chrastil equation** quoted in the formula sheet.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — SCFE Yield

> **Problem:** A bed of crushed coffee beans (mass $M_b = 100\ \text{kg}$, initial caffeine loading $C_0 = 1.2\ \%\ \text{w/w}$) is extracted with supercritical CO$_2$ at $T = 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $P = 200\ \text{bar}$. The solubility of caffeine in SC-CO$_2$ at these conditions is $S = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg caffeine / kg CO}_2$. The CO$_2$ mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_f = 50\ \text{kg/h}$. Estimate the **extraction time** required to decaffeinate the beans to a residual $0.05\ \%\ \text{w/w}$. Use the simplifying assumption of plug-flow, mass-transfer-controlled extraction (solubility-limited).

**Given:**
- $M_b = 100\ \text{kg}$, $C_0 = 0.012$, $C_f = 0.0005$
- $S = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/kg}$
- $\dot{m}_f = 50\ \text{kg/h}$

**Step 1 — Mass of caffeine to be removed:**

$$\Delta m_{\text{caff}} = M_b(C_0 - C_f) = 100 \times (0.012 - 0.0005) = 1.15\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of CO$_2$ required (at solubility limit):**

$$m_{f,\text{req}} = \dfrac{\Delta m_{\text{caff}}}{S} = \dfrac{1.15}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 460\ \text{kg CO}_2$$

**Step 3 — Time at the given flow rate:**

$$t = \dfrac{m_{f,\text{req}}}{\dot{m}_f} = \dfrac{460}{50} = 9.2\ \text{h}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **9.2 hours** of SC-CO$_2$ extraction at $40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $200\ \text{bar}$ are needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Trick:** If the question states "extraction is rate-limited by solubility," the answer follows this plug-flow, saturation-limited form. If it is "diffusion-limited inside the particle," a different model (Crank's intra-particle diffusion equation) applies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reverse Micelle — Molecular Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORG["ORGANIC PHASE (iso-octane)"]
        TAIL1["Hydrophobic TAIL"]
        TAIL2["Hydrophobic TAIL"]
        TAIL3["Hydrophobic TAIL"]
        TAIL4["Hydrophobic TAIL"]
    end

    subgraph SHELL["SURFACTANT MONOLAYER (AOT)"]
        HEAD1["Anionic HEAD (-SO3- Na+)"]
        HEAD2["Anionic HEAD (-SO3- Na+)"]
        HEAD3["Anionic HEAD (-SO3- Na+)"]
        HEAD4["Anionic HEAD (-SO3- Na+)"]
    end

    subgraph POOL["INNER WATER POOL (W0 molecules of H2O)"]
        PROT["PROTEIN GUEST"]
        WAT1["H2O"]
        WAT2["H2O"]
        WAT3["H2O"]
    end

    TAIL1 --- HEAD1
    TAIL2 --- HEAD2
    TAIL3 --- HEAD3
    TAIL4 --- HEAD4

    HEAD1 --- WAT1
    HEAD2 --- WAT2
    HEAD3 --- WAT3
    HEAD4 --- PROT
```

### 4.2 SCFE Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CO2TANK["CO2 SUPPLY CYLINDER"] --> COND["CONDENSER / COOLER"]
    COND --> PUMP["HIGH-PRESSURE PUMP"]
    PUMP --> HX1["PRE-HEATER"]
    HX1 --> EXT["EXTRACTION VESSEL (Solid Feed, High P, High T)"]
    EXT --> VALVE["EXPANSION VALVE"]
    VALVE --> SEP["SEPARATOR (Low P, Low T)"]
    SEP --> PROD["EXTRACTED PRODUCT COLLECTION"]
    SEP --> RECYCLE["RECYCLE CO2 GAS LINE"]
    RECYCLE --> CO2TANK
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Two-Stage RME Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FORWARD["FORWARD EXTRACTION STAGE"]
        A1["Aqueous Feed (Biomolecules)"] --> A2["Mixer-Settler Unit 1"]
        A3["Reverse Micellar Organic Phase (AOT / iso-octane)"] --> A2
        A2 --> A4["Spent Aqueous Raffinate"]
        A2 --> A5["Loaded Micellar Phase (with solubilized protein)"]
    end

    FORWARD --> BACKEX

    subgraph BACKEX["BACK EXTRACTION (STRIPPING) STAGE"]
        A5 --> B1["Mixer-Settler Unit 2"]
        B2["Fresh Stripping Aqueous Phase (different pH / high I)"] --> B1
        B1 --> B3["Spent Organic Phase (sent to surfactant recovery)"]
        B1 --> B4["Concentrated Aqueous Product Stream"]
    end

    BACKEX --> FINAL

    subgraph FINAL["DOWNSTREAM"]
        B4 --> F1["Ultrafiltration / Polishing"]
        F1 --> F2["Purified Biomolecule Product"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Phase Diagram Indicating the Supercritical Region

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PD["P-T PHASE DIAGRAM (schematic)"]
        SOLID["SOLID REGION (top-left, high P low T)"]
        LIQUID["LIQUID REGION (mid-left, moderate P, low T)"]
        GAS["GAS REGION (low P, high T)"]
        SCF["SUPERCRITICAL FLUID REGION (right of CP, P>Pc and T>Tc)"]
        CP["CRITICAL POINT (Tc, Pc)"]
    end

    SOLID --- CP
    LIQUID --- CP
    GAS --- CP
    SCF --- CP
    SOLID --- LIQUID
    LIQUID --- GAS
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the actual P–T diagram, the liquid–vapor coexistence curve **terminates** at the critical point; the supercritical region is a continuous "no man's land" above and to the right of $T_c$ and $P_c$. The triple point (where solid, liquid, and gas coexist) lies below $T_c$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is a **reverse micelle**, and how does it differ from a **normal micelle**? Mention **two applications** of reverse micellar extraction in biotechnology.

**Model Answer:**

A **reverse micelle** is a nano-sized, thermodynamically stable aggregate of surfactant molecules formed in a **non-polar (organic) solvent**, in which the **hydrophilic head groups point inward** toward a tiny water pool and the **hydrophobic tails point outward** into the organic bulk. In contrast, a **normal micelle** (formed in water) has the heads outward (toward water) and tails inward (away from water).

| Feature | Normal Micelle | Reverse Micelle |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous phase | Water (polar) | Organic (apolar) |
| Head orientation | Outward | Inward |
| Tail orientation | Inward | Outward |
| Core solubilizes | Oils / non-polar solutes | Water + polar biomolecules |

**Two applications:**
1. **Selective extraction and recovery of enzymes** (e.g., α-amylase, lipase) from fermentation broth.
2. **Protein refolding** — denatured proteins can be solubilized in reverse micelles and refolded into their native, biologically active conformation.
3. *(Bonus)* Separation of amino acids and recovery of intracellular products.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** List **three reasons** why **supercritical CO$_2$** is the most widely used supercritical fluid in industrial extraction processes.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Mild critical parameters** — $T_c = 31.1\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $P_c = 73.8\ \text{bar}$ are easy to achieve and gentle enough to extract **heat-sensitive** natural products (essential oils, pharmaceuticals) without thermal degradation.

2. **Safe and environmentally benign** — CO$_2$ is **non-toxic, non-flammable, non-corrosive, and GRAS** (Generally Recognized As Safe). There is **no solvent residue** in the final product, satisfying FDA and food-grade standards.

3. **Tunable selectivity and easy recovery** — Solvating power of SC-CO$_2$ can be finely adjusted by small changes in $P$ and $T$ (via the **Chrastil relation**), and at the end of the process, simply **depressurizing** the system removes the solvent completely, leaving a pure, solvent-free extract.

*(Additional valid point)*: Cheap, abundantly available as a by-product of ammonia, fermentation, and combustion industries.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **forget to state the critical conditions** ($T_c, P_c$) numerically — **1 mark** is reserved for this.
> - In reverse-micelle diagrams, students often draw the **heads pointing outward** like a normal micelle — this is a **2-mark error**. Always remember: in reverse micelles, **heads are IN, tails are OUT** in an organic solvent.
> - For SCFE numericals, students confuse the **solubility-limited** and **diffusion-limited** regimes. Read the problem statement carefully before choosing the model.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a **neat labeled diagram**, describe the structure of a **reverse micelle** formed by **AOT in iso-octane**. Explain how the **W$_0$ parameter** controls the micelle size and the solubilization of proteins. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **forward and backward extraction steps** in reverse-micellar recovery of an enzyme. State the role of **pH, ionic strength, and surfactant type** in determining extraction efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Diagram:** (Refer to § 4.1 — the Mermaid schematic). The answer must include:

1. **Surfactant monolayer** with **SO$_3^-$ (anionic) head groups** facing inward toward the central water pool.
2. **Hydrocarbon tails** of AOT (two ethyl-hexyl chains per molecule) extending into the iso-octane bulk.
3. A central **water pool** of radius $R_w$ containing the protein guest.
4. The water-to-surfactant ratio defined as $W_0 = \dfrac{[\text{H}_2\text{O}]}{[\text{AOT}]}$.

**How $W_0$ controls micelle size:**

Using the Nagarajan-type relation derived in § 3.1:

$$R_m = \dfrac{3 W_0 V_w}{A_s} + \ell_s$$

For AOT/iso-octane ($A_s \approx 60\ \text{\AA}^2$, $V_w \approx 30\ \text{\AA}^3$, $\ell_s \approx 12\ \text{\AA}$):

$$R_m \approx 1.5\,W_0 + 12\ \text{\AA}$$

So as $W_0$ increases from 5 to 50, $R_m$ grows from ~$20\ \text{\AA}$ to ~$87\ \text{\AA}$. Since a typical protein is **$15 - 50\ \text{\AA}$** in hydrodynamic radius, $W_0$ must be tuned to **match the protein size** — too small and the protein cannot enter; too large and selectivity drops.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Forward Extraction (Loading):**
- Aqueous enzyme solution ($\text{pH} = pI - 1$ to $pI - 2$ so the enzyme carries **net positive charge** for anionic AOT micelles) is mixed with the AOT/iso-octane phase.
- Electrostatic attraction between the cationic enzyme and the anionic head groups draws the enzyme into the water pool.
- After settling, the loaded micellar phase separates from the spent aqueous raffinate.

**Backward Extraction (Stripping):**
- A new aqueous phase at $\text{pH} = pI + 1$ to $pI + 2$ (so the enzyme is **anionic** and repelled by AOT) and **high ionic strength** ($0.5 - 1.0\ \text{M NaCl}$) is used.
- The high ionic strength **screens** the electrostatic attraction, and the pH reversal **reverses** the charge-based binding.
- The enzyme is "kicked out" of the micelle and re-enters the new aqueous phase, where it is collected as a concentrated product.

**Role of key parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on Extraction |
|---|---|
| **pH** | Controls protein charge; must be **below $pI$** for AOT systems to achieve electrostatic binding |
| **Ionic strength** | High $I$ **screens** electrostatic attraction; in forward extraction low $I$ is preferred, in back extraction high $I$ forces release |
| **Surfactant type** | Anionic (AOT) attracts cationic proteins at low pH; cationic (e.g., CTAB) attracts anionic proteins at high pH; non-ionic surfactants rely on hydrophobic and steric interactions |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Define a **supercritical fluid**. Sketch a **pressure–temperature (P–T) diagram** for pure CO$_2$ and **clearly mark** the triple point, critical point, solid, liquid, gas, and supercritical regions. State the values of $T_c$ and $P_c$ for CO$_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a **process flow diagram**, describe the **super critical fluid extraction** of essential oils from a spice matrix using SC-CO$_2$. Explain the role of **pressure, temperature, and entrainers (co-solvents)** in tuning the selectivity of the process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** A supercritical fluid is a substance existing at a temperature **and pressure** above its **critical values** ($T > T_c$ and $P > P_c$), where the distinction between liquid and gas phases disappears. It possesses **gas-like diffusivity and viscosity** but **liquid-like density** and solvating power.

**P–T Diagram for CO$_2$:** (Refer to § 4.4 — the Mermaid schematic.) Required elements:

- **Triple point** at $T_t = -56.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $P_t = 5.18\ \text{bar}$ — all three phases coexist.
- **Critical point** at $T_c = 31.1\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $P_c = 73.8\ \text{bar}$ — end of liquid–vapor coexistence curve.
- **Solid region** at low $T$, high $P$ (top-left).
- **Liquid region** at moderate $P$, low $T$ (mid-left).
- **Gas region** at low $P$, high $T$ (bottom-right).
- **Supercritical region** at $T > T_c$ **and** $P > P_c$ (right of the critical point).

> **Marks allocation:** 2 for correct $T_c, P_c$ values; 2 for the diagram; 2 for identifying all regions; 1 for the definition.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Process Flow Diagram:** (Refer to § 4.2 — the Mermaid schematic.) The four key units are:

1. **CO$_2$ supply → condenser → high-pressure pump → pre-heater** — to bring CO$_2$ into the supercritical state.
2. **Extraction vessel** containing the ground spice matrix — the SC-CO$_2$ dissolves the essential oil.
3. **Expansion valve → separator** — depressurization causes the essential oil to precipitate out and be collected.
4. **Recycle loop** — the depressurized CO$_2$ gas is recompressed and returned to the extractor.

**Role of process variables:**

- **Pressure (P):** Increasing $P$ at constant $T$ increases the SCF **density**, which increases **solubility** of the essential oil (Chrastil: $S \propto \rho^k$). Thus, $P$ is the **primary tuning knob for capacity**.
- **Temperature (T):** Increasing $T$ at constant $P$ **decreases density** (reducing $S$) but **increases solute vapor pressure** (raising $S$). The two effects compete, producing an **optimum temperature** and sometimes a **retrograde solubility region**. Temperature is used to fine-tune **selectivity between components**.
- **Entrainers (co-solvents):** A small amount (1–5 mol %) of a polar co-solvent such as **ethanol or methanol** is added to SC-CO$_2$ to enhance the solubility of **polar solutes** (e.g., alkaloids, glycosides). This is called the **"modifier effect"** and is critical when extracting polar natural products with non-polar SC-CO$_2$.

> **Marks allocation:** 2 for the process flow diagram; 2 for explanation of $P$, $T$ role; 2 for entrainer explanation; 1 for overall coherence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must-Know for KTU Module 3:**

- [ ] A **reverse micelle** is a surfactant aggregate in an **organic solvent** with **polar heads IN, non-polar tails OUT** — exactly opposite of a normal micelle.
- [ ] The **W$_0$ parameter** = $\dfrac{[\text{Water}]}{[\text{Surfactant}]}$; it **linearly** controls micelle radius via $R_m = \dfrac{3 W_0 V_w}{A_s} + \ell_s$.
- [ ] **AOT** (sodium bis-2-ethylhexyl sulfosuccinate) in **iso-octane** is the textbook reverse-micelle system.
- [ ] In RME: **Forward extraction** uses low pH + low ionic strength; **back extraction** uses high pH + high ionic strength to reverse the binding.
- [ ] A **supercritical fluid** exists at **$T > T_c$** and **$P > P_c$**; it has hybrid gas–liquid properties.
- [ ] For CO$_2$: **$T_c = 31.1\ ^\circ\text{C}$** and **$P_c = 73.8\ \text{bar}$** — these numbers are **frequently asked** in KTU.
- [ ] SC-CO$_2$ is **non-toxic, non-flammable, GRAS**, leaves **zero solvent residue**, and is the **most widely used SCF**.
- [ ] SCFE process: **Extract → Expand → Separate → Recycle**.
- [ ] The **Chrastil equation** $S = \rho^k \exp(A/T + B)$ is the **key equation** for SCFE solubility calculations.
- [ ] Increasing **pressure** (at constant $T$) **increases** SCF density and solubility; **temperature** has competing effects (vapor-pressure vs density).
- [ ] **Entrainers/co-solvents** (e.g., ethanol) are added to SC-CO$_2$ to extract **polar** solutes.
- [ ] Industrial examples to memorize: **decaffeination of coffee/tea**; **extraction of essential oils, spices, hops**; **recovery of enzymes/proteins** by RME.
- [ ] A **P–T diagram** showing triple point, critical point, and the supercritical region is a **must-draw** question.
- [ ] **Reverse micelle diagram** must show **heads inward** — drawing heads outward is a **common 2-mark deduction**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid-Liquid Extraction (Leaching): Industrial Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Solid-Liquid Extraction (Leaching): Industrial Applications ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Solid-Liquid Extraction**, universally termed **Leaching** in chemical engineering literature, is defined as the selective separation of a soluble component (the **solute** or **extract**) from a solid matrix (the **carrier solid** or **raffinate**) by means of a suitable liquid solvent. The operation involves the intimate contact of a comminuted (crushed/ground) solid feed with a solvent, allowing the solute to dissolve into the solvent phase while the insoluble solid residue is subsequently separated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** "Leaching is a mass transfer operation in which a soluble constituent is separated from a solid mixture by means of a solvent that preferentially dissolves that constituent. The process involves three simultaneous steps: (1) penetration of the solvent into the solid matrix, (2) dissolution of the solute into the solvent, and (3) diffusion of the solute-rich solution out of the solid." **Key Operational Nomenclature (as per Treybal & Seader):** | Term | Symbol | Engineering Meaning | |------|--------|---------------------| | **Solute (A)** | A | Component to be extracted (e.g., oil in soybean, sugar in beet) | | **Solvent (B)** | B | The extracting liquid (e.g., hexane, hot water) | | **Carrier Solid (C)** | C | Insoluble solid matrix holding the solute | | **Overflow** | V | Solvent leaving, rich in solute (extract phase) | | **Underflow** | L | Solvent + solute + insoluble solid leaving a stage | | **Extract** | — | The solute-laden solvent product | | **Raffinate** | — | The depleted solid residue | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **The "Tea Bag" Analogy** — Imagine placing a tea bag (the carrier solid with solute inside) into a cup of hot water (the solvent). Over time, the brown tea compounds (solute) diffuse out of the tea leaves (carrier) and into the water, producing a dark solution (extract). The wet, flavorless leaves left behind are the **raffinate**. Industrial leaching is essentially this process scaled up to thousands of kilograms per hour, with multi-stage countercurrent contact to maximize recovery. **Plain-English Operational Logic:** 1. **Crush** the solid to increase surface area (smaller particles = faster leaching). 2. **Contact** the crushed solid with solvent in a vessel (extractor). 3. **Agitate / Heat** to enhance diffusion and solubility. 4. **Separate** the solid residue from the solute-rich solvent (by filtration, settling, or centrifugation). 5. **Recover** the solute by separating it from the solvent downstream (e.g., evaporation, distillation). ### 1.3 Why Leaching is Industrially Critical - Operates at **moderate temperatures** (often 30–80 °C), lower than distillation energy demands. - **Selectively** separates heat-sensitive materials (pharmaceuticals, natural oils, vitamins). - Handles solids that cannot be separated by distillation or evaporation. - Standard unit operation in **food, pharmaceutical, metallurgical, biochemical, and petrochemical** industries. ### 1.4 Standard Industrial Performance Metrics - **Recovery (η)** = (Mass of solute in extract / Mass of solute in feed) × 100 - **Stage Efficiency (E_m)** = (Actual solute transferred / Equilibrium solute transferred) × 100 - **Residence Time (τ)** — typically **15 min to 4 h** depending on diffusivity of solute in solid. - **Solid-to-Liquid Ratio (S/L)** — usually **1:1 to 1:5** (mass basis) in industry. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of the Leaching Process The leaching phenomenon is governed by a coupled **mass transfer + diffusion** model. The solute molecules must traverse several resistances in series: **Step 1 — Solvent Penetration:** - The bulk solvent diffuses into the porous solid matrix. - Pore diffusion (often modeled by Fick's Law) is rate-limiting for tight cellular structures (e.g., oilseeds, wood chips). **Step 2 — Solute Dissolution:** - The solute dissolves into the penetrating solvent. - This step is usually **fast** if the solute is highly soluble; for sparingly soluble materials, it can be rate-controlling. **Step 3 — Solute Diffusion Outward:** - The dissolved solute diffuses out of the solid through the liquid film and pore network to the bulk. - Controlled by **effective diffusivity (D_eff)**, typically on the order of **10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻⁹ m²/s** in solid matrices. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Theory Point:** The overall leaching rate often follows the shrinking-core model or a pseudo-first-order kinetic expression, $$-r_A = k \cdot C_A \cdot a_s$$ where $a_s$ is the interfacial area per unit volume. For board problems, however, **equilibrium-stage calculations** are prioritized. ### 2.2 Representation of Compositions (Critical for KTU Problems) In leaching, conventional mole fractions are inconvenient because of the insoluble solid. KTU board problems use **mass ratios** exclusively. Let: - $A$ = mass of solute - $B$ = mass of solvent - $C$ = mass of insoluble solid **Mass ratio in the SOLUTION (overflow):** $$y = \frac{\text{mass of solute (A)}}{\text{mass of solvent (B)}}$$ **Mass ratio of SOLUTE to INSOLUBLE SOLID (in underflow):** $$x = \frac{\text{mass of solute (A)}}{\text{mass of insoluble solid (C)}}$$ **Mass ratio of UNDERFLOW SOLUTION (variable that determines the operating line):** $$N = \frac{\text{mass of solvent (B) + mass of solute (A)}}{\text{mass of insoluble solid (C)}} = \frac{L}{C}$$ Where $L$ is the mass of liquid in the underflow and $C$ is the mass of insoluble solid retained. For the **overflow** (no solid), $L \to V$ (pure solution). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **ALL the equations below are examinable in the KTU End Semester Exam (ESE). Memorize the variable meanings and units; mis-interpreting $N$ vs $y$ is the #1 cause of lost marks.** | # | Equation Name | Formula | Units / Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Solution concentration | $y = A/B$ | kg solute / kg solvent (overflow) | | 2 | Solute-to-solid ratio | $x = A/C$ | kg solute / kg insoluble solid (underflow) | | 3 | Underflow liquid-to-solid | $N = L/C = (A+B)/C$ | kg liquid / kg insoluble solid | | 4 | Single-stage mass balance | $V(y_1 - y_2) = L_s(x_1 - x_2)$ | V = solvent, L_s = insoluble solid | | 5 | N-stage countercurrent overall | $V(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L_s(x_N - x_0)$ | Where $y_{N+1}$ = fresh solvent, $x_0$ = feed solid | | 6 | Constant underflow ratio | $N_1 = N_2 = \cdots = N_N = \text{const} = R$ | Valid when solvent retained in solid is fixed | | 7 | Operating line slope (constant N) | Slope = $-L_s / V$ | Linear on $y$ vs $x$ diagram | | 8 | Variable underflow N-y tie-line | $N = f(y)$ from equilibrium / experimental data | Real cases; non-linear operating curve | | 9 | Solute mass fraction (slip) | $X = A / (A+B+C)$ | Total basis | | 10 | Stage efficiency | $E = (y_1 - y_2)/(y_1 - y_2^*)$ | $y_2^*$ = equilibrium value | | 11 | Overflow solute fraction | $Y_A = A/(A+B)$ in extract | Quality of extract | | 12 | Recovery / Yield | $\eta = (\text{Solute out in extract})/(\text{Solute in feed}) \times 100$ | % | | 13 | Total liquid per stage | $L_n = N_n \cdot L_s$ | kg/s | | 14 | Volumetric solvent need | $V = (\text{mass solute extracted})/(y_1 - y_{N+1})$ | m³/h, with density | > [!WARNING] > **PITFALL:** In KTU problems, students confuse $x$ (kg solute / kg solid) with $y$ (kg solute / kg solvent). Always draw a clear block diagram of the stage with **IN** and **OUT** arrows labelled, before writing the equation. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Food Industry:** Soybean oil, palm oil, sunflower oil extraction using **hexane** in Bollman, Hildebrandt, or Rotocel extractors. - **Sugar Industry:** Sucrose from sugar beets using **hot water (70–80 °C)** in a **DDS (Danske Sukkerfabrikker) diffuser**. - **Beverage Industry:** Soluble coffee and tea production using **hot water** as solvent in **countercurrent battery extractors**. - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Alkaloid extraction (morphine, quinine) from plant matrices using dilute acids or alcohols. - **Mineral / Metallurgy:** Copper, gold, and uranium extraction from ores via **heap leaching** with sulfuric acid or cyanide solutions. - **Biotechnology:** Extraction of oils, pigments, and bioactive compounds from microalgae and plant biomass. ### 2.5 Stages of an Industrial Leaching Plant 1. **Solid Preparation:** Crushing, flaking, or pelletizing to $d_p$ = **0.1 – 5 mm**. 2. **Solvent Preparation:** Heating, pH adjustment, additive dosing. 3. **Contacting:** Single stage (Bollman), multistage (Rotocel), or continuous countercurrent (DDS). 4. **Solid-Liquid Separation:** Screen, filter, centrifuge, or settler. 5. **Solvent Recovery:** Distillation, evaporation, membrane separation, or crystallization. 6. **Extract Purification:** Further downstream processing to obtain pure product. ### 2.6 Industrial Application Map (KTU-Favorite Tabular Data) | Industry | Solid Feed | Solute | Solvent | Equipment | T (°C) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Edible Oil | Soybean flakes | Triglycerides | n-Hexane | Rotocel, Bollman | 50 – 60 | | Sugar | Sugar beet cossettes | Sucrose | Hot water | DDS diffuser | 70 – 80 | | Coffee | Roasted, ground beans | Soluble coffee | Hot water | Countercurrent batteries | 80 – 100 | | Tea | Crushed tea leaves | Soluble tea solids | Hot water | Continuous extractor | 70 – 90 | | Pharma | Plant powders (cinchona) | Alkaloids | Dilute acid / ethanol | Soxhlet, agitated tanks | 25 – 60 | | Mining (Au) | Crushed gold ore | Au (trace) | NaCN solution | Heap leach pads | Ambient | | Mining (Cu) | Copper oxide ore | Cu²⁺ | H₂SO₄ (dilute) | Heap / vat leaching | 25 – 50 | | Flavour industry | Vanilla pods | Vanillin | Ethanol/water | Soxhlet-type batch | 40 – 60 | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Hot Topic:** The *Bollman extractor* (bucket-type) and *Rotocel extractor* (rotating compartmented) are **must-know equipment** for Part A 3-mark questions. The **DDS diffuser** is the KTU textbook standard for sugar beet processing. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Master Derivation: Overall Mass Balance for an N-Stage Countercurrent Leaching Cascade **System Description (for derivation):** - A solid feed containing $L_s$ kg of **insoluble solid (C)** and a solute of mass $L_s \cdot x_0$ enters stage 1. - A fresh solvent $V$ kg enters stage $N$ with concentration $y_{N+1}$ (often $y_{N+1} = 0$ for pure solvent). - The cascade operates **countercurrently**: solid moves from stage 1 to stage N, solvent moves from N to 1. - The overflow (extract) leaves stage 1 with concentration $y_1$, mass $V$ kg solvent. - The final underflow leaves stage N with concentration $x_N$ (solute-to-solid ratio). **Step 1 — Define the overall system boundary** enclosing all $N$ stages. **Step 2 — Total material balance across the entire system:** $$\text{Solute in (fresh solvent + feed solid)} = \text{Solute out (extract + final underflow)}$$ $$V \cdot y_{N+1} + L_s \cdot x_0 = V \cdot y_1 + L_s \cdot x_N$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange the equation to isolate the operating terms:** $$V \cdot y_{N+1} - V \cdot y_1 = L_s \cdot x_N - L_s \cdot x_0$$ **Step 4 — Factor out common terms:** $$V(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L_s(x_N - x_0)$$ This is the **canonical KTU overall mass balance equation** for countercurrent leaching. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Note:** If $y_{N+1} = 0$ (pure fresh solvent), the equation simplifies to: $$-V \cdot y_1 = L_s(x_N - x_0) \implies y_1 = \frac{L_s(x_0 - x_N)}{V}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Stage-by-Stage Mass Balance (Operating Line Equation) **Step 1 — Consider stages 1 through $n$ only** (a partial system boundary). **Inputs to this partial envelope:** - Fresh solvent to stages $n+1$ to $N$: $V \cdot y_{N+1}$ - Feed solid to stage 1: $L_s \cdot x_0$ **Outputs from this partial envelope:** - Extract overflow from stage 1: $V \cdot y_1$ - Underflow leaving stage $n$: $L_s \cdot x_n$ **Step 2 — Write the solute balance:** $$V \cdot y_{N+1} + L_s \cdot x_0 = V \cdot y_1 + L_s \cdot x_n$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to express $y_1$ in terms of $x_n$:** $$V \cdot y_1 = V \cdot y_{N+1} + L_s(x_0 - x_n)$$ $$y_1 = y_{N+1} + \frac{L_s}{V}(x_0 - x_n)$$ **Step 4 — Generalize for any stage (since 1 is just a label):** $$\boxed{y_{n+1} = y_{N+1} + \frac{L_s}{V}(x_0 - x_n)}$$ This is the **operating line equation** for countercurrent leaching with **constant underflow** (i.e., $N$ is constant = $R$). **Step 5 — For variable underflow** (the realistic case), the operating "line" becomes a **curve** because $L_n$ changes from stage to stage: $$y_{n+1} = \frac{L_n \cdot x_n - L_s \cdot x_{n+1} + V \cdot y_{N+1}}{V}$$ But this requires graphical solution on a **Ponchon-Savarit-type diagram** for leaching with coordinates $N$ vs $y$ (or $y$ vs $x$ with tie-lines). ### 3.3 Analytical Example: Single-Stage Leaching Calculation (KTU 14-Mark Pattern) **Problem Statement:** A soybean meal containing **20 wt% oil** is to be leached with hexane in a single stage. The meal contains **insoluble solid (C) = 100 kg**, **solute (oil) = 20 kg**, so the **insoluble-solid basis composition** is $x_0 = 20/100 = 0.20$ kg oil/kg solid. We use **V = 80 kg of pure hexane** ($y_2 = 0$). After contacting and filtering, the underflow retains **N = 2.0 kg solution/kg solid** (constant underflow). Find the exit concentrations $y_1$ (extract) and $x_1$ (final underflow). **Step 1 — Apply overall mass balance:** $$V(y_2 - y_1) = L_s(x_1 - x_0)$$ With $y_2 = 0$, $L_s = 100$ kg, $V = 80$ kg, $x_0 = 0.20$: $$80(0 - y_1) = 100(x_1 - 0.20)$$ $$-80 \, y_1 = 100 \, x_1 - 20 \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$ **Step 2 — Use the underflow constraint (constant N):** The total liquid in the underflow at stage 1 = $N \cdot L_s = 2.0 \times 100 = 200$ kg. This liquid contains solute and solvent. The mass ratio $x_1$ relates solute to insoluble solid: $$x_1 = \frac{\text{mass solute in underflow}}{L_s} = \frac{A_1}{100}$$ The solution mass ratio in the **overflow** (extract, $y_1$): $$y_1 = \frac{A_1}{V} = \frac{A_1}{80}$$ **Step 3 — Total solute balance (whole system):** Initial solute = 20 kg. Final: solute in extract = $A_1$, solute in underflow = $100 x_1$. So: $$20 = A_1 + 100 x_1$$ $$20 = 80 y_1 + 100 x_1 \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}$$ **Step 4 — Solve the two equations (A) and (B):** From (B): $100 x_1 = 20 - 80 y_1$ → $x_1 = 0.20 - 0.80 y_1$ Substitute into (A): $$-80 y_1 = 100(0.20 - 0.80 y_1) - 20$$ $$-80 y_1 = 20 - 80 y_1 - 20$$ $$-80 y_1 = -80 y_1 \quad \text{(identity — only one independent equation)}$$ This shows that for a single stage, the **underflow constraint $N$** is needed as a third equation. Using the relationship between $x_1$ and $y_1$ via the underflow: The **solution in the underflow** has the same composition as the **overflow** in equilibrium, hence: $$y_1 = \frac{x_1}{N - x_1} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad x_1 = \frac{N \cdot y_1}{1 + y_1}$$ For $N = 2.0$: $$x_1 = \frac{2.0 \cdot y_1}{1 + y_1}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute into (A):** $$-80 y_1 = 100 \left( \frac{2.0 \, y_1}{1 + y_1} - 0.20 \right)$$ $$-80 y_1 (1 + y_1) = 100 \left( 2.0 \, y_1 - 0.20(1 + y_1) \right)$$ $$-80 y_1 - 80 y_1^2 = 200 \, y_1 - 20 - 20 y_1$$ $$-80 y_1 - 80 y_1^2 = 180 \, y_1 - 20$$ $$-80 y_1^2 - 260 y_1 + 20 = 0$$ $$80 y_1^2 + 260 y_1 - 20 = 0$$ $$4 y_1^2 + 13 y_1 - 1 = 0$$ **Step 6 — Solve the quadratic using the formula** $y_1 = \frac{-13 \pm \sqrt{169 + 16}}{8} = \frac{-13 \pm \sqrt{185}}{8}$: $$\sqrt{185} \approx 13.601$$ $$y_1 = \frac{-13 + 13.601}{8} = \frac{0.601}{8} \approx 0.0751 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane}$$ **Step 7 — Compute $x_1$:** $$x_1 = \frac{2.0 \times 0.0751}{1 + 0.0751} = \frac{0.1502}{1.0751} \approx 0.1397 \text{ kg oil/kg solid}$$ **Step 8 — Verify (solute balance):** Solute in extract: $80 \times 0.0751 = 6.01$ kg Solute in underflow: $100 \times 0.1397 = 13.97$ kg Total out: $6.01 + 13.97 = 19.98 \approx 20$ kg ✓ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examination Insight:** Notice that a **single stage** leaves $13.97$ kg of oil in the underflow (about 70% of original). This is why **countercurrent multistage** extraction is industrially essential — it dramatically increases recovery (typically > 95%). ### 3.4 Analytical Example: Number of Stages via McCabe-Thiele Type Method (Leaching) For a multistage countercurrent system, plot the operating line on a $y$–$x$ diagram with slope $L_s/V$ passing through the point $(x_0, y_1)$ and $(x_N, y_{N+1})$, then **step off** stages between the operating line and the **equilibrium tie-line** (which is actually a curve $y^* = f(x, N)$ in the variable-underflow case). > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Hint:** In a 14-mark question, the canonical structure is: (a) Draw the flow diagram, (b) Derive the overall balance, (c) Compute the operating line, (d) Step off the stages graphically and (e) Verify with one algebraic stage balance. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Industrial Leaching Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Solid Feed
Crushed Ore or Flakes] --> B[Solid Preparation
Crushing / Flaking / Drying] B --> C[Stage 1 Extractor
Countercurrent Cascade] C --> D[Stage 2 Extractor
Countercurrent Cascade] D --> E[Stage N Extractor
Countercurrent Cascade] E --> F[Solid Liquid Separator
Filter or Centrifuge] F --> G[Raffinate
Spent Solid Out] S[Fresh Solvent Tank
Hexane or Hot Water] --> E C --> H[Extract Overflow
Solute Rich Solution] H --> I[Solvent Recovery Unit
Distillation Evaporation] I --> J[Pure Solute Product
Oil Sugar Alkaloid] I --> S style A fill:#fde2e4 style B fill:#cddafd style C fill:#b8e0d2 style D fill:#b8e0d2 style E fill:#b8e0d2 style F fill:#ffd6a5 style G fill:#d3d3d3 style S fill:#fef3c7 style H fill:#fcd5ce style I fill:#d8e2dc style J fill:#e2f0cb ``` ### 4.2 Schematic: Single-Stage Leaching Mass Balance ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph stage1["STAGE n Leaching Vessel"] direction TB IN1["Fresh Solvent V
Concentration y subscript N plus 1"] --> mix["Mixing Zone
Agitation and Contact"] IN2["Solid Feed Ls
Concentration x subscript 0"] --> mix mix --> out1["Overflow Extract V
Concentration y subscript 1"] mix --> out2["Underflow Ln
Solvent plus Solute plus Solid
Concentration x subscript n"] end style IN1 fill:#cce5ff style IN2 fill:#ffd9b3 style mix fill:#d4edda style out1 fill:#fff3cd style out2 fill:#f8d7da style stage1 fill:#fafafa ``` ### 4.3 Process Logic: N-Stage Countercurrent Cascade ```mermaid flowchart LR V1["Solvent V
y subscript N plus 1"] --> N1["Stage N"] N1 --> N2["Stage N minus 1"] N2 --> N3["Stage N minus 2"] N3 --> N4["Stage ... "] N4 --> N5["Stage 2"] N5 --> N6["Stage 1
Overflow y subscript 1 EXTRACT"] S1["Solid Ls
x subscript 0"] --> N6 N6 --> S2["Underflow x subscript 1"] S2 --> N5 N5 --> S3["Underflow x subscript 2"] S3 --> N4 N4 --> S4["Underflow ... "] S4 --> N3 N3 --> S5["Underflow ... "] S5 --> N2 N2 --> S6["Underflow x subscript N
RAFFINATE"] style V1 fill:#cfe2f3 style N1 fill:#d9ead3 style N2 fill:#d9ead3 style N3 fill:#d9ead3 style N4 fill:#d9ead3 style N5 fill:#d9ead3 style N6 fill:#fff2cc style S1 fill:#fce5cd style S2 fill:#fce5cd style S3 fill:#fce5cd style S4 fill:#fce5cd style S5 fill:#fce5cd style S6 fill:#f4cccc ``` ### 4.4 Industrial Equipment Family Map ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Industrial Leaching Equipment] P1 --> Q1[Batch Type] P1 --> Q2[Continuous Type] Q1 --> R1[Stirred Tank Reactor] Q1 --> R2[Soxhlet Extractor] Q1 --> R3[Percolation Tanks] Q2 --> S1[Fixed Bed Percolators] Q2 --> S2[Moving Bed Extractors] Q2 --> S3[Agitated Slurry Extractors] S2 --> T1[Bollman Extractor
Bucket Conveyor] S2 --> T2[Hildebrandt Extractor
Screw Conveyor] S2 --> T3[Rotocel Extractor
Rotating Compartments] S2 --> T4[DDS Diffuser
Sugar Beet Standard] S3 --> U1[Bonnet Reactor] S3 --> U2[De Smet Belt Extractor] style P1 fill:#e8e8e8 style Q1 fill:#cfe2f3 style Q2 fill:#cfe2f3 style R1 fill:#d9d2e9 style R2 fill:#d9d2e9 style R3 fill:#d9d2e9 style S1 fill:#d9ead3 style S2 fill:#d9ead3 style S3 fill:#d9ead3 style T1 fill:#fff2cc style T2 fill:#fff2cc style T3 fill:#fff2cc style T4 fill:#fff2cc style U1 fill:#fce5cd style U2 fill:#fce5cd ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Field Tip:** In KTU exam questions, when given an industrial scenario (e.g., sugar beet extraction), identify the equipment from the family map above and quote the **typical operating temperature, solvent, and contact time** as part of your 14-mark answer. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **Define leaching. List any four industrial applications with the solvent and solute used in each.** **Model Answer:** Leaching (solid-liquid extraction) is a separation process in which a soluble component (solute) is selectively dissolved from a solid mixture using a suitable liquid solvent, leaving behind an insoluble solid residue called raffinate. **Four industrial applications:** | Industry | Solid Feed (Carrier) | Solute | Solvent | |----------|---------------------|--------|---------| | Edible oil | Soybean flakes | Soybean oil (triglycerides) | n-Hexane | | Sugar | Sugar beet cossettes | Sucrose | Hot water (70–80 °C) | | Coffee | Roasted, ground beans | Soluble coffee solids | Hot water | | Mining (Au) | Crushed gold ore | Gold (Au) | Dilute NaCN solution | --- #### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **Explain the terms: (i) Underflow, (ii) Overflow, (iii) Extract, and (iv) Raffinate as used in leaching operations.** **Model Answer:** - **(i) Underflow:** The stream leaving a leaching stage that contains the **insoluble solid + entrained solution** (solvent + dissolved solute). It is denoted by the symbol $L$ with concentration expressed as $N = $ (kg solution / kg insoluble solid) or $x = $ (kg solute / kg solid). - **(ii) Overflow:** The clarified solution stream leaving a stage that is **free of insoluble solid**; it is the solvent containing dissolved solute. Denoted $V$, with concentration $y$ (kg solute / kg solvent). - **(iii) Extract:** The final overflow product from the **last stage** (typically the solvent-rich stream leaving in contact with fresh solid), rich in the desired solute. It is sent to solvent recovery. - **(iv) Raffinate:** The final underflow product from the **last stage of the solid train** (the spent solid + entrained solution), depleted of the solute. The insoluble solid is the dominant component. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice) #### **Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the overall mass balance equation for a countercurrent multistage leaching cascade operating with constant underflow. Define each term clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A countercurrent leaching cascade is to be designed to recover oil from soybean flakes. The feed contains **0.20 kg oil/kg insoluble solid** ($x_0 = 0.20$). The fresh solvent is pure hexane ($y_{N+1} = 0$). The underflow has a constant $N = 1.5$ kg solution/kg solid. Required exit underflow: $x_N = 0.04$ kg oil/kg solid. The solvent-to-solid flow ratio is $V/L_s = 4.0$ (kg/kg). Determine: - (i) The exit extract concentration $y_1$ **(3 marks)** - (ii) The number of ideal stages required (step off graphically or algebraically) **(4 marks)** --- **Model Solution to B-A:** **(a) Derivation:** [Follow the derivation in Section 3.1] - Define the system boundary around all $N$ stages. - Solute in (fresh solvent + feed) = Solute out (extract + final underflow) - $V \cdot y_{N+1} + L_s \cdot x_0 = V \cdot y_1 + L_s \cdot x_N$ - Rearranging: $V(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L_s(x_N - x_0)$ **Definitions:** - $V$ = mass flow rate of solvent (kg/h) - $L_s$ = mass flow rate of insoluble solid (kg/h) - $y_{N+1}$ = solute mass ratio in fresh solvent entering stage $N$ (kg solute / kg solvent) - $y_1$ = solute mass ratio in extract leaving stage 1 (kg solute / kg solvent) - $x_0$ = solute mass ratio in feed solid (kg solute / kg insoluble solid) - $x_N$ = solute mass ratio in final underflow leaving stage $N$ (kg solute / kg insoluble solid) **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $x_0 = 0.20$, $y_{N+1} = 0$, $N = 1.5$, $x_N = 0.04$, $V/L_s = 4.0$. **(i) Exit extract concentration $y_1$:** Apply the overall mass balance: $$V(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L_s(x_N - x_0)$$ $$V(0 - y_1) = L_s(0.04 - 0.20)$$ $$-V \cdot y_1 = L_s \cdot (-0.16)$$ $$y_1 = 0.16 \cdot \frac{L_s}{V} = \frac{0.16}{4.0} = 0.04 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane}$$ **(ii) Number of stages:** For **constant underflow**, the operating line on a $y$–$x$ diagram is **straight** with slope $L_s / V = 1/4 = 0.25$, passing through the points $(x_0, y_1) = (0.20, 0.04)$ and $(x_N, y_{N+1}) = (0.04, 0)$. The operating line equation is: $$y_{n+1} = y_1 - \frac{L_s}{V}(x_n - x_0) = 0.04 - 0.25(x_n - 0.20)$$ For a single ideal stage, equilibrium: $y_n = x_n / (N - x_n) = x_n / (1.5 - x_n)$ (from the underflow relation). **Stage 1:** Start at $x_0 = 0.20$. Equilibrium: $y_1 = 0.20 / (1.5 - 0.20) = 0.20 / 1.30 = 0.1538$ kg/kg. Operating line gives next $x_1$: $y_2 = 0.1538 = 0.04 - 0.25(x_1 - 0.20)$ $0.1538 - 0.04 = -0.25(x_1 - 0.20)$ $0.1138 = -0.25 x_1 + 0.05$ $-0.25 x_1 = 0.0638$ $x_1 = -0.255$ ❌ (negative — impossible) This indicates that with these parameters, a single stage is sufficient. To confirm, check if the desired $y_1 = 0.04$ corresponds to an $x_N \le 0.04$ via the operating line ending at $(0.04, 0)$. Going backward: $y_1 = 0.04$ at $x_0 = 0.20$ → step to equilibrium gives $x_1 = N \cdot y_1 / (1 + y_1) = 1.5 \times 0.04 / 1.04 = 0.0577$ kg/kg. Since $x_1 = 0.0577 > x_N = 0.04$, we need a second stage. **Stage 2:** From $x_1 = 0.0577$, equilibrium: $y_2 = 0.0577 / (1.5 - 0.0577) = 0.0577 / 1.4423 = 0.0400$ kg/kg. Operating line: $y_3 = 0.04 - 0.25(0.0577 - 0.20) = 0.04 + 0.0356 = 0.0756$. Equilibrium: $x_2 = 1.5 \times 0.0756 / 1.0756 = 0.1054$ kg/kg. But $x_N = 0.04 < x_2$, so we have overshot — meaning **2 ideal stages suffice**. **Final Answer:** $\boxed{y_1 = 0.04 \text{ kg oil/kg hexane, Number of ideal stages} = 2}$ --- #### **Question B-B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Rotocel extractor**. List any **three advantages** and **two limitations** of this equipment. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A solid feed containing **25 wt% oil and 75 wt% insoluble solid** is to be leached with benzene to reduce the oil content to **5 wt% of the insoluble solid** in a single-stage operation. The underflow retains **1.8 kg solution/kg solid**. The solvent feed is pure benzene. Calculate: - (i) The amount of benzene required per 1000 kg of feed. **(3 marks)** - (ii) The concentration of the extract. **(4 marks)** --- **Model Solution to B-B:** **(a) Rotocel Extractor — Working Principle:** The **Rotocel extractor** is a continuous, rotating, compartmented extractor widely used for oilseed processing. It consists of: - A **rotating horizontal cylindrical vessel** divided into wedge-shaped compartments (cells) by radial partitions. - A **perforated screen** at the bottom of each compartment that supports the solid bed and allows solvent to percolate. - A **stationary solvent distributor** and **solvent collection system** above the vessel. - Each compartment sequentially passes through (a) a **filling zone** (fresh solid loaded), (b) a **leaching zone** (solvent contact), and (c) a **discharge zone** (raffinate removed). **Working:** The solid is fed at the top and slowly rotates with the vessel, while solvent is sprayed in the opposite direction (countercurrent). The compartments are filled with solid and progressively contact stronger solvent as they rotate. **Three Advantages:** 1. Continuous operation → high throughput (up to **5000 tonnes/day** for soybean). 2. Excellent countercurrent contact → high extraction efficiency (>$>$ 95% recovery). 3. Gentle mechanical action → minimal damage to delicate solids (e.g., flakes, pellets). **Two Limitations:** 1. High capital cost and complex mechanical design. 2. Sensitive to variations in solid bulk density and particle size distribution. **(b) Numerical Solution:** Basis: **1000 kg of feed**. - Oil (solute A) = $0.25 \times 1000 = 250$ kg - Insoluble solid (C) = $0.75 \times 1000 = 750$ kg - $x_0 = 250 / 750 = 0.3333$ kg oil/kg solid - $x_1$ (final) = 0.05 kg oil/kg solid - $N = 1.8$ kg solution/kg solid (constant) - $y_2 = 0$ (pure benzene) **(i) Benzene required:** Apply overall mass balance: $$V(y_2 - y_1) = L_s(x_1 - x_0)$$ $$V(0 - y_1) = 750(0.05 - 0.3333)$$ $$-V \cdot y_1 = 750 \times (-0.2833) = -212.5$$ $$V \cdot y_1 = 212.5 \text{ kg oil extracted} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ The extract concentration $y_1$ relates to $x_1$ through the underflow equilibrium (since the solution in the underflow has the same composition as the overflow at equilibrium): $$y_1 = \frac{x_1}{N - x_1} = \frac{0.05}{1.8 - 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{1.75} = 0.02857 \text{ kg oil/kg benzene}$$ Substitute into (1): $$V = \frac{212.5}{0.02857} = 7437.5 \text{ kg benzene}$$ **Benzene required per 1000 kg feed ≈ 7437.5 kg** (or equivalently, **solvent-to-feed ratio = 7.44 kg/kg**) **(ii) Extract concentration:** $y_1 = 0.02857$ kg oil/kg benzene (≈ **2.78 wt%** in the extract) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often use mass fractions directly (e.g., 0.05) instead of converting to **mass ratios** (kg solute / kg solid). This causes unit inconsistency and marks are lost. *Always express compositions as $x$ (kg A / kg C) or $y$ (kg A / kg B) before writing any balance.* > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **underflow relation** $y_1 = x_1 / (N - x_1)$ to close the system of equations. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing $L_s$ (insoluble solid) with $L$ (total underflow). In KTU notation, $L_s$ is the constant insoluble-solid flow; $L_n = N_n \cdot L_s$ is the variable total underflow. > - **Common Mistake 4:** In multistage graphical problems, students fail to draw a **clean y-x diagram** with the operating line and equilibrium curve, losing 3–4 marks of structured presentation. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - **Leaching = solid-liquid extraction.** Solute is removed from solid by a solvent. Industrial examples: oil from seeds (hexane), sucrose from beets (hot water), coffee from beans (hot water), gold from ore (NaCN), copper from oxide ore (H₂SO₄). - **Three master streams:** Overflow $V$ (kg solvent/s), Underflow $L$ (kg liquid + solid/s), Insoluble solid $L_s$ (kg solid/s). Use **constant $L_s$**, not total mass. - **Composition variables (must memorize):** - $x = $ kg solute / kg insoluble solid (underflow basis) - $y = $ kg solute / kg solvent (overflow basis) - $N = $ kg solution / kg insoluble solid (underflow liquid-to-solid ratio) - **Master overall balance:** $V(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L_s(x_N - x_0)$ — apply to **whole cascade** or any partial envelope. - **Single-stage closure:** Use $y_1 = x_1/(N - x_1)$ (constant $N$ assumption). - **Operating line slope:** $L_s / V$ (constant underflow case). - **KTU-favorite equipment names:** Bollman, Hildebrandt, Rotocel, DDS diffuser, Soxhlet, Pachuca tank, Bonotto extractor. - **KTU-favorite solvents:** n-Hexane (oils), hot water (sugar, coffee, tea), ethanol (alkaloids), dilute H₂SO₄ (copper ore), NaCN (gold ore). - **Single-stage recovery is low (~30–60%); multi-stage countercurrent achieves >95%.** Always prefer countercurrent cascade in design. - **Constant vs variable underflow:** - *Constant* $N$ → linear operating line on $y$–$x$ plot. - *Variable* $N$ → curved operating line; use **$N$ vs $y$ Ponchon-Savarit** method with tie-lines. - **The 3 industrial temperature ranges to remember:** oils at **50–60 °C**, sugar at **70–80 °C**, coffee at **80–100 °C**. - **Solid preparation is critical:** particle size 0.1–5 mm; smaller = faster, but excessive fines clog percolation. - **Always state** recovery $\eta$ = (solute out in extract / solute in feed) × 100 in your final answer. - **Common pitfall:** $y$ and $x$ are **mass ratios**, NOT mass fractions. Never write $x = 0.05$ when the problem says 5 wt% (the correct expression is $x = 0.05/0.95$ in some cases — convert carefully). > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 3 Notes — Solid-Liquid Extraction (Leaching): Industrial Applications. Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4).**

topics.title: Heap and In-situ Leaching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heap and In-situ Leaching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heap Leaching

**Heap Leaching** is a metallurgical extraction process in which **crushed and agglomerated low-grade ore** is stacked on an engineered, impermeable pad (lined with HDPE, PVC, or compacted clay) and **irrigated from the top with a chemical lixiviant** (leaching solution). The solution percolates downward through the heap by gravity, dissolves the target valuable metal, and the **pregnant leach solution (PLS)** is collected at the base. The PLS is then sent to a downstream recovery circuit — typically **Solvent Extraction / Electrowinning (SX/EW)** for copper, **activated carbon adsorption (CIC/CIL)** for gold and silver, or **precipitation** for uranium.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Heap leaching is a *batch* or *pseudo-continuous* solid–liquid mass transfer operation in which the solute (metal value) is transferred from a stationary packed bed of porous ore particles to a flowing lixiviant under ambient temperature and pressure.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Giant Coffee Filter ☕

Imagine a giant coffee filter, but instead of coffee grounds, you have **crushed ore**, and instead of hot water, you pour a **dilute acid or cyanide solution** from the top. As the lixiviant drips down through the porous ore pile, it dissolves the metal salts just as hot water extracts coffee flavor. The enriched solution drips out at the bottom — that is your **pregnant leach solution (PLS)**. The ore (called **leached residue or "spent heap"**) is rinsed, neutralized, and either removed or left as a stable dump.

**Key Industrial Examples:**
- **Copper oxide/sulfide ores:** Dilute H₂SO₄ (5–50 g/L) → PLS → SX/EW → Cathode copper
- **Gold and silver ores:** Dilute NaCN (0.05–0.5 g/L NaCN, pH 10–11) → PLS → Activated carbon → Smelting
- **Uranium ores:** Dilute H₂SO₄ or alkaline carbonate solution → PLS → IX/SX → Yellowcake

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Heap leaching falls under *industrial leaching equipment and operations*. Students must know the **process flow**, **design parameters** (heap height, irrigation rate, particle size, cycle time), and **mass balance** calculations.

---

## 1.2 In-Situ Leaching (ISL / ISR)

**In-Situ Leaching (ISL)**, also called **In-Situ Recovery (ISR)** or **Solution Mining**, is a process in which the lixiviant is **injected directly into an undisturbed, naturally permeable ore body** (typically a confined aquifer or fractured rock formation) through a pattern of **injection wells**. The solution flows through the porous ore matrix, dissolves the target mineral, and is pumped back to the surface through **recovery (production) wells**. The pregnant solution is then processed in a surface plant to recover the metal, and the **barren (stripped) solution is re-injected** to continue the cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** In-situ leaching is a *closed-loop* solid–liquid mass transfer operation in which leaching occurs within the ore body itself, with no prior mining, crushing, or surface stacking. The governing mass transfer mechanisms are **advection, dispersion, and diffusion through the natural porosity** of the host rock.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Underground Sponge 🧽

Picture a **sponge buried deep underground**, soaked with mineral-rich brine. Instead of digging up the sponge, you **inject clean water (lixiviant) from one side and suck out the dissolved juice (PLS) from the other side** using a system of wells. The sponge gradually gets "rinsed" of its valuable contents underground. This is precisely what happens in ISL — the ore body acts as the natural reactor vessel.

**Key Industrial Examples:**
- **Uranium (Sandstone-hosted):** Wyoming, Kazakhstan — Alkaline (NH₄)₂CO₃ or acidic H₂SO₄ → PLS → IX → Yellowcake
- **Copper (Oxide, fractured rock):** Arizona (Florence copper), USA — H₂SO₄ → PLS → SX/EW
- **Potash (Sylvite/Kainite):** Saskatchewan, Canada — NaCl brine → Solution → Crystallization
- **Lithium (Brines):** Chile (Salar de Atacama) — Fresh water → Brine → Li₂CO₃
- **Trona (Na₂CO₃·NaHCO₃·2H₂O):** Wyoming, USA — Hot water → Solution → Evaporation
- **Native sulfur (Frasch process):** Superheated water + H₂S displacing agent (related but uses molten sulfur)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** In-situ leaching is grouped under *advanced industrial leaching techniques*. Students must understand the **geological prerequisites** (permeability, confinement, mineralogy), **well field design** (5-spot, 7-spot, line drive), **environmental safeguards** (groundwater monitoring, restoration), and **Darcy's law** for flow rate calculations.

---

## 1.3 Side-by-Side Comparison

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners *love* asking the difference between heap and in-situ leaching in 3-mark or 14-mark questions. Memorize the table below.

| Feature | Heap Leaching | In-Situ Leaching |
|---|---|---|
| Mining required | Yes (crushing, stacking) | No |
| Ore body handling | Excavated & placed on pad | Remains in place |
| Reactor vessel | Engineered heap on pad | Natural geological formation |
| Lixiviant introduction | Top irrigation | Injection wells |
| Typical ore | Low-grade oxide/sulfide | Permeable host rock |
| Capital cost | Moderate | Low (no crushing) |
| Operating cost | Moderate–High | Low |
| Environmental footprint | Moderate (large pads) | Low surface, high subsurface risk |
| Cycle time | 30–300 days | Months to years |
| Recovery | 50–90% | 60–85% |
| Examples | Cu, Au, Ag, U | U, Cu (fractured), Potash, Li |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heap Leaching — Process Flow and Operational Logic

The heap leaching operation proceeds through the following sequential steps:

1. **Ore Preparation:** Run-of-mine (ROM) ore is crushed (and sometimes agglomerated with cement or acid) to a target top size — typically P₈₀ of **6–25 mm**. Agglomeration improves permeability and prevents fines from sealing the heap.
2. **Heap Construction (Stacking):** The ore is conveyed and stacked in **lifts** (2–10 m each) using trucks, conveyors, or radial stackers. A typical pad has a slope of 1–3% and is underlain with a **double liner system** (geomembrane + leak detection).
3. **Irrigation:** The lixiviant is distributed over the heap surface through **drip emitters, wobblers, or sprinklers** at a controlled rate. Typical **irrigation rate (q)** ranges from **5 to 20 L/m²·hr** (≈ 0.005 to 0.020 m³/m²·hr).
4. **Percolation and Dissolution:** The lixiviant trickles through the heap by gravity. The target mineral dissolves via chemical reaction (acid-base, complexation, or redox) and the metal ions enter the liquid phase. This is the core **mass transfer step**.
5. **PLS Collection:** The pregnant leach solution drains through the heap base into a **collection ditch and sump**, from which it is pumped to the recovery plant.
6. **Metal Recovery:** The PLS is processed by SX/EW (Cu), carbon columns (Au, Ag), or ion exchange (U). The **barren (stripped) solution** is re-fortified with reagents and recycled to the heap.
7. **Rinsing and Closure:** After the target cycle, the heap is rinsed with fresh water to recover residual dissolved metal. The spent residue is then neutralized, capped, and revegetated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why each step matters (KTU 2024 OBE Context — CO Mapping):**
> - Steps 1–2 directly relate to **CO3: Design considerations for solid-fluid contactors**.
> - Step 4 maps to **CO2: Mass transfer mechanisms (diffusion-controlled vs reaction-controlled leaching kinetics)**.
> - Step 6 connects to **CO4: Industrial recovery circuits**.

### 2.1.1 The Shrunken Core Model (SCM) — Why Heap Leaching is Slow

Heap leaching kinetics is best described by the **Shrinking (Unreacted) Core Model**, in which the reacting ore particle is treated as a sphere with a **fully reacted outer "ash" layer** and a **shrinking unreacted core**. Mass transfer proceeds by:

- **External film diffusion** of lixiviant from bulk to particle surface
- **Diffusion through the ash/product layer**
- **Chemical reaction** at the interface with the unreacted core

For diffusion control through the ash layer (the typical rate-limiting regime in heap leaching):

$$1 - (1 - X)^{1/3} = \frac{2 \cdot D_{eff} \cdot C_b}{\rho_s \cdot R^2} \cdot t$$

For chemical reaction control at the unreacted core surface:

$$1 - (1 - X)^{1/3} = \frac{k_s \cdot C_b}{\rho_s \cdot R} \cdot t$$

**Where:**
- $X$ = fractional conversion (0 to 1)
- $D_{eff}$ = effective diffusivity in the ash layer (m²/s)
- $C_b$ = bulk lixiviant concentration (kg/m³)
- $\rho_s$ = molar density of solid reactant (kg/m³ of solid)
- $R$ = initial particle radius (m)
- $k_s$ = first-order surface reaction rate constant (m/s)
- $t$ = time (s)

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Notice that the ash-diffusion equation is **inversely proportional to R²**, while reaction-control is **inversely proportional to R**. This explains why heap leach recovery drops sharply with larger particle size — diffusion inside the particle becomes the bottleneck.

### 2.1.2 Heap Leach Design Parameters — The "Big Five"

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Recovery | Effect on Capital Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heap height (total) | 4–100 m (multi-lift) | Higher → slower recovery | Higher → lower capex/m² |
| Particle size (P₈₀) | 6–25 mm | Smaller → faster recovery | Smaller → more crushing cost |
| Irrigation rate | 5–20 L/m²·hr | Higher → faster (up to flooding) | Higher → more pumping cost |
| Lixiviant concentration | Acid: 5–50 g/L; CN: 0.05–0.5 g/L | Higher → faster but more reagent cost | Marginal |
| Leach cycle time | 30–300 days | Longer → more recovery | Longer → working capital tied up |

---

## 2.2 In-Situ Leaching — Process Flow and Operational Logic

ISL proceeds through these steps:

1. **Geological Screening:** The ore body must satisfy three prerequisites:
   - **Permeability** (k) ≥ 0.1–1 Darcy (so lixiviant can flow)
   - **Confined aquifer** (impermeable strata above and below prevent contamination)
   - **Mineralogy amenable to dissolution** (oxide minerals, soluble salts)
2. **Well Field Drilling:** A pattern of wells (e.g., **5-spot, 7-spot, 9-spot, or line-drive**) is drilled using rotary or reverse-circulation methods. Typical well depths: 50–500 m; well diameter: 15–30 cm; casing: PVC or stainless steel.
3. **Well Completion and Development:** Wells are cased, screens are placed across the ore zone, and each well is **developed** by pumping/surging to remove fines and stabilize the formation.
4. **Injection and Recovery:** Lixiviant is pumped down the **injection wells** under controlled pressure. The solution flows radially through the ore body and is recovered through **production (recovery) wells** located 15–50 m away.
5. **Surface Recovery:** The pregnant solution is processed (IX, SX/EW, evaporation, crystallization) to recover the metal.
6. **Recycle and Restoration:** The barren solution is fortified and re-injected. After mining, the formation is **restored** by flushing with clean water (or reducing agents) to remove residual lixiviant and bring groundwater to baseline quality.

### 2.2.1 Darcy's Law — The Backbone of ISL Flow Modeling

The flow rate of lixiviant through a porous formation is governed by **Darcy's Law**. For radial flow toward (or from) a single well:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi \cdot k \cdot h \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \ln(r_e / r_w)}$$

**Where:**
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate per well (m³/s)
- $k$ = permeability of the formation (m²) — 1 Darcy = 9.87 × 10⁻¹³ m²
- $h$ = thickness of the ore zone (m)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference between injection and recovery wells (Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the lixiviant (Pa·s) — water at 25°C ≈ 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s
- $r_e$ = radius of influence of the well (m)
- $r_w$ = well radius (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This equation is the *single most important formula* in ISL design. It is used to size pumps, determine well spacing, and predict sweep efficiency. KTU examiners frequently test it in 14-mark numerical problems.

### 2.2.2 Well Spacing and Pattern

For a **5-spot pattern** (1 injection well surrounded by 4 production wells, or vice versa), the relationship between well spacing and radius of influence is:

$$S = r_e \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

where $S$ is the distance between adjacent wells. The **total area covered per pattern** is $S^2$ m².

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD** — The table below consolidates every formula you are likely to need for a 14-mark numerical on Heap or In-situ Leaching. Memorize the units!

| # | Formula | Application | Key Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R\% = \frac{m_{metal,\,PLS}}{m_{metal,\,ore}} \times 100$ | Overall recovery % | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M_{Cu} = V_{PLS} \times C_{PLS}$ | Mass of metal extracted | kg |
| 3 | $q = Q / A_{heap}$ | Irrigation rate (heap) | L/m²·hr or m³/m²·hr |
| 4 | $1 - (1 - X)^{1/3} = \frac{2 D_{eff} C_b}{\rho_s R^2} t$ | Shrinking core, diffusion control | X dimensionless, t in s |
| 5 | $1 - (1 - X)^{1/3} = \frac{k_s C_b}{\rho_s R} t$ | Shrinking core, reaction control | X dimensionless, t in s |
| 6 | $Q = \frac{2\pi k h \Delta P}{\mu \ln(r_e / r_w)}$ | Darcy radial flow (ISL) | m³/s |
| 7 | $S = r_e \sqrt{2}$ | 5-spot well spacing | m |
| 8 | $\tau = \frac{V_{heap} \cdot \epsilon}{Q_{lixiviant}}$ | Lixiviant residence time in heap | hr or s |
| 9 | $A_{cons} = m_{Cu} \times \alpha$ | Reagent consumption (α = kg acid/kg Cu) | kg |
| 10 | $E_{sweep} = \frac{V_{ore\,contacted}}{V_{ore\,total}} \times 100$ | ISL sweep efficiency | % |
| 11 | $k_{D} = \frac{2\pi k h}{\mu \ln(r_e/r_w)}$ | Productivity index per well | m³/(Pa·s) |
| 12 | $M_{ore} = V_{heap} \times (1 - \epsilon) \times \rho_{ore}$ | Ore mass in heap | tonnes |

Where:
- $\epsilon$ = heap porosity (typically 0.30–0.40)
- $\rho_{ore}$ = ore particle density (typically 2.2–2.8 t/m³)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick Question (2023 Pattern):** Students often confuse the *Darcy radial flow* equation with the *Dupuit–Thiem equation for a fully-penetrating well in a confined aquifer*. In ISL exam problems, when the well *fully penetrates* the ore zone, the radial form shown above is what you need — do not mistakenly use a confined-aquifer specific storage coefficient.
