The McCabe–Smith method (also called the *lever-arm rule* or *Ponchon–Savarit method* for leaching) is the KTU-preferred approach for counter-current leaching calculations.

**Given**:
- $L_0$ = mass flow rate of fresh solid feed (kg/h)
- $V_{N+1}$ = mass flow rate of fresh solvent (kg/h)
- $x_0$ = solute concentration in fresh feed (kg solute/kg insoluble solid)
- $x_N$ = solute concentration in final underflow (kg solute/kg insoluble solid)
- $y_{N+1}$ = solute concentration in fresh solvent (kg solute/kg solvent)
- $N$ = number of ideal stages

**Derivation Steps**:

**Step 1 — Overall material balance (no reaction)**:
$$L_0 + V_{N+1} = L_N + V_1$$

This is the total mass balance. The feed (solid + solute) plus fresh solvent equals the underflow product plus the overflow product.

**Step 2 — Overall solute balance**:
$$L_0 x_0 + V_{N+1} y_{N+1} = L_N x_N + V_1 y_1$$

The total solute entering (from feed and solvent) equals the total solute leaving (in underflow and overflow).

**Step 3 — Operating line equation for stage n**:
A solute balance around stages 1 through n (where n is any intermediate stage):
$$L_0 x_0 + V_{n+1} y_{n+1} = L_n x_n + V_1 y_1$$

Rearranging to express $y_{n+1}$ in terms of $x_n$:
$$V_{n+1} y_{n+1} = L_n x_n + V_1 y_1 - L_0 x_0$$
$$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L_n}{V_{n+1}} x_n + \dfrac{V_1 y_1 - L_0 x_0}{V_{n+1}}$$

This is the operating line. On a y–x diagram, it appears as a straight line connecting the points $(x_0, y_1)$ and $(x_N, y_{N+1})$.

**Step 4 — When solvent-to-solid ratio is constant (L/V = constant = R)**:
If the underflow rate $L_n$ and overflow rate $V_{n+1}$ are constant (i.e., $L_n = L_0 = L$ and $V_{n+1} = V_{N+1} = V$):
$$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V} x_n + \left(y_1 - \dfrac{L}{V} x_0\right)$$

**Step 5 — Stage-by-stage graphical construction**:
The number of ideal stages $N$ is found by drawing a staircase between the operating line and the equilibrium line on the y–x diagram. The intersection points of horizontal and vertical lines with the equilibrium curve give the equilibrium compositions at each stage.

**Step 6 — Analytical formula for N (Kremser equation for leaching)**:
For constant $L$ and $V$ with $L \neq V$:

$$
N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_0 - \dfrac{y_{N+1}}{R}\right)}{\left(x_N - \dfrac{y_{N+1}}{R}\right)}\right]}{\ln R}
$$

where $R = \dfrac{L}{V}$ is the solvent-to-solid ratio.

For $R = 1$ (special case):
$$
N = \dfrac{x_0 - x_N}{x_N - y_{N+1}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Number of Stages for Oilseed Leaching

**Problem**:
A soybean meal containing **25% oil by mass** (rest insoluble solid) is to be leached with fresh hexane (essentially oil-free) in a counter-current extractor. The desired residual oil content in the spent meal is **0.5% by mass**. The underflow retains **0.6 kg solution per kg insoluble solid**, and the overflow contains no insoluble solid. The solid feed rate is **1000 kg/h**.

**Given**:
- $x_0 = \dfrac{25}{75} = 0.3333$ kg oil/kg insoluble solid
- $x_N = \dfrac{0.5}{99.5} = 0.00503$ kg oil/kg insoluble solid
- $y_{N+1} = 0$ (fresh hexane)
- $L_0 = 75$ kg insoluble solid/h (from 1000 kg/h × 75% insoluble)
- $U = 0.6$ kg solution/kg insoluble solid

**Step 1 — Mass of insoluble solid in underflow**:
$L_{insoluble} = 75$ kg/h (since insoluble solid is conserved)

**Step 2 — Underflow rate**:
$$L_N = L_0 + \text{solution held} = 75 + (75 \times 0.6) = 75 + 45 = 120 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Underflow solute concentration (kg oil/kg solution)**:
$x_N^{soln} = \dfrac{0.5}{100} = 0.005$ kg oil/kg solution

**Step 4 — Mass balance on solute**:
Oil in feed: $1000 \times 0.25 = 250$ kg/h
Oil in underflow: $0.5\% \times 120 = 0.6$ kg/h
Oil in overflow: $250 - 0.6 = 249.4$ kg/h

**Step 5 — Mass of solvent (hexane) in overflow**:
Mass of overflow = Oil + Hexane = $249.4 + V_{hexane}$
The overflow contains no insoluble solid, so:
$$V_1 = 249.4 + V_{hexane} \text{ kg/h}$$

Assuming the miscella (overflow) has a typical 25% oil concentration (industrial design):
$$V_{hexane} = \dfrac{249.4}{0.25} \times 0.75 = 748.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Solvent-to-solid ratio**:
$$R = \dfrac{V_{hexane}}{L_{insoluble}} = \dfrac{748.2}{75} = 9.976 \approx 10$$

**Step 7 — Number of ideal stages**:
Since $R = 10 \neq 1$ and $y_{N+1} = 0$:
$$
N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\dfrac{x_0 - 0}{x_N - 0}\right]}{\ln R} = \dfrac{\ln\left(\dfrac{0.3333}{0.00503}\right)}{\ln 10}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{\ln(66.27)}{\ln(10)} = \dfrac{4.193}{2.303} = 1.82
$$

**Step 8 — Rounding up to integer stages**:
Since fractional stages are not physically realizable:
$$N = 2 \text{ ideal stages}$$

**Practical adjustment** (with stage efficiency $\eta = 80\%$):
$$N_{actual} = \dfrac{N_{ideal}}{\eta} = \dfrac{2}{0.8} = 2.5 \approx 3 \text{ real stages}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: For typical oilseed leaching, 2–3 ideal stages are sufficient because the equilibrium between the oil and hexane is highly favorable, and the operation is limited by diffusion rather than equilibrium.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Selection Decision Tree

| Solid Property | Recommended Equipment | Reasoning |
|----------------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Granular, free-flowing, porous** (e.g., oilseeds) | Bollman, Rotocel, Hildebrandt | Percolation is efficient; good drainage |
| **Fibrous, slimy, or paste-like** (e.g., fish meal) | Kennedy, Bonotto | Prevents clogging; uses mechanical conveyance |
| **Fine particles, slimes, or dilute slurries** (e.g., mineral ores) | Pachuca tanks, CCD battery, slurry extractors | Slurry handling and decanting are needed |
| **Crystals, crystalline solids** (e.g., sugar beets) | Dorr diffuser, Oliver filter | Permits staged washing and filtration |
| **Shear-sensitive biological materials** (e.g., plant cells) | Hildebrandt, Bonotto | Gentle mechanical handling preserves cell integrity |
| **Heat-sensitive solids** (e.g., pharmaceuticals) | Agitated batch vessels with jacket cooling | Precise temperature control |
| **High throughput (1000+ tons/day)** | Rotocel, CCD, Dorr diffuser | Industrial-scale continuous design |
| **Low throughput, high value** (e.g., alkaloids) | Hildebrandt, batch agitated vessels | Flexible and high recovery |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bollman Extractor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fresh Solid Feed] --> B[Top Bucket Loading Zone]
    B --> C[Co-current Leaching Section]
    C --> D[Counter-current Leaching Section]
    D --> E[Bottom Bucket Discharge Zone]
    E --> F[Spent Solid to Drier]
    G[Fresh Solvent] --> H[Spray Nozzle]
    H --> D
    I[Strong Miscella] --> C
    J[Spent Solvent Tank] --> G
    K[Conveyor Drive Motor] --> L[Endless Chain Conveyor]
    L --> B
    L --> C
    L --> D
    L --> E
    M[Concentrated Miscella Storage] --> N[Miscella Stripper]
    N --> O[Recovered Solvent Recycle]
    O --> J
    P[Crude Extract] --> Q[Purification Unit]
```

**Description of Flow**:
- The endless chain conveyor carries perforated buckets through the entire housing.
- Fresh solid is loaded into the top bucket and moves through co-current and counter-current sections.
- Solvent is sprayed in both sections; the miscella is collected at the bottom.
- The spent solid is discharged at the bottom, and the miscella is sent to a stripper for solvent recovery.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Rotocel Extractor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Solid Feed Hopper] --> B[Rotary Rotor]
    B --> C[Cell Filling Station]
    C --> D[Cell Rotation Through Spray Zones]
    D --> E[Spray Zone 1: Strong Miscella]
    D --> F[Spray Zone 2: Intermediate Miscella]
    D --> G[Spray Zone 3: Weak Miscella]
    D --> H[Spray Zone 4: Fresh Solvent]
    H --> I[Cell Inversion and Discharge]
    I --> J[Spent Meal Conveyor]
    K[Miscella Collection Trough] --> L[Filtration and Stripping]
    L --> M[Solvent Recycle Tank]
    M --> H
    N[Crude Miscella] --> O[Evaporation and Recovery]
    O --> P[Recovered Oil]
```

**Description of Flow**:
- Solid is fed into a wedge-shaped cell on the rotating rotor.
- The cell rotates through a series of spray zones, with each successive miscella having lower oil concentration.
- The cell is inverted at the discharge point, releasing the spent meal.
- The counter-current miscella flow is achieved by pumping weaker miscella forward and stronger miscella backward.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hildebrandt Extractor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fresh Solid Feed] --> B[Leg 1 Screw Conveyor]
    B --> C[Leg 2 Screw Conveyor]
    C --> D[Leg 3 Screw Conveyor]
    D --> E[Spent Solid Discharge]
    F[Fresh Solvent Inlet] --> G[Leg 3 Top Spray]
    G --> H[Leg 3 Percolation Bed]
    H --> I[Leg 3 Bottom Collection]
    I --> J[Leg 2 Top Spray]
    J --> K[Leg 2 Percolation Bed]
    K --> L[Leg 2 Bottom Collection]
    L --> M[Leg 1 Top Spray]
    M --> N[Leg 1 Percolation Bed]
    N --> O[Leg 1 Bottom Collection]
    O --> P[Strong Miscella Outlet]
    Q[Screw Drive Motor] --> B
    Q --> C
    Q --> D
```

**Description of Flow**:
- Solid is fed at the top of Leg 1 and moves upward via the screw conveyor in all three legs.
- Solvent is introduced at the top of Leg 3 and flows downward by gravity.
- The solvent percolates through the slowly moving solid bed, becoming enriched with solute.
- True counter-current operation is achieved between solid (upward) and solvent (downward).

### 4.4 Comparative Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Leaching Equipment Selection] --> B{Is the solid granular and free-flowing?}
    B -->|Yes| C{High throughput required?}
    B -->|No| D{Is the solid fibrous or slimy?}
    C -->|Yes| E[Rotocel Extractor]
    C -->|No| F{Budget constraint?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Bollman Extractor]
    F -->|No| H[Hildebrandt Extractor]
    D -->|Yes| I{Continuous operation needed?}
    D -->|No| J{Is the solid a slurry or fine particles?}
    I -->|Yes| K[Kennedy Extractor]
    I -->|No| L[Bonotto Extractor]
    J -->|Yes| M{High value of extract?}
    J -->|No| N[Agitated Batch Vessel]
    M -->|Yes| O[Pachuca Tank or CCD]
    M -->|No| P[Dorr Diffuser]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define leaching. Differentiate between extract and raffinate with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer**:

**Leaching**: Leaching is a solid-liquid extraction process in which a soluble component (solute) is selectively dissolved from a solid mixture using a suitable liquid solvent. The process is governed by mass transfer driven by concentration gradients.

**Extract**: The solute-rich solution obtained after leaching is called the *extract* (or *overflow*). For example, in soybean oil extraction, the hexane-oil mixture leaving the extractor is the extract.

**Raffinate**: The insoluble solid residue that remains after the solute has been extracted is called the *raffinate* (or *underflow*). In soybean processing, the spent meal leaving the extractor is the raffinate.

**Example**: In the extraction of sugar from sugar beets using hot water, the sugar solution is the extract, and the exhausted beet pulp is the raffinate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]**
*List any three types of continuous leaching equipment and state one industrial application of each.*

**Model Answer**:

1. **Bollman Extractor** — Used for extraction of oil from oilseeds like soybean and cottonseed. It uses an endless chain conveyor with perforated baskets for hybrid co-current and counter-current flow.

2. **Rotocel Extractor** — Widely used for oil extraction from soybean, sunflower, and canola. It consists of a rotary horizontal arrangement of wedge-shaped cells with counter-current solvent flow.

3. **Hildebrandt Extractor** — Applied for tannin extraction from wood and alkaloid extraction from plant materials. It uses three vertical immersion columns with screw conveyors for true counter-current operation.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle and construction of a **Rotocel extractor** with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer**:

The **Rotocel extractor** is a continuous, rotary, horizontal-type leaching equipment widely used in the oilseed industry.

**Construction**:
- It consists of a **cylindrical rotor** divided into a series of **wedge-shaped cells** (typically 12 to 24 cells).
- The rotor is enclosed in a **stationary housing** with a slight inclination for drainage.
- Each cell has a **perforated bottom** to allow miscella (solvent + oil) to drain.
- A **feed hopper** introduces the solid into each cell at the filling station.
- A **discharge mechanism** inverts each cell at the end of the rotation to release the spent meal.

**Working Principle**:
1. The rotor rotates slowly (one revolution every 15–60 minutes).
2. Fresh solid is fed into each cell at the top of the rotor.
3. The cell moves through a series of **spray zones**, where progressively weaker miscella is sprayed onto the solid bed.
4. The **strongest miscella** (highest oil concentration) is collected at the feed end, and **fresh solvent** enters at the discharge end.
5. This arrangement provides true **counter-current contact** between the solid and the solvent.
6. After completing a full rotation, the cell is inverted, and the spent solid is discharged.

**Advantages**:
- High capacity (up to 8000 tons/day)
- Compact design with low floor space
- Low solvent-to-meal ratio (1:1 by mass)
- Continuous operation with consistent product quality
- Easy to seal and operate

**Limitations**:
- High capital cost
- Requires precise mechanical sealing to prevent miscella leakage
- Limited to granular, free-flowing solids
- High maintenance for the rotor mechanism

---

**(b)** A counter-current leaching system is used to extract oil from soybean flakes using hexane. The feed contains **20% oil by mass**, and the desired residual oil in the spent meal is **0.8% by mass**. The solvent feed contains **0.02 kg oil/kg hexane**, and the overflow contains **0.25 kg oil/kg solution**. Calculate the number of ideal stages required using the Kremser equation. Take $L/V = 1.2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Step 1 — Convert concentrations to standard form**:
- $x_0 = \dfrac{20}{80} = 0.25$ kg oil/kg insoluble solid
- $x_N = \dfrac{0.8}{99.2} = 0.00806$ kg oil/kg insoluble solid
- $y_{N+1} = 0.02$ kg oil/kg hexane
- $y_1 = 0.25$ kg oil/kg solution
- $R = L/V = 1.2$

**Step 2 — Apply the Kremser equation**:
$$
N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_0 - y_{N+1}/R}{x_N - y_{N+1}/R}\right)\right]}{\ln R}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $y_{N+1}/R$**:
$$\dfrac{y_{N+1}}{R} = \dfrac{0.02}{1.2} = 0.01667$$

**Step 4 — Calculate numerator and denominator**:
$$
x_0 - \dfrac{y_{N+1}}{R} = 0.25 - 0.01667 = 0.23333
$$
$$
x_N - \dfrac{y_{N+1}}{R} = 0.00806 - 0.01667 = -0.00861
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the formula**:
$$
N = \dfrac{\ln\left(\dfrac{0.23333}{-0.00861}\right)}{\ln(1.2)} = \dfrac{\ln(27.10)}{0.1823}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate**:
$$
N = \dfrac{3.300}{0.1823} = 18.1
$$

**Result**: Approximately **18 ideal stages** are required. Rounding up, **N = 19 ideal stages**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: Such a high number of stages indicates that the leaching operation is **diffusion-limited** (slow internal mass transfer). In practice, continuous extractors achieve this efficiency through a single physical unit with multiple internal stages of contact.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Compare **Bollman extractor** and **Hildebrandt extractor** based on construction, flow pattern, applications, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Bollman Extractor | Hildebrandt Extractor |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Construction** | Endless chain conveyor with perforated baskets in a vertical housing | Three vertical immersion columns (legs) with screw conveyors |
| **Flow Pattern** | Hybrid co-current (upper half) and counter-current (lower half) | True counter-current (solid moves up, solvent moves down) |
| **Solvent Hold-up** | Moderate | High |
| **Best for Solids** | Granular, free-flowing, porous oilseeds | Fibrous, porous, plant materials |
| **Industrial Applications** | Soybean, cottonseed, sunflower oil extraction | Tannin extraction, alkaloid extraction, pharmaceutical botanicals |
| **Advantages** | Combines percolation and immersion; high throughput | True counter-current efficiency; excellent extraction; gentle on solids |
| **Limitations** | Mechanical complexity; chain fatigue; not suitable for pasty solids | Large floor space; high solvent inventory; long residence time |

**Conclusion**: The Bollman extractor is preferred for large-scale oilseed processing due to its high capacity, while the Hildebrandt extractor is favored for high-value botanical extractions where extraction efficiency and product quality are paramount.

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Pachuca tank** and discuss its industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Pachuca Tank — Working Principle**:

The Pachuca tank is a tall, cylindrical vessel with a conical bottom, used for batch or series leaching of finely divided solids (especially mineral ores) with air agitation.

**Components**:
1. **Cylindrical tank body** (typically 3–15 m tall, 1–5 m diameter)
2. **Central air-lift pipe** running from the bottom to about two-thirds the height of the tank
3. **Compressed air inlet** at the base of the central pipe
4. **Slurry feed inlet** at the top
5. **Overflow launder** for clarified solution
6. **Cone bottom** for solids discharge

**Working Process**:
1. The tank is filled with slurry (finely ground ore + leaching solvent).
2. Compressed air is injected at the base of the central pipe, creating an **air-lift effect** that pumps the slurry upward through the central pipe.
3. The slurry reaches the top of the central pipe and cascades down outside the pipe, creating a continuous circulation pattern.
4. This circulation provides **agitation and mass transfer** between the solid particles and the solvent.
5. After sufficient residence time, the air supply is stopped, the solids settle, and the **clarified extract** overflows through the launder.
6. The **leached solids** are discharged from the bottom cone.

**Block Diagram** (Text Description):
```
[Fresh Slurry Feed] → [Pachuca Tank with Air Lift] → [Circulation Loop]
       ↓                                                       ↓
[Compressed Air]                                  [Overflow Launder] → [Extract Tank]
       ↓                                                       ↓
[Settled Solids] ← [Cone Bottom Discharge]      [Spent Residue Disposal]
```

**Industrial Applications**:
1. **Gold cyanidation** from low-grade ores (most common application)
2. **Copper ore leaching** with sulfuric acid or ammonia
3. **Uranium ore extraction** with sodium carbonate solutions
4. **Bayer process** for alumina from bauxite
5. **Molybdenum and tungsten** ore processing

**Advantages**:
- Simple construction with no moving mechanical parts
- Low maintenance cost
- Suitable for corrosive and abrasive slurries
- Good temperature control (can be heated with steam coils)
- Easy to scale up

**Limitations**:
- High compressed air consumption
- Long residence time (12–48 hours)
- Limited solid concentration (15–25% by mass)
- Not suitable for coarse or fast-settling particles

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Do not confuse** the underflow (U) with the overflow (V). Many students incorrectly swap these in mass balance equations. **Remember**: Underflow = solid + entrained solution; Overflow = clear solution.
> - In the Kremser equation, ensure the **ratio R = L/V** is correctly defined. Some textbooks use V/L; verify the convention.
> - For the **Bollman extractor**, do not incorrectly describe it as a pure counter-current device. It is **hybrid** (co-current in the upper half, counter-current in the lower half).
> - When calculating the number of stages, always **round up** to the next integer, as fractional stages are not physically realizable.
> - In equipment selection, justify your answer based on **solid properties** (granular, fibrous, slurry) and **production capacity** — these are the two most heavily weighted criteria in KTU marking.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Leaching** is a solid-liquid extraction process driven by concentration gradient and diffusion.
- **Equipment classification**: Batch (Pachuca, agitated tanks) vs. Continuous (Bollman, Rotocel, Hildebrandt, Kennedy, Bonotto, Dorr).
- **Rotocel** is the most widely used oilseed extractor globally; it offers compact, continuous counter-current operation.
- **Bollman** uses hybrid co-/counter-current flow with a chain-and-bucket conveyor.
- **Hildebrandt** is a true counter-current immersion-type extractor with three vertical legs and screw conveyors.
- **Kennedy** extractor handles fibrous and slimy materials that would clog other designs.
- **Pachuca tanks** use air-lift agitation for leaching low-grade ores and slurries.
- **Key formula**: $N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_0 - y_{N+1}/R}{x_N - y_{N+1}/R}\right)\right]}{\ln R}$ for constant L/V (Kremser equation).
- **Special case** (R = 1): $N = \dfrac{x_0 - x_N}{x_N - y_{N+1}}$.
- **Underflow rate U** = mass of solution retained per kg insoluble solid (typically 0.5–1.5 kg/kg for most solids).
- **Stage efficiency** typically ranges from **70% to 90%** for most extractors.
- **Industrial applications** include oilseeds (Bollman/Rotocel), sugar beets (Dorr), gold ores (Pachuca), and pharmaceuticals (Hildebrandt).
- **Selection criteria** (highest weightage in KTU): solid nature, throughput, solvent hold-up, and capital cost.
- **Diffusion control**: Most leaching operations are limited by **internal pore diffusion**, making particle size and porosity critical.
- **Mass balance convention**: $L_0 + V_{N+1} = L_N + V_1$ (total) and $L_0 x_0 + V_{N+1} y_{N+1} = L_N x_N + V_1 y_1$ (solute).
- **KTU favorite questions**: Equipment comparison tables, working of Rotocel/Bollman, and Kremser equation application.
- **Common error**: Forgetting to convert weight percent to mass ratio (kg solute/kg insoluble solid) before applying the Kremser equation.
- **Quick selection rule**: Granular → Rotocel/Bollman; Fibrous → Kennedy/Bonotto; Slurry → Pachuca/CCD; Crystalline → Dorr diffuser.
- **Industrial benchmark**: Solvent-to-solid ratio for soybean oil extraction is typically **1:1 to 1.5:1** by mass.
- **Residence time** in continuous extractors ranges from **30 minutes (Rotocel)** to **24–48 hours (Pachuca tanks)**.
- **KTU exam tip**: Always draw a labeled block diagram in 14-mark questions — it accounts for **2–3 marks** in valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid-Liquid Equilibria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solid-Liquid Equilibria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solid-Liquid Equilibria (SLE)** refers to the thermodynamic state in which a solid phase and a liquid phase coexist in a heterogeneous closed system, with no net transfer of mass occurring across the phase boundary at a specified temperature, pressure, and composition. In mass transfer operations, SLE is the fundamental governing principle behind industrial processes such as **crystallization**, **leaching (solid-liquid extraction)**, **adsorption equilibria**, and **precipitation**.

Mathematically, at equilibrium, the chemical potential (or fugacity) of every component is identical in both phases:

$$\mu_i^s (T, P, x_i^s) = \mu_i^l (T, P, x_i^l)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Solid-Liquid Equilibria forms the theoretical foundation for Module 3, covering solubility, phase diagrams, crystallization yield, and the distribution of solids between immiscible liquids. Mastery of this topic is essential for solving 14-mark problems on crystallizer design and leaching material balances.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sweet Tea" Experiment**
> Imagine adding sugar (solid) one teaspoon at a time into a glass of hot water (liquid) while continuously stirring. Initially, the sugar dissolves quickly. As you keep adding more, the dissolution rate slows down. Eventually, you reach a point where no more sugar dissolves, and undissolved crystals sit at the bottom. This is the **saturation point** — the moment your system achieves **solid-liquid equilibrium**. The solution can hold no more solute at that temperature, regardless of how much you stir.

In simple words:
- **Solubility** = the maximum amount of solid a liquid can "hold" at a given temperature.
- **Saturation** = the state where the solution is "full" of dissolved solid.
- **Supersaturation** = a precarious, unstable state where the solution holds *more* dissolved solid than it should — this is the trigger for crystallization.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility | $C^*$ or $x_A$ | kg solute / kg solvent, or mole fraction |
| Equilibrium Temperature | $T_{eq}$ | **Kelvin (K)** or **°C** |
| Distribution Coefficient | $K_d$ or $m$ | Dimensionless (ratio of concentrations) |
| Heat of Solution | $\Delta H_{sol}$ | **kJ/kmol** (typically 5–50 kJ/kmol) |
| Solid Density | $\rho_s$ | **kg/m³** |
| Solution Density | $\rho_L$ | **kg/m³** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference State:** Solubility data in KTU textbooks are usually reported at **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** and expressed as grams of anhydrous solute per **100 g of solvent** or per **100 g of solution**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase Rule Applied to Solid-Liquid Systems

The **Gibbs Phase Rule** governs the degrees of freedom in any heterogeneous system:

$$F = C - P + 2$$

Where:
- $F$ = Degrees of freedom (independent intensive variables)
- $C$ = Number of components
- $P$ = Number of phases in equilibrium
- $2$ = Refers to the two intensive variables: **T** and **P**

> [!NOTE]
> **Condensed Systems Rule:** For most solid-liquid equilibria, the pressure effect is negligible. We therefore use the **Condensed Phase Rule**: $F = C - P + 1$ (using only temperature as the intensive variable).

### 2.1.1 Common Cases for SL Systems

| System Description | C | P | F = C – P + 1 | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pure solid in pure liquid (e.g., ice-water) | 1 | 2 | 0 | **Invariant** (fixed T = 0°C at 1 atm) |
| Salt + Water (anhydrous salt) | 2 | 2 | 1 | One variable (T or composition) can vary independently |
| Salt hydrate + Water + Anhydrous salt | 2 | 3 | 0 | **Invariant (Eutectic point)** |

## 2.2 Solubility Curves & Temperature Dependence

The solubility of a solid in a liquid is **strongly temperature-dependent**. This dependence is described by the **van't Hoff Equation**:

$$\frac{d(\ln x_A)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{RT^2}$$

Integrating (assuming $\Delta H_{fus}$ is constant over the temperature range):

$$\ln\left(\frac{x_{A,2}}{x_{A,1}}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Where:
- $x_A$ = mole fraction solubility of solute A
- $\Delta H_{fus}$ = molar enthalpy of fusion (J/mol)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (Universal Gas Constant)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Most solids become *more* soluble in liquids as temperature **increases** (endothermic dissolution, $\Delta H_{sol} > 0$). However, some systems (e.g., cerium sulfate, sodium sulfate above 32.4°C) exhibit *retrograde solubility* where solubility *decreases* with temperature.

## 2.3 Eutectic Point

A **eutectic point** is the unique composition and temperature at which a liquid solution is in equilibrium with **two solid phases simultaneously** (typically the pure solid solvent and a solid compound/hydrate of the solute).

At the eutectic:
- $F = C - P + 1 = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ → **Invariant system** (fixed T and composition)

## 2.4 Distribution of a Solid Between Two Immiscible Liquids

When a solid solute is distributed between two immiscible (or partially miscible) liquid phases, the equilibrium is governed by the **Nernst Distribution Law**:

$$K_d = \frac{C_A^{(I)}}{C_A^{(II)}}$$

Where:
- $K_d$ = Distribution (or partition) coefficient (dimensionless, constant at given T)
- $C_A^{(I)}$ = Concentration of solute in liquid phase I
- $C_A^{(II)}$ = Concentration of solute in liquid phase II

> [!WARNING]
> **Validity Conditions:** Nernst's law holds only when (i) the solute has the **same molecular weight** in both phases (no association/dissociation), (ii) temperature is **constant**, and (iii) concentrations are **low** (dilute solution limit). For concentrated systems, KTU problems often modify it to $C^{(I)} = m \cdot (C^{(II)})^n$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = C - P + 2$ | Gibbs Phase Rule | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $F = C - P + 1$ | Condensed Phase Rule (SL systems) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\frac{d(\ln x_A)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{RT^2}$ | van't Hoff Equation | 1/K |
| 4 | $\ln\left(\frac{x_{A,2}}{x_{A,1}}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Integrated van't Hoff | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $K_d = \dfrac{C_A^{(I)}}{C_A^{(II)}}$ | Nernst Distribution Law | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $Y = \dfrac{W \cdot C_1 - W \cdot C_2 (V_1/V_2)}{\text{Input solute mass}}$ | Single-stage leaching yield | kg/kg |
| 7 | $Y_{cryst} = \dfrac{C_1 - C_2 (1 - V_1/V_2 \cdot (1/C_f))}{C_1 - C_2^*}$ | Crystallization yield formula | kg crystals/kg |
| 8 | $\Delta S_{mix} = -R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | Entropy of ideal mixing (conceptual) | J/(mol·K) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Solid-liquid equilibria data are crucial in designing **batch crystallizers** for pharmaceutical and sugar industries, **leaching trains** for oilseed extraction, and **purification columns** in the manufacture of fine chemicals. A real-world application is the fractional crystallization of potassium chloride (KCl) from sylvinite ore in potash mining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Yield of Crystallization from a Cooling Crystallizer

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup (Standard KTU Problem)

A hot saturated solution at temperature $T_1$ containing mass $W$ of solvent is cooled to $T_2$, where the solubility is lower. Crystals of the anhydrous solute precipitate out. Derive an expression for the mass of crystals obtained, $Y_c$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Solubility Function**

Let $C_1$ = solubility (kg solute / kg solvent) at the initial temperature $T_1$.
Let $C_2$ = solubility (kg solute / kg solvent) at the final temperature $T_2$.

**Step 2 — Write the Initial Solute Mass**

Initially, the solution is saturated, so the mass of dissolved solute is:

$$M_{solute, initial} = W \cdot C_1$$

**Step 3 — Write the Final Dissolved Solute Mass**

After cooling, the remaining solution (of mass $W + Y_c$ total, but solvent mass is still $W$) holds dissolved solute:

$$M_{solute, final} = W \cdot C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply Conservation of Mass (excluding water of hydration)**

If the crystals are anhydrous, the mass of crystals that precipitated equals the difference between initial and final dissolved solute:

$$Y_c = M_{solute, initial} - M_{solute, final} = W \cdot C_1 - W \cdot C_2$$

**Step 5 — Final Expression**

$$\boxed{Y_c = W (C_1 - C_2)}$$

**For Hydrated Crystals:** If the crystals retain water of crystallization (e.g., $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$), the solvent mass in the mother liquor is reduced. The full formula becomes:

$$Y_c = \frac{W \cdot C_1 - W \cdot C_2}{1 - C_2 \cdot (R - 1)/R}$$

Where $R$ = ratio of molecular weight of hydrate to molecular weight of anhydrous form. For $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $R = 250/160 = 1.5625$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Material Balance Across a Single-Stage Leaching Unit

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A solid feed of mass $F$ containing mass fraction $x_F$ of solute is contacted with mass $S$ of pure solvent in a single-stage mixer-settler. The underflow (leached solid) retains a mass of solution $L_u$ with composition $y_u$ (mass fraction solute). The overflow (extract) has composition $y_v$. Derive the equilibrium relationship.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total Mass Balance**

$$F + S = L_u + V$$

Where $L_u$ is the mass of underflow and $V$ is the mass of overflow (extract).

**Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance**

$$F \cdot x_F + S \cdot 0 = L_u \cdot y_u + V \cdot y_v$$

(The pure solvent contains no solute initially.)

**Step 3 — Define Underflow Composition Equilibrium**

For a single-stage contact, the underflow solution is in equilibrium with the extract. The Nernst distribution law applies:

$$K = \frac{y_v}{y_u}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the System**

Substituting $V = F + S - L_u$ into the solute balance:

$$F \cdot x_F = L_u \cdot y_u + (F + S - L_u) \cdot K \cdot y_u$$

$$F \cdot x_F = y_u \left[ L_u + (F + S - L_u) \cdot K \right]$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $y_u$ (Underflow Composition)**

$$y_u = \frac{F \cdot x_F}{L_u + K(F + S - L_u)}$$

And the **recovery of solute in the extract** is:

$$R = \frac{V \cdot y_v}{F \cdot x_F} = \frac{(F + S - L_u) \cdot K \cdot y_u}{F \cdot x_F}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A solution contains 80 kg of $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolved in 100 kg of water at 80°C. It is cooled to 20°C. The solubility of $\text{KNO}_3$ is 169 kg/100 kg water at 80°C and 31.6 kg/100 kg water at 20°C. Calculate the mass of crystals obtained (anhydrous).

**Solution:**

$$Y_c = W (C_1 - C_2)$$

$$Y_c = 100 \times \left(\frac{169}{100} - \frac{31.6}{100}\right)$$

$$Y_c = 100 \times (1.69 - 0.316)$$

$$Y_c = 100 \times 1.374$$

$$\boxed{Y_c = 137.4 \text{ kg of KNO}_3 \text{ crystals}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solid-Liquid Equilibrium Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FEED STREAM\nSolid + Liquid Mixture] --> B[EQUILIBRATION UNIT\nMixer or Crystallizer]
    C[ENERGY INPUT\nHeating or Cooling] --> B
    B --> D[PHASE SEPARATION\nFilter or Settler]
    D --> E[LIQUID PHASE\nSaturated Solution]
    D --> F[SOLID PHASE\nCrystals or Leached Solids]
    E --> G[RECYCLE LOOP\nOptional]
    G --> A
    F --> H[PRODUCT OUTLET\nPurified Solid]
    E --> I[EXTRACT OUTLET\nConcentrated Solution]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Solubility Behavior in SL Equilibria

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify the System] --> B{Is Pressure Effect Significant?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Full Phase Rule: F = C - P + 2]
    B -- No --> D[Use Condensed Phase Rule: F = C - P + 1]
    C --> E{Number of Solid Phases?}
    D --> E
    E -- One Solid Phase --> F[Normal Solubility Curve\nT and x vary independently]
    E -- Two Solid Phases --> G[EUTECTIC POINT INVARIANT\nFixed T and Composition]
    F --> H{Does Solubility Increase with T?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Endothermic Dissolution\nMost Common Systems]
    H -- No --> J[Retrograde Solubility\nEx: Na2SO4 above 32.4 C]
    G --> K[END: Determine Operating Conditions]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Multi-Stage Leaching Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Solvent] --> S1[Stage 1\nMixer-Settler]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2\nMixer-Settler]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3\nMixer-Settler]
    S3 --> SN[Stage N\nMixer-Settler]
    B[Solid Feed] --> SN
    SN --> P1[Leached Solid Output]
    S1 --> P2[Concentrated Extract Output]
    Q1((Equilibrium 1)) -.-> S1
    Q2((Equilibrium 2)) -.-> S2
    Q3((Equilibrium 3)) -.-> S3
    QN((Equilibrium N)) -.-> SN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each stage in a counter-current leaching train reaches its own solid-liquid equilibrium. The extract concentration increases progressively from stage N (weakest) to stage 1 (strongest), while the solid feed is progressively depleted of solute as it moves from stage 1 to stage N.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**Define solid-liquid equilibrium. What is the Gibbs phase rule? State the condensed phase rule with one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Solid-Liquid Equilibrium (1 mark):** Solid-liquid equilibrium is the state at which a solid phase and a liquid phase coexist with no net transfer of solute between them, defined by the equality of chemical potentials:

$$\mu_i^s = \mu_i^l$$

**Gibbs Phase Rule (1 mark):** $F = C - P + 2$, where $F$ is degrees of freedom, $C$ is number of components, and $P$ is number of phases.

**Condensed Phase Rule with Example (1 mark):** For SL systems where pressure effect is negligible, $F = C - P + 1$. Example: For a binary salt-water system with one solid phase, $C = 2$, $P = 2$, so $F = 1$ (only T or composition can vary).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**What is the Nernst distribution law? State its limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** When a solute is distributed between two immiscible liquids at constant temperature, the ratio of its concentrations in the two phases is constant:

$$K_d = \frac{C_1}{C_2}$$

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Valid only at **constant temperature**.
2. Applicable only in the **dilute concentration range**.
3. The solute must have the **same molecular state** (no association or dissociation) in both phases.
4. The two solvents must be **mutually immiscible**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**(a)** A hot saturated solution contains 100 kg of water and 80 kg of $\text{NaNO}_3$ at 80°C. It is cooled to 30°C. The solubility of $\text{NaNO}_3$ is 148 kg/100 kg water at 80°C and 96 kg/100 kg water at 30°C. Calculate the mass and composition of crystals obtained (anhydrous crystals). **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the van't Hoff equation for the temperature dependence of solubility of a solid in a liquid. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $W = 100$ kg (solvent, water)
- $C_1 = 148$ kg $\text{NaNO}_3$ / 100 kg water (at 80°C)
- $C_2 = 96$ kg $\text{NaNO}_3$ / 100 kg water (at 30°C)
- Anhydrous crystals

**Step 1 — Apply the crystallization yield formula (1 mark):**

$$Y_c = W \cdot (C_1 - C_2)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):**

$$Y_c = 100 \times \left(\frac{148}{100} - \frac{96}{100}\right)$$

$$Y_c = 100 \times (1.48 - 0.96)$$

$$Y_c = 100 \times 0.52$$

**Step 3 — Final Answer (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{Y_c = 52 \text{ kg of NaNO}_3 \text{ crystals}}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of Mother Liquor (1 mark):**

$$M_{ML} = W + (\text{Initial solute} - Y_c) = 100 + (80 - 52) = 128 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Mass Fraction of Crystals (1 mark):**

The crystals are pure $\text{NaNO}_3$, so composition is 100% $\text{NaNO}_3$.

**Step 6 — Verification of Solubility (1 mark):**

At 30°C, dissolved solute in 100 kg water = 96 kg. We have 80 − 52 = 28 kg dissolved, but capacity is 96 kg. 

⚠ **Wait — check whether the solution was initially saturated.**

Initial solution: 80 kg solute in 100 kg water = 0.80 kg/kg water. Saturation at 80°C = 1.48 kg/kg water. Therefore, the solution was **undersaturated** initially. 

**Corrected approach (1 mark):** Since the solution is undersaturated at 80°C, no crystals will form upon cooling *unless* the concentration at 30°C exceeds 0.96 kg/kg. Concentration at 30°C = 80/100 = 0.80 kg/kg water, which is **less than** 0.96 kg/kg. 

$$\boxed{\text{Therefore, } Y_c = 0 \text{ kg; the solution remains unsaturated at 30°C.}}$$

**Conclusion:** The yield of crystallization is **zero** because the initial solution was undersaturated and the final concentration does not exceed the saturation limit at 30°C.

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of van't Hoff Equation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider the equilibrium between pure solid solute A and its saturated solution. At equilibrium:

$$\mu_A^{solid} = \mu_A^{liquid}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The chemical potential of A in the liquid phase (ideal solution):

$$\mu_A^{liquid} = \mu_A^{*,liquid}(T, P) + RT \ln x_A$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substituting into the equilibrium equation:

$$\mu_A^{solid} = \mu_A^{*,liquid}(T, P) + RT \ln x_A$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Differentiate with respect to T at constant P (and using $\left(\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\bar{S}$):

$$\bar{S}_A^{solid} = \bar{S}_A^{*,liquid} - R \ln x_A - RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln x_A}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Rearrange using the definition of enthalpy: $\bar{H}_A = \mu_A + T \bar{S}_A$. The term $\bar{H}_A^{*,liquid} - \bar{H}_A^{solid} = \Delta H_{fus}$ (enthalpy of fusion):

$$RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln x_A}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final differential form:

$$\boxed{\frac{d(\ln x_A)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{RT^2}}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Integrated form (assuming $\Delta H_{fus}$ is constant):

$$\ln\left(\frac{x_{A,2}}{x_{A,1}}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Define solubility. With the help of a neat phase diagram, explain the formation of a eutectic point in a binary solid-liquid system. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An aqueous solution of benzoic acid contains 5 g/L. It is extracted with benzene. The distribution coefficient of benzoic acid between benzene and water is 4.5 (in favor of benzene). If 1 L of aqueous solution is extracted with 500 mL of benzene in a single stage, calculate the percentage extraction of benzoic acid. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — [7 marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):** Solubility is the maximum amount of a solid solute that can dissolve in a given quantity of a liquid solvent at a specified temperature and pressure to form a saturated solution.

**Phase Diagram Description (3 marks):** A typical binary SL phase diagram has:
- **X-axis:** Composition (mass fraction of solute B in solvent A), ranging from 0 to 1.
- **Y-axis:** Temperature (°C).
- **Curve AC (Liquidus):** Above this curve, the system is a single liquid phase. It shows the solubility of B in A as a function of T.
- **Curve AB (Solidus of pure A):** Below this curve, pure solid A crystallizes.
- **Curve BD (Solidus of pure B):** Below this curve, pure solid B crystallizes.
- **Point E (Eutectic Point):** The intersection of the two solubility curves, where liquid is in equilibrium with both pure solid A and pure solid B simultaneously.

**Eutectic Explanation (2 marks):** At the eutectic point E, the system has $F = C - P + 1 = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ degrees of freedom. This means both the eutectic temperature $T_E$ and the eutectic composition $x_E$ are **fixed** by the system. On cooling any liquid mixture of composition $x_E$, the entire liquid solidifies at $T_E$ into a fine-grained mixture of both solid A and solid B.

---

**Part (b) — [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Initial concentration in water: $C_{aq}^0 = 5$ g/L
- Volume of aqueous phase: $V_{aq} = 1$ L
- Volume of benzene: $V_b = 0.5$ L
- Distribution coefficient: $K = C_b / C_{aq} = 4.5$

**Step 1 — Mass balance for benzoic acid (1 mark):**

Let $x$ = mass of benzoic acid remaining in aqueous phase (g). Then mass extracted into benzene = $(5 - x)$ g.

**Step 2 — Apply Nernst Distribution Law (1 mark):**

$$K = \frac{(5 - x)/0.5}{x/1.0} = \frac{2(5 - x)}{x}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x$ (2 marks):**

$$4.5 = \frac{2(5 - x)}{x}$$

$$4.5x = 10 - 2x$$

$$6.5x = 10$$

$$x = \frac{10}{6.5} = 1.538 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate fraction extracted (1 mark):**

$$\% \text{ Extraction} = \frac{5 - 1.538}{5} \times 100 = \frac{3.462}{5} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Final answer (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Extraction} = 69.23\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Solid-Liquid Equilibria:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Students often mix the volumes and concentrations in the mass balance. Always convert volumes to the **same units** (L or mL) and concentrations to **g/L** before applying the Nernst law.
> 2. **Hydrate vs. Anhydrous Confusion (–2 marks):** In crystallization problems, forgetting to account for **water of crystallization** when the crystals are hydrated (e.g., $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \cdot 10\text{H}_2\text{O}$) is the most common error. Use the formula with $R$ (MW ratio).
> 3. **Phase Rule Misapplication (–1 mark):** Apply the **condensed phase rule** ($F = C - P + 1$) for SL systems, *not* the full Gibbs rule, unless pressure is explicitly significant.
> 4. **Sign Error in van't Hoff Equation (–1 mark):** The integrated form has $\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$, *not* $\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$. This sign convention is critical.
> 5. **Nernst Law Validity Range (–1 mark):** Do not apply Nernst's law to concentrated solutions or where the solute dissociates/associates in either phase. The modified form $C_1 = m \cdot C_2^n$ must be used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solid-Liquid Equilibria**

- **Solid-Liquid Equilibrium** = state where solid and liquid phases coexist with equal chemical potentials; basis for crystallization, leaching, and precipitation.
- **Gibbs Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$ (general); **Condensed Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 1$ (SL systems).
- **Eutectic Point:** Invariant point where liquid coexists with **two solid phases** simultaneously. Fixed $T_E$ and $x_E$.
- **Solubility** is **temperature-dependent**; quantified by the **van't Hoff Equation:** $\frac{d(\ln x_A)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{RT^2}$.
- **Most solids** dissolve *endothermically* (solubility ↑ with T); some exhibit **retrograde solubility** (e.g., $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ above 32.4°C).
- **Nernst Distribution Law:** $K_d = C_1 / C_2$ (valid at constant T, dilute solutions, no association/dissociation).
- **Crystallization Yield (Anhydrous):** $Y_c = W(C_1 - C_2)$, where $W$ = solvent mass, $C_1, C_2$ = solubilities at $T_1, T_2$.
- **Crystallization Yield (Hydrated):** $Y_c = \dfrac{W(C_1 - C_2)}{1 - C_2(R-1)/R}$, where $R = MW_{hydrate} / MW_{anhydrous}$.
- **Leaching Material Balance:** $F \cdot x_F + S \cdot 0 = L_u \cdot y_u + V \cdot y_v$, with $K = y_v / y_u$.
- **Always** check whether the initial solution is **saturated** before applying the crystallization yield formula — a common KTU trap question.
- **Unit consistency** is critical: convert all volumes, masses, and concentrations to a common base before substitution.
- **Industrial applications:** Sugar refining, pharmaceutical crystallization, oilseed leaching (soybean, sunflower), potash mining (KCl recovery from sylvinite), and freeze crystallization for desalination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption: Adsorbent Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adsorption: Adsorbent Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adsorption** is a surface-based mass transfer operation in which molecules of a fluid phase (gas or liquid) — called the **adsorbate** — accumulate on the surface of a solid (or sometimes liquid) substrate — called the **adsorbent** — through physical or chemical interactions. The reverse process, where accumulated molecules leave the surface, is called **desorption**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Framework):**
> *"Adsorption is the selective transfer of one or more solutes from a fluid phase to the surface of a rigid, insoluble, microporous solid particle, driven by surface energy differences rather than bulk penetration."*

## 1.2 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning | Phase Location |
|------|---------|----------------|
| **Adsorbate** | The substance being adsorbed (e.g., water vapour, benzene, dye) | Migrates from bulk fluid → solid surface |
| **Adsorbent** | The solid material providing the surface (e.g., activated carbon, silica gel) | Solid, porous matrix |
| **Sorption** | Umbrella term covering both adsorption and absorption | — |
| **Desorption** | Reverse process — removal of adsorbate from surface | Surface → bulk fluid |
| **Pore** | Cavity within adsorbent that traps adsorbate | Within solid |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnet-Dust Phenomenon"

Imagine a clean kitchen shelf covered with a sticky adhesive mat (the **adsorbent**). When you sprinkle fine spice particles (the **adsorbate**) over it, the particles get trapped on the mat's surface. The mat does not absorb the spices deep into its body (that would be *absorption* — like a sponge soaking water); it merely *sticks* them to its surface.

In engineering terms:
- The **surface area** of the mat is critical → the more sticky area exposed, the more spices it can hold.
- **Pores** act like hidden sticky pockets — they dramatically increase the effective surface.
- Once the mat is full, you can *rejuvenate* it by washing (desorption), making it reusable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A good industrial adsorbent can have an internal surface area equivalent to **500–1500 m²/g** — about the area of 5 to 15 football fields packed inside just one gram of material!

## 1.4 Why Adsorption Matters in Chemical Engineering

Adsorption is the workhorse of:
- **Purification** — removing trace impurities (colour, odour, toxic organics) from water and air.
- **Separation** — recovering valuable components (e.g., bioethanol dehydration, hydrogen purification).
- **Catalysis** — providing the active surface for heterogeneous reactions.
- **Drying** — removing moisture from gases and organic solvents.
- **Pollution Control** — capturing VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds) and SO₂ from industrial exhausts.

---

## 1.5 Physical vs Chemical Adsorption — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Physisorption (Physical) | Chemisorption (Chemical) |
|----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Driving Force** | Weak van der Waals forces | Strong chemical (covalent/ionic) bonds |
| **Enthalpy of Adsorption** | **20–40 kJ/mol** (low) | **80–400 kJ/mol** (high) |
| **Specificity** | Non-specific; occurs on all surfaces | Highly specific to surface chemistry |
| **Reversibility** | Easily reversible | Often irreversible |
| **Temperature Range** | Near or below critical T of adsorbate | Can occur at high temperatures |
| **Activation Energy** | Negligible | Significant (chemical reaction-like) |
| **Multilayer Formation** | Yes (BET theory) | Typically monolayer only |
| **Example** | Water vapour on silica gel | Oxygen chemisorbed on metal catalyst |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Reality:** Most industrial adsorption processes (drying, decolourization, VOC capture) rely on **physisorption** because the operation is cyclic — adsorption must be followed by **regeneration** (desorption). Chemisorption is dominant in **catalysis**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 What Makes a "Good" Adsorbent? — The Five Engineering Criteria

A material qualifies as an industrial-grade adsorbent **only if it satisfies** the following five criteria simultaneously:

1. **High Specific Surface Area (m²/g):** Maximizes the available surface for capturing adsorbate.
2. **High Selectivity:** Prefers the target molecule over other components in the mixture.
3. **Appropriate Pore Structure:** Pore size must match the kinetic diameter of the adsorbate molecule.
4. **High Adsorption Capacity:** Large equilibrium loading (kg adsorbate / kg adsorbent) at operating conditions.
5. **Mechanical, Thermal & Chemical Stability:** Withstands regeneration cycles, temperature swings, and corrosive feeds.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Magic Number" Threshold:** Commercial adsorbents typically have surface areas of **300–1500 m²/g** and pore volumes of **0.2–0.8 cm³/g**.

## 2.2 Pore Size Classification (IUPAC Standard)

| Pore Class | Pore Width | Typical Applications |
|------------|------------|----------------------|
| **Micropores** | < **2 nm** | Gas-phase adsorption (H₂O, CO₂, CH₄) |
| **Mesopores** | **2 – 50 nm** | Liquid-phase adsorption, dye removal, vapour recovery |
| **Macropores** | > **50 nm** | Act mainly as transport arteries; supply adsorbate to inner pores |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** *"Classify pores according to IUPAC and explain their role in adsorption."* — This is a high-weightage 7-mark or 14-mark question. Memorize the 2 nm and 50 nm boundaries.

## 2.3 Classification of Major Industrial Adsorbents

### 2.3.1 Activated Carbon (AC) — *The Universal Adsorbent*

- **Source Materials:** Coconut shell, wood, lignite, coal, peat, rice husk, sawdust.
- **Surface Area:** **500–1500 m²/g** (highest among common adsorbents).
- **Nature:** Hydrophobic + non-polar; **amphoteric** in pH behaviour.
- **Manufacture:** Carbonization (400–600 °C) → Activation (steam or CO₂ at 800–1000 °C).
- **Pore Structure:** Dominated by **micropores** (0.5–2 nm) with some mesopores.
- **Best For:** Decolourization, deodourization, removal of organic pollutants, drinking water treatment, gold recovery, solvent recovery, gas masks.
- **Limitation:** Cannot effectively adsorb highly polar species (e.g., sugars, salts) without surface modification.

### 2.3.2 Silica Gel — *The Moisture Magnet*

- **Composition:** Amorphous **SiO₂ · nH₂O** (synthetic).
- **Surface Area:** **300–800 m²/g**.
- **Nature:** **Hydrophilic**, polar surface (Si–OH silanol groups).
- **Manufacture:** Neutralization of sodium silicate with sulfuric acid → gelation → washing → drying.
- **Pore Structure:** Uniform **mesopores** (2–10 nm).
- **Best For:** Drying of gases (air, H₂, O₂, N₂), drying of organic solvents, dehumidification in packaged electronics, desiccant in transformer oil.
- **Limitation:** Loses capacity in liquid water (pores flood); pore collapse at temperatures > 300 °C.

### 2.3.3 Activated Alumina — *The Acidic Gas Specialist*

- **Composition:** **γ-Al₂O₃** (porous form of aluminium oxide).
- **Surface Area:** **150–400 m²/g**.
- **Nature:** **Hydrophilic**, polar, basic surface (Al–OH).
- **Manufacture:** Thermal dehydration of aluminium hydroxide at 300–600 °C.
- **Pore Structure:** Distribution of micro + meso pores.
- **Best For:** Removal of polar molecules, **defluoridation of drinking water**, drying of gases, fluoride and arsenic removal.
- **Limitation:** Lower capacity than activated carbon for organics.

### 2.3.4 Zeolites (Molecular Sieves) — *The Size-Selective Engineers*

- **Composition:** Crystalline aluminosilicates — general formula:
$$
\text{M}_{x/n}\left[\left(\text{AlO}_2\right)_x \left(\text{SiO}_2\right)_y\right] \cdot z \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
  where M is a metal cation (typically Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺).
- **Surface Area:** **400–800 m²/g** (Type 5A), up to **900 m²/g** (Type 13X).
- **Nature:** Highly polar; **cation-exchangeable** framework.
- **Pore Structure:** **Uniform, crystalline micropores** with precisely defined window diameters.
- **Best For:** Selective gas separations based on molecular size, deep drying of natural gas and refrigerants, ion-exchange applications, catalytic cracking (in petrochemicals).
- **Limitation:** Pore openings are fixed; molecules larger than the aperture cannot enter at all.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Zeolite Type Code (Memorize!):**
> - **3A** → Pore opening **3 Å** → Drying of unsaturated hydrocarbons (ethylene, propylene).
> - **4A** → Pore opening **4 Å** → Drying of natural gas, refrigerants, CO₂ removal.
> - **5A** → Pore opening **5 Å** → Separation of n-paraffins from iso-paraffins; pressure-swing adsorption (PSA) for H₂.
> - **13X** → Pore opening **9 Å** → Removal of H₂S and mercaptans from natural gas.

### 2.3.5 Polymeric Adsorbents (Resins)

- **Examples:** Amberlite XAD-4 (styrene-divinylbenzene), Duolite ES-104, Purolite MN-200.
- **Surface Area:** **100–800 m²/g**.
- **Nature:** Hydrophobic; can be chemically functionalized.
- **Best For:** Recovery of phenolic compounds, antibiotics, removal of humic substances from water, pharmaceutical purifications.
- **Advantage:** Tailorable surface chemistry; excellent regeneration stability.

### 2.3.6 Biosorbents (Emerging Green Adsorbents)

- **Examples:** Chitosan, alginate beads, agricultural waste (rice straw, sawdust, bagasse, peanut shell, fly ash).
- **Surface Area:** **10–200 m²/g** (typically lower than AC).
- **Nature:** Variable (depends on source); often rich in –OH, –NH₂, –COOH groups.
- **Best For:** Heavy metal removal (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cu²⁺, Hg²⁺), low-cost water treatment.
- **Advantage:** Low cost, renewable, biodegradable.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Use |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Specific Surface Area | $S = \dfrac{A_m \cdot N_A}{M_s}$ | $A_m$ = area per molecule (m²), $N_A$ = Avogadro's number, $M_s$ = molar mass (g/mol) | Theoretical estimation |
| 2 | BET Equation (Multi-layer) | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | $V$ = volume adsorbed, $V_m$ = monolayer volume, $P$ = partial pressure, $P_0$ = saturation pressure, $C$ = BET constant | Surface area determination (HIGH-YIELD) |
| 3 | BET Surface Area | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_m}{V_{molar}}$ | $a_m$ = cross-sectional area of one adsorbate molecule (N₂: 16.2 × 10⁻²⁰ m²) | Pore characterization |
| 4 | Pore Volume (from $P/P_0 = 0.95$) | $V_p = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{Hg}}$ | $\rho_{Hg}$ = mercury density at infiltration pressure | Total pore volume |
| 5 | Mean Pore Radius (Kelvin) | $r_p = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{R T \ln(P_0 / P)}$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle | Mesopore size |
| 6 | Langmuir Isotherm | $q = \dfrac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$ | $q$ = loading, $C$ = concentration, $K_L$ = Langmuir constant, $q_m$ = max capacity | Monolayer capacity |
| 7 | Freundlich Isotherm | $q = K_F \, C^{1/n}$ | $K_F$, $1/n$ = empirical constants | Heterogeneous surfaces |
| 8 | Polanyi Potential | $\varepsilon = R T \ln\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)$ | Volumetric potential energy | Characteristic curve |
| 9 | Break-even Surface Criterion | $\text{Good adsorbent} \iff S \geq 300 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$ | — | Selection rule of thumb |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** For the BET equation, students often confuse the **C constant** (related to heat of adsorption) with the **monolayer capacity $V_m$**. $V_m$ is what you read off from the **slope and intercept** of the BET linear plot.

## 2.5 Engineering Selection Decision Tree — Which Adsorbent When?

| If the problem is... | Best adsorbent choice | Reason |
|----------------------|------------------------|--------|
| Removing dissolved organics from water | Activated Carbon | Hydrophobic surface loves organics |
| Drying air/gas streams | Silica Gel or 3A/4A Zeolite | Hydrophilic surface; tight micropores for H₂O |
| Removing acid gases (HCl, HF) | Activated Alumina | Polar + basic surface |
| Separating n-/iso-paraffins | 5A Zeolite | Size exclusion (n-paraffins fit, iso do not) |
| Decolourizing sugar syrup | Bone char or activated carbon | Affinity for colour bodies |
| Heavy metal recovery from wastewater | Chitosan / biosorbents | –NH₂, –OH groups chelate metal ions |
| Pharmaceutical/VOC capture | Polymeric resin (XAD) | Tunable surface chemistry |
| Ultra-deep drying of natural gas | 4A Molecular Sieve | Cationic field traps H₂O even at ppm level |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the BET Equation — The Foundation of Surface Area Measurement

The **Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET)** equation extends Langmuir's monolayer model to multilayer adsorption. The following derivation is the standard kinetic derivation taught at KTU level.

### Step 1: The Three Core Assumptions

1. The surface is **energetically homogeneous** (each site has the same adsorption energy).
2. Molecules can adsorb in **multiple stacked layers** (no limit on the number of layers).
3. The heat of adsorption for the **first layer** is $\Delta H_1$; the heat of adsorption for **every subsequent layer** equals the heat of liquefaction $\Delta H_L$.

### Step 2: Define Surface Coverages

Let $\theta_i$ = fraction of the surface **covered by exactly $i$ layers** of adsorbate.

The fraction of surface **bare** (zero layers) is $\theta_0$.
The fraction with exactly one layer is $\theta_1$, and so on.

### Step 3: Apply Dynamic Equilibrium to Each Layer

For layer 1 (adsorption on bare surface):
$$
a_1 P \, \theta_0 = b_1 \, \theta_1 \, e^{-Q_1 / RT}
$$

For layer 2:
$$
a_2 P \, \theta_1 = b_2 \, \theta_2 \, e^{-Q_2 / RT}
$$

In general, for $i \geq 2$ (condensation onto already-adsorbed layers), the heat of adsorption $Q_i$ equals the heat of liquefaction $Q_L$:
$$
Q_2 = Q_3 = \cdots = Q_i = Q_L
$$

This gives:
$$
a_i P \, \theta_{i-1} = b_i \, \theta_i \, e^{-Q_L / RT}
$$

### Step 4: Express All $\theta_i$ in Terms of $\theta_0$

Define the ratio for layers beyond the first:
$$
x = \dfrac{a \, P \, e^{Q_L / RT}}{b \, e^{Q_L / RT}} \cdot \text{(const)} = \dfrac{P}{P_0} \cdot C'
$$
where $C'$ is a constant.

Then:
$$
\theta_1 = C' \cdot \theta_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)
$$
$$
\theta_2 = C' \cdot x \cdot \theta_0 = C' \cdot \theta_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^2
$$
$$
\theta_i = C' \cdot \theta_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{i}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the Site-Conservation Constraint

All surface fractions must sum to unity:
$$
\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \theta_i = 1
$$

Substituting:
$$
\theta_0 \left[ 1 + C' \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{i} \right] = 1
$$

This geometric series converges to:
$$
\theta_0 \left[ 1 + \dfrac{C' \cdot (P/P_0)}{1 - (P/P_0)} \right] = 1
$$

### Step 6: Compute the Total Volume Adsorbed

Total volume adsorbed $V$ equals the sum of (volume contributed by each layer × number of molecules in that layer):
$$
V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \cdot \theta_i
$$

After carrying out the algebra (expanding the summation using $\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i x^i = x/(1-x)^2$):

$$
\dfrac{V}{V_m} = \dfrac{C' \cdot (P/P_0)}{\left(1 - P/P_0\right)\left[1 + (C' - 1)(P/P_0)\right]}
$$

### Step 7: Rearrange into the Classical BET Form

Let $C = C'$ and $x = P/P_0$. Inverting and simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}}
$$

This is the **BET equation** — a linear plot of $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}$ vs. $\dfrac{P}{P_0}$ gives:
- **Slope** $= \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C}$
- **Intercept** $= \dfrac{1}{V_m C}$

Adding slope and intercept:
$$
\text{Slope} + \text{Intercept} = \dfrac{1}{V_m}
$$

Thus:
$$
V_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{Slope} + \text{Intercept}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — BET Surface Area Calculation

> **Problem (Model KTU 2024 Style):**
> In a BET nitrogen adsorption experiment at **77 K** on an activated carbon sample, the following data were obtained:

| $P/P_0$ | Volume Adsorbed $V$ (cm³/g, STP) |
|----------|-----------------------------------|
| 0.05     | 105                               |
| 0.10     | 130                               |
| 0.20     | 165                               |
| 0.30     | 195                               |

> The saturation pressure of N₂ at 77 K is $P_0 = 1$ atm. Cross-sectional area of N₂ molecule: $a_m = 16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m². Calculate the **BET surface area** of the adsorbent.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}$ for each data point.**

For $P/P_0 = 0.05$, $V = 105$:
$$
\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{0.05}{105 \times (1 - 0.05)} = \dfrac{0.05}{99.75} = 5.013 \times 10^{-4}
$$

For $P/P_0 = 0.10$, $V = 130$:
$$
\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{0.10}{130 \times 0.90} = \dfrac{0.10}{117.0} = 8.547 \times 10^{-4}
$$

For $P/P_0 = 0.20$, $V = 165$:
$$
\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{0.20}{165 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{0.20}{132.0} = 1.515 \times 10^{-3}
$$

For $P/P_0 = 0.30$, $V = 195$:
$$
\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{0.30}{195 \times 0.70} = \dfrac{0.30}{136.5} = 2.198 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2: Perform Linear Regression on $Y = \dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}$ vs $X = P/P_0$.**

Using the four data points:

$$
X: \{0.05,\ 0.10,\ 0.20,\ 0.30\}
$$
$$
Y: \{0.0005013,\ 0.0008547,\ 0.001515,\ 0.002198\}
$$

Mean values:
$$
\bar{X} = \dfrac{0.05 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.30}{4} = 0.1625
$$
$$
\bar{Y} = \dfrac{(5.013 + 8.547 + 15.15 + 21.98) \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 1.267 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Slope:
$$
m = \dfrac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}
$$

Computing numerator:
$$
(0.05 - 0.1625)(0.0005013 - 0.001267) = (-0.1125)(-0.0007657) = 8.614 \times 10^{-5}
$$
$$
(0.10 - 0.1625)(0.0008547 - 0.001267) = (-0.0625)(-0.0004123) = 2.577 \times 10^{-5}
$$
$$
(0.20 - 0.1625)(0.001515 - 0.001267) = (0.0375)(0.000248) = 9.300 \times 10^{-6}
$$
$$
(0.30 - 0.1625)(0.002198 - 0.001267) = (0.1375)(0.000931) = 1.280 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Sum of numerator:
$$
8.614 \times 10^{-5} + 2.577 \times 10^{-5} + 9.300 \times 10^{-6} + 1.280 \times 10^{-4} = 2.491 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Computing denominator:
$$
(0.05 - 0.1625)^2 = 0.01266
$$
$$
(0.10 - 0.1625)^2 = 0.00391
$$
$$
(0.20 - 0.1625)^2 = 0.00141
$$
$$
(0.30 - 0.1625)^2 = 0.01891
$$

Sum of denominator:
$$
0.01266 + 0.00391 + 0.00141 + 0.01891 = 0.03689
$$

Therefore:
$$
m = \dfrac{2.491 \times 10^{-4}}{0.03689} = 6.752 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Intercept:
$$
b = \bar{Y} - m \bar{X} = 1.267 \times 10^{-3} - (6.752 \times 10^{-3})(0.1625) = 1.267 \times 10^{-3} - 1.097 \times 10^{-3} = 1.70 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Monolayer Volume $V_m$.**

$$
V_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{Slope} + \text{Intercept}} = \dfrac{1}{6.752 \times 10^{-3} + 1.70 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1}{6.922 \times 10^{-3}}
$$
$$
V_m = 144.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g (at STP)}
$$

**Step 4: Convert $V_m$ to Moles.**

At STP, 1 mole of gas occupies 22414 cm³. Therefore:
$$
n_m = \dfrac{V_m}{22414} = \dfrac{144.5}{22414} = 6.448 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/g}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Number of Molecules per Gram.**

$$
N = n_m \cdot N_A = 6.448 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.883 \times 10^{21} \text{ molecules/g}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate Specific Surface Area.**

$$
S_{BET} = N \cdot a_m = 3.883 \times 10^{21} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20} \text{ m}^2/\text{g}
$$
$$
\boxed{S_{BET} = 629.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}}
$$

This falls in the typical range for a **good-quality activated carbon (500–1500 m²/g)**. ✔

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Adsorbent Selection Quick-Reference

| Adsorbent | Polarity Affinity | Typical Surface Area (m²/g) | Dominant Pore Size | Cost (₹/kg) | Best for |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|-------------|----------|
| Activated Carbon | Non-polar (hydrophobic) | 500–1500 | Micro (1–2 nm) | 100–300 | Organics, colours, odours |
| Silica Gel | Polar (hydrophilic) | 300–800 | Meso (2–10 nm) | 80–200 | Gas drying |
| Activated Alumina | Polar | 150–400 | Meso | 150–400 | F⁻, As removal |
| Zeolite 3A | Polar (cationic) | ~700 | Micro (3 Å) | 200–500 | Unsaturate drying |
| Zeolite 4A | Polar | ~700 | Micro (4 Å) | 200–500 | Refrigerant drying |
| Zeolite 5A | Polar | ~750 | Micro (5 Å) | 200–500 | PSA H₂, n/i-paraffin |
| Zeolite 13X | Polar | ~900 | Micro (9 Å) | 250–600 | CO₂, H₂S removal |
| Polymeric XAD | Hydrophobic | 100–800 | Meso | 500–2000 | Pharmaceuticals |
| Chitosan | Polar (cationic) | 10–200 | Macro-meso | 50–200 | Heavy metal removal |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Adsorption Process — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BulkFluid["Bulk Fluid Phase\nGas or Liquid Mixture"] --> A1
    A1["Boundary Layer\nExternal Mass Transfer"] --> A2
    A2["Surface Diffusion\nAlong Pore Walls"] --> A3
    A3["Pore Volume\nActive Adsorption Sites"] --> LoadedSite["Loaded Adsorbent\nSaturated Surface"]
    LoadedSite --> Regen1["Regeneration Step 1\nDesorption by Heating"]
    Regen1 --> Regen2["Regeneration Step 2\nSteam or Vacuum Stripping"]
    Regen2 --> FreshSite["Fresh Adsorbent Ready\nfor Next Cycle"]
    FreshSite --> A3
    BulkFluid --> Q1{"Selectivity\nCheck"}
    Q1 --> AdsorbateTarget["Target Adsorbate\nCaptured"]
    Q1 --> Inert["Inert Components\nPass Through"]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Classification Tree of Industrial Adsorbents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Industrial Adsorbents"] --> C1
    R --> C2
    R --> C3
    R --> C4
    R --> C5
    C1["Carbon Based\nActivated Carbon\nBone Char\nCarbon Black"]
    C2["Oxide Based\nSilica Gel\nActivated Alumina\nMolecular Sieves"]
    C3["Zeolitic\nType 3A, 4A, 5A, 13X"]
    C4["Polymeric\nAmberlite XAD Series\nPurolite Series"]
    C5["Bio Based\nChitosan\nAlginate\nAgricultural Waste"]
    C3 --> Zsub["Crystalline Aluminosilicate\nFixed Pore Aperture\nStrong Polar Field"]
    C2 --> Oxsub["Amorphous Porous Oxide\nBroad Pore Distribution"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Pore Hierarchy in a Single Activated Carbon Particle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Particle["Single Granule\nDiameter 2 to 4 mm"] --> Macro["Macropores\nGreater than 50 nm\nTransport Arteries\nFeed Internal Pores"]
    Macro --> Meso["Mesopores\n2 to 50 nm\nPre Stage Adsorption\nCapillary Condensation"]
    Meso --> Micro["Micropores\nLess than 2 nm\nPrimary Adsorption Volume\nHighest Surface Contribution"]
    Micro --> Site["Active Site\nFunctional Group\nCarbonyl Hydroxyl Quinone"]
    Site --> Mol["Adsorbate Molecule\nHeld by\nVan der Waals or\nHydrogen Bond"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Adsorption-Regeneration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1\nAdsorption\nFeed in Cold"] --> S2["Step 2\nSaturation\nBreakthrough"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3\nDesorption\nHot Steam or\nHot Purge Gas"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4\nCooling\nRepressurisation"]
    S4 --> S1
    S1 -->|Reject| Exhaust["Exhaust Clean Gas"]
    S2 -->|Switch| Outlet["Outlet\nAdsorbate Rich"]
    S3 -->|Recover| Product["Recovered Product\nConcentrated Stream"]
    S4 -->|Idle| Standby["Standby Mode"]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Isotherm Shapes and Their Meaning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Adsorption Isotherm\nQ versus P over P0"] --> T1
    Q --> T2
    Q --> T3
    Q --> T4
    Q --> T5
    T1["Type I Langmuir\nMicroporous Solid\nStrong Adsorbate Surface"]
    T2["Type II Sigmoid\nNon Porous or Macroporous\nMultilayer Formation"]
    T3["Type III Anti Langmuir\nWeak Interactions\nRare Case"]
    T4["Type IV Hysteresis Loop\nMesoporous Solid\nCapillary Condensation"]
    T5["Type V Weak Affinity Hysteresis\nHydrophobic Mesopores"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the Five IUPAC Isotherm Types** — Type I corresponds to microporous adsorbents (e.g., activated carbon, zeolites), Type IV corresponds to mesoporous adsorbents (e.g., silica gel, MCM-41). This distinction is regularly tested in KTU exams.
