# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption. State any two industrial applications where physisorption is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Bonding force | Van der Waals (weak) | Covalent/ionic (strong) |
| Enthalpy of adsorption | 20–40 kJ/mol | 80–400 kJ/mol |
| Reversibility | Reversible | Often irreversible |
| Layers | Multilayer possible | Monolayer only |

**Two industrial applications of physisorption:**
1. **Drying of gases** using silica gel or 3A molecular sieves (adsorption of water vapour).
2. **Decolourization of sugar syrup** using activated carbon (adsorption of colour bodies).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mnemonic:** **"Ph-Physic-low-Phuel"** → Physisorption → Low Enthalpy → Reversible.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any three characteristic properties required of an industrial adsorbent. Give an example of a commercial adsorbent and state one specific application of it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three essential properties of an industrial adsorbent:**
1. **High specific surface area** (typically > 300 m²/g) to provide maximum adsorbing surface.
2. **High selectivity** towards the target component in the mixture.
3. **Good mechanical and thermal stability** to withstand repeated adsorption–desorption cycles.

**Example:** **Activated Carbon** — Application: **Removal of dissolved organic contaminants and odour from municipal drinking water**.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation 14-mark questions** carry an internal choice. You must attempt **one of the two alternatives**. Both alternatives below are full 14-mark sub-divided questions with detailed model solutions.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the term *adsorbent*. Classify the important industrial adsorbents and discuss the characteristic features, surface area range, and typical applications of any **two** adsorbents in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **classification of pores** as per IUPAC. How does pore size influence the choice of adsorbent for a given separation problem? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
An **adsorbent** is a porous solid material with high internal surface area, used to selectively accumulate (adsorb) one or more components from a fluid (gas or liquid) phase onto its surface. The phenomenon is driven by surface energy differentials.

**Classification (2 Marks):**
Industrial adsorbents are classified as:
1. **Carbon-based:** Activated carbon, bone char, carbon molecular sieves.
2. **Oxide-based:** Silica gel, activated alumina, titania, zirconia.
3. **Zeolitic / Molecular sieves:** 3A, 4A, 5A, 13X.
4. **Polymeric:** XAD resins, hypercrosslinked polymers.
5. **Bio-based / Natural:** Chitosan, alginate, agricultural residues.

**Detailed Discussion of Two Adsorbents (3 Marks):**

**(i) Activated Carbon**
- **Surface area:** 500–1500 m²/g
- **Manufacture:** Carbonization of precursors (coconut shell, coal, wood) at 400–600 °C in inert atmosphere, followed by activation with steam/CO₂ at 800–1000 °C.
- **Pore structure:** Predominantly microporous; some mesopores.
- **Surface chemistry:** Amphoteric, hydrophobic, non-polar.
- **Applications:** Drinking water purification, decolourization, gold recovery, gas masks, solvent recovery, air purifiers.

**(ii) Silica Gel**
- **Surface area:** 300–800 m²/g
- **Manufacture:** Reaction of sodium silicate with sulfuric acid → hydrogel → drying.
- **Pore structure:** Uniform mesopores (2–10 nm).
- **Surface chemistry:** Hydrophilic, polar silanol (–Si–OH) groups.
- **Applications:** Drying of air, gases, and organic solvents; dehumidification in electronics packaging; desiccant in double-glazed windows.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Pore Classification Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
Porous Solids → IUPAC Pore Classification
│
├── Micropores: width < 2 nm
│   → High surface area contribution
│   → Strong adsorption potential (overlapping wall forces)
│   → Example: Activated carbon, zeolites
│
├── Mesopores: 2 nm < width < 50 nm
│   → Capillary condensation occurs
│   → Type IV isotherm with hysteresis
│   → Example: Silica gel, MCM-41
│
└── Macropores: width > 50 nm
    → Transport arteries for adsorbate
    → Negligible adsorption capacity
    → Example: Cracks in pellets
```

**Influence of Pore Size on Adsorbent Choice (4 Marks):**

The **kinetic diameter** of the target adsorbate molecule must be smaller than the **pore aperture** of the adsorbent for adsorption to occur. Selection logic:

1. **Gas drying (H₂O removal, kinetic diameter 0.265 nm):**
   Use **zeolite 3A or 4A** (aperture 0.3–0.4 nm) — micropores strongly hold polar water molecules via cation field.

2. **n-Paraffin / iso-Paraffin separation (C₅H₁₂ vs C₅H₁₂ isomer):**
   Use **zeolite 5A** (5 Å aperture). The linear n-paraffin enters, the branched iso-paraffin is excluded.

3. **Liquid-phase dye removal (large organic molecules, 1–5 nm):**
   Use **activated carbon with mesopores** — microporous zeolites would exclude these large molecules.

4. **Vapour recovery at ppm levels:**
   Use **carbon molecular sieves with uniform micropores (0.4–0.5 nm)** matched to the adsorbate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently write only the **definition** of pores and forget the **engineering implication** — that **molecular size matching is the deciding factor** in industrial adsorbent choice. Marks are specifically awarded for the *selection logic* in part (b). Don't lose those 4 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** What is the **BET equation**? State the assumptions and derive the linear form of the BET equation for multilayer adsorption. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The BET plot obtained from a nitrogen adsorption experiment on a silica gel sample at 77 K yields the following linear fit:
$$
\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = 1.20 \times 10^{-3} + 4.50 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0} \quad \text{(units: g/cm}^3\text{)}
$$
Given: cross-sectional area of N₂ molecule = $16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m², molar volume at STP = 22414 cm³/mol. Calculate the **BET monolayer volume** and the **specific surface area** of the silica gel. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The BET Equation (1 Mark):**
The **Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation** describes multilayer physical adsorption on a solid surface and is the standard method to determine the **specific surface area** of porous materials.

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**
1. Surface is **energetically homogeneous** (all sites have equal energy).
2. Molecules adsorb in **successive layers** with no limit on the number of layers.
3. The **first layer** has heat of adsorption $Q_1$; **all subsequent layers** have heat of adsorption equal to the **heat of liquefaction** $Q_L$.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Let $\theta_i$ be the fraction of surface covered by exactly $i$ layers. The dynamic equilibrium condition for each layer is:

For the first layer:
$$
a_1 P \, \theta_0 = b_1 \, \theta_1 \, e^{Q_1 / RT}
$$

For layers $i \geq 2$:
$$
a_i P \, \theta_{i-1} = b_i \, \theta_i \, e^{Q_L / RT}
$$

Defining $x = P/P_0$ and $C = \dfrac{a_1 b \, e^{(Q_1 - Q_L)/RT}}{a \, b_1}$, solving the recurrence relation:

$$
\theta_i = C \cdot \theta_0 \cdot x^i
$$

The site-conservation condition $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \theta_i = 1$ gives:

$$
\theta_0 = \dfrac{1 - x}{1 - x + C x}
$$

The total volume adsorbed per unit mass is the sum of contributions from all layers:

$$
V = V_m \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} i \, \theta_i
$$

Carrying out the summation and substituting $\theta_0$:

$$
V = V_m \cdot \dfrac{C x}{(1 - x)\left[1 + (C - 1)x\right]}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Identify slope and intercept (1 Mark).**

From the linear fit:
$$
\text{Intercept} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} = 1.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^3
$$
$$
\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C} = 4.50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 2: Compute monolayer volume $V_m$ (2 Marks).**

$$
V_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{Slope} + \text{Intercept}} = \dfrac{1}{(4.50 + 1.20) \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1}{5.70 \times 10^{-3}}
$$
$$
\boxed{V_m = 175.4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g (at STP)}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute BET constant $C$ (1 Mark).**

$$
C = 1 + \dfrac{\text{Slope}}{\text{Intercept}} = 1 + \dfrac{4.50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.20 \times 10^{-3}} = 1 + 3.75 = 4.75
$$

(C > 2 indicates strong adsorbate–adsorbent interaction, consistent with N₂ on silica gel.)

**Step 4: Compute moles of N₂ in the monolayer (1 Mark).**

$$
n_m = \dfrac{V_m}{22414} = \dfrac{175.4}{22414} = 7.825 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/g}
$$

**Step 5: Compute number of molecules per gram (1 Mark).**

$$
N = n_m \cdot N_A = 7.825 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 4.713 \times 10^{21} \text{ molecules/g}
$$

**Step 6: Compute BET specific surface area (1 Mark).**

$$
S_{BET} = N \cdot a_m = 4.713 \times 10^{21} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20} \text{ m}^2/\text{g}
$$
$$
\boxed{S_{BET} = 763.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}}
$$

This is consistent with the **typical surface area of silica gel (300–800 m²/g)**. ✔

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common error is to:
> 1. Forget the **factor of 22414 cm³/mol** for STP — writing 22.4 L/mol as **22,400 cm³/mol** is correct, but students often incorrectly use 22,400 instead of 22,414, leading to ~0.06% error (acceptable) — but using 22.4 directly instead of 22414 leads to a **1000× magnitude error** → full marks lost.
> 2. Forget to **convert $V_m$ from cm³ to molecules** via Avogadro's number before multiplying by $a_m$. Two-step unit conversion must be shown explicitly.
> 3. Forget to add slope + intercept when computing $V_m$. Many students invert the slope or intercept separately — this is **wrong**.

---

## 🚀 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your final 5-minute revision sheet before the exam.**

- ✅ **Adsorption = surface phenomenon**, *not* bulk penetration. Opposite is **desorption**.
- ✅ **Adsorbate** = the migrating molecule; **Adsorbent** = the solid receiving it.
- ✅ **Physisorption** = weak, reversible, multilayer, low $\Delta H$ (20–40 kJ/mol).
- ✅ **Chemisorption** = strong, often irreversible, monolayer, high $\Delta H$ (80–400 kJ/mol).
- ✅ **IUPAC Pore Classes:** Micro (< 2 nm), Meso (2–50 nm), Macro (> 50 nm).
- ✅ **Good industrial adsorbent** = Surface area ≥ 300 m²/g + high selectivity + pore match + mechanical stability.
- ✅ **Activated Carbon** = 500–1500 m²/g, microporous, hydrophobic, for organics and colour removal.
- ✅ **Silica Gel** = 300–800 m²/g, mesoporous, hydrophilic, for gas drying.
- ✅ **Activated Alumina** = 150–400 m²/g, polar, for fluoride/arsenic removal and acid gas drying.
- ✅ **Zeolites 3A/4A/5A/13X** = crystalline, 3/4/5/9 Å apertures, for size-selective gas separations and deep drying.
- ✅ **Polymeric resins (XAD)** = tunable surface, for pharmaceutical and VOC capture.
- ✅ **Biosorbents (chitosan)** = low cost, for heavy metal removal.
- ✅ **BET Equation** (linear form): $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$.
- ✅ **$V_m$** is obtained from $V_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{Slope} + \text{Intercept}}$.
- ✅ **BET surface area** is computed as $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot a_m}{V_{molar}}$.
- ✅ **N₂ cross-sectional area** = $16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m²; Molar volume at STP = 22414 cm³/mol.
- ✅ **Five IUPAC isotherm types** — Type I (microporous), Type IV (mesoporous, with hysteresis) are the most industrially relevant.
- ✅ **Adsorbent selection** is governed by **molecular size matching** (kinetic diameter vs pore aperture), **polarity matching** (polar surface for polar adsorbates), and **operating conditions** (thermal stability for regeneration temperature).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption Equilibrium - Freundlich Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adsorption Equilibrium - Freundlich Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adsorption Equilibrium** refers to the dynamic state established at the interface of two phases (typically a solid adsorbent and a fluid containing the adsorbate) when the rate of molecules adhering to the solid surface equals the rate of molecules leaving the surface (desorption). At this stage, the bulk concentration of the solute in the fluid phase remains constant at a value known as the **Equilibrium Concentration ($C_e$)**, while the corresponding solid-phase loading is the **Equilibrium Loading ($q_e$)**.

The **Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm** is an **empirical relationship** that describes the non-ideal, heterogeneous, and multilayer adsorption behavior of solutes onto solid surfaces. Unlike the Langmuir isotherm (which assumes a monolayer of identical sites), the Freundlich equation accounts for the fact that real solid surfaces possess active sites of varying affinities.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
q_e = K_f \cdot C_e^{1/n}
$$

Where:
- $q_e$ = equilibrium solid-phase loading (mass of solute adsorbed / mass of adsorbent)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium liquid-phase concentration of the solute
- $K_f$ = **Freundlich capacity constant** (an indicator of adsorption capacity)
- $1/n$ = **Freundlich intensity constant** (an indicator of surface heterogeneity and adsorption favorability)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Freundlich equation is strictly classified as an **empirical isotherm** in the KTU Module 3 syllabus. It does not possess a rigorous theoretical basis like the BET or Langmuir models. However, it remains the most commonly applied industrial correlation for low-to-moderate concentration ranges due to its simplicity and reliable curve-fitting behavior.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge Café

Imagine a large, multi-level sponge café where hungry guests (adsorbate molecules) arrive to occupy seats (adsorption sites) at tables (the adsorbent surface).

- **The Café Opens:** Guests start filling the available seats. Initially, the best window seats (high-energy, high-affinity sites) fill up first because they are the most attractive.
- **As the Café Fills:** Guests are forced to take less desirable seats—corner spots, bar stools, or cramped corners. These lower-affinity sites get occupied progressively as concentration rises.
- **The Equilibrium:** At any given moment, some guests leave their seats (desorption) and are immediately replaced by others arriving. The *net* occupancy remains fixed for a given crowd size outside the café.

The **Freundlich equation** is essentially the manager's logbook that says: *"The more crowded the street outside ($C_e$), the more guests we pack inside ($q_e$), but the rate of packing slows down at the corners."* The constant **$1/n < 1$** mathematically captures this diminishing affinity as surface sites get filled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:**
> - If $1/n = 1$ → Linear isotherm (constant partition, all sites identical).
> - If $1/n < 1$ → Favorable adsorption (heterogeneous surface, high affinity at low $C_e$).
> - If $1/n > 1$ → Unfavorable adsorption (cooperative adsorption or surface modification effects).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The Freundlich isotherm emerged from experimental observations by Herbert Freundlich (1909). The underlying physical logic rests on three pillars:

1. **Surface Heterogeneity:** Real adsorbents (activated carbon, silica gel, alumina, zeolites) possess active sites of *varying binding energies*. The exponential term $(1/n)$ is essentially a distribution function for these energies.
2. **Multilayer Adsorption:** It does not restrict adsorption to a single molecular layer. As bulk concentration rises, solute molecules can stack on already-adsorbed layers.
3. **No Saturation Plateau:** Unlike the Langmuir model, Freundlich predicts *unbounded* loading as $C_e \to \infty$. This is a mathematical limitation but holds true in the low-to-moderate concentration range typically encountered in industrial practice.

## 2.2 Dimensional Analysis and Boundary Behavior

| Parameter | Physical Meaning | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $q_e$ | Mass of adsorbate / Mass of adsorbent | 0 to 1000 mg/g | Determines adsorbent dosage in column design |
| $C_e$ | Equilibrium residual concentration | 0 to saturation | Design basis for effluent discharge compliance |
| $K_f$ | Freundlich capacity constant | 0.01 to 1000 | Higher $K_f$ indicates greater adsorption capacity |
| $1/n$ | Heterogeneity / Intensity factor | 0 to 1 (favorable) | Lower $1/n$ indicates stronger surface affinity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Check (KTU Examiner's favorite):**
> When $C_e = 0$, $q_e = 0$. When $C_e = 1$ (in the chosen units), $q_e = K_f$. This is why $K_f$ is *not dimensionless*; its units depend on the exponent $1/n$.

## 2.3 Linearization for Graphical Determination

Direct plotting of $q_e$ vs $C_e$ yields a non-linear curve (a power function). For experimental data reduction, the equation is linearized by taking logarithms (base 10 or natural log) of both sides:

$$
\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_f) + \frac{1}{n} \ln(C_e)
$$

This is a straight line of the form $y = mx + c$:
- **Y-axis:** $\ln(q_e)$
- **X-axis:** $\ln(C_e)$
- **Slope:** $1/n$
- **Intercept:** $\ln(K_f)$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application | Adsorbent Used |
|---|---|---|
| Water Treatment | Removal of color, odor, micropollutants | Activated Carbon |
| Pharmaceutical | Decolorization of antibiotics, vitamins | Activated Carbon, Resins |
| Petrochemical | Solvent recovery, hydrocarbon separation | Silica Gel, Zeolite 13X |
| Food Processing | Sugar refining, juice clarification | Activated Carbon, Bentonite |
| Air Purification | VOC removal, mercury capture | Impregnated Activated Carbon |

The Freundlich equation is extensively used in the design of **fixed-bed adsorbers** and **batch contactors** to predict breakthrough behavior and determine adsorbent dosage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linearized Form

**Step 1:** Start with the fundamental Freundlich equation in its exponential form.

$$
q_e = K_f \cdot C_e^{1/n}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the natural logarithm ($\ln$) to both sides. This step is mathematically valid because both $q_e$ and $C_e$ are strictly positive quantities in any real adsorption system.

$$
\ln(q_e) = \ln\left(K_f \cdot C_e^{1/n}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the logarithmic product rule: $\ln(a \cdot b) = \ln(a) + \ln(b)$.

$$
\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_f) + \ln(C_e^{1/n})
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the logarithmic power rule: $\ln(x^a) = a \cdot \ln(x)$.

$$
\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_f) + \frac{1}{n} \cdot \ln(C_e)
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the structure of a straight line: $Y = mX + C$.

$$
\underbrace{\ln(q_e)}_{Y} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{n}}_{m} \cdot \underbrace{\ln(C_e)}_{X} + \underbrace{\ln(K_f)}_{C}
$$

**Step 6:** Identify the parameter extraction strategy.
- Plot experimental $\ln(q_e)$ versus $\ln(C_e)$.
- Perform linear regression to obtain the slope ($m$) and intercept ($C$).
- Compute the intensity parameter: $\dfrac{1}{n} = m$.
- Compute the capacity parameter: $K_f = e^{C}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Full KTU Valuation Style

**Problem Statement:** In a batch adsorption experiment for the removal of phenol from wastewater using activated carbon, the following equilibrium data was obtained at $30^{\circ}\text{C}$:

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) |
|---|---|
| 0.5 | 0.62 |
| 1.0 | 0.94 |
| 2.5 | 1.65 |
| 5.0 | 2.51 |
| 10.0 | 3.80 |
| 25.0 | 6.45 |

**Determine the Freundlich constants $K_f$ and $1/n$.**

**Step 1:** Compute the natural logarithms of both $C_e$ and $q_e$.

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | $\ln(C_e)$ | $q_e$ (mg/g) | $\ln(q_e)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | $-0.6931$ | 0.62 | $-0.4780$ |
| 1.0 | $0.0000$ | 0.94 | $-0.0619$ |
| 2.5 | $0.9163$ | 1.65 | $0.5008$ |
| 5.0 | $1.6094$ | 2.51 | $0.9203$ |
| 10.0 | $2.3026$ | 3.80 | $1.3350$ |
| 25.0 | $3.2189$ | 6.45 | $1.8641$ |

**Step 2:** Apply the linearized Freundlich equation to compute the slope.

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{N \sum (\ln C_e \cdot \ln q_e) - \sum \ln C_e \sum \ln q_e}{N \sum (\ln C_e)^2 - (\sum \ln C_e)^2}
$$

Where $N = 6$.

**Step 3:** Compute the individual sums.

$$
\sum \ln C_e = -0.6931 + 0.0000 + 0.9163 + 1.6094 + 2.3026 + 3.2189 = 7.3541
$$

$$
\sum \ln q_e = -0.4780 + (-0.0619) + 0.5008 + 0.9203 + 1.3350 + 1.8641 = 4.0803
$$

$$
\sum (\ln C_e)^2 = 0.4804 + 0.0000 + 0.8396 + 2.5902 + 5.3019 + 10.3613 = 19.5734
$$

$$
\sum (\ln C_e \cdot \ln q_e) = (0.3314) + (0.0000) + (0.4589) + (1.4810) + (3.0739) + (6.0000) = 11.3452
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the slope formula.

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{(6)(11.3452) - (7.3541)(4.0803)}{(6)(19.5734) - (7.3541)^2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{68.0712 - 30.0078}{117.4404 - 54.0828}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{38.0634}{63.3576} = 0.6008
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the intercept using the mean values.

$$
\overline{\ln C_e} = \frac{7.3541}{6} = 1.2257
$$

$$
\overline{\ln q_e} = \frac{4.0803}{6} = 0.6801
$$

$$
\ln K_f = \overline{\ln q_e} - \frac{1}{n} \cdot \overline{\ln C_e}
$$

$$
\ln K_f = 0.6801 - (0.6008)(1.2257) = 0.6801 - 0.7363 = -0.0562
$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate to obtain $K_f$.

$$
K_f = e^{-0.0562} = 0.9453
$$

**Step 7:** Final Freundlich Equation.

$$
q_e = 0.9453 \cdot C_e^{0.6008}
$$

**Step 8 (Verification Check):** Predict $q_e$ at $C_e = 10$ mg/L.

$$
q_e = 0.9453 \cdot (10)^{0.6008} = 0.9453 \cdot 3.987 = 3.77 \text{ mg/g}
$$

Experimental value = 3.80 mg/g. **Percentage error ≈ 0.79%** → Excellent fit, confirming the validity of the Freundlich model for this dataset.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Experimental Determination of Freundlich Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Wastewater Sample with Solute Concentration C0"] --> B["Prepare Multiple Bottles with Equal Mass of Adsorbent m"]
    B --> C["Add Varying Volumes of Solute Solution"]
    C --> D["Agitate at Constant Temperature for Equilibrium Time t_eq"]
    D --> E["Filter to Separate Solid and Liquid Phases"]
    E --> F["Analyze Filtrate for Equilibrium Concentration C_e"]
    F --> G["Compute Solid Phase Loading q_e from Mass Balance"]
    G --> H["Tabulate Pairs of C_e and q_e Values"]
    H --> I["Take Natural Log of Both C_e and q_e"]
    I --> J["Plot ln q_e versus ln C_e on Linear Graph Paper"]
    J --> K{"Is Linear Fit Acceptable? R2 above 0.95?"}
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Extract Slope equals 1 by n"]
    L --> M["Extract Intercept equals ln K_f"]
    M --> N["Final Freundlich Equation q_e equals K_f times C_e to power 1 by n"]
    K -- "No" --> O["Recheck Data, Consider Langmuir or BET Models"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Adsorption Isotherm Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        IN1["Bulk Concentration C0 mg per L"]
        IN2["Adsorbent Mass m grams"]
        IN3["Equilibrium Data Pairs C_e and q_e"]
    end

    subgraph Engine["MODEL SELECTION ENGINE"]
        M1["Check Surface Homogeneity Assumption"]
        M2["Check Concentration Range Domain"]
        M3["Compute Correlation Coefficient R2"]
    end

    subgraph Output["ISOTHERM OUTPUT MODULE"]
        O1["Langmuir Model q_max and b"]
        O2["Freundlich Model K_f and 1 by n"]
        O3["BET Model q_B and C_BET"]
    end

    Input --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 -- "Heterogeneous Surface" --> O2
    M3 -- "Homogeneous Monolayer" --> O1
    M3 -- "Multilayer at High P by P0" --> O3
```

## 4.3 Log-Log Plot Schematic — Visual Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Origin X axis: ln C_e, Y axis: ln q_e"]
    P2["Plot all data points as scatter markers"]
    P3["Draw best fit straight line through points"]
    P4["Identify slope m equals 1 by n"]
    P5["Identify Y intercept C equals ln K_f"]
    P6["Interpretation: m less than 1 means favorable adsorption"]
    P7["Interpretation: m equals 1 means linear partitioning"]
    P8["Interpretation: m greater than 1 means unfavorable adsorption"]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P6
    P4 --> P7
    P4 --> P8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**State the Freundlich adsorption isotherm and explain the physical significance of the constants $K_f$ and $1/n$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Freundlich adsorption isotherm is given by $q_e = K_f \cdot C_e^{1/n}$, where $q_e$ is the equilibrium solid-phase loading and $C_e$ is the equilibrium liquid-phase concentration. The constant **$K_f$** is the Freundlich capacity constant, which indicates the relative adsorption capacity of the adsorbent; a higher $K_f$ implies greater adsorptive capacity. The constant **$1/n$** is the intensity parameter, which reflects surface heterogeneity and adsorption favorability. A value of $1/n$ between 0 and 1 indicates favorable adsorption, with smaller values denoting stronger affinity between the solute and the adsorbent surface. (3 Marks)

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Why is the Freundlich isotherm classified as an empirical equation? Mention its major limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Freundlich equation is classified as empirical because it was derived purely from experimental observations of adsorption data by Herbert Freundlich in 1909 and lacks a rigorous thermodynamic or kinetic foundation based on first principles. It does not derive from assumptions about monolayer coverage, interaction energies, or molecular orientations. **Its major limitation** is that it does not predict a finite saturation capacity; mathematically, as $C_e$ approaches infinity, $q_e$ also approaches infinity. This violates the physical reality that a finite adsorbent surface can hold only a finite mass of solute. Hence, the Freundlich model is valid only within the experimentally calibrated concentration range and fails at very high concentrations approaching monolayer saturation. (3 Marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**a)** Derive the linearized form of the Freundlich adsorption isotherm. Explain how the constants $K_f$ and $1/n$ are determined graphically. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** The following equilibrium data was obtained for the adsorption of a dye on activated carbon at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$:

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | 1 | 2 | 4 | 8 | 16 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_e$ (mg/g) | 1.2 | 1.8 | 2.6 | 3.7 | 5.2 |

Determine the Freundlich isotherm constants $K_f$ and $1/n$. Predict the equilibrium loading at an effluent concentration of $C_e = 32$ mg/L. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a):

The fundamental Freundlich equation in exponential form is:

$$
q_e = K_f \cdot C_e^{1/n}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_f) + \frac{1}{n} \ln(C_e)
$$

This is a straight line with $\ln(C_e)$ as the abscissa and $\ln(q_e)$ as the ordinate. The slope of the line equals $1/n$ and the intercept equals $\ln(K_f)$. Graphically, the experimental data is plotted as $\ln(q_e)$ versus $\ln(C_e)$, a best-fit straight line is drawn, and the constants are extracted from the slope and intercept. (7 Marks)

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1:** Compute $\ln(C_e)$ and $\ln(q_e)$ for each data point.

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | $\ln(C_e)$ | $q_e$ (mg/g) | $\ln(q_e)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0000 | 1.2 | 0.1823 |
| 2 | 0.6931 | 1.8 | 0.5878 |
| 4 | 1.3863 | 2.6 | 0.9555 |
| 8 | 2.0794 | 3.7 | 1.3083 |
| 16 | 2.7726 | 5.2 | 1.6487 |

**Step 2:** Apply the least-squares formula for slope.

$$
\sum \ln C_e = 0.0000 + 0.6931 + 1.3863 + 2.0794 + 2.7726 = 6.9314
$$

$$
\sum \ln q_e = 0.1823 + 0.5878 + 0.9555 + 1.3083 + 1.6487 = 4.6826
$$

$$
\sum (\ln C_e)^2 = 0.0000 + 0.4804 + 1.9218 + 4.3239 + 7.6873 = 14.4134
$$

$$
\sum (\ln C_e)(\ln q_e) = 0.0000 + 0.4074 + 1.3246 + 2.7207 + 4.5709 = 9.0236
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the slope formula with $N = 5$.

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{(5)(9.0236) - (6.9314)(4.6826)}{(5)(14.4134) - (6.9314)^2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{45.1180 - 32.4559}{72.0670 - 48.0443}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{12.6621}{24.0227} = 0.5271
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the intercept.

$$
\overline{\ln C_e} = \frac{6.9314}{5} = 1.3863
$$

$$
\overline{\ln q_e} = \frac{4.6826}{5} = 0.9365
$$

$$
\ln K_f = 0.9365 - (0.5271)(1.3863) = 0.9365 - 0.7308 = 0.2057
$$

$$
K_f = e^{0.2057} = 1.2284
$$

**Step 5:** Final Freundlich equation:

$$
q_e = 1.2284 \cdot C_e^{0.5271}
$$

**Step 6:** Prediction at $C_e = 32$ mg/L:

$$
q_e = 1.2284 \cdot (32)^{0.5271} = 1.2284 \cdot 6.378 = 7.83 \text{ mg/g}
$$

Therefore, the equilibrium loading at $C_e = 32$ mg/L is approximately **$7.83$ mg/g**. (7 Marks)

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**a)** Compare the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherms with respect to their underlying assumptions, mathematical form, and physical predictions. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** An industrial effluent containing $50$ mg/L of a toxic organic compound is treated with activated carbon in a batch contactor. The equilibrium isotherm follows the Freundlich equation $q_e = 2.5 \cdot C_e^{0.45}$. Calculate the equilibrium concentration of the solute and the amount adsorbed if $0.4$ g of activated carbon is added to $1$ L of effluent. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a):

| Feature | Langmuir Isotherm | Freundlich Isotherm |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Theoretical (1918) | Empirical (1909) |
| Assumptions | Monolayer coverage, identical sites, no interactions | Heterogeneous surface, multilayer, exponential energy distribution |
| Mathematical Form | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$ | $q_e = K_f C_e^{1/n}$ |
| Saturation Behavior | Predicts finite saturation capacity $q_m$ | Does not predict saturation; loading increases indefinitely |
| Concentration Range | Valid over wider range including saturation | Valid only at low to moderate $C_e$ |
| Surface Energy | Single uniform binding energy | Exponential distribution of binding energies |
| Number of Constants | 2 ($q_m$, $b$) | 2 ($K_f$, $1/n$) |

**Engineering Preference:** Freundlich is preferred for rough industrial screening and heterogeneous surfaces like activated carbon. Langmuir is preferred for crystalline or homogeneous adsorbents and when monolayer saturation data is required. (7 Marks)

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Given:**
- Initial concentration $C_0 = 50$ mg/L
- Mass of adsorbent $m = 0.4$ g
- Volume of solution $V = 1$ L
- Freundlich equation: $q_e = 2.5 \cdot C_e^{0.45}$

**Step 1:** Write the mass balance equation. The amount of solute removed from solution equals the amount adsorbed on the solid.

$$
q_e \cdot m = (C_0 - C_e) \cdot V
$$

$$
q_e = \frac{V (C_0 - C_e)}{m} = \frac{1 \cdot (50 - C_e)}{0.4} = 250 - 2.5 C_e
$$

**Step 2:** Equate with the Freundlich isotherm expression.

$$
2.5 \cdot C_e^{0.45} = 250 - 2.5 C_e
$$

$$
C_e^{0.45} = 100 - C_e
$$

**Step 3:** Solve by iterative numerical method. Let $f(C_e) = C_e^{0.45} + C_e - 100 = 0$.

| Trial | $C_e$ (mg/L) | $C_e^{0.45} + C_e$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 8.74 + 50 = 58.74 |
| 2 | 80 | 10.99 + 80 = 90.99 |
| 3 | 90 | 11.51 + 90 = 101.51 |
| 4 | 88 | 11.37 + 88 = 99.37 |
| 5 | 88.3 | 11.39 + 88.3 = 99.69 |
| 6 | 88.5 | 11.41 + 88.5 = 99.91 |
| 7 | 88.55 | 11.41 + 88.55 = 99.96 |

**Step 4:** Converged solution: $C_e \approx 88.55$ mg/L.

**Step 5:** Compute $q_e$.

$$
q_e = 250 - 2.5 \times 88.55 = 250 - 221.38 = 28.62 \text{ mg/g}
$$

**Verification:** Using Freundlich directly: $q_e = 2.5 \cdot (88.55)^{0.45} = 2.5 \times 11.41 = 28.53$ mg/g ✓ (Matches within rounding tolerance).

Therefore, the equilibrium concentration is approximately **$C_e = 88.55$ mg/L** and the amount adsorbed is **$q_e \approx 28.6$ mg/g**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Freundlich Equation:**
> - **Major Loss Point 1:** Students frequently write the equation in the wrong form ($q_e = K_f \cdot C_e^{-1/n}$ or with the exponent inverted). Always write $q_e = K_f \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ exactly as per the textbook convention.
> - **Major Loss Point 2:** When $C_e$ has units (mg/L), the constant $K_f$ is *not dimensionless*. Students often forget to attach units to $K_f$, leading to unit mismatch errors. For $n = 2$, units of $K_f$ are L/mg; for general $n$, $K_f$ has units of (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$.
> - **Major Loss Point 3:** In numerical problems, students forget to compute both the slope and intercept of the log-log plot. KTU examiners expect the full regression calculation, not just a graphical estimate.
> - **Major Loss Point 4:** Confusing the meaning of $1/n$. Higher $1/n$ does **not** mean higher capacity — it indicates *less favorable* adsorption (closer to linear or unfavorable regime).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> ⭐ **Freundlich Equation Master Form:** $q_e = K_f \cdot C_e^{1/n}$
>
> ⭐ **Linearized Plot Form:** $\ln(q_e) = \ln(K_f) + (1/n) \cdot \ln(C_e)$ — Straight line on log-log axes.
>
> ⭐ **Graphical Extraction:** Slope of log-log plot $= 1/n$; Intercept $= \ln(K_f)$.
>
> ⭐ **Favorability Rule:** $0 < 1/n < 1$ for favorable adsorption; $1/n = 1$ for linear (partition); $1/n > 1$ for unfavorable.
>
> ⭐ **Units Warning:** $K_f$ is *not dimensionless*; its units depend on the exponent $1/n$.
>
> ⭐ **Empirical Nature:** The Freundlich isotherm has no theoretical derivation from first principles — it is purely curve-fitted from experimental data.
>
> ⭐ **Limitation:** No saturation plateau; invalid at very high $C_e$ approaching monolayer capacity.
>
> ⭐ **Valid Domain:** Low to moderate equilibrium concentrations (typically $C_e < 0.5 \times$ solubility).
>
> ⭐ **Industrial Use:** Dominant isotherm for activated carbon adsorption in water/wastewater treatment.
>
> ⭐ **Common Mistake:** Using $q_e = K_f / C_e^{n}$ instead of $q_e = K_f \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ — pay close attention to the **positive** exponent convention.
>
> ⭐ **Mass Balance Companion Equation:** $q_e \cdot m = V \cdot (C_0 - C_e)$ — used in batch contactor design problems to couple with the isotherm equation.
>
> ⭐ **Comparison Anchor:** Langmuir assumes monolayer + uniform sites; Freundlich assumes multilayer + heterogeneous sites.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment Operation - Adsorption Column Dynamics - Fixed Bed and Agitated Bed Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equipment Operation - Adsorption Column Dynamics - Fixed Bed and Agitated Bed Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 1.1 Adsorption Column Dynamics — Formal KTU Definition

**Adsorption Column Dynamics** is the branch of separation technology that studies the **time-dependent mass transfer behavior of solute molecules** between a fluid phase and a stationary (or moving) solid adsorbent phase inside an industrial contactor. The dynamics govern how an adsorption front propagates through a bed, how a **breakthrough curve** is generated, and how the **sorbent capacity is utilized** before regeneration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Board Examiner Wording):**
> *Adsorption column dynamics refers to the transient unsteady-state operation of an adsorption bed in which the concentration of solute in the effluent changes with time, characterized by a breakthrough curve and a Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ).*

## 🧠 1.2 The Two Principal Equipment Geometries

| # | Equipment Type | Mode of Operation | Industrial Tag |
|---|----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Fixed Bed Adsorber** | Continuous fluid flow over a *stationary* packed bed | **Type I** |
| 2 | **Agitated Bed (Slurry) Adsorber** | *Discontinuous* solid–fluid contact in a stirred tank | **Type II** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Stress Point:** The syllabus explicitly classifies the equipment as **Fixed Bed** and **Agitated Bed** — *moving/fluidized beds* (e.g., FCC regenerators) are mentioned only for comparison, not for derivation.

## 🌟 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Coffee Filter" Analogy for Fixed Bed:**
> Imagine pouring contaminated water through a tall column of activated charcoal. The top layer of charcoal becomes saturated first, but the dirty water still emerges *clean* for a long time — because clean, fresh charcoal is still available below. Eventually, the saturation front (a thin, dark-stained band) reaches the bottom, and dirty water *suddenly* appears in your collection beaker. The *time* between the start of the run and the appearance of contamination in the effluent is the **breakthrough time**, and the corresponding concentration profile is the **breakthrough curve**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Washing Machine" Analogy for Agitated Bed:**
> Picture doing laundry — you dump dirty clothes (the adsorbent) into a tub of detergent-laden water (the solvent/solute), stir it, and after some time, the dirt is removed. The clothes are *not held in a column*; instead, the contact is **batch-wise and turbulent**. This perfectly mirrors an **agitated slurry adsorber** where a powdered adsorbent is dispersed in a fluid, mixed thoroughly, and later separated by filtration or sedimentation.

## 🔑 1.4 The Three Zones in a Fixed-Bed Adsorber (Mandatory KTU Term)

When a solute-laden fluid flows downward through a fixed bed of fresh adsorbent, **three distinct zones** develop:

1. **Equilibrated (Saturated) Zone** — *Top of the bed*. The solid is in **equilibrium** with the inlet concentration $C_0$. No further uptake possible.
2. **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) / Adsorption Zone** — *A narrow migrating band* where the bulk of the concentration change occurs.
3. **Fresh (Virgin) Zone** — *Bottom of the bed*. Solid is *still capable* of adsorbing solute; effluent concentration $\approx 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **MTZ travels down the column as a wave** of constant shape (under constant inlet conditions). When the MTZ first reaches the bed exit, *breakthrough* begins; when it leaves the column, *exhaustion* is reached.

## 📊 1.5 Standard Operating Parameters (with Bold Numerical Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics for KTU Problems:**
> - **Superficial velocity** ($u_s$): **0.1 – 0.3 m/s**
> - **Bed porosity** ($\varepsilon$): **0.35 – 0.45**
> - **Particle diameter** ($d_p$): **0.5 – 3 mm**
> - **Bed height (L) to particle diameter ratio**: $\frac{L}{d_p} > \mathbf{50}$ (to avoid channeling)
> - **Residence time** ($\tau$): typically **5 – 60 min** per cycle
> - **Regeneration temperature**: **100 – 350 °C** (thermal swing) or **elevated pressure** (pressure swing)

## 🎓 1.6 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for KTU 2024

| KTU Course Outcome | Bloom's Level | Topic Coverage |
|--------------------|---------------|----------------|
| **CO3** — Design adsorption equipment | Apply / Analyze | Fixed bed sizing, LUB calculation |
| **CO4** — Compare sorption processes | Understand | Fixed vs. Agitated bed selection |
| **CO5** — Evaluate column performance | Evaluate | Breakthrough curve interpretation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 2.1 Detailed Working of a Fixed-Bed Adsorber

The fluid (gas or liquid) containing the solute is fed continuously at the top, and clean fluid emerges at the bottom. As time progresses, the system exhibits **unsteady-state** behavior.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Mass Transfer Logic

- **Step 1 — Initial Contact:** Fluid enters the column. The topmost layer of adsorbent particles begins to capture solute molecules via **external film diffusion**, then **intraparticle pore diffusion**, then **surface adsorption**.

- **Step 2 — Saturation Wave Formation:** After a short lead time, the top layer reaches equilibrium ($q = q^*$). A sharp concentration gradient develops.

- **Step 3 — Propagation of the MTZ:** The saturated region grows downward. The MTZ is a *self-sharpening* (favorable equilibrium) or *spreading* (unfavorable equilibrium) wave that travels at a velocity lower than the fluid velocity.

- **Step 4 — Breakthrough:** The leading edge of the MTZ just reaches the bed exit. Effluent concentration rises above the desired limit $C_b$.

- **Step 5 — Exhaustion:** The trailing edge of the MTZ also exits. Effluent concentration equals inlet $C_0$; the bed is fully spent.

- **Step 6 — Regeneration / Cycle Closure:** The bed is taken offline and regenerated (thermal, pressure, or concentration swing). A second adsorber is brought online — this is the **lead–lag arrangement** common in industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MTZ Matters (Examiner's View):** The MTZ length determines the *unused capacity* of the bed at breakthrough. A short MTZ means high utilization; a long MTZ means significant bed remains unused.

## 🧮 2.2 Derivation of the Stoichiometric (Ideal) Front Velocity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation — Stoichiometric Time ($\tau_s$):** This is the *theoretical* time required for a sharp adsorption front to travel the *entire* bed length.

Consider a solute balance over the entire bed. The total solute fed from $t = 0$ to $t = \tau_s$ must equal the *maximum stoichiometric capacity* of the bed:

$$u_s \, C_0 \, \tau_s \, A_c = L \, A_c \, \rho_b \, q_\infty$$

Solving for $\tau_s$:

$$\boxed{\;\tau_s = \dfrac{L \, \rho_b \, q_\infty}{u_s \, C_0}\;}$$

where:
- $L$ = bed length (m)
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density of adsorbent (kg/m³)
- $q_\infty$ = equilibrium loading at $C_0$ (kg solute / kg adsorbent)
- $u_s$ = superficial velocity (m/s)
- $C_0$ = inlet solute concentration (kg/m³)
- $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of bed (m²)

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** $\tau_s$ is the time you would get if the bed behaved *ideally* (i.e., the MTZ had zero length). In reality, breakthrough occurs *before* $\tau_s$ because the front is not infinitely sharp.

## 📈 2.3 The Michaels Equation for Length of Unused Bed (LUB)

The breakthrough curve is approximately **S-shaped** (sigmoidal). Michaels (1952) developed the LUB concept by drawing a horizontal line at the breakpoint ratio and a tangent at the inflection point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation — Length of Unused Bed (LUB):**

$$\boxed{\;LUB = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b^*}{\tau_s}\right)\;}$$

where $t_b^*$ is the *equivalent stoichiometric time* corresponding to the breakpoint. The actual usable capacity is:

$$\boxed{\;LUB = L \left(\dfrac{\tau_s - t_b}{\tau_s - t_b(1-f)}\right)\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In words:** LUB is the height of bed that is *still virgin* at the moment of breakthrough. Designers multiply LUB by a **safety factor of 1.1 – 1.5** for industrial design.

## 📉 2.4 Operating Line (Mass Balance) for a Differential Bed Element

A differential mass balance on a slice of bed $dz$ over time $dt$ yields:

$$\boxed{\;u_s \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} + \rho_b \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} + \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{ax} \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}\;}$$

This is the **Axial Dispersion Model (ADM)**. Under the common KTU assumption of *plug flow* ($D_{ax} \to 0$) and *negligible fluid-phase accumulation* ($\varepsilon \ll \rho_b$):

$$\boxed{\;u_s \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} = -\rho_b \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t}\;}$$

## 🧪 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **All formulas below are examinable for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam).**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Valid For |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | $\tau_s = \dfrac{L \, \rho_b \, q_\infty}{u_s \, C_0}$ | Stoichiometric (ideal) breakthrough time | s | Sharp-front assumption |
| 2 | $LUB = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{\tau_s}\right)$ | Length of unused bed | m | Ideal-front (Michaels) |
| 3 | $HTU = \dfrac{u_s}{K_c \, a_v}$ | Height of a transfer unit | m | Linear driving force |
| 4 | $NTU = \int_{C_b}^{C_e} \dfrac{dC}{C - C^*}$ | Number of transfer units | – | Integral form |
| 5 | $H = HTU \times NTU$ | Total bed height (HETP analog) | m | Design equation |
| 6 | $t_b^* = \int_0^{t_b} \left(1 - \dfrac{C}{C_0}\right) dt$ | Equivalent stoichiometric time at breakpoint | s | Real breakthrough |
| 7 | $W = u_s \, C_0 \, A_c \, t_b$ | Total solute adsorbed (working capacity) | kg | Industrial throughput |
| 8 | $\Delta q = q^* - q_i$ | Change in solid loading | kg/kg | Regeneration calc. |
| 9 | $\tau_c = \dfrac{L}{u_s}$ | Fluid residence time in empty bed | s | Sizing |
| 10 | $Pe = \dfrac{u_s \, d_p}{D_{ax}}$ | Péclet number (axial dispersion) | – | Flow regime check |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency Pitfall:** Always convert $q_\infty$ from (kg/kg) to a volumetric basis *or* keep $\rho_b$ in the equation. Mixing $\rho_s$ (particle density) with $\rho_b$ (bulk density) costs **2 marks** in ESE.

## 🏭 2.6 Industrial Application Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> - **Fixed-bed adsorbers** dominate: water purification (GAC columns), air drying (silica gel towers), solvent recovery (activated carbon), sugar decolorization, hydrogen purification (molecular sieves), and nuclear iodine removal.
> - **Agitated-bed (slurry) adsorbers** dominate: pharmaceutical purification, dye removal from textile effluent, gold recovery via activated carbon (CIP/CIL in mining), and batch decolorization of vegetable oils.

## ⚙️ 2.7 Working Principle of an Agitated-Bed (Slurry) Adsorber

The slurry adsorber is essentially a **stirred-tank contactor** (similar to a CSTR) in which:

1. Powdered or granular adsorbent is suspended in the liquid feed.
2. A mechanical impeller (Pfaudler, Rushton, propeller) provides **turbulent mixing**.
3. Mass transfer occurs from liquid → solid surface via film diffusion.
4. After a calculated **contact time**, the slurry is pumped to a filter, screen, or settler to recover the loaded adsorbent.
5. The adsorbent is regenerated and recycled.

### 2.7.1 Mass Transfer Equation for an Agitated Slurry Tank

For a *well-mixed* batch slurry adsorber, the governing ODE is:

$$V \, \dfrac{dC}{dt} = -K_L \, a_v \, (C - C^*) \, V$$

Integrating with constant $K_L a_v$:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{C_t - C^*}{C_0 - C^*} = \exp(-K_L \, a_v \, t)\;}$$

For a *continuous stirred tank adsorber* (CSTA) operating at steady state:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{C_\text{out}}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L \, a_v \, \tau}\;}$$

where $\tau = V/Q$ is the mean residence time of the liquid.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stoichiometric Breakthrough Time ($\tau_s$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Principle (KTU Board Expectation):** *In a fixed-bed adsorber, the total mass of solute that can be adsorbed equals the total mass of solute fed to the column until the stoichiometric (ideal) breakthrough time.*

### Step 1 — Total Mass of Solute Fed

Fluid enters with concentration $C_0$ and superficial velocity $u_s$ through a bed of cross-section $A_c$ for a duration $\tau_s$:

$$M_\text{fed} = u_s \, C_0 \, A_c \, \tau_s$$

### Step 2 — Total Mass of Solute Adsorbed at Equilibrium

The bed has volume $L \cdot A_c$ and bulk density $\rho_b$. The mass of solid is $\rho_b \, L \, A_c$. The maximum loading per unit mass is $q_\infty$:

$$M_\text{ads} = \rho_b \, L \, A_c \, q_\infty$$

### Step 3 — Equating Feed to Adsorption (Material Balance Closure)

$$u_s \, C_0 \, A_c \, \tau_s = \rho_b \, L \, A_c \, q_\infty$$

### Step 4 — Cancelling the Cross-Sectional Area $A_c$

$$u_s \, C_0 \, \tau_s = \rho_b \, L \, q_\infty$$

### Step 5 — Solving for $\tau_s$

$$\tau_s = \dfrac{\rho_b \, L \, q_\infty}{u_s \, C_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\;\tau_s = \dfrac{\rho_b \, L \, q_\infty}{u_s \, C_0} \quad \text{[seconds]}\;}$$

This is the *theoretical upper limit* of breakthrough time. The *actual* breakthrough time $t_b$ is always **less** than $\tau_s$ because of the finite width of the MTZ.

---

## 🧮 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Length of the Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)

The MTZ length is the distance between the breakthrough point ($C/C_0 = 0.05$ to $0.10$) and the exhaustion point ($C/C_0 = 0.90$ to $0.95$).

### Step 1 — Fraction of Bed Utilized at Breakthrough

The fractional capacity used up to time $t_b$ is the ratio of the *area under the breakthrough curve up to $t_b$* to the *area up to $\tau_s$* (i.e., the total area under the S-curve).

Define the **breakpoint fraction** $f_b$:

$$f_b = \dfrac{\text{Area under breakthrough curve from 0 to } t_b}{\text{Total area under curve from 0 to } \tau_s}$$

### Step 2 — Mass Transfer Zone Length (Michaels, 1952)

Michaels showed that the MTZ length is:

$$MTZ = L \left(1 - \dfrac{f_b \cdot t_b^*}{\tau_s}\right)$$

After substituting the equivalent stoichiometric time at the breakpoint $t_b^* = \int_0^{t_b} (1 - C/C_0) \, dt$:

$$MTZ = L \left(1 - \dfrac{\int_0^{t_b} (1 - C/C_0) \, dt}{\tau_s}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Simplification to a Common Form

Recognizing that $\int_0^{t_b} (1 - C/C_0) \, dt = t_b - \int_0^{t_b} (C/C_0) \, dt = t_b^*$ (definition):

$$\boxed{\;MTZ = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b^*}{\tau_s}\right)\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** $t_b^* / \tau_s$ is the *fraction of stoichiometric capacity* used at the moment of breakthrough. The complement $(1 - t_b^* / \tau_s)$ is the *fractional length* of the bed that is *still unused* — this is the LUB.

---

## 🧮 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: HTU–NTU Model for Fixed-Bed Design

### Step 1 — Mass Transfer Flux Across a Differential Element

The local flux of solute from bulk fluid to the solid surface is driven by the concentration difference:

$$\dfrac{d(C \, u_s)}{dz} = -K_L \, a_v \, (C - C^*)$$

where:
- $K_L$ = overall liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $a_v$ = specific surface area of adsorbent (m²/m³)
- $C^*$ = fluid concentration in equilibrium with the local solid loading $q$

### Step 2 — Defining the HTU

The **Height of a Transfer Unit** is the bed length required to effect **one natural log of concentration change**:

$$HTU = \dfrac{u_s}{K_L \, a_v}$$

### Step 3 — Defining the NTU (Number of Transfer Units)

$$NTU = \int_{C_\text{in}}^{C_\text{out}} \dfrac{dC}{C - C^*}$$

For a clean bed, $C^* \approx 0$, simplifying to:

$$NTU = \ln \left(\dfrac{C_\text{in}}{C_\text{out}}\right) = \ln \left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Total Bed Height

$$\boxed{\;H = HTU \times NTU = \dfrac{u_s}{K_L \, a_v} \times \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_b}\right)\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU-preferred design equation** for a single fixed-bed adsorber. Many ESE questions (14-mark problems) are built on this equation.

---

## 🧮 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Continuous Stirred Tank Adsorber (CSTA)

### Step 1 — Steady-State Mass Balance

For a CSTA of volume $V$ with volumetric flow rate $Q$:

$$Q \, C_0 = Q \, C + K_L \, a_v \, V \, (C - C^*)$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Outlet Concentration

$$Q \, C_0 - Q \, C = K_L \, a_v \, V \, (C - C^*)$$

$$Q \, (C_0 - C) = K_L \, a_v \, V \, (C - C^*)$$

Divide by $Q$ and substitute $\tau = V/Q$:

$$C_0 - C = K_L \, a_v \, \tau \, (C - C^*)$$

### Step 3 — Rearranging for Outlet Concentration

$$C_0 = C + K_L \, a_v \, \tau \, (C - C^*) = C (1 + K_L \, a_v \, \tau) - K_L \, a_v \, \tau \, C^*$$

$$C (1 + K_L \, a_v \, \tau) = C_0 + K_L \, a_v \, \tau \, C^*$$

$$C = \dfrac{C_0 + K_L \, a_v \, \tau \, C^*}{1 + K_L \, a_v \, \tau}$$

### Step 4 — Linear Equilibrium Assumption ($C^* = 0$ for a fresh adsorbent)

For a *linear isotherm* with $C^* = m \, C$ (Henry's law):

$$C = \dfrac{C_0}{1 + K_L \, a_v \, \tau (1 - m)}$$

Or in fractional form:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{C}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L \, a_v \, \tau (1 - m)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As $\tau \to \infty$, $C/C_0 \to 0$ (perfect removal). As $\tau \to 0$, $C/C_0 \to 1$ (no removal). The *characteristic time* of the CSTA is $1/[K_L \, a_v (1-m)]$.

---

## 🧮 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation: Batch Agitated Adsorber (Integrated Form)

### Step 1 — Differential Mass Balance in the Slurry

$$V \dfrac{dC}{dt} = -K_L \, a_v \, V \, (C - C^*)$$

### Step 2 — For Linear Equilibrium ($C^* = m \, C$)

$$V \dfrac{dC}{dt} = -K_L \, a_v \, V \, (1 - m) \, C$$

### Step 3 — Separation of Variables

$$\dfrac{dC}{C} = -K_L \, a_v \, (1 - m) \, dt$$

### Step 4 — Integration

Integrating from $C_0$ at $t = 0$ to $C_t$ at $t = t$:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -K_L \, a_v \, (1 - m) \, t$$

### Step 5 — Exponential Decay Form

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{C_t}{C_0} = \exp[-K_L \, a_v \, (1 - m) \, t]\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Use:** Plot $\ln(C_t / C_0)$ versus $t$. The slope is $-K_L \, a_v (1-m)$, which is experimentally determined in a *bench-scale agitated flask* test before scale-up.

---

## 📊 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Fixed vs. Agitated Bed

| # | Parameter | Fixed Bed | Agitated Bed |
|---|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Flow regime** | Plug flow (PFR analog) | Back-mix (CSTR analog) |
| 2 | **Concentration vs. time** | Transient breakthrough curve | Exponential decay (batch) or single steady value (CSTR) |
| 3 | **Solid usage efficiency** | Lower (LUB → 10–30% unused) | Higher (close to equilibrium) |
| 4 | **Driving force** | High at top, low at bottom | Uniform (well-mixed) |
| 5 | **Equipment footprint** | Tall column (large L) | Compact tank |
| 6 | **Continuous operation** | Yes (with twin beds) | Inherently batch (CSTR variant is continuous) |
| 7 | **Scale-up criterion** | $L/d_p$ ≥ 50; uniform packing | Power number $N_p$, impeller tip speed |
| 8 | **Pressure drop** | Significant (Ergun equation) | Negligible |
| 9 | **Solid handling** | Static; no attrition | Mechanical attrition possible |
| 10 | **Best suited for** | High-purity polishing (gas/liquid) | Bulk impurity removal (slurries) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of a Fixed-Bed Adsorber

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture** showing the position of the MTZ, the three zones, and the breakthrough curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Inlet Fluid C0 Q]:::inlet
    B[Fresh Virgin Zone]:::virgin
    C[Mass Transfer Zone MTZ]:::mtz
    D[Equilibrated Saturated Zone]:::sat
    E[Outlet Fluid C less than Cb]:::outlet
    F[Breakthrough Curve Plot C vs t]:::plot
    G[Regeneration Loop TSA PSA]:::regen
    H[Cycle 1 Adsorb]:::cycle
    I[Cycle 2 Desorb]:::cycle2

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    C -.->|data| F
    E -.->|data| F
    D -.->|exhausted| G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> A

    classDef inlet fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef virgin fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mtz fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sat fill:#808080,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef outlet fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef plot fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef regen fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef cycle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef cycle2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 🗺️ 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Workflow of an Agitated-Bed Slurry Adsorber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Tank Solute C0]:::inlet
    B[Slurry Mixer Impeller Tank]:::mix
    C[Contact Time t seconds]:::time
    D[Filter or Settler]:::filter
    E[Clean Liquid Outlet]:::outlet
    F[Spent Adsorbent]:::spent
    G[Regeneration Column]:::regen
    H[Fresh Adsorbent Recycle]:::fresh

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> B

    classDef inlet fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mix fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef time fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef filter fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef outlet fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef spent fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef regen fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fresh fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 🗺️ 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Breakthrough Curve Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Phase I Initial Period 0 to t1]:::p1
    P2[Phase II Useful Service t1 to tb]:::p2
    P3[Phase III Breakthrough tb to te]:::p3
    P4[Phase IV Exhaustion te onward]:::p4
    P5[Effluent Concentration C out]:::out
    P6[Breakpoint Concentration Cb]:::bp
    P7[Inlet Concentration C0]:::c0

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P1 -.-> P5
    P2 -.-> P5
    P3 -.-> P5
    P4 -.-> P5

    classDef p1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef p2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000

```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Chart:** Phase II is the **useful adsorption service period** (the entire reason the unit is built). Phase III is the **transition** — operators must switch beds at the *end* of Phase II to avoid contamination. Phase IV means the bed is **fully exhausted** and must be regenerated.
