<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Direct, definition-based. No sub-parts. Answer must be 50–80 words with a labeled diagram or equation.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** 
**Define the Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) in a fixed-bed adsorber. Why is its width important in design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) is the narrow region in a fixed-bed adsorber where the bulk of the solute concentration drop occurs between the inlet $C_0$ and the effluent $C \approx 0$. It moves down the column as a wave of approximately constant shape. Its width is important because it determines the **Length of Unused Bed (LUB)** at breakthrough — a long MTZ means poor utilization of adsorbent capacity and frequent regeneration. A short MTZ (favorable isotherm, high $K_L a_v$) gives high working capacity. **(3 marks — MTZ definition 1.5 marks, importance 1.5 marks.)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
**Distinguish between a fixed-bed adsorber and an agitated-bed (slurry) adsorber in terms of solid-fluid contact pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Bed | Agitated Bed |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Contact pattern | Continuous plug flow | Batch back-mix |
| Concentration profile | Transient (breakthrough) | Uniform / exponentially decaying |
| Solid state | Stationary | Suspended slurry |

A fixed-bed adsorber operates as a *plug-flow reactor analog* with a stationary solid, while an agitated-bed adsorber behaves as a *CSTR analog* with the solid suspended in the fluid. Fixed beds are favored for high-purity polishing; agitated beds are favored for bulk impurity removal from slurries. **(3 marks — distinction with industrial use.)**

---

## 📝 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Sub-parts are *compulsory within the chosen question*.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

#### **Q A(a).** Derive the expression for the **stoichiometric breakthrough time** $\tau_s$ of a fixed-bed adsorber. A water-treatment column uses granular activated carbon (GAC) with the following data:

- Bed length, $L = 1.5$ m
- Bulk density, $\rho_b = 480$ kg/m³
- Equilibrium loading, $q_\infty = 0.32$ kg phenol / kg GAC
- Superficial velocity, $u_s = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s
- Inlet phenol concentration, $C_0 = 0.05$ kg/m³

Calculate $\tau_s$ and the **Length of Unused Bed (LUB)** if the actual breakthrough occurs at $t_b = 6.0$ h. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derivation (3 marks):**
Total solute fed = $u_s \, C_0 \, A_c \, \tau_s$
Total solute adsorbed = $L \, A_c \, \rho_b \, q_\infty$

Equating:
$$u_s \, C_0 \, A_c \, \tau_s = L \, A_c \, \rho_b \, q_\infty$$

$$\boxed{\tau_s = \dfrac{L \, \rho_b \, q_\infty}{u_s \, C_0}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values (2 marks):**

Convert units: $L = 1.5$ m, $\rho_b = 480$ kg/m³, $q_\infty = 0.32$, $u_s = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, $C_0 = 0.05$ kg/m³.

$$\tau_s = \dfrac{1.5 \times 480 \times 0.32}{(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.05}$$

$$\tau_s = \dfrac{230.4}{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\tau_s = 1.8432 \times 10^{6} \text{ s} = 512 \text{ h}$$

**Step 3 — LUB Calculation (2 marks):**
$t_b = 6.0$ h, $\tau_s = 512$ h, $L = 1.5$ m.

$$LUB = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{\tau_s}\right) = 1.5 \left(1 - \dfrac{6.0}{512}\right)$$

$$LUB = 1.5 \times (1 - 0.01172) = 1.5 \times 0.98828$$

$$\boxed{LUB = 1.4824 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A very common error is to forget to convert $t_b$ and $\tau_s$ to the *same units*. Some students also use $C_0 = 0.05$ g/L and forget to convert to kg/m³. This leads to a 1000× error and **loses 2 marks**.

---

#### **Q A(b).** Explain the **HTU–NTU model** for designing a fixed-bed adsorber. Derive the equation $H = HTU \times NTU$ and state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass Transfer Concept (2 marks):**
The HTU–NTU model is a design analog of the McCabe–Thiele method. The bed is conceptually divided into a series of *transfer units*, each producing a small concentration change.

The differential mass balance:
$$u_s \, dC = -K_L \, a_v \, (C - C^*) \, dz$$

**Step 2 — Define HTU and NTU (2 marks):**

$$HTU = \dfrac{u_s}{K_L \, a_v} \quad \text{[m]}$$

$$NTU = \int_{C_\text{out}}^{C_\text{in}} \dfrac{dC}{C - C^*}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Design Equation (2 marks):**

$$\int_0^H dz = \int_{C_\text{out}}^{C_\text{in}} \dfrac{u_s \, dC}{K_L \, a_v \, (C - C^*)} = \dfrac{u_s}{K_L \, a_v} \int_{C_\text{out}}^{C_\text{in}} \dfrac{dC}{C - C^*}$$

$$\boxed{H = HTU \times NTU}$$

**Step 4 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Plug flow ($D_{ax} = 0$).
2. Constant $K_L$ and $a_v$ along bed.
3. Linear equilibrium ($C^* = mC$).
4. Isothermal operation.
5. Negligible pressure drop variation.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

#### **Q B(a).** With a neat sketch, explain the **breakthrough curve** of a fixed-bed adsorber. Mark the breakthrough time, exhaustion time, and the stoichiometric time on the curve. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sketch Description (3 marks):**
The breakthrough curve is a plot of effluent concentration $C$ (or $C/C_0$) versus time $t$ at the bed exit. It has a characteristic **S-shape** (sigmoid):

- Initially $C \approx 0$ (clean effluent).
- After time $t_b$, $C$ rises sharply.
- At time $t_e$ (exhaustion), $C \to C_0$.

The curve can be divided into four phases (described in Section 4.3).

**Step 2 — Key Markings (2 marks):**
- $t_b$ — breakthrough time (typically when $C = 0.05 \, C_0$ to $0.10 \, C_0$).
- $t_e$ — exhaustion time (when $C = 0.90 \, C_0$ to $0.95 \, C_0$).
- $\tau_s$ — stoichiometric (ideal) time (the *theoretical* breakthrough time for a perfectly sharp front).

The area under the curve from $0$ to $\tau_s$ is proportional to the *total* adsorption capacity.

**Step 3 — Explanation of Shape (2 marks):**
The S-shape arises from the *finite width* of the MTZ. The leading edge (front) of the MTZ reaches the exit first, causing the slow initial rise. The trailing edge follows, causing the asymptotic approach to $C_0$. The asymmetry depends on the **isotherm type**: a *favorable isotherm* (Langmuir) gives a *self-sharpening* front (steep); an *unfavorable isotherm* gives a *spreading* front (gradual).

---

#### **Q B(b).** An agitated slurry adsorber is used to treat $V = 2$ m³ of dye-laden wastewater. The initial concentration is $C_0 = 200$ mg/L, the equilibrium relation is $C^* = 0.05 \, C$, and the mass transfer coefficient times area is $K_L a_v = 0.04$ s⁻¹. Calculate the time required to reduce the dye concentration to **20 mg/L** (i.e., 90% removal). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation (2 marks):**
For a batch agitated adsorber with linear isotherm $C^* = mC$:

$$\dfrac{C_t}{C_0} = \exp[-K_L \, a_v \, (1 - m) \, t]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values (2 marks):**
$C_t = 20$ mg/L, $C_0 = 200$ mg/L, $K_L a_v = 0.04$ s⁻¹, $m = 0.05$.

$$\dfrac{20}{200} = 0.10 = \exp[-0.04 \times (1 - 0.05) \times t]$$

$$0.10 = \exp[-0.04 \times 0.95 \times t] = \exp[-0.038 \, t]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Time (2 marks):**
Take natural log of both sides:
$$\ln(0.10) = -0.038 \, t$$

$$t = \dfrac{-2.3026}{-0.038} = 60.6 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer with Units (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{t \approx 60.6 \text{ seconds} \approx 1.01 \text{ min}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to include the $(1 - m)$ term. Without it, the answer would be $57.6$ s, which is wrong. Always use the **equilibrium slope $m$** in the exponential form for a *linear isotherm*. **Loses 1 mark** if missed.

---

## 🧾 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing seconds with hours, or mg/L with kg/m³ without conversion. (*−2 marks*)
> 2. **Confusing $\rho_b$ (bulk) with $\rho_s$ (particle) density.** The LUB and $\tau_s$ equations use $\rho_b$. (*−1 mark*)
> 3. **Forgetting $(1 - m)$ in the agitated-bed exponential equation.** (*−1 mark*)
> 4. **Assuming the breakthrough curve is a step function.** It is an S-curve, not a square wave. (*−1 mark*)
> 5. **Omitting assumptions in derivations.** The HTU–NTU derivation requires listing the assumptions explicitly. (*−1 mark*)
> 
> **Pro Tip:** Always re-derive the LUB and $\tau_s$ from *first principles* (mass balance) in the exam — examiners award full method marks even if the final number is wrong.

---

## 🔁 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This!):**

- ✅ **Fixed Bed:** Plug flow, stationary solid, transient breakthrough curve.
- ✅ **Agitated Bed:** Back-mix, suspended solid, exponential decay (batch) or single steady-state (CSTR).
- ✅ **MTZ** = Mass Transfer Zone — narrow band where the concentration changes sharply.
- ✅ **Three zones:** Saturated (top) → MTZ → Virgin (bottom).
- ✅ **Stoichiometric time:** $\tau_s = \dfrac{\rho_b \, L \, q_\infty}{u_s \, C_0}$.
- ✅ **LUB:** $LUB = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{\tau_s}\right)$ — bed height that is *still virgin* at breakthrough.
- ✅ **HTU–NTU design:** $H = HTU \times NTU$ where $HTU = u_s / (K_L a_v)$.
- ✅ **Agitated batch:** $\dfrac{C_t}{C_0} = \exp[-K_L a_v (1-m) t]$.
- ✅ **Agitated CSTR:** $\dfrac{C}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L a_v (1-m) \tau}$.
- ✅ **Industrial LUB safety factor:** 1.1 – 1.5 ×.
- ✅ **Bed design rule:** $L / d_p \geq 50$ to avoid channeling.
- ✅ **Standard isotherm types:** Henry (linear), Langmuir (favorable — self-sharpening), Freundlich (intermediate).
- ✅ **Regeneration methods:** TSA (Thermal Swing Adsorption), PSA (Pressure Swing Adsorption), VSA (Vacuum Swing).
- ✅ **Twin-bed (lead–lag) operation** is the standard industrial configuration.
- ✅ **Favorable isotherm** → narrow MTZ → high bed utilization.
- ✅ **Unfavorable isotherm** → broad MTZ → low bed utilization.
- ✅ **Slurry adsorbers** are used when solids content is high or feed is viscous.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption Wave - Break Through Curves and Rates of Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adsorption Wave - Break Through Curves and Rates of Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **fixed-bed adsorption**, a fluid containing a solute (adsorbate) is passed continuously through a stationary, packed bed of porous solid particles (adsorbent). As the solute molecules contact the fresh solid, they are preferentially held on the internal and external surface of the particles. The bed does **not** become saturated instantly; instead, a **concentration wave** (also called the **Mass Transfer Zone, MTZ**, or **adsorption wave**) travels through the column in the direction of fluid flow. The breakthrough curve is the experimental fingerprint of this wave: it is the plot of the **outlet (effluent) solute concentration, C**, normalized by the **inlet solute concentration, C₀**, plotted against either **time (t)** at constant flow, or **throughput volume (V)**, or **bed-depth equivalent (z)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner-Verbatim):**
> "A *breakthrough curve* is a plot of the ratio of the outlet to inlet concentration (C/C₀) of the solute versus time (or volume of effluent treated) obtained at the exit of a fixed-bed adsorber. The *breakpoint* is defined as the time (t_b) when the effluent concentration reaches 5 % (or 10 %, per design specification) of the inlet value, and the *exhaustion point* (t_e) is the time when C reaches 95 % of C₀."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a long, narrow hallway lined floor-to-ceiling with sticky fly-trap paper. You walk in from the left, holding a bucket of flies, and release them steadily. Near the entrance, the paper grabs almost all the flies — your bucket is empty if you stand at the exit. But once that first section of paper is "full" of flies, the next section starts catching them. A *zone of active catching* slowly moves down the hallway. The *front* of this zone is the **leading edge (saturated/equilibrium end)** and the *rear* of the zone is the **trailing edge (fresh/active end)**. The *length of the hallway occupied by this active zone* is the **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)**.

- If you sample the air at the **exit (right end)** of the hallway, you will see **zero flies for a long time**, then suddenly a few, then many, then a *flood* of flies — this rising S-shape is the **breakthrough curve**.
- The moment you first see a few flies at the exit is the **breakpoint (t_b)** — your hall is "breaking through."
- The moment flies pour out uncontrolled is the **exhaustion point (t_e)** — the hall is fully saturated.

In an actual industrial adsorber, the "hallway" is the **packed bed**, the "fly-trap paper" is the **adsorbent (e.g., activated carbon, zeolite)**, and the "flies" are the **solute molecules** (e.g., VOCs, dyes, water vapour, color bodies).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The **breakpoint time (t_b)** is *always* less than the **stoichiometric time (t\*)**, which corresponds to the *hypothetical* vertical (ideal plug-flow) wave. The unused portion of the bed between t_b and t\* is called the **Length of Unused Bed (LUB)** — the most important design parameter derived from the breakthrough curve.

## 1.3 Why the Wave Exists — The Physical Picture

Three resistances in series dictate the *shape and speed* of the adsorption wave:

1. **External (film) resistance** — diffusion through the boundary layer of fluid surrounding each particle, characterized by a **film mass-transfer coefficient k_c [m/s]**.
2. **Internal (pore) diffusion** — molecular or Knudsen diffusion through the tortuous pore network, characterized by the **effective diffusivity D_e [m²/s]**.
3. **Surface diffusion** — hopping of the adsorbed molecule along the pore wall, characterized by the **surface diffusivity D_s [m²/s]**.

A steep wave (sharp transition) means low overall resistance; a shallow, drawn-out wave means high resistance and inefficient use of the bed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Regions of a Fixed-Bed Adsorber

For a single adsorption cycle (after start-up transient dies out), the bed can be divided into three distinct zones, viewed from inlet to outlet:

| Zone | Description | Solid Loading (q) | Fluid Conc. (C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equilibrium / Saturated Zone** | Adsorbent is fully loaded. No more uptake possible. | q* (equilibrium with C₀) | C₀ (inlet) |
| **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** | Active adsorption occurring. Solid loading varies from q* down to ~0. | 0 < q < q* | C₀ → C₀ (outlet value) |
| **Fresh / Unused Zone** | Adsorbent is virgin, no solute contacted. | 0 | 0 (pure carrier) |

The **MTZ moves down the bed** as the saturated zone grows. The breakthrough curve is the *signature* of the trailing edge of the MTZ sweeping past the bed exit.

## 2.2 Characteristic Points on the Breakthrough Curve

| Symbol | Name | Definition (Common Convention) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| t_b | Breakpoint | Time when C = 0.05 C₀ (or 0.10 C₀) | Bed taken OFF-LINE in industry; treated volume = usable capacity |
| t_e | Exhaustion point | Time when C = 0.95 C₀ (or 0.99 C₀) | Bed fully spent; regeneration required |
| t* | Stoichiometric (ideal) time | Time to saturate the entire bed with a vertical (ideal) wave | Maximum theoretical capacity |
| t_f | Formation time | t_e − t_b | Time required for the MTZ to pass the exit |
| t_1/2 | Half-time | t at C/C₀ = 0.5 | Centre of the MTZ exit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer's Golden Rule:** The bed is *always* shut down at **t_b**, never at **t_e**. Letting it run to t_e would pollute the product stream and is uneconomical.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C/C_0 = f(t)$ | Normalized exit concentration vs time | dimensionless | Definition of breakthrough curve |
| 2 | $t^{*} = \dfrac{q^{*} \, \rho_b \, L}{C_0 \, u}$ | Stoichiometric time for an ideal vertical wave | s | Idealised capacity |
| 3 | $LUB = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{t^{*}}\right) = L \left(\dfrac{t^{*} - t_b}{t^{*}}\right)$ | Length of unused bed (geometric) | m | Quick design from curve |
| 4 | $HMTZ = L \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t^{*} - 0.5(t_e - t_b)}$ | Height of mass transfer zone | m | Full MTZ correlation |
| 5 | $LUB \approx HMTZ \cdot \dfrac{(t^{*} - t_b)}{(t_e - t_b)}$ | Alternative LUB expression | m | When t\* is unknown |
| 6 | $J_A = k_c (C - C^{*})$ | External (film) mass flux | kg/(m²·s) | External mass-transfer control |
| 7 | $J_A = \dfrac{D_e}{r_p}\,(C^* - \bar{C}_p)$ | Internal pore diffusion flux | kg/(m²·s) | Pore diffusion control |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_p (q^{*} - q)$ | Linear Driving Force (LDF) | 1/s | Overall rate approximation |
| 9 | $k_p \approx \dfrac{15 D_e}{r_p^2}$ | Glueckauf LDF approximation | 1/s | Pore-diffusion LDF |
| 10 | $k_p \approx \dfrac{3 D_s}{r_p^2}$ | Surface-diffusion LDF | 1/s | Surface-diffusion LDF |
| 11 | $F = 0.05$ to $0.10$ | Fractional breakthrough (design) | — | Defines t_b |
| 12 | $\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1}{(1-F)^2} - 1\right] = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2D_e}{r_p^2}\right) + \ln(t)$ | Boyd plot (kinetic control test) | — | Distinguish film vs pore control |
| 13 | $Nu = \dfrac{k_c \, d_p}{D_{AB}} = 2 + 1.8 Re_p^{0.5} Sc^{0.33}$ | Sherwood correlation | dimensionless | Film coefficient in packed bed |
| 14 | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho \, u \, d_p}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number | dimensionless | Packed-bed correlations |
| 15 | $Sc = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \, D_{AB}}$ | Schmidt number | dimensionless | Packed-bed correlations |

Where the symbols are:
- $C_0$ = inlet fluid concentration [kmol/m³]
- $C$ = exit fluid concentration at time t [kmol/m³]
- $q^*$ = equilibrium solid loading corresponding to $C_0$ [kmol/kg]
- $q$ = current average solid loading [kmol/kg]
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density of the bed [kg/m³]
- $L$ = total bed length [m]
- $u$ = superficial fluid velocity [m/s]
- $r_p, d_p$ = particle radius / diameter [m]
- $D_e$ = effective pore diffusivity [m²/s]
- $D_s$ = surface diffusivity [m²/s]
- $D_{AB}$ = molecular diffusivity of A in B [m²/s]
- $k_c$ = external film mass-transfer coefficient [m/s]
- $k_p$ = LDF overall mass-transfer coefficient [1/s]
- $F = C/C_0$ = fractional breakthrough
- $t$ = time [s]
- $\mu, \rho$ = fluid viscosity and density
- $C^{*}$ = concentration at particle surface (in equilibrium with surface $q$)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Breakthrough curve analysis is the *backbone* of every industrial adsorber design:

- **Pressure-Swing Adsorption (PSA) for O₂/N₂ separation:** the cycle time and bed length are *both* derived from the breakthrough curve, the former from t_b and the latter from LUB.
- **Water treatment (GAC filters for removing pesticides, colour, COD):** GAC bed diameters and replacement intervals are sized using LUB and HMTZ.
- **Solvent recovery (activated carbon canisters in paint shops, printing):** the run length (steam-regeneration interval) is set by t_b.
- **Gas drying (silica gel / molecular sieves in compressed air):** the moisture breakthrough curve dictates the regeneration frequency.
- **Chromatographic separations (pharmaceutical, biotech):** the breakthrough curve *is* the elution profile and determines yield vs purity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stoichiometric Time $t^{*}$

**Starting point:** A material balance on the entire column at the moment the *ideal* vertical wave has just reached the exit.

**Step 1 — Solute fed to the bed in time t\*:**
Volumetric flow rate of solute-laden fluid = $Q$ [m³/s]. Inlet concentration = $C_0$ [kmol/m³]. Time elapsed = $t^{*}$.

$$
\text{Total moles of solute fed} = Q \, C_0 \, t^{*}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles stored on the solid (entire bed saturated):**
Bulk volume of bed = $A \, L$, where $A$ = cross-sectional area, $L$ = length. Bulk density = $\rho_b$ [kg solid / m³ bed]. Equilibrium loading = $q^{*}$ [kmol / kg solid].

$$
\text{Moles adsorbed at saturation} = \rho_b \, A \, L \, q^{*}
$$

**Step 3 — Material balance (input = stored for the ideal vertical wave):**

$$
Q \, C_0 \, t^{*} = \rho_b \, A \, L \, q^{*}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce superficial velocity** $u = Q / A$ [m/s]:

$$
t^{*} = \dfrac{\rho_b \, L \, q^{*}}{C_0 \, u}
$$

> This is **Equation 2** in the formula sheet. It is the *maximum* time the bed could operate under ideal (instantaneous) mass transfer. In reality, the *useful* time is $t_b < t^{*}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Length of Unused Bed (LUB)

**Geometric reasoning:** A bed of length $L$ has an *ideal* vertical wave that would take time $t^{*}$ to traverse it. A real wave, however, requires an *extra* time $(t^{*} - t_b)$ to finish the last bit of loading near the exit. The fraction of the bed *not yet used at breakpoint* is the ratio of the *extra time* to the *total ideal time*.

$$
LUB = L \cdot \dfrac{t^{*} - t_b}{t^{*}}
$$

**Algebraic rearrangement (alternative form often used in KTU problems):**

$$
LUB = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{t^{*}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is **non-dimensionalised by L**, so the result is automatically in *metres*. Always quote the answer in the same units as L.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Height of Mass Transfer Zone (HMTZ)

**Concept:** The MTZ has a *length* (HMTZ) that is constant in time (for constant inlet conditions). The bed is conceptually divided into:

- A **saturated section** of length $(L - HMTZ)$ that has done all the work it can.
- The **MTZ** of length HMTZ itself, where the actual wave is smeared.
- A small **fresh section** of length $LUB$ at the very exit that has not been used.

**Step 1 — Equate the unused capacity to the LUB *fraction* of the MTZ:**
The LUB represents the *lower triangle* of the MTZ (the part not yet loaded at t_b). The upper triangle of the MTZ represents the part loaded between $t_b$ and $t_e$. The *area* of the lower triangle of the MTZ divided by the *total area* of the MTZ is $LUB / HMTZ$.

$$
\dfrac{LUB}{HMTZ} = \dfrac{t^{*} - t_b}{t_e - t_b}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for HMTZ:**

$$
HMTZ = LUB \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t^{*} - t_b}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the LUB expression $LUB = L(t^{*} - t_b)/t^{*}$:**

$$
HMTZ = L \cdot \dfrac{t^{*} - t_b}{t^{*}} \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t^{*} - t_b}
$$

$$
HMTZ = L \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t^{*}}
$$

**Step 4 — A more practical, commonly-quoted form** uses the *centroid* of the MTZ (occurs at $t = 0.5(t_b + t_e)$ rather than $t_b$):

$$
HMTZ = L \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t^{*} - 0.5(t_e - t_b)}
$$

> The two forms give *slightly* different numbers depending on which definition of the MTZ *midpoint* is chosen. **KTU generally uses the second form.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Linear Driving Force (LDF) Approximation

**Physical start:** The exact pore-diffusion equation in a spherical particle is

$$
\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} = \dfrac{D_e}{r^2} \dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2 \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial r}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Glueckauf (1955) approximation:** assume the *solid-phase concentration profile* inside a particle can be replaced by a *parabola*, which allows exact averaging. After integrating across the sphere, the average rate becomes:

$$
\dfrac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = \dfrac{15 \, D_e}{r_p^2}\,(q^{*} - \bar{q})
$$

**Step 2 — Define the overall LDF coefficient:**

$$
k_p = \dfrac{15 \, D_e}{r_p^2} \quad \text{(pore-diffusion LDF)}
$$

**Step 3 — Analogous expression for surface diffusion (when $D_s$ dominates):**

$$
k_p = \dfrac{15 \, D_s}{r_p^2}
$$

Some texts use $3/r_p^2$ instead of $15/r_p^2$ (a factor-of-five difference, depending on whether the driving force is $q^{*} - q$ or $C - \bar{C}_p$). **KTU follows the convention $k_p = 15 D_e / r_p^2$ for the $q$-basis LDF.**

**Step 4 — Compact LDF form:**

$$
\dfrac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = k_p\,(q^{*} - \bar{q})
$$

This is the **Equation 8** in the formula sheet.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Model Answer Pattern)

> **Problem:** A fixed-bed adsorber of length $L = 1.5$ m, cross-section $A = 0.05$ m², packed with activated carbon of bulk density $\rho_b = 500$ kg/m³, treats a gas stream containing a VOC at $C_0 = 0.01$ kmol/m³, with a superficial velocity $u = 0.10$ m/s. The equilibrium loading at $C_0$ is $q^{*} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/kg. The breakthrough curve gives $t_b = 1800$ s, $t_e = 3600$ s. Calculate $t^{*}$, LUB and HMTZ.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric time:**

$$
t^{*} = \dfrac{\rho_b \, L \, q^{*}}{C_0 \, u} = \dfrac{500 \times 1.5 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01 \times 0.10}
$$

$$
t^{*} = \dfrac{3.0}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 3000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Length of Unused Bed:**

$$
LUB = L\left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{t^{*}}\right) = 1.5\left(1 - \dfrac{1800}{3000}\right) = 1.5 \times 0.4 = 0.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Height of Mass Transfer Zone** (using the centroid form):

$$
HMTZ = L \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t^{*} - 0.5(t_e - t_b)} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{3600 - 1800}{3000 - 0.5(3600 - 1800)}
$$

$$
HMTZ = 1.5 \times \dfrac{1800}{3000 - 900} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{1800}{2100} = 1.5 \times 0.857 = 1.286 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The MTZ occupies ~86 % of the bed — a *very* inefficient system. The bed is mostly MTZ, with little truly-saturated solid. **Industrial implication:** reduce particle size, increase residence time, or change adsorbent to sharpen the wave.

---

## 3.6 Distinguishing the Rate-Limiting Step — Boyd Plot

The **Boyd equation** (also called the *Bhatia-Boyd* in modern texts) tests whether external (film) or internal (pore) diffusion controls the rate. For a *batch* adsorbent particle of radius $r_p$ exposed to a step change in fluid concentration $C_0$:

$$
F = 1 - \dfrac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{n^2 \pi^2 D_e t}{r_p^2}\right)
$$

If **internal diffusion controls**, the early-time asymptote gives:

$$
1 - F = \dfrac{6}{\pi^{1/2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{D_e t}{r_p^2}} - 3\dfrac{D_e t}{r_p^2}
$$

A plot of $Bt = \dfrac{\pi^2 D_e t}{r_p^2}$ vs. time (the *Boyd plot*) should be a *straight line through the origin* if internal diffusion controls; if it curves and the slope is too small, external film resistance is dominant.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Fixed-Bed Adsorption Process

    A2 -- Fixed flow --> B1[PACKED BED]

    B1 --> R2[2. MASS TRANSFER ZONE MTZ]
    B1 --> R3[3. FRESH UNUSED ZONE LUB]
    R2 -- Wave trailing edge --> C1[EXIT CONC. C]
    C1 --> D1[BREAKTHROUGH CURVE PLOT C/C0 vs t]
    D1 --> P1[bp t_b at C/C0 = 0.05]
    D1 --> P2[exhaust t_e at C/C0 = 0.95]
    D1 --> P3[stoich t* ideal vertical]
    D1 -- analysis --> E1[LUB and HMTZ DESIGN]
```

```mermaid

    F0[FLUID BULK C0] -->|"1. Film diffusion J = k_c C - C*"| F1[Particle EXTERNAL SURFACE C*]

    F2 -->|"3. Surface diffusion D_s along wall"| F3[ADSORPTION SITE eq. with q*]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: Define breakthrough curve and list the characteristic points marked on it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **breakthrough curve** is a plot of the *effluent to inlet solute concentration ratio (C/C₀)* as a function of *time t* (or treated volume V) at the exit of a fixed-bed adsorber operating under constant inlet conditions.

**Characteristic points:**

1. **Breakpoint (t_b):** The time at which the exit concentration reaches the *maximum allowable* value — conventionally **5 %** (or 10 %) of C₀. This defines the **useful service life** of the bed.
2. **Stoichiometric time (t\*):** The *ideal* time required for a perfectly vertical (plug-flow) wave to traverse the bed; computed from the material balance $t^{*} = \rho_b L q^{*}/(C_0 u)$.
3. **Exhaustion point (t_e):** The time at which the exit concentration approaches the inlet value, conventionally **95 %** of C₀. The bed is now *saturated* and must be regenerated.
4. **Midpoint (t_{1/2}):** The time at which C/C₀ = 0.5, representing the *centre* of the MTZ at the exit.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four points]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`

**Q: What is the Length of Unused Bed (LUB)? State the formula and explain its design significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Length of Unused Bed (LUB)** is the *fractional length of the bed near the exit* that is *not yet loaded* with solute when the breakpoint is reached. Because the adsorption wave is *not* vertical, the bottom portion of the bed remains *unutilised* at t_b.

**Formula:**

$$
LUB = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{t^{*}}\right)
$$

where $L$ = total bed length, $t_b$ = breakpoint time, $t^{*}$ = stoichiometric time.

**Design significance:**

- A **small LUB** (say < 10 % of L) indicates a *sharp, efficient* adsorption wave and good adsorbent utilisation.
- A **large LUB** (say > 40 % of L) indicates *poor* utilisation — the wave is smeared, often due to high mass-transfer resistance (large particles, low diffusivity, channeling).
- LUB is *directly* used in industrial design to *add extra bed length* to achieve a target run time: $L_{design} = LUB \times (t_{desired}/t_b)$.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for significance]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of an adsorption wave travelling through a fixed-bed adsorber. With a neat sketch, describe the three zones that develop in the bed and the corresponding S-shaped breakthrough curve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fixed-bed adsorber has the following data:
- Bed length L = 2.0 m, diameter = 0.10 m
- Inlet concentration C₀ = 0.02 kmol/m³, superficial velocity u = 0.05 m/s
- Bulk density ρ_b = 600 kg/m³, equilibrium loading q\* = 5 × 10⁻³ kmol/kg
- Breakpoint t_b = 4500 s, Exhaustion point t_e = 7500 s

**Calculate:** (i) the stoichiometric time t\*, (ii) the Length of Unused Bed (LUB), and (iii) the Height of the Mass Transfer Zone (HMTZ).

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Mechanism & Zones (7 Marks)

**The Three Zones** (1.5 Marks for description + 1.5 for sketch):

- **Zone 1 — Saturated/Equilibrium zone:** Near the inlet, the adsorbent has been in contact with feed for a long time and is fully loaded ($q = q^{*}$). The fluid leaves this zone still at concentration $C_0$ (no more uptake capacity). The length of this zone *grows* with time.
- **Zone 2 — Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ):** A finite length over which the concentration drops from $C_0$ to nearly zero. Here the solid loading varies from $q^{*}$ down to $\sim 0$. This is the *active* part of the bed and moves downstream as a *wave* at constant speed (for constant inlet conditions).
- **Zone 3 — Fresh/Unused zone:** Downstream of the MTZ the solid has not yet seen the solute ($q = 0$) and the fluid is essentially solute-free. At the very tip, the *Length of Unused Bed (LUB)* is the portion that will *not* be utilised at the breakpoint.

**Sketch description** (2 Marks): Plot C/C₀ vs. t. The curve is **S-shaped** with a long flat plateau at 0 (no solute in outlet), a steep rise through the MTZ, and a final plateau at 1.0 (inlet value reached). Mark t_b at C/C₀ = 0.05 and t_e at C/C₀ = 0.95, and the vertical *ideal* wave at t\*.

**Mechanism (Wave Movement)** (2 Marks): The wave travels from inlet to outlet. Its speed is dictated by the *overall mass-transfer resistance* — film, pore, and surface diffusion. Lower resistance → steeper, faster wave → sharper S-curve. Higher resistance → shallower, drawn-out wave → earlier breakthrough, larger LUB.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**(i) Stoichiometric time (2 Marks):**

$$
t^{*} = \dfrac{\rho_b L q^{*}}{C_0 u} = \dfrac{600 \times 2.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.02 \times 0.05}
$$

$$
t^{*} = \dfrac{6.0}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 6000 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Length of Unused Bed (2 Marks):**

$$
LUB = L \left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{t^{*}}\right) = 2.0\left(1 - \dfrac{4500}{6000}\right) = 2.0 \times 0.25 = 0.5 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Height of Mass Transfer Zone (3 Marks):** Using the centroid form,

$$
HMTZ = L \cdot \dfrac{t_e - t_b}{t^{*} - 0.5(t_e - t_b)} = 2.0 \times \dfrac{7500 - 4500}{6000 - 0.5(7500 - 4500)}
$$

$$
HMTZ = 2.0 \times \dfrac{3000}{6000 - 1500} = 2.0 \times \dfrac{3000}{4500} = 2.0 \times 0.667 = 1.333 \text{ m}
$$

**Final numerical answer:** t\* = 6000 s; LUB = 0.5 m; HMTZ = 1.33 m (≈ 67 % of bed length).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various *rates* of adsorption in a fixed bed. Explain with suitable equations how the Linear Driving Force (LDF) approximation is used to represent the *overall* rate of adsorption.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an air-drying unit using silica gel:
- C₀ (humidity) = 0.0018 kmol water/m³
- Bed length L = 1.2 m, u = 0.20 m/s, ρ_b = 720 kg/m³
- q\* = 3.0 × 10⁻³ kmol/kg at C₀
- t_b = 1500 s, t_e = 3300 s

**Find:** (i) t\*, (ii) LUB, (iii) bed utilisation efficiency at t_b, defined as $\eta = (L - LUB)/L \times 100\%$.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Rates of Adsorption (7 Marks)

**Three resistances in series** (1.5 Marks each):

1. **External (film) mass transfer:** The solute must diffuse through the *boundary layer* surrounding each particle.
$$
J_A = k_c(C - C^{*})
$$
The film coefficient $k_c$ is obtained from the Sherwood correlation:
$$
Sh = 2 + 1.8\, Re_p^{0.5}\, Sc^{0.33}
$$
with $Re_p = \rho u d_p / \mu$ and $Sc = \mu/(\rho D_{AB})$.

2. **Internal pore diffusion:** Once across the boundary layer, the solute diffuses through the *tortuous pore network* of the particle, described by an effective diffusivity $D_e$ (combines molecular and Knudsen diffusion in series).
$$
J_A = \dfrac{D_e}{r_p}(C^{*} - \bar{C}_p)
$$

3. **Surface diffusion:** The adsorbed molecules can *hop* along the pore wall, described by $D_s$.
$$
J_{s} = D_s \dfrac{d q}{d r}
$$

**Overall rate via LDF** (2.5 Marks): The rigorous pore-diffusion PDE has an *analytical* solution, but it is inconvenient in design. **Glueckauf's LDF approximation** replaces the exact internal profile by a parabola and reduces the PDE to a simple ODE for the *average* solid loading $\bar{q}$:
$$
\dfrac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = k_p (q^{*} - \bar{q})
$$
where $k_p = 15 D_e / r_p^2$ for pore-diffusion control, or $k_p = 15 D_s / r_p^2$ for surface-diffusion control. The LDF is the *workhorse* of fixed-bed design because it linearises the kinetics and is easily coupled with the column mass balance.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**(i) Stoichiometric time (2 Marks):**

$$
t^{*} = \dfrac{\rho_b L q^{*}}{C_0 u} = \dfrac{720 \times 1.2 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0018 \times 0.20}
$$

$$
t^{*} = \dfrac{2.592}{3.6 \times 10^{-4}} = 7200 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) LUB (2 Marks):**

$$
LUB = L\left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{t^{*}}\right) = 1.2\left(1 - \dfrac{1500}{7200}\right) = 1.2 \times 0.7917 = 0.95 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Bed utilisation efficiency (3 Marks):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{L - LUB}{L} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.95}{1.2} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.25}{1.2} \times 100\% = 20.83\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only ~21 % of the bed is *effectively* used at breakpoint — a very inefficient operation suggesting the need for smaller particles, lower velocity, or different adsorbent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (C₀ in kmol/m³, q\* in kmol/kg, u in m/s, ρ_b in kg/m³) — a single unit slip gives 0 marks for the numerical.
> 2. **Mixing up the two HMTZ formulas** — the *simple* form $HMTZ = L(t_e - t_b)/t^{*}$ is for the *leading-edge* definition; the *centroid* form (used by KTU) has the $0.5(t_e - t_b)$ correction. Pick the right one based on which point is the "centroid" of your breakthrough.
> 3. **Confusing t_b and t_e** — t_b is the *useful* operating time, t_e is the *regeneration-trigger* time. Always design for t_b, never t_e.
> 4. **Treating LDF coefficient as constant across the bed** — in reality, k_p varies with local loading and temperature; the LDF is an *approximation*.
> 5. **Ignoring the third resistance** — students often mention only film or only pore diffusion. KTU expects *all three* in long answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Adsorption wave** = the moving concentration front in a fixed-bed; physically identical to the **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)**.
- **Breakthrough curve** = plot of C/C₀ vs. t (or V) at the bed exit; S-shaped.
- **Breakpoint (t_b)** = C/C₀ = 0.05 (5 % breakthrough); **service life** of the bed.
- **Exhaustion (t_e)** = C/C₀ = 0.95; bed must be regenerated.
- **Stoichiometric time (t\*)** = ideal time for a *vertical* wave; computed from $t^{*} = \rho_b L q^{*}/(C_0 u)$.
- **LUB** = $L(1 - t_b / t^{*})$; the unused portion of the bed at breakpoint.
- **HMTZ** = $L (t_e - t_b)/[t^{*} - 0.5(t_e - t_b)]$; length of the active wave.
- **Bed utilisation efficiency** = $(L - LUB)/L \times 100\%$.
- **Three rate resistances:** (1) external film, $k_c$; (2) internal pore diffusion, $D_e$; (3) surface diffusion, $D_s$.
- **LDF approximation:** $dq/dt = k_p (q^{*} - q)$, with $k_p \approx 15 D_e / r_p^2$ (pore) or $\approx 15 D_s / r_p^2$ (surface).
- **Sherwood correlation** for film coefficient: $Sh = 2 + 1.8 Re_p^{0.5} Sc^{0.33}$.
- **Boyd plot** = diagnostic test for film vs. pore-diffusion control.
- **Industrial uses:** PSA for air separation, GAC for water treatment, solvent recovery on activated carbon, gas drying on silica gel/molecular sieves.
- **Design rules of thumb:** LUB < 10 % L = excellent; LUB 10–30 % = acceptable; LUB > 30 % = redesign needed.
- **Steep breakthrough curve = good adsorbent and small particle size.**
- **Shallow breakthrough curve = high mass-transfer resistance; consider smaller particles or longer contact time.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scale Up of Adsorption Processes - LUB Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Scale Up of Adsorption Processes - LUB Method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Length of Unused Bed (LUB) Method** is a classical empirical-procedural technique used to scale up laboratory-scale fixed-bed adsorption data to industrial-size adsorbers. It was developed by Collins and co-workers, and is based on the principle that in any fixed-bed adsorption column, only a finite portion of the bed (called the **Mass Transfer Zone, MTZ**) is actively engaged in adsorbing solute at any instant of time, while the remainder of the bed is either fully saturated (spent) or completely unused. The LUB is formally defined as the difference between the **total bed length (L)** and the **length of the bed actually utilized at breakthrough (L_b)**: $$\text{LUB} = L - L_b = L\left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right)$$ where: - $t_b$ = breakthrough time (time when outlet concentration reaches the breakthrough limit) - $t_s$ = stoichiometric time (time for complete saturation of bed, obtained graphically from breakthrough curve) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** The LUB method assumes that the **shape of the normalized breakthrough curve and the LUB length are invariant** with bed length, provided the operating conditions (superficial velocity, particle size, feed concentration, temperature) are kept identical between laboratory and industrial scales. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Plain English Analogy) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Spinning Conveyor Belt of a Toll Booth** > Imagine a long highway toll-booth lane. Cars enter the lane at a constant rate. At the front of the line, a few booths are actively collecting tolls — those booths are doing the **actual work**. Behind them, a queue of cars has already been processed and are just sitting in a long, useless line (the **unused portion**). The LUB method recognizes that this "active working section" stays roughly the same length whether you have 100 cars or 10,000 cars — only the **junk (unused) section** grows when the lane is longer. In the same way, in a packed adsorption bed: - The **MTZ** is like the active toll booths (doing real mass transfer work). - The **LUB** is like the unused cars-in-line behind (bed that is either saturated or never touched). - When you make the column longer, the LUB increases proportionally, but the MTZ length stays practically constant. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Nomenclature | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units | |---|---|---| | $L$ | Total bed length | **m** | | $L_{MTZ}$ | Length of Mass Transfer Zone | m | | $\text{LUB}$ | Length of Unused Bed | m | | $t_b$ | Breakthrough time | s or min | | $t_s$ | Stoichiometric (saturation) time | s or min | | $q_s$ | Saturation loading (capacity) | kg solute / kg adsorbent | | $c_0$ | Inlet solute concentration | kg/m³ | | $u_s$ | Superficial velocity | m/s | | $\rho_b$ | Bulk density of bed | kg/m³ | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Conceptual Foundation (Why LUB works) The LUB method rests on three experimentally validated observations: 1. **Similar Breakthrough Curve Shape:** When the breakthrough curve is plotted in normalized form (outlet concentration $c/c_0$ vs. time $t$, or vs. throughput volume), its **shape is essentially independent of bed length** for a given system at fixed operating conditions. 2. **Constant MTZ Length:** The width of the active mass transfer zone (measured along the bed) does not grow with bed length because it is governed by intrinsic mass transfer kinetics, not by the bulk flow rate through the column. 3. **Linear $t_b$ vs. $L$ Relationship:** The breakthrough time is a linear function of bed length, since $t_b = (L - \text{LUB})/u_s \cdot \rho_b q_s / c_0$. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure ### Step 1 — Run a Laboratory Column Operate a small packed-bed column (typically 1–5 cm diameter, 10–60 cm length) at the proposed industrial conditions: - Same superficial velocity $u_s$ - Same particle size $d_p$ - Same inlet concentration $c_0$ - Same temperature $T$ ### Step 2 — Record Breakthrough Curve Plot outlet concentration $c$ (or $c/c_0$) versus time $t$. Define the breakthrough point when $c/c_0$ reaches the **breakthrough limit** (commonly **0.05 or 0.10**, depending on process specification). ### Step 3 — Determine Stoichiometric Time $t_s$ $t_s$ is found by graphical integration of the breakthrough curve: $$t_s = \int_0^\infty \left(1 - \frac{c}{c_0}\right) dt$$ Equivalently, $t_s$ is the area of the rectangular region bounded by the line $c/c_0 = 1$ and the breakthrough curve, divided by the height of that rectangle (often approximated as the time at which $c/c_0 = 0.5$, or by curve-fitting). ### Step 4 — Compute LUB $$\boxed{\;\text{LUB} = L_{\text{lab}}\left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right)\;}$$ ### Step 5 — Apply to Industrial Column For the proposed industrial bed length $L_{\text{ind}}$, the expected breakthrough time is: $$t_{b,\text{ind}} = \frac{L_{\text{ind}} - \text{LUB}}{u_s}\cdot \frac{\rho_b\, q_s}{c_0}$$ Or, alternatively, given a desired breakthrough time $t_b$ in the industrial unit, the required bed length is: $$L_{\text{ind}} = \text{LUB} + u_s\, t_b \cdot \frac{c_0}{\rho_b\, q_s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $u_s$, $c_0$, $d_p$, and $T$ must be matched between lab and plant for the LUB to remain valid. If any of these are altered, a new LUB must be measured — the LUB is **NOT a universal constant of the adsorbent**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-grade essentials — memorize the boxed ones.** | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{LUB} = L\left(1 - \dfrac{t_b}{t_s}\right)$ | **Core LUB equation** | | 2 | $t_s = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty \left(1 - \dfrac{c}{c_0}\right)dt$ | Stoichiometric time from breakthrough curve | | 3 | $t_b = \dfrac{(L-\text{LUB})\,\rho_b\, q_s}{u_s\, c_0}$ | Breakthrough time (working/used bed portion only) | | 4 | $L = \text{LUB} + \dfrac{u_s\, c_0\, t_b}{\rho_b\, q_s}$ | Required industrial bed length for a desired $t_b$ | | 5 | $\theta_b = \dfrac{t_b}{(L/u_s)(\rho_b q_s / c_0)}$ | Dimensionless breakthrough time fraction | | 6 | $\rho_b = (1-\varepsilon)\rho_p$ | Bed bulk density in terms of particle density $\rho_p$ and porosity $\varepsilon$ | **Critical constraint for scale-up validity:** $$\boxed{\;u_{s,\text{lab}} = u_{s,\text{ind}},\quad c_{0,\text{lab}} = c_{0,\text{ind}},\quad d_{p,\text{lab}} = d_{p,\text{ind}},\quad T_{\text{lab}} = T_{\text{ind}}\;}$$ --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the LUB Equation from a Material Balance Consider a packed bed of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $L$, through which a fluid of superficial velocity $u_s$ and solute concentration $c_0$ is passed. Perform a solute material balance over the entire bed at time $t$: $$\text{Solute in} - \text{Solute out} = \text{Solute accumulated on solid}$$ The solute fed up to time $t$ is $c_0\, u_s\, A\, t$. The solute leaving the bed up to time $t$ is $\int_0^t u_s A\, c(t')\, dt'$. Therefore: $$c_0 u_s A t - u_s A \int_0^t c(t')\, dt' = \rho_b A L \, \bar{q}(t)$$ where $\bar{q}(t)$ is the mean solid-phase loading averaged over the bed. Rearranging: $$\bar{q}(t) = \frac{c_0 u_s t}{L\,\rho_b} - \frac{u_s}{L\,\rho_b}\int_0^t c(t')\,dt'$$ At the **stoichiometric time** $t_s$, the bed is in equilibrium with the inlet fluid everywhere, so $\bar{q}(t_s) = q_s$ and the outlet concentration $c$ equals $c_0$. The second integral reduces to $c_0 t_s$: $$q_s = \frac{c_0 u_s t_s}{L\rho_b} - \frac{u_s c_0 t_s}{L\rho_b} = 0 + \frac{c_0 u_s (t_s - t_s)}{L\rho_b}\;\;(\text{consistent})$$ A more useful expression is obtained by setting $t = t_b$, the breakthrough time, where the solute accumulated is $L_b\, \rho_b\, q_s$ (only the "used" portion $L_b$ is fully loaded; the unused portion is empty): $$c_0 u_s t_b = L_b\, \rho_b\, q_s$$ Solving for $L_b$: $$L_b = \frac{c_0 u_s t_b}{\rho_b q_s}$$ By definition, $\text{LUB} = L - L_b$, hence: $$\text{LUB} = L - \frac{c_0 u_s t_b}{\rho_b q_s}$$ The stoichiometric time is found by considering the entire column at saturation: $$c_0 u_s t_s = L\, \rho_b\, q_s \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{c_0 u_s}{\rho_b q_s} = \frac{L}{t_s}$$ Substituting this into the LUB expression: $$\text{LUB} = L - \frac{L\, t_b}{t_s} = L\left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\;\text{LUB} = L\left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right)\;}\quad\blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-mark Style) **Problem:** A laboratory adsorption column of length **30 cm** is used to study the removal of an organic vapor from air. The breakthrough time (at $c/c_0 = 0.05$) is measured as **48 min**, and the stoichiometric time obtained from the breakthrough curve is **75 min**. A full-scale adsorber is to be designed to operate for **6 hours** at breakthrough, under identical conditions. The saturation capacity of the adsorbent is **0.42 kg/kg**, the bulk density is **450 kg/m³**, the inlet concentration is **0.012 kg/m³**, and the superficial velocity is **0.30 m/s**. Determine: (a) The Length of Unused Bed (LUB) from the lab experiment. (b) The required bed length for the industrial column. (c) Verify the LUB and discuss its constancy. **Solution:** ### Part (a) — LUB from Laboratory Data Using the core LUB formula with $L_{\text{lab}} = 30$ cm, $t_b = 48$ min, $t_s = 75$ min: $$\text{LUB} = L_{\text{lab}}\left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right) = 30\,\text{cm}\left(1 - \frac{48}{75}\right)$$ Compute the fraction: $$\frac{48}{75} = 0.640$$ $$\text{LUB} = 30 \times (1 - 0.640) = 30 \times 0.360 = 10.8\,\text{cm}$$ > **Answer (a): LUB = 10.8 cm** ### Part (b) — Required Industrial Bed Length Given the desired breakthrough time $t_b = 6$ hours $= 360$ min. The stoichiometric time scales linearly with bed length: $$\frac{t_{s,\text{ind}}}{L_{\text{ind}}} = \frac{t_{s,\text{lab}}}{L_{\text{lab}}} = \frac{75\,\text{min}}{30\,\text{cm}} = 2.5\,\text{min/cm}$$ Alternatively, use the operating-condition approach. Since operating conditions match: $$L_{\text{ind}} = \text{LUB} + \frac{u_s\, c_0\, t_b}{\rho_b\, q_s}$$ Substituting values (with consistent units: $t_b = 360$ min $= 21{,}600$ s): $$L_{\text{ind}} = 0.108\,\text{m} + \frac{(0.30\,\text{m/s})(0.012\,\text{kg/m}^3)(21600\,\text{s})}{(450\,\text{kg/m}^3)(0.42\,\text{kg/kg})}$$ Numerator of the second term: $$0.30 \times 0.012 \times 21600 = 77.76\,\text{kg/m}^2$$ Denominator of the second term: $$450 \times 0.42 = 189\,\text{kg/m}^3$$ Second term: $$\frac{77.76}{189} = 0.4114\,\text{m}$$ Therefore: $$L_{\text{ind}} = 0.108 + 0.4114 = 0.519\,\text{m} \approx 51.9\,\text{cm}$$ > **Answer (b): Industrial bed length ≈ 0.519 m or 51.9 cm** ### Part (c) — Verification of LUB Constancy The stoichiometric time for the industrial bed is: $$t_{s,\text{ind}} = 2.5 \times L_{\text{ind}} = 2.5 \times 51.9 = 129.75\,\text{min}$$ Compute LUB for the industrial bed: $$\text{LUB}_{\text{ind}} = L_{\text{ind}}\left(1 - \frac{t_{b,\text{ind}}}{t_{s,\text{ind}}}\right) = 51.9\left(1 - \frac{360}{129.75}\right)$$ Wait — $t_{b,\text{ind}} > t_{s,\text{ind}}$, which is **physically impossible**. This indicates the desired breakthrough time cannot be achieved with this LUB at this bed length. Let us re-check the math. Recalculation: $t_{s,\text{ind}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{ind}}}{L_{\text{lab}}} t_{s,\text{lab}}$ Using the **alternative** derivation where $t_{s,\text{ind}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{ind}}\,\rho_b q_s}{u_s c_0}$: $$t_{s,\text{ind}} = \frac{(0.519)(450)(0.42)}{(0.30)(0.012)} = \frac{98.13}{0.0036} = 27{,}258\,\text{s} = 454.3\,\text{min}$$ Now compute the industrial LUB: $$\text{LUB}_{\text{ind}} = 0.519\left(1 - \frac{360}{454.3}\right) = 0.519 \times (1 - 0.7924) = 0.519 \times 0.2076 = 0.1077\,\text{m} \approx 10.8\,\text{cm}$$ > **Answer (c):** LUB ≈ 10.8 cm — **identical to the lab LUB**, confirming the scale-up invariance of LUB. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick:** Students often confuse the LUB with the MTZ length. Remember: **LUB = (MTZ portion that is not used at breakthrough)** = portion behind the stoichiometric front. The MTZ moves down the bed; the LUB stays as a "tail" of unused bed behind it. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map of the LUB Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Industrial Adsorption Scale Up Required"] --> B["Step 1: Run Laboratory Column Experiment
Match u_s, c_0, d_p, T"] B --> C["Step 2: Record Breakthrough Curve
c/c_0 vs t"] C --> D["Step 3: Identify t_b at c/c_0 limit
typically 0.05 or 0.10"] C --> E["Step 4: Graphically Integrate
to find t_s"] D --> F["Step 5: Compute LUB_lab
LUB = L_lab (1 - t_b/t_s)"] E --> F F --> G["Step 6: Choose Industrial t_b_desired
or L_ind"] G --> H["Step 7: Apply Scale Up Equation
L_ind = LUB + u_s c_0 t_b / rho_b q_s"] H --> I["Step 8: Verify LUB is constant
LUB_ind should equal LUB_lab"] I --> J["Final: Industrial Bed Length Determined"] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of Bed Zones During Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph COL["FIXED BED ADSORBER (length L)"] direction LR Z1["Saturated Zone
Already used
Length: L - L_b"] Z2["Mass Transfer Zone MTZ
Active solute transfer
Length: L_MTZ"] Z3["Unused Bed LUB
No contact with MTZ yet
Length: LUB"] IN["Inlet: c = c_0"] OUT["Outlet: c = 0 at t less than t_b"] IN --> Z1 Z1 --> Z2 Z2 --> Z3 Z3 --> OUT end ``` ## 4.3 Breakthrough Curve Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph BC["BREAKTHROUGH CURVE: c/c_0 vs t"] direction LR P1["Region A: c/c_0 approx 0
Outlet clean
Bed doing useful work"] P2["Region B: c/c_0 rises 0 to 1
MTZ has reached outlet
Breakthrough begins"] P3["Region C: c/c_0 approx 1
Bed exhausted"] TB["Mark t_b: 5 percent breakthrough"] TS["Mark t_s: stoichiometric time
Area = L times rho_b q_s / u_s c_0"] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 TB -.-> P2 TS -.-> P3 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In the bed schematic, the **MTZ** is the working section whose length stays roughly constant. The **saturated zone** (already used) and the **LUB** (yet unused) grow as the MTZ travels downstream. The ratio $(L - \text{LUB})/L = t_b/t_s$ is the **fraction of bed utilized at breakthrough**. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Length of Unused Bed (LUB)** in a fixed-bed adsorber and state the governing equation. **Model Answer:** The Length of Unused Bed (LUB) is the portion of the fixed-bed adsorber that is either fully saturated (exhausted) or has not yet been contacted by the mass transfer zone at the moment of breakthrough. It represents the bed length that is **not effectively utilized** when the outlet concentration reaches the breakthrough limit. $$\text{LUB} = L\left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right)$$ where $L$ = total bed length, $t_b$ = breakthrough time, $t_s$ = stoichiometric time. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List the **four operating parameters that must be kept identical** between the laboratory and industrial adsorber for the LUB method to be valid. **Model Answer:** 1. Superficial velocity $u_s$ 2. Inlet solute concentration $c_0$ 3. Adsorbent particle diameter $d_p$ 4. Operating temperature $T$ Any change in these parameters alters the mass transfer kinetics and changes the LUB, invalidating the scale-up. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the **principle of the LUB method** for scale-up of fixed-bed adsorption processes. Derive the expression for LUB starting from a solute material balance over the bed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A laboratory column 50 cm long gave a breakthrough time of 80 min at the 5% breakthrough point, and a stoichiometric time of 110 min. If the saturation capacity of the adsorbent is 0.38 kg/kg, bed bulk density is 500 kg/m³, and the inlet gas concentration is 0.020 kg/m³, determine the required industrial bed length for a service time of 8 hours at breakthrough. Take superficial velocity = 0.25 m/s. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Principle and Derivation:** The LUB method is based on the observation that, at fixed operating conditions, the shape of the normalized breakthrough curve and the LUB length remain invariant with bed length. Only the unused tail of the bed grows proportionally as the bed is made longer. **Derivation (from solute material balance):** Solute entering bed up to time $t$ = $c_0 u_s A t$ Solute leaving bed up to time $t$ = $u_s A \int_0^t c\, dt'$ Solute accumulated on solid = $\rho_b A L\, \bar{q}(t)$ Material balance: $$c_0 u_s A t - u_s A \int_0^t c\, dt' = \rho_b A L\, \bar{q}(t)$$ At breakthrough ($t = t_b$), only the used length $L_b$ is saturated: $$c_0 u_s t_b = \rho_b q_s L_b \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; L_b = \frac{c_0 u_s t_b}{\rho_b q_s}$$ At stoichiometric time, the entire bed $L$ is saturated: $$c_0 u_s t_s = \rho_b q_s L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{c_0 u_s}{\rho_b q_s} = \frac{L}{t_s}$$ Therefore: $$\text{LUB} = L - L_b = L - \frac{L\, t_b}{t_s} = L\left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right)$$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute LUB from laboratory data:** $$\text{LUB} = 50\,\text{cm}\left(1 - \frac{80}{110}\right) = 50 \times 0.2727 = 13.64\,\text{cm} = 0.1364\,\text{m}$$ **Step 2 — Convert desired breakthrough time to SI:** $$t_b = 8\,\text{h} = 8 \times 3600 = 28{,}800\,\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Apply scale-up equation:** $$L_{\text{ind}} = \text{LUB} + \frac{u_s\, c_0\, t_b}{\rho_b\, q_s}$$ $$L_{\text{ind}} = 0.1364 + \frac{(0.25)(0.020)(28800)}{(500)(0.38)}$$ Numerator: $0.25 \times 0.020 \times 28800 = 144\,\text{kg/m}^2$ Denominator: $500 \times 0.38 = 190\,\text{kg/m}^3$ Second term: $144 / 190 = 0.7579\,\text{m}$ $$L_{\text{ind}} = 0.1364 + 0.7579 = 0.8943\,\text{m} \approx 0.894\,\text{m}$$ > **Answer (b):** Industrial bed length ≈ **0.894 m or 89.4 cm** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch of the breakthrough curve, explain the terms **breakthrough time, stoichiometric time, mass transfer zone, and length of unused bed**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pilot-plant adsorption run gave the following data: bed length 25 cm, breakthrough time 35 min (at $c/c_0 = 0.05$), saturation capacity 0.32 kg/kg, bulk density 480 kg/m³, $c_0 = 0.015\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $u_s = 0.20\,\text{m/s}$. If the stoichiometric time is 60 min, calculate (i) the LUB, (ii) the required industrial bed length for a service time of 5 hours, and (iii) the LUB in the industrial column (verify invariance). **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Sketch and Definitions (7 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BC2["BREAKTHROUGH CURVE c/c_0 vs t"] direction LR A["Start: c/c_0 = 0
Bed is fresh"] B["Rise begins: c/c_0 increases
MTZ reaches outlet"] C["Asymptote: c/c_0 = 1
Bed fully exhausted"] TB2["t_b: 5 percent breakthrough time"] TS2["t_s: Stoichiometric time"] A --> B B --> C TB2 -.-> B TS2 -.-> C end ``` - **Breakthrough time ($t_b$):** Time at which outlet concentration reaches the specified breakthrough limit (commonly $c/c_0 = 0.05$ or $0.10$). - **Stoichiometric time ($t_s$):** Time corresponding to complete saturation of the bed, found by graphical integration: $t_s = \int_0^\infty (1 - c/c_0)\, dt$. - **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ):** The active region of the bed where the solute concentration changes from $c_0$ to nearly zero; its length $L_{MTZ}$ is governed by mass transfer kinetics. - **Length of Unused Bed (LUB):** The portion of the bed behind the MTZ that is either fully saturated (used but exhausted) or untouched at the breakthrough instant; calculated as $\text{LUB} = L(1 - t_b/t_s)$. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) LUB from pilot data:** $$\text{LUB} = 25\,\text{cm}\left(1 - \frac{35}{60}\right) = 25 \times 0.4167 = 10.42\,\text{cm} = 0.1042\,\text{m}$$ **(ii) Industrial bed length for $t_b = 5\,\text{h} = 18{,}000\,\text{s}$:** $$L_{\text{ind}} = 0.1042 + \frac{(0.20)(0.015)(18000)}{(480)(0.32)}$$ Numerator: $0.20 \times 0.015 \times 18000 = 54\,\text{kg/m}^2$ Denominator: $480 \times 0.32 = 153.6\,\text{kg/m}^3$ Second term: $54/153.6 = 0.3516\,\text{m}$ $$L_{\text{ind}} = 0.1042 + 0.3516 = 0.4558\,\text{m} \approx 0.456\,\text{m}$$ **(iii) Verification of LUB invariance:** Industrial stoichiometric time: $$t_{s,\text{ind}} = \frac{L_{\text{ind}}\,\rho_b\, q_s}{u_s\, c_0} = \frac{0.4558 \times 480 \times 0.32}{0.20 \times 0.015}$$ Numerator: $0.4558 \times 153.6 = 70.01$ Denominator: $0.003$ $$t_{s,\text{ind}} = \frac{70.01}{0.003} = 23{,}337\,\text{s} = 388.95\,\text{min}$$ Industrial LUB: $$\text{LUB}_{\text{ind}} = L_{\text{ind}}\left(1 - \frac{t_{b,\text{ind}}}{t_{s,\text{ind}}}\right) = 0.4558\left(1 - \frac{300}{388.95}\right)$$ $$= 0.4558 \times (1 - 0.7713) = 0.4558 \times 0.2287 = 0.1043\,\text{m} = 10.43\,\text{cm}$$ > **Answer:** Industrial LUB = **10.43 cm**, which matches the pilot LUB of **10.42 cm** within rounding error. LUB invariance confirmed. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $t_b$ in minutes with $u_s$ in m/s is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction. Always convert $t_b$ to seconds before substituting into the scale-up equation. > 2. **Confusing LUB with MTZ length:** LUB is the *unused* portion **behind** the MTZ at breakthrough. MTZ is the *active* transfer region. Examiners routinely test this distinction. > 3. **Forgetting the LUB term in industrial design:** Some students compute $L_{\text{ind}} = u_s c_0 t_b / (\rho_b q_s)$ alone, missing the LUB additive term. This is **incomplete** and loses at least 2 marks. > 4. **Stoichiometric time by integration:** $t_s$ is **not** simply the time at $c/c_0 = 0.5$. It must come from graphical integration of the breakthrough curve (or equivalently, from the material balance $t_s = L\rho_b q_s / (u_s c_0)$). > 5. **Ignoring operating-condition match:** Stating "LUB is a constant for the adsorbent" without qualifying that $u_s, c_0, d_p, T$ must match costs marks for missing the **scale-up validity conditions**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — LUB Method** > > - ☐ **LUB** = portion of bed not utilized at breakthrough: $\text{LUB} = L(1 - t_b/t_s)$. > - ☐ **$t_b$** = time when outlet concentration hits breakthrough limit (commonly 5% of $c_0$). > - ☐ **$t_s$** = stoichiometric time, found by integration or $t_s = L\rho_b q_s / (u_s c_0)$. > - ☐ **MTZ** is the active transfer zone; its length is constant with bed scaling (under matched conditions). > - ☐ **Scale-up equation:** $L_{\text{ind}} = \text{LUB} + u_s c_0 t_b / (\rho_b q_s)$. > - ☐ **Match conditions** for valid LUB scale-up: $u_s, c_0, d_p, T$ must be identical lab ↔ plant. > - ☐ **LUB is invariant** with bed length when operating conditions are matched (verified numerically). > - ☐ **Industrial $t_b$** is computed from the **used** bed length $(L - \text{LUB})$, not the total length. > - ☐ **Higher $u_s$ → larger LUB** (faster flow = wider MTZ = more unused bed). > - ☐ **Higher $d_p$ → larger LUB** (larger particles = more intraparticle diffusion resistance). > - ☐ **Advantages of LUB:** simple, requires only one lab experiment, no need for full kinetic model. > - ☐ **Limitations:** Empirical; fails if operating conditions differ; assumes plug flow; ignores axial dispersion rigorously. ---

topics.title: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle of Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying** is defined as the **mass transfer operation** in which the moisture (typically water) present in a wet solid is removed by **thermal means** (application of heat) to yield a **solid product** of low moisture content. The moisture is transferred by **convective heat and mass transfer** from the surface of the solid into an unsaturated gas phase (usually hot air).

The term *Drying* is generally reserved for the **final removal of moisture** from a material, in contrast to **evaporation**, which is the removal of water from a solution.

### Classification of Moisture Removal Operations

| Operation | Initial State | Final State | Driving Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dehydration** | Solid + Liquid | Solid (low moisture) | Temperature / Humidity gradient |
| **Evaporation** | Liquid solution | Concentrated solution | Temperature gradient |
| **Drying** | Wet solid | Dry solid | Vapor-pressure gradient |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 scheme, the official terminology used is **"Drying"** — and the principle section covers the fundamental definitions, the mechanism, and the classification of drying equipment.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Laundry Analogy:** Imagine you have just washed a cotton shirt and want it to dry. You hang it on a clothesline on a hot, dry, breezy day. The water in the cloth begins to evaporate from the surface, escapes into the air, and is carried away by the wind. Eventually the shirt becomes dry enough to wear. This real-world phenomenon IS industrial drying — except instead of a shirt, it's a chemical, food, or pharmaceutical product, and instead of sunshine and wind, we use **heated air in a controlled chamber called a dryer**.

**Why drying matters in industry:**
- Prevents microbial spoilage (food industry)
- Reduces shipping weight (minerals, chemicals)
- Improves product shelf life (pharmaceuticals)
- Allows handling of powders (cement, fertilizers, detergents)

---

## 1.3 Key Industrial Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Humid heat (Cs)** ≈ **1.005 + 1.88 H** kJ/kg dry air
- **Latent heat of vaporization of water (λ)** ≈ **2260 kJ/kg** (at 100 °C) — varies with temperature
- **Universal gas constant, R** = **8.314 J/mol·K**
- **Standard drying temperature range:** 60 °C – 200 °C for most products

---

## 1.4 Scope of the Topic (as per KTU 2024 Module 4)

The first sub-topic **"Principle"** of Module 4 covers:

1. Definition of drying
2. **Batch vs Continuous drying** (broad classification)
3. **Mechanism of drying** — internal vs external moisture migration
4. **Drying equilibria** — bound, unbound, free, and equilibrium moisture
5. **Drying rate curves** — constant and falling rate periods
6. **Calculation of drying time** (core numerical part)

> [!NOTE]
> Detailed construction, working, design equations, and the specific industrial dryers (tray, rotary, spray, fluidized bed, etc.) are covered in the subsequent topic of Module 4.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Drying (The "How" Behind Drying)

The drying of a wet solid occurs in **two simultaneous steps**:

### Step 1: Internal Mass Transfer (Moisture Migration to the Surface)
- Moisture moves from the **interior of the solid** to the **surface**.
- Mechanisms involved: **liquid diffusion**, **capillary flow**, and (for high-temperature) **vapour diffusion** through the pores.
- This step is **rate-controlling** in the *falling rate period*.

### Step 2: External Mass Transfer (Evaporation from the Surface)
- Surface moisture evaporates into the surrounding gas phase.
- The driving force is the **vapour pressure difference** between the surface of the solid and the bulk gas stream.
- This step is **rate-controlling** in the *constant rate period*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The shift from constant rate to falling rate drying is the most critical concept in this module.** It occurs at the **critical moisture content (Xc)**, the moisture level below which the surface can no longer be kept fully wet by the internal moisture flow.

---

## 2.2 Types of Moisture in a Solid

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free Moisture** | X | Moisture in excess of equilibrium content | Can be removed by drying at given conditions |
| **Equilibrium Moisture** | X* | Moisture content in equilibrium with the drying air | Cannot be removed by air of given humidity |
| **Unbound Moisture** | — | Moisture in excess of X* at 100 % RH | Holds same vapour pressure as pure water |
| **Bound Moisture** | — | Moisture held at vapour pressure < pure water | Held in capillaries / cell walls |
| **Initial Moisture** | X1 | Moisture content at start of drying | — |
| **Critical Moisture** | Xc | Moisture at which constant rate period ends | Marks the transition point |

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$X = X^* + X_{free}$$

---

## 2.3 Classification of Drying Operations

### A. Batch Drying
- The solid is charged, dried, and discharged in **discrete batches**.
- Long drying cycle, flexible operation.
- Examples: **Tray dryer, Agitated pan dryer, Vacuum shelf dryer.**

### B. Continuous Drying
- Wet solid is fed **continuously** and dry product discharged.
- Steady-state operation, high throughput.
- Examples: **Rotary dryer, Spray dryer, Fluidized bed dryer, Belt dryer.**

---

## 2.4 Drying Rate Curve — The Heart of Module 4

The **drying rate curve** plots the rate of drying (N = kg water / h·m²) against:
- **Moisture content X** (most common), OR
- **Time t** (during batch operation).

It consists of **four distinct regions**:

```
N  |
Rc |==================================●  (A→B→C : constant rate)
   |                                   \
   |                                    \  (C→D : first falling rate)
   |                                     ●
   |                                      \  (D→E : second falling rate)
   |                                       \____
   |________________________________________________ X
        X*      Xc           X1
```

| Region | Name | Controlling Step | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A → B** | Initial transient | Surface warming | — |
| **B → C** | **Constant Rate Period** | External mass transfer (surface evaporation) | N = Rc = constant |
| **C → D** | **First Falling Rate Period** | Partially wet surface (unsaturated surface drying) | N decreases linearly with X |
| **D → E** | **Second Falling Rate Period** | Internal moisture migration (diffusion-controlled) | N ∝ (X − X*) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Constant Rate Period** exists **only if the initial moisture X1 is greater than the critical moisture Xc**. For materials that have X1 < Xc, drying occurs entirely in the falling rate period.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Drying Time Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most tested section** in KTU University Exam Part B questions from Module 4.

### Core Definitions

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Moisture content (dry basis)** | $X = \dfrac{W - W_s}{W_s}$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| **Free moisture** | $X - X^*$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| **Drying rate** | $N = \dfrac{dW}{A \cdot dt}$ | kg / (h·m²) |
| **Humid heat** | $C_s = 1.005 + 1.88\,H$ | kJ / (kg dry air·K) |

### Drying Time — Constant Rate Period

$$t_c = \dfrac{L}{A \cdot R_c} \left( X_1 - X_c \right)$$

Where:
- $L$ = mass of **bone-dry** solid (kg)
- $A$ = drying surface area (m²)
- $R_c$ = constant drying rate (kg / m²·h)
- $X_1, X_c$ = initial and critical moisture (dry basis)

### Drying Time — First Falling Rate Period (Linear)

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{L}{A \cdot R_c} \left( X_c - X^* \right) \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

Where $X_2$ is the final moisture content (dry basis).

### Drying Time — Second Falling Rate Period

If the falling rate curve passes through the origin (i.e., $N \propto X$), then:

$$t_{f2} = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

(Form is similar; the difference lies in the slope determined from data.)

### Total Drying Time

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_{f1} + t_{f2}$$

### Special Case — Only Falling Rate Period Exists

If $X_1 < X_c$ (no constant rate period):

$$t_f = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_1 - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_1 - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Drying Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Food** | Milk powder, instant coffee, spices | Spray dryer, Fluidized bed |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Antibiotics, tablets | Tray dryer, Vacuum dryer |
| **Cement** | Clinker cooling and drying | Rotary kiln |
| **Ceramics** | Bricks, tiles | Tunnel dryer |
| **Chemicals** | Polymers, dyes | Spray / Flash dryer |
| **Paper** | Pulp sheets | Cylinder dryer |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Drying Time — Constant Rate Period

### Starting Point (Mass Balance)

Consider a batch dryer with $L$ kg of **bone-dry** solid. The drying rate is defined as:

$$N = -\dfrac{L}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$$

The negative sign indicates that moisture content $X$ **decreases** as time $t$ increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this equation?** We are measuring the rate at which moisture leaves the solid per unit drying area. The total water mass in the solid is $L \cdot X$ kg; if $X$ decreases by $dX$ in time $dt$, the water lost per unit time is $L\,dX/dt$, and dividing by area $A$ gives the flux $N$.

### Step 1: Separate the Variables

$$dt = -\dfrac{L}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{N}$$

### Step 2: Integrate Over the Constant Rate Period

During the constant rate period, $N = R_c$ (a constant). Integrate from $X_1$ (initial) to $X_c$ (critical) and from $t = 0$ to $t = t_c$:

$$\int_0^{t_c} dt = -\dfrac{L}{A \cdot R_c} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral

$$t_c = -\dfrac{L}{A \cdot R_c} \Big[ X \Big]_{X_1}^{X_c}$$

$$t_c = -\dfrac{L}{A \cdot R_c} \left( X_c - X_1 \right)$$

### Step 4: Final Form

$$\boxed{\,t_c = \dfrac{L}{A \cdot R_c} \left( X_1 - X_c \right)\,}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for constant rate drying time. The positive sign arises because $X_1 > X_c$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Drying Time — First Falling Rate Period (Linear Variation)

### Step 1: Assume Linear Variation of Drying Rate with Moisture

In the first falling rate period, the drying rate decreases linearly with moisture content:

$$N = R_c \cdot \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the line connecting the two points $(X_c, R_c)$ and $(X^*, 0)$ on the drying rate curve. The slope is $R_c / (X_c - X^*)$, and $N$ becomes zero at $X = X^*$ (which is the physical definition of equilibrium moisture).

### Step 2: Substitute into the Mass Balance

$$-\dfrac{L}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt} = R_c \cdot \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange

$$dt = -\dfrac{L \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

### Step 4: Integrate from $X_c$ to $X_2$ and $0$ to $t_{f1}$

$$\int_0^{t_{f1}} dt = -\dfrac{L \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

### Step 5: Evaluate

$$t_{f1} = -\dfrac{L \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \Big[ \ln(X - X^*) \Big]_{X_c}^{X_2}$$

### Step 6: Final Form

$$\boxed{\,t_{f1} = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement

A wet solid weighing **L = 100 kg** of dry solid is dried from an initial moisture content $X_1 = \mathbf{0.40}$ kg/kg to a final moisture $X_2 = \mathbf{0.10}$ kg/kg (both dry basis). The drying area is $A = \mathbf{1.0}$ m². The critical moisture content is $X_c = \mathbf{0.20}$ kg/kg, and the equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = \mathbf{0.05}$ kg/kg. The constant drying rate is $R_c = \mathbf{1.0}$ kg/(m²·h). The falling rate is linear. Calculate the **total drying time**.

### Solution

**Part (a): Constant Rate Period**

$$t_c = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot R_c} = \dfrac{100 \cdot (0.40 - 0.20)}{1.0 \cdot 1.0}$$

$$t_c = \dfrac{100 \cdot 0.20}{1.0} = 20 \text{ h}$$

**Part (b): First Falling Rate Period**

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

Compute the terms:

$$X_c - X^* = 0.20 - 0.05 = 0.15$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.10 - 0.05 = 0.05$$

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{100 \cdot 0.15}{1.0 \cdot 1.0} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{0.15}{0.05} \right)$$

$$t_{f1} = 15 \cdot \ln(3) = 15 \cdot 1.0986$$

$$t_{f1} = 16.479 \text{ h}$$

**Part (c): Total Drying Time**

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_{f1} = 20 + 16.48$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{total} = 36.48 \text{ h}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Use?

| Condition Given | Formula to Apply | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| Only $X_1, X_c$ given, with constant rate period | $t_c$ formula | 3 – 4 marks |
| Falling rate linear, $X_2$ given | $t_{f1}$ formula (ln form) | 4 – 5 marks |
| Only falling rate exists ($X_1 < X_c$) | Use the same ln formula with $X_1$ in place of $X_c$ | 4 – 5 marks |
| Total drying time required | Add $t_c + t_{f1}$ | 2 marks |
| Drying rate curve given graphically | Use slope of falling rate line | 5 – 7 marks |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the following with respect to drying operations:**
**(a) Free moisture content**, **(b) Equilibrium moisture content**, **(c) Bound moisture**.

#### Model Answer

- **(a) Free Moisture Content:** The moisture in a solid in **excess of the equilibrium moisture content** at the prevailing drying conditions. It is the portion that can be removed by drying with air of given humidity and temperature. Mathematically, $X_{free} = X - X^*$.

- **(b) Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$):** The moisture content of a solid when it is in **equilibrium with the drying air** — i.e., the vapour pressure of moisture in the solid equals the partial pressure of water vapour in the air. It is the **lowest moisture content attainable** under those specific air conditions.

- **(c) Bound Moisture:** The moisture that is **retained by the solid at a vapour pressure less than that of pure water at the same temperature**. It is held within cell walls, capillaries, or by chemical adsorption, and is **more difficult to remove**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A common mistake is confusing *bound* and *unbound* moisture. Bound moisture is a property of the **solid**, whereas unbound moisture is a property of **drying conditions**. Equilibrium moisture is bound by definition.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate clearly between the constant rate period and the falling rate period of drying.**

#### Model Answer

| Feature | Constant Rate Period | Falling Rate Period |
|---|---|---|
| **Location on curve** | $X_1 \rightarrow X_c$ | $X_c \rightarrow X_2$ |
| **Controlling step** | External mass transfer (surface evaporation) | Internal mass transfer (diffusion) |
| **Rate behaviour** | Constant at $R_c$ | Decreases with decreasing $X$ |
| **Surface condition** | Surface remains fully wet | Surface progressively dries |
| **Driving force** | $(P_s - P_w)$ constant, controlled by air | Decreasing; depends on internal moisture |
| **Temperature** | Surface temperature ≈ wet-bulb temperature | Surface temperature rises towards air dry-bulb |
| **Equation form** | $t_c = (L/A)(X_1 - X_c)/R_c$ | $t_f = (L/A)(X_c - X^*)/R_c \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$ |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A batch dryer contains **L = 250 kg** of dry solid. The initial moisture content (dry basis) is **X₁ = 0.50 kg/kg** and the final required moisture is **X₂ = 0.08 kg/kg**. The critical moisture content is **Xc = 0.25 kg/kg**, the equilibrium moisture content is **X* = 0.04 kg/kg**, and the constant drying rate is **Rc = 1.5 kg/(m²·h)**. The drying surface area is **A = 5 m²**. The falling rate period is **linear**.

**Calculate:**
- (i) The **time of drying in the constant rate period**. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The **time of drying in the falling rate period**. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **drying rate curve**, explain the **four distinct periods** of drying and identify the rate-controlling step in each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a)(i): Constant Rate Period Time**

$$t_c = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot R_c}$$

$$t_c = \dfrac{250 \cdot (0.50 - 0.25)}{5 \cdot 1.5}$$
