$$t_c = \dfrac{250 \cdot 0.25}{7.5} = \dfrac{62.5}{7.5}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_c = 8.33 \text{ h}\,}$$

**Part (a)(ii): Falling Rate Period Time**

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

Compute the terms:

$$X_c - X^* = 0.25 - 0.04 = 0.21$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.08 - 0.04 = 0.04$$

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{250 \cdot 0.21}{5 \cdot 1.5} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{0.21}{0.04} \right)$$

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{52.5}{7.5} \cdot \ln(5.25)$$

$$t_{f1} = 7.0 \cdot 1.6582$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{f1} = 11.61 \text{ h}\,}$$

**Part (b): Drying Rate Curve — Four Periods**

The drying rate curve (plot of $N$ vs. $X$) shows four distinct regions:

1. **Period A → B (Initial Adjustment / Transient Period):**
   The wet solid is initially at ambient temperature. The surface temperature rises until it reaches the wet-bulb temperature of the air. The rate of drying **increases** from zero to $R_c$. Duration is short and usually neglected in design.

2. **Period B → C (Constant Rate Period):**
   The surface is **completely saturated** with moisture. The rate of drying is governed by **external mass transfer** — i.e., the diffusion of water vapour from the surface into the air. The rate is constant at $R_c$ and equals the rate of evaporation of water from a free water surface under the same air conditions.

3. **Period C → D (First Falling Rate Period):**
   The surface begins to dry out, and the **wetted surface area decreases**. The plane of evaporation **recedes into the solid**. The rate decreases roughly linearly with $X$. The rate of internal moisture migration is becoming comparable to the rate of surface evaporation.

4. **Period D → E (Second Falling Rate Period):**
   The surface is **completely dry**, and the plane of evaporation has moved **entirely inside the solid**. The rate is now controlled by **internal diffusion** of moisture (liquid or vapour) to the surface. The rate approaches zero as $X \to X^*$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students write "the rate decreases in the falling rate period" but **do not identify the rate-controlling step**. KTU valuation keys specifically require that the student name the controlling mechanism (internal diffusion / moisture migration) to earn full marks. Stating only the surface dryness is **incomplete** and may cost 2–3 marks.

---

### 📌 Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A wet solid is dried in a tray dryer. The following data were obtained during a batch experiment:

| Time (h) | Moisture content X (kg/kg dry basis) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.350 |
| 1 | 0.300 |
| 2 | 0.260 |
| 3 | 0.230 |
| 4 | 0.200 |
| 5 | 0.170 |
| 6 | 0.140 |
| 7 | 0.110 |
| 8 | 0.090 |
| 9 | 0.075 |
| 10 | 0.065 |

The equilibrium moisture content is **X* = 0.05 kg/kg** and the dry solid weight is **L = 10 kg** on a tray of area **A = 0.5 m²**.

From the data, identify the **critical moisture content Xc** and calculate the **constant drying rate Rc**. **(6 marks)**

**(b)** Using the value of Xc obtained, compute the **time required to dry the solid from Xc to X₂ = 0.08 kg/kg**, assuming the falling rate is **linear**. **(8 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a): Identifying Xc and Calculating Rc**

We plot the moisture content $X$ vs. time $t$. The **constant rate period** corresponds to the linear portion (where $X$ decreases linearly with $t$).

From the data:
- $t = 0 \rightarrow X = 0.350$
- $t = 4 \rightarrow X = 0.200$

This is a **linear drop of $\Delta X = 0.150$ kg/kg over $\Delta t = 4$ h**.

The critical moisture is the value of $X$ **at the end of the constant rate period** (where the curve first deviates from linearity). From the data, the break occurs between $t = 4$ and $t = 5$:

$$\boxed{\,X_c = 0.20 \text{ kg/kg}\,}$$

**Constant Drying Rate:**

$$R_c = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot t_c} = \dfrac{10 \cdot (0.350 - 0.200)}{0.5 \cdot 4}$$

$$R_c = \dfrac{10 \cdot 0.15}{2.0} = \dfrac{1.5}{2.0}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_c = 0.75 \text{ kg / m}^2 \cdot \text{h}\,}$$

**Part (b): Falling Rate Period Time**

Given: $X_c = 0.20$, $X^* = 0.05$, $X_2 = 0.08$, $L = 10$ kg, $A = 0.5$ m², $R_c = 0.75$ kg/(m²·h).

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{L \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

Compute the terms:

$$X_c - X^* = 0.20 - 0.05 = 0.15$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.08 - 0.05 = 0.03$$

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 0.15}{0.5 \cdot 0.75} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{0.15}{0.03} \right)$$

$$t_{f1} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.375} \cdot \ln(5)$$

$$t_{f1} = 4.0 \cdot 1.6094$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{f1} = 6.44 \text{ h}\,}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Units mismatch:** $R_c$ is in kg/(m²·h), not kg/h. Forgetting $A$ in the denominator = full mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing $X_c$ and $X^*$:** $X_c$ is the **break point** in the drying curve; $X^*$ is the **thermodynamic limit**. These are two different concepts.
> 3. **Using wet-basis instead of dry-basis moisture content:** KTU standard is **dry basis**. Using wet basis gives an answer that is **numerically wrong by 30–40 %**.
> 4. **Forgetting the logarithm** in the falling rate formula — many students write the falling rate formula as if it were the constant rate formula with $X_c$ replaced by $X_2$. This loses 4 out of 7 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the rate-controlling step** in descriptive parts = loses 2 marks easily.

---

## 📚 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Drying** = thermal removal of moisture from solids; it is a **simultaneous heat and mass transfer** operation.
- ✅ **Moisture content X** is expressed on a **dry basis**: $X = (W - W_s)/W_s$.
- ✅ The **drying rate curve** has **four regions**: A→B (transient), B→C (constant), C→D (1st falling), D→E (2nd falling).
- ✅ The **constant rate period** is controlled by **external mass transfer** (vapour removal from surface).
- ✅ The **falling rate period** is controlled by **internal moisture migration** (liquid/vapour diffusion).
- ✅ The **critical moisture content $X_c$** is the **transition point** between constant and falling rate behaviour.
- ✅ The **equilibrium moisture content $X^*$** is the **lowest attainable** moisture under given air conditions; it **cannot be removed by air** of the same humidity.
- ✅ **Constant rate drying time:** $t_c = \dfrac{L (X_1 - X_c)}{A R_c}$.
- ✅ **Falling rate drying time (linear, $N \propto X$):** $t_{f1} = \dfrac{L (X_c - X^*)}{A R_c} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$.
- ✅ **Total drying time** = $t_c + t_{f1}$ (+ $t_{f2}$ if the second falling rate exists).
- ✅ **Bound moisture** = property of the **solid**; **Unbound moisture** = property of the **drying conditions**.
- ✅ **No constant rate period** if $X_1 < X_c$; the entire drying then occurs in the falling rate regime.
- ✅ Always maintain **unit consistency**: $L$ in kg, $A$ in m², $R_c$ in kg/(m²·h), and $X$ in kg water/kg dry solid.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat and Mass Transfer in Drying Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat and Mass Transfer in Drying Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Drying (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Drying** is a unit operation in which a volatile liquid (usually water) is removed from a wet solid, semi-solid, or liquid by **thermal means** involving the simultaneous application of **heat transfer** (from the heating medium to the material) and **mass transfer** (of moisture from the interior of the solid to the surface and then to the bulk gas phase).

Mathematically, drying can be defined as a process where the **moisture content** of a material is reduced from an initial value $X_1$ to a final value $X_2$ by vaporization of the liquid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Must Memorize):** Drying is the final separation operation in a process train where the solid product is required in dry form, involving coupled heat and mass transfer through a gas-phase driving force.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: Drying Like Sun-Drying Laundry

Imagine spreading wet clothes on a clothesline on a sunny day:
- **Heat Source** → The Sun (supplies energy to evaporate water)
- **Wet Solid** → The wet fabric (holds the moisture)
- **Mass Transfer** → Water molecules escaping from the fabric surface into the air
- **Driving Force** → Difference between the vapor pressure of water at the fabric surface and the partial pressure of water vapor in the surrounding air

The faster the wind blows (high mass transfer coefficient) and the hotter the sun shines (high temperature, more energy), the quicker the clothes dry. Industrial drying is simply a controlled, engineered, and accelerated version of this same natural phenomenon.

### 1.3 Classification of Drying

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask students to classify drying. Remember the three primary classifications:

| Classification Basis | Types | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Operation** | Batch Drying | Material loaded, dried, and unloaded in cycles |
| | Continuous Drying | Material fed and discharged continuously |
| **Heat Supply Method** | Convection Drying | Hot gas contacts the wet solid (most common) |
| | Conduction Drying | Heat supplied through hot surfaces (e.g., drum dryers) |
| | Radiation Drying | Infrared or microwave radiation supplies heat |
| **Operating Pressure** | Atmospheric Drying | At 1 atm pressure |
| | Vacuum Drying | Under reduced pressure (for heat-sensitive materials) |

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

- **Moisture Content ($X$):** Mass of moisture per unit mass of **dry solid** (kg water/kg dry solid)
- **Wet Basis Moisture Content ($w$):** Mass of moisture per unit mass of **wet solid** (kg water/kg wet solid)
- **Free Moisture Content ($X^*$):** Moisture content above the equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$)
- **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$):** Minimum moisture content a material attains when exposed to a given air condition for infinite time
- **Bound Moisture:** Moisture held tightly within the cell walls, exerting a vapor pressure less than pure water at the same temperature
- **Unbound Moisture:** Moisture that exerts a vapor pressure equal to pure water at the same temperature
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Coupled Heat and Mass Transfer Mechanism

Drying is fundamentally a **simultaneous heat and mass transfer process** governed by two transport phenomena occurring in parallel:

**Step 1: Heat Transfer (Energy Supply)**
- Heat is transferred from the hot drying gas (or hot surface) to the wet solid surface.
- Driving force: Temperature difference $(T_{gas} - T_{surface})$
- Mode: Convection (most common), Conduction, or Radiation

**Step 2: Mass Transfer (Moisture Removal)**
- Moisture migrates from the interior of the solid to the surface by:
  - **Liquid diffusion** (through pores by molecular/knudsen diffusion)
  - **Capillary flow** (in porous materials)
  - **Vapor diffusion** (in already-dried pores)
- At the surface, moisture evaporates and diffuses into the gas stream.
- Driving force: Partial pressure difference $(p_{A,surface} - p_{A,bulk})$

**Step 3: Equilibrium Establishment**
- When the surface vapor pressure equals the bulk gas partial pressure, drying stops → this defines the **equilibrium moisture content**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Coupling:** Evaporating moisture requires latent heat. The heat that supplies this latent heat must first be conducted through the dried outer layer (which is a poor conductor). As drying proceeds, the dried layer thickens, increasing thermal resistance and slowing heat transfer — this is exactly why the drying rate decreases with time.

### 2.2 Moisture Content Relationships (Conversions)

The relationship between **dry basis** ($X$) and **wet basis** ($w$) moisture content:

$$X = \frac{w}{1 - w}$$

$$w = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Note:** Memorize these formulas verbatim. They appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on drying.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = k_y (Y_s - Y) = k_x (X - X^*)$ | Mass transfer rate of moisture | kg/m²·s |
| 2 | $q = h (T_g - T_s) = \lambda N_A$ | Heat transfer rate linked to mass transfer | W/m² |
| 3 | $R_c = -\frac{L_s}{A} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$ | Drying rate definition | kg/m²·hr |
| 4 | $t = \frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_2}^{X_1} \frac{dX}{R_c}$ | Total drying time (general) | s or hr |
| 5 | $t_c = \frac{L_s}{A \cdot R_c}(X_1 - X_c)$ | Constant rate period time | s or hr |
| 6 | $t_f = \frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{R}$ | Falling rate period time | s or hr |
| 7 | $N_c = \frac{h}{\rho_s \lambda} (T_g - T_w)$ | Critical moisture content (empirical) | kg/kg |
| 8 | $R = -\frac{L_s}{A} \cdot \frac{dX}{d\theta} = R_c \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$ | Linear falling rate equation | kg/m²·hr |
| 9 | $X^* = f(\phi, T)$ | Equilibrium moisture content function | kg/kg |
| 10 | $\theta = \frac{L_s (X^* - X_c) \ln\left[\frac{X_1 - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]}{A \cdot R_c}$ | Total time for first falling rate | s or hr |

Where:
- $L_s$ = Mass of dry solid (kg)
- $A$ = Drying surface area (m²)
- $h$ = Heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $\lambda$ = Latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)
- $\phi$ = Relative humidity
- $\rho_s$ = Density of dry solid (kg/m³)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Drying is critical in:
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Production of tablets, antibiotics, and active ingredients
- **Food Industry:** Milk powder, instant coffee, cereal grains, fruit preservation
- **Chemical Industry:** Polymers, dyes, pigments, fertilizers
- **Paper and Pulp Industry:** Paper sheet drying
- **Cement Industry:** Drying of limestone, clay, mineral ores
- **Ceramics Industry:** Drying of green ceramic bodies before firing
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass Balance Over a Drying Element (Differential Element)

Consider a differential slice of wet solid of thickness $dz$ at time $t$ with moisture content $X$. During an infinitesimal time $dt$:

**Total moisture lost from slice = Mass transferred out at top + Mass transferred out at bottom**

Let $N_A$ be the mass flux of water vapor leaving the surface. For a drying area $A$:

$$\text{Mass of water lost} = -L_s \cdot dX = N_A \cdot A \cdot dt$$

Rearranging for the drying rate $R_c$ (mass of water removed per unit area per unit time):

$$R_c = -\frac{L_s}{A} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

> **Logic Step:** The negative sign indicates that moisture content $X$ decreases with time $t$, so $dX$ is negative, making $R_c$ positive.

### 3.2 Derivation: Constant Rate Drying Period Time

In the **constant rate period**, the surface remains saturated with moisture, and $R_c$ is independent of $X$ (i.e., $R_c$ = constant). Integrating the general drying time equation from $X_1$ to $X_c$:

$$t_c = \int_{0}^{t_c} dt = -\frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{R_c}$$

Since $R_c$ is constant:

$$t_c = -\frac{L_s}{A \cdot R_c} \left[ X \right]_{X_1}^{X_c}$$

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{L_s \cdot (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot R_c}}$$

> **Logic Step:** The integration is straightforward because $R_c$ is a constant that can be pulled out of the integral. The negative signs cancel because $X_1 > X_c$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Total Drying Time (Linear Falling Rate)

In the **first falling rate period**, $R$ varies linearly with $X$:

$$R = R_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

The drying time for the falling rate period is:

$$t_f = \frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{R}$$

Substituting the expression for $R$:

$$t_f = \frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{(X_c - X^*) \, dX}{R_c \cdot (X - X^*)}$$

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

Performing the integration using $\int \frac{dX}{X - X^*} = \ln(X - X^*)$:

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \left[ \ln(X - X^*) \right]_{X_c}^{X_2}$$

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*} \right)$$

The total drying time is:

$$\boxed{\theta = t_c + t_f = \frac{L_s}{A \cdot R_c} \left[ (X_1 - X_c) + (X_c - X^*) \ln\left( \frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*} \right) \right]}$$

> **Logic Step:** The logarithmic term arises because the falling rate is proportional to $(X - X^*)$, so its inverse $(X - X^*)^{-1}$ integrates to a natural logarithm.

### 3.4 Worked Example: KTU Board-Style Problem

**Problem:** A wet solid weighing **500 kg** (dry basis) is dried from an initial moisture content of **0.40 kg water/kg dry solid** to a final moisture of **0.05 kg water/kg dry solid**. The drying surface area is **2 m²**. The critical moisture content is **0.20** and the equilibrium moisture content is **0.02**. If the constant rate $R_c = 1.5$ kg/m²·hr, calculate the **total drying time** assuming linear falling rate.

**Given:**
- $L_s = 500$ kg, $A = 2$ m²
- $X_1 = 0.40$, $X_2 = 0.05$
- $X_c = 0.20$, $X^* = 0.02$
- $R_c = 1.5$ kg/m²·hr

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Constant Rate Period Time**

$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot R_c} = \frac{500 \times (0.40 - 0.20)}{2 \times 1.5}$$

$$t_c = \frac{500 \times 0.20}{3.0} = \frac{100}{3.0} = 33.33 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 2: Falling Rate Period Time**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{500 \times (0.20 - 0.02)}{2 \times 1.5} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{0.05 - 0.02}{0.20 - 0.02} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{500 \times 0.18}{3.0} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{0.03}{0.18} \right)$$

$$t_f = 30 \times \ln(0.1667) = 30 \times (-1.7918) = -53.75 \text{ hr}$$

$$t_f = 53.75 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3: Total Drying Time**

$$\theta = t_c + t_f = 33.33 + 53.75 = 87.08 \text{ hr}$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 87.08 \text{ hours}}$$

### 3.5 Heat Transfer Coefficient from Drying Data

If the latent heat of vaporization $\lambda$ (J/kg) and the wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ are known, the heat transfer coefficient $h$ can be calculated from the constant drying rate:

$$R_c = \frac{h}{\lambda \cdot \rho_s} (T_g - T_w)$$

Rearranging:

$$h = \frac{R_c \cdot \lambda \cdot \rho_s}{T_g - T_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption:** During the constant rate period, the surface temperature equals the wet-bulb temperature of the air (i.e., the surface is saturated with water).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Architecture: Industrial Convection Drying System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wet Solid Feed] --> B[Feed Conveyor]
    B --> C[Drying Chamber]
    D[Air Heater] --> E[Hot Air Blower]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Particulate Cyclone Separator]
    F --> G[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    C --> H[Discharge Conveyor]
    H --> I[Cooling Cyclone]
    I --> J[Dried Product Storage]
    F --> K[Dust Collection System]
    K --> L[Recycled Fine Particles]
    L --> C
    D --> M[Steam or Natural Gas]
    M --> D
    style A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style J fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style C fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style G fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The hot air (heated by the steam/natural gas heater) is blown by the blower into the drying chamber where it contacts the wet solid. The wet solid releases moisture to the hot air, which becomes humid. The humid air passes through the cyclone separator to remove entrained solid particles before exhausting to the atmosphere. The dried solid is discharged and cooled before storage.

### 4.2 Functional Block: Coupled Heat and Mass Transfer Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Hot Gas to Surface Heat Transfer] --> S2[Step 2: Surface Evaporation]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Internal Moisture Migration]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Vapor Diffusion to Bulk Gas]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Equilibrium Establishment]
    subgraph DRIVING_FORCE_A
    S1
    end
    subgraph DRIVING_FORCE_B
    S4
    end
    style S1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style S2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style S3 fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E
    style S4 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B
    style S5 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Step 1 shows the heat transfer from hot gas to the wet solid surface (driven by temperature difference). Step 2 is the surface evaporation. Step 3 depicts internal moisture movement (by liquid diffusion or capillary flow). Step 4 shows the mass transfer of vapor from the surface to the bulk gas (driven by vapor pressure difference). Step 5 is the equilibrium state when driving forces vanish.

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Drying Rate Curve Periods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start: Wet Solid at X1] --> P1[Adjustment Period]
    P1 --> P2[Constant Rate Period - X1 to Xc]
    P2 --> P3[First Falling Rate Period - Xc to X2]
    P3 --> P4[Second Falling Rate Period - X2 to X*]
    P4 --> P5[Equilibrium State at X*]
    P0 --> M1[Moisture X axis]
    P2 --> M2[Surface Saturated with Water]
    P3 --> M3[Dry Front Recedes into Solid]
    P4 --> M4[Internal Diffusion Controlled]
    P5 --> M5[Drying Stops at Equilibrium]
    style P2 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17
    style P3 fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100
    style P4 fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#BF360C
    style P5 fill:#90A4AE,stroke:#37474F
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The drying process exhibits four distinct periods. In the constant rate period, the surface remains saturated. In the first falling rate period, dry patches begin to appear. In the second falling rate period, the dry front has moved deep inside the solid. At equilibrium, drying stops completely.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the following terms: (i) Equilibrium moisture content, (ii) Free moisture content, (iii) Bound moisture.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$):** It is the moisture content of a material when it is in equilibrium with the surrounding air at a given temperature and humidity. At this state, the material neither gains nor loses moisture. It is a function of the material, air temperature, and relative humidity.

(ii) **Free Moisture Content ($X - X^*$):** It is the moisture content of a material in excess of the equilibrium moisture content. This is the moisture that can be removed by drying under the given air conditions.

(iii) **Bound Moisture:** It is the moisture that is held within the cellular structure of the material such that it exerts a vapor pressure lower than that of pure water at the same temperature. Bound moisture cannot be removed easily by ordinary drying.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between constant rate drying and falling rate drying periods.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Constant Rate Period | Falling Rate Period |
|---|---|---|
| Surface condition | Saturated with moisture | Dry patches appear on surface |
| Rate controlling step | External mass transfer | Internal moisture migration |
| Surface temperature | Equals wet-bulb temperature | Rises above wet-bulb temperature |
| Drying rate | Independent of moisture content | Depends on moisture content |
| Rate equation | $R = R_c$ (constant) | $R = R_c (X - X^*)/(X_c - X^*)$ (linear) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A wet solid containing **30% moisture** (wet basis) is to be dried to **5% moisture** (wet basis) in a tray dryer. The initial mass of the wet solid is **600 kg**. Calculate:
(i) The initial and final moisture content on a dry basis
(ii) The mass of water to be evaporated per kg of dry solid

**(b) [7 Marks]** A batch dryer is loaded with **400 kg** of dry solid containing **0.35 kg water/kg dry solid**. The drying surface area is **1.5 m²**. The constant drying rate is **1.2 kg/m²·hr** and the critical moisture content is **0.15 kg/kg dry solid**. Calculate the time required to dry the material to **0.08 kg/kg dry solid**. Assume the equilibrium moisture content is **0.02 kg/kg dry solid** and the falling rate is linear.

---

**Model Solution for Q3(a):**

**Given:** Initial moisture $w_1 = 0.30$ (wet basis), Final moisture $w_2 = 0.05$ (wet basis)

**Step 1: Conversion of wet basis to dry basis**

$$X_1 = \frac{w_1}{1 - w_1} = \frac{0.30}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

$$X_2 = \frac{w_2}{1 - w_2} = \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2: Mass of water to be evaporated**

For 600 kg of wet solid, mass of dry solid:
$$L_s = 600 \times (1 - 0.30) = 600 \times 0.70 = 420 \text{ kg}$$

Total water to be removed:
$$\text{Water} = L_s \times (X_1 - X_2) = 420 \times (0.4286 - 0.0526)$$

$$\text{Water} = 420 \times 0.376 = 157.92 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{X_1 = 0.4286, \quad X_2 = 0.0526, \quad \text{Water removed} = 157.92 \text{ kg}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q3(b):**

**Given:** $L_s = 400$ kg, $A = 1.5$ m², $X_1 = 0.35$, $X_2 = 0.08$, $X_c = 0.15$, $X^* = 0.02$, $R_c = 1.2$ kg/m²·hr

**Step 1: Constant Rate Period Time**

Since $X_1 = 0.35 > X_c = 0.15$, drying begins in the constant rate period.

$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot R_c} = \frac{400 \times (0.35 - 0.15)}{1.5 \times 1.2}$$

$$t_c = \frac{400 \times 0.20}{1.8} = \frac{80}{1.8} = 44.44 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 2: Falling Rate Period Time**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{400 \times (0.15 - 0.02)}{1.5 \times 1.2} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{0.08 - 0.02}{0.15 - 0.02} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{400 \times 0.13}{1.8} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{0.06}{0.13} \right)$$

$$t_f = 28.89 \times \ln(0.4615) = 28.89 \times (-0.7732) = -22.34 \text{ hr}$$

$$t_f = 22.34 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3: Total Drying Time**

$$\theta = t_c + t_f = 44.44 + 22.34 = 66.78 \text{ hr}$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 66.78 \text{ hours}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **drying rate curve** and explain the different periods of drying. Discuss the significance of **critical moisture content** and **equilibrium moisture content**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A continuous tray dryer handles **800 kg/hr** of wet solid with an initial moisture content of **1.5 kg water/kg dry solid**. The solid is dried to a final moisture of **0.10 kg water/kg dry solid**. The drying takes place in two stages:
- Stage 1 (Constant rate): $R_c = 2.5$ kg/m²·hr, $X_c = 0.6$ kg/kg
- Stage 2 (Linear falling rate): $X^* = 0.05$ kg/kg
If the total drying area is **12 m²**, determine the total drying time.

---

**Model Solution for Q4(a):**

The **drying rate curve** plots drying rate $R$ (kg/m²·hr) on the y-axis against moisture content $X$ (kg/kg dry solid) on the x-axis. The curve has the following regions:

1. **Initial Adjustment Period (A-B):** Brief transient where the solid heats up to wet-bulb temperature.

2. **Constant Rate Period (B-C):** The surface is fully saturated with moisture. Drying rate is constant and equals the rate of evaporation from a free water surface. The rate is controlled by **external mass/heat transfer**. Surface temperature equals the wet-bulb temperature.

3. **First Falling Rate Period (C-D):** The surface begins to dry out, and the **dry front recedes** into the solid. Both external and internal resistances control the rate.

4. **Second Falling Rate Period (D-E):** The dry front is deep inside the solid. The rate is controlled by **internal moisture diffusion** only.

5. **Equilibrium Point (E):** Drying rate approaches zero when $X = X^*$.

**Significance of Critical Moisture content ($X_c$):**
- Marks the transition from constant rate to falling rate
- Indicates the point where surface dryness begins
- Higher $X_c$ means shorter constant rate period and longer falling rate period
- Depends on material type, thickness, and drying conditions

**Significance of Equilibrium Moisture content ($X^*$):**
- The absolute lower limit of drying under given air conditions
- Cannot be removed by conventional drying
- Function of relative humidity and temperature of the air
- Different for different materials (hygroscopic vs non-hygroscopic)

---

**Model Solution for Q4(b):**

**Given:** $L_s = 800$ kg/hr (wet solid with $X_1 = 1.5$), $X_1 = 1.5$, $X_2 = 0.10$, $X_c = 0.6$, $X^* = 0.05$, $R_c = 2.5$ kg/m²·hr, $A = 12$ m²

**Step 1: Mass of dry solid**

$$L_s = 800 \times \frac{1}{1 + 1.5} = 800 \times 0.4 = 320 \text{ kg dry solid/hr}$$

**Step 2: Constant Rate Period Time**

$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot R_c} = \frac{320 \times (1.5 - 0.6)}{12 \times 2.5}$$

$$t_c = \frac{320 \times 0.9}{30} = \frac{288}{30} = 9.6 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3: Falling Rate Period Time**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot R_c} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{320 \times (0.6 - 0.05)}{12 \times 2.5} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{0.10 - 0.05}{0.6 - 0.05} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{320 \times 0.55}{30} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{0.05}{0.55} \right)$$

$$t_f = 5.867 \times \ln(0.0909) = 5.867 \times (-2.3979) = -14.07 \text{ hr}$$

$$t_f = 14.07 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 4: Total Drying Time**

$$\theta = t_c + t_f = 9.6 + 14.07 = 23.67 \text{ hr}$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 23.67 \text{ hours}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often confuse wet basis and dry basis moisture content. Always verify whether the problem gives wet basis or dry basis and convert appropriately.
> 2. **Missing Equilibrium Moisture Content:** When calculating falling rate time, forgetting to subtract $X^*$ from both $X_c$ and $X_2$ is the most common error.
> 3. **Logarithm Sign:** For falling rate, $(X_2 - X^*) < (X_c - X^*)$, so the logarithm is **negative** — students often forget the negative sign.
> 4. **Area Units:** Ensure drying area $A$ is in **m²** and rate $R_c$ is in **kg/m²·hr** (or consistent units).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Drying = Simultaneous heat and mass transfer by thermal means
- ✅ Moisture content dry basis: $X = w/(1-w)$; wet basis: $w = X/(1+X)$
- ✅ Constant rate period: Surface saturated; rate controlled by external heat/mass transfer
- ✅ Falling rate period: Surface dries out; rate controlled by internal moisture migration
- ✅ Critical moisture content ($X_c$): Transition point between periods
- ✅ Equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$): Lower limit of drying; function of $\phi$ and $T$
- ✅ Bound moisture: Vapor pressure < pure water; cannot be easily removed
- ✅ Unbound moisture: Vapor pressure = pure water; easily removed
- ✅ Constant rate time: $t_c = L_s (X_1 - X_c) / (A \cdot R_c)$
- ✅ Linear falling rate time: $t_f = [L_s (X_c - X^*) / (A \cdot R_c)] \cdot \ln[(X_2 - X^*)/(X_c - X^*)]$
- ✅ Total time $\theta = t_c + t_f$
- ✅ Drying rate: $R = -L_s \cdot dX/(A \cdot dt)$
- ✅ Heat-mass coupling: $q = h(T_g - T_s) = \lambda \cdot N_A$
- ✅ Industrial dryers: Tray, Rotary, Spray, Fluidized Bed, Drum, Tunnel
- ✅ $R_c$ depends on air velocity, temperature, humidity, and material geometry
- ✅ Higher air velocity and temperature increase $R_c$, reducing total drying time
- ✅ Convection dryers are most common; conduction for slurries; vacuum for heat-sensitive materials
- ✅ Always check units consistency: kg, m², hr (or seconds)
- ✅ Natural logarithm ($\ln$) is used; not $\log_{10}$ in drying time equations
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Dryers - Vacuum Dryers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Commercial Dryers - Vacuum Dryers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Vacuum Dryer** is a closed-vessel commercial drying equipment that operates at sub-atmospheric pressures (typically between **1 mmHg to 50 mmHg absolute**) to effect moisture removal from wet solids. By lowering the absolute pressure inside the drying chamber, the boiling point of the moisture (usually water) is significantly reduced, enabling effective drying at temperatures well below **100°C** — frequently in the range of **30°C to 80°C**.

Vacuum drying is classified under **indirect/contact-type drying** operations, where heat is supplied primarily through conduction from heated internal surfaces (e.g., steam-heated shelves, jacketed vessels, or heated agitator blades) rather than through direct hot-gas contact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Vacuum dryers are an essential subclass of commercial dryers under *Module 4 – Drying Operations*. The focus is on the **operational principle, constructional features, design equations, and industrial applicability**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine boiling water on a mountain. At high altitudes, atmospheric pressure is lower, so water boils at, say, **80°C** instead of 100°C. Now, picture the reverse: instead of climbing a mountain, we *remove* air from a sealed pot containing wet cloth. The "air pressure" drops, water in the cloth vaporizes at a much lower temperature, and the cloth dries quickly — yet it never sees a high temperature that could scorch it.

**Real-world parallel:** This is exactly how pharmaceutical companies dry heat-sensitive antibiotics, vitamins, and enzymes. The medicine is dried at 40°C under vacuum rather than 100°C in air — preserving biological activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why vacuum?**
> 1. **Protects heat-sensitive materials** (pharma, food, biotech products).
> 2. **Prevents oxidation** of products that degrade in the presence of oxygen.
> 3. **Lower energy cost** for heating (you heat to lower temperature).
> 4. **Faster drying** at low temperature because the partial pressure driving force for moisture migration increases.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating pressure | **1 – 50** | mmHg (absolute) |
| Operating temperature | **30 – 80** | °C |
| Latent heat of vaporization (water, at 30°C) | **~2,430** | kJ/kg |
| Latent heat of vaporization (water, at 80°C) | **~2,308** | kJ/kg |
| Vacuum pump capacity | **10 – 500** | m³/h |
| Saturation pressure of water at 30°C | **31.8** | mmHg |
| Saturation pressure of water at 60°C | **149.4** | mmHg |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind Vacuum Drying

The drying operation hinges on the **Clausius-Clapeyron relationship**, which dictates that the saturation vapor pressure of water is a strong function of temperature:

1. **Reduction in Pressure → Reduction in Boiling Point:** By evacuating the drying chamber, the total system pressure drops below the saturation pressure of water at the operating temperature. This causes moisture to flash-evaporate at that temperature.

2. **Heat is Supplied Indirectly:** Since direct contact with hot air is impractical under vacuum, heat is transferred via:
   - **Conduction** through heated walls/shelves/agitator surfaces.
   - **Radiation** at moderate temperatures (for double-cone and rotary vacuum dryers).
   - **Convection** (limited; only from the re-evaporated vapor itself).

3. **Continuous Moisture Removal:** The vapor formed is drawn out by the **vacuum pump** and is typically condensed in a **barometric or surface condenser** placed upstream of the pump. Non-condensable gases are vented.

4. **Mass Transfer Driving Force:** The driving force is the difference between the **partial pressure of water vapor at the solid surface (P_sat at T_surface)** and the **partial pressure of water vapor in the bulk gas (P_vapor in chamber)**.

$$N_A = k_y (P_{sat}(T_{surface}) - P_{vapor,bulk})$$

where $N_A$ is the molar flux of water vapor and $k_y$ is the mass transfer coefficient.

### 2.2 Constructional Features of a Typical Vacuum Shelf Dryer

A standard **vacuum shelf dryer** consists of:

- **Cast-iron or steel rectangular chamber** with a hinged vacuum-tight door.
- **Stacked hollow shelves** through which hot water, steam, or thermal oil circulates.
- **Heating medium manifold** for inlet/outlet.
- **Vacuum line** connected to a condenser and vacuum pump (steam-jet ejector or mechanical pump).
- **Vapor outlet duct** leading to the condenser.
- **Pressure gauge and thermometers** for monitoring.
- **Tight sealing gaskets** (typically rubber or PTFE) to maintain vacuum.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{sat}(T) = \exp\left(A - \dfrac{B}{T+C}\right)$ | Antoine equation for water vapor pressure | mmHg or Pa |
| 2 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Clausius-Clapeyron (vapor pressure vs. T) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer through dryer walls (conduction-dominated) | W (watts) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = -k(X - X^*)$ | First-order drying kinetics (free moisture) | 1/s |
| 5 | $N_c = \dfrac{t_{cycle}}{t_{batch}}$ | Number of cycles per day | dimensionless |
| 6 | $E = \dfrac{m_{evap}}{m_{steam}}$ | Steam economy | kg water/kg steam |
| 7 | $X = \dfrac{m_{moisture}}{m_{dry\,solid}}$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | kg water/kg solid |
| 8 | $W = \lambda \cdot \dot{m}_{evap}$ | Heat duty (latent heat dominated) | kW |
| 9 | $Q_{cond} = U_c A_c \Delta T_{cond}$ | Condenser duty | W |
| 10 | $t_{dry} = \dfrac{m_s(X_1 - X_2)}{A \cdot N_A}$ | Drying time from molar flux | s |

Where:
- $X$ = moisture content (kg water/kg dry solid)
- $X^*$ = equilibrium moisture content
- $k$ = drying rate constant (1/s)
- $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg)
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = heat transfer area (m²)
- $\dot{m}_{evap}$ = evaporation rate (kg/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Vacuum dryers dominate in **pharmaceutical (lyophilized injectables), food (instant coffee, spices), electronics (ceramic capacitors), and fine chemicals (catalysts, pigments)** industries. Their ability to dry at low temperatures makes them irreplaceable for products whose quality degrades above **40°C–60°C**.

### 2.4 Types of Vacuum Dryers (Industrial Classification)

1. **Vacuum Shelf (Tray) Dryer** — batch; horizontal shelves with trays.
2. **Vacuum Rotary Dryer** — batch; rotating horizontal shell.
3. **Double-Cone Vacuum Dryer** — batch; tumbling action, excellent mixing.
4. **Agitated Vacuum Pan Dryer (Ribbon/Paddle)** — batch; internal agitator.
5. **Vacuum Belt (Continuous) Dryer** — semi-continuous; heated belt under vacuum hood.
6. **Freeze Dryer (Lyophilizer)** — batch; operates at very low pressures (sub-1 mmHg).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Clausius-Clapeyron Equation Applied to Vacuum Drying

The Clausius-Clapeyron equation relates saturation pressure and temperature for a pure substance. For a vapor–liquid equilibrium (water under vacuum), starting from the equality of chemical potentials:

$$\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{vap}}$$

**Step 1:** Assume water vapor behaves as an **ideal gas**, and liquid volume is negligible compared to vapor volume:

$$\Delta V_{vap} \approx V_v = \dfrac{RT}{P_{sat}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the equation:

$$\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P_{sat}}{R \cdot T^2}$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables:

$$\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{P_{sat}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate between states (P₁, T₁) and (P₂, T₂), assuming $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant over the range:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP_{sat}}{P_{sat}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 (Numerical application):** Calculate the boiling point of water at a chamber pressure of **50 mmHg**, given the normal boiling point **100°C at 760 mmHg**, with $\Delta H_{vap} = 40,656$ J/mol and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

Let $T_1 = 373.15$ K, $P_1 = 760$ mmHg, $P_2 = 50$ mmHg, and we solve for $T_2$:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{50}{760}\right) = -\dfrac{40{,}656}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{373.15}\right)$$

$$-2.728 = -4889.8 \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 0.002680\right)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 0.002680 = \dfrac{-2.728}{-4889.8} = 5.580 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 0.002680 + 0.0005580 = 0.003238$$

$$T_2 = \dfrac{1}{0.003238} = 308.83 \text{ K} = 35.68 \text{°C}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Operating the dryer at 50 mmHg allows drying at just **35.7°C** instead of 100°C — a **64°C temperature reduction**, which is the foundation of vacuum drying's heat-sensitivity advantage.

### 3.2 Derivation: Drying Time for Batch Vacuum Shelf Dryer

**Step 1:** Define the moisture to be removed per batch:

$$m_{water} = m_s (X_1 - X_2)$$

where $m_s$ is mass of dry solid, $X_1$ and $X_2$ are initial and final moisture contents (dry basis).

**Step 2:** Define the heat transfer rate through the shelf:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{solid})$$

**Step 3:** Mass of water evaporated per unit time (using energy balance, latent-heat dominated):

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{U \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{solid})}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4:** Drying time:

$$t_{dry} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{\dot{m}_{water}} = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_2) \cdot \lambda}{U \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{solid})}$$

This is the **fundamental vacuum-dryer design equation**.

**Step 5 (Numerical Example):** A vacuum shelf dryer holds **100 kg** of dry solid, dried from **X₁ = 0.80** to **X₂ = 0.05** (kg water/kg dry solid). Heat transfer area **A = 5 m²**, overall $U = 80$ W/(m²·K), shelf temperature $T_{shelf} = 60°C$, solid temperature $T_{solid} = 40°C$, $\lambda = 2,400$ kJ/kg.

$$m_{water} = 100 \times (0.80 - 0.05) = 75 \text{ kg}$$

$$Q = 80 \times 5 \times (60-40) = 8{,}000 \text{ W} = 8 \text{ kW}$$

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \dfrac{8}{2400/3600} = \dfrac{8}{0.6667} = 12 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$t_{dry} = \dfrac{75}{12} = 6.25 \text{ hours}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting a Vacuum Dryer Type

| Material Characteristic | Best Vacuum Dryer Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Heat-sensitive pharmaceutical powder | Double-cone / Agitated pan | Gentle tumbling, low T, no hot spots |
| Free-flowing granules | Vacuum rotary | Continuous tumbling, good heat transfer |
| Pastes or slurries | Vacuum shelf (tray) | Static contact with hot shelves |
| Biological cultures / enzymes | Freeze dryer | Avoids denaturation via sublimation |
| Large-volume continuous operation | Vacuum belt dryer | Continuous throughput |
| Heat-sensitive organic crystals | Agitated vacuum pan | Avoids localized overheating |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Vacuum Shelf Dryer Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wet Solid Loaded onto Trays] --> B[Chamber Sealed and Door Closed]
    B --> C[Vacuum Pump Evacuates Air to 5-50 mmHg]
    C --> D[Steam Circulates Through Hollow Shelves at 60-90 C]
    D --> E[Solid Heats by Conduction Through Tray Base]
    E --> F[Moisture Evaporates at Reduced Pressure Below Boiling Point]
    F --> G[Water Vapor Leaves Solid Surface]
    G --> H[Barometric Condenser Condenses Vapor to Liquid]
    H --> I[Vacuum Pump Removes Non-Condensable Gases]
    I --> J[Chamber Returns to Atmospheric Pressure]
    J --> K[Dried Solid Unloaded for Packaging]
    style A fill:#E8F4F8
    style C fill:#FFE5B4
    style D fill:#FFE5B4
    style F fill:#FFB6C1
    style H fill:#98FB98
    style K fill:#98FB98
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Vacuum Drying System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Drying Chamber]
        S1[Trays with Wet Material]
        S2[Heated Shelves with Steam Inlet]
        S3[Vacuum Tight Door with Gasket]
    end
    subgraph B[Vacuum and Condensation Train]
        S4[Vacuum Line]
        S5[Barometric Condenser with Cooling Water]
        S6[Receiver Tank for Condensate]
    end
    subgraph C[Vacuum Generation]
        S7[Steam Jet Ejector or Mechanical Vacuum Pump]
        S8[Discharge to Atmosphere or Scrubber]
    end
    subgraph D[Instrumentation and Control]
        S9[Pressure Gauge]
        S10[Temperature Sensors on Shelves]
        S11[Moisture Analyzer]
    end
    S1 --> S4
    S2 --> S1
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S5 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S9 --> A
    S10 --> A
    S11 --> A
    style A fill:#FFF8DC
    style B fill:#E0F8E0
    style C fill:#FFE0E0
    style D fill:#E0E0FF
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Drying Cycle Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Loading - 15 min] --> P2[Phase 2: Sealing and Vacuum Pull Down - 20 min]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Heating to Operating T - 30 min]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Constant Rate Drying - 2 to 4 h]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Falling Rate Drying - 2 to 3 h]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Cool Down Under Vacuum - 30 min]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: Vacuum Break with Dry Air - 15 min]
    P7 --> P8[Phase 8: Unloading - 15 min]
    style P1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style P4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style P5 fill:#FFB6C1
    style P8 fill:#98FB98
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Schematics:** Mermaid is used to render **block-level functional architecture** rather than physical isometric drawings. This is a deliberate choice to ensure clean compilation, focus on flow logic, and convey the system's operational architecture without ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**List any three advantages of vacuum drying over atmospheric drying. State two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

*Advantages:*
1. **Low temperature operation** — protects heat-sensitive materials.
2. **Absence of oxygen** — prevents oxidative degradation of products.
3. **Faster drying rate** at low temperature due to high driving force (P_sat − P_chamber).

*Industrial Applications:*
1. Drying of pharmaceutical products (e.g., antibiotics, vitamins, enzymes).
2. Drying of food products (e.g., instant coffee, spice extracts).

*(3 marks: 1.5 for advantages, 1 for applications, 0.5 for clear presentation)*

---

#### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Explain why the boiling point of water reduces under vacuum. How is this principle used in vacuum dryers?**

**Model Answer:**

Under vacuum, the total pressure above the water surface is reduced. The water molecules require only enough kinetic energy to overcome this lower external pressure to escape into the vapor phase. Hence, the saturation temperature (boiling point) decreases — quantified by the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation**:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

In vacuum dryers, the chamber pressure is reduced (typically to **5–50 mmHg**), allowing the moisture in the solid to evaporate at temperatures as low as **30–40°C**. Heat is supplied via **conduction** through heated shelves, and the vapor is removed by a vacuum pump through a condenser. This is essential for drying heat-sensitive products like **pharmaceuticals and food**.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for boiling point reduction logic, 1.5 for application in vacuum dryer)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **vacuum shelf dryer**. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and typical applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** A vacuum shelf dryer contains **200 kg of dry solid** (dry basis) with an initial moisture content of **X₁ = 0.75** (kg water/kg dry solid). It is dried to a final moisture content of **X₂ = 0.04**. The effective heat transfer area is **10 m²**, the overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 75 W/(m²·K)**, the shelf temperature is **70°C**, the solid temperature is **40°C**, and the latent heat of vaporization of water at operating conditions is **λ = 2,390 kJ/kg**. Calculate:
1. Total moisture to be removed (kg).
2. Heat transfer rate through the shelves (kW).
3. Drying time (hours).

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Construction of Vacuum Shelf Dryer:**
- A **rectangular, cast-iron/steel chamber** fitted with a vacuum-tight hinged door and rubber/PTFE gasket.
- Inside, **horizontal hollow shelves** are stacked vertically with a small clearance for tray placement.
- **Steam or hot water** circulates through the shelves, which are connected to inlet and outlet headers.
- The chamber is connected via a **vapor line** to a **barometric condenser**, which is in turn connected to a **vacuum pump** (steam-jet ejector or mechanical pump).
- **Pressure gauge, thermometers, and safety relief valves** are mounted on the chamber.

**Working:**
1. Wet material is spread on **trays** and placed on heated shelves.
2. The door is closed and sealed.
3. The vacuum pump evacuates air, dropping chamber pressure to **5–50 mmHg**.
4. Steam enters the shelves, heating the trays by **conduction**.
5. Moisture in the solid evaporates at the reduced boiling point (e.g., 35–50°C at 50 mmHg).
6. Water vapor leaves through the vapor line, condenses in the condenser, and the condensate is collected.
7. After drying, the chamber is **vacuum-broken** with dry air or nitrogen, and dried product is unloaded.

**Advantages:**
- Suitable for **heat-sensitive, toxic, or explosive** materials.
- **Uniform drying** due to large shelf area.
- Closed system — no contamination, no product loss.
- **Low temperature operation** preserves product quality.

**Limitations:**
- **Batch operation** — low throughput.
- **High capital cost** for vacuum equipment.
- **Long drying times** compared to spray dryers.
- **Manual handling** of trays (labor-intensive).

**Applications:**
- Pharmaceuticals (sulfonamides, vitamins, antibiotics).
- Food (spice powders, fruit extracts).
- Fine chemicals (catalysts, pigments).
- Electronics (ceramic capacitors).

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for working, 1.5 for advantages/limitations, 1.5 for applications)*

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Total moisture to be removed**

$$m_{water} = m_s \times (X_1 - X_2)$$

$$m_{water} = 200 \times (0.75 - 0.04) = 200 \times 0.71 = 142 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Heat transfer rate through the shelves**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{solid})$$

$$Q = 75 \times 10 \times (70 - 40) = 75 \times 10 \times 30 = 22{,}500 \text{ W} = 22.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Drying time**

First, calculate the evaporation rate (latent-heat balance):

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{22.5 \text{ kW}}{2390 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.00941 \text{ kg/s} = 33.89 \text{ kg/h}$$

Then, drying time:

$$t_{dry} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{\dot{m}_{water}} = \dfrac{142}{33.89} = 4.19 \text{ hours}$$

**Final Answers:**
1. **m_water = 142 kg**
2. **Q = 22.5 kW**
3. **t_dry ≈ 4.19 hours**

*(7 marks: 2 for step 1, 2 for step 2, 2 for step 3, 1 for correct units and final answer)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** for the vapor pressure of water. Using this, calculate the boiling point of water at a chamber pressure of **30 mmHg**, given that water boils at **100°C at 760 mmHg** and $\Delta H_{vap} = 40,656$ J/mol.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **vacuum shelf dryers, double-cone vacuum dryers, and freeze dryers** based on:
1. Operating pressure
2. Heat transfer mechanism
3. Product form handled
4. Typical drying time
5. Capital cost
6. Industrial application
7. Mode of operation (batch/continuous)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The derivation follows the **5-step integration** shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $P_1 = 760$ mmHg, $T_1 = 373.15$ K, $P_2 = 30$ mmHg, $\Delta H_{vap} = 40,656$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{30}{760}\right) = -\dfrac{40{,}656}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{373.15}\right)$$

$$\ln(0.03947) = -4889.8 \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 0.002680\right)$$

$$-3.232 = -4889.8 \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 0.002680\right)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 0.002680 = \dfrac{3.232}{4889.8} = 6.609 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 0.002680 + 0.0006609 = 0.003341$$

$$T_2 = \dfrac{1}{0.003341} = 299.3 \text{ K} = 26.2 \text{°C}$$

**Final Answer:** Water boils at approximately **26.2°C** at 30 mmHg.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2.5 for numerical calculation, 1.5 for correct substitution and final answer)*

**Part (b):**

| # | Parameter | Vacuum Shelf Dryer | Double-Cone Vacuum Dryer | Freeze Dryer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operating pressure | 5–50 mmHg | 5–30 mmHg | < 1 mmHg (typically 0.1–0.5 mmHg) |
| 2 | Heat transfer mechanism | Conduction through heated shelves | Conduction + radiation from jacketed walls | Conduction through heated shelves (frozen state) |
| 3 | Product form handled | Pastes, slurries, wet granules | Free-flowing powders, crystals | Pre-frozen solutions, biologicals |
| 4 | Typical drying time | 4–12 hours | 2–6 hours | 12–48 hours |
| 5 | Capital cost | Moderate | Moderate–High | Very High |
| 6 | Industrial application | Pharmaceuticals, food | APIs, fine chemicals | Injectables, vaccines, enzymes |
| 7 | Mode of operation | Batch | Batch | Batch |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per correct entry, 7 parameters = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Converting between W and kW, or between seconds and hours, is a frequent source of mark loss. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Antoine vs. Clausius-Clapeyron:** Examiners accept either, but mixing constants (e.g., using mmHg with R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) without ensuring $\Delta H_{vap}$ is in J/mol) is a **common error** that costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Dry vs. Wet Basis:** Moisture content $X$ in the formula $m_{water} = m_s(X_1 - X_2)$ MUST be on a **dry basis** (kg water/kg dry solid). Using wet-basis moisture will give wrong answers.
> 4. **Shelf Temperature vs. Solid Temperature:** Many students confuse $T_{shelf}$ with $T_{solid}$. The driving force $\Delta T = T_{shelf} - T_{solid}$.
> 5. **Equilibrium Moisture:** The falling-rate equation $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = -k(X - X^*)$ assumes the equilibrium moisture $X^*$ is known. If not given, assume $X^* = 0$ (safe default for soluble solids under vacuum).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Vacuum drying** operates at sub-atmospheric pressure (**1–50 mmHg**) to reduce the boiling point of water, enabling low-temperature drying.
- [x] The **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** is the foundational relation: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\Delta H_{vap}/R \cdot (1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$.
- [x] At **30 mmHg**, water boils at **~26°C**; at **50 mmHg**, it boils at **~36°C**.
- [x] Heat transfer is **predominantly conductive** (heated shelves, jacketed walls) — not convective.
- [x] Vapour is **condensed** in a barometric condenser and removed by a **vacuum pump** (steam jet or mechanical).
- [x] **Key design equation:** $t_{dry} = m_s(X_1 - X_2)\lambda / [U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T]$.
- [x] **Industrial use:** Pharmaceuticals, food, electronics, fine chemicals — anywhere heat-sensitive products are dried.
- [x] **Advantages:** Low temperature, no oxidation, fast drying, good product quality.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Batch operation, high capital cost, manual handling, long drying cycles.
- [x] **Types:** Shelf, rotary, double-cone, agitated pan, vacuum belt, **freeze dryer** (sub-1 mmHg).
- [x] **Moisture content** in design equations is on **dry basis** unless otherwise stated.
- [x] **Drying kinetics:** Constant rate period (surface moisture) followed by falling rate period (bound moisture) — same as atmospheric drying, but at lower $T$.
- [x] **Freeze dryers** (lyophilizers) are the extreme case — operate at sub-1 mmHg to enable **sublimation** of frozen water.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluidized Bed Dryers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluidized Bed Dryers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fluidized Bed Dryer (FBD)** is a solids-handling unit operation in which a bed of granular or crystalline material is suspended (fluidized) in an upward-flowing stream of heated gas (commonly air), such that the gas–solid mixture behaves kinetically like a boiling liquid. The hot gas simultaneously serves two roles: it acts as the **fluidizing medium** (providing the drag force to support particle weight) and as the **heat-transfer medium** (carrying the thermal energy required to vaporize moisture from the wet solid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *Fluidized bed drying is a process in which a stream of hot gas is passed through a bed of granular solids such that the bed is supported by the gas stream and behaves as a fluid. Intensive gas–solid contact yields high rates of heat and mass transfer.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring a handful of dry sand onto the surface of a gently boiling pot of water. The sand grains do not sink — they bounce, swirl, and "float" in dynamic suspension. Now replace the water with a stream of hot air rising from below, and the sand particles with wet granules of a pharmaceutical powder or fertilizer.

> The wet particles appear to "boil" as the hot air rushes through them, while the moisture evaporates almost instantly into the rising air stream.

That suspended, churning, liquid-like mass of particles **IS** the fluidized bed. Because every particle is individually bathed in hot gas, the surface area exposed for drying is enormous, and the entire bed reaches a nearly **isothermal** condition (typically within ±2 °C).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Operating gas velocity, $U$ | **1.1 – 1.5 × $U_{mf}$** (or 0.4 – 0.8 × $U_t$) |
| Bed temperature | **40 °C – 200 °C** |
| Pressure drop across bed, $\Delta P$ | Equal to **bed weight per unit area** |
| Static bed height, $L_0$ | **0.1 – 0.5 m** (typical) |
| Expanded bed height, $L$ | **1.1 – 1.5 × $L_0$** |
| Inlet air temperature | **80 °C – 250 °C** |
| Particle size, $d_p$ | **20 µm – 6 mm** (Geldart A, B) |
| Minimum fluidization velocity, $U_{mf}$ | **0.01 – 0.5 m/s** |
| Density of air (operating), $\rho_g$ | **1.0 – 1.2 kg/m³** |

> [!NOTE]
> The two velocities that govern the safe operating window of an FBD are the **minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$)** and the **terminal (free-settling) velocity ($U_t$)**. Operating between these two bounds is the heart of FBD design.

## 1.4 Classification of FBDs

**A. By mode of operation**
- **Batch FBD** (most common in pharma, agro-chemicals)
- **Continuous FBD** (large-scale fertilizer, food, polymer industries)

**B. By gas–solid contacting pattern**
- **Conventional (bubbling) FBD**
- **Circulating FBD (CFB)** – for Geldart A solids
- **Vibro-Fluidized Bed** – for cohesive (Geldart C) powders

**C. By application**
- **Dryer-only**, **Granulator–Dryer combination**, **Coater–Dryer combination**

## 1.5 The Geldart Particle Classification (Critical for Design)

| Class | Particle Size | Density | Fluidization Behaviour | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 20 – 100 µm | Low | Aeratable; stable bed expansion | Cracking catalyst |
| **B** | 40 – 500 µm | Moderate | Bubbles form at $U_{mf}$ | Sand, glass beads |
| **C** | < 30 µm | Low | Cohesive; channelling | Talc, flour |
| **D** | > 600 µm | High | Spouting needed; large bubbles | Wheat, plastic pellets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU question on "selection of fluidization regime" almost always hinges on the **Geldart class** of the solid. Memorize the four classes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Fluidization — A Step-by-Step Logic Map

**Step 1: Fixed Bed Stage**
At very low gas velocities, the gas simply percolates through the void spaces of the static bed. The pressure drop $\Delta P$ increases with velocity (per the Ergun equation). The particles remain stationary. Frictional drag < Particle weight.

**Step 2: Incipient Fluidization ($U = U_{mf}$)**
The drag force exerted by the gas exactly balances the effective weight of the particles. The bed becomes a quasi-fluid — it can be stirred, will adopt the shape of its container, and its pressure drop **plateaus**.

$$\Delta P_{\text{bed}} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_g) g L_{mf}$$

**Step 3: Fluidization Stage ($U_{mf} < U < U_t$)**
The bed expands, bubbles form, gas–solid mixing is vigorous, and $\Delta P$ remains essentially **constant** (= bed weight per unit area). This is the working regime of the FBD.

**Step 4: Elutriation / Entrainment ($U \to U_t$)**
As gas velocity approaches the terminal velocity of the smallest particles, fines are carried out of the bed by the gas stream. A **cyclone separator** is installed downstream to recover them.

**Step 5: Pneumatic Transport ($U > U_t$)**
All particles are entrained — the bed ceases to exist as a coherent phase. This regime is used in **Circulating Fluidized Beds**, not conventional FBDs.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important design rule: **The operating velocity $U_{\text{op}}$ must always lie in the window $U_{mf} < U_{\text{op}} < U_t$**, with typical design choice $U_{\text{op}} = 1.2\, U_{mf}$ to $1.5\, U_{mf}$.

## 2.2 The Archimedes Number — A Convenient Dimensionless Group

The Archimedes number characterizes the ratio of gravitational to viscous forces on a particle:

$$Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \, \rho_g \, (\rho_s - \rho_g) \, g}{\mu^2}$$

It is independent of gas velocity and depends only on **particle and gas properties** — making it the most fundamental dimensionless number in fluidization calculations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ergun Equation (general)** | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150\,\mu\, U\,(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3\, d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75\,\rho_g\, U^2\,(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3\, d_p}$ | Valid for whole fixed-bed to fluidization transition |
| 2 | **$U_{mf}$ — Small particles (laminar)** | $U_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^2\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{150\,\mu}\cdot\dfrac{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1-\varepsilon_{mf}}$ | $Re_{mf} < 20$; $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$–$0.5$ |
| 3 | **$U_{mf}$ — Large particles (turbulent)** | $U_{mf}^2 = \dfrac{d_p\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{1.75\,\rho_g}\cdot\dfrac{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1-\varepsilon_{mf}}$ | $Re_{mf} > 1000$ |
| 4 | **Wen & Chu Correlation** | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408\, Ar} - 27.2$ | Universal; valid for ALL $Re_{mf}$ |
| 5 | **Stokes Terminal Velocity** | $U_t = \dfrac{d_p^2\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{18\,\mu}$ | $Re_p < 0.1$ (laminar) |
| 6 | **Newton's Terminal Velocity** | $U_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\,d_p\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{\rho_g\,C_D}}$ | General form |
| 7 | **Sphericity** | $\phi = \dfrac{\text{Surface area of sphere of same volume}}{\text{Actual surface area of particle}}$ | $\phi = 1$ for sphere; $\phi < 1$ for irregular |
| 8 | **Effective diameter for non-spherical** | $d_{p,\text{eff}} = \phi \cdot d_p$ | Substitute into all above |
| 9 | **Bed pressure drop (at fluidization)** | $\Delta P = (1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_g) g L_{mf}$ | Constant in fluidized regime |
| 10 | **Bed expansion ratio** | $L/L_0 = (1-\varepsilon_0)/(1-\varepsilon)$ | $L_0$ is static bed height |
| 11 | **Reynolds at minimum fluidization** | $Re_{mf} = d_p U_{mf} \rho_g / \mu$ | Used to select Equation (2) vs (3) |
| 12 | **Heat-transfer coefficient (Maxwell)** | $h_{bed} = 0.03\, k_g\, Re_p^{1.3}\, (L/d_p)^{-0.5}$ | Approximate empirical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every KTU problem, **start with the Archimedes number**, then plug it into the **Wen & Chu correlation** to get $Re_{mf}$, then back-calculate $U_{mf}$. This three-step recipe is the universally accepted exam solution.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry** — drying of active ingredients, granulation, and tablet coating in batch FBDs.
- **Fertilizer industry** — drying of ammonium nitrate, urea, and NPK granules (continuous FBDs handling 50–200 t/h).
- **Food industry** — drying of instant coffee, sugar, salt, milk powder.
- **Polymer industry** — drying of polyethylene, PVC, nylon chips.
- **Coal preparation** — drying of lignite and coking coal.

In all these applications, the FBD's high heat-transfer coefficient (typically **200 – 500 W/m²·K**) reduces drying times from hours (in tray dryers) to minutes, dramatically shrinking equipment footprint.

## 2.5 The Five Hallmarks of a Good FBD Design

1. **Pressure drop plateau** — clearly observable on a $\Delta P$ vs $U$ plot.
2. **Smooth fluidization** — no channelling, slugging, or dead zones.
3. **Uniform temperature** — $\Delta T < 2$ °C across the bed.
4. **Stable operation** — no excessive pressure fluctuations.
5. **Minimal elutriation** — dust load in exhaust < design specification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop Across a Fixed Bed (Ergun Equation)

**Starting point:** Consider a vertical cylindrical column of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $L$ packed with spherical particles of diameter $d_p$. Gas of viscosity $\mu$ and density $\rho_g$ flows upward at superficial velocity $U$.

**Force balance on the bed (steady state):**

The net upward force on the gas equals the drag exerted by the gas on the particles, which equals the buoyant weight of the particles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P \cdot A &= (\text{Solid volume}) \times (\text{Buoyant weight density}) \\
\Delta P \cdot A &= (1 - \varepsilon)\, A\, L\, (\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta P = (1 - \varepsilon)\, L\, (\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g$$

**Ergun argued** that the pressure drop has two additive contributions: **viscous losses (laminar)** + **inertia losses (turbulent)**.

**Viscous term** (Hagen–Poiseuille analogue, modified for porosity):

$$
\Delta P_{\text{visc}} = \frac{150\,\mu\, U\, L\, (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3\, d_p^2}
$$

**Inertia term** (Burke–Plummer):

$$
\Delta P_{\text{inertia}} = \frac{1.75\,\rho_g\, U^2\, L\, (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3\, d_p}
$$

**Combining the two contributions gives the full Ergun Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150\,\mu\, U\,(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3\, d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75\,\rho_g\, U^2\,(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3\, d_p}\;}
$$

This is the foundational equation that, evaluated at $U = U_{mf}$ and $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{mf}$, yields the minimum fluidization velocity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$) for Small Particles (Laminar Limit)

**Assumption:** At minimum fluidization, $Re_{mf} < 20$, so the **inertia term is negligible** and only the viscous term in the Ergun equation survives.

**At $U = U_{mf}$**, the pressure drop equals the buoyant weight of the bed per unit length:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\bigg|_{U_{mf}} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g
$$

**Equating with the viscous term of the Ergun equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g &= \frac{150\,\mu\, U_{mf}\, (1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3\, d_p^2} \\
(\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g &= \frac{150\,\mu\, U_{mf}\, (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3\, d_p^2} \\
U_{mf} &= \frac{d_p^2\, (\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g}{150\, \mu} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result (laminar case):**

$$
\boxed{\;U_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^2\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{150\,\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1-\varepsilon_{mf}}\;}
$$

For most Geldart A/B systems, $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$, so $\dfrac{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}} \approx 0.15$ — a useful round number for quick exam estimation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Wen & Chu Universal Correlation

**Problem with Equations (2) and (3):** Each is valid in a different Reynolds regime. Wen & Chu (1966) proposed a single correlation by **non-dimensionalizing the Ergun equation** at $U = U_{mf}$.

**Start with the Ergun equation** and divide both sides by $(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{150\,\mu\, U_{mf}}{d_p^2\, \varepsilon_{mf}^3} + \frac{1.75\,\rho_g\, U_{mf}^2}{d_p\, \varepsilon_{mf}^3} &= (\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{d_p^3\, \rho_g}{\mu^2}$** to introduce $Re_{mf}$ and $Ar$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{150}{d_p^2\,\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\cdot\frac{d_p^3\, \rho_g}{\mu^2}\,U_{mf} + \frac{1.75\,\rho_g}{d_p\,\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\cdot\frac{d_p^3\, \rho_g}{\mu^2}\,U_{mf}^2 &= \frac{d_p^3\, \rho_g\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{\mu^2} \\
\frac{150}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\,Re_{mf} + \frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\,Re_{mf}^2 &= Ar \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**For typical $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$**, Wen & Chu empirically fit:

$$
\frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} \approx 0.0408 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{150}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} \approx 27.2
$$

**Substituting:**

$$
0.0408\, Re_{mf}^2 + 27.2\, Re_{mf} - Ar = 0
$$

**Solving the quadratic for $Re_{mf}$:**

$$
\boxed{\;Re_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408\, Ar} - 27.2\;}
$$

This single expression works for **all** $Re_{mf}$ values — from Stokes regime to fully turbulent.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Terminal Velocity ($U_t$) by Stokes' Law

**Force balance on a single free-falling particle:**

$$
\text{Drag force} = \text{Net gravitational force}
$$

For $Re_p < 0.1$ (laminar regime), drag coefficient $C_D = 24 / Re_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3\,\pi\,\mu\, d_p\, U_t &= \frac{\pi\, d_p^3}{6}\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g \\
U_t &= \frac{d_p^2\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{18\,\mu} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;U_t = \dfrac{d_p^2\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{18\,\mu}\;}
$$

**Validity:** $Re_p = d_p U_t \rho_g / \mu < 0.1$. For larger particles, use the Newton's-law form with $C_D = 0.44$:

$$
U_t = \sqrt{\frac{4\, d_p\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{3\, \rho_g\, C_D}} = 1.74\, \sqrt{\frac{d_p\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{\rho_g}}
$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Sand particles of diameter $d_p = 200\,\mu m$ and density $\rho_s = 2500\, kg/m^3$ are to be fluidized with air at 60 °C ($\rho_g = 1.06\, kg/m^3$, $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}\, Pa \cdot s$). Take $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.45$. Calculate (a) the Archimedes number, (b) $U_{mf}$ using the Wen & Chu correlation, (c) $U_t$ by Stokes' law, and (d) the safe operating velocity.

**Given:**
- $d_p = 2 \times 10^{-4}\, m$
- $\rho_s = 2500\, kg/m^3$
- $\rho_g = 1.06\, kg/m^3$
- $\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}\, Pa \cdot s$
- $g = 9.81\, m/s^2$
- $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.45$

**Step (a) — Archimedes Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ar &= \frac{d_p^3\, \rho_g\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\, g}{\mu^2} \\
&= \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4})^3 \times 1.06 \times (2500 - 1.06) \times 9.81}{(2 \times 10^{-5})^2} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.06 \times 2498.94 \times 9.81}{4 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 1.06 \times 2498.94 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= \frac{208{,}054.4 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= 0.520 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{Ar \approx 0.520}
$$

**Step (b) — $U_{mf}$ using Wen & Chu:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_{mf} &= \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \times 0.520} - 27.2 \\
&= \sqrt{739.84 + 0.0212} - 27.2 \\
&= \sqrt{739.86} - 27.2 \\
&= 27.200 - 27.200 \\
&\approx 3.84 \times 10^{-4} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf}\, \mu}{d_p\, \rho_g} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.06} = 3.62 \times 10^{-5}\, m/s
$$

**Step (c) — $U_t$ by Stokes' Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_t &= \frac{d_p^2\,(\rho_s - \rho_g)\,g}{18\, \mu} \\
&= \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times 2498.94 \times 9.81}{18 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 10^{-8} \times 24{,}514.6}{3.6 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{9.806 \times 10^{-4}}{3.6 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 2.72\, m/s \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d) — Safe Operating Velocity:**

$$
U_{op} = 1.3 \times U_{mf} = 1.3 \times 3.62 \times 10^{-5} \approx 4.71 \times 10^{-5}\, m/s
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The operating window here spans nearly **5 orders of magnitude** (from $3.6 \times 10^{-5}$ to $2.7\, m/s$) — a hallmark of Geldart A/B particle behaviour and a great illustration of the FBD's flexibility.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fluidized Bed Dryer

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete FBD system — feed, fluidization, heat supply, gas cleaning, and instrumentation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wet Solid Feed Hopper] --> B[Screw Feeder]
    B --> C[Fluidization Chamber with Distributor Plate]
    H[Air Blower] --> G[Heat Exchanger or Electric Heater]
    G --> I[Plenum Chamber Below Distributor]
    I --> C
    C --> J[Expanded Fluidized Bed Region]
    J --> K[Settling Zone Freeboard]
    K --> L[Cyclone Separator]
    L --> M[Bag Filter]
    M --> N[Induced Draft Fan]
    N --> O[Stack Exhaust]
    L --> P[Cyclone Solids Return Line]
    P --> C
    C --> Q[Dried Product Discharge Nozzle]
    C --> R[Temperature Sensors TC1 and TC2]
    C --> S[Pressure Transmitter dP]
    R --> T[Control Panel PLC]
    S --> T
    T --> H
    T --> G
```

## 4.2 Pressure Drop vs Gas Velocity Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Pressure Drop versus Superficial Gas Velocity (Fixed to Fluidized Bed)"
    x-axis "Gas velocity U, m/s" [0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10, 0.12, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30, 0.50]
    y-axis "Pressure drop, kPa" 0 --> 12
    line [0, 3.0, 5.8, 8.4, 10.2, 10.5, 10.5, 10.5, 10.5, 10.5, 10.5]
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Linear rise** at low $U$ → **fixed-bed** regime (Ergun regime).
- **Knee** at $U_{mf}$ → **incipient fluidization** begins.
- **Plateau** for $U_{mf} < U < U_t$ → **working FBD regime** — pressure drop equals bed weight.
- Beyond $U_t$ (off-chart) → entrainment and pneumatic transport.

## 4.3 Functional State-Machine of a Fluidized Bed

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FixedBed
    FixedBed --> IncipientFluidization : U reaches Umf
    IncipientFluidization --> BubblingBed : U between Umf and Ut
    BubblingBed --> Elutriation : fines leave with gas
    BubblingBed --> Slugging : U too high, particles coalesce
    Slugging --> BubblingBed : reduce U
    Elutriation --> [*]
    BubblingBed --> Discharge : batch complete
    Discharge --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Component List & Function Table

| Tag | Component | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | Wet solid feed hopper | Stores and meters wet feed material |
| B | Screw or rotary feeder | Provides uniform feed rate to bed |
| C | Fluidization chamber | Houses the bed; primary drying zone |
| G | Air heater (steam coil / electric / gas burner) | Raises air temperature to design inlet |
| H | Blower / fan | Supplies air at required volumetric flow rate |
| I | Plenum chamber | Distributes air uniformly before distributor |
| J | Fluidized bed region | Gas–solid contacting zone |
| K | Freeboard (settling zone) | Reduces elutriation by slowing gas |
| L | Cyclone separator | Recovers entrained fines |
| M | Bag filter | Final dust collection for environmental compliance |
| N | Induced draft fan | Pulls gas through system, maintains negative pressure |
| R / S | Instrumentation | Monitors bed temperature and pressure drop |
| T | PLC / DCS controller | Modulates blower and heater to maintain setpoint |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define minimum fluidization velocity. List any two factors on which it depends. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$) is defined as the **superficial gas velocity at which the drag force exerted by the upward-flowing gas on the particles first equals the effective (buoyant) weight of the particles**, causing the bed to transition from a packed (fixed) state to a fluidized state. Below $U_{mf}$ the bed is a fixed bed; above $U_{mf}$ the bed behaves as a pseudo-fluid with a constant pressure drop equal to the bed weight per unit area.

**Factors (1 mark — any two):**
1. Particle diameter $d_p$ (increases sharply with $d_p$)
2. Density difference $(\rho_s - \rho_g)$
3. Gas viscosity $\mu$
4. Bed porosity at minimum fluidization $\varepsilon_{mf}$

---

### **Q2.** Differentiate between a fixed bed and a fluidized bed. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Fixed Bed | Fluidized Bed |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure drop $\Delta P$ | Increases with gas velocity | **Constant** (≈ bed weight/area) |
| Particle motion | Stationary | Suspended, vigorous mixing |
| Bed surface | Defined, flat | Bubbling, dynamic |
| Heat transfer | Poor, conductive only | Excellent, convective |
| Channel formation | Possible, common | Eliminated by fluidization |
| Operating velocity | $U < U_{mf}$ | $U_{mf} < U < U_t$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Internal Choice: Answer **ONE** of the two questions below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the minimum fluidization velocity using the Ergun equation. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Glass beads of diameter $d_p = 1.0\, mm$ and density $\rho_s = 2500\, kg/m^3$ are to be fluidized with air at 25 °C. Given:
- $\rho_g = 1.184\, kg/m^3$
- $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}\, Pa \cdot s$
- $g = 9.81\, m/s^2$
- $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.42$

Calculate (i) the Archimedes number, (ii) $U_{mf}$ using the Wen & Chu correlation, and (iii) the operating gas velocity assuming a fluidization number of 1.3. State whether the particles are suitable for smooth fluidization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Write the Ergun equation (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150\,\mu\, U\,(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3\, d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75\,\rho_g\, U^2\,(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3\, d_p}
$$
