# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Differentiate between freeze drying and spray drying on the basis of:**
**(a)** phase change involved, **(b)** typical operating pressure, **(c)** particle nature of product.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Freeze Drying | Spray Drying |
|---|---|---|
| Phase change | Solid → Vapor (**sublimation**) | Liquid → Vapor (**evaporation**) |
| Operating pressure | High vacuum (**10 – 100 Pa**) | Atmospheric (≈ 1 atm = **101 325 Pa**) |
| Product form | Bulk porous cake/sheet (no fixed shape) | Discrete spherical particles (**10 – 500 μm**) |

*(1 mark per correct contrast pair — full marks only when all three are tabulated.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List the three industrial types of atomizers used in spray dryers. State the principle of operation of the pressure nozzle type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Pressure nozzle** (1 mark)
2. **Two-fluid (pneumatic) nozzle** (½ mark)
3. **Centrifugal (rotary) disc atomizer** (½ mark)

**Principle of Pressure Nozzle (1 mark):**
A high-pressure pump forces the liquid (**20 – 200 bar**) through a small orifice. As the liquid jet emerges, it becomes a thin conical sheet due to **swirl inserts** or tangential entry. This sheet is unstable and disintegrates into fine droplets because surface tension cannot sustain it. Smaller orifice or higher pressure gives smaller droplets.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]****

A spray dryer is to produce **900 kg/h** of detergent powder from a slurry feed containing **55 % moisture (wet basis)**. The inlet air is at **220 °C, humidity 0.010 kg/kg dry air**, and leaves at **90 °C, humidity 0.045 kg/kg dry air**. The product moisture is **2 % (wet basis)**. Ambient air is at **30 °C**.
**(a)** Calculate the **dry-air requirement** and the **rate of water evaporation**.
**(b)** Determine the **steam consumption (kg/h)** if saturated steam at 1.4 bar is used (latent heat = 2240 kJ/kg). Assume 8 % heat loss.

#### **Sub-part (a) Solution (7 marks)**
**Step 1: Dry-basis moisture content** (1 mark)

$$X_{1} = \dfrac{0.55}{0.45} = 1.2222\ \text{kg water/kg dry solid}\quad;\quad X_{2} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.98} = 0.0204$$

**Step 2: Dry solid flow rate** (1 mark)

$$L_{s} = 900 \times (1 - 0.02) = 882\ \text{kg dry solid/h}$$

**Step 3: Evaporation rate** (2 marks)

$$\dot{m}_{w} = L_{s}(X_{1} - X_{2}) = 882(1.2222 - 0.0204) = 882\times 1.2018 = 1060.0\ \text{kg water/h}$$

**Step 4: Dry-air requirement** (3 marks)

$$G_{s} = \dfrac{L_{s}(X_{1} - X_{2})}{Y_{2} - Y_{1}} = \dfrac{1060.0}{0.045 - 0.010} = \dfrac{1060.0}{0.035} = 30{,}286\ \text{kg dry air/h}$$

> **Final Answer (a):** Water evaporated = **1060 kg/h**; Dry air = **30 286 kg/h**.

#### **Sub-part (b) Solution (7 marks)**
**Step 1: Enthalpy of inlet air at T₀ = 30 °C, Y₁ = 0.010** (2 marks)

$$H_{0} = 1.005(30) + 0.010\times(2500 + 1.88\times 30) = 30.15 + 25.564 = 55.71\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Step 2: Enthalpy of outlet air at T₂ = 90 °C, Y₂ = 0.045** (2 marks)

$$H_{2} = 1.005(90) + 0.045\times(2500 + 1.88\times 90) = 90.45 + 0.045\times 2669.2 = 90.45 + 120.11 = 210.56\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Step 3: Total heat duty per hour** (1 mark)

$$Q = G_{s}(H_{2} - H_{0}) = 30{,}286\times(210.56 - 55.71) = 30{,}286\times 154.85 = 4{,}689{,}790\ \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 4: Steam required with 8 % loss** (2 marks)

$$Q_{\text{steam}} = 4{,}689{,}790\times 1.08 = 5{,}064{,}973\ \text{kJ/h}$$

$$m_{\text{steam}} = \dfrac{5{,}064{,}973}{2240} = 2261.2\ \text{kg steam/h}$$

> **Final Answer (b):** Steam consumption ≈ **2261 kg/h**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)****

A pharmaceutical company is freeze-drying a **heat-sensitive antibiotic** in vials. The product slab is **0.012 m** thick, frozen at **−40 °C**, with the following data:
- Sublimation interface temperature $T_{i} = -25\ \text{°C}$
- Shelf temperature $T_{s} = +35\ \text{°C}$
- Chamber pressure $P_{c} = 20\ \text{Pa}$
- Vapor pressure of ice at $-25\ \text{°C}$ = $63.5\ \text{Pa}$
- Dried-layer thermal conductivity $k_{d} = 0.020\ \text{W/m·K}$
- Density of frozen slab $\rho = 950\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$
- Initial ice fraction $w_{ice} = 0.85$
- Heat of sublimation $\lambda_{sub} = 2838\ \text{kJ/kg}$

**(a)** Compute the **sublimation driving pressure** and the **heat-transfer-limited flux**.
**(b)** Estimate the **primary drying time** in hours. Comment on whether the process is heat- or mass-transfer limited.

#### **Sub-part (a) Solution (7 marks)**
**Step 1: Sublimation driving pressure** (2 marks)

$$\Delta P = P_{i} - P_{c} = 63.5 - 20 = 43.5\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2: Heat flux through dried layer** (3 marks)

$$q = \dfrac{k_{d}}{\ell}\,(T_{s} - T_{i}) = \dfrac{0.020}{0.012}\times[35 - (-25)]$$

$$q = 1.667 \times 60 = 100.0\ \text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Sublimation flux from heat-transfer limit** (2 marks)

$$\dot{m}''_{H} = \dfrac{q}{\lambda_{sub}} = \dfrac{100.0}{2.838\times 10^{6}} = 3.52\times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/(m}^{2}\text{·s)}$$

#### **Sub-part (b) Solution (7 marks)**
**Step 1: Initial ice mass per unit area** (2 marks)

$$m_{0}'' = \rho\,\ell\,w_{ice} = 950 \times 0.012 \times 0.85 = 9.69\ \text{kg/m}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Apply the integrated receding-front equation** (3 marks)

$$t = \dfrac{m_{0}''\,\lambda_{sub}\,\ell}{2\,k_{d}\,(T_{s} - T_{i})}$$

$$t = \dfrac{9.69 \times 2.838\times 10^{6} \times 0.012}{2\times 0.020\times 60} = \dfrac{330{,}034}{2.4} = 137{,}514\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to hours** (1 mark)

$$t = \dfrac{137{,}514}{3600} = 38.2\ \text{h}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Comparing with the mass-flux estimate using a typical $R_{p}\approx 1.3\times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa·m}^{2}\text{·s/kg}$ for pharma products, $\dot{m}''_M = 43.5 / 1.3\times 10^{4} = 3.35\times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/(m}^{2}\text{·s)}$, which is **~100× greater** than $\dot{m}''_H$. The process is therefore **heat-transfer limited** — improvements should target shelf heating (e.g., radiant shelf heating) rather than chamber vacuum.

> **Final Answer (b):** Primary drying time ≈ **38 hours**, process is **heat-transfer limited**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Moisture content mix-up**: Always convert wet-basis → dry-basis before applying the air–product balance. Using wet-basis values in $G_s$ calculation loses 1–2 marks consistently.
> 2. **Ignoring humidity in inlet air**: $G_s$ refers to *bone-dry* air. Total air = $G_s(1 + Y_1)$. Forget this and you'll be 0.8 % off in design calculations.
> 3. **Heat of sublimation vs latent heat of vaporization**: Use $\lambda_{sub} = 2838\ \text{kJ/kg}$ for freeze drying; use $\lambda_{vap} = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$ for spray drying. Mixing them is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Units of $R_p$ in freeze drying**: Always quote in $\text{Pa·m}^{2}\text{·s/kg}$ or convert appropriately.
> 5. **Forget to convert to kJ/s before steam calculation**: The ratio $Q/\lambda_{st}$ requires *consistent* units per hour.
> 6. **Residence time ≠ Drying time** for spray dryers: Residence time is for the droplet to *exit* the chamber; drying time is the intrinsic time to remove moisture — they should be matched.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Freeze drying** is **sublimation** (solid → vapor) under high vacuum; **spray drying** is **evaporation** (liquid → vapor) at atmospheric pressure.
- Three stages of freeze drying: **Freezing → Primary drying (sublimation) → Secondary drying (desorption)**.
- Three elements of a spray dryer: **Atomizer + Hot-Air Source + Particle Separator (cyclone)**.
- **Wet-bulb temperature** governs droplet temperature in spray drying — product never reaches the inlet air temperature.
- **Freeze-drying drying time ∝ L²** (receding-front geometry). Pharmaceutical vials are intentionally shallow.
- Key formula — Air requirement: $\,G_{s} = \dfrac{L_{s}(X_{1} - X_{2})}{Y_{2} - Y_{1}}$
- Key formula — Drying flux (freeze): $\,\dot{m}'' = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_{p}}$ (mass-limited) **OR** $\,\dot{m}'' = \dfrac{q}{\lambda_{sub}}$ (heat-limited) — **the smaller one wins**.
- Pressure nozzle: $\,d_{p} \propto d_{o}\sqrt{\sigma/(\rho_{L}Q_{L})}$ — smaller orifice & higher flow → smaller droplets.
- Centrifugal disc: $\,d_{p} \propto Q_{L}^{0.61}/(N^{0.71}\,D^{0.74})$ — higher disc speed → smaller droplets.
- Energy balance: $Q = G_{s}\,(H_{\text{out}} - H_{\text{in}})$, with $H = C_{p,a}T + Y(2500 + 1.88T)$.
- Heat of sublimation: $\lambda_{sub}\approx 2838\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (ice) | Latent heat of vaporization: $\lambda_{vap}\approx 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (water at 100 °C).
- For heat-sensitive materials: spray dryer if liquid feed available, freeze dryer if heat-degradation threshold < 40 °C.
- KTU exam keyword: **"Co-current"** is the standard spray-dryer configuration for **heat-sensitive products**; counter-current gives more uniform drying but higher product temperature.
- In freeze drying, **chamber pressure controls mass transfer**, **shelf temperature controls heat transfer**. Balance both to avoid collapse of the matrix.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Regimes of Drying in Standard Rate of Drying Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Different Regimes of Drying in Standard Rate of Drying Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Different Regimes of Drying in the Standard Rate of Drying Curve

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying:** Drying is a **simultaneous heat and mass transfer operation** in which **moisture** is removed from a **wet solid** by **thermal evaporation** into a carrier gas stream, producing a **dry solid** and a **wet vapour**.

The **Standard Rate of Drying Curve** is a plot of the **instantaneous drying rate** $N$ (kg water evaporated / m²·h) against the **moisture content of the solid** $X$ (kg water / kg dry solid). It is the **fundamental design tool** used by chemical engineers to size industrial dryers, estimate residence times, and classify the internal transport mechanism of moisture inside a porous solid.

The curve is divided into **two major regimes** and **four distinct periods**:

| Symbol | Period | KTU Designation |
|--------|--------|-----------------|
| A–B | Initial Adjustment Period | Transient Heating Zone |
| B–C | Constant Rate Period | Evaporation-Controlled Zone |
| C–D | First Falling Rate Period | Partially Saturated Surface Zone |
| D–E | Second Falling Rate Period | Internal Diffusion-Controlled Zone |

Where:
- **A** = Initial point (wet solid enters dryer)
- **B** = End of initial transient / Start of constant rate
- **C** = **Critical point** (Critical moisture content $X_c$)
- **D** = End of first falling rate
- **E** = **Equilibrium point** (Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Wet Sponge on a Hot Plate

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine squeezing water out of a thick kitchen sponge placed on a hot pan.**

> - **Stage A–B (Initial adjustment):** The cold sponge touches the hot pan. For a few seconds, nothing seems to happen — the sponge is "warming up." The drying rate is irregular.
> - **Stage B–C (Constant rate):** The sponge surface is now fully covered with a visible film of liquid water. Water evaporates as easily as it would from a lake surface. **As long as the surface stays wet, the evaporation rate is constant** — it depends only on the pan temperature and air flow, not on how wet the sponge is inside.
> - **Stage C–D (First falling rate):** The surface film starts breaking. **Dry patches appear** among the wet patches. Less area is wet, so evaporation slows. Think of a road drying after rain — the dark wet areas shrink.
> - **Stage D–E (Second falling rate):** The surface is now dry, but the **inside of the sponge is still damp**. Water must slowly **diffuse outwards** through the sponge's internal pores. This internal journey is much slower, so the rate keeps falling until equilibrium with the surrounding air is reached at point E.

This analogy directly maps to the four regimes of the standard drying curve.

### 1.3 Key Moisture Content Definitions (KTU Standard Notation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five moisture levels** every KTU student must memorize:

1. **Initial Moisture Content ($X_1$ or $X_t$):** Moisture in the solid as it enters the dryer.
2. **Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$ or $X^*_{critical}$):** The moisture content at which the **constant rate period ends** and the falling rate period begins. Above $X_c$ → surface evaporation. Below $X_c$ → internal transport controls.
3. **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$):** The moisture content at which the solid is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the drying air. Drying effectively **stops** here. $X^*$ is a function of the **air's temperature and relative humidity** — it is a property of the solid–air pair.
4. **Free Moisture Content ($X_F$):** $X_F = X - X^*$ = moisture that can **actually be removed** by drying at given air conditions.
5. **Bound Moisture Content ($X_B$):** $X_B = X - X^*$ (for hygroscopic materials) = moisture held so tightly by the solid that it exerts a **vapour pressure lower than pure water** at the same temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** $X^*$ and $X_c$ are **NOT the same**. $X_c$ is a process-dependent transition point; $X^*$ is an equilibrium thermodynamic limit. Conflating them costs marks in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Regimes — Step-by-Step Logic

#### **Regime A–B: Initial Adjustment Period**

- **What happens:** The wet solid, initially at room temperature, suddenly contacts hot drying air.
- **Physical picture:** Sensible heat is transferred from air → solid. The solid's temperature rises until it approaches the **wet-bulb temperature** of the air.
- **Drying rate behaviour:** May **rise, fall, or fluctuate** depending on whether the initial solid temperature is below or above the wet-bulb temperature.
- **Duration:** Usually **very short** and is often **neglected in KTU design problems**.
- **Why it matters:** It establishes the thermal "baseline" from which the constant rate begins.

#### **Regime B–C: Constant Rate Period (CRP)**

This is the **most important regime for KTU calculations** — it dominates most industrial dryers.

- **Surface condition:** The solid's outer surface is **completely saturated** with free water. The solid behaves as if it were a pool of water.
- **Controlling resistance:** All resistance is in the **external gas film**. The solid's internal structure plays **no role**.
- **Controlling mechanism:** Combined **convective heat transfer** to the surface supplies the latent heat of vaporization, and **convective mass transfer** removes the vapour.
- **Rate is independent of $X$:** Hence the name — **"constant rate."**
- **Heat-transfer form (KTU Preferred):**

$$N_c = \dfrac{h_t \, (T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_w}$$

- **Mass-transfer form (equivalent):**

$$N_c = k_H \, (H_w - H)$$

- **Where:**
  - $h_t$ = gas-film heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
  - $T_g, T_w$ = dry-bulb and wet-bulb temperatures of air (°C or K)
  - $\lambda_w$ = latent heat of vaporization at $T_w$ (J/kg)
  - $k_H$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·ΔH)
  - $H_w, H$ = humidity at saturation and bulk (kg water/kg dry air)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the **inverse coupling** between the two equations. The heat-transfer equation uses the temperature driving force $(T_g - T_w)$, while the mass-transfer equation uses the humidity driving force $(H_w - H)$. At steady state, **heat in = heat out** and **mass transferred = heat supplied / $\lambda_w$**.

- **Time for constant rate drying:**

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{L_s \, (X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c}$$

Where $L_s$ = mass of bone-dry solid (kg) and $A$ = drying area (m²).

#### **Regime C–D: First Falling Rate Period (FFRP)**

- **Surface condition:** The surface is **no longer fully wet**. Dry patches begin to appear interspersed with wet patches. The wetted area shrinks progressively.
- **Controlling mechanism:** Transition zone. Surface evaporation is still partly dominant, but **internal capillary transport** of liquid water from inside the solid to the surface becomes rate-limiting.
- **Rate behaviour:** Drying rate **decreases** with decreasing $X$. For many materials, the decrease is **approximately linear** in this period — this is a key KTU assumption.
- **Linear model (KTU Standard):**

$$N = N_c \, \dfrac{(X - X^*)}{(X_c - X^*)}$$

- **Time for first falling rate:**

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = \dfrac{L_s \, (X_c - X^*)}{A \, N_c} \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X - X^*}\right)$$

#### **Regime D–E: Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP)**

- **Surface condition:** Surface is **completely dry**. The plane of evaporation **recedes inside the solid**.
- **Controlling mechanism:** Pure **internal moisture movement** — diffusion of liquid (or vapour) through the porous structure. The rate is now **independent** of the external air conditions.
- **Rate behaviour:** Drying rate continues to fall but **more slowly and asymptotically**, approaching zero as $X \to X^*$.
- **Diffusion-controlled model:**

$$N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{d\theta} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta_{FRP_2} = \dfrac{L_s}{A}\int_{X^*}^{X_2}\dfrac{dX}{N(X)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Past Exam Fact:** In part (b) of ESE Module 4 questions, students are often asked to compute **total drying time** as $\theta_{total} = \theta_B + \theta_c + \theta_{FRP_1} + \theta_{FRP_2}$, where $\theta_B$ (initial transient) is often taken as zero unless specified.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities in SI units. Note:** The pipe symbol is rendered as `\vert` in tables to preserve markdown table structure.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{d\theta}$ | Fundamental definition of drying rate | $N$ = kg/m²·h, $L_s$ = kg dry solid, $A$ = m², $X$ = kg/kg, $\theta$ = h |
| 2 | $X = \dfrac{W - W_s}{W_s}$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | $W$ = total mass (kg), $W_s$ = dry solid mass (kg) |
| 3 | $N_c = \dfrac{h_t \,(T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_w}$ | Constant rate (heat-transfer form) | $h_t$ = W/m²·K, $\lambda_w$ = J/kg |
| 4 | $N_c = k_H\,(H_w - H)$ | Constant rate (mass-transfer form) | $k_H$ = kg/m²·s·ΔH |
| 5 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{L_s(X_1 - X_c)}{A\,N_c}$ | Time for constant rate period | $\theta_c$ = h |
| 6 | $N = N_c\,\dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$ | Linear FFRP model | Dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | $\theta_{FRP_1} = \dfrac{L_s(X_c - X^*)}{A\,N_c}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | Time for first falling rate (linear model) | $\theta$ = h |
| 8 | $X_F = X - X^*$ | Free moisture content | kg water / kg dry solid |
| 9 | $X_B = X - X^*$ (hygroscopic region) | Bound moisture content | kg water / kg dry solid |
| 10 | $\theta_{total} = \theta_c + \theta_{FRP_1} + \theta_{FRP_2}$ | Total drying time (neglecting A–B) | h |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Drying of granules in **fluidized bed dryers** — operates primarily in the **constant rate period**. Designers size the dryer using $N_c$ to predict throughput.
- **Food industry:** Drying of fruits, grains, and milk powder — falls largely in the **falling rate period**, requiring integration of internal diffusion models.
- **Cement and ceramics:** Tray dryers and rotary dryers are sized using the **linear falling rate model** because the materials spend most of their residence time in FFRP.
- **Pulp and paper:** Drying of paper sheets in **convection dryers** — boundary between CRP and FFRP is critical to avoid **case-hardening** (surface dries too fast, trapping moisture inside).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Constant Rate Drying Rate from Heat Balance

**Starting point:** At steady state in the constant rate period, **all heat transferred from the hot air to the wet surface** is used to **vaporize free moisture at the surface**.

**Step 1 — Convective heat transfer to the surface:**

$$q = h_t \, A \, (T_g - T_w)$$

This is the rate of heat flow (W or J/s) from the bulk air at $T_g$ to the wet surface at $T_w$.

**Step 2 — Mass evaporated by this heat:**

Since each kilogram of water requires $\lambda_w$ joules of latent heat:

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \dfrac{q}{\lambda_w} = \dfrac{h_t \, A \, (T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_w}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to drying rate $N$ (kg/m²·s):**

$$N_c = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{water}}{A} = \dfrac{h_t \, (T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_w}$$

This is the **KTU-standard heat-transfer form** of the constant rate drying equation.

**Step 4 — Derive the equivalent mass-transfer form using the psychrometric relation:**

At wet-bulb temperature $T_w$, the Lewis relation links heat and mass transfer:

$$\dfrac{h_t}{k_H \, c_s} = \lambda_w \, \rho_{air}$$

where $c_s$ = humid heat of air and $\rho_{air}$ = density of air. Rearranging:

$$h_t \, (T_g - T_w) = k_H \, \lambda_w \, \rho_{air} \, (H_w - H)$$

**Step 5 — Combine with Step 3:**

$$N_c = \dfrac{h_t (T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_w} = k_H \, \rho_{air} \, (H_w - H)$$

For dilute systems where humid volume ≈ dry-air volume, this reduces to:

$$\boxed{N_c = k_H \, (H_w - H)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Time for Constant Rate Period (Mass Balance)

**Starting principle:** The total mass of water removed during CRP equals $L_s(X_1 - X_c)$.

**Step 1 — Drying rate definition:**

$$N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A}\,\dfrac{dX}{d\theta} \quad\Rightarrow\quad d\theta = -\dfrac{L_s}{A\,N}\,dX$$

**Step 2 — Substitute constant $N = N_c$ and integrate from $X_1$ to $X_c$:**

$$\theta_c = \int_{0}^{\theta_c} d\theta = -\dfrac{L_s}{A\,N_c}\int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{L_s}{A\,N_c}\,(X_1 - X_c)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick:** $L_s$ brings the solid mass, $A$ brings the area, $N_c$ brings the rate — what remains is moisture difference $(X_1 - X_c)$, giving **time**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Time for First Falling Rate Period (Linear Model)

**Step 1 — Rate is linear in $X$:**

$$N = N_c \,\dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

**Step 2 — Write the differential equation:**

$$d\theta = -\dfrac{L_s}{A\,N}\,dX = -\dfrac{L_s\,(X_c - X^*)}{A\,N_c\,(X - X^*)}\,dX$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $X_c$ to $X_2$ (where $X_2$ is the moisture content at the end of FFRP):**

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = -\dfrac{L_s\,(X_c - X^*)}{A\,N_c}\int_{X_c}^{X_2}\dfrac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

**Step 4 — Standard integral $\int dx/x = \ln(x)$:**

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = \dfrac{L_s\,(X_c - X^*)}{A\,N_c}\Bigl[-\ln(X - X^*)\Bigr]_{X_c}^{X_2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the limits:**

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = \dfrac{L_s\,(X_c - X^*)}{A\,N_c}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Four Regimes

| Feature | A–B (Initial) | B–C (CRP) | C–D (FFRP) | D–E (SFRP) |
|---------|---------------|-----------|------------|------------|
| **Surface state** | Heating up | Fully wet | Partial wet patches | Fully dry |
| **Controlling resistance** | Transient | External gas film | Gas film + internal capillary | Internal diffusion only |
| **Rate vs $X$** | Variable | **Constant** | Decreasing (often linear) | Decreasing (asymptotic) |
| **Mathematical model** | Neglected | $N = N_c$ | $N = N_c(X-X^*)/(X_c-X^*)$ | Diffusion integral |
| **External air effect on $N$?** | Yes | **Strongly yes** | Moderate | Negligible |
| **Solid temperature** | Rises to $T_w$ | $\approx T_w$ | Rises above $T_w$ | Approaches $T_g$ |
| **Practical duration** | Minutes | Hours (often dominant) | Hours | Hours |
| **KTU design priority** | Low | **High** | High | Medium |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A wet solid is dried from $X_1 = 0.40$ to $X_2 = 0.05$ kg water/kg dry solid. Critical moisture content $X_c = 0.20$ and equilibrium moisture content $X^* = 0.02$. The constant drying rate $N_c = 1.5$ kg/m²·h, and the dry solid mass per unit area is $L_s/A = 25$ kg/m². Calculate the total drying time. Assume linear FFRP and neglect A–B and D–E.

**Step 1 — Time for constant rate period (B → C):**

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{L_s}{A\,N_c}\,(X_1 - X_c) = \dfrac{25}{1.5}\,(0.40 - 0.20)$$

$$\theta_c = 16.67 \times 0.20 = 3.33\;\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Time for first falling rate period (C → D):**

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = \dfrac{L_s}{A\,N_c}\,(X_c - X^*)\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 16.67 \times (0.20 - 0.02)\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.18}{0.03}\right)$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 16.67 \times 0.18 \times \ln(6) = 16.67 \times 0.18 \times 1.7918$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 5.375\;\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Total drying time:**

$$\theta_{total} = \theta_c + \theta_{FRP_1} = 3.33 + 5.375 = 8.71\;\text{h}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Standard Rate of Drying Curve — Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block is a **block-level functional flow** that maps the sequential regimes of drying, the dominant physical mechanism in each, and the corresponding mathematical model used in KTU problems. It avoids any reserved keyword collision and uses alphanumeric node identifiers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Wet Solid at Troom]:::start --> A2[Regime A-B Initial Adjustment Period]
    A2 --> A3{Surface Temp Reached Tw}:::decision
    A3 --> A4[Regime B-C Constant Rate Period]
    A4 --> A5[Surface fully wet]
    A5 --> A6[Drying rate equals Nc]
    A6 --> A7[Reaches Xc Critical Point]
    A7 --> A8[Regime C-D First Falling Rate Period]
    A8 --> A9[Surface wet patches shrink]
    A9 --> A10[Internal capillary transport begins]
    A10 --> A11[Rate decreases linearly]
    A11 --> A12[Regime D-E Second Falling Rate Period]
    A12 --> A13[Surface fully dry]
    A13 --> A14[Internal diffusion controls]
    A14 --> A15[Rate approaches zero]
    A15 --> A16[Reaches Xstar Equilibrium]
    A16 --> A17[Dry Solid at Tg]:::end

    A2 -.Heat transfer.-> A3
    A4 -.External gas film controls.-> A5
    A8 -.Linear model.-> A9
    A12 -.Diffusion integral.-> A13

    classDef start fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Moisture States and Transitions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Free Moisture XF equals X minus Xstar]:::moist
    M2[Bound Moisture XB hygroscopic region]:::moist
    M3[Unbound Moisture removable above Xstar]:::moist
    M4[Equilibrium Moisture Xstar non-removable]:::moist

    M1 --> M5{Is X greater than Xstar?}
    M5 -->|Yes| M3
    M5 -->|No| M4
    M3 --> M6[Region above Xc surface controlled]
    M6 --> M7[Region between Xstar and Xc internal controlled]
    M7 --> M4

    classDef moist fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Engineering Significance Map

| Industrial Application | Dominant Regime | Design Implication |
|------------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Spray dryer for milk | A–B + B–C | Very short residence; use $N_c$ |
| Rotary dryer for fertilizer | B–C + C–D | Both must be modeled; long residence |
| Tray dryer for pigments | C–D + D–E | Use linear FFRP + diffusion |
| Fluidized bed for granules | B–C dominant | High $h_t$ → high $N_c$ |
| Solar drying of crops | D–E | Limited by internal diffusion; slow |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **constant rate period** and **falling rate period** of drying.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Constant Rate Period (B–C) | Falling Rate Period (C–E) |
|--------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| Surface state | Fully saturated with free moisture | Surface partially or fully dry |
| Controlling resistance | External gas film | Internal (capillary + diffusion) |
| Rate dependence on $X$ | Independent of $X$ | Decreasing function of $X$ |
| Solid temperature | Constant at $T_w$ | Rises from $T_w$ toward $T_g$ |
| Mathematical form | $N = N_c$ (constant) | $N = f(X)$, linear or diffusion-based |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define **critical moisture content** and **equilibrium moisture content**. Why are they different?

**Model Answer:**

**Critical moisture content ($X_c$):** The moisture content of the solid at which the **constant rate period ends** and the falling rate period begins. It is the point where the surface is no longer fully saturated.

**Equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$):** The moisture content at which the solid is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the surrounding air, and drying effectively stops.

**Why different:** $X_c$ is a **kinetic transition point** determined by the relative rates of surface evaporation and internal moisture transport — it depends on drying conditions and solid thickness. $X^*$ is a **thermodynamic property** of the solid–air pair at given $T$ and RH — it represents the limit of drying, not a transition.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **different regimes of drying** in a standard rate of drying curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet solid is dried from an initial moisture content of $X_1 = 0.35$ kg water/kg dry solid to a final moisture content of $X_2 = 0.08$ kg water/kg dry solid. The critical moisture content is $X_c = 0.18$ and the equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = 0.025$. The constant rate of drying is $N_c = 1.2$ kg/m²·h. The mass of dry solid per unit drying area is $L_s/A = 20$ kg/m². Assuming a linear falling rate period and neglecting the initial adjustment period, calculate the **total drying time**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Description of regimes:**

The standard rate of drying curve plots **drying rate $N$** (y-axis) vs **moisture content $X$** (x-axis). The curve is divided into **four regimes**:

1. **A–B (Initial Adjustment Period):** The wet solid heats up from its initial temperature to the wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ of the air. Drying rate is irregular and this period is short.

2. **B–C (Constant Rate Period):** The surface is fully wet. Drying proceeds as if from a free water surface. The rate is **constant** and is given by $N_c = h_t(T_g - T_w)/\lambda_w = k_H(H_w - H)$. **External gas-film resistance** controls the rate.

3. **C–D (First Falling Rate Period):** The surface starts showing dry patches. The wetted area decreases, and the rate falls. The rate is **linearly proportional** to the free moisture content: $N = N_c(X - X^*)/(X_c - X^*)$.

4. **D–E (Second Falling Rate Period):** The surface is fully dry. Moisture is removed by **internal diffusion** through the solid. The rate continues to fall asymptotically to zero at $X = X^*$.

**(b) Numerical calculation:**

**Step 1 — Time for constant rate period:**

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{L_s}{A\,N_c}\,(X_1 - X_c) = \dfrac{20}{1.2}\,(0.35 - 0.18)$$

$$\theta_c = 16.67 \times 0.17 = 2.833\;\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Time for first falling rate period:**

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = \dfrac{L_s}{A\,N_c}\,(X_c - X^*)\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 16.67 \times (0.18 - 0.025)\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.155}{0.055}\right)$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 16.67 \times 0.155 \times \ln(2.818)$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 16.67 \times 0.155 \times 1.0362 = 2.677\;\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Total drying time:**

$$\theta_{total} = 2.833 + 2.677 = 5.51\;\text{h}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of moisture movement** in the falling rate period. Why does the drying rate decrease continuously in this period? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch dryer contains 100 kg of dry solid. The initial moisture content is $X_1 = 0.30$ kg/kg and the final moisture content required is $X_2 = 0.06$ kg/kg. The critical moisture content is $X_c = 0.15$ kg/kg and the equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg. The constant rate of drying is $N_c = 2.0$ kg/m²·h and the drying surface area is $A = 5$ m². Calculate the **time required for the constant rate period** and the **time required for the falling rate period** (linear model). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of moisture movement in falling rate period:**

In the falling rate period, the **surface of the solid is no longer fully saturated** with moisture. The plane of evaporation gradually **recedes into the interior** of the solid.

The mechanism proceeds in two sub-stages:

- **First Falling Rate Period (C–D):** Liquid water moves from the interior of the solid to the surface by **capillary action**. As the surface dries, the rate of capillary supply becomes insufficient to keep the surface fully wet, so dry patches appear. The rate decreases because the **effective wetted area shrinks**.

- **Second Falling Rate Period (D–E):** The surface is completely dry. Moisture now travels as **liquid or vapour through the porous solid structure** by **diffusion**. This is a much slower process than capillary flow, and the rate continues to decrease until the solid reaches **equilibrium moisture content $X^*$**, at which point the vapour pressure of moisture in the solid equals the partial pressure of water vapour in the air, and the net rate of evaporation becomes zero.

The drying rate decreases continuously because:
1. The driving force for moisture transfer (concentration gradient) decreases as the solid dries.
2. The path length for moisture to travel to the surface increases.
3. The external driving forces $(T_g - T_s)$ and $(H_w - H)$ become irrelevant once the surface is dry — internal diffusion alone controls.

**(b) Numerical calculation:**

**Given:** $L_s = 100$ kg, $A = 5$ m², $N_c = 2.0$ kg/m²·h, $X_1 = 0.30$, $X_c = 0.15$, $X_2 = 0.06$, $X^* = 0.02$.

**Step 1 — Time for constant rate period:**

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{L_s}{A\,N_c}\,(X_1 - X_c) = \dfrac{100}{5 \times 2.0}\,(0.30 - 0.15)$$

$$\theta_c = 10 \times 0.15 = 1.50\;\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Time for first falling rate period:**

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = \dfrac{L_s}{A\,N_c}\,(X_c - X^*)\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 10 \times (0.15 - 0.02)\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.13}{0.04}\right)$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 10 \times 0.13 \times \ln(3.25)$$

$$\theta_{FRP_1} = 10 \times 0.13 \times 1.1787 = 1.532\;\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Total drying time:**

$$\theta_{total} = 1.50 + 1.532 = 3.03\;\text{h}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly lose marks on this topic due to the following mistakes:
>
> 1. **Confusing $X^*$ and $X_c$:** Many students substitute $X_c$ for $X^*$ in the falling rate time equation. Always check: $X^*$ is a **fixed equilibrium value**; $X_c$ is the **transition point** where CRP ends.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $X_2$ and $X^*$ to dry basis:** Some problems give moisture on **wet basis** (kg water/kg wet solid). The drying rate formulas **require dry basis**. Marks deducted: 1–2.
>
> 3. **Mixing units:** If $N_c$ is in kg/m²·h and $L_s/A$ in kg/m², the result for $\theta$ comes out in **hours**. Using seconds or minutes will give wrong numerical answers.
>
> 4. **Skipping the initial transient:** KTU sometimes provides data for region A–B. If asked, **always include it** even if numerically small.
>
> 5. **Linear FFRP assumption not stated:** When using the linear model, the answer should mention "assuming linear FFRP" — silent assumption costs 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 6. **Missing the Ln term:** The integration in the falling rate period gives a **natural logarithm**, not a simple ratio. Several students write $\theta = (L_s/A)(X_c - X_2)/N_c$ — **this is wrong**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Different Regimes of Drying**

- ☐ The standard drying rate curve plots **$N$ vs $X$** with four segments: **A–B, B–C, C–D, D–E**.
- ☐ The **constant rate period (B–C)** is governed entirely by **external gas-film heat/mass transfer** — independent of solid type.
- ☐ The **falling rate period (C–E)** is governed by **internal moisture transport** — solid-specific.
- ☐ Two key moisture contents: $X_c$ (critical, kinetic) and $X^*$ (equilibrium, thermodynamic) — **never confuse them**.
- ☐ Free moisture $X_F = X - X^*$ is the moisture that can be removed; bound moisture cannot.
- ☐ The constant rate formula: $N_c = h_t(T_g - T_w)/\lambda_w = k_H(H_w - H)$.
- ☐ Time for CRP: $\theta_c = (L_s/A)(X_1 - X_c)/N_c$.
- ☐ Linear FFRP model: $N = N_c(X - X^*)/(X_c - X^*)$.
- ☐ Time for linear FFRP: $\theta_{FRP_1} = (L_s/A)(X_c - X^*)/N_c \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
- ☐ Total drying time: $\theta_{total} = \theta_B + \theta_c + \theta_{FRP_1} + \theta_{FRP_2}$.
- ☐ In **constant rate period**, solid surface temperature $\approx T_w$ (wet-bulb).
- ☐ In **falling rate period**, solid surface temperature rises from $T_w$ to $T_g$.
- ☐ Higher $N_c$ → shorter CRP → smaller dryer; achieved by increasing $h_t$ via higher air velocity.
- ☐ Material thickness, porosity, and binding of moisture shift $X_c$ to **higher values** → longer FFRP.
- ☐ KTU design problems almost always assume **linear FFRP** unless diffusion data is given.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material and Energy Balance in a Continuous Counter Current Dryer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Material and Energy Balance in a Continuous Counter Current Dryer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Counter-Current Dryer (CCCD):** A continuous-flow industrial drying apparatus in which the **wet solid** and the **hot drying air** travel in **opposite directions** through the equipment. The fresh, hot, low-humidity air contacts the nearly-dry product at one end, while the moist, low-temperature air contacts the freshly-fed wet solid at the other end. The driving force for mass transfer (water vapour transfer from solid to air) is maintained along the length of the dryer.

The two fundamental laws governing such a dryer are:

1. **Conservation of Mass (Material Balance):** Mass of water entering the system = Mass of water leaving the system.
2. **Conservation of Energy (Energy/Heat Balance):** Total enthalpy entering the dryer = Total enthalpy leaving the dryer + Heat losses.

These balances are written on a **bone-dry basis** (i.e., per kg of moisture-free solid and per kg of moisture-free air), which is the standard KTU reference frame.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Towel on a Windy Beach"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine drying a wet towel in a steady breeze.
> - The **towel (wet solid)** enters at one end soaked, and exits at the other end almost dry.
> - The **breeze (hot air)** blows in the *opposite direction* — fresh dry air at the dry end, becoming humid as it sweeps past wetter sections.
> - At every point, water molecules migrate from towel → air because the air there is "thirsty" (low humidity). This is **counter-current flow**.
> - The towel also warms up and cools down as it travels, releasing and absorbing heat — which is the **energy balance** in action.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Symbols (KTU Standard)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $L_s$ | Mass flow rate of bone-dry solid | kg dry solid / hr |
| $X_1, X_2$ | Moisture content of solid (inlet, outlet) on dry basis | kg water / kg dry solid |
| $G_s$ | Mass flow rate of bone-dry air | kg dry air / hr |
| $Y_1, Y_2$ | Humidity of air (inlet, outlet) | kg water / kg dry air |
| $T_1, T_2$ | Dry-bulb temperature of air (inlet, outlet) | °C |
| $t_1, t_2$ | Temperature of solid (inlet, outlet) | °C |
| $C_{p,s}$ | Heat capacity of dry solid | kJ / (kg·K) |
| $C_{p,L}$ | Heat capacity of liquid water = **4.187 kJ/(kg·K)** | kJ / (kg·K) |
| $C_{p,a}$ | Heat capacity of dry air = **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** | kJ / (kg·K) |
| $C_{p,v}$ | Heat capacity of water vapour = **1.88 kJ/(kg·K)** | kJ / (kg·K) |
| $\lambda_w$ | Latent heat of vaporisation at 0 °C ≈ **2501 kJ/kg** | kJ / kg |
| $q$ | Specific air rate $= G_s / L_s$ | kg dry air / kg dry solid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference State Convention (KTU Standard):** All enthalpies are evaluated with respect to **0 °C** as the datum for water (liquid), ice, and dry air. The latent heat of vaporisation $\lambda_w = 2501$ kJ/kg is the value used at 0 °C unless otherwise stated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Setup and Stream Identification

In a **counter-current** dryer with two inlet streams and two outlet streams, the streams are arranged as follows:

| End of Dryer | Solid Stream | Air Stream |
|---|---|---|
| **Left End (Solid Inlet)** | Wet solid ($X_1$, $t_1$) enters | Exhaust air ($Y_2$, $T_2$) leaves |
| **Right End (Solid Outlet)** | Dry product ($X_2$, $t_2$) leaves | Fresh hot air ($Y_1$, $T_1$) enters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-current advantage:** The hottest, driest air meets the driest solid, and the coolest, most humid air meets the wettest solid. This keeps the **temperature gradient** and **humidity gradient** driving forces more uniform across the dryer length compared to co-current flow — leading to higher thermal efficiency.

### 2.2 Material Balance — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the control volume:** The entire dryer shell is the control volume, steady-state.

**Step 2 — Water balance on bone-dry basis:**

- Water in with wet solid = $L_s \cdot X_1$
- Water in with inlet air = $G_s \cdot Y_1$
- Water out with dried solid = $L_s \cdot X_2$
- Water out with exhaust air = $G_s \cdot Y_2$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation:**

$$L_s X_1 + G_s Y_1 = L_s X_2 + G_s Y_2$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$L_s (X_1 - X_2) = G_s (Y_2 - Y_1)$$

**Step 5 — Define specific air rate** $q = G_s / L_s$:

$$q = \dfrac{X_1 - X_2}{Y_2 - Y_1}$$

The LHS $L_s(X_1 - X_2)$ is the **rate of water evaporation** $W$ (kg water/hr). This single equation lets us solve for any one unknown if the others are given.

### 2.3 Energy Balance — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define enthalpies with reference at 0 °C:**

- Enthalpy of inlet air (per kg dry air) = $C_{p,a} \cdot T_1 + Y_1 \cdot C_{p,v} \cdot T_1 + Y_1 \cdot \lambda_w$
- Enthalpy of outlet air = $C_{p,a} \cdot T_2 + Y_2 \cdot C_{p,v} \cdot T_2 + Y_2 \cdot \lambda_w$
- Enthalpy of wet solid inlet (per kg dry solid) = $(C_{p,s} + X_1 \cdot C_{p,L}) \cdot t_1$
- Enthalpy of dried solid outlet = $(C_{p,s} + X_2 \cdot C_{p,L}) \cdot t_2$

**Step 2 — Apply First Law of Thermodynamics (steady state, adiabatic):**

$$\sum H_{in} = \sum H_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$G_s \left[ (C_{p,a} + Y_1 C_{p,v}) T_1 + Y_1 \lambda_w \right] + L_s (C_{p,s} + X_1 C_{p,L}) t_1$$
$$= G_s \left[ (C_{p,a} + Y_2 C_{p,v}) T_2 + Y_2 \lambda_w \right] + L_s (C_{p,s} + X_2 C_{p,L}) t_2 + Q_L$$

where $Q_L$ is the heat loss to surroundings (taken as **0 for adiabatic** drying).

**Step 4 — Introduce the "Humid Heat" concept:** $C_s = C_{p,a} + Y \cdot C_{p,v}$ (kJ per kg dry air per K).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = L_s (X_1 - X_2)$ | Rate of moisture evaporation (kg water/hr) |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{X_1 - X_2}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \dfrac{G_s}{L_s}$ | Specific air consumption |
| 3 | $L_s = F(1 - x_F) = D(1 - x_D)$ | Bone-dry solid rate from wet-basis feeds |
| 4 | $X = \dfrac{x}{1 - x}$ | Dry-basis moisture from wet-basis |
| 5 | $C_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \, Y$ | Humid heat of moist air (kJ/kg dry air·K) |
| 6 | $C_{s,\text{solid}} = C_{p,s} + X \cdot C_{p,L}$ | Humid heat of wet solid (kJ/kg dry solid·K) |
| 7 | $\lambda_w = 2501$ kJ/kg | Latent heat of vaporisation at 0 °C |
| 8 | $H_{\text{air}} = C_s T + Y \, \lambda_w$ | Total enthalpy of moist air per kg dry air |
| 9 | $H_{\text{solid}} = (C_{p,s} + X \cdot C_{p,L}) \, t$ | Enthalpy of wet solid per kg dry solid |
| 10 | $G_s [C_{s1} T_1 + Y_1 \lambda_w] + L_s \, C_{L1} \, t_1 = G_s [C_{s2} T_2 + Y_2 \lambda_w] + L_s \, C_{L2} \, t_2$ | Master energy balance (adiabatic) |
| 11 | $q = \dfrac{C_{L2} t_2 - C_{L1} t_1}{C_{s1} T_1 + Y_1 \lambda_w - C_{s2} T_2 - Y_2 \lambda_w}$ | Specific air rate from energy balance |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{W \cdot \lambda_w}{G_s \, C_{s1} (T_1 - T_2)}$ | Thermal efficiency (≈ 0.6–0.8 typical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:** In adiabatic drying, the **enthalpy of the inlet air stream equals the enthalpy of the outlet air stream** only if no solid heating occurs. When solid heating is significant, the difference is supplied by the air — this is why the energy balance couples $q$ and $Y_2$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

These balances are critical for designing industrial dryers used in:
- **Cement industry** — drying limestone and clinker raw mix
- **Fertiliser industry** — drying urea, ammonium nitrate
- **Food industry** — drying grains, milk powder, sugar crystals
- **Pharmaceutical industry** — drying active ingredients
- **Mineral processing** — drying sand, ores, coal

Accurate balance calculations minimise fuel consumption, control product quality (final moisture), and prevent over-drying (which wastes energy) or under-drying (which causes spoilage).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Energy Balance Equation (from First Principles)

**Derivation Goal:** Obtain the expression for $q = G_s / L_s$ purely in terms of inlet and outlet conditions.

Starting from the master balance (Eq. 10 above):

$$G_s [C_{s1} T_1 + Y_1 \lambda_w] + L_s \, C_{L1} \, t_1 = G_s [C_{s2} T_2 + Y_2 \lambda_w] + L_s \, C_{L2} \, t_2$$

where $C_{L1} = C_{p,s} + X_1 C_{p,L}$ and $C_{L2} = C_{p,s} + X_2 C_{p,L}$.

**Step A — Divide throughout by $L_s$ and substitute $q = G_s / L_s$:**

$$q [C_{s1} T_1 + Y_1 \lambda_w] + C_{L1} t_1 = q [C_{s2} T_2 + Y_2 \lambda_w] + C_{L2} t_2$$

**Step B — Collect all terms containing $q$ on the left side:**

$$q [C_{s1} T_1 + Y_1 \lambda_w - C_{s2} T_2 - Y_2 \lambda_w] = C_{L2} t_2 - C_{L1} t_1$$

**Step C — Solve for $q$:**

$$q = \dfrac{C_{L2} t_2 - C_{L1} t_1}{C_{s1} T_1 + Y_1 \lambda_w - C_{s2} T_2 - Y_2 \lambda_w}$$

This is the **working energy balance** used in KTU exam problems. Note that the denominator contains $Y_2$, which is usually unknown — so the material balance equation (Eq. 2) must be solved *simultaneously* with this energy balance.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Comprehensive Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A continuous counter-current **adiabatic** dryer handles **2000 kg/hr** of wet granular solid from an initial moisture of **30 % (wet basis)** to a final moisture of **5 % (wet basis)**. The wet solid enters at **30 °C** and leaves at **70 °C**. Air enters at **130 °C** with humidity **$Y_1 = 0.015$ kg/kg** and leaves at **$T_2 = 80$ °C**. Given $C_{p,s} = 1.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_{p,L} = 4.187$ kJ/(kg·K), and $\lambda_w = 2501$ kJ/kg.
> **Calculate:** (a) The mass flow rate of bone-dry air $G_s$, (b) The exit air humidity $Y_2$.

---

#### Step 1 — Convert wet-basis moisture to dry-basis

The moisture content on a dry basis is given by:

$$X = \dfrac{x}{1 - x}$$

Applying to inlet:

$$X_1 = \dfrac{0.30}{1 - 0.30} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

Applying to outlet:

$$X_2 = \dfrac{0.05}{1 - 0.05} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

---

#### Step 2 — Calculate the dry-solid flow rate $L_s$

The bone-dry solid is conserved:

$$L_s = F (1 - x_F) = 2000 (1 - 0.30) = 2000 \times 0.70 = 1400 \text{ kg dry solid/hr}$$

---

#### Step 3 — Calculate the rate of water evaporation

$$W = L_s (X_1 - X_2) = 1400 (0.4286 - 0.0526) = 1400 \times 0.3760 = 526.4 \text{ kg water/hr}$$

This is the moisture that must be absorbed by the air.

---

#### Step 4 — Set up the material balance (to relate $Y_2$ and $q$)

$$q = \dfrac{X_1 - X_2}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \dfrac{0.3760}{Y_2 - 0.015}$$

Rearranging:

$$Y_2 - 0.015 = \dfrac{0.3760}{q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Y_2 = 0.015 + \dfrac{0.3760}{q}$$

---

#### Step 5 — Compute the humid-heat terms

**Solid humid heats:**

$$C_{L1} = C_{p,s} + X_1 C_{p,L} = 1.5 + (0.4286)(4.187) = 1.5 + 1.7946 = 3.2946 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$C_{L2} = C_{p,s} + X_2 C_{p,L} = 1.5 + (0.0526)(4.187) = 1.5 + 0.2203 = 1.7203 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Air humid heats:**

$$C_{s1} = 1.005 + 1.88 \, Y_1 = 1.005 + (1.88)(0.015) = 1.005 + 0.0282 = 1.0332 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$C_{s2} = 1.005 + 1.88 \, Y_2 \quad \text{(contains unknown } Y_2\text{)}$$

---

#### Step 6 — Write the energy balance

$$q [C_{s1} T_1 + Y_1 \lambda_w] + C_{L1} t_1 = q [C_{s2} T_2 + Y_2 \lambda_w] + C_{L2} t_2$$

Substituting known numerical values:

$$q \left[ (1.0332)(130) + (0.015)(2501) \right] + (3.2946)(30) = q \left[ C_{s2} (80) + Y_2 (2501) \right] + (1.7203)(70)$$

Compute each numerical bracket:

$$(1.0332)(130) = 134.316 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

$$(0.015)(2501) = 37.515 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Sum (inlet air enthalpy excess):** $134.316 + 37.515 = 171.831$ kJ/kg dry air

$$(3.2946)(30) = 98.838 \text{ kJ/kg dry solid (inlet solid)}$$

$$(1.7203)(70) = 120.421 \text{ kJ/kg dry solid (outlet solid)}$$

So the energy balance becomes:

$$q (171.831) + 98.838 = q [80 \, C_{s2} + 2501 \, Y_2] + 120.421$$

$$171.831 \, q - 98.838 = 80 \, q \, C_{s2} + 2501 \, q \, Y_2 - 120.421$$

Wait — let me carefully rearrange. Starting from:

$$q (171.831) + 98.838 = q [80 \, C_{s2} + 2501 \, Y_2] + 120.421$$

Move solid enthalpies to one side:

$$q (171.831) - q [80 \, C_{s2} + 2501 \, Y_2] = 120.421 - 98.838$$

$$q \left[ 171.831 - 80 \, C_{s2} - 2501 \, Y_2 \right] = 21.583 \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$

---

#### Step 7 — Substitute the material balance relation

Recall: $Y_2 = 0.015 + \dfrac{0.3760}{q}$

Also $C_{s2} = 1.005 + 1.88 \, Y_2 = 1.005 + 1.88 \left( 0.015 + \dfrac{0.3760}{q} \right)$

$$C_{s2} = 1.005 + 0.0282 + \dfrac{0.7069}{q} = 1.0332 + \dfrac{0.7069}{q}$$

So:

$$80 \, C_{s2} = 80 \left( 1.0332 + \dfrac{0.7069}{q} \right) = 82.656 + \dfrac{56.552}{q}$$

$$2501 \, Y_2 = 2501 \left( 0.015 + \dfrac{0.3760}{q} \right) = 37.515 + \dfrac{940.376}{q}$$

Therefore the bracket in Eq. (A) becomes:

$$171.831 - 82.656 - \dfrac{56.552}{q} - 37.515 - \dfrac{940.376}{q}$$

$$= (171.831 - 82.656 - 37.515) - \left( \dfrac{56.552 + 940.376}{q} \right)$$

$$= 51.660 - \dfrac{996.928}{q}$$

So Eq. (A) simplifies to:

$$q \left[ 51.660 - \dfrac{996.928}{q} \right] = 21.583$$

$$51.660 \, q - 996.928 = 21.583$$

---

#### Step 8 — Solve for $q$ (specific air consumption)

$$51.660 \, q = 21.583 + 996.928 = 1018.511$$

$$q = \dfrac{1018.511}{51.660} = 19.717 \text{ kg dry air / kg dry solid}$$

---

#### Step 9 — Solve for $Y_2$ (exit humidity)

$$Y_2 = 0.015 + \dfrac{0.3760}{19.717} = 0.015 + 0.01907 = 0.03407 \text{ kg water / kg dry air}$$

---

#### Step 10 — Calculate $G_s$ (dry-air mass flow rate)

$$G_s = q \cdot L_s = 19.717 \times 1400 = 27{,}604 \text{ kg dry air/hr}$$

---

#### Step 11 — Verification by energy balance

Compute outlet air enthalpy:

$$C_{s2} = 1.005 + 1.88 (0.03407) = 1.005 + 0.06405 = 1.06905 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$H_{\text{air,in}} = (1.0332)(130) + (0.015)(2501) = 134.316 + 37.515 = 171.831 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

$$H_{\text{air,out}} = (1.06905)(80) + (0.03407)(2501) = 85.524 + 85.209 = 170.733 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

Check per kg dry solid:

$$\text{LHS: } q \cdot H_{\text{air,in}} + C_{L1} t_1 = (19.717)(171.831) + 98.838 = 3387.84 + 98.84 = 3486.68 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

$$\text{RHS: } q \cdot H_{\text{air,out}} + C_{L2} t_2 = (19.717)(170.733) + 120.421 = 3366.27 + 120.42 = 3486.69 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

✅ **Energy balance verified (rounding error ≈ 0.01 kJ).**

---

### 3.3 Final Answer Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $L_s$ | **1400 kg dry solid/hr** |
| $W$ (water evaporated) | **526.4 kg/hr** |
| $q$ (specific air consumption) | **19.72 kg dry air / kg dry solid** |
| $G_s$ (dry-air mass flow rate) | **27,604 kg dry air/hr** |
| $Y_2$ (exit air humidity) | **0.0341 kg water / kg dry air** |
| Thermal efficiency $\eta$ | ≈ **47.8 %** (using $\eta = W \lambda_w / (G_s C_{s1}(T_1-T_2))$) |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solving Strategy for KTU Problems

| Scenario Type | Given | Find | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type 1 | $X_1, X_2, Y_1, Y_2$ | $G_s$ | Material balance only |
| Type 2 | $X_1, X_2, Y_1, T_1, T_2, t_1, t_2$ | $G_s, Y_2$ | Simultaneous mat. + energy balance |
| Type 3 | All except $X_2$ | $X_2$ | Simultaneous mat. + energy balance |
| Type 4 | Air rate, $T_1, T_2$, inlet conditions | Heat duty $Q$ | Energy balance with $Q_L \neq 0$ |
| Type 5 | Efficiency defined | Inlet air $T_1$ | Reverse from $\eta$ relation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The material balance gives ONE equation; the energy balance gives a SECOND equation. Together they uniquely determine the two unknowns — almost always $G_s$ (or $q$) and $Y_2$.

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Counter-Current Dryer — Mass & Energy Flows

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inletWetSolid["WET SOLID INLET\nX1, t1 degC"] --> dryerShell
    inletHotAir["FRESH HOT AIR INLET\nY1, T1 degC"] --> dryerShell
    dryerShell["COUNTER CURRENT\nDRYER SHELL\nADIABATIC"] --> outletDrySolid
    dryerShell --> outletExhaustAir
    outletDrySolid["DRIED PRODUCT OUTLET\nX2, t2 degC"]
    outletExhaustAir["EXHAUST AIR OUTLET\nY2, T2 degC"]
    dryerShell -. "HEAT LOSS QL" .-> surroundings["SURROUNDINGS"]
    dryerShell -. "EVAPORATION ZONE" .-> waterVapor["WATER VAPOUR\nTRANSFER ZONE"]
```

        direction LR

### 4.3 Energy Flow Architecture — Enthalpy Inputs and Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q_in_air["Q AIR IN\nCs1 T1 plus Y1 lambda"] --> balance
    Q_in_solid["Q SOLID IN\nCL1 t1"] --> balance
    balance["ENERGY BALANCE NODE\nSteady State\nAdiabatic"]
    Q_out_air["Q AIR OUT\nCs2 T2 plus Y2 lambda"] --> balance
    Q_out_solid["Q SOLID OUT\nCL2 t2"] --> balance
    Q_loss["HEAT LOSS QL"] --> balance

```

| Dried product | Right end | Solid + trace moisture | $L_s$, $X_2$, $t_2$ |
| Exhaust air | Left end | Gas (humid) | $G_s$, $Y_2$, $T_2$ |
