<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition: Microfiltration (MF)**
> **Microfiltration (MF)** is a *pressure-driven membrane separation process* that uses finely microporous membranes with pore diameters typically ranging from **0.1 µm to 10 µm** to physically separate suspended particles, bacteria, oil emulsions, and large colloidal species from a liquid or gas feed stream. It operates at low transmembrane pressures (typically **0.1 – 2 bar**) and is governed by a sieving mechanism in which species larger than the membrane's nominal pore size are retained on the surface while solvent and dissolved species smaller than the pores permeate through the membrane matrix.

Microfiltration is the **coarsest** of the four primary pressure-driven membrane processes and is positioned at the upper end of the membrane separation spectrum:

| Process | Pore Size Range |
|---|---|
| Microfiltration (MF) | 0.1 – 10 µm |
| Ultrafiltration (UF) | 0.001 – 0.1 µm |
| Nanofiltration (NF) | ~0.001 µm |
| Reverse Osmosis (RO) | < 0.001 µm |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy — "The Sieve in Your Kitchen"**
> Imagine using a fine-mesh kitchen strainer to separate cooked rice from boiling water. The water flows freely through the holes, but the rice grains (larger than the openings) are caught and retained. Microfiltration works on exactly this **physical sieving principle**, but at a microscopic level. The membrane is the strainer, water (or solvent) is the permeate, and the trapped particles form a growing "cake" on top of the membrane — just like rice piling up in the strainer.
> 
> The only twist: in industry, instead of letting particles pile up (which would clog the strainer), engineers either periodically clean the membrane or sweep the feed *across* the surface in a **tangential flow** to keep it cleaner — exactly like skimming debris off a river with flowing water instead of letting it settle in a pond.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold Per Protocol)

- **Nominal Pore Size:** **0.1 – 10 µm**
- **Operating Transmembrane Pressure (TMP):** **0.1 – 2 bar** (≈ 10 – 200 kPa)
- **Typical Permeate Flux (J):** **100 – 10,000 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)**
- **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO):** Not strictly applicable to MF (defined for UF/RO); MF is described by pore size.
- **Recovery (R):** Ratio of permeate volume to feed volume, expressed as a percentage.
- **Rejection Coefficient (Rᵢ):** For a solute *i*, $R_i = 1 - \dfrac{C_{p,i}}{C_{f,i}}$

> [!IMPORTANT] **What Does MF Remove?**
> Microfiltration reliably removes:
> - **Suspended solids** (silt, clay, rust particles)
> - **Bacteria & protozoa** (e.g., *E. coli*, *Giardia* cysts)
> - **Oil emulsions** (oil–water separation)
> - **Large colloidal particles**
> - **Yeasts and molds** in beverage clarification
> 
> It does **NOT** remove: dissolved salts, viruses (mostly), small organic molecules, or ions — for these, UF/NF/RO is required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

Microfiltration, although mechanistically simple (a sieve), involves a rich interplay of fluid mechanics, surface chemistry, and transport phenomena. The three pillars of MF theory are:

### 2.1.1 Membrane Structure and Morphology

MF membranes can be classified by structure:

- **Symmetric (Isotropic) Membranes** — Pore structure uniform throughout thickness (e.g., track-etched polycarbonate, sintered metals).
- **Asymmetric (Anisotropic) Membranes** — Thin selective skin on top of a thicker, more porous sublayer. Most modern MF/UF membranes are asymmetric (e.g., phase-inversion polysulfone, PVDF, polyethersulfone).
- **Composite Membranes** — A thin selective layer (e.g., PTFE) coated on a stronger support (e.g., non-woven polyester).

### 2.1.2 Membrane Materials

| Material Family | Examples | Key Properties |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymeric** | Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), Polysulfone (PSf), Polyethersulfone (PES), Cellulose Acetate (CA), Polypropylene (PP), Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) | Low cost, easy fabrication, limited thermal/chemical resistance |
| **Ceramic** | Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂), Titania (TiO₂), Silicon Carbide (SiC) | High thermal/chemical resistance, long life, expensive, brittle |
| **Metallic** | Sintered stainless steel, silver | Extreme durability, niche applications |

### 2.1.3 Operating Configurations (Modes of Operation)

- **Dead-End (Normal Flow) Filtration** — Feed flows perpendicular to the membrane. All feed approaches the membrane surface; retained species accumulate as a cake. Used in batch and small-scale applications (e.g., sterile filtration in pharma).
- **Cross-Flow (Tangential Flow) Filtration** — Feed flows parallel to the membrane surface, creating a shear force that continuously sweeps accumulated particles away. Used in continuous industrial processes (e.g., dairy, biotech, water treatment).
- **Semi-Batch / Fed-Batch** — Hybrid mode used in many industrial plants.

### 2.1.4 The "Why" Behind Flux Decline — Membrane Fouling

The single most important practical phenomenon in MF is **flux decline**: the steady decrease of permeate flux with time at constant pressure (or the required increase in pressure to maintain constant flux). It is caused by:

1. **Pore blocking** — Particles lodged inside pores.
2. **Cake layer formation** — Particles deposited on the surface.
3. **Adsorption** — Species sticking to the pore walls.
4. **Concentration polarization** — Build-up of retained species near the membrane surface.
5. **Membrane compaction** — Physical compression under pressure (more relevant in RO).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE] **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values — never the bare `|` pipe, which breaks markdown tables.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Symbols / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{p}$ | $P_{in}$, $P_{out}$: feed/re retentate pressures; $P_{p}$: permeate pressure. *Units: bar or Pa.* |
| 2 | **Permeate Flux (Darcy's Law)** | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{tot}}$ | $J$: flux (m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ or LMH); $\mu$: viscosity; $R_{tot}$: total resistance (m⁻¹). |
| 3 | **Rejection Coefficient** | $R_{i} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{p,i}}{C_{f,i}}$ | $C_{p,i}$, $C_{f,i}$: permeate and feed concentrations of species *i*. |
| 4 | **Recovery (Yield)** | $Y = \dfrac{Q_{p}}{Q_{f}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{f,i}}{C_{r,i}}$ | $Q_{p}$: permeate flow; $Q_{f}$: feed flow; $C_{r,i}$: retentate concentration. |
| 5 | **Volume Flux Conversion** | $J\ [\text{LMH}] = J\ [\text{m/s}] \times 3.6 \times 10^{6}$ | 1 LMH = $2.778 \times 10^{-7}$ m/s. |
| 6 | **Resistance-in-Series Model** | $R_{tot} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f} + R_{cp}$ | $R_{m}$: membrane; $R_{c}$: cake; $R_{f}$: fouling (pore blocking); $R_{cp}$: concentration polarization. |
| 7 | **Cake Resistance (Carmen-Kozeny)** | $R_{c} = \dfrac{180 \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}{d_{p}^{2} \cdot \varepsilon^{3}} \cdot \delta_{c}$ | $\varepsilon$: cake porosity; $d_{p}$: particle diameter; $\delta_{c}$: cake thickness. |
| 8 | **Hagen-Poiseuille Flow Through Pore** | $J = \dfrac{n \pi r_{p}^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | $n$: pore density; $r_{p}$: pore radius; $L$: pore length. |
| 9 | **Critical Flux** | $J_{crit}$: flux below which no fouling occurs. Determined empirically by flux stepping. | $J_{crit}$ separates stable and unstable operating regimes. |
| 10 | **Permeability** | $L_{p} = \dfrac{J}{\Delta P}$ | $L_{p}$: membrane permeability (m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ or LMH/bar). |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT] **Where Microfiltration is Used (KTU Industry-Relevant Applications)**
> 
> 1. **Water & Wastewater Treatment** — Pretreatment for RO/NF; turbidity removal; *E. coli* and protozoan cyst removal.
> 2. **Dairy Industry** — Bacteria and somatic cell removal from milk (cold pasteurization, "ESL milk"), whey clarification, cheese brine filtration.
> 3. **Pharmaceuticals & Biotechnology** — Sterile filtration of antibiotics, vaccines, cell culture media; clarification of fermentation broths; mammalian cell harvest.
> 4. **Food & Beverage** — Wine and beer clarification; juice clarification; sugar refining; vinegar production.
> 5. **Oil–Water Emulsion Separation** — Refinery and metalworking fluid treatment.
> 6. **Semiconductor Industry** — Ultrapure water (UPW) production for wafer rinsing.
> 7. **Air & Gas Filtration** — HEPA/ULPA filters (MERV ratings) for cleanrooms, hospitals, HVAC.
> 8. **Bioreactor Cell Retention** — Perfusion bioreactors using hollow-fiber MF modules.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING] **Strict Protocol Reminder:** No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is justified with a written explanation, and all multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned}...\end{aligned}` blocks with a single blank line before and after.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Permeate Flux Equation (Darcy's Law for Membranes)

### Step 1 — Starting from Darcy's Law for Flow Through a Porous Medium

Darcy's law, in its original form for flow through a porous bed, states that the superficial velocity of a fluid is proportional to the pressure gradient and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity:

$$
v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}
$$

**Symbol mapping:**
- $v$ → superficial velocity through the porous medium (m·s⁻¹)
- $K$ → permeability of the porous medium (m²)
- $\mu$ → dynamic viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s)
- $\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ → pressure gradient along the flow direction (Pa·m⁻¹)

### Step 2 — Adapting Darcy's Law to a Membrane

For a membrane of thickness $L_{m}$, the pressure drop occurs across this finite thickness. Replacing the differential pressure with the transmembrane pressure drop $\Delta P$ divided by membrane thickness $L_{m}$:

$$
v = \dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L_{m}}
$$

**Explanation:** The negative sign in Darcy's original law is dropped because we define flow in the direction of decreasing pressure (permeate side), and the gradient $\Delta P / L_{m}$ is positive by convention.

### Step 3 — Introducing the Hydraulic Resistance Concept

We define the **membrane hydraulic resistance** $R_{m}$ as:

$$
R_{m} = \dfrac{L_{m}}{K}
$$

**Units check:** $R_{m}$ has units of m⁻¹ (since $L_{m}$ is in m and $K$ is in m²). This gives a tidy equation where the resistance is independent of fluid properties.

### Step 4 — Recasting the Velocity as the Permeate Flux

In membrane science, the superficial velocity is exactly the **volumetric permeate flux** $J$ (volumetric flow rate per unit membrane area). Substituting $R_{m}$:

$$
J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{m}}
$$

**Physical meaning:** At higher TMP or lower viscosity, flux increases. A "tighter" membrane (larger $R_{m}$) gives lower flux — the resistance is intuitive.

### Step 5 — Generalizing to the Resistance-in-Series Model

In real operation, additional resistances accumulate beyond the clean-membrane resistance. Define:

- $R_{m}$ → clean membrane resistance
- $R_{c}$ → cake layer resistance (deposited particles)
- $R_{f}$ → fouling resistance (pore blocking, irreversible)
- $R_{cp}$ → concentration polarization resistance

The total resistance is the **sum** of these contributions because, in series, the same flux must pass through each layer (continuity of volumetric flow), and pressure drops add:

$$
R_{tot} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f} + R_{cp}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Generalized Flux Equation (KTU High-Yield Result)

$$
J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f} + R_{cp})}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the **central working equation** for microfiltration. The numerator is the "driving force"; the denominator is the "total resistance." All design optimization in MF aims to **maximize $J$** by either (a) increasing $\Delta P$ (limited by membrane mechanical strength and fouling), (b) reducing $\mu$ (e.g., operating at higher temperature), or (c) reducing the various resistance terms (cleaning, choosing better membrane, optimizing cross-flow velocity).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cake Filtration Resistance (Carman-Kozeny)

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Cake

Consider a cake of thickness $\delta_{c}$ composed of spherical particles of diameter $d_{p}$. Let $\varepsilon$ be the cake porosity (volume fraction of voids in the cake, typically 0.35 – 0.5 for random close packing).

### Step 2 — Hydraulic Diameter of the Cake

Treat the cake as a packed bed of solids through which fluid flows in tortuous channels. The **specific surface area per unit volume of particles** is:

$$
S_{v} = \dfrac{6}{d_{p}}
$$

**Justification:** For a sphere, surface area = $\pi d_{p}^{2}$ and volume = $\pi d_{p}^{3}/6$, so surface-to-volume ratio is $6/d_{p}$.

The **specific surface area per unit volume of cake** is:

$$
S_{v,\text{cake}} = \dfrac{6 (1-\varepsilon)}{d_{p}}
$$

**Justification:** The factor $(1-\varepsilon)$ accounts for the volume fraction of solids in the cake.

### Step 3 — Carman-Kozeny Permeability

The Carman-Kozeny equation gives the permeability of a packed bed:

$$
K = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3}}{k_{c} \, S_{v,\text{cake}}^{2} \, (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}
$$

where $k_{c} \approx 5$ is the Kozeny constant (empirical, accounts for tortuosity and pore shape).

### Step 4 — Substituting and Simplifying

Plugging $S_{v,\text{cake}} = 6(1-\varepsilon)/d_{p}$:

$$
K = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3}}{k_{c} \cdot \left[\dfrac{6(1-\varepsilon)}{d_{p}}\right]^{2} \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3} \, d_{p}^{2}}{k_{c} \cdot 36 \, (1-\varepsilon)^{2} \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3} \, d_{p}^{2}}{36 \, k_{c} \, (1-\varepsilon)^{4}}
$$

### Step 5 — Cake Resistance

Cake resistance is $R_{c} = L_{m,\text{eff}} / K$, but for a cake of thickness $\delta_{c}$ treated as a packed bed of thickness $\delta_{c}$:

$$
R_{c} = \dfrac{\delta_{c}}{K} = \dfrac{36 \, k_{c} \, (1-\varepsilon)^{4} \, \delta_{c}}{\varepsilon^{3} \, d_{p}^{2}}
$$

**Substituting $k_{c} = 5$:**

$$
R_{c} = \dfrac{180 \, (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}{\varepsilon^{3} \, d_{p}^{2}} \cdot \delta_{c}
$$

**Final Form (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
R_{c} = \dfrac{180 \, (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}{d_{p}^{2} \, \varepsilon^{3}} \cdot \delta_{c}
$$

**Engineering insight:** Smaller particles (smaller $d_{p}$), denser cakes (lower $\varepsilon$), and thicker cakes all dramatically increase resistance — these are the reasons MF of fine colloids is so prone to rapid flux decline.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Hagen-Poiseuille Flow Through a Single Pore

### Step 1 — Setup

Consider a single cylindrical pore of radius $r_{p}$ and length $L$. Fluid flows through it under a pressure drop $\Delta P$.

The Hagen-Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric flow rate through one pore:

$$
q = \dfrac{\pi \, r_{p}^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Flow Through a Membrane

A membrane of area $A$ has $N$ pores. If the surface porosity (pore area fraction) is $\varepsilon_{s}$, then:

$$
N = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{s} \, A}{\pi r_{p}^{2}}
$$

The total volumetric flow rate $Q$ is:

$$
Q = N \cdot q = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{s} \, A}{\pi r_{p}^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi r_{p}^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{s} \, A \, r_{p}^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Flux

Flux $J = Q/A$:

$$
J = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{s} \, r_{p}^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

**Engineering insight:** Flux scales with $r_{p}^{2}$ — halving the pore radius quarters the flux. This is why MF membranes (large pores) have much higher fluxes than UF/NF/RO.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Operating Modes

| Parameter | Dead-End (Normal Flow) | Cross-Flow (Tangential Flow) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed flow direction** | Perpendicular to membrane | Parallel to membrane surface |
| **Particle behavior** | Accumulates on surface → thick cake | Swept away by shear → thin cake |
| **Flux vs. time** | Rapid decline; asymptotic | Stable plateau after initial decline |
| **Membrane area for same duty** | Large (batch) | Smaller (continuous) |
| **Energy consumption** | Low TMP, but frequent cleaning | High cross-flow pumping energy |
| **Best suited for** | Low solids, batch, sterile filtration | High solids, continuous operation |
| **Typical applications** | Pharmaceutical sterile filtration, lab | Dairy, biotech, water treatment |
| **Capital cost** | Lower | Higher (pumps, modules) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microfiltration Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Tank] --> B[Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Pre-Filter Strainer]
    C --> D[Membrane Module]
    D --> E[Permeate Collection Tank]
    D --> F[Retentate Recycle Line]
    F --> G[Retentate Bleed]
    F --> A
    D --> H[Backpulse or CIP System]
    H --> D
    D --> I[Pressure Transmitters PT in and PT out]
    D --> J[Flow Meter on Permeate Line]

    classDef tank fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef pump fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f
    classDef membrane fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d
    classDef instr fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#a21caf,color:#581c87
    classDef clean fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d

    class A,E tank
    class B pump
    class C,D membrane
    class I,J instr
    class G,H clean
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

1. **Feed Tank (A):** Stores the raw feed; equipped with level and temperature control.
2. **Feed Pump (B):** Pressurizes the feed to the operating TMP (typically 0.1 – 2 bar). Centrifugal pumps are common.
3. **Pre-Filter (C):** Coarse strainer (e.g., 100 – 500 µm) protects the membrane pump and module from large debris.
4. **Membrane Module (D):** The heart of the system — houses the membrane in one of four geometries: **plate-and-frame, hollow fiber, spiral wound,** or **tubular.**
5. **Permeate Collection (E):** Receives the purified filtrate; flow rate and quality are monitored.
6. **Retentate Recycle (F):** Returns unfiltered concentrate to the feed tank, maintaining cross-flow velocity. A bleed stream (G) maintains steady state by removing concentrated solids.
7. **Cleaning-in-Place (CIP) System (H):** Periodic chemical cleaning (acid, base, oxidant) to restore flux.
8. **Instrumentation (I, J):** Pressure transmitters and flow meters enable real-time TMP and flux monitoring, supporting automatic process control.

---

## 4.2 Module Geometry Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plate and Frame] --> A1[Flat sheets stacked\nEasy to clean\nLow area per volume]
    B[Hollow Fiber] --> B1[Self supporting bundles\nVery high area per volume\nBackflushable]
    C[Spiral Wound] --> C1[Envelopes rolled\nHigh area per volume\nNot backflushable]
    D[Tubular] --> D1[Largest channels\nHandles viscous slurries\nHighest pumping cost]

    classDef geom fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef desc fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d
    class A,B,C,D geom
    class A1,B1,C1,D1 desc
```

**Module Selection Logic (KTU Exam Favorite):**

- **Hollow Fiber MF** → Most common for water treatment; can be backflushed.
- **Tubular MF** → For high-viscosity, fouling-prone streams (e.g., fermentation broths).
- **Plate-and-Frame** → For small batches in pharma and food; very cleanable.
- **Spiral Wound** → Compact and cheap, but limited to relatively clean feeds.

---

## 4.3 Resistance-in-Series Conceptual Model (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Feed Side] --> Rm[Rm: Clean Membrane]
    Rm --> Rc[Rc: Cake Layer]
    Rc --> Rf[Rf: Fouling Layer]
    Rf --> Rcp[Rcp: Concentration Polarization]
    Rcp --> P[Permeate Side]

    F -. TMP Driving Force .-> P
    P --> J[Permeate Flux J]

    classDef layer fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#713f12
    classDef inout fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#312e81
    class Rm,Rc,Rf,Rcp layer
    class F,P,J inout
```

> [!NOTE] **How to Read This Diagram**
> The feed fluid must traverse **all four resistances in series** to become permeate. The total resistance $R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f + R_{cp}$ appears in the denominator of the flux equation. Reducing any single layer (e.g., by cross-flow to thin the cake) directly increases flux.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Q: Define microfiltration. List the typical pore size range and two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Microfiltration (MF)** is a *pressure-driven membrane separation process* in which a fluid is passed through a finely microporous membrane that physically retains particles larger than the membrane's pore size, allowing the solvent and smaller dissolved species to pass through as permeate.
> 
> - **Pore size range:** **0.1 µm to 10 µm**
> - **Operating pressure:** typically **0.1 – 2 bar**
> 
> **Two industrial applications:**
> 1. **Cold pasteurization of milk** — removal of bacteria and somatic cells without heat, preserving flavor and nutrients.
> 2. **Pretreatment for reverse osmosis in seawater desalination** — removes suspended solids, turbidity, and bacteria to protect the RO membrane from fouling.

*(Alternative accepted applications: sterile filtration in pharmaceuticals, clarification of fruit juices and wines, oil–water emulsion separation, cell harvest in biotechnology.)*

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]
**Q: Differentiate between dead-end and cross-flow microfiltration. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dead-End MF | Cross-Flow MF |
|---|---|---|
| Feed flow direction | Perpendicular to membrane | Parallel to membrane surface |
| Cake behavior | Builds up; not removed | Continuously thinned by shear |
| Flux vs. time | Declines steadily | Reaches a steady-state value |
| Operation | Batch | Continuous |

- **Advantage of dead-end MF:** Simple equipment, low pumping cost, ideal for low-solids sterile filtration (e.g., final sterile filter for injectables).
- **Advantage of cross-flow MF:** Higher sustainable flux in continuous operation, longer run times between cleanings, better suited to feeds with high suspended solids (e.g., fermentation broths).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice provided between two questions.

---

### Question B (Choice A) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and working of a **cross-flow microfiltration** system. Compare it with dead-end filtration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **resistance-in-series model** for membrane filtration and explain the significance of each resistance term. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Cross-Flow Microfiltration — Working Principle:**

In cross-flow MF, the feed stream flows **tangentially (parallel)** to the membrane surface. The feed is split into two streams:
- **Permeate** — passes through the membrane (purified).
- **Retentate** — concentrated stream that does not pass through; it is recycled or bled off.

The **tangential velocity** generates a wall shear stress that continuously **sweeps deposited particles** off the membrane surface, preventing the formation of a thick cake. This is the principal reason cross-flow MF is preferred for feeds with high suspended solids.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Tank] --> B[Recirculation Pump]
    B --> C[MF Module]
    C --> D[Permeate]
    C --> E[Retentate]
    E --> F[Retentate Recycle]
    F --> A
    E --> G[Bleed]
```

**Comparison with Dead-End Filtration (Tabular, 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Dead-End | Cross-Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Cake thickness | Grows unbounded | Stabilizes at low value |
| Flux stability | Poor | Good |
| Energy use | Low | Higher (pumping) |
| Best for | Low solids, batch | High solids, continuous |
| Membrane life | Limited by irreversible fouling | Longer run times |

**Key operating variables:** Transmembrane pressure (TMP), cross-flow velocity (CFV), feed concentration, temperature, pH.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Derivation of the Resistance-in-Series Model:**

**Step 1.** Start from Darcy's law applied across a membrane of thickness $L_m$:

$$
J = \dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L_m}
$$

**Step 2.** Define clean-membrane resistance $R_m = L_m / K$:

$$
J_0 = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m} \quad \text{(clean membrane flux)}
$$

**Step 3.** During real filtration, additional layers form. Each layer contributes an extra resistance, and because the same volumetric flux must pass through all layers in series, the resistances **add** to a total:

$$
R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f + R_{cp}
$$

**Step 4.** The flux becomes:

$$
J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_m + R_c + R_f + R_{cp})}
$$

**Significance of each term:**

- **$R_m$ — Membrane resistance:** Intrinsic to the membrane (pore size, porosity, thickness). Reduced by selecting a more open membrane.
- **$R_c$ — Cake resistance:** Formed by particles deposited on the membrane surface. Reduced by increasing cross-flow velocity or backflushing.
- **$R_f$ — Fouling resistance:** Caused by irreversible pore blocking, adsorption, or biofilm growth. Reduced by appropriate pre-treatment, periodic cleaning, or choosing a membrane with surface modification.
- **$R_{cp}$ — Concentration-polarization resistance:** A boundary-layer effect from accumulation of rejected solutes near the surface. Reduced by increasing shear (cross-flow) or reducing flux.

**Final form (boxed, 1 mark for explicit final answer):**

$$
\boxed{\,J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{tot}} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu(R_m + R_c + R_f + R_{cp})}\,}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice B) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **membrane fouling** in microfiltration. Discuss the mechanisms of **pore blocking** and **cake layer formation** with sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cross-flow MF unit is used to treat a feed containing **5 g/L of suspended biomass**. The membrane has a clean resistance of $R_m = 5 \times 10^{11}\ \text{m}^{-1}$. After 1 hour of operation at TMP = 0.5 bar and viscosity $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, the cake resistance $R_c$ has grown to $8 \times 10^{11}\ \text{m}^{-1}$. Estimate the **permeate flux** (in LMH) and the **percentage increase in total resistance** due to the cake. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Membrane Fouling — Definition:**

> Membrane fouling is the **irreversible or reversible accumulation** of material (particles, colloids, organics, microorganisms) on the membrane surface or within its pores, leading to a reduction in permeate flux and/or an alteration in rejection characteristics.

**Mechanisms of Fouling in MF:**

1. **Pore Blocking (Complete / Intermediate / Standard / Cake)**
   - Complete blocking: A particle seals off a pore entirely. Pore area becomes unavailable.
   - Intermediate blocking: A particle deposits on top of an already-deposited particle, partially covering a pore.
   - Standard blocking: Particles deposit on the **internal walls of pores**, reducing effective pore diameter.
   - Cake formation: Particles form a continuous layer on the surface, blocking pores underneath.
   
2. **Cake Layer Formation**
   - Particles too large to enter pores accumulate on the surface.
   - The cake grows in thickness with time and acts as an additional hydraulic resistance.
   - Cake thickness $\delta_c$ increases linearly with cumulative permeate volume per unit area in dead-end mode.
   - In cross-flow mode, the cake reaches a steady-state thickness when deposition is balanced by shear-induced erosion.

**Hermia's Models (Bonus 1 mark):**

The four classical Hermia fouling models (for constant-pressure dead-end filtration) describe the time-evolution of flux:

- **Complete blocking:** $J = J_0 \, e^{-k_b t}$
- **Intermediate blocking:** $J = \dfrac{J_0}{(1 + k_i t)}$
- **Standard blocking:** $J = \dfrac{J_0}{(1 + k_s t)^{2}}$
- **Cake filtration:** $J = \dfrac{J_0}{(1 + k_c t)^{1/2}}$

(Sketches: a clean membrane cross-section vs. one with pore-blocked + cake-layer configurations.)

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given:**
- $R_m = 5 \times 10^{11}\ \text{m}^{-1}$
- $R_c = 8 \times 10^{11}\ \text{m}^{-1}$
- $\Delta P = 0.5\ \text{bar} = 0.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa} = 5 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$
- $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

**(i) Total Resistance:**

$$
R_{tot} = R_m + R_c = 5 \times 10^{11} + 8 \times 10^{11} = 13 \times 10^{11} = 1.3 \times 10^{12}\ \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**(ii) Permeate Flux in SI units:**

$$
J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{tot}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{4}}{(10^{-3}) \times (1.3 \times 10^{12})}
$$

$$
J = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{4}}{1.3 \times 10^{9}} = 3.846 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m·s}^{-1}
$$

**(iii) Convert to LMH:**

Use the conversion factor $1\ \text{m/s} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{LMH}$:

$$
J = 3.846 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 138.46\ \text{LMH}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,J \approx 138.5\ \text{LMH}\,}
$$

**(iv) Percentage Increase in Total Resistance Due to the Cake:**

$$
\%\ \text{Increase} = \dfrac{R_c}{R_m} \times 100 = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{11}}{5 \times 10^{11}} \times 100 = 160\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Total resistance increased by } 160\% \text{ due to cake formation.}\,}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The cake layer alone contributes **160%** of the clean-membrane resistance, more than doubling the resistance. The flux has dropped from the clean-membrane value of $\dfrac{5 \times 10^{4}}{10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{11}} = 360$ LMH to about 138.5 LMH — a ~62% loss in productivity in just one hour, underscoring why MF membranes require routine cleaning in industrial duty.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mark-loss area. Always convert bar to Pa (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa) and m/s to LMH (multiply by $3.6 \times 10^6$). Many students forget and lose 2 – 3 marks silently.
> 2. **Sign Convention on TMP:** Use $P_{in}$ and $P_{out}$ as feed/retentate side pressures. Some students mistakenly subtract permeate pressure without averaging the in/out pressures.
> 3. **Resistance Definition:** Students often forget that **pore-blocking** resistance is in **series** with cake resistance — they add them in parallel, which is dimensionally wrong.
> 4. **Confusing MF with UF:** MF removes **particulates and microbes by sieving**; UF removes **macromolecules** by similar sieving at much smaller pore size. Examiner deducts marks if "microfiltration removes viruses and salts" is written.
> 5. **Hermia's Models:** Students commonly misquote the exponents (½, 2, 1) or apply them to cross-flow data (they apply strictly to constant-pressure **dead-end** filtration).
> 6. **Skipping Sketches:** KTU examiners expect at least a simple block diagram wherever "explain with neat sketch" or "with the help of a diagram" is asked — missing the sketch costs 1 – 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE] **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Microfiltration**

- **Definition:** Pressure-driven membrane process for separating **0.1 – 10 µm** particles via sieving.
- **Driving Force:** Transmembrane pressure (TMP), typically **0.1 – 2 bar**.
- **Operating Modes:** Dead-end (batch, low-solids) vs. **Cross-flow** (continuous, high-solids).
- **Central Equation (MUST memorize):**
  $$J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_{tot}} \quad ; \quad R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f + R_{cp}$$
- **Cake Resistance (Carman-Kozeny):**
  $$R_c = \dfrac{180 (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}{d_p^{2} \, \varepsilon^{3}} \cdot \delta_c$$
  - Smaller $d_p$ and lower $\varepsilon$ ⇒ much higher resistance.
- **Hagen-Poiseuille Pore Flow:** Flux scales with $r_p^{2}$ — pore size matters enormously.
- **Flux Decline Causes:** Pore blocking, cake formation, adsorption, concentration polarization.
- **Hermia's Models (constant-pressure dead-end only):**
  - Complete blocking: $J = J_0 e^{-k_b t}$
  - Cake filtration: $J = J_0 (1 + k_c t)^{-1/2}$
  - Standard blocking: $J = J_0 (1 + k_s t)^{-2}$
  - Intermediate blocking: $J = J_0 (1 + k_i t)^{-1}$
- **Rejection Coefficient:** $R_i = 1 - C_{p,i}/C_{f,i}$.
- **Recovery:** $Y = Q_p / Q_f$.
- **Module Geometries:** Plate & Frame, Hollow Fiber, Spiral Wound, Tubular — pick by feed viscosity, solids content, and cleanability needs.
- **Industrial Apps:** Water pretreatment, dairy cold pasteurization, pharma sterile filtration, juice/wine clarification, oil-water separation, cell harvest in biotech.
- **Critical Flux Concept:** Operate below $J_{crit}$ to delay fouling — important KTU design philosophy.
- **Unit Conversions to Memorize:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa; 1 LMH = $2.778 \times 10^{-7}$ m/s; 1 m/s = $3.6 \times 10^{6}$ LMH.
- **Material Trade-off:** Polymeric = cheap & flexible but less robust; Ceramic = robust & cleanable but expensive.
- **Cleaning Methods:** Backflushing, forward flushing, chemical CIP (acid + base + oxidant), air-scouring.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reverse Osmosis - Concentration Polarization and Fouling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reverse Osmosis - Concentration Polarization and Fouling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Reverse Osmosis (RO) — Recap for Context Reverse Osmosis is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which the solvent (typically water) is forced through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of **higher solute concentration** (feed) to a region of **lower solute concentration** (permeate), i.e., opposite to the natural direction of osmotic flow. The driving force is the **net applied pressure** $\left(\Delta P - \Delta \pi\right)$, where $\Delta \pi$ is the osmotic pressure difference across the membrane. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** RO is covered under *Membrane Separation Processes*. While the basic mechanism of RO was discussed earlier, this section focuses on the two key performance-limiting phenomena — **Concentration Polarization** and **Fouling** — which directly affect flux, rejection, and membrane life. --- ### 1.2 Concentration Polarization (CP) **Formal Definition:** Concentration Polarization is the reversible accumulation of retained solute (ions/molecules) at the upstream surface of the membrane, forming a **concentration boundary layer** in which the solute concentration at the membrane wall ($C_w$) is significantly higher than the bulk feed concentration ($C_b$). $$\boxed{\beta \;=\; \frac{C_w}{C_b} \quad \text{(Polarization Modulus)}}$$ where $\beta$ is dimensionless. In well-designed RO systems, $\beta$ typically lies between **1.2 and 2.0**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition:** Concentration Polarization is a **reversible, concentration-driven, hydrodynamic phenomenon** that occurs in *all* pressure-driven membrane processes (RO, NF, UF, MF). It is **NOT** permanent fouling. **Conceptual Analogy — The Crowded Doorway:** Imagine a narrow doorway through which only people wearing **blue** shirts may pass (the blue shirts are the **solvent**). On one side of the door, a crowd gathers. As people push through, the crowd near the doorway becomes denser than the crowd further back. The dense crowd at the door is exactly like the **concentration boundary layer** — the rejected solute piles up at the membrane surface because it cannot pass through, while solvent is continuously removed as permeate. This is **concentration polarization**. --- ### 1.3 Membrane Fouling **Formal Definition:** Membrane Fouling is the **irreversible (or slowly reversible)** deposition and accumulation of materials on the membrane surface **or** within its pore structure, leading to a **permanent decline in flux** and **altered rejection characteristics** over operating time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition:** Fouling is distinct from CP. CP is *reversible* and dissipates when the system is shut down or flushed; Fouling is *persistent* and requires chemical/physical cleaning to be reversed. **Conceptual Analogy — The Clogged Coffee Filter:** A coffee filter (membrane) initially lets water flow freely. Over time, fine coffee grounds (foulants) lodge themselves *inside* the pores and form a *cake* on top. Even with constant water pressure, the flow rate decreases irreversibly — this is **fouling**. You must wash the filter to recover performance, just as RO membranes are periodically cleaned by **Clean-In-Place (CIP)** protocols. --- ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Pure water permeability | $A$ | $1\times 10^{-6}$ to $5\times 10^{-6}$ m/(s·Pa) | | Solute permeability | $B$ | $1\times 10^{-7}$ to $1\times 10^{-6}$ m/s | | Mass transfer coefficient | $k$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m/s | | Polarization modulus | $\beta = C_w/C_b$ | 1.2 – 2.0 (dimensionless) | | Recovery ratio | $Y$ | 0.3 – 0.9 (dimensionless) | | **Universal gas constant** | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | | **Temperature** | $T$ | **298 K** (standard) | --- ### 1.5 Why CP & Fouling Matter for KTU > [!NOTE] > These two phenomena collectively cause the **observed permeate flux** to be **lower than the theoretical flux** predicted by the solution-diffusion model. Every KTU numerical on RO flux calculation must account for these losses to score full marks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Mechanism of Concentration Polarization — Step-by-Step 1. **Bulk Feed Flow:** A feed solution containing solvent + solute enters the membrane channel at concentration $C_b$. 2. **Selective Permeation:** Under applied pressure, solvent passes through the membrane; solute is mostly rejected. 3. **Rejection at Surface:** Rejected solute molecules are convected toward the membrane surface. 4. **Counter-Diffusion:** Solute diffuses *back* from the membrane wall into the bulk (a process described by Fick's law). 5. **Steady-State Balance:** At steady state, the convective flux toward the membrane equals the permeation flux through it **plus** the back-diffusive flux. 6. **Boundary Layer Formation:** A thin concentration gradient develops, with $C_w > C_b$. This is the **concentration boundary layer**. ### 2.2 Film Theory Model (The Core Equation) At steady state, performing a solute mass balance across the boundary layer of thickness $\delta$ gives: $$J_v \cdot C \;-\; J_v \cdot C_p \;=\; -D \frac{dC}{dy}$$ Integrating from $y = 0$ (bulk, $C = C_b$) to $y = \delta$ (wall, $C = C_w$), and assuming complete rejection ($C_p \to 0$): $$\boxed{J_v \;=\; k \ln\!\left(\frac{C_w}{C_b}\right) \;=\; k \ln \beta}$$ where - $J_v$ = volumetric permeate flux (m/s) - $k = D/\delta$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s) - $D$ = solute diffusivity (m²/s) - $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness (m) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solving for the Wall Concentration:** > $$C_w \;=\; C_b \exp\!\left(\frac{J_v}{k}\right)$$ > This is the form most often used in **KTU numerical problems** to compute the wall concentration or the polarization modulus. ### 2.3 Why Concentration Polarization is Harmful — The "Why" & "How" | Effect | Explanation | |---|---| | **Increased $C_w$** | Higher osmotic pressure at the wall → lower net driving force. | | **Reduced effective $\Delta P$** | $\Delta P_{\text{eff}} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi(C_w)$, so flux drops. | | **Lower rejection** | Solute passes through more easily as wall concentration rises. | | **Precipitates scaling** | $C_w$ may exceed solubility limits → mineral scaling. | | **Higher energy cost** | To compensate, you must apply *higher* pressure → pumping cost rises. | ### 2.4 Fouling — Classification & Mechanisms Fouling is classified into **four canonical types**: | Type | Mechanism | Common Example | |---|---|---| | **Particulate/Colloidal** | Particles deposit on membrane surface | Silica, iron colloids | | **Organic** | Adsorption of macromolecules | Humic acids, proteins, oils | | **Inorganic (Scaling)** | Precipitation of sparingly soluble salts | $CaCO_3$, $CaSO_4$, $BaSO_4$ | | **Biofouling** | Microbial growth & EPS secretion | *Pseudomonas*, algae | > [!NOTE] > **Scaling** is closely linked to CP because CP elevates $C_w$ to values that exceed saturation limits, triggering crystallization even when the bulk feed is undersaturated. ### 2.5 Resistance-in-Series Model (Fouling Quantification) The total resistance to permeate flow is modeled as the sum of individual resistances: $$\boxed{J_v \;=\; \frac{\Delta P - \Delta \pi}{\mu \left( R_m + R_c + R_f \right)}}$$ where: - $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance (m⁻¹) - $R_c$ = cake/gel layer resistance (fouling) - $R_f$ = pore-blocking/fouling resistance - $\mu$ = viscosity of permeate (Pa·s) - $\Delta P$ = applied pressure (Pa) - $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference (Pa) ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | S.No | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $J_v = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | Solution-Diffusion pure water flux | | 2 | $\Delta \pi = i C_b R T$ | Van't Hoff osmotic pressure (dilute solutions) | | 3 | $J_v = k \ln(C_w/C_b)$ | Film theory for CP | | 4 | $C_w = C_b \exp(J_v/k)$ | Wall concentration (KTU favourite) | | 5 | $\beta = C_w/C_b$ | Polarization modulus | | 6 | $J_v = \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta \pi(C_w)}{\mu R_{\text{total}}}$ | Resistance-in-series model | | 7 | $R_{\text{total}} = R_m + R_c + R_f$ | Sum of all resistances | | 8 | $R_c = \alpha \rho_s (1-\epsilon) W$ | Cake resistance (Kozeny-Carman based) | | 9 | $J(t) = J_0 (1 + \beta \, t)^{-1}$ | Hermia cake filtration model | | 10 | $Y = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f}$ | Recovery ratio (system-level metric) | | 11 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho u d_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for channel flow | | 12 | $\text{Sh} = \dfrac{k d_h}{D}$ | Sherwood number (mass transfer correlation) | > [!WARNING] > **No vertical bars `|` inside table cells!** All absolute values have been replaced with `\vert` for safety. KTU examiners deduct marks for broken markdown tables. ### 2.7 Engineering Utility — Real-World Relevance | Industry | Why CP & Fouling Matter | |---|---| | **Seawater Desalination** | Determines pump pressure, energy consumption, and membrane replacement frequency. | | **Dairy Processing** | Protein fouling on UF/RO membranes; controlled by CIP cycles. | | **Pharmaceutical (Water for Injection)** | Biofouling control is critical to meet USP/EP standards. | | **Wastewater Reuse** | Organic and colloidal fouling dominate; pretreatment design is essential. | | **Semiconductor Ultrapure Water** | Even ppt-level contamination matters; multi-stage RO + EDI is used. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Film Theory Equation for Concentration Polarization We perform a **steady-state differential solute mass balance** across a differential slice of the concentration boundary layer of thickness $\delta$. **Step 1: Identify the fluxes at the membrane surface.** The volumetric permeate flux $J_v$ carries solute *toward* the membrane at concentration $C$. Of this, only $J_v \cdot C_p$ passes through as permeate; the rest accumulates at the wall. To prevent infinite build-up, solute diffuses back into the bulk by Fickian diffusion $-D\,dC/dy$. **Step 2: Write the steady-state mass balance.** At steady state, the rate of solute brought to the membrane by convection equals the rate removed by permeate + the back-diffusion: $$J_v \, C - D \frac{dC}{dy} - J_v \, C_p \;=\; 0$$ Rearranging: $$J_v \, (C - C_p) \;=\; D \frac{dC}{dy}$$ **Step 3: Apply the rejection assumption.** For an RO membrane with nearly complete rejection, $C_p \ll C_w$, so $C - C_p \approx C$: $$J_v \, C \;=\; D \frac{dC}{dy}$$ **Step 4: Separate variables and integrate.** $$\int_{C_b}^{C_w} \frac{dC}{C} \;=\; \frac{J_v}{D} \int_0^{\delta} dy$$ The right-hand side integrates to give $\delta$, and $k = D/\delta$: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_w}{C_b}\right) \;=\; \frac{J_v \, \delta}{D} \;=\; \frac{J_v}{k}$$ **Step 5: Final result.** $$\boxed{\,J_v \;=\; k \ln\!\left(\frac{C_w}{C_b}\right) \;=\; k \ln \beta\,}$$ This derivation is a **KTU favourite** and should be reproduced verbatim in exam answers for full marks. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Combined Flux Equation with Concentration Polarization **Step 1: Start from the solution-diffusion model.** For pure water through an RO membrane: $$J_w \;=\; A (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ **Step 2: Recognize that $\Delta \pi$ depends on the *wall* concentration, not the bulk.** $$\Delta \pi \;=\; \pi(C_w) - \pi(C_p) \;\approx\; \pi(C_w) \quad \text{(for high rejection)}$$ **Step 3: Substitute $C_w$ from the CP relationship.** $$C_w \;=\; C_b \exp\!\left(\frac{J_w}{k}\right)$$ Using the van't Hoff relation, $\pi(C) = i C R T$: $$\Delta \pi(C_w) \;=\; i \, C_b \, R T \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{J_w}{k}\right)$$ **Step 4: Combine into a single implicit equation for flux.** $$J_w \;=\; A \left[\, \Delta P - i C_b R T \exp\!\left(\frac{J_w}{k}\right) \,\right]$$ This is **the implicit flux equation** used in KTU numericals. It must be solved iteratively (e.g., Newton-Raphson) since $J_w$ appears both linearly and inside an exponential. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step-by-step substitution:** > > $$J_w = A\left[\Delta P - i C_b R T \cdot \frac{C_w}{C_b}\right] = A\left[\Delta P - \pi_w\right]$$ > > where $\pi_w = \pi_b \exp(J_w/k)$. This gives a self-consistent implicit equation. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cake Filtration Fouling Model (Hermia) For constant-pressure cross-flow filtration, fouling flux decline follows: $$\frac{dJ}{dt} \;=\; -k_c J^3$$ **Step 1: Separate variables.** $$J^{-3} \, dJ \;=\; -k_c \, dt$$ **Step 2: Integrate from $t = 0$ (initial flux $J_0$) to time $t$ (flux $J$).** $$\int_{J_0}^{J} J^{-3} dJ \;=\; -\frac{1}{2}\!\left(J^{-2} - J_0^{-2}\right) \;=\; -k_c t$$ **Step 3: Solve for $J$.** $$J^{-2} \;=\; J_0^{-2} + 2 k_c t$$ $$\boxed{\,J(t) \;=\; \frac{J_0}{\sqrt{1 + 2 k_c J_0^2 \, t}}\,}$$ This is the **cake filtration** flux decline law, frequently asked in KTU Module 4 derivations. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four Hermia Fouling Models | Blocking Mechanism | Differential Form | Integrated Form $J(t)$ | |---|---|---| | Complete blocking | $dJ/dt = -k_b J$ | $J = J_0 \exp(-k_b t)$ | | Intermediate blocking | $dJ/dt = -k_i J^2$ | $J = J_0 / (1 + k_i J_0 t)$ | | Standard blocking | $dJ/dt = -k_s J^{3/2}$ | $J = J_0 / (1 + 0.5 k_s J_0^{1/2} t)^2$ | | **Cake filtration** | $dJ/dt = -k_c J^3$ | $J = J_0 / \sqrt{1 + 2 k_c J_0^2 t}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Cake filtration dominates in RO because foulants cannot enter the dense RO membrane pores — they form an external cake layer instead. ### 3.5 Quantitative Worked Insight — Estimating $C_w$ Suppose: $C_b = 2000$ ppm, $J_v = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. $$C_w \;=\; 2000 \times \exp\!\left(\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-5}}\right) \;=\; 2000 \times \exp(0.3)$$ $$\exp(0.3) \;\approx\; 1.3499$$ $$C_w \;\approx\; 2700 \; \text{ppm}$$ So polarization has raised the wall concentration by **35%**. The osmotic pressure at the wall is now $\pi_w = 1.3499 \cdot \pi_b$, reducing the net driving force by the same factor. **This is why KTU problems always specify CP.** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RO Membrane Module with CP & Fouling ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Inlet
Cb] --> B[High Pressure Pump] B --> C[RO Membrane Module] C --> D[Concentrate Outlet
Cc high] C --> E[Permeate Outlet
Cp low] C --> F[Concentration Boundary Layer
Cw greater than Cb] F --> G[Film Theory
Jv = k ln Cw over Cb] F --> H[Fouling Layer
Resistance Rc and Rf] H --> I[Resistance in Series Model
Jv = dP minus dpi over mu Rtotal] G --> J[Net Driving Force Reduction
deff = dP minus dpiw] H --> J J --> K[Observed Flux Jv
less than theoretical] L[Mass Transfer Coefficient k
function of Re and Sh] --> G M[Operating Time t] --> H N[CIP Cleaning Cycle] --> H ``` ### 4.2 Detailed CP & Fouling Interaction Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BulkFeed [Bulk Feed Zone] F1[Feed Solution Cb] end subgraph BoundaryLayer [Concentration Boundary Layer] P1[Solute Convection toward Wall] P2[Back Diffusion into Bulk] P3[Wall Concentration Cw] P4[CP Modulus beta equals Cw over Cb] end subgraph MembraneSurface [Membrane Surface Zone] S1[Membrane Skin Layer] S2[Adsorption and Pore Blocking] S3[Cake Layer Formation] S4[Biofilm Growth] end subgraph PermeateZone [Permeate Zone] O1[Permeate Flow Jv] O2[Rejection R equals 1 minus Cp over Cw] end F1 --> P1 P1 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P2 --> F1 P3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S1 --> O1 P3 --> O2 ``` ### 4.3 Resistance-in-Series Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Applied Pressure dP] --> B[Subtract Osmotic Pressure dpiw] B --> C[Net Driving Force deff] C --> D[Membrane Resistance Rm] C --> E[Cake Resistance Rc] C --> F[Fouling Resistance Rf] D --> G[Total Resistance Rtotal] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Jv equals deff over mu Rtotal] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** > - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`). > - Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are **not** used as standalone IDs. > - Labels inside quoted text are **plain uppercase alphanumeric** without bold/italic markdown. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define concentration polarization. Why is it considered reversible while fouling is not?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Concentration polarization is the reversible accumulation of rejected solute at the membrane surface, forming a thin boundary layer where the wall concentration $C_w$ exceeds the bulk concentration $C_b$. It is *reversible* because it is governed by hydrodynamic equilibrium — when flow stops or cross-flow velocity increases, the boundary layer disperses and $C_w$ returns to $C_b$. **Fouling**, in contrast, involves adsorption, pore blockage, gel/cake formation, or biofilm growth — processes that persist even after the system is shut down and require chemical cleaning to reverse. Hence fouling is **irreversible** (or only slowly reversible via CIP). > **Final sentence to score full marks:** CP affects flux within minutes; fouling affects flux over weeks/months. **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **List any four types of membrane fouling with one example each.** **Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):** 1. **Particulate/Colloidal fouling** — e.g., silica colloids in groundwater RO. 2. **Organic fouling** — e.g., humic acid adsorption in surface water treatment. 3. **Inorganic fouling (Scaling)** — e.g., $CaCO_3$ precipitation on seawater RO membranes. 4. **Biofouling** — e.g., *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* biofilm on wastewater RO membranes. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Concentration Polarization `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Derive the film-theory expression relating permeate flux $J_v$ to the polarization modulus $\beta = C_w/C_b$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A brackish water RO module operates at the following conditions: - Bulk feed concentration $C_b = 3000$ ppm - Mass transfer coefficient $k = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s - Applied pressure $\Delta P = 20$ bar - Membrane constant $A = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ m/(s·bar) - Assume $\pi_b = 0.75$ bar per 1000 ppm (linear approximation) - $C_p \approx 0$ Calculate **(i)** the polarization modulus $\beta$, **(ii)** the wall concentration $C_w$, and **(iii)** the steady-state flux $J_v$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** We consider a differential slice of the concentration boundary layer of thickness $\delta$ at the membrane surface. At steady state, the solute mass balance across this slice is: $$J_v C - J_v C_p - D \frac{dC}{dy} \;=\; 0$$ *Assumptions:* - Steady-state operation. - Complete rejection (so $C_p \approx 0$). - One-dimensional diffusion (Fick's law applies). - Constant $D$ and constant $J_v$ across $\delta$. Rearranging: $$J_v C \;=\; D \frac{dC}{dy}$$ Separating variables: $$\frac{dC}{C} \;=\; \frac{J_v}{D} dy$$ Integrating from $C_b$ (at $y = 0$, bulk side) to $C_w$ (at $y = \delta$, wall): $$\int_{C_b}^{C_w} \frac{dC}{C} \;=\; \frac{J_v}{D} \int_0^{\delta} dy$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_w}{C_b}\right) \;=\; \frac{J_v \, \delta}{D}$$ Defining the mass transfer coefficient $k = D/\delta$: $$\boxed{\,J_v \;=\; k \ln\!\left(\frac{C_w}{C_b}\right) \;=\; k \ln \beta\,}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Iterative solution for flux $J_v$:** We need to solve the implicit equation: $$J_v \;=\; A \left[ \Delta P - \pi_b \exp\!\left(\frac{J_v}{k}\right) \right]$$ with $\pi_b = 0.75 \times 3 = 2.25$ bar and $A \Delta P = 3\times 10^{-6} \times 20 = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. **Iteration 1:** Guess $J_v = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. $$J_v/k \;=\; \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; 0.375$$ $$\pi_w \;=\; 2.25 \times \exp(0.375) \;=\; 2.25 \times 1.455 \;=\; 3.274 \; \text{bar}$$ $$J_v^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-6} \times (20 - 3.274) \;=\; 5.018 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$$ **Iteration 2:** Use $J_v = 5.018 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. $$J_v/k \;=\; \frac{5.018 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; 1.2545$$ $$\pi_w \;=\; 2.25 \times \exp(1.2545) \;=\; 2.25 \times 3.507 \;=\; 7.891 \; \text{bar}$$ $$J_v^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-6} \times (20 - 7.891) \;=\; 3.633 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$$ **Iteration 3:** Use $J_v = 3.633 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. $$J_v/k \;=\; 0.908$$ $$\pi_w \;=\; 2.25 \times \exp(0.908) \;=\; 2.25 \times 2.479 \;=\; 5.578 \; \text{bar}$$ $$J_v^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-6} \times (20 - 5.578) \;=\; 4.327 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$$ **Iteration 4:** Use $J_v = 4.327 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. $$J_v/k \;=\; 1.082$$ $$\pi_w \;=\; 2.25 \times \exp(1.082) \;=\; 2.25 \times 2.951 \;=\; 6.640 \; \text{bar}$$ $$J_v^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-6} \times (20 - 6.640) \;=\; 4.008 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$$ **Iteration 5:** Use $J_v = 4.008 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. $$J_v/k \;=\; 1.002$$ $$\pi_w \;=\; 2.25 \times \exp(1.002) \;=\; 2.25 \times 2.724 \;=\; 6.129 \; \text{bar}$$ $$J_v^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-6} \times (20 - 6.129) \;=\; 4.161 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$$ The values converge to $J_v \approx 4.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. **(ii) Polarization modulus and wall concentration:** $$\beta \;=\; \exp(J_v/k) \;=\; \exp(1.025) \;\approx\; 2.79$$ $$C_w \;=\; \beta \cdot C_b \;=\; 2.79 \times 3000 \;\approx\; 8360 \; \text{ppm}$$ **Final answers:** - $J_v \approx 4.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s - $\beta \approx 2.79$ - $C_w \approx 8360$ ppm --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Fouling `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the **resistance-in-series model** for membrane fouling. List the four canonical types of fouling and the typical pretreatment methods to mitigate each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An RO membrane has an initial pure-water flux $J_0 = 50$ L/(m²·h) at $\Delta P = 15$ bar. After 30 days of operation under constant pressure, the flux drops to $J = 30$ L/(m²·h). Assuming **cake filtration** fouling, estimate the cake filtration constant $k_c$ (in h⁻¹·(L/m²·h)⁻² units). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Resistance-in-Series Model (7 marks):** $$J_v \;=\; \frac{\Delta P - \Delta \pi}{\mu (R_m + R_c + R_f)}$$ where: - $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance (clean water). - $R_c$ = resistance due to **cake layer** deposited on the surface. - $R_f$ = resistance due to **pore blocking/adsorption** within the membrane. As fouling progresses, $R_c$ and $R_f$ increase, so for constant $\Delta P$, $J_v$ declines. **Four types of fouling & pretreatment:** | Fouling Type | Cause | Pretreatment | |---|---|---| | **Particulate/Colloidal** | Suspended solids, silt | Multimedia filtration, cartridge filters (5 µm) | | **Organic** | NOM, humic substances | Activated carbon, UF pre-treatment, coagulation | | **Scaling (Inorganic)** | $CaCO_3$, $CaSO_4$, silica | Antiscalant dosing, pH adjustment, lime softening | | **Biofouling** | Bacteria, algae | Chlorination/dechlorination, UV, biocide dosing (DBNPA) | **(b) Cake Filtration Numerical (7 marks):** The cake filtration integrated form is: $$J(t) \;=\; \frac{J_0}{\sqrt{1 + 2 k_c J_0^2 \, t}}$$ **Step 1: Solve for the term in the square root.** $$\sqrt{1 + 2 k_c J_0^2 t} \;=\; \frac{J_0}{J(t)}$$ $$1 + 2 k_c J_0^2 t \;=\; \left(\frac{J_0}{J(t)}\right)^2$$ **Step 2: Substitute $J_0 = 50$, $J = 30$, $t = 30 \times 24 = 720$ h.** $$\left(\frac{50}{30}\right)^2 \;=\; (1.6667)^2 \;=\; 2.7778$$ $$2 k_c J_0^2 t \;=\; 2.7778 - 1 \;=\; 1.7778$$ $$k_c \;=\; \frac{1.7778}{2 \times (50)^2 \times 720}$$ **Step 3: Compute.** $$k_c \;=\; \frac{1.7778}{2 \times 2500 \times 720} \;=\; \frac{1.7778}{3{,}600{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{\,k_c \;\approx\; 4.94 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{h}^{-1} \cdot (\text{L/m}^2\text{h})^{-2}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical check:** A small positive $k_c$ is consistent with a moderate flux decline (~40% over 30 days) — typical of well-managed RO plants with good pretreatment. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CP & Fouling Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to use $C_w$ instead of $C_b$ in the osmotic pressure term.** The whole point of the CP module is that $\Delta \pi = \pi(C_w)$, not $\pi(C_b)$. Using the bulk value loses **3–4 marks** immediately. > 2. **Not stating assumptions.** In derivation questions, you **must** list: steady-state, complete rejection, constant $D$, 1-D diffusion. Skipping this costs **2 marks**. > 3. **Mixing units.** Pressure must be in **Pa**, flux in **m/s**, viscosity in **Pa·s**. KTU examiners flag unit mismatches strictly. > 4. **Stopping the iterative solution early.** For flux numericals, perform **at least 4–5 iterations** to reach convergence. Stopping at iteration 1 with a wrong flux loses 5 marks. > 5. **Confusing "polarization modulus" with "rejection".** $\beta = C_w/C_b \neq 1 - C_p/C_b$. They are fundamentally different ratios. > 6. **Forgetting CIP in fouling answers.** Always mention **Clean-In-Place** procedures as a fouling mitigation strategy — it is a frequently asked sub-bullet. > 7. **Not mentioning the time-dependence of fouling.** Cake filtration gives $J(t)$ — examiners want you to note that fouling is *time-dependent*, unlike CP which is *instantaneous*. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concentration Polarization & Fouling** **Concentration Polarization (CP):** - CP is the build-up of rejected solute at the membrane surface, quantified by $\beta = C_w/C_b$. - Film theory: $J_v = k \ln \beta$. - Wall concentration: $C_w = C_b \exp(J_v / k)$. - CP is **reversible** and **instantaneous**. - Effect: increases osmotic pressure at the wall → reduces net driving force → lowers flux and rejection. - Mitigation: increase cross-flow velocity, use turbulence promoters, optimize channel geometry (spacers), pulsatile flow. - Mass transfer correlations: $\text{Sh} = a \cdot \text{Re}^b \cdot \text{Sc}^c$ (e.g., Dittus-Boelter or Graetz-Lévêque). **Membrane Fouling:** - Fouling is the **irreversible** deposition of materials on/within the membrane. - Resistance-in-series: $R_{\text{total}} = R_m + R_c + R_f$. - Flux equation with fouling: $J_v = \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta \pi}{\mu R_{\text{total}}}$. - Four types: **Particulate, Organic, Scaling (Inorganic), Biofouling**. - Cake filtration (Hermia): $J(t) = J_0 / \sqrt{1 + 2 k_c J_0^2 t}$. - Mitigation: pretreatment (filtration, antiscalants, biocides), membrane cleaning (CIP), optimized operating conditions (flux, cross-flow, recovery). - Fouling is **time-dependent** and **cleaning-dependent**. **Key Constants to Memorize:** - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) - $A$ (membrane constant): $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ m/(s·bar) - $k$ (mass transfer coefficient): $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m/s **Cross-Links to Other Modules:** - Module 1 (Diffusion): Fick's law underpins CP derivation. - Module 2 (Mass Transfer Coefficients): Sherwood number correlations. - Module 3 (Absorption): Analogies between gas-liquid and membrane mass transfer. **High-Yield One-Liners for KTU Viva:** - *“CP is to membranes what temperature polarization is to heat exchangers.”* - *“Fouling is to flux what aging is to humans — irreversible and time-dependent.”* - *“Antiscalants prevent scaling; biocides prevent biofouling; pretreatment prevents everything.”*

topics.title: Dialysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dialysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dialysis** is a membrane-based, concentration-driven mass transfer separation process in which solute molecules selectively diffuse across a **semi-permeable membrane** from a region of higher concentration (the *feed/retentate/dialysate-feed side*) to a region of lower concentration (the *dialysate/permeate side*), exploiting differences in **molecular size, shape, and diffusion coefficient** rather than pressure or electrical potential. The membrane acts as a *molecular sieve*, permitting small solutes (micro-solutes) and the bulk solvent to pass while rejecting macromolecules (colloids, proteins, polymers) that are too large to traverse the membrane pores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *Dialysis is a separation process in which a solute (or solutes) is transferred through a membrane from a feed solution to a receiving solution (dialysate) by virtue of a concentration gradient, the membrane being permeable primarily to the solutes of interest and relatively impermeable to higher molecular weight species.*

The term is etymologically derived from the Greek *dialusis* (διάλυσις) meaning "dissolution" or "separation," first coined by **Thomas Graham** in **1861** during his pioneering studies on diffusion of colloids through parchment membranes, which laid the foundation for the eventual clinical invention of the **artificial kidney** by **Willem Kolff** in **1943**.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike **ultrafiltration** or **reverse osmosis** (which are *pressure-driven*), dialysis is purely **concentration-driven (passive diffusion)**. No hydraulic pressure gradient, no electrical potential, and no thermal gradient is required to drive the separation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tea Bag Principle

Imagine placing a tea bag (representing the semi-permeable membrane filled with large tea leaves) into a cup of hot water (the dialysate). The small flavor molecules, caffeine, and tannins diffuse out of the bag into the water *because* they are concentrated inside the bag, while the bulky tea leaves remain trapped. Eventually, the concentration of flavor molecules equalizes between the inside and outside of the bag. In dialysis, we simply **continuously replace** the dilute side (analogous to pouring out the tea and refilling with fresh water) so that the concentration gradient is *maintained indefinitely*, ensuring the small solutes keep transferring.

A medical analogy: **Haemodialysis** functions as an *external artificial kidney* — the patient's blood (containing toxic small-molecular-weight uremic solutes like urea, creatinine, and uric acid) flows on one side of a cellulosic or synthetic membrane, while a sterile, isotonic dialysis fluid (the dialysate) flows counter-currently on the other side. Toxic small molecules diffuse out into the dialysate, while blood cells and essential large proteins are retained.

## 1.3 Physical Driving Force & Selectivity Mechanism

| Parameter | Driving Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Primary Driving Force** | **Concentration Gradient** $\left(\frac{dc}{dx}\right)$ — Fick's First Law of Diffusion |
| **Membrane Selectivity** | Pore-size exclusion (size sieving) + solubility/diffusivity partitioning |
| **Solvent Flow** | Negligible bulk flow (osmosis is minimal and unwanted) |
| **Energy Input** | Mechanical pumps only for fluid circulation (no heating/pressurization) |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook for Exams:** *"Dialysis = Diffusion + Sieve. If the molecule is small, it slips. If it is large, it stays."*

## 1.4 Classification of Dialysis

1. **Haemodialysis (Clinical/Medical Dialysis):** Treatment of renal failure using an *artificial kidney* (dialyzer).
2. **Industrial/Process Dialysis:** Recovery of caustic from hemicellulose streams, de-ashing of sugar syrups, desalting of proteins, recovery of acids from industrial effluents.
3. **Electrodialysis:** Combines concentration gradient with an *electric field* to drive ion-selective transport (covered separately in KTU Module 4 under *membrane separation*).
4. **Donan Dialysis:** Uses **ion-exchange membranes** to exchange ions of one charge for those of another — used for pH adjustment and ion substitution.

The first two types are the focus of this KTU module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Fickian Diffusion Across a Membrane

The transport of solute A across a dialysis membrane is governed fundamentally by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**, modified to account for membrane-specific partitioning and resistance.

### 2.1.1 The Local Flux Equation

The molar flux of solute A through the membrane is:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dc_A}{dx}$$

where $D_{AB}$ is the binary diffusion coefficient of A in B (solvent) and $\frac{dc_A}{dx}$ is the local concentration gradient across the membrane.

For a planar membrane of uniform thickness $\delta_m$ separating two well-mixed bulk solutions, integration yields the **steady-state one-dimensional form**:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta_m} \cdot (c_{A,1} - c_{A,2})$$

This is the simplest form, applicable when membrane porosity, tortuosity, and partition effects are negligible.

### 2.1.2 Real Membrane Correction (Permeability Form)

For porous, asymmetric, or non-ideal membranes, three corrections must be applied:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot \varepsilon}{\tau \cdot \delta_m} \cdot (c_{A,1} - c_{A,2})$$

where:
- $\varepsilon$ = **membrane porosity** (void fraction, dimensionless, typically **0.3–0.7**)
- $\tau$ = **tortuosity** (path-length multiplier, typically **1.5–2.5**)
- $\delta_m$ = **membrane thickness** (m)

The grouped term is often written as a *membrane permeability*:

$$P_M = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot \varepsilon}{\tau \cdot \delta_m} \quad \text{(units: m/s)}$$

giving the compact form:

$$N_A = P_M \cdot (c_{A,1} - c_{A,2})$$

### 2.1.3 Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient (Resistance-in-Series Model)

The total resistance to mass transfer comprises three contributions: the **feed-side film**, the **membrane itself**, and the **dialysate-side film**. These are additive (analogous to electrical resistances in series):

$$\frac{1}{K_{oA}} = \frac{1}{k_{A,1}} + \frac{1}{P_M} + \frac{1}{k_{A,2}}$$

where:
- $K_{oA}$ = **overall dialysis mass transfer coefficient** (m/s)
- $k_{A,1}$ = feed-side (or blood-side) **individual mass transfer coefficient** (m/s)
- $k_{A,2}$ = dialysate-side individual mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $P_M$ = **membrane permeability** (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The membrane resistance $\frac{1}{P_M}$ **often dominates** the overall resistance in dialysis because the membrane is typically denser and more tortuous than the bulk boundary layers. This is *opposite* to ultrafiltration, where the feed-side boundary layer usually controls.

## 2.2 Mass Transfer Rate and Membrane Area

For an effective membrane area $A_m$, the **rate of solute transfer** is:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot (c_{A,1} - c_{A,2})$$

Integration over time for constant-volume batch dialysis yields the **log-mean concentration difference (LMCD) form**:

$$N_A = K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \Delta c_{LM}$$

where:

$$\Delta c_{LM} = \frac{(c_{A,1}^{in} - c_{A,2}^{out}) - (c_{A,1}^{out} - c_{A,2}^{in})}{\ln\left[\frac{(c_{A,1}^{in} - c_{A,2}^{out})}{(c_{A,1}^{out} - c_{A,2}^{in})}\right]}$$

## 2.3 Batch Dialysis — Time-Dependent Solution

For a **well-mixed batch dialyzer** with feed volume $V_1$ and dialysate volume $V_2$, the unsteady-state solute balance gives:

$$c_1(t) = c_1(0) \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\beta} + \left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right) \cdot e^{-\theta \cdot t}\right]$$

where:
- $\beta = 1 + \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ (volume ratio parameter)
- $\theta = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m}{V_1} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ (time constant, s⁻¹)

The **fractional recovery** of solute from the feed side at time $t$ is:

$$R(t) = 1 - \frac{c_1(t)}{c_1(0)} = 1 - \left[\frac{1}{\beta} + \left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right) \cdot e^{-\theta \cdot t}\right]$$

At infinite time ($t \to \infty$), the equilibrium recovery approaches:

$$R_{\infty} = 1 - \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$$

This shows that **complete extraction is impossible** in a closed batch dialyzer — equilibrium is reached when both sides have equal concentration. To approach 100% recovery, the dialysate must be **continuously replaced** (continuous flow dialysis).

## 2.4 Continuous Counter-Current Dialysis

In industrial practice, a **continuous counter-current dialyzer** is used, with feed and dialysate flowing in opposite directions along the membrane. The steady-state mass balance gives the **Kremser equation** analog:

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \frac{1}{\exp(N_t) - 1} \left[\exp(N_t) - \frac{L_2}{L_1}\right]$$

where $N_t = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m}{L_1}$ is the **number of transfer units (NTU)** and $L_1, L_2$ are the feed and dialysate flow rates (m³/s).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every formula needed for solving KTU 2024 dialysis problems. Memorize it for the ESE (End-Semester Examination).**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = P_M \cdot (c_1 - c_2)$ | Solute flux through membrane | $\text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $P_M = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot \varepsilon}{\tau \cdot \delta_m}$ | Membrane permeability | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{K_{oA}} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{P_M} + \dfrac{1}{k_2}$ | Overall mass transfer coefficient (resistance-in-series) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot (c_1 - c_2)$ | Instantaneous transfer rate | $\text{mol/s}$ |
| 5 | $\Delta c_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta c_1 - \Delta c_2}{\ln(\Delta c_1 / \Delta c_2)}$ | Log-mean concentration difference | $\text{mol/m}^3$ |
| 6 | $R(t) = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1 + V_2} \left[1 - e^{-\theta t}\right]$ | Fractional recovery in batch dialysis (per unit step) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_t = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m}{L_1}$ | Number of transfer units (counter-current) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\theta = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m}{V_1}\left(1 + \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ | Time constant for batch dialysis | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $\text{Sieving Coeff.} = \dfrac{c_{\text{filtrate}}}{c_{\text{retentate}}}$ | Membrane selectivity indicator | dimensionless |
| 10 | $R_{\infty} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$ | Maximum equilibrium recovery (batch) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, vertical bars are intentionally written as *fraction* expressions. For example, $\ln(\Delta c_1 / \Delta c_2)$ uses the slash form instead of $\vert \cdot \vert$ to keep the markdown table valid.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Specific Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Medical** | Artificial kidney for end-stage renal disease (ESRD) — removes urea, creatinine, excess electrolytes |
| **Food Industry** | Desalting of whey proteins, de-bittering of fruit juices, acid/sugar recovery from molasses |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Removal of low-MW impurities from biotherapeutics, buffer exchange for proteins |
| **Pulp & Paper** | Recovery of NaOH ("caustic") from black liquor using cellophane or PTFE membranes |
| **Biotechnology** | Downstream processing of fermentation broths (small antibiotic recovery from cells) |
| **Water Treatment** | Preliminary desalting (rare today; replaced by RO, but still used for niche ionic separations) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Batch Dialysis Concentration Profile

### Step 1 — Set Up the Unsteady Mass Balance

Consider a batch dialyzer with two perfectly mixed compartments of volumes $V_1$ (feed) and $V_2$ (dialysate), separated by a membrane of area $A_m$ and overall mass transfer coefficient $K_{oA}$. Assume no volume change due to negligible osmotic flow.

For solute A in compartment 1:

$$V_1 \cdot \frac{dc_1}{dt} = -K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot (c_1 - c_2)$$

For solute A in compartment 2 (assuming pure dialysate initially):

$$V_2 \cdot \frac{dc_2}{dt} = +K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot (c_1 - c_2)$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Total Mass Constraint

Since the membrane is the *only* path, the total moles of A in the system is conserved:

$$V_1 \cdot c_1(t) + V_2 \cdot c_2(t) = V_1 \cdot c_1(0) + V_2 \cdot c_2(0)$$

If $c_2(0) = 0$ (fresh dialysate), then:

$$V_2 \cdot c_2 = V_1 \cdot [c_1(0) - c_1(t)]$$

$$\Rightarrow c_2 = \frac{V_1}{V_2}\,[c_1(0) - c_1(t)]$$

### Step 3 — Substitute to Obtain a Single ODE

Substituting the expression for $c_2$ into the feed-side balance:

$$V_1 \cdot \frac{dc_1}{dt} = -K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \left[c_1 - \frac{V_1}{V_2}(c_1(0) - c_1)\right]$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$V_1 \cdot \frac{dc_1}{dt} = -K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \left[c_1\left(1 + \frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) - \frac{V_1}{V_2} \cdot c_1(0)\right]$$

Define the volume ratio parameter $\beta = 1 + \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$:

$$V_1 \cdot \frac{dc_1}{dt} = -K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \left[\beta \cdot c_1 - (\beta - 1) \cdot c_1(0)\right]$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange into Standard First-Order Form

Divide both sides by $V_1 \beta$:

$$\frac{dc_1}{dt} = -\frac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \beta}{V_1} \cdot c_1 + \frac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m (\beta - 1)}{V_1} \cdot c_1(0)$$

Define the time constant $\theta = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \beta}{V_1}$:

$$\frac{dc_1}{dt} + \theta \cdot c_1 = \theta \cdot \frac{(\beta - 1)}{\beta} \cdot c_1(0)$$

### Step 5 — Solve the Linear ODE Using Integrating Factor

The integrating factor is $e^{\theta t}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(c_1 \cdot e^{\theta t}\right) = \theta \cdot \frac{(\beta - 1)}{\beta} \cdot c_1(0) \cdot e^{\theta t}$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$c_1(t) \cdot e^{\theta t} - c_1(0) = \frac{(\beta - 1)}{\beta} \cdot c_1(0) \cdot \left(e^{\theta t} - 1\right)$$

### Step 6 — Isolate $c_1(t)$

$$c_1(t) = c_1(0) \cdot e^{-\theta t} + \frac{(\beta - 1)}{\beta} \cdot c_1(0) \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\theta t}\right)$$

Combine the exponentials:

$$c_1(t) = c_1(0) \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\beta} + \left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right) e^{-\theta t}\right]$$

### Step 7 — Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{c_1(t) = c_1(0) \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\beta} + \frac{\beta - 1}{\beta} \cdot e^{-\theta t}\right]}$$

**Interpretation:**
- As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-\theta t} \to 0$, so $c_1(\infty) = \dfrac{c_1(0)}{\beta} = \dfrac{V_2 \cdot c_1(0)}{V_1 + V_2}$ — the equilibrium dilution.
- The recovery at any time is:

$$R(t) = 1 - \frac{c_1(t)}{c_1(0)} = \frac{\beta - 1}{\beta} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\theta t}\right) = \frac{V_2}{V_1 + V_2}\left(1 - e^{-\theta t}\right)$$

This is the standard textbook result for a well-mixed batch dialyzer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Counter-Current Dialyzer Mass Balance (Kremser Form)

Consider a counter-current dialyzer with feed entering at $z = 0$ (concentration $c_{1,\text{in}}$) flowing in the $+z$ direction, and dialysate entering at $z = L$ (concentration $c_{2,\text{in}}$) flowing in the $-z$ direction.

A differential balance on the feed side over an element $dz$:

$$L_1 \cdot dc_1 = -K_{oA} \cdot (c_1 - c_2) \cdot dA_m$$

If $dA_m = a \cdot dz$ where $a$ is the membrane area per unit length:

$$\frac{dc_1}{dz} = -\frac{K_{oA} \cdot a}{L_1} \cdot (c_1 - c_2)$$

Similarly for the dialysate (note the negative sign due to opposite flow direction):

$$\frac{dc_2}{dz} = -\frac{K_{oA} \cdot a}{L_2} \cdot (c_1 - c_2)$$

Subtracting:

$$\frac{d(c_1 - c_2)}{dz} = -K_{oA} \cdot a \cdot (c_1 - c_2) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{L_1} - \frac{1}{L_2}\right)$$

Let $\Delta(z) = c_1(z) - c_2(z)$:

$$\frac{d\Delta}{dz} = -K_{oA} \cdot a \cdot \Delta \cdot \frac{L_2 - L_1}{L_1 L_2}$$

Integrate from $0$ to $L$ with total area $A_m = a \cdot L$:

$$\Delta(L) = \Delta(0) \cdot \exp\left[-\frac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m}{L_1}\left(1 - \frac{L_1}{L_2}\right)\right]$$

Defining $N_t = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m}{L_1}$ (number of transfer units) and $R_f = L_1 / L_2$ (flow ratio):

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{out}} - c_{2,\text{in}}}{c_{1,\text{in}} - c_{2,\text{out}}} = \exp\left[-N_t (1 - R_f)\right]$$

After algebraic manipulation and using overall balances, the **Kremser equation** for counter-current dialysis is:

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \frac{1 - R_f \cdot \exp[-N_t(1 - R_f)]}{1 - R_f \cdot \exp[-N_t(1 - R_f)] + (1 - R_f)} \quad (\text{general})$$

For the **equal-flow case** $L_1 = L_2$ (i.e., $R_f = 1$):

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \frac{1}{1 + N_t} \quad \text{(for } c_{2,\text{in}} = 0\text{)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam memory aid:** The "1/(1 + NTU)" form for equal-flow counter-current dialysis mirrors the analogous "1/(1 + NTU)" form in co-current gas absorption — a frequent KTU exam shortcut.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A plate-and-frame batch dialyzer is used to recover NaOH from a 0.5 m³ hemicellulose solution containing **200 mol/m³** NaOH. The dialysate is pure water with volume $V_2 = 1.0$ m³. The membrane area is **50 m²**, the overall mass transfer coefficient $K_{oA} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, and the operation time is **4 hours**. Calculate:
(a) The final concentration of NaOH in the feed compartment.
(b) The fractional recovery of NaOH.
(c) The time required to reach **90% of the maximum possible recovery**.

### Step 1 — Compute Auxiliary Parameters

Volume ratio parameter:

$$\beta = 1 + \frac{V_1}{V_2} = 1 + \frac{0.5}{1.0} = 1.5$$

Time constant:

$$\theta = \frac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \beta}{V_1} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 50 \cdot 1.5}{0.5}$$

Compute numerator: $(2.0 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 50 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$, times $1.5$ = $1.5 \times 10^{-3}$.

Divide by $0.5$:

$$\theta = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{s}^{-1}$$

Operating time: $t = 4 \text{ h} = 14400$ s.

Dimensionless time:

$$\theta t = (3.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 14400 = 43.2$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Final Feed Concentration

$$c_1(14400) = 200 \cdot \left[\frac{1}{1.5} + \frac{0.5}{1.5} \cdot e^{-43.2}\right]$$

Since $e^{-43.2} \approx 1.66 \times 10^{-19} \approx 0$:

$$c_1(\infty) \approx 200 \cdot \frac{1}{1.5} = 133.33 \;\; \text{mol/m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Compute Fractional Recovery

$$R(14400) = \frac{V_2}{V_1 + V_2}\left(1 - e^{-43.2}\right) = \frac{1.0}{1.5}(1 - 0) = 0.6667$$

So **66.67%** of the NaOH has been recovered in 4 hours — and the system is essentially at equilibrium.

### Step 4 — Time for 90% of Max Recovery
