Maximum recovery: $R_{\infty} = V_2 / (V_1 + V_2) = 0.6667$.

Target: $0.9 \cdot R_{\infty} = 0.9 \cdot 0.6667 = 0.6000$.

$$0.6000 = 0.6667 \cdot (1 - e^{-\theta t})$$

$$1 - e^{-\theta t} = 0.9 \Rightarrow e^{-\theta t} = 0.1$$

$$\theta t = \ln(10) = 2.3026$$

$$t = \frac{2.3026}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 767.5 \;\; \text{s} \approx 12.79 \;\; \text{min}$$

**Answer:** Just under 13 minutes is enough to reach 90% of the maximum batch-recovery value.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quick Comparison of Operating Modes

| Parameter | Batch Dialysis | Continuous Counter-Current | Continuous Co-Current |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass Transfer Driving Force** | Decays with time | Maintained high throughout | Decreases along length |
| **NTU Efficiency** | Time-dependent | Highest | Lowest |
| **Equipment Volume** | Largest | Smallest | Intermediate |
| **Recovery Achievable** | Limited by $V_2/(V_1+V_2)$ | Approaches 100% | Approaches 100% (slower) |
| **Capital Cost** | Lowest | Highest | Intermediate |
| **Industrial Use** | Lab, small pharma | Pulp/paper, food, biotech | Rare |
| **Mathematical Model** | Exponential decay (Eq. derived above) | Kremser equation | Kremser with opposite sign |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Continuous Counter-Current Dialyzer

The following Mermaid diagram captures the functional architecture, material flow, and control logic of a typical industrial counter-current dialyzer module. (The diagram is drawn as a *block architecture* rather than a physical drawing, per KTU protocol for non-physical schematics.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedIn["FEED INLET - HIGH CONC SOLUTION"] --> feedPump["FEED PUMP - VARIABLE SPEED"]
    feedPump --> feedHeader["FEED DISTRIBUTION HEADER - INLET MANIFOLD"]
    feedHeader --> memModuleA["MEMBRANE MODULE A - 50 M2 AREA"]
    memModuleA --> memModuleB["MEMBRANE MODULE B - 50 M2 AREA"]
    memModuleB --> memModuleC["MEMBRANE MODULE C - 50 M2 AREA"]
    memModuleC --> retentateOut["RETENTATE OUTLET - DILUTED FEED"]

    dialysateIn["DIALYSATE INLET - PURE SOLVENT"] --> dialysatePump["DIALYSATE PUMP - EQUAL FLOW"]
    dialysatePump --> dialysateHeader["DIALYSATE DISTRIBUTION HEADER"]
    dialysateHeader -. Counter-Current Flow .-> memModuleC
    memModuleC -. Counter-Current Flow .-> memModuleB
    memModuleB -. Counter-Current Flow .-> memModuleA
    memModuleA --> permeateOut["PERMEATE OUTLET - ENRICHED DIALYSATE"]

    sensorC1["CONCENTRATION SENSOR C1 - FEED SIDE"] --> controller["PID CONTROLLER"]
    sensorC2["CONCENTRATION SENSOR C2 - PERMEATE SIDE"] --> controller
    controller --> feedPump
    controller --> dialysatePump
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the Mermaid block above, **counter-current flow** is depicted using *dotted arrows* (`.->`) while the main feed flow uses *solid arrows* (`-->`). The three membrane modules A, B, C represent the conceptual stage-wise division of a single long membrane into segments for analytical modelling (the NTU / Kremser approach).

## 4.2 Membrane Module Internal Geometry (Hollow-Fibre Dialyzer)

The most common industrial and clinical dialyzer uses a **hollow-fibre configuration** — thousands of microporous capillary tubes bundled inside a cylindrical shell. The functional layout is:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph shellSpace["SHELL SIDE - DIALYSATE CHAMBER"]
        dIn["DIALYSATE IN"] --> dFlow["AXIAL FLOW"]
        dFlow --> dOut["DIALYSATE OUT - ENRICHED WITH SOLUTES"]
    end

    subgraph lumenSpace["LUMEN SIDE - FEED OR BLOOD"]
        bIn["BLOOD OR FEED IN"] --> bFlow["THROUGH FIBRE LUMENS"]
        bFlow --> bOut["BLOOD OR FEED OUT - PURIFIED"]
    end

    fibreWall["MEMBRANE WALL - POROUS THIN FILM"] -. Separates .-> shellSpace
    fibreWall -. Separates .-> lumenSpace
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Batch vs Continuous Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START - DIALYSIS OPERATION"] --> mode{OPERATING MODE}
    mode -- BATCH --> batchLoop["LOOP - FILL BOTH COMPARTMENTS"]
    batchLoop --> batchWait["WAIT FOR MASS TRANSFER"]
    batchWait --> batchCheck{"EQUILIBRIUM REACHED"}
    batchCheck -- NO --> batchWait
    batchCheck -- YES --> batchDrain["DRAIN BOTH COMPARTMENTS"]

    mode -- CONTINUOUS --> contStart["FEED PUMP STARTED"]
    contStart --> contMix["MIXER FOR DIALYSATE MAKE-UP"]
    contMix --> contModule["ENTER COUNTER-CURRENT MODULE"]
    contModule --> contSplit["SPLIT RETENTATE AND PERMEATE"]
    contSplit --> contRecycle{"RECOVERY TARGET MET"}
    contRecycle -- NO --> contModule
    contRecycle -- YES --> contEnd["END - PRODUCT COLLECTED"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Memory Trick for the Exam:** A *batch* dialyzer is like a **closed straw** with one end permeable — fluid eventually equalizes. A *continuous counter-current* dialyzer is like a **river flowing against a current** — solute is constantly carried away, so the gradient never dies.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define dialysis and state its primary driving force. How does it differ from ultrafiltration?

**Model Answer:**

Dialysis is a membrane separation process in which solute molecules diffuse from a feed solution across a semi-permeable membrane into a dialysate (receiving) solvent, driven by a **concentration gradient**. The membrane is selective on the basis of molecular size, allowing small solutes to permeate while retaining macromolecules.

**Primary driving force:** concentration gradient $\left(\dfrac{dc}{dx}\right)$, in accordance with Fick's First Law of Diffusion.

**Difference from ultrafiltration:**

| Feature | Dialysis | Ultrafiltration |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Concentration gradient | **Hydraulic pressure** |
| Solvent flow | Negligible | Significant bulk flow |
| Separation basis | Molecular size and diffusivity | Size exclusion by membrane pores |
| Application | Artificial kidney, desalting | Protein concentration, water purification |
| Membrane | Dense or microporous | Tightly microporous |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for driving force, 1 mark for differentiation)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List the three resistances that contribute to the overall mass transfer resistance in dialysis and write the resistance-in-series equation.

**Model Answer:**

The three resistances in series are:

1. **Feed-side (or blood-side) film resistance:** $\dfrac{1}{k_{A,1}}$ — controlled by the boundary layer on the upstream side of the membrane.
2. **Membrane resistance:** $\dfrac{1}{P_M}$ — controlled by the membrane's thickness, porosity, and tortuosity.
3. **Dialysate-side film resistance:** $\dfrac{1}{k_{A,2}}$ — controlled by the boundary layer on the downstream side.

The resistance-in-series equation is:

$$\frac{1}{K_{oA}} = \frac{1}{k_{A,1}} + \frac{1}{P_M} + \frac{1}{k_{A,2}}$$

where $K_{oA}$ is the overall dialysis mass transfer coefficient. In most practical dialysis operations, the membrane resistance $\dfrac{1}{P_M}$ is the controlling (largest) term.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for the three resistances, 1 mark for the equation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **hollow-fibre dialyzer** used in haemodialysis. Explain why counter-current flow is preferred over co-current flow.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A continuous counter-current dialyzer is used to recover **NaOH from a spent industrial stream**. The feed flow rate is $L_1 = 0.005$ m³/s with inlet concentration $c_{1,\text{in}} = 4.0$ kmol/m³. The dialysate (pure water) flow rate is $L_2 = 0.006$ m³/s. The membrane area is 200 m² and the overall mass transfer coefficient is $K_{oA} = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. Calculate the **exit concentration of NaOH in the feed stream** and the **overall recovery** in a single pass.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Construction and Working of Hollow-Fibre Dialyzer**

A hollow-fibre dialyzer consists of several thousand **microporous polymer capillary tubes** (typical internal diameter 200–300 µm, wall thickness 20–50 µm) packed inside a cylindrical **acrylic or polycarbonate shell**. The fibres are potted at both ends with epoxy resin, leaving the open lumens accessible through header caps.

**Construction details:**
- Shell-side inlet and outlet for dialysate.
- Lumen-side (blood) inlet and outlet through header caps.
- Total membrane area: typically **0.5 to 2.5 m²** per clinical unit (industrial units may have 50–500 m²).
- Typical materials: regenerated cellulose (Cuprophan), polysulfone, polyethersulfone, polyamide, or PMMA.

**Working principle:**
- The patient's blood (or industrial feed) enters the lumen side and flows through the inside of the fibres.
- The dialysate enters the shell side and flows **counter-current** to the blood.
- Small solutes (urea, creatinine, NaOH) diffuse from blood → dialysate.
- Blood cells, plasma proteins, and large macromolecules are retained.
- The purified blood and enriched dialysate exit through their respective outlets.

**Why counter-current flow?**
- It maintains a **near-constant concentration gradient** along the entire membrane length.
- It maximizes the **log-mean driving force**, leading to higher NTU efficiency and lower required membrane area compared to co-current flow.
- For equal flow rates, the outlet concentrations in co-current flow are limited (the streams approach a common equilibrium value), whereas counter-current flow allows the dialysate to approach the feed inlet concentration at the feed outlet side, maximizing recovery.

*(7 marks — 3 for sketch + description, 2 for working, 2 for counter-current justification)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

Given:
- $L_1 = 0.005$ m³/s, $c_{1,\text{in}} = 4.0$ kmol/m³
- $L_2 = 0.006$ m³/s, $c_{2,\text{in}} = 0$
- $A_m = 200$ m², $K_{oA} = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute the Number of Transfer Units (NTU):**

$$N_t = \frac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m}{L_1} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 200}{0.005} = \frac{0.008}{0.005} = 1.6$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Flow Ratio:**

$$R_f = \frac{L_1}{L_2} = \frac{0.005}{0.006} = 0.8333$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Kremser Equation for Counter-Current Operation:**

The general Kremser form for counter-current mass transfer with $c_{2,\text{in}} = 0$ is:

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \frac{1}{\exp[N_t(1 - R_f)] + R_f \cdot (\exp[N_t(1 - R_f)] - 1) \cdot \frac{1}{?}}$$

Let us use the standard form valid for $R_f \ne 1$:

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \frac{1 - R_f}{\exp[N_t(1 - R_f)] - R_f}$$

Verify the formula's derivation: For counter-current, the general NTU form is

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{in}} - c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}} - c_{2,\text{in}}} = \frac{1 - \exp[-N_t(1 - R_f)]}{1 - R_f \cdot \exp[-N_t(1 - R_f)]}$$

With $c_{2,\text{in}} = 0$:

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{in}} - c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \frac{1 - \exp[-N_t(1 - R_f)]}{1 - R_f \cdot \exp[-N_t(1 - R_f)]}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Exponent:**

$$N_t(1 - R_f) = 1.6 \cdot (1 - 0.8333) = 1.6 \cdot 0.1667 = 0.2667$$

$$\exp[-0.2667] = 0.7660$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Fractional Unconverted Fraction:**

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{in}} - c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \frac{1 - 0.7660}{1 - 0.8333 \cdot 0.7660}$$

Compute numerator: $1 - 0.7660 = 0.2340$.

Compute denominator: $0.8333 \cdot 0.7660 = 0.6383$. Then $1 - 0.6383 = 0.3617$.

Fractional removal:

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{in}} - c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \frac{0.2340}{0.3617} = 0.6470$$

**Step 6 — Compute Outlet Concentration:**

$$c_{1,\text{out}} = c_{1,\text{in}} \cdot (1 - 0.6470) = 4.0 \cdot 0.3530 = 1.412 \;\; \text{kmol/m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Compute Overall Recovery:**

The total recovery (transfer to dialysate) is the fractional removal:

$$\text{Recovery} = 64.70\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- $c_{1,\text{out}} \approx \mathbf{1.41 \; kmol/m^3}$
- Recovery $\approx \mathbf{64.7\%}$

*(7 marks — 1 for NTU, 1 for $R_f$, 2 for setting up the Kremser equation, 2 for numerical evaluation, 1 for recovery)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the unsteady-state material balance equation for a **batch dialyzer** with feed volume $V_1$, dialysate volume $V_2$, membrane area $A_m$, and overall mass transfer coefficient $K_{oA}$. Show that the concentration in the feed compartment varies as:

$$c_1(t) = c_1(0)\left[\frac{1}{\beta} + \left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)e^{-\theta t}\right]$$

Clearly define $\beta$ and $\theta$.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A pharmaceutical batch dialyzer is used to remove **urea from 2.0 L of a protein solution**. The initial urea concentration is $c_1(0) = 50$ mmol/L. The dialysate (pure buffer) volume is $V_2 = 5.0$ L. The membrane area is 0.8 m² and $K_{oA} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. Determine:
(i) The equilibrium concentration of urea in the protein solution.
(ii) The time required to achieve **95% of the maximum possible removal**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Derivation of Batch Dialyzer Equation**

The complete derivation is provided in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key results are:

- **Volume ratio parameter:** $\beta = 1 + \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$
- **Time constant:** $\theta = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \beta}{V_1}$
- **Final equation:**

$$c_1(t) = c_1(0)\left[\frac{1}{\beta} + \left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)e^{-\theta t}\right]$$

The derivation proceeds through:
1. Unsteady mass balance on the feed compartment.
2. Total mass conservation to eliminate $c_2$.
3. Substitution to obtain a first-order linear ODE.
4. Solution by the integrating factor method.
5. Final isolation of $c_1(t)$.

*(7 marks — 1 for setup, 2 for ODE formation, 2 for solution, 1 for final expression, 1 for definitions)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

Given:
- $V_1 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³, $V_2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $c_1(0) = 50$ mmol/L = 50 mol/m³
- $A_m = 0.8$ m², $K_{oA} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s

**(i) Equilibrium Concentration**

$$\beta = 1 + \frac{V_1}{V_2} = 1 + \frac{2.0}{5.0} = 1.4$$

$$c_1(\infty) = \frac{c_1(0)}{\beta} = \frac{50}{1.4} = 35.71 \;\; \text{mmol/L}$$

**Maximum removal:**

$$R_{\infty} = 1 - \frac{1}{\beta} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.4} = 0.2857 = 28.57\%$$

Note: Even at infinite time, **only 28.57%** of the urea can be removed because the dialysate is a *closed* batch.

**(ii) Time for 95% of Max Removal**

Target removal: $0.95 \cdot 0.2857 = 0.2714$.

From the recovery equation:

$$R(t) = \frac{\beta - 1}{\beta}\left(1 - e^{-\theta t}\right)$$

$$0.2714 = 0.2857 \cdot (1 - e^{-\theta t})$$

$$1 - e^{-\theta t} = 0.95 \Rightarrow e^{-\theta t} = 0.05$$

$$\theta t = -\ln(0.05) = 2.9957$$

**Compute the time constant:**

$$\theta = \frac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \beta}{V_1} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 0.8 \cdot 1.4}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator: $(1.5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 0.8 = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$, times $1.4$ = $1.68 \times 10^{-5}$.

Divide by $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$\theta = 8.4 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Compute the time:**

$$t = \frac{2.9957}{8.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 356.6 \;\; \text{s} \approx 5.94 \;\; \text{minutes}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $c_1(\infty) = 35.71$ mmol/L (max removal = 28.57%)
- (ii) $t \approx 5.94$ minutes to reach 95% of max removal

*(7 marks — 1 for $\beta$, 1 for $c_1(\infty)$, 1 for setting up target, 1 for exponential, 1 for $\theta$, 1 for $t$, 1 for final value)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing dialysis with ultrafiltration:** Students often write "pressure gradient" as the driving force for dialysis. This is wrong — dialysis is purely concentration-driven.
> 2. **Missing the volume ratio $\beta$:** In batch problems, many students forget to use $V_1/V_2$ in the final expression and end up with the simplified form $c_1(t) = c_1(0) \cdot e^{-Kt/V_1}$ which is only valid for an *infinite* dialysate reservoir ($V_2 \to \infty$).
> 3. **Wrong sign of exponent in Kremser equation:** Counter-current and co-current have opposite signs in the exponent. Mixing them up leads to negative outlet concentrations — a dead giveaway of incomplete preparation.
> 4. **Forgetting the natural log brackets:** When writing LMCD, students sometimes drop the $\ln$ brackets, leading to dimensionally inconsistent answers.
> 5. **Mixing $K_{oA}$ with $P_M$:** $K_{oA}$ includes boundary layer effects; $P_M$ is membrane-only. Using one in place of the other gives wrong answers.
> 6. **Treating $c_1(\infty) = 0$ in batch problems:** This is a classic trap. Equilibrium concentration is $c_1(0)/\beta$, *not* zero.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Dialysis (KTU Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Concentration-driven membrane separation exploiting Fickian diffusion and size-based selectivity.
- **Driving force:** $\Delta c$ (concentration gradient). **NOT** pressure, **NOT** voltage, **NOT** temperature.
- **Fick's First Law:** $N_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \dfrac{dc}{dx}$ — the foundational equation of dialysis.
- **Membrane permeability:** $P_M = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot \varepsilon}{\tau \cdot \delta_m}$.
- **Overall mass transfer coefficient (resistance-in-series):**

$$\frac{1}{K_{oA}} = \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{P_M} + \frac{1}{k_2}$$

- **Controlling resistance:** Usually the **membrane** ($\frac{1}{P_M}$ dominates) — not the boundary layers.
- **Batch dialysis equation:**

$$c_1(t) = c_1(0)\left[\frac{1}{\beta} + \left(1 - \frac{1}{\beta}\right)e^{-\theta t}\right]$$

with $\beta = 1 + \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ and $\theta = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m \cdot \beta}{V_1}$.

- **Maximum batch recovery (closed system):** $R_{\infty} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$ — *never* 100% in a closed batch.
- **Counter-current advantage:** Maintains a near-constant driving force, giving $\frac{c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + N_t}$ for equal flow rates and pure dialysate.
- **Number of Transfer Units (NTU):** $N_t = \dfrac{K_{oA} \cdot A_m}{L_1}$.
- **Kremser form (general counter-current):**

$$\frac{c_{1,\text{in}} - c_{1,\text{out}}}{c_{1,\text{in}} - c_{2,\text{in}}} = \frac{1 - \exp[-N_t(1 - R_f)]}{1 - R_f \cdot \exp[-N_t(1 - R_f)]}$$

- **Equipment types:** Plate-and-frame, hollow-fibre, coil, and tubular. Hollow-fibre dominates in modern practice due to high area/volume ratio (up to **30,000 m²/m³**).
- **Clinical application:** Haemodialysis for end-stage renal disease (ESRD). Removes urea, creatinine, uric acid.
- **Industrial applications:** Caustic recovery (pulp/paper), whey desalting (dairy), antibiotic recovery (pharma), acid purification.
- **Selectivity indicator:** Sieving coefficient $= c_{\text{filtrate}} / c_{\text{retentate}}$. Values near 1 = fully permeable; near 0 = fully rejected.
- **Comparison cousins:** Pressure-driven (UF, RO, NF) and electrically driven (ED) membrane processes are *not* dialysis.
- **Key historical fact:** Thomas Graham (**1861**) is the father of dialysis science; Willem Kolff (**1943**) built the first practical artificial kidney.
- **Numerical sanity checks:** $\beta > 1$ always; $0 < R_{\infty} < 1$ always; $c_1(\infty) < c_1(0)$ always; $\theta$ has units of s⁻¹.
- **For an exam, remember:** Always write units in **bold** when notating $K_{oA}$, $P_M$, $A_m$, and the concentrations.
- **Pitfall-proof rule:** Whenever you see a *closed* batch problem, immediately think of $V_2$ limitation. Whenever you see a *continuous* problem with $c_{2,\text{in}} = 0$, immediately think of the Kremser equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrodialysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrodialysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Electrodialysis (ED)** is an electrochemical membrane separation process in which **ionic species are selectively transported through ion-exchange membranes** from a feed (diluate) stream to a concentrate stream under the influence of a **direct current (DC) electric field**. The driving force is the **electrical potential gradient (ΔE)** measured in **V/m**, and the mass transfer occurs via **ionic migration** (not convection or ordinary diffusion).

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncer at a Club"

Imagine a packed nightclub with two doors:
- **Door A (Cation Exchange Membrane)** allows only positively charged people (cations, $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$) to pass.
- **Door B (Anion Exchange Membrane)** allows only negatively charged people (anions, $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) to pass.

The **bouncer (electric field)** pushes the crowd in one direction. Ions of the "right charge" walk through their designated door, but opposite-charge ions are blocked. By alternating these doors in a series, you end up with alternating **Dilute compartments** (where ions are pulled OUT) and **Concentrate compartments** (where ions are pushed IN). This is exactly how an electrodialysis stack works!

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize for KTU Board Exams**
> - **Faraday's Constant: $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/eq}$** (≈ 96,500 for quick calculations)
> - **Electronic charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$**
> - **Avogadro's Number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ /mol}$**

### 1.2 Industrial Importance (Why study ED in Mass Transfer?)

| Application | Industry | Purpose |
|-------------|----------|---------|
| **Seawater / Brackish water desalination** | Water Treatment | Producing potable water |
| **Dairy demineralization** | Food Tech | Whey de-ashing |
| **Tartaric acid stabilization** | Winemaking | Potassium bitartrate removal |
| **Salt production** | Chemical | Concentrating NaCl from brine |
| **Effluent treatment** | Environmental | Recovery of heavy metals & acids |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of an Electrodialysis Stack

A typical ED cell is composed of the following hardware arranged in series:

- **Cation Exchange Membrane (CEM)**: Fixed negative charges (e.g., $-\text{SO}_3^-$). Permits only cations ($\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$); **blocks anions** by electrostatic repulsion (Donnan exclusion).
- **Anion Exchange Membrane (AEM)**: Fixed positive charges (e.g., $-\text{NR}_3^+$). Permits only anions ($\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$); **blocks cations**.
- **Anode (+)**: Typically Ti coated with Pt/IrO₂; oxidation reactions occur (e.g., $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$).
- **Cathode (−)**: Stainless steel; reduction (e.g., $2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^-$).
- **Spacer Gaskets**: Define compartment thickness (typically 0.5–2 mm) and channel flow.
- **Electrode Rinse Streams**: Separate loops to remove gases ($\text{H}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$ or $\text{O}_2$) and prevent membrane fouling at electrodes.

### 2.2 Process Logic — Step-by-Step "Why & How"

1. **Feed (Diluate)** enters every alternate compartment.
2. **DC voltage** applied across the stack creates an electric field $E = V/L$.
3. **Cations** ($+$) migrate toward the **cathode**, passing through CEMs but stopped by AEMs.
4. **Anions** ($-$) migrate toward the **anode**, passing through AEMs but stopped by CEMs.
5. Ions leave the diluate → enter concentrate → diluate becomes **desalinated**.
6. Adjacent compartments accumulate ions → become **brine (concentrate)**.
7. Continuous flow through multiple "cell pairs" achieves target purity.

> [!TIP]
> **A "cell pair" = 1 CEM + 1 AEM + 1 Diluate + 1 Concentrate compartment.** Industrial stacks have **100–600 cell pairs** in parallel hydraulic series.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Meaning |
|---|----------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | $F = N_A \cdot e = 96{,}485 \text{ C/eq}$ | $F$ = Faraday constant | Charge per mole of electrons |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{V}{L} \;\;[\text{V/m}]$ | $V$ = applied voltage, $L$ = membrane spacing | Electric field strength |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{z \cdot F \cdot n_{\text{actual}}}{I \cdot t}$ | $\eta$ = current efficiency, $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $n_{\text{actual}}$ = moles transferred | Fraction of current doing useful ion transfer |
| 4 | $I = z \cdot F \cdot N \quad [\text{A}]$ | $N$ = molar flux (mol/s), $z$ = charge | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis |
| 5 | $E_{\text{cell}} = I \cdot R_{\text{cell}} \;\;[\text{V}]$ | $R_{\text{cell}}$ = total cell resistance (Ω) | Ohm's Law for electrolytic cells |
| 6 | $i_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{z \cdot F \cdot D \cdot C}{\delta(t_+ - t_-)} \;[\text{A/m}^2]$ | $i_{\text{lim}}$ = limiting current density, $D$ = diffusivity, $C$ = conc., $\delta$ = boundary layer, $t_+, t_-$ = ion transport numbers | **Critical Equation** — beyond this, water splits |
| 7 | $W = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{\eta} \;\;[\text{J or kWh}]$ | $W$ = electrical energy consumed | Energy requirement |
| 8 | $\Delta C = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F \cdot V_{\text{dil}}} \;[\text{eq/L}]$ | $\Delta C$ = change in concentration | Salt removal per pass |
| 9 | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | TPR = Total Percent Removal | Performance metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Transport number $t_+$ and $t_-$ satisfy $t_+ + t_- = 1$ for a binary electrolyte in free solution. Inside a CEM, $t_+ \to 1$; inside an AEM, $t_- \to 1$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hybrid RO + ED plants** for high-recovery desalination (RO concentrates further treated by ED).
- **Chlor-alkali process** uses membrane electrolysis (closely related technology) producing $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{H}_2$.
- **Lithium extraction** from brines uses selective ED with monovalent selective membranes.
- **EDR (Electrodialysis Reversal)** periodically switches polarity to prevent scale/fouling — a critical industrial variant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Faraday's Law Applied to Salt Removal

**Starting Premise:** According to Faraday's first law of electrolysis, the mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is proportional to the charge passed.

**Statement:** The moles of ions $n$ transferred across a membrane is related to the current by:

$$n = \frac{Q}{z \cdot F}$$

**Derivation Steps:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Charge} \;\; Q &= I \cdot t \quad \text{(definition, I in A, t in s)} \\
\text{One mole of electrons} &= F \text{ coulombs} \\
\text{Moles of electrons passed} &= \frac{I \cdot t}{F} \\
\text{Since each ion carries charge } z \text{ (equivalents):} \\
\text{Moles of ions transferred } n &= \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why this matters in ED:** This equation sets the **theoretical maximum** salt removal. Real systems lose efficiency due to:
- **Water splitting** at high voltages.
- **Co-ion leakage** (cations sneaking through AEMs).
- **Back-diffusion** of concentrated ions.
- **Membrane fouling** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ scaling).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Current Efficiency (η)

Current efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of **useful ionic current** to **total current passed**.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Actual moles of salt removed} \times z \times F}{\text{Total charge passed}}$$

$$\eta = \frac{z \cdot F \cdot n_{\text{actual}}}{I \cdot t}$$

**Example Numerical Evaluation:**

> A laboratory ED cell removes **0.05 moles of NaCl** in **30 minutes** at **1.5 A**.
> For NaCl, $z = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \cdot t = 1.5 \times (30 \times 60) = 2700 \text{ C} \\
n_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{2700}{96485} = 0.02798 \text{ mol} \\
\eta &= \frac{n_{\text{actual}}}{n_{\text{theoretical}}} = \frac{0.05}{0.02798} = 1.787
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\eta > 1$ here indicates an error in measurement OR that current is passing even during the removal of ions already present (concentration polarization effect). For valid ED operation, **0 < η ≤ 1** is the physical limit (since current efficiency ≥ 100% is only possible if co-ion transport assists, which is rare in well-designed membranes).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Limiting Current Density ($i_{\text{lim}}$)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU boards. It determines the maximum safe current before **water dissociation** occurs.

**Setup:** Consider a CEM with dilute solution flowing past it. At the membrane surface, ion concentration drops due to depletion.

Using the **Nernst-Planck equation** in 1D at steady state:

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT} \frac{d\phi}{dx} + C_i v$$

At the membrane surface (no convection, $v = 0$), the **cation flux** equals the **current density** divided by $zF$:

$$\frac{i}{zF} = -D_+ \frac{dC_+}{dx} - \frac{z_+ F D_+ C_+}{RT} \frac{d\phi}{dx}$$

Through algebraic manipulation using the **transport number difference** at the boundary, the classical result emerges:

$$\boxed{i_{\text{lim}} = \frac{z F D C_b}{(t_+^m - t_+)} \cdot \frac{1}{\delta}}$$

Where:
- $C_b$ = bulk concentration (mol/m³)
- $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s)
- $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness (m)
- $t_+^m$ = cation transport number **inside** membrane (≈ 1 for ideal CEM)
- $t_+$ = cation transport number in **bulk solution**

**Operational Interpretation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{When } i < i_{\text{lim}} &: \text{Normal ion transport, no water splitting} \\
\text{When } i = i_{\text{lim}} &: \text{Ion concentration at membrane surface } \to 0 \\
\text{When } i > i_{\text{lim}} &: \text{Water dissociation } (\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^-)
\end{aligned}$$

**Why this matters:** Operating above $i_{\text{lim}}$ wastes energy as heat, alters pH, damages membranes, and reduces current efficiency. Industrial ED stacks are designed to operate at **70–80% of $i_{\text{lim}}$**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Consumption

$$W = V \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{V^2 \cdot t}{R_{\text{cell}}}$$

Often normalized per **kg of salt removed**:

$$\boxed{W_{\text{specific}} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot V}{m_{\text{salt}}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kWh}}{\text{kg}}\right]}$$

Substituting $m_{\text{salt}} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{\text{salt}}}{z \cdot F}$:

$$W_{\text{specific}} = \frac{z \cdot F \cdot V}{\eta \cdot M_{\text{salt}} \cdot 3600 \times 1000} \quad [\text{kWh/kg}]$$

(where $M_{\text{salt}}$ = molecular weight in kg/kmol, and the factor handles unit conversion).

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hack:** Typical brackish water ED: **0.5–2.5 kWh per m³**. Seawater ED: **2.5–5.5 kWh/m³**. Reverse Osmosis is generally more energy-efficient for seawater, but ED excels for low-salinity feeds (< 5000 ppm).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define electrodialysis. What are the two main types of membranes used in an ED cell?**

**Model Answer:**
Electrodialysis is an electrochemical membrane separation process in which ions are transported through ion-selective membranes from a feed stream to a concentrate stream under the influence of a DC electric potential gradient. The driving force is the electrical potential difference, and the transport mechanism is ionic migration through selectively permeable membranes.

The two main types of membranes are:
1. **Cation Exchange Membrane (CEM)** — has fixed negative groups (e.g., $-\text{SO}_3^-$) and permits only cations to pass.
2. **Anion Exchange Membrane (AEM)** — has fixed positive groups (e.g., $-\text{NR}_3^+$) and permits only anions to pass.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is meant by "limiting current density" in electrodialysis? Why must it not be exceeded?**

**Model Answer:**
Limiting current density ($i_{\text{lim}}$) is the maximum current density that can be passed through an ED membrane before the ion concentration at the membrane surface drops to zero, causing **water dissociation** ($\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^-$).

It must not be exceeded because:
- Operating above $i_{\text{lim}}$ causes **water splitting**, wasting electrical energy.
- It leads to **pH changes** that damage membranes.
- Current efficiency drops sharply.
- **Scaling and fouling** accelerate at the membrane surface.

The empirical formula is:
$$i_{\text{lim}} = \frac{zFD C_b}{(t_+^m - t_+) \delta}$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the limiting current density in electrodialysis and explain the significance of operating below this limit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A laboratory electrodialysis unit operates at 2.0 A current for 2 hours to treat 5 L of a NaCl solution. The initial concentration is 0.1 M and the final is 0.025 M. Calculate: **(i)** Current efficiency, **(ii)** Energy consumed if voltage = 12 V. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation: As shown in **Section 3.3** above. The final expression is:

$$i_{\text{lim}} = \frac{z F D C_b}{(t_+^m - t_+) \cdot \delta}$$

**Significance of operating below $i_{\text{lim}}$:**

| Aspect | Below $i_{\text{lim}}$ | Above $i_{\text{lim}}$ |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Energy use | Efficient | Wasted on water splitting |
| pH | Stable | Acidic/basic shifts |
| Membrane life | Long | Damaged by H⁺/OH⁻ |
| Current efficiency | High (>90%) | Low (<60%) |

Industrial practice: Operate at **70–80% of $i_{\text{lim}}$** to ensure safety margin.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $I = 2.0$ A, $t = 2 \text{ h} = 7200$ s, $V_{\text{dil}} = 5$ L, $C_{\text{in}} = 0.1$ M, $C_{\text{out}} = 0.025$ M, $V_{\text{cell}} = 12$ V, $z = 1$ (NaCl).

**Step 1: Actual moles removed**

$$n_{\text{actual}} = (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}) \times V_{\text{dil}} = (0.1 - 0.025) \times 5 = 0.375 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2: Theoretical moles (Faraday's Law)**

$$n_{\text{theor}} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F} = \frac{2.0 \times 7200}{1 \times 96485} = 0.14925 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3: Current Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{n_{\text{actual}}}{n_{\text{theor}}} = \frac{0.375}{0.14925} = 2.512 \approx 251\%$$

**Interpretation:** This value >100% indicates that the assumption of only NaCl being removed is wrong — other ions are also migrating, OR there's measurement error. In a well-designed lab cell, η should approach 1 (100%) for NaCl-only systems.

**Step 4: Energy Consumed**

$$W = V \cdot I \cdot t = 12 \times 2.0 \times 7200 = 172{,}800 \text{ J} = 0.048 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\boxed{W = 172.8 \text{ kJ} \text{ (or } 0.048 \text{ kWh)}}$$

---

**Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an electrodialysis cell. Discuss the role of cation and anion exchange membranes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A brackish water feed containing 3000 ppm of NaCl (MW = 58.44) is to be desalinated to 500 ppm using an ED unit operating at 80% current efficiency. If the stack draws 50 A at 100 V and operates for 4 hours, calculate: **(i)** Total salt removed (kg), **(ii)** Specific energy consumption in kWh/kg salt removed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to **Section 2.1** and **Section 4 diagrams** above. Key points to mention:

- DC power supply drives cation migration to cathode, anion migration to anode.
- CEM permits cations, blocks anions (Donnan exclusion due to fixed $-\text{SO}_3^-$ groups).
- AEM permits anions, blocks cations (fixed $-\text{NR}_3^+$ groups).
- Alternating arrangement creates **diluate and concentrate compartments**.
- Electrode rinse removes gases ($\text{H}_2$ at cathode, $\text{Cl}_2$ or $\text{O}_2$ at anode).
- Cell pair = 1 CEM + 1 AEM defines a repeating unit.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $I = 50$ A, $t = 4 \text{ h} = 14{,}400$ s, $V = 100$ V, $\eta = 0.80$, $z = 1$, $M_{\text{NaCl}} = 58.44$ g/mol.

**Step 1: Theoretical moles (Faraday's Law)**

$$n_{\text{theor}} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F} = \frac{50 \times 14400}{1 \times 96485} = 7.463 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2: Actual moles (with 80% efficiency)**

$$n_{\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot n_{\text{theor}} = 0.80 \times 7.463 = 5.970 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3: Mass of Salt Removed**

$$m_{\text{salt}} = n_{\text{actual}} \times M_{\text{NaCl}} = 5.970 \times 58.44 = 348.9 \text{ g} = 0.3489 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Energy Consumed**

$$W = V \cdot I \cdot t = 100 \times 50 \times 14400 = 7.2 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 20 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5: Specific Energy Consumption**

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{W}{m_{\text{salt}}} = \frac{20 \text{ kWh}}{0.3489 \text{ kg}} = 57.32 \text{ kWh/kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{salt removed}} \approx 0.349 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{SEC} \approx 57.3 \text{ kWh/kg}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\eta$ factor** in Faraday's Law: The actual salt removed = η × (I·t)/(z·F). Marks lost = 2.
> 2. **Unit confusion in SEC**: kWh/kg ≠ kJ/kg. Convert J → kWh by dividing by $3.6 \times 10^6$.
> 3. **Confusing transport numbers**: In CEM, $t_+ \approx 1$ (cations carry current). In AEM, $t_- \approx 1$. Students often write the opposite.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption**: Always state "at steady state, 1D transport, no concentration polarization beyond boundary layer" in derivations.
> 5. **Missing the cell pair concept**: A single membrane is NOT a cell pair; the pair is CEM + AEM.
> 6. **Forgetting Faraday's constant value** = 96,485 C/eq. Memorize it.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Electrodialysis = Ionic migration driven by DC electric field through selective membranes.**
- ✅ **CEM** has fixed negative charges; **AEM** has fixed positive charges (Donnan exclusion principle).
- ✅ **Faraday's Law:** $n = I t / (zF)$ — theoretical max for ion transfer.
- ✅ **Current Efficiency η** = (Actual moles removed × z × F) / (I × t); should be ≤ 1 in good systems.
- ✅ **Limiting Current Density:** $i_{\text{lim}} = zFD C_b / [(t_+^m - t_+)\delta]$. Never operate above this — causes water splitting.
- ✅ **Energy:** $W = VIt$. SEC in kWh/kg = benchmark metric.
- ✅ **Industrial operating current:** 70–80% of $i_{\text{lim}}$.
- ✅ **Cell pair** = CEM + AEM + diluate + concentrate compartment.
- ✅ **EDR (Electrodialysis Reversal)** periodically switches polarity to combat fouling.
- ✅ **Pretreatment** (filtration, softening) is critical to prevent membrane scaling by Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺.
- ✅ **Typical applications:** brackish water desalination, whey demineralization, salt concentration, acid recovery.
- ✅ **Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/eq** — must be memorized for numerical problems.
- ✅ **Transport numbers** satisfy $t_+ + t_- = 1$ in solution; in ideal membranes, one approaches unity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pervaporation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pervaporation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pervaporation (PV)** is a **membrane-based separation process** in which a **liquid feed mixture** is contacted with one face of a **non-porous (dense) selective membrane**, while the permeate is withdrawn from the opposite face as a **vapour**, by maintaining a lower partial pressure (typically by applying vacuum or sweeping with an inert gas). The name is a contraction of **"permeation"** + **"evaporation"** — components selectively *permeate* through the membrane and *evaporate* on the downstream side.

The process achieves separation because different components have **different chemical affinities and diffusivities** within the membrane polymer. Unlike reverse osmosis or ultrafiltration, the phase change from **liquid → vapor** is the defining feature, and the membrane is essentially **non-porous** (no convective flow through discrete pores).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a *selective sponge wall* in a swimming pool.
> - The pool side (feed) has a mix of red and blue water (solvent + solute).
> - On the other side of the wall, a strong fan continuously blows air, *evaporating* whatever passes through.
> - The sponge (membrane) is chemically "allergic" to red dye — it **rejects red dye** but **lets blue water through easily**.
> - Result: the air-side (permeate) gets **enriched in blue water**, and the pool side gets **depleted in blue water**.

That is essentially what pervaporation does — it uses a **chemically selective polymer film** plus a **vapor-pressure driving force** to split a liquid mixture that may even boil at the *same temperature* (azeotropes), where ordinary distillation fails.

## 1.3 Where Pervaporation is Used (Standard Engineering Applications)

- **Dehydration of organic solvents** — ethanol, isopropanol, acetone (removing trace water).
- **Breaking azeotropes** — ethanol–water azeotrope (~95.6 wt% ethanol), IPA–water, THF–water.
- **Removal of VOCs from water** — trace toluene, chloroform, trichloroethylene.
- **Separation of close-boiling isomers** — xylene isomers, C8 aromatics.
- **Recovery of flavour/fragrance compounds** — in food and pharma industries.
- **Dewatering of esterification reactions** — shifting equilibrium in reactive distillation hybrids.

## 1.4 Key Boundary Metrics You Must Memorise

| Term | Symbol | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Membrane thickness | $\ell$ | **1 – 50 µm** |
| Permeate pressure | $P_p$ | **0.001 – 0.05 atm** (high vacuum) |
| Operating temperature | $T$ | **30 – 80 °C** |
| Transmembrane flux | $J$ | **0.1 – 10 kg·m⁻²·h⁻¹** |
| Separation factor | $\alpha_{ij}$ | **5 – 10,000** (depending on system) |

> [!TIP]
> **Single keyword examiners love:** *"Selective sorption–diffusion–evaporation"* — state this verbatim in any definitional KTU answer and you'll instantly bag 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Concept (Solution–Diffusion Model)

The mass transfer through a pervaporation membrane is universally described by the **Solution–Diffusion model**, which postulates three sequential steps:

1. **Selective Sorption (at the feed/membrane interface)**
   - Liquid components partition into the upstream face of the dense membrane.
   - The component with higher solubility in the polymer is preferentially absorbed.
   - Quantified by the **solubility coefficient** $S_i$ (analogous to Henry's law constant).

2. **Selective Diffusion (through the membrane bulk)**
   - The sorbed molecules diffuse down their **chemical potential gradient**.
   - The smaller / more "flexible" molecule moves faster → quantified by the **diffusivity** $D_i$.
   - Diffusion is the rate-limiting step in most PV systems.

3. **Desorption / Evaporation (at the permeate/membrane interface)**
   - On the downstream side, pressure is kept very low (vacuum or sweep gas).
   - The molecules *desorb* into the vapour phase — this step is **fast and non-selective**.
   - The vapour is condensed to recover the permeate as liquid (in most industrial units).

> **Why this works:** The selectivity of pervaporation comes from the **product** of sorption selectivity and diffusion selectivity, i.e., $\alpha_{ij}^{PV} = (S_i/S_j) \times (D_i/D_j)$.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **only set of equations** you need to solve any numerical or descriptive KTU question on Pervaporation.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ | **Permeability** = Diffusivity × Solubility |
| 2 | $J_i = \dfrac{P_i}{\ell}\bigl(x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat} - y_i\,P_p\bigr)$ | **Component flux** through dense membrane |
| 3 | $J_i \;\Big\vert_{\,P_p \to 0} \;=\; \dfrac{P_i\,x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat}}{\ell}$ | Simplified flux when permeate pressure is negligible |
| 4 | $\alpha_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{y_i / y_j}{x_i / x_j}$ | **Separation factor** of component $i$ over $j$ |
| 5 | $\alpha_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{P_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat}}{P_j\,\gamma_j\,P_j^{sat}}$ | Separation factor in terms of membrane & thermodynamic properties |
| 6 | $\theta \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_{permeate}}{\dot{m}_{feed}}$ | **Stage cut** (mass or molar) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{K_{ov}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{\ell}{P_i} + \dfrac{1}{k_g\,(P_i^{sat}/P_p)}$ | **Overall resistance** in series (feed boundary layer + membrane + permeate side) |
| 8 | $\text{PSI} \;=\; J_{total} \cdot (\alpha - 1)$ | **Pervaporation Separation Index** — combined performance metric |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $P_i$ (permeability) is a *membrane property* (units: kmol·m/m²·s·Pa or kg·m/m²·h·atm), and is **different** from $P_i^{sat}$ (saturation vapour pressure, units: Pa or atm). Do not confuse the two — KTU examiners *deliberately* test this.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pervaporation + Distillation hybrid columns** — used by Sulzer, GFT (now Pervatech) to dehydrate bioethanol economically below the azeotropic point.
- **Membrane material selection** driven by the **solution–diffusion trade-off**:
  - *Hydrophilic membranes* (e.g., NaA zeolite, PVA) → used for **water removal** (dehydration).
  - *Organophilic / hydrophobic membranes* (e.g., PDMS, silicone rubber) → used for **VOC recovery** from water.
- **Energy advantage:** No latent heat of vaporisation is required for the *entire* feed — only the small fraction that permeates. This makes PV drastically **more energy-efficient than distillation** for low-concentration species.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Component Flux Equation (From First Principles)

We start from the **Solution–Diffusion model**. The driving force inside the membrane is the **chemical potential gradient** of component $i$.

**Step 1 — Express chemical potential inside the membrane (Fickian form).**
For an isothermal, isobaric system inside the membrane, the molar flux of component $i$ along the membrane coordinate $z$ is given by Fick's first law:

$$
J_i \;=\; -D_i\,\frac{dC_i}{dz}
$$

where $C_i$ is the molar concentration of $i$ dissolved in the membrane polymer (kmol·m⁻³) and $D_i$ is its diffusivity (m²·s⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Relate membrane concentration to external partial pressure (Henry's law analogue).**
At the membrane–fluid interface, equilibrium is established between the partial pressure of $i$ in the adjacent fluid and its concentration inside the membrane:

$$
C_i \;=\; S_i\,p_i
$$

where $S_i$ is the solubility coefficient (kmol·m⁻³·Pa⁻¹) and $p_i$ is the partial vapour pressure of $i$ in the adjacent fluid.

**Step 3 — Compute partial pressures on each side of the membrane.**
- **Upstream (feed liquid side):** The partial pressure of $i$ in equilibrium with the liquid is given by modified Raoult's law:

$$
p_i^{feed} \;=\; x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat}
$$

where $x_i$ is the mole fraction in the liquid, $\gamma_i$ is the activity coefficient, and $P_i^{sat}$ is the pure-component saturation vapour pressure at the operating temperature.

- **Downstream (permeate vapour side):** The partial pressure of $i$ in the vapour is:

$$
p_i^{perm} \;=\; y_i\,P_p
$$

where $y_i$ is the mole fraction in the permeate vapour and $P_p$ is the total permeate-side pressure.

**Step 4 — Solve the differential equation with the two boundary conditions.**
Substituting the Henry-type relation into Fick's law, and assuming $D_i$ and $S_i$ are constant across the thin membrane:

$$
J_i \;=\; \frac{D_i\,S_i}{\ell}\bigl(p_i^{feed} - p_i^{perm}\bigr)
$$

Substituting the expressions for the partial pressures:

$$
J_i \;=\; \frac{D_i\,S_i}{\ell}\bigl(x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat} - y_i\,P_p\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the permeability coefficient $P_i$.**
Defining the **permeability** $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ (units: kmol·m/m²·s·Pa):

$$
\boxed{\,J_i \;=\; \frac{P_i}{\ell}\bigl(x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat} - y_i\,P_p\bigr)\,}
$$

This is the **master flux equation** for pervaporation.

**Step 6 — Simplification under high-vacuum conditions.**
When $P_p \to 0$ (typical industrial operation), the second term vanishes:

$$
J_i \;\approx\; \frac{P_i\,\gamma_i\,x_i\,P_i^{sat}}{\ell}
$$

In this regime the permeate composition $y_i$ no longer affects the flux — a *huge* simplification that KTU examiners love to ask you to "state and justify".

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Separation Factor in Terms of Membrane & Thermodynamic Properties

**Step 1 — Take the ratio of component fluxes.**

$$
\frac{J_i}{J_j} \;=\; \frac{y_i}{y_j} \cdot \frac{\dot{n}_{perm}}{\dot{n}_{perm}} \;=\; \frac{y_i}{y_j}\;\;\text{(at low }P_p\text{, fluxes } \propto \text{ partial feed pressures)}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate flux ratio to selectivity expression.**

$$
\frac{J_i}{J_j} \;=\; \frac{P_i\,x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat}}{P_j\,x_j\,\gamma_j\,P_j^{sat}}
$$

By the **defining relation** for the separation factor $\alpha_{ij} = (y_i/y_j)/(x_i/x_j)$, the ratio of compositions on the permeate side relates to the flux ratio as:

$$
\frac{y_i}{y_j} \;=\; \alpha_{ij}\,\frac{x_i}{x_j}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the separation factor.**

$$
\alpha_{ij} \;=\; \frac{P_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat}}{P_j\,\gamma_j\,P_j^{sat}}
$$

This shows that the **PV separation factor is the product of three physical contributions**:

$$
\alpha_{ij}^{PV} \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{P_i}{P_j}}_{\text{membrane selectivity}} \times \underbrace{\frac{\gamma_i}{\gamma_j}}_{\text{thermodynamic (non-ideality)}} \times \underbrace{\frac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}}_{\text{volatility}}
$$

> **Key insight (KTU favourite):** Even if the membrane is non-selective ($P_i = P_j$), a difference in volatility ($P_i^{sat} \neq P_j^{sat}$) or non-ideality ($\gamma_i \neq \gamma_j$) will still create separation. This is why PV can break azeotropes.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem statement:** A pervaporation unit processes a liquid feed of **10 wt% ethanol + 90 wt% water** at **60 °C** through a **polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) membrane** of thickness $\ell = 5\,\mu m$. The permeate side is maintained at a vacuum of **0.01 atm**. The following data apply:

| Property | Ethanol (A) | Water (B) |
|---|---|---|
| Permeability $P_i$ (kg·m / m²·h·atm) | $1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ | $5.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Saturation vapour pressure $P_i^{sat}$ (atm) | $0.17$ | $0.12$ |
| Activity coefficient $\gamma_i$ | $1.50$ | $1.10$ |

Feed mass fractions → mole fractions:
- $x_A = \dfrac{10/46}{10/46 + 90/18} = 0.0416$
- $x_B = 1 - x_A = 0.9584$

**(a) Component flux assuming very low permeate pressure ($P_p \to 0$):**

$$
J_A \;=\; \frac{P_A}{\ell}\,\gamma_A\,x_A\,P_A^{sat}
\;=\; \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg·m/(m²·h·atm)}}{5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}} \times 1.50 \times 0.0416 \times 0.17
$$

Evaluating term by term:
- $\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.0\,\text{kg/(m²·h·atm)}$
- $1.50 \times 0.0416 \times 0.17 = 0.01061\,\text{atm}$

$$
J_A \;=\; 3.0 \times 0.01061 \;=\; 0.0318\,\text{kg·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}
$$

Similarly for water:

$$
J_B \;=\; \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \times 1.10 \times 0.9584 \times 0.12
\;=\; 1.0 \times 0.1265
\;=\; 0.1265\,\text{kg·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}
$$

**(b) Permeate composition and separation factor:**

$$
y_A \;=\; \frac{J_A}{J_A + J_B} \;=\; \frac{0.0318}{0.0318 + 0.1265} \;=\; 0.201
$$

$$
\alpha_{AB} \;=\; \frac{y_A/y_B}{x_A/x_B} \;=\; \frac{0.201 / 0.799}{0.0416 / 0.9584} \;=\; \frac{0.2516}{0.0434} \;\approx\; 5.80
$$

**Result:** The membrane enriches ethanol from 4.16 mol% in the feed to **20.1 mol% in the permeate**, with a separation factor of **≈ 5.8**. This is the typical KTU numerical answer format.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Full Industrial Pervaporation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Tank\nLiquid Mixture] --> B[Feed Pump\nBooster Pump]
    B --> C[Heater\nPre Heater Unit]
    C --> D[Membrane Module\nDense Selective Film]
    D --> E[Vacuum Pump\nLow Pressure Side]
    D --> F[Retentate Outlet\nDepleted Liquid]
    E --> G[Condenser\nVapor to Liquid]
    G --> H[Permeate Receiver\nEnriched Product]
    H --> I[Pump Recycle\nOptional Loop]
    I --> A

    classDef unit fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef product fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    class A,F,H product
    class B,C,D,E,G,I unit
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Solution–Diffusion Mechanism Inside the Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Liquid Feed Side\nComponents A and B] --> S1[Step 1 Selective Sorption\nAt Upstream Interface]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2 Selective Diffusion\nAcross Membrane Polymer]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Desorption Evaporation\nAt Downstream Interface]
    S3 --> V[Permeate Vapor\nEnriched in A]
    R[Retentate Stream\nEnriched in B] --> L

    S1 -.- M1[Membrane\nDense Non Porous Film]
    S2 -.- M1
    S3 -.- M1

    classDef stepBox fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
```
