
## 4.3 Concentration / Driving-Force Profile Across the Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Position 1\nUpstream Interface\nHigh Activity] --"Partial Pressure p feed"--> P2[Position 2\nInside Membrane\nLinear Gradient]
    P2 --"Permeate Side"--> P3[Position 3\nDownstream Interface\nLow Activity Vacuum]
    P3 --"Desorption"--> P4[Permeate Vapor\nStream]

    P1 -.- noteA[Activity falls\nlinearly across\nmembrane thickness l]
    P2 -.- noteA
    P3 -.- noteA

    classDef nodeA fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef nodeB fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    class P1,P2,P3 nodeA
    class P4 nodeB
```

> [!TIP]
> In your KTU answer sheet, **always draw the membrane cross-section with three labelled zones** (sorption, diffusion, desorption) and the arrows showing the chemical-potential drop. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks purely for the *quality and labelling* of this diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**Define pervaporation. List any four industrial applications of this separation process.**

**Model Answer:**

Pervaporation is a membrane separation process in which a **liquid feed mixture** is selectively separated by partial **vapourisation** through a **dense (non-porous) membrane**, with the permeate removed as a **vapour** on the downstream side maintained at low pressure.

**Four industrial applications:**
1. Dehydration of organic solvents (e.g., ethanol, isopropanol, acetone).
2. Separation of azeotropic mixtures (e.g., ethanol–water, THF–water).
3. Recovery of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from aqueous streams.
4. Concentration of heat-sensitive products in the food and pharmaceutical industries.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + ½ mark per application × 4)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the solution–diffusion mechanism for mass transfer through a pervaporation membrane.**

**Model Answer:**

The solution–diffusion mechanism describes the transport of components through a dense pervaporation membrane in **three sequential steps**:

1. **Selective sorption** — At the upstream face, components from the liquid feed partition into the membrane based on their solubility in the polymer. The more soluble component is preferentially absorbed.
2. **Selective diffusion** — The sorbed molecules diffuse through the membrane bulk under a chemical-potential gradient. The smaller, more mobile molecule diffuses faster.
3. **Desorption (evaporation)** — At the downstream face, the molecules leave the membrane and enter the vapour phase, driven by the low permeate-side pressure maintained by a vacuum pump or sweep gas.

The **permeability** $P_i$ of a component is the product of its **diffusivity** $D_i$ and **solubility** $S_i$ in the membrane: $P_i = D_i S_i$.

*(3 marks: 1 for the three steps + 1 for explanation + 1 for the permeability expression)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Confusing **permeability** $P_i$ (membrane property) with **saturation vapour pressure** $P_i^{sat}$ (thermodynamic property). They have *different units*.
> - ❌ Forgetting to **multiply by $\gamma_i$** in the flux equation when the feed is non-ideal. (This costs ~2 marks every time.)
> - ❌ Drawing the membrane as **porous** in the diagram — it MUST be shown as a **dense, non-porous film**.
> - ❌ Skipping the "**vacuum pump**" in the process flow diagram — it's the *driving force* for evaporation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Derive the flux equation for a component through a pervaporation membrane using the solution–diffusion model. State the assumptions clearly.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*(Step 1 — 1 mark)* Express the molar flux by Fick's first law inside the membrane:

$$
J_i \;=\; -D_i\,\frac{dC_i}{dz}
$$

*(Step 2 — 1 mark)* Relate membrane concentration to adjacent fluid partial pressure using Henry-type equilibrium:

$$
C_i \;=\; S_i\,p_i
$$

*(Step 3 — 2 marks)* Express partial pressures on both sides:
- Upstream (feed liquid, modified Raoult's law): $p_i^{feed} = x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat}$
- Downstream (permeate vapour): $p_i^{perm} = y_i\,P_p$

*(Step 4 — 2 marks)* Substitute boundary conditions and integrate across thickness $\ell$:

$$
J_i \;=\; \frac{D_i S_i}{\ell}\,\bigl(x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat} - y_i\,P_p\bigr) \;=\; \frac{P_i}{\ell}\,\bigl(x_i\,\gamma_i\,P_i^{sat} - y_i\,P_p\bigr)
$$

*(Step 5 — 1 mark)* State the key assumptions:
- Steady-state, isothermal operation.
- Constant $D_i$ and $S_i$ across the membrane.
- Equilibrium established at both membrane interfaces.
- Negligible pressure drop across the dense membrane.

#### **(b) A pervaporation unit separates an ethanol–water mixture. The feed is 12 wt% ethanol, the permeate is 60 wt% ethanol (both on a mass basis). The membrane area is 2 m², the feed rate is 200 kg/h, and the permeate rate is 40 kg/h. Calculate:** *(7 marks)*
- **(i) Component fluxes $J_A$ (ethanol) and $J_B$ (water).**
- **(ii) Separation factor $\alpha_{AB}$.**
- **(iii) Stage cut $\theta$.**

**Model Solution:**

*Mass fractions → mole fractions (MW: ethanol = 46, water = 18):*

$$
x_A^{feed} \;=\; \frac{12/46}{12/46 + 88/18} \;=\; \frac{0.2609}{0.2609 + 4.8889} \;=\; 0.0506
$$

$$
x_B^{feed} \;=\; 0.9494
$$

*Permeate composition (60 wt% ethanol):*

$$
y_A^{perm} \;=\; \frac{60/46}{60/46 + 40/18} \;=\; \frac{1.3043}{1.3043 + 2.2222} \;=\; 0.370
$$

$$
y_B^{perm} \;=\; 0.630
$$

**(i) Component fluxes:**

$$
J_A \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{A,perm}}{A} \;=\; \frac{0.60 \times 40}{2} \;=\; 12.0\,\text{kg·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}
$$

$$
J_B \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{B,perm}}{A} \;=\; \frac{0.40 \times 40}{2} \;=\; 8.0\,\text{kg·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}
$$

**(ii) Separation factor:**

$$
\alpha_{AB} \;=\; \frac{y_A/y_B}{x_A/x_B} \;=\; \frac{0.370 / 0.630}{0.0506 / 0.9494} \;=\; \frac{0.5873}{0.0533} \;\approx\; 11.02
$$

**(iii) Stage cut:**

$$
\theta \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{permeate}}{\dot{m}_{feed}} \;=\; \frac{40}{200} \;=\; 0.20 \;\;(20\%)
$$

**Results:** $J_A = 12.0\,\text{kg·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}$, $J_B = 8.0\,\text{kg·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}$, $\alpha_{AB} \approx 11.0$, $\theta = 0.20$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) With the help of a neat schematic, describe the working principle of a pervaporation system. Discuss the role of the membrane, vacuum pump, and condenser.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Schematic (refer to Section 4.1 process-flow diagram for the standard KTU sketch).**

**Working Principle — step by step:**

1. The **liquid feed mixture** (e.g., ethanol–water) is pumped from a feed tank through a pre-heater (to enhance flux and adjust viscosity).
2. The hot liquid enters the **membrane module**, contacting the upstream face of the **dense (non-porous) membrane**.
3. Components with higher affinity and diffusivity (e.g., water in a dehydration membrane) selectively **sorb and diffuse** through the polymer film.
4. A **vacuum pump** on the permeate side maintains a very low absolute pressure (typically 0.001–0.05 atm), which:
   - Creates the **chemical-potential gradient** that drives diffusion.
   - Causes the desorbed molecules to **flash-evaporate** immediately.
5. The **permeate vapour** flows to a **condenser**, where it is cooled and liquefied to give the enriched product.
6. The **retentate** (depleted liquid) exits the module — in industry it is often recycled for further processing.

**Roles of key components:**
- **Membrane:** Provides the **selectivity** (chemical discrimination between species).
- **Vacuum pump:** Provides the **driving force** and removes the permeate to prevent saturation.
- **Condenser:** Recovers the permeate as a useful **liquid product** and maintains the low downstream pressure.

*(7 marks: 2 for schematic, 3 for principle steps, 2 for component roles)*

#### **(b) Compare pervaporation with conventional distillation for separating azeotropic mixtures. Why is pervaporation often used in hybrid distillation–membrane systems?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Distillation | Pervaporation |
|---|---|---|
| Phase change | Full feed (vaporisation + condensation) | Only the permeate fraction |
| Azeotrope handling | Requires entrainer / pressure-swing / extractive distillation | **Breaks azeotrope directly** via selective sorption |
| Energy intensity | High (latent heat of full feed) | Low (only permeate evaporates) |
| Selectivity source | Relative volatility $\alpha_{ij}^{VLE}$ | Membrane permeability + thermodynamic non-ideality |
| Operating temperature | Near boiling point | Typically 30–80 °C (suitable for heat-sensitive products) |
| Modular scalability | Limited by column hydraulics | Easily modular (add membrane area) |

**Why hybrid distillation–PV systems dominate the industry:**

1. **Distillation is unbeatable for the bulk separation** (cheap per kg of solvent handled).
2. **The last few percent (e.g., going past 95.6 wt% ethanol in the azeotrope) become exponentially expensive** in distillation because of the steep equilibrium curve near the azeotrope.
3. **Pervaporation takes over precisely in this region** — where it shows its highest selectivity, because the trace component (water) has the largest driving force gradient.
4. The **hybrid system** cuts overall energy consumption by **30–60%** compared to extractive distillation and avoids the cost of an entrainer (e.g., benzene, ethylene glycol).

**Industrial examples:** GFT/Sulzer ethanol dehydration units worldwide; biodiesel purification (glycerol–methanol separation); pharma-grade solvent recovery.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 2 for hybrid justification, 2 for examples)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Pervaporation (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Liquid feed → dense membrane → vapour permeate. Name = Permeation + Evaporation.
- ✅ **Membrane type:** Non-porous (dense), NOT porous. Use *solution–diffusion* model, not pore-flow.
- ✅ **Three steps:** (1) Selective sorption → (2) Selective diffusion → (3) Desorption–evaporation.
- ✅ **Permeability equation:** $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$.
- ✅ **Master flux equation:** $J_i = \dfrac{P_i}{\ell}\bigl(x_i\gamma_i P_i^{sat} - y_i P_p\bigr)$.
- ✅ **High-vacuum simplification:** When $P_p \to 0$, $J_i = \dfrac{P_i \gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}}{\ell}$.
- ✅ **Separation factor:** $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{P_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}}{P_j \gamma_j P_j^{sat}}$ (no permeate-pressure dependence at high vacuum).
- ✅ **Stage cut:** $\theta = \dot{m}_{permeate} / \dot{m}_{feed}$.
- ✅ **Overall resistance:** $\dfrac{1}{K_{ov}} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{\ell}{P_i} + \dfrac{1}{k_g (P_i^{sat}/P_p)}$.
- ✅ **PSI metric:** $\text{PSI} = J_{total} \cdot (\alpha - 1)$ — used to rank membrane performance.
- ✅ **Membrane materials:** NaA zeolite / PVA → dehydration; PDMS / silicone rubber → organics/VOC.
- ✅ **Key application:** Breaking azeotropes (ethanol–water, IPA–water) and dehydration of organic solvents.
- ✅ **Energy edge:** Only the *permeate* phase-changes, not the whole feed — far more energy-efficient than distillation for low-concentration species.
- ✅ **Hybrid:** Distillation handles the bulk → PV polishes past the azeotrope. Used in 90%+ of industrial bioethanol plants.
- ✅ **Don't confuse symbols:** $P_i$ (permeability) ≠ $P_i^{sat}$ (saturation vapour pressure) ≠ $P_p$ (permeate total pressure). Examiners *will* test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perstraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Perstraction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **PERSTRACTION (Membrane Extraction / Non-Dispersive Extraction):** > Perstraction is a **membrane-based separation process** in which a feed mixture (typically a liquid) is contacted with a **selective membrane**, and the selectively permeated species is removed from the downstream side of the membrane by an **immiscible liquid sweep (extractant solvent)**. The permeating component dissolves into the sweep liquid rather than evaporating into a vapor phase, thereby combining the selectivity of a membrane with the recovery advantages of a liquid-liquid extraction. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Perstraction belongs to the family of **Membrane Separation Processes** and is often discussed in conjunction with **Pervaporation** and **Dialysis**. The defining characteristic is that the permeate phase on the downstream side is a **liquid (sweep solvent)** and **NOT a vapor or vacuum**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Coffee Filter with Honey Sweep" Analogy:** > Imagine a thin, ultra-fine cloth stretched across the mouth of a cup containing honey. If you pour a mixture of water and coffee grounds on top of the cloth, the water (and dissolved coffee flavor) can pass through the cloth's microscopic pores, but the grounds are physically held back. Now, instead of letting the water drip into air (which would evaporate), the cloth is dipped into a jar of honey. The water that passes through gets immediately absorbed by the honey, which acts as the **sweep liquid**. The cloth is the **membrane**, the water-coffee mix is the **feed**, the honey is the **sweep solvent**, and the absorbed water-coffee mixture in honey is the **permeate**. > The cloth never dissolves, never evaporates anything, and the honey (sweep) can be regenerated later by gentle heating or back-extraction. ## 1.3 Distinguishing Perstraction from Neighbouring Processes | Feature | Perstraction | Pervaporation | Dialysis | |---|---|---|---| | **Downstream phase** | Immiscible liquid sweep | Vapor / vacuum | Pure liquid (often water) | | **Phase change?** | No (liquid → liquid) | Yes (liquid → vapor) | No (liquid → liquid) | | **Driving force** | Concentration gradient (partial pressure) | Partial pressure / activity gradient | Concentration gradient | | **Selectivity source** | Membrane + solvent affinity | Membrane sorption + diffusion | Membrane pore size / charge | | **Typical application** | Removal of organics from water using an oil sweep | Dehydration of ethanol, aroma recovery | Artificial kidney, HF removal | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Highlighted:** > The permeate flux is reported in **L/m²·h (LMH)** or **kg/m²·h**. Selectivity (α) is **dimensionless**. Operating pressure is typically near **atmospheric (101.325 kPa)** unless a pressurized sweep or feed is used. ## 1.4 Key Terminology - **Feed (Retentate side):** The mixture containing the target solute that is to be separated. - **Sweep (Permeate side solvent):** An immiscible liquid used to carry away the permeated species. - **Membrane:** A dense (non-porous) or microporous barrier that permits selective transport of the target component. - **Permeate:** The mixture of the target solute dissolved in the sweep solvent. - **Selectivity (α):** Ratio of permeability of the more permeable component to the less permeable one. - **Flux (J):** Rate of mass transfer per unit membrane area per unit time. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic > [!NOTE] > Perstraction operates on the principle of **solution-diffusion coupled with simultaneous extraction**. The solute does not have to be vaporized; it travels across the membrane in a dissolved state and is continuously removed by the sweep. 1. **Feed Introduction:** The liquid feed (e.g., an aqueous solution containing a target organic acid) flows on one side of a flat-sheet or hollow-fibre membrane. 2. **Membrane Contact:** The target solute preferentially **sorbs** into the membrane material at the upstream face (Feed–Membrane interface). The selectivity is determined by the solubility of the component in the membrane polymer. 3. **Diffusion Through Membrane:** The sorbed molecule **diffuses** through the membrane matrix under a chemical potential gradient. No phase change occurs. 4. **Desorption at Downstream Face:** At the membrane–sweep interface, the molecule **desorbs** from the membrane into the sweep solvent because the activity (concentration) of the solute in the sweep is kept low by continuous flow. 5. **Sweep Removal:** The sweep solvent, now loaded with the target component, is sent to a **regeneration unit** (distillation, back-extraction, or stripping) where the solute is recovered and the solvent is recycled. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why a membrane?** To provide a physical, non-dispersive interface that prevents direct mixing of feed and sweep. This eliminates issues like emulsion formation, flooding, and loading found in conventional liquid-liquid extraction columns. - **Why a sweep liquid?** To maintain a high chemical-potential gradient across the membrane. Without a sweep, the permeate side would saturate, and the driving force would collapse. - **Why no phase change?** Perstraction avoids the latent heat penalty of pervaporation, making it **energy-efficient** for recovering non-volatile or thermally sensitive components. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All symbols and equations below are **direct high-yield items** for KTU University Examination. Master every one of them. | # | Quantity / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Permeate Flux (Solution-Diffusion) | $J_i = \dfrac{P_i}{l}\,\bigl(x_{i,f}\,\gamma_{i,f} - x_{i,p}\,\gamma_{i,p}\bigr)$ | kg/m²·s or mol/m²·s | $P_i$ = permeability, $l$ = membrane thickness, $x$ = mole fraction, $\gamma$ = activity coefficient | | 2 | Permeability | $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ | m²/s | $D_i$ = diffusivity in membrane, $S_i$ = sorption coefficient | | 3 | Membrane Thickness (Resistances in Series) | $R_{tot} = R_m + R_f + R_p$ | m⁻¹ | Total resistance = membrane + feed boundary layer + permeate boundary layer | | 4 | Flux with Resistances | $J_i = \dfrac{\Delta C_i}{R_{tot}}$ | kg/m²·s | Driving concentration difference divided by total resistance | | 5 | Selectivity (Ideal) | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{P_i}{P_j} = \dfrac{D_i S_i}{D_j S_j}$ | dimensionless | $i$ = more permeable species | | 6 | Selectivity (Actual, with activities) | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{(x_i/x_j)_{p}}{(x_i/x_j)_{f}} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma_{i,p}/\gamma_{j,p}}{\gamma_{i,f}/\gamma_{j,f}}$ | dimensionless | Tie between membrane transport and equilibrium at interfaces | | 7 | Permeate Concentration (Mass Balance) | $C_{p} = C_{f}\,\bigl(1 - \exp(-kA/Q)\bigr)$ | kg/m³ | $k$ = overall mass-transfer coefficient, $A$ = membrane area, $Q$ = volumetric flow rate | | 8 | Hollow-Fibre Membrane Area | $A = n \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L$ | m² | $n$ = number of fibres, $d$ = fibre diameter, $L$ = effective length | | 9 | Mass Transfer Coefficient (Hollow Fibre, Wilson) | $Sh = 1.62\,(Re\cdot Sc\cdot d/L)^{1/3}$ | dimensionless | Lumen-side (shell-side has Leveque or similar) | | 10 | Stage Cut (θ) | $\theta = \dfrac{Q_{p}}{Q_{f}}$ | dimensionless | $Q_p$ = permeate flow, $Q_f$ = feed flow | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical industry:** Recovery of heat-sensitive antibiotics (e.g., penicillin, erythromycin) from fermentation broths using an organic sweep — avoids the thermal degradation of vacuum evaporation. - **Food & Beverage:** Dealcoholization of wine and beer; recovery of natural aroma compounds (esters, aldehydes) from fruit juices. - **Biotechnology:** In-situ product recovery in bioreactors, where toxic metabolic products (e.g., ethanol, butanol, lactic acid) are continuously stripped by a membrane-immobilized solvent. - **Wastewater Treatment:** Removal of priority pollutants (phenols, chlorinated hydrocarbons, aromatic amines) from industrial effluents. - **Petrochemical:** Separation of close-boiling isomers (e.g., $m$-/$p$-xylene) where conventional distillation fails. - **Analytical Chemistry:** Used as a sample-preparation step for trace analysis (perstraction-GC hyphenated techniques). > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" in Engineering:** In conventional LLE columns, the two liquid phases disperse and must be separated later via gravity settlers or centrifuges — this is capital and energy intensive. Perstraction replaces dispersion with a **fixed membrane interface**, which means no flooding, no entrainment, no emulsions, and a modular, compact footprint. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Permeate Flux from First Principles (Solution-Diffusion Model) The solution-diffusion model treats the membrane as a **stagnant, dense film** through which the penetrant dissolves at the upstream face and diffuses under a chemical potential gradient. **Starting premise:** At steady state, the flux $J_i$ of component $i$ through the membrane is constant (no accumulation in the membrane). ### Step 1 — Fick's First Law in the Membrane $$ J_i = -D_i \,\frac{dC_i}{dz} $$ Here, $D_i$ is the effective diffusion coefficient of species $i$ within the membrane (assumed constant for an isothermal, dilute system), and $C_i(z)$ is the local concentration of $i$ inside the membrane at depth $z$. ### Step 2 — Separate the Variables $$ J_i \, dz = -D_i \, dC_i $$ ### Step 3 — Integrate Across the Membrane Thickness Integrate from the upstream face ($z = 0$, concentration $C_{i,m,f}$) to the downstream face ($z = l$, concentration $C_{i,m,p}$): $$ J_i \int_{0}^{l} dz = -D_i \int_{C_{i,m,f}}^{C_{i,m,p}} dC_i $$ This gives: $$ J_i \, l = D_i \,\bigl(C_{i,m,f} - C_{i,m,p}\bigr) $$ Therefore: $$ J_i = \frac{D_i}{l}\,\bigl(C_{i,m,f} - C_{i,m,p}\bigr) $$ ### Step 4 — Apply Sorption Equilibrium at Both Interfaces At the membrane–fluid interfaces, the local concentration inside the membrane is related to the bulk fluid concentration via a sorption coefficient (Henry-type for dilute systems): $$ C_{i,m,f} = S_i \, C_{i,f} \quad \text{and} \quad C_{i,m,p} = S_i \, C_{i,p} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute Back $$ J_i = \frac{D_i \, S_i}{l}\,\bigl(C_{i,f} - C_{i,p}\bigr) $$ Recognizing the permeability $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$: $$ J_i = \frac{P_i}{l}\,\bigl(C_{i,f} - C_{i,p}\bigr) $$ ### Step 6 — Generalize to Mole Fractions and Activities For non-ideal mixtures, replace concentrations with activity products: $$ J_i = \frac{P_i}{l}\,\bigl(x_{i,f}\,\gamma_{i,f} - x_{i,p}\,\gamma_{i,p}\bigr) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The flux is proportional to (a) the **mobility** $D_i$ of the penetrant in the polymer, (b) the **solubility** $S_i$ at the interface, and (c) the **driving force** $(C_{i,f} - C_{i,p})$. It is **inversely proportional** to the membrane thickness $l$ — hence the industry drive toward **thin-film composite (TFC)** and **hollow-fibre** membranes. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Selectivity of the Membrane–Sweep System For a binary feed of components $i$ (more permeable) and $j$ (less permeable): ### Step 1 — Write Individual Flux Equations $$ J_i = \frac{P_i}{l}\,(C_{i,f} - C_{i,p}) \quad \text{and} \quad J_j = \frac{P_j}{l}\,(C_{j,f} - C_{j,p}) $$ ### Step 2 — Form the Ratio $$ \frac{J_i}{J_j} = \frac{P_i\,(C_{i,f} - C_{i,p})}{P_j\,(C_{j,f} - C_{j,p})} $$ ### Step 3 — Define the Separation Factor The practical selectivity of the process (combining membrane + sweep solvent effects) is: $$ \alpha_{ij} = \frac{C_{i,p}/C_{j,p}}{C_{i,f}/C_{j,f}} $$ ### Step 4 — Combine with Material Balance If the sweep is solute-free and we operate at low stage cut, $C_{i,p} \ll C_{i,f}$, so $(C_{i,f} - C_{i,p}) \approx C_{i,f}$. Then: $$ \frac{J_i}{J_j} \approx \frac{P_i}{P_j}\,\frac{C_{i,f}}{C_{j,f}} $$ Equating this ratio to the permeate composition ratio gives the **ideal selectivity**: $$ \alpha_{ij} = \frac{P_i}{P_j} = \frac{D_i S_i}{D_j S_j} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Take-away for KTU:** Selectivity is governed by **two independent factors** — the diffusivity selectivity (kinetic, controlled by polymer free volume and penetrant size) and the sorption selectivity (thermodynamic, controlled by membrane–penetrant interaction). This dual nature is what makes perstraction a designer process. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass-Transfer Resistance Model (Resistance-in-Series) In real perstraction modules, the feed and sweep liquids also have **boundary-layer resistances** outside the membrane. ### Step 1 — Define Individual Resistances - $R_m = l/P_i$ : Membrane resistance - $R_f = 1/k_f$ : Feed-side boundary layer resistance - $R_s = 1/k_s$ : Sweep-side boundary layer resistance ### Step 2 — Write Flux in Terms of Overall Driving Force $$ J_i = \frac{C_{i,f}^{bulk} - C_{i,s}^{bulk}}{R_m + R_f + R_s} $$ ### Step 3 — Express Total Resistance $$ R_{tot} = \frac{l}{P_i} + \frac{1}{k_f} + \frac{1}{k_s} $$ ### Step 4 — Calculate Individual Contributions For a hydrophobic membrane separating an aqueous feed of an organic solute into an oil sweep: - $R_m$ typically dominates (50–80 % of total) - $R_f$ depends on cross-flow velocity - $R_s$ depends on sweep viscosity and diffusivity > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Increasing the **cross-flow velocity** in the feed channel reduces $R_f$ and increases $J_i$, but only up to a practical limit set by the pumping cost and the risk of membrane compaction. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Perstraction with Other Membrane Processes | Process | Phase Change? | Selectivity Source | Energy Intensity | Typical α | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Perstraction** | No | Membrane + sweep solvent | **Low** | 10 – 500 | | **Pervaporation** | Yes (liquid → vapor) | Membrane + vapor–liquid equilibrium | Medium (latent heat) | 5 – 10,000 | | **Reverse Osmosis** | No | Solution-diffusion, size exclusion | High (high pressure) | 1 – 10 | | **Dialysis** | No | Pore size / charge | Very Low | 2 – 50 | | **Gas Separation** | No (gas → gas) | Diffusivity + sorption | Low | 5 – 100 | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem (KTU style, 14-mark standard):** > A perstraction module is used to recover phenol from an aqueous feed containing $5.0 \times 10^{-2}$ kg phenol/m³ using an octanol sweep. The membrane has thickness $l = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m, permeability of phenol in the silicone-rubber membrane is $P_{phenol} = 6.0 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s, and the sweep-side phenol concentration can be assumed negligible. The membrane area is $A = 1.0$ m². Calculate the steady-state flux of phenol and the recovery rate in kg/h. **Solution:** ### Step 1 — Identify Driving Force Since $C_{i,p} \approx 0$ (high sweep rate), the driving force is: $$ \Delta C = C_{i,f} - C_{i,p} = 5.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Permeate Flux Equation $$ J_{phenol} = \frac{P_{phenol}}{l}\,\Delta C = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-10}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-2} $$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate Numerically $$ J_{phenol} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-2} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} $$ ### Step 4 — Calculate Recovery Rate $$ \dot{m} = J \cdot A = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0 = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{kg/s} $$ Convert to kg/h: $$ \dot{m} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 1.08 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{kg/h} $$ > **Answer:** $J_{phenol} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/(m²·s) and $\dot{m} = 1.08 \times 10^{-2}$ kg/h. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students forget to convert seconds to hours in the final step. Marks are specifically allotted for **correct unit conversions**. Always write units at every stage. # 4.
