topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chromatography: Classification of Techniques - Principle and Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chromatography** is a physical method of separation, identification, and quantification of the components of a mixture, in which the components to be separated are distributed between two immiscible phases: a **stationary phase** (fixed bed or immobilized liquid film) and a **mobile phase** (gas, liquid, or supercritical fluid) that percolates through or over the stationary phase.

The technique fundamentally relies on the principle that **different solutes have different affinities** for the two phases, leading to differential migration rates and hence separation. It is a **dynamic mass transfer operation** that is governed by equilibria, kinetics, and transport phenomena.

### The Three Universal Entities in Any Chromatographic System

1. **Stationary Phase** — Immobilized; offers selective resistance/sorption.
2. **Mobile Phase** — Mobile; acts as the carrier (eluent) transporting the solute.
3. **Analyte / Solute** — The species to be separated from the mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (KTU Curious Corner):** The term *chromatography* was coined by the Russian-Italian botanist **Mikhail Tsvet (1906)**. He separated plant pigments (chlorophyll, xanthophylls, carotenes) using a calcium carbonate column, producing colored bands — hence the name *chroma* (color) + *graphein* (to write).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "River Raft" Intuition

Imagine a river flowing over a bed of stones. Drop a mixture of objects (a leaf, a wooden ball, a heavy stone) at the upstream end:

- The **leaf** is carried quickly by the water because it has no affinity for the stones.
- The **wooden ball** tumbles slowly — it interacts weakly with the stones.
- The **heavy stone** barely moves — it has very high affinity for the rocky bed.

After a given time, the three objects emerge at different downstream positions, having been **separated spatially**. The river is the **mobile phase**, the rocky bed is the **stationary phase**, and the objects are the **analytes**. Chromatography is the exact physical realization of this natural sorting process, controlled with engineering precision.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** The **selectivity factor (α)** of the system is analogous to the size difference between the objects and the stones. A larger size difference (higher α) → easier, sharper separation.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in Chromatography

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Retention Time | $t_R$ | seconds to minutes |
| Dead Time | $t_M$ | seconds to minutes |
| Adjusted Retention Time | $t_R'$ | seconds to minutes |
| Retention Volume | $V_R$ | mL or L |
| Plate Height (HETP) | $H$ | **μm** to mm |
| Number of Theoretical Plates | $N$ | dimensionless |
| Selectivity Factor | $\alpha$ | $\geq 1$ (dimensionless) |
| Resolution | $R_s$ | dimensionless |
| Distribution Coefficient | $K$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> All of these are **board-exam favorites**. Each appears at least once in the KTU model question paper for Mass Transfer Operations II.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Mass Transfer Heart of Chromatography

The separation in chromatography is governed by the **differential distribution of solutes** between the two phases. At the microscopic level, this involves **three sequential mass transfer steps** that occur repeatedly as the solute band travels through the column:

1. **External Mass Transfer** — Solute diffuses from the bulk mobile phase to the **external surface** of the stationary phase particles.
2. **Internal (Pore) Diffusion** — Solute diffuses through the **pores** of the stationary phase particles to the active sites.
3. **Sorption / Desorption at Active Site** — Actual **adsorption, partition, ion-exchange, or sieving** takes place at the molecular interface.

The process reverses during the regeneration half-cycle. The **overall rate** is governed by the slowest step (rate-limiting step).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The distribution of solute between phases at equilibrium is quantified by the **distribution (or partition) coefficient**:
> $$ K = \frac{C_s}{C_m} $$
> where $C_s$ = concentration in stationary phase (kg solute / kg stationary) and $C_m$ = concentration in mobile phase (kg solute / kg mobile). **Higher $K$ → solute is held back longer → higher $t_R$.**

---

## 2.2 Classification of Chromatographic Techniques (KTU Board Priority Topic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires a **multi-level classification**. We organize it under four orthogonal classification schemes:

### **A. Classification Based on the Physical State of the Mobile Phase**

| Technique | Mobile Phase | Stationary Phase | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas Chromatography (GC)** | Gas (He, N₂, H₂) | Liquid film on solid support or solid | Volatile organics, petroleum products |
| **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** | Liquid (water, organic solvents) | Liquid film or bonded phase on solid | Pharmaceuticals, biomolecules |
| **Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC)** | Supercritical CO₂ | Bonded silica | Thermally labile, non-volatile analytes |

### **B. Classification Based on the Shape of the Chromatographic Bed**

| Technique | Bed Geometry | Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Planar Chromatography** | Flat (paper or thin-layer plate) | Paper Chromatography (PC), Thin-Layer Chromatography (TLC) |
| **Column Chromatography** | Cylindrical packed tube | Packed Column, Open Tubular (Capillary) |

### **C. Classification Based on the Mechanism of Separation (KTU High-Yield)**

| Mechanism | Governing Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Adsorption Chromatography** | Differential **adsorption** on solid surface (silica, alumina) | Separation of non-polar lipids, hydrocarbons |
| **Partition Chromatography** | Differential **solubility** between two immiscible liquids | HPLC reverse-phase separation of organics |
| **Ion-Exchange Chromatography** | Differential **Coulombic attraction** to charged groups | Water softening, amino acid separation |
| **Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC / GPC)** | Differential **molecular size** — pore diffusion | Polymer molecular weight distribution |
| **Affinity Chromatography** | Specific **biochemical lock-and-key** binding | Enzyme purification, antibody isolation |
| **Chiral Chromatography** | Differential interaction with **chiral stationary phase** | Enantiomer separation in pharmaceuticals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Trap:** A column can simultaneously use **more than one mechanism** (e.g., reverse-phase HPLC involves both partition and adsorption). The classification is by the **dominant** mechanism.

### **D. Classification Based on the Mode of Development (Elution Mode)**

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Elution Development** | Continuous flow of pure mobile phase; sample is injected as a narrow band; components emerge as separated bands. **Most common in industry & KTU exams.** |
| **Frontal Development** | Continuous feed of the sample mixture; least-retained component emerges pure first; later components co-elute. Used in trace impurity removal. |
| **Displacement Development** | After sample loading, a displacer stronger than all sample components is introduced; pushes components into adjacent sharp bands. Highest throughput, sharpest separation. |

---

## 2.3 The Chromatographic Process — Step-by-Step Engineering Picture

A typical **column chromatographic separation** follows this operational sequence:

1. **Column Preparation (Packing)** — Stationary phase is uniformly packed into a vertical glass/metal column. A frit or glass wool retains the packing.
2. **Equilibration** — Mobile phase is flushed through until the stationary phase is saturated and the column baseline is stable.
3. **Sample Injection** — A small, sharp pulse of the sample mixture is introduced at the column inlet.
4. **Elution / Development** — Continuous mobile-phase flow carries sample components through the column. Each component moves at its characteristic velocity, governed by its $K$.
5. **Detection** — Components eluting at the column outlet pass through a detector (UV, RI, FID, MS), producing a **chromatogram** (signal vs. time).
6. **Quantification** — Peak area (or height) is proportional to component concentration. Identification is by **retention time match** with a standard.
7. **Column Regeneration** — After separation, the column is regenerated by stripping strongly retained species, returning to initial state.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Distribution Coefficient | $K = C_s / C_m$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Retention Factor (k′) | $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M = K \cdot (\beta)$ | $\beta$ = phase ratio |
| 3 | Selectivity Factor | $\alpha = K_2 / K_1 = k_2' / k_1'$ (for $\alpha \geq 1$) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Number of Theoretical Plates | $N = 16 \cdot (t_R / w)^2$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Plate Height (HETP) | $H = L / N$ | Length (typically cm or mm) |
| 6 | Resolution | $R_s = 2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})/(w_1 + w_2)$ | $\geq 1.5$ for baseline |
| 7 | Resolution – Master Equation | $R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'}$ | KTU Hallmark Equation |
| 8 | van Deemter Equation | $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ | $u$ = linear velocity |
| 9 | Capacity Factor Relation | $t_R = t_M (1 + k')$ | $t_M$ = dead time |
| 10 | Linear Velocity (optimum) | $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$ | From van Deemter |

> [!NOTE]
> ⚠ **CRITICAL MARKDOWN NOTE:** In the table above, the absolute value bars are deliberately avoided in formulas to keep the table parser safe. In LaTeX, write `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` if you must.

> [!TIP]
> The **Resolution Master Equation** ($R_s$ formula) is the single most-tested equation in KTU board exams for chromatography. Memorize the three multipliers: **column efficiency, selectivity, retention**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Chromatography is the **workhorse of the modern chemical, pharmaceutical, food, petroleum, and biotechnology industries**:

- **Petroleum Industry:** GC separates and quantifies hydrocarbons in crude oil, gasoline, and diesel.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** HPLC purifies Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) and detects impurities at ppm levels.
- **Food Industry:** Detects additives, pesticides, mycotoxins in food products.
- **Biotechnology:** Affinity chromatography isolates monoclonal antibodies and vaccines (e.g., COVID-19 vaccine purification).
- **Environmental Engineering:** Detects trace pollutants in water and air (PAHs, PCBs, VOCs).
- **Forensic Science:** Identifies drugs, poisons, and trace evidence.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Plate Theory — Quantitative Foundation

The **Plate Theory** (Martin & Synge, 1941) models the chromatographic column as a series of $N$ discrete theoretical plates, each behaving as a tiny equilibrium stage.

### Step 1: Material Balance on the n-th Plate

Consider the $n$-th plate at time $t$. The mass of solute in the **mobile phase** is $M \cdot p_n$ and in the **stationary phase** is $M \cdot q_n$, where $p_n, q_n$ are mass fractions.

**Conservation of solute** as mobile phase volume $\Delta V$ moves from plate $n$ to plate $n+1$:

$$
M \cdot p_n = M \cdot p_{n+1} + M \cdot (q_{n+1} - q_n)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
p_n - p_{n+1} = q_{n+1} - q_n
$$

### Step 2: Equilibrium Relation Between Phases

Assuming instantaneous local equilibrium with a linear isotherm:

$$
q_n = K \cdot p_n
$$

Substituting into the balance:

$$
p_n - p_{n+1} = K \cdot (p_{n+1} - p_n)
$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Ratio

$$
p_n - p_{n+1} = K p_{n+1} - K p_n
$$

$$
p_n (1 + K) = p_{n+1} (1 + K)
$$

$$
\frac{p_{n+1}}{p_n} = \frac{1}{1 + K} = \frac{1}{1 + k'}
$$

This is the **transfer fraction** per plate. For a column with $N$ plates, the cumulative retention is:

$$
\frac{p_N}{p_0} = \left( \frac{1}{1+k'} \right)^{N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows that **higher $N$ (better column efficiency)** and **higher $k'$ (stronger retention)** both sharpen the band. The exponential dependence on $N$ is the heart of column efficiency.

### Step 4: Connection to Plate Number

A Gaussian peak eluting from such a column has variance $\sigma^2 = N (1+k')^2 / k'^2$. Relating peak width to standard deviation ($w = 4\sigma$):

$$
N = 16 \left( \frac{t_R}{w} \right)^2
$$

$$
H = \frac{L}{N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Tangential Derivation** for KTU. The full Gaussian-based derivation is given below.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The van Deemter Equation (Rate Theory)

The van Deemter equation describes **band broadening** as a function of mobile phase velocity. It is the sum of three independent contributions:

$$
H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u
$$

### Step 1: Eddy Diffusion Term ($A$)

Different flow paths through the packed bed have different lengths. Solute molecules in different paths take different times to traverse the column, broadening the band.

$$
H_{eddy} = A = 2 \lambda d_p
$$

where $\lambda$ = packing irregularity factor, $d_p$ = particle diameter.

### Step 2: Longitudinal (Molecular) Diffusion Term ($B/u$)

Solute diffuses axially (along flow direction) due to concentration gradients. The contribution:

$$
H_{long} = \frac{B}{u} = \frac{2 \gamma D_m}{u}
$$

where $D_m$ = molecular diffusion coefficient in mobile phase, $\gamma$ = obstruction factor.

### Step 3: Mass Transfer Resistance Term ($C \cdot u$)

Finite rate of mass transfer between phases. Two sub-contributions:

$$
C = C_s + C_m
$$

$$
C_s = \frac{2 k' \cdot d_p^2}{3 (1 + k')^2 \cdot D_s}
$$

$$
C_m = \frac{0.01 k'^2 \cdot d_p^2}{(1+k')^2 \cdot D_m}
$$

where $D_s$ = diffusion coefficient in stationary phase.

### Step 4: Optimum Velocity and Minimum Plate Height

Differentiate $H(u)$ with respect to $u$ and set $dH/du = 0$:

$$
\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0
$$

$$
u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
H_{min} = A + 2 \sqrt{B \cdot C}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The **optimum velocity** point is the most efficient operating point. KTU examiners love asking: *"At what flow rate is the column most efficient?"* — Answer: at $u_{opt}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resolution Master Equation

**Resolution** quantifies the **degree of separation between two adjacent peaks** in a chromatogram.

### Step 1: Definition

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5 (w_1 + w_2)}
$$

For closely eluting peaks, $w_1 \approx w_2 = w$:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{w} = \frac{\Delta t_R}{w}
$$

### Step 2: Express in Terms of $N$, $\alpha$, $k'$

Using the relation $w = 4 t_R / \sqrt{N}$ and $t_{R2} - t_{R1} = t_{M} (k_2' - k_1')$:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_M (k_2' - k_1')}{4 t_R / \sqrt{N}} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k_2' - k_1'}{1 + k_2'}
$$

### Step 3: Introduce Selectivity Factor

Since $\alpha = k_2' / k_1'$:

$$
k_2' - k_1' = k_2' \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\alpha} \right) = k_2' \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}
$$

For closely eluting peaks, $k_2' \approx k' = (k_1' + k_2')/2$:

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Resolution Master Equation**. The three bracketed terms represent the **three engineering knobs** to improve separation: **column efficiency ($N$), selectivity ($\alpha$), retention ($k'$).**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Improve Resolution

| Knob | Term in $R_s$ | How to Increase | Engineering Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ (Column Efficiency) | $\sqrt{N}/4$ | Longer column, smaller particles | Higher pressure, longer analysis time |
| $\alpha$ (Selectivity) | $(\alpha - 1)/\alpha$ | Change stationary phase, change mobile phase composition, change temperature | Re-validation of method |
| $k'$ (Retention) | $k'/(1+k')$ | Adjust mobile phase strength | Limited range (optimum $k' = 2$ to 10) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Increasing $k'$ beyond ~10 gives **diminishing returns** because $k'/(1+k') \to 1$. Beyond this, $R_s$ improvement is negligible but analysis time becomes prohibitively long.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2023]:** Two components A and B have retention times 6.0 min and 7.2 min, respectively, with peak widths (at base) of 0.5 min and 0.6 min. The dead time $t_M$ is 1.0 min. Calculate:
> (i) Resolution $R_s$
> (ii) Number of theoretical plates $N$ for component B
> (iii) Selectivity factor $\alpha$
> (iv) Retention factor $k'$ for component B

### Solution:

**Part (i) — Resolution:**

$$
R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2} = \frac{2(7.2 - 6.0)}{0.5 + 0.6}
$$

$$
R_s = \frac{2(1.2)}{1.1} = \frac{2.4}{1.1} = 2.18
$$

Since $R_s > 1.5$, **baseline separation is achieved**. ✅

**Part (ii) — Plates for Component B:**

$$
N_B = 16 \left( \frac{t_{R,B}}{w_B} \right)^2 = 16 \left( \frac{7.2}{0.6} \right)^2
$$

$$
N_B = 16 \times (12)^2 = 16 \times 144 = 2304 \text{ plates}
$$

**Part (iii) — Selectivity Factor:**

First compute retention factors:

$$
k_A' = \frac{t_{R,A} - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{6.0 - 1.0}{1.0} = 5.0
$$

$$
k_B' = \frac{t_{R,B} - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{7.2 - 1.0}{1.0} = 6.2
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{k_B'}{k_A'} = \frac{6.2}{5.0} = 1.24
$$

**Part (iv) — Retention Factor of B:** Already computed as $k_B' = 6.2$. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The $N$ value of ~2300 is typical of a well-packed HPLC column. The selectivity $\alpha = 1.24$ is moderate; chemical engineering methods can push it to 1.5+ to dramatically improve $R_s$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

> **Define chromatography. List and briefly explain any two mechanisms by which components are separated in chromatographic techniques.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Chromatography is a physical separation technique in which the components of a mixture are distributed between two phases — a **stationary phase** (immobilized) and a **mobile phase** (moving) — and separated based on their differential affinities for the two phases.

**Mechanism 1 — Adsorption:** Components are retained on the surface of a solid stationary phase (e.g., silica, alumina) due to **differential adsorption** strengths. More strongly adsorbed species move slower; less strongly adsorbed species elute first. (1.5 marks)

**Mechanism 2 — Partition:** Components distribute between two immiscible liquids (one mobile, one stationary) according to their **differential solubilities**. A species with higher solubility in the stationary phase is retained longer. This is the mechanism in reverse-phase HPLC. (1.5 marks)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

> **What is meant by 'retention time' and 'dead time' in chromatography? Why is the difference between them significant?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Retention Time ($t_R$):** The total time a solute spends in the column from injection to detection at the peak maximum. (1 mark)
- **Dead Time ($t_M$):** Also called *hold-up time*; the time an unretained species (one with zero interaction with stationary phase, e.g., air in GC) takes to traverse the column. (1 mark)

**Significance of the Difference ($t_R' = t_R - t_M$):**

The **adjusted retention time** $t_R' = t_R - t_M$ isolates the time the solute *actually interacted* with the stationary phase, excluding the time spent merely traveling with the mobile phase. It is the direct measure of the **strength of interaction** with the stationary phase and is used to compute the retention factor $k' = t_R'/t_M$. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme: *Each Part B question carries 14 marks. Sub-division is typically 7 + 7. Internal choice is mandatory.*

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Classify chromatographic techniques based on (i) the physical state of the mobile phase, (ii) the mechanism of separation, and (iii) the mode of development. Give **two examples** under each classification.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Resolution Master Equation** for chromatography and explain how each term can be engineered to improve separation.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i) Classification by Mobile Phase State** (2 marks):
- **Gas Chromatography (GC):** Mobile phase is a carrier gas (He, N₂, H₂). Example: GLC (Gas-Liquid Chromatography) for hydrocarbons; GSC for permanent gases.
- **Liquid Chromatography (LC/HPLC):** Mobile phase is a liquid (water, methanol, acetonitrile). Example: HPLC for pharmaceuticals; Ion Chromatography for anions.
- **Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC):** Mobile phase is supercritical CO₂. Example: chiral separations; lipid analysis.

**(ii) Classification by Mechanism** (3 marks):
- **Adsorption:** Differential surface adsorption. Example: TLC on silica for dye separation.
- **Partition:** Differential solubility between liquids. Example: Reverse-phase HPLC of organic acids.
- **Ion-Exchange:** Differential Coulombic attraction. Example: Water demineralization; amino acid analysis.
- **Size-Exclusion:** Differential molecular size. Example: GPC for polymer molecular weight distribution.
- **Affinity:** Specific biochemical binding. Example: Protein A chromatography for antibody purification.

**(iii) Classification by Development Mode** (2 marks):
- **Elution Development:** Pure mobile phase flows continuously. Example: Standard HPLC analysis.
- **Frontal Development:** Sample mixture is fed continuously. Example: Trace impurity removal from process streams.
- **Displacement Development:** A displacer is added after sample. Example: Industrial-scale bioseparations.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Derivation of the Resolution Master Equation:**

Starting from the definition of resolution for two adjacent peaks 1 and 2:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5 (w_1 + w_2)}
$$

For closely-spaced peaks where $w_1 \approx w_2 = w$:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{w} \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

From the plate theory, the peak width is related to retention time and plate number:

$$
w = \frac{4 t_R}{\sqrt{N}} \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

Substituting (2) into (1) and using $t_R \approx t_{R2}$ for the closer-eluting species:

$$
R_s = \frac{(t_{R2} - t_{R1}) \sqrt{N}}{4 t_{R2}} \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

The retention times are related to the retention factors: $t_R = t_M (1 + k')$. Thus:

$$
t_{R2} - t_{R1} = t_M (k_2' - k_1')
$$

Substituting into (3):

$$
R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k_2' - k_1'}{1 + k_2'} \quad \text{...(4)}
$$

Using the definition of selectivity $\alpha = k_2' / k_1'$ (with $k_2' > k_1'$), we obtain $k_2' - k_1' = k_2'(\alpha - 1)/\alpha$. For close peaks, $k_2' \approx (k_1' + k_2')/2 = k'$:

$$
\boxed{R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'}}
$$

**Engineering each term:** (3 marks for the explanation)

1. **$\sqrt{N}/4$ — Column Efficiency:** Increase $N$ by using a longer column, smaller stationary phase particles (e.g., sub-2 μm), or lower viscosity mobile phase. Doubling $N$ increases $R_s$ by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$. **Cost:** Higher pressure drop, longer analysis time.

2. **$(\alpha - 1)/\alpha$ — Selectivity:** Change the chemical nature of stationary phase or mobile phase, or change temperature. Small changes in $\alpha$ near 1 produce large changes in $R_s$. **Cost:** Method re-validation required.

3. **$k'/(1+k')$ — Retention:** Adjust mobile phase strength. Optimal range $k' = 2$ to $10$. Below 2, peaks elute too fast and overlap; above 10, analysis time is excessive.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a typical **Gas Chromatograph**. Discuss the role of the **stationary phase, mobile phase, injector, column oven, and detector** in achieving separation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A packed GC column of length 2.0 m shows a plate height of 0.50 mm at the optimum flow rate. The retention times of two components A and B are 4.8 min and 5.2 min, respectively, with peak widths of 0.40 min and 0.42 min. The dead time is 1.0 min. Calculate: (i) Number of theoretical plates (ii) Resolution between A and B (iii) Selectivity factor (iv) Retention factor of B.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

A Gas Chromatograph consists of the following subsystems, each playing a critical role:

| Component | Role | Typical Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **Carrier Gas (Mobile Phase)** | Inert gas that transports sample through column without chemical interaction | He, N₂, or H₂ at high purity |
| **Sample Injector** | Vaporizes liquid sample and introduces it as a narrow pulse | Split / Splitless injector at 200–300 °C |
| **Column** | Site where separation actually occurs; coated with stationary phase | Packed (3–6 m) or Capillary (15–60 m) |
| **Column Oven** | Provides precise isothermal or temperature-programmed heating | ±0.1 °C stability |
| **Detector** | Senses eluting components and produces electrical signal | FID, TCD, MS, ECD |

**Working Principle:** (3 marks of text)

1. Carrier gas flows at constant rate through the chromatographic column housed in a temperature-controlled oven.
2. Liquid sample is injected via a micro-syringe into the heated injector, where it vaporizes instantly.
3. The vapor is swept by the carrier gas as a narrow plug into the column head.
4. Inside the column, components partition between the **stationary liquid phase** (coated on solid support) and the **mobile gas phase**. A component with **higher solubility in the liquid phase** has higher $K$ and is **retained longer**.
5. As the temperature is programmed to rise, components elute in order of increasing boiling point.
6. The detector at the column outlet produces a chromatogram — a plot of detector signal vs. time — with each component appearing as a peak.

**Role of Each Part:**

- **Stationary phase** provides the **selective interaction** that gives different $K$ values for different analytes.
- **Mobile phase** serves as the **inert carrier**; it does not contribute to selectivity.
- **Injector** ensures sample enters as a **narrow, well-defined band** (bandwidth minimization at inlet).
- **Column oven** controls temperature — **temperature programming** is used for mixtures with a wide boiling-point range.
- **Detector** provides the **quantitative signal** proportional to component mass.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Column length $L = 2.0$ m = 2000 mm
- Plate height $H = 0.50$ mm
- $t_{R,A} = 4.8$ min, $t_{R,B} = 5.2$ min
- $w_A = 0.40$ min, $w_B = 0.42$ min
- Dead time $t_M = 1.0$ min

**Part (i) — Number of Theoretical Plates:**

$$
N = \frac{L}{H} = \frac{2000 \text{ mm}}{0.50 \text{ mm}} = 4000 \text{ plates}
$$

**Part (ii) — Resolution:**

$$
R_s = \frac{2(t_{R,B} - t_{R,A})}{w_A + w_B} = \frac{2(5.2 - 4.8)}{0.40 + 0.42}
$$

$$
R_s = \frac{2(0.4)}{0.82} = \frac{0.8}{0.82} = 0.976
$$

Since $R_s < 1.5$, **baseline separation is NOT achieved** ❌. The components are partially overlapping.

**Part (iii) — Selectivity Factor:**

$$
k_A' = \frac{t_{R,A} - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{4.8 - 1.0}{1.0} = 3.8
$$

$$
k_B' = \frac{t_{R,B} - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{5.2 - 1.0}{1.0} = 4.2
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{k_B'}{k_A'} = \frac{4.2}{3.8} = 1.105
$$

**Part (iv) — Retention Factor of B:**

$$
k_B' = 4.2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake students make is using **$L$ in meters** with $H$ in **mm** without unit conversion. Always check that $L$ and $H$ are in **the same units** before dividing. Also, in Resolution formula, students often forget the factor of 2 in the numerator — losing 1 full mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

### **A. Conceptual Pillars**

- ✅ Chromatography = separation based on **differential distribution** between two phases.
- ✅ **Three entities** are always present: **stationary phase, mobile phase, analyte**.
- ✅ **Tsvet (1906)** coined the term while separating plant pigments.

### **B. Classification Mastery**

- ✅ By **Mobile Phase:** GC, LC/HPLC, SFC.
- ✅ By **Bed Shape:** Planar (PC, TLC), Column (Packed, Capillary).
- ✅ By **Mechanism:** Adsorption, Partition, Ion-Exchange, Size-Exclusion, Affinity, Chiral.
- ✅ By **Development:** Elution (most common), Frontal, Displacement.

### **C. Formula Arsenal (Top 7)**

- ✅ $K = C_s / C_m$
- ✅ $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$
- ✅ $\alpha = k_2'/k_1'$
- ✅ $N = 16 (t_R/w)^2$
- ✅ $H = L/N$
- ✅ $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4) \cdot [(\alpha-1)/\alpha] \cdot [k'/(1+k')]$
- ✅ $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ (van Deemter) with $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$

### **D. Engineering Knobs to Improve Resolution**

- ✅ Increase **$N$** → longer column, smaller $d_p$, lower viscosity mobile phase.
- ✅ Increase **$\alpha$** → change chemistry, temperature, mobile phase composition.
- ✅ Optimize **$k'$** → adjust mobile phase strength, keep $k'$ in **2 to 10** range.
- ✅ Optimum velocity $u_{opt}$ → from van Deemter analysis.

### **E. Quick Numerical Traps to Avoid**

- ⚠ Always check **unit consistency** for $L$ and $H$.
- ⚠ Resolution $< 1.5$ = no baseline separation; Resolution $\geq 1.5$ = baseline separation.
- ⚠ $N$ depends on the **specific component's peak**; compute $N$ separately for each peak.
- ⚠ Selectivity $\alpha \geq 1$ always (definition uses larger $k'$ in numerator).

### **F. Real-World Connection**

- ✅ GC → petroleum, environmental VOCs, forensic drugs.
- ✅ HPLC → pharmaceuticals, food safety, biochemistry.
- ✅ Affinity → antibody/vaccine purification (industrial biotech).
- ✅ Ion-exchange → water softening, nuclear decontamination.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU Exam:** Memorize the **Resolution Master Equation** derivation step-by-step, the **van Deemter equation** with all three terms explained, and be ready to draw a **labeled sketch of a Gas Chromatograph**. These three items together cover approximately **70% of all marks** asked from this topic in previous KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Elution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types - Elution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Elution

In the context of **Mass Transfer Operations**, **Elution** is a unit operation in which a solute (the **solute** or **eluate**) is selectively transferred from a solid matrix into a flowing liquid solvent (the **eluent** or **eluting solvent**). The solid acts as the inert carrier holding the solute (typically by physical adsorption or absorption in its pores), and the solvent sweeps the solute out of the solid by establishing a concentration gradient that drives the diffusion of solute molecules from the bulk solid phase into the bulk liquid phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Elution is the process of removing a solute from a solid by bringing it into intimate contact with a liquid solvent. It is essentially the reverse of absorption (in solid–liquid systems). The driving force is the concentration gradient of the solute between the solid (where solute is initially rich) and the solvent (where solute is initially lean or zero).*

The mass transfer mechanism in elution involves three sequential steps:
1. **External mass transfer** – convective transport of solute from the bulk solvent to the external surface of the solid particle.
2. **Internal mass transfer** – diffusion of solute through the pores of the solid (often described by the **effective diffusivity** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{eff}}$, with units of **m²/s**).
3. **Equilibrium at the interface** – partitioning of solute between the solid and liquid phases, governed by an **equilibrium distribution coefficient** $K_D$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Coffee Cup Analogy"

Imagine you have a sponge soaked with a sugary syrup. If you simply place the sponge in a cup of clean water:
- Initially, the water in the cup is colorless and clear (low sugar concentration).
- The syrup is concentrated inside the sponge.
- Nature spontaneously drives the syrup out of the sponge and into the water, because molecules move from **high concentration** → **low concentration**.
- After sufficient time, the syrup concentration inside the sponge and outside in the water becomes uniform — this is **equilibrium**.

The sponge is your **solid matrix**, the syrup is your **solute**, and the water is your **eluent**. By using the water repeatedly or continuously, you effectively "extract" the syrup from the sponge. That is **Elution**!

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world industrial applications of Elution:**
> - Recovery of gold from activated carbon using hot caustic-cyanide solution (the **Zadra process**).
> - Desorption of **bioactive molecules (e.g., antibiotics, enzymes)** from ion-exchange resins.
> - **Decaffeination** of coffee beans using supercritical CO₂ or organic solvents.
> - Purification of **sugars, vitamins, and pharmaceuticals** from chromatographic columns.
> - **Soil remediation** – washing contaminated soil to leach out heavy metals.

## 1.3 Classification of Elution Types

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, the term "Types – Elution" refers to the classification of elution equipment/processes based on the **flow arrangement and number of stages** used to contact the solvent with the solute-bearing solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Classification – Types of Elution:**
> 1. **Single-Stage Elution (One ideal stage)**
> 2. **Multi-Stage Cross-Current Elution (Co-current, parallel flow)**
> 3. **Multi-Stage Counter-Current Elution (Most efficient, industrial preferred)**
> 4. **Differential (Continuous) Contact Elution** (e.g., in packed/rotating columns, chromatographic columns)

The selection of a particular type depends on:
- **Recovery efficiency** desired.
- **Solvent availability** (solvent cost).
- **Solid-to-liquid (S/L) ratio** in feed.
- **Equilibrium distribution** between solute in solid vs. liquid.
- **Final product concentration** requirements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of Each Type

### 2.1.1 Single-Stage Elution
A **single contacting device** (an ideal mixer-settler or tank) is used. The entire batch of solute-laden solid is contacted with the **entire batch of fresh solvent** in one equilibrium stage. After thorough mixing, the slurry is separated into a **clarified solvent-rich extract (overflow)** and a **washed solid (underflow)**.

- **Merits**: Simplest design, low capital cost, low operating complexity.
- **Demerits**: Lowest solute recovery; high solvent consumption per unit mass of solute recovered; final raffinate (residual solid) still holds substantial solute unless distribution strongly favors the liquid.
- **Industrial use**: Only when solute is highly soluble or solid is expensive to retain.

### 2.1.2 Multi-Stage Cross-Current Elution
The solid is contacted with **fresh solvent in each stage**, and the extract from each stage is collected separately. The solid moves from stage 1 → stage 2 → stage 3, etc., while each stage receives **fresh solvent**.

- **Merits**: Higher recovery than single-stage; relatively simple flow scheme.
- **Demerits**: Solvent consumption is high because each stage uses fresh solvent; large total solvent volume → dilute final extract.
- **Industrial use**: Laboratory-scale leaching of ores, batch pharmaceutical purification.

### 2.1.3 Multi-Stage Counter-Current Elution
The solid and the solvent flow in **opposite directions**. The solute-rich solid enters at one end and the **fresh solvent** enters at the other. The most solute-rich solvent (final extract) leaves one end, while the most solute-depleted solid (spent solid) leaves the opposite end.

- **Merits**: Highest solute recovery for a given solvent-to-solid ratio; lowest solvent consumption; most concentrated final extract.
- **Demerits**: Higher capital cost; more complex control (requires pumps/elevators for solid transport, e.g., bucket elevators, screw conveyors).
- **Industrial use**: Standard for **gold–cyanide elution**, **bauxite washing in Bayer process**, **sugar beet diffusion**, **oilseed extraction (multistage)**.

### 2.1.4 Differential (Continuous) Contact Elution
The solid and solvent move continuously in **counter-current contact** in a vertical tower (e.g., **Bollman extractor, Hildebrandt extractor, Rotocel extractor** for oilseeds; **CCD – Continuous Counter-Current Decanter** for sugar; **packed chromatographic columns** for biochemicals).

- **Merits**: Operates continuously, very high mass transfer efficiency, lower solvent inventory.
- **Demerits**: Modeling requires the **Péclet number** and stage-efficiency corrections; sensitive to solid feed consistency.

## 2.2 Why Counter-Current is Superior – The "Lever-Arm" Argument

In cross-current, every stage receives fresh solvent, but the solvent in the **last stage** only sees solid that is **already nearly stripped**. So most of its solvent-solute carrying capacity is **wasted**. In counter-current, the **fresh solvent contacts the already nearly-stripped solid**, maximizing the driving force where it matters most — and the **fresh solid (rich in solute) contacts the solute-rich solvent**, which still has a substantial equilibrium deficit.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for KTU exam:** "Counter-current elution uses solvent most efficiently — like drying your hands with a single towel where you start at the cleanest part and move toward the dirtiest, instead of using one fresh towel per finger!"

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

The following table contains every key equation you must memorize for Module 4 – Types of Elution.

| **#** | **Quantity / Stage** | **Governing Equation** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solvent-to-Solid ratio** | $R = \dfrac{L}{S}$ | dimensionless (kg solvent/kg solid) | $L$ = solvent mass, $S$ = solute-free solid mass |
| 2 | **Single-stage equilibrium** (extract concentration) | $y_1 = \dfrac{x_0 \,-\, (S/L)\,x_1}{1}$ or, with $K_D = y/x$: | – | Mass balance |
| 3 | **Single-stage exit solid concentration** | $x_1 = \dfrac{x_0}{1 \,+\, (L/S)\,K_D}$ | kg solute/kg solid | $x_0$ = initial solid concentration |
| 4 | **Single-stage exit liquid concentration** | $y_1 = K_D \cdot x_1 = \dfrac{K_D \, x_0}{1 \,+\, (L/S)\,K_D}$ | kg solute/kg solvent | – |
| 5 | **Fraction extracted in 1 stage** | $E_1 = \dfrac{L \cdot y_1}{S \cdot x_0} = \dfrac{(L/S)\,K_D}{1 \,+\, (L/S)\,K_D}$ | dimensionless | Also written $E = 1 - (x_n / x_0)$ |
| 6 | **Fraction unextracted after $n$ stages (single solvent feed per stage)** | $\phi_n = \left(\dfrac{1}{1 \,+\, (L/S)\,K_D}\right)^{n}$ | dimensionless | Cross-current |
| 7 | **Fraction extracted in $n$ stages, cross-current** | $E_n = 1 \,-\, \phi_n = 1 \,-\, \left(\dfrac{1}{1 \,+\, (L/S)\,K_D}\right)^{n}$ | dimensionless | – |
| 8 | **Counter-current relationship** | $L \cdot y_n \,+\, S \cdot x_0 = L \cdot y_{n+1} \,+\, S \cdot x_n$ | – | Overall material balance |
| 9 | **Counter-current exit solid concentration** | $x_n = \dfrac{x_0 \,-\, (L/S)\,y_1}{1}$ | kg solute/kg solid | – |
| 10 | **Operating line, counter-current** | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{S}{L}\,(x_n - x_N) + y_N$ | – | $y_N$ = inlet solvent concentration |
| 11 | **Ideal stages by Kremser equation (counter-current)** | $N = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K_D}{x_N - y_{N+1}/K_D}\right)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{S}{L\,K_D}\right) + \dfrac{S}{L\,K_D}\right]}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{L\,K_D}{S}\right)}$ | dimensionless | For $L\,K_D / S \ne 1$ |
| 12 | **Height of transfer unit (HTU)** | $H_{OG} = \dfrac{G}{K_y \cdot a}$ | m | Continuous contact |
| 13 | **NTU** | $N_{OG} = \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \dfrac{(1-y)^{*}_{f}\,dy}{(1-y)(y^{*}-y)}$ | – | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical memory points:**
> - **$K_D = y/x$** at equilibrium (units = kg solvent / kg solid, but used as a ratio).
> - The **counter-current** configuration can achieve the **same recovery with less solvent** than cross-current, OR **higher recovery with the same solvent** — this is a board favorite!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Single-Stage Elution – Complete Derivation

**Problem Setup:** A solid of mass $S$ (kg solute-free solid) contains $x_0$ kg solute per kg solid. It is contacted with $L$ kg of solute-free solvent in one ideal stage. The equilibrium relation is linear: $y = K_D \cdot x$, where $y$ is in kg solute / kg solvent and $x$ is in kg solute / kg solid.

### 3.1.1 Total Material Balance (Solute)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Solute in (initial)} &= \text{Solute out (final)} \\[4pt]
S \cdot x_0 + L \cdot y_0 &= S \cdot x_1 + L \cdot y_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since fresh solvent is solute-free, $y_0 = 0$. So:

$$
S \cdot x_0 = S \cdot x_1 + L \cdot y_1
$$

### 3.1.2 Equilibrium at Exit

$$
y_1 = K_D \cdot x_1
$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting Equilibrium into Material Balance

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot x_0 &= S \cdot x_1 + L \cdot K_D \cdot x_1 \\[4pt]
S \cdot x_0 &= x_1 \,(S + L \cdot K_D) \\[4pt]
x_1 &= \dfrac{S \cdot x_0}{S + L \cdot K_D} = \dfrac{x_0}{1 + (L/S) \cdot K_D}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Fraction of Solute Extracted

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \dfrac{\text{Solute leaving in liquid}}{\text{Solute initially in solid}} \\[4pt]
E_1 &= \dfrac{L \cdot y_1}{S \cdot x_0} = \dfrac{L \cdot K_D \cdot x_1}{S \cdot x_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $x_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \dfrac{L \cdot K_D \cdot \dfrac{x_0}{1 + (L/S) K_D}}{S \cdot x_0} \\[4pt]
E_1 &= \dfrac{(L/S) \cdot K_D}{1 + (L/S) \cdot K_D}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical single-stage elution efficiency equation**.

## 3.2 Multi-Stage Cross-Current Elution – Derivation

**Problem Setup:** $n$ stages in series. **Each stage receives the same mass $L$ of fresh solvent.** The solid remains the same mass $S$ as it transfers from stage 1 → stage 2 → ... → stage $n$.

### 3.2.1 Stage 1

By analogy with single-stage:

$$
x_1 = \dfrac{x_0}{1 + (L/S) K_D}
$$

### 3.2.2 Stage 2 (input is $x_1$)

$$
x_2 = \dfrac{x_1}{1 + (L/S) K_D} = \dfrac{x_0}{\left[1 + (L/S) K_D\right]^2}
$$

### 3.2.3 Induction to Stage $n$

$$
x_n = \dfrac{x_0}{\left[1 + (L/S) K_D\right]^{n}}
$$

### 3.2.4 Fraction Extracted in $n$ Cross-Current Stages

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_n &= 1 - \dfrac{x_n}{x_0} \\[4pt]
E_n &= 1 - \left[\dfrac{1}{1 + (L/S) K_D}\right]^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** Increasing $n$ increases recovery, but with **diminishing returns** because the geometric term decays exponentially.

## 3.3 Multi-Stage Counter-Current Elution – Derivation (Kremser Approach)

**Setup:** $n$ stages in series. Solid moves stage 1 → stage $n$ with mass flow rate $S$ (kg solvent-free solid/hr), carrying solute at concentrations $x_0, x_1, ..., x_n$. Solvent moves in the opposite direction: stage $n$ ← stage 1, mass flow rate $L$, carrying solute at concentrations $y_1, y_2, ..., y_{n+1}$, where $y_{n+1}$ is the inlet (fresh solvent) concentration.

### 3.3.1 Material Balance Around the Whole Cascade

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot x_0 + L \cdot y_{n+1} &= S \cdot x_n + L \cdot y_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Material Balance Around the First $m$ Stages (Solid side)

$$
S \cdot (x_0 - x_m) = L \cdot (y_{m+1} - y_{n+1})
$$

Solving for $y_{m+1}$:

$$
y_{m+1} = \dfrac{S}{L}(x_0 - x_m) + y_{n+1}
$$

### 3.3.3 Equilibrium at Stage $m$

$$
y_m = K_D \cdot x_m \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; x_m = \dfrac{y_m}{K_D}
$$

### 3.3.4 Substituting into Stage-$(m+1)$ Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{y_{m+1}}{K_D} &= x_{m+1} = \text{expression from M.B. around stages 1..m+1} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow\; y_{m+1} &= \dfrac{S}{L}(x_0 - x_{m+1}) + y_{n+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $x_{m+1} = y_{m+1}/K_D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{m+1} &= \dfrac{S}{L}\left(x_0 - \dfrac{y_{m+1}}{K_D}\right) + y_{n+1} \\[4pt]
y_{m+1}\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{L K_D}\right) &= \dfrac{S \cdot x_0}{L} + y_{n+1} \\[4pt]
y_{m+1}\!\left(\dfrac{L K_D + S}{L K_D}\right) &= \dfrac{S \cdot x_0}{L} + y_{n+1} \\[4pt]
y_{m+1} &= \dfrac{K_D\,(S \cdot x_0 + L\,y_{n+1})}{L K_D + S}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.5 Closed-Form for $x_n$ via the **Kremser Equation**

For $n$ ideal counter-current stages with linear equilibrium $y = K_D x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_n &= \dfrac{x_0 \,-\, y_{n+1}/K_D \,+\, \left(\dfrac{S}{L K_D}\right)\!\left(y_1 \,-\, y_{n+1}\right)}{1 \,-\, (S/(L K_D))^{n+1}} \,\cdot\; \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

The standard, exam-friendly **Kremser formula** for exit solid composition:

$$
\boxed{\;
\dfrac{x_N - y_{N+1}/K_D}{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K_D} = \dfrac{(S/(L K_D)) - 1}{(S/(L K_D))^{N+1} - 1}
\;}
$$

This is the equation to write down directly when asked to determine $N$ for a target $x_N$.

### 3.3.6 Equivalent – Solving for $N$

For given $x_0, x_N, y_{N+1}, K_D, L, S$, the number of ideal counter-current stages is:

$$
N = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K_D}{x_N - y_{N+1}/K_D}\right]\,-\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{S}{L K_D}\right)}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{L K_D}{S}\right)}
$$

(valid for $\dfrac{L K_D}{S} \neq 1$).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (for self-practice)

**Given:** $S = 100$ kg solid/hr, $x_0 = 0.20$ kg solute/kg solid, $L = 50$ kg solvent/hr, $K_D = 2.0$, fresh solvent ($y_{n+1}=0$).

### (a) Single Stage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= \dfrac{x_0}{1 + (L/S) K_D} = \dfrac{0.20}{1 + (50/100)(2.0)} = \dfrac{0.20}{1 + 1.0} = \dfrac{0.20}{2.0} = 0.10 \;\text{kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

So extraction $E_1 = 50\%$.

### (b) Two-Stage Cross-Current (each stage: $L = 50$ kg):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2 &= \dfrac{x_0}{[1 + (L/S) K_D]^2} = \dfrac{0.20}{(2.0)^2} = \dfrac{0.20}{4.0} = 0.05 \;\text{kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Extraction $E_2 = 75\%$.

### (c) Two-Stage Counter-Current (with $y_3 = 0$):

$$
\dfrac{L K_D}{S} = \dfrac{50 \cdot 2.0}{100} = 1.0
$$

Since $L K_D / S = 1$, Kremser reduces to a linear relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_N &= x_0 - \dfrac{L}{S}(y_1 - y_3) / N_{\text{term}}
\end{aligned}
$$

But more directly, using the special-case Kremser simplification for $A = 1$:

$$
x_N = x_0 - N \cdot \dfrac{L}{S}\,K_D(x_0 - y_{n+1}/K_D)\,\Big/\,\ldots
$$

The standard method: operating line and equilibrium intersect graphically, or use:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_N - y_{n+1}/K_D &= \dfrac{x_0 - y_{n+1}/K_D}{N+1} \quad\text{for } \dfrac{L K_D}{S} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N = 2$, $y_{n+1}=0$:

$$
x_2 = \dfrac{x_0}{N+1} = \dfrac{0.20}{3} \approx 0.0667 \;\text{kg/kg}
$$

So $E_2 = (0.20 - 0.0667)/0.20 = 66.7\%$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Summary for this example:**
> - 1-stage: $E = 50.0\%$
> - 2-stage cross-current: $E = 75.0\%$
> - 2-stage counter-current: $E = 66.7\%$
>
> Cross-current wins **only when solvent is essentially free and solid must be maximally stripped**. Counter-current wins when solvent is expensive or a **concentrated extract** is desired.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ELUTION TYPES]:::root
    A --> B[SINGLE-STAGE ELUTION]:::stage
    A --> C[MULTI-STAGE ELUTION]:::stage
    A --> D[DIFFERENTIAL CONTINUOUS ELUTION]:::stage

    B --> B1[One mixer-settler or batch tank]:::detail
    B --> B2[Fresh solvent contacts whole solid batch]:::detail
    B --> B3[Lowest recovery, simplest design]:::detail

    C --> E[CROSS-CURRENT ELUTION]:::branch
    C --> F[COUNTER-CURRENT ELUTION]:::branch

    E --> E1[Solid moves forward stage to stage]:::detail
    E --> E2[Fresh solvent added at every stage]:::detail
    E --> E3[High solvent usage, dilute extract]:::detail

    F --> F1[Solid moves forward stage to stage]:::detail
    F --> F2[Solvent moves in opposite direction]:::detail
    F --> F3[Low solvent usage, concentrated extract]:::detail
    F --> F4[Industrial preferred choice]:::highlight

    D --> G[PACKED TOWER]:::detail
    D --> H[ROTATING EXTRACTOR e.g. BOLLMAN ROTOCEL]:::detail
    D --> I[CHROMATOGRAPHIC COLUMN]:::detail

    classDef root fill:#1f3a93,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0c1d54,stroke-width:3px
    classDef stage fill:#e8eaf6,color:#000000,stroke:#3f51b5,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#fff3e0,color:#000000,stroke:#ff9800,stroke-width:2px
    classDef detail fill:#f5f5f5,color:#000000,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:1px
    classDef highlight fill:#c8e6c9,color:#000000,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Counter-Current Multi-Stage Elution – Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED[SOLID FEED\nSolute-rich solid]:::input
    SOLV[FRESH SOLVENT\nSolute-free]:::input

    subgraph CCS[COUNTER-CURRENT ELUTION CASCADE]
        direction LR
        STG1[STAGE 1]:::stageNode
        STG2[STAGE 2]:::stageNode
        STG3[STAGE 3]:::stageNode
        STGN[STAGE N]:::stageNode
        STG1 -->|solid: x1| STG2
        STG2 -->|solid: x2| STG3
        STG3 -.-> STGN
    end

    EXTRACT[CONCENTRATED EXTRACT\nOverflow]:::output
    RAFFINATE[SPENT SOLID\nUnderflow]:::output

    FEED -->|x0| STG1
    SOLV -->|y_{N+1}=0| STGN
    STG1 -->|y1 overflow| EXTRACT
    STGN -->|xN underflow| RAFFINATE

    classDef input fill:#bbdefb,color:#000000,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,color:#000000,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stageNode fill:#ffe0b2,color:#000000,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Operating-Line vs. Equilibrium-Line Schematic (McCabe–Thiele Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Mass Transfer Driving Force Diagram]:::title
    X --> A[X-AXIS: x, kg solute/kg solid]:::axis
    X --> B[Y-AXIS: y, kg solute/kg solvent]:::axis
    X --> C[EQ LINE: y = K_D x, straight through origin]:::line
    X --> D[OPERATING LINE: counter-current, above eq line]:::line
    X --> E[Distance between lines = driving force]:::note
    X --> F[Stages = staircase between operating and eq lines]:::note
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define elution. List the major types of elution used in chemical engineering practice.**

**Model Answer:**

**Elution** is a solid–liquid mass transfer operation in which a solute is removed from a solid matrix by contacting it with a suitable solvent (the *eluent*) under conditions where the concentration gradient drives the solute out of the solid and into the bulk liquid phase. The driving force is governed by Fick's law and the equilibrium distribution coefficient $K_D$.

**Types of Elution:**
1. **Single-stage elution** – one ideal equilibrium contact.
2. **Multi-stage cross-current elution** – fresh solvent added at every stage.
3. **Multi-stage counter-current elution** – solvent and solid flow in opposite directions.
4. **Differential (continuous) contact elution** – in packed towers, Bollman/Rotocel extractors, chromatographic columns.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Compare cross-current and counter-current multi-stage elution in terms of (i) solvent consumption, (ii) extract concentration, and (iii) industrial applicability.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Criterion** | **Cross-Current** | **Counter-Current** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Solvent consumption | **Higher** – fresh solvent in every stage | **Lower** – single solvent stream |
| (ii) Extract concentration | **Dilute** – all extract streams combined are dilute | **Concentrated** – final extract richest |
| (iii) Industrial applicability | Lab / batch processes with cheap solvent | Continuous industrial processes (oilseeds, sugar, gold, ores) |

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** You must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **fraction of solute extracted in a single-stage ideal elution** when the equilibrium relation is $y = K_D \, x$.

**(b)** A solid containing **0.18 kg solute/kg solute-free solid** is to be washed in a single-stage elution using a solvent at a solvent-to-solid ratio $L/S = 4.0$ (by mass). The equilibrium distribution coefficient $K_D = 1.5$. Calculate:
   (i) The fraction of solute extracted in one stage.
   (ii) The exit liquid concentration $y_1$ in kg solute/kg solvent.
   (iii) The exit solid concentration $x_1$ in kg solute/kg solid.

#### Model Solution – (a) [7 Marks]

Step 1: **Material balance** on solute around the single equilibrium stage (in = out):
$$ S \cdot x_0 + L \cdot y_0 = S \cdot x_1 + L \cdot y_1 $$

With fresh solvent $y_0 = 0$:
$$ S \cdot x_0 = S \cdot x_1 + L \cdot y_1 $$

Step 2: **Equilibrium relation** at stage exit:
$$ y_1 = K_D \cdot x_1 $$

Step 3: **Eliminate $y_1$**:
$$ S \cdot x_0 = S \cdot x_1 + L \cdot K_D \cdot x_1 = x_1 \,(S + L \, K_D) $$

Step 4: Solve for $x_1$:
$$ x_1 = \dfrac{x_0}{1 + (L/S) \, K_D} $$

Step 5: Compute extraction fraction:
$$ E = \dfrac{L \cdot y_1}{S \cdot x_0} = \dfrac{L \cdot K_D \cdot x_1}{S \cdot x_0} = \dfrac{(L/S) \, K_D}{1 + (L/S) \, K_D} \;\;\boxed{} $$

#### Model Solution – (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $x_0 = 0.18$ kg/kg, $L/S = 4.0$, $K_D = 1.5$.

(i) **Fraction extracted in one stage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \dfrac{(L/S) \cdot K_D}{1 + (L/S) \cdot K_D} = \dfrac{4.0 \cdot 1.5}{1 + 4.0 \cdot 1.5} = \dfrac{6.0}{7.0} = 0.8571
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ 85.71% of solute is extracted in one stage.**

(ii) **Exit solid concentration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= \dfrac{x_0}{1 + (L/S) K_D} = \dfrac{0.18}{7.0} = 0.0257 \;\text{kg solute/kg solid}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iii) **Exit liquid concentration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= K_D \cdot x_1 = 1.5 \cdot 0.0257 = 0.0386 \;\text{kg solute/kg solvent}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ $y_1 \approx 0.0386$ kg/kg.**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the working of **multi-stage counter-current elution**. State its two main advantages over cross-current operation.

**(b)** A counter-current elution cascade has **4 ideal stages**. Solid enters at $x_0 = 0.25$ kg solute/kg solid and leaves at $x_4 = 0.025$ kg/kg. The solvent is solute-free ($y_5 = 0$) and the equilibrium relation is $y = 2.0 \, x$. The solvent-to-solid ratio is $L/S = 0.80$. Verify if these operating conditions are feasible. If not, find the minimum number of stages required to achieve the same $x_4$ using the **Kremser equation**.

#### Model Solution – (a) [7 Marks]

In a **counter-current elution cascade**, the solute-bearing solid and the solvent flow in **opposite directions** through a series of $n$ ideal mixing-settler stages (or a continuous tower). The fresh solvent enters the stage where the **nearly-stripped solid** exits, while the fresh solid enters where the **solute-rich solvent** exits. This creates a high driving force at every stage along the cascade.

**Sketch (text representation):**

```
       ┌────────────┐
S0 ──▶│  STAGE  1  │──▶ EXTRACT (y1, concentrated)
       └─────┬──────┘
             │ x1 ↓
       ┌────────────┐
       │  STAGE  2  │
       └─────┬──────┘
             │ x2 ↓
       ┌────────────┐
       │  STAGE  3  │
       └─────┬──────┘
             │ x3 ↓
       ┌────────────┐
       │  STAGE  4  │◀── FRESH SOLVENT (y5 = 0)
       └─────┬──────┘
             ▼
          SPENT SOLID (x4)
```

**Two advantages over cross-current:**
1. **Lower solvent consumption** for the same recovery (or higher recovery for the same solvent).
2. **More concentrated extract**, reducing downstream solvent-recovery costs.

#### Model Solution – (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $N = 4$, $x_0 = 0.25$, $x_4 = 0.025$, $y_5 = 0$, $K_D = 2.0$, $L/S = 0.80$.

**Absorption factor:**
$$ A = \dfrac{L \cdot K_D}{S} = 0.80 \cdot 2.0 = 1.6 $$

**Kremser equation** (general form):

$$
\dfrac{x_N - y_{N+1}/K_D}{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K_D} = \dfrac{A - 1}{A^{N+1} - 1}
$$

Substituting $y_{N+1} = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{x_N}{x_0} = \dfrac{A - 1}{A^{N+1} - 1}
$$

For $N = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{x_4}{x_0} &= \dfrac{1.6 - 1}{(1.6)^{5} - 1} = \dfrac{0.6}{10.48576 - 1} = \dfrac{0.6}{9.48576} \approx 0.0633
\end{aligned}
$$

So with $N = 4$:

$$
x_4^{\text{calc}} = 0.0633 \cdot 0.25 = 0.0158 \;\text{kg/kg}
$$

Since $x_4^{\text{calc}} = 0.0158 < x_4^{\text{target}} = 0.025$, the **specified 4 stages are MORE than enough** — recovery is even better than required. So $N = 4$ is feasible.

However, if the question asks for **minimum number of stages to just achieve $x_4 = 0.025$**, set up:

$$
\dfrac{0.025}{0.25} = 0.10 = \dfrac{0.6}{1.6^{N+1} - 1}
$$

Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.6^{N+1} - 1 &= 6.0 \\[4pt]
1.6^{N+1} &= 7.0 \\[4pt]
(N+1)\,\ln 1.6 &= \ln 7.0 \\[4pt]
N+1 &= \dfrac{\ln 7.0}{\ln 1.6} = \dfrac{1.9459}{0.4700} \approx 4.14 \\[4pt]
N &\approx 3.14 \;\Rightarrow\; N_{\min} = 4 \;\text{ideal stages (round up to next integer).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Minimum $N = 4$ ideal stages** are needed.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Elution problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $K_D = y/x$ vs. $K_D = x/y$**: The convention matters! KTU adopts $y = K_D \cdot x$ in most module questions.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units**: $x$ is per kg **solute-free solid**, not per kg total slurry. $y$ is per kg **pure solvent**, not per kg solution.
> 3. **Using single-stage formula for multi-stage**: The single-stage $E_1$ formula is **NOT** linearly extrapolated to $N$ stages for counter-current. Use Kremser.
> 4. **Confusing "cross-current" and "counter-current"**: In cross-current, **solvent is fresh at every stage**. In counter-current, **solvent flows opposite to solid**.
> 5. **Wrong $y_{N+1}$ convention**: $y_{N+1}$ is the **inlet solvent** concentration (at the end where solid exits), not at the feed.
> 6. **Skipping the induction step in derivation**: For cross-current, board examiners expect the explicit "stage 1 → stage 2 → ... → stage $n$" induction, not just the final formula.
> 7. **Missing the equilibrium relation statement**: Always write $y = K_D x$ explicitly before using it in derivations.

## 5.4 Topic Recap – Important Things to Remember

- **Elution** = solvent extraction from a solid matrix (the "reverse of adsorption").
- **Types:** Single-stage, **Cross-current**, **Counter-current**, Differential/Continuous.
- **Cross-current**: fresh solvent every stage → high recovery but **dilute extract, high solvent use**.
- **Counter-current**: solvent opposes solid flow → **best solvent economy + concentrated extract** → industrial gold standard.
- **Single-stage formula**: $E_1 = \dfrac{(L/S) K_D}{1 + (L/S) K_D}$
- **Cross-current $n$-stage formula**: $E_n = 1 - \left[\dfrac{1}{1 + (L/S) K_D}\right]^n$
- **Counter-current formula (Kremser)**:
$$ \dfrac{x_N - y_{N+1}/K_D}{x_0 - y_{N+1}/K_D} = \dfrac{A - 1}{A^{N+1} - 1}, \quad A = \dfrac{L\,K_D}{S} $$
- **Operating line (counter-current)**: $y_{n+1} = (S/L)(x_n - x_N) + y_N$
- **Industrial examples**: Gold–cyanide (Zadra), Bayer process (alumina), oilseed extraction (Rotocel/Bollman), sugar beet diffusion (CCD), pharmaceutical chromatographic purification.
- **Selection rule**: Use **counter-current** unless solvent is essentially free, in which case **cross-current** may give marginally better recovery.
- **Driving force**: Always $(\,y^* - y\,)$ or $(\,x - x^*\,)$ — never zero at any stage in an ideal cascade.
- **Assumptions**: Linear equilibrium ($y = K_D x$), ideal stages, constant $L$ and $S$ flows (no solute in inlet solid's inert mass, no solvent in inlet solvent's inert mass).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT503 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas-Liquid

---

# Gas–Liquid Contacting Operations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas–Liquid (G–L) Contacting Operations** are a class of mass transfer operations in which a **gas phase** and a **liquid phase** are brought into intimate contact to effect the transfer of one or more soluble components between the phases. These operations are governed by **interfacial mass transfer** driven by concentration gradients and are typified by **gas absorption** (transfer from gas to liquid), **desorption/stripping** (transfer from liquid to gas), **humidification/dehumidification**, and **distillation**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, gas–liquid operations are classified under **diffusion-controlled, two-film interfacial transport** phenomena, where the rate of transfer is dictated by the **overall mass transfer coefficient** $K_G$ or $K_L$, the **interfacial area** $a$, and the **concentration driving force** $(\Delta y$ or $\Delta x)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCBBT503 – Module 4):**
> Study of **absorption equipment** (plate columns, packed columns, spray towers, venturi scrubbers, bubble columns), operating characteristics, choice of solvent, choice between plate and packed columns, and design parameters like **HETP**, **HTU**, **NTU**, and overall column height $H$.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of **gas absorption** like a sponge (the liquid) soaking up colored vapor (the gas) in a closed room. The faster the sponge is exposed to fresh vapor and the larger the contact surface, the more vapor it absorbs. The "thirst" of the sponge is the **concentration driving force**, the "wetness" of the surface is the **mass transfer coefficient**, and the "total surface area" of sponge exposed is the **interfacial area**.

A real-world example: A **soda water carbonator** — pressurized $\text{CO}_2$ gas contacts water inside a packed tower. The water absorbs $\text{CO}_2$ until it becomes carbonated. The smaller the bubbles (more interfacial area) and the higher the pressure (greater driving force), the faster the absorption.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Henry's constant | $H$ | **atm·m³/kmol** (gas solubility indicator) |
| Diffusivity in gas | $D_{AB}$ | $\sim 10^{-5}$ **m²/s** |
| Diffusivity in liquid | $D_{AB,L}$ | $\sim 10^{-9}$ **m²/s** |
| Packed column pressure drop | $\Delta P$ | **200 – 600 Pa/m** (typical design) |
| Operating temperature | $T$ | **298 – 333 K** (industrial absorption) |
| Operating pressure | $P$ | **1 – 30 atm** (pressure absorption) |

---

## 2. Why Gas–Liquid Operations Matter

Gas–liquid operations are the **workhorses of the chemical, petrochemical, fertilizer, and pollution-control industries**. They are used for:

- **Pollution abatement** — scrubbing $\text{SO}_2$, $\text{NO}_x$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ from flue gas.
- **Ammonia synthesis** — absorbing $\text{NH}_3$ from synthesis gas.
- **Natural gas sweetening** — removing $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ and $\text{CO}_2$ using amine solutions.
- **Refrigeration** — absorbing refrigerant vapor in absorbent liquid.
- **Carbon capture** — contacting $\text{CO}_2$ with amines or alkaline solvents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Gas–Liquid Contacting Equipment

### A. Plate (Tray) Columns
- Liquid flows **horizontally across** each plate; gas **bubbles up** through the liquid.
- Dispersed phase: **gas**; Continuous phase: **liquid**.
- Operate in **stage-wise** manner — each tray approximates an **equilibrium stage**.
- Common types: **sieve tray, valve tray, bubble-cap tray**.

### B. Packed Columns
- Liquid flows **downward** over packing; gas flows **upward** through void spaces.
- Provide **continuous differential contact** (not stage-wise).
- Packing types: **Raschig rings, Pall rings, Berl saddles, Intalox saddles, structured packings**.
- Operate in **loading** and **flooding** regimes.

### C. Spray Columns
- Liquid is **atomized** through nozzles; gas flows through the spray.
- Very **low pressure drop**, low interfacial area.
- Used for **particulate removal** and **highly soluble gas absorption** (e.g., $\text{HCl}$, $\text{NH}_3$).

### D. Venturi Scrubbers
- Gas accelerated through a **converging–diverging** throat; liquid injected at throat.
- Used for **dust and gas absorption simultaneously**.

### E. Bubble Columns
- Gas sparged into a **pool of liquid** at the bottom.
- Used in **fermentation** and **slurry reactors** (e.g., $\text{Cl}_2$ absorption in water).

---

## 2.2 The Two-Film Theory (Foundation of G–L Mass Transfer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Whitman's Two-Film Theory (1923):** Mass transfer between two fluid phases is governed by **stagnant films** adjacent to the interface. Steady-state diffusion occurs through these films; the bulk phases are perfectly mixed.

For component $A$ transferring from gas to liquid:

$$N_A = k_G (y_A - y_{A,i}) = k_L (x_{A,i} - x_A)$$

where:
- $k_G$ = **individual gas-phase mass transfer coefficient** (kmol/m²·s·Δy)
- $k_L$ = **individual liquid-phase mass transfer coefficient** (kmol/m²·s·Δx)
- $y_A, x_A$ = bulk compositions
- $y_{A,i}, x_{A,i}$ = interfacial compositions

### Overall Mass Transfer Coefficients

$$N_A = K_G (y_A - y_A^*) = K_L (x_A^* - x_A)$$

where $y_A^*$ is the gas composition in equilibrium with $x_A$, and $x_A^*$ is the liquid composition in equilibrium with $y_A$.

The relationship between overall and individual coefficients (for dilute systems):

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$

$$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{m \cdot k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L}$$

where $m = dy/dx$ is the **slope of the equilibrium line** at the operating point.

---

## 2.3 Equilibrium: Henry's Law

For **dilute gas absorption** (the most common KTU problem), the equilibrium relationship is linear:

$$y_A = m \cdot x_A \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad p_A = H \cdot c_A$$

where:
- $m$ = slope of equilibrium line (dimensionless, in mole fraction terms) = $H/P$
- $H$ = **Henry's constant** (Pa·m³/mol or atm·m³/kmol)

---

## 2.4 Operating Line and Minimum Solvent Rate

For a **counter-current packed absorption column**:

$$L \cdot dX = G \cdot dY \quad \Rightarrow \quad L(X_1 - X_2) = G(Y_1 - Y_2)$$

Operating line (linear when L, G are constant):

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} X + \left( Y_2 - \frac{L}{G} X_2 \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Liquid Rate $L_{\min}$:** The straight line joining $(X_2, Y_2)$ and touching the equilibrium curve at one point. As $L \to \infty$, the operating line becomes vertical; as $L \to L_{\min}$, the column height becomes infinite. Design $L$ is chosen as $1.2$ to $1.5 \times L_{\min}$.

$$\frac{L_{\min}}{G} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$$

where $X_1^*$ is the liquid composition in equilibrium with $Y_1$.

---

## 2.5 Height of Packed Column

The required column height is calculated by:

$$H = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} \quad \text{or} \quad H = H_{OL} \cdot N_{OL}$$

where:
- $N_{OG}$ = **number of overall gas-phase transfer units** (dimensionless)
- $H_{OG}$ = **height of an overall gas-phase transfer unit** (m)

$$N_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$$

$$H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_G \cdot a \cdot (1 - y)_M \cdot \Omega}$$

where $\Omega$ is the cross-sectional area of the column, $a$ is the interfacial area per unit volume, and $(1 - y)_M$ is the **log-mean correction for high concentrations**.

### Height of Transfer Units (HTU) Formulas

$$\frac{1}{H_{OG}} = \frac{1}{H_G} + \frac{mG}{L H_L}$$

For dilute systems: $(1 - y)_M \approx 1$ and $H_{OG} \approx \frac{G}{K_G a}$.

---

## 2.6 Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP)

For **plate columns**:

$$H = HETP \cdot N_{theoretical}$$

- **HETP** is the height of packing that gives the same separation as one theoretical plate.
- Typical HETP values: **0.4 – 0.6 m** for Raschig rings, **0.3 – 0.5 m** for Pall rings.

---

## 2.7 Flooding & Loading in Packed Columns

> [!WARNING]
> **Flooding is the catastrophic operating limit** in packed columns. At flooding, liquid accumulates in the packing and gas can no longer pass upward. **Always design below 70–80% of the flooding velocity.**

Flooding velocity is estimated using the **Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC)** by Sherwood, Leva, Eckert, and Kister — a plot of:
- $X$-axis: $\left( \frac{L}{G} \right) \left( \frac{\rho_G}{\rho_L} \right)^{0.5}$
- $Y$-axis: $\frac{G^2 \cdot F_p \cdot \mu_L^{0.2}}{g_c \cdot \rho_G \cdot \rho_L}$

where $F_p$ is the **packing factor** (m⁻¹).

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **definitive reference** for solving KTU Module 4 problems. Memorize the formulas, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two-film transfer rate | $N_A = k_G(y_A - y_{A,i}) = k_L(x_{A,i} - x_A)$ | $k_G$ (kmol/m²·s), $k_L$ (kmol/m²·s) | Interfacial transfer |
| 2 | Overall rate (gas-side) | $N_A = K_G(y_A - y_A^*)$ | $K_G$ (kmol/m²·s) | Bulk driving force |
| 3 | Overall rate (liquid-side) | $N_A = K_L(x_A^* - x_A)$ | $K_L$ (kmol/m²·s) | Bulk driving force |
| 4 | Resistance-in-series | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | $m$ (slope of eq. line) | Combine phase resistances |
| 5 | Resistance-in-series | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G} + \dfrac{1}{k_L}$ | — | Combine phase resistances |
| 6 | Henry's law | $y_A = m \cdot x_A$ | $m$ (dimensionless) | Dilute systems |
| 7 | Mass balance | $L(X_1 - X_2) = G(Y_1 - Y_2)$ | $L, G$ (kmol/s) | Inlet/outlet streams |
| 8 | Operating line | $Y = \dfrac{L}{G} X + \left( Y_2 - \dfrac{L}{G} X_2 \right)$ | — | Counter-current |
| 9 | Minimum L/G | $\left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \dfrac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$ | — | Design starting point |
| 10 | Column height | $H = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | $H$ (m) | Packed column |
| 11 | $N_{OG}$ integral | $N_{OG} = \displaystyle\int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \dfrac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$ | Dimensionless | Graphical or numerical |
| 12 | $N_{OG}$ (linear) | $N_{OG} = \dfrac{Y_1 - Y_2}{\Delta Y_{\ln}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{\Delta Y_1}{\Delta Y_2}\right)$ | — | Linear equilibrium |
| 13 | $H_{OG}$ definition | $H_{OG} = \dfrac{G}{K_G a\,(1-y)_M}$ | m | Packing property |
| 14 | HTU relationship | $\dfrac{1}{H_{OG}} = \dfrac{1}{H_G} + \dfrac{mG}{L H_L}$ | m | Combine $H_G$ and $H_L$ |
| 15 | Colburn equation | $N_{OG} = \displaystyle\int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \dfrac{(1-y)_M \, dY}{(1-y)(Y-Y^*)}$ | Dimensionless | High concentration |
| 16 | Fick's law (gas) | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,p_{BM}} \cdot \dfrac{dy_A}{dz}$ | $D_{AB}$ (m²/s) | Stagnant film diffusion |
| 17 | Kremser equation | $\dfrac{Y_1 - mX_2}{Y_2 - mX_2} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | $A = L/(mG)$ | Plate column (constant $m$) |
| 18 | HETP | $H = HETP \times N_{theoretical}$ | m | Plate column |
| 19 | Flooding condition | $\Delta P / \Delta P_{\text{flood}} \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$ | — | Design criterion |
| 20 | Absorber factor | $A = L / (mG)$ | Dimensionless | Stage calculations |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pollution control**: $\text{SO}_2$ absorption in limestone slurry (Flue Gas Desulfurization).
- **Ammonia industry**: $\text{NH}_3$ absorption from syngas using water.
- **Petroleum refining**: $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ removal from natural gas using MEA/DEA (Amine treating).
- **Pharmaceutical**: $\text{CO}_2$ removal from fermentation off-gas.
- **Carbon capture**: $\text{CO}_2$ absorption in MEA, MDEA, or hot potassium carbonate solutions.
- **Chlor-alkali**: $\text{Cl}_2$ absorption in water to make HCl.
